The functional design specification is typically captured in a **Functional Design Specification (FDS)** or **Product Design Specification (PDS)** document. This document is a living, version-controlled artifact that bridges the *voice of the customer (VOC)* with the *voice of the engineer (VOE)*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Designing a School Backpack:**
> Before you stitch a single seam, you *functionally* decide that the bag must (a) carry **10 kg** of books without strap failure, (b) survive being **dragged across rough concrete**, (c) be **water-resistant** in Kerala monsoons, (d) remain **comfortable** on a teenager's shoulders, and (e) look **aesthetically appealing** enough to be purchased. These are *functional requirements* — they define the job. The choice of *nylon 6,6* vs *polyester* vs *canvas* is *configuration/material design*. The seam pattern and zipper placement is *detail design*. Functional design is the **briefing document** that says "this bag must do X, Y, Z," and engineering then reverse-engineers the rest.

In the same way, for a polymer product (say, a **polypropylene automotive bumper beam**), the functional design phase answers:
- What crash energy must it absorb? (**15 kJ at 4 km/h**)
- What temperature range? (**−40 °C to +85 °C**)
- What UV exposure? (**3 years outdoor weathering**)
- What stiffness? (**Flexural modulus ≥ 1.5 GPa**)
- What mass budget? (**≤ 4.2 kg**)

### 1.3 The Seven Pillars of Functional Design

Functional design of a polymer product is decomposed into **seven orthogonal requirement domains** (this is the canonical KTU classification for PCPOT601):

| # | Requirement Domain | Engineering Question Answered | Typical Polymers |
|---|-------------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | Will it carry the load without yielding, fracturing, or excessive deflection? | PA 6, POM, PC, PP, GF-reinforced grades |
| 2 | **Thermal** | Will it survive the service temperature without softening, distorting, or oxidizing? | PPS, PEEK, PTFE, PEI |
| 3 | **Electrical** | Will it insulate, conduct, or dissipate static as required? | PE, XLPE, PP (insulators); PC/ABS, PS (anti-static grades) |
| 4 | **Chemical / Environmental** | Will it resist fuels, acids, alkalis, UV, humidity, and ozone? | PTFE, PVDF, PP, HDPE |
| 5 | **Optical** | Must it be transparent, translucent, opaque, or colored? | PMMA, PC, PS, transparent ABS |
| 6 | **Aesthetic / Ergonomic** | Will the user find it visually appealing, comfortable, and safe to touch? | TPEs, soft-touch overmoulded polymers |
| 7 | **Manufacturing / Assembly** | Can it be injection-moulded, extruded, blow-moulded, and snap-fit? | All processable thermoses/thermosets |

### 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics in Functional Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Functional Design Specification is **always quantitative**. Qualitative wishes ("strong," "durable") are **not acceptable** in KTU board answers. Every functional requirement must be expressed with a **numerical value + a tolerance + a test method**.

Mandatory metric qualifiers in a KTU-grade FDS:
- **Magnitude** (e.g., tensile strength ≥ 60 MPa)
- **Tolerance** (e.g., ± 5 %)
- **Test standard** (e.g., ASTM D638, ISO 527)
- **Service condition** (e.g., at 23 °C / 50 % RH)
- **Failure criterion** (e.g., no yielding, no cracking under 10× magnification)

### 1.5 Why Functional Design Comes First — The Causal Chain

> [!NOTE]
> A polymer product designer who **skips** functional design will inevitably face one of these failures:
> 1. **Brittle fracture** of a snap-fit because impact strength was not specified.
> 2. **Creep rupture** of a polymer gear because long-term modulus was not considered.
> 3. **Thermal distortion** of an electrical housing because HDT was below operating temperature.
> 4. **Stress cracking** of a fuel-line component because ESC resistance was unspecified.
> 5. **UV degradation** of a garden chair because UV stabilizer package was not mandated.

Functional design is therefore the **insurance policy** of product engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Functional Design Process — Structured Logic

The KTU 2024 Scheme treats functional design as a **systematic 6-step closed-loop process**. The steps are:

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Need (Problem Definition)**
   - Market research, customer interviews, regulatory study, competitive benchmarking.
   - Output: a list of *needs* expressed as functional verbs (carry, seal, insulate, resist, transmit).

2. **Step 2 — Translate Needs into Measurable Specifications (QFD / House of Quality)**
   - Each need is mapped to a measurable engineering metric.
   - The **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** matrix (House of Quality) correlates *customer requirements* (WHATs) with *technical requirements* (HOWs).
   - Output: a *weighted* list of specifications with target values.

3. **Step 3 — Establish Operating Conditions & Service Environment**
   - Loading type (static, dynamic, impact, cyclic, fluctuating).
   - Temperature range (min, max, gradient, cycling).
   - Chemical exposure (concentration, duration, temperature).
   - Radiation (UV, gamma, electron beam).
   - Biological factors (microbial, body fluids for medical devices).

4. **Step 4 — Apply Engineering Principles to Define Geometry & Material Class**
   - Stress analysis → wall thickness, rib design, fillet radii.
   - Thermal analysis → heat sinks, insulation barriers.
   - Fluid flow analysis → channel cross-sections, gate locations.

5. **Step 5 — Apply Safety Factors and Design Allowables**
   - Use **design allowables** (A-basis, B-basis, S-basis per MIL-HDBK-17 for composites; analogous philosophy for polymers).
   - Apply **safety factor (n)** based on:
     - Confidence in load data
     - Confidence in material data
     - Failure consequences
     - Service environment severity
     - Inspection accessibility

6. **Step 6 — Validate, Iterate, and Document**
   - FEA / CAE simulation (Moldflow, ANSYS Polyflow, Abaqus).
   - Prototype testing per ASTM/ISO standards.
   - Design review (DFMEA — Design Failure Mode and Effects Analysis).
   - Update FDS document.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step (Engineering Reasoning)

- **Step 1 matters** because without a clear problem, the engineering team optimizes for the wrong target. Classic example: *designing the world's strongest plastic fork that nobody wants to buy*.
- **Step 2 matters** because qualitative requirements lead to subjective disputes. *"Make it strong enough"* is unanswerable; *"withstand a 50 N tip-load with < 2 mm deflection"* is testable.
- **Step 3 matters** because polymers are **highly sensitive** to environment. Nylon 6,6 loses ~50 % of its stiffness when wet; PC yellows under UV; PMMA softens at 90 °C.
- **Step 4 matters** because the **same function can be achieved with different geometries** — a stiff beam can be a solid I-section, a ribbed plate, or a sandwich panel.
- **Step 5 matters** because polymer properties have **high scatter** (coefficient of variation of strength can be 8–15 %, vs 3–5 % for metals). Safety factors of **3 to 10** are routine in polymer design (vs 1.5–2.5 for steels).
- **Step 6 matters** because polymers are **time-dependent viscoelastic materials**; short-term tests may not predict 10-year service life. Validation through **accelerated ageing** is essential.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all board-essential equations** for functional design of polymer products. Memorize the formula, the meaning of every symbol, the unit, and the **typical polymer value range**.

| # | Engineering Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical SI Unit | Typical Polymer Value |
|---|----------------------|---------|----------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | F = tensile force, A₀ = original cross-section | Pa (N/m²) | 20 – 250 MPa |
| 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | ΔL = elongation, L₀ = gauge length | dimensionless | 0.02 – 5.0 (2 % – 500 %) |
| 3 | Young's Modulus (Tensile) | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | σ = stress, ε = strain in elastic region | Pa | 0.05 – 10 GPa |
| 4 | Flexural Modulus (3-point bend) | $E_f = \dfrac{L^3 \, m}{4 \, b \, d^3}$ | L = span, m = slope of load-deflection, b = width, d = thickness | Pa | 1 – 300 GPa (incl. composites) |
| 5 | Deflection of Cantilever Beam | $\delta = \dfrac{F \, L^3}{3 \, E \, I}$ | F = tip load, L = length, I = 2nd moment of area | m | depends on geometry |
| 6 | Deflection of Simply Supported Beam (center load) | $\delta = \dfrac{F \, L^3}{48 \, E \, I}$ | F = center load | m | depends on geometry |
| 7 | Second Moment of Area (rectangular) | $I = \dfrac{b \, h^3}{12}$ | b = width, h = height | m⁴ | — |
| 8 | Polar Second Moment (circular shaft) | $J = \dfrac{\pi \, d^4}{32}$ | d = shaft diameter | m⁴ | — |
| 9 | Shear Modulus | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | ν = Poisson's ratio | Pa | 0.02 – 4 GPa |
| 10 | Poisson's Ratio (typical polymer) | $\nu \approx 0.35$ to $0.40$ | — | dimensionless | 0.35 – 0.45 (rubber → 0.49) |
| 11 | Safety Factor (Stress-based) | $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ or $n = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$ | subscript = property type | dimensionless | 3 – 10 for polymers |
| 12 | Allowable / Design Stress | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{ult}}}{n}$ | n = safety factor | Pa | — |
| 13 | Creep Modulus (Isochronous) | $E_c(t) = \dfrac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon(t)}$ | σ₀ = applied stress, ε(t) = strain at time t | Pa | drops 2× – 5× over decades |
| 14 | Stress Relaxation Modulus | $E_r(t) = \dfrac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$ | σ(t) = decaying stress at fixed strain | Pa | — |
| 15 | Time-Temperature Superposition Shift | $\log a_T = \dfrac{-C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$ | WLF equation, C₁ ≈ 17.44, C₂ ≈ 51.6 K | dimensionless | T above Tg |
| 16 | Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) | load = $0.45$ MPa or $1.82$ MPa; deflection = $0.25$ mm | — | °C | 60 – 300 °C |
| 17 | Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0 \, \Delta T}$ | ΔL = thermal expansion, ΔT = temperature change | K⁻¹ | 30 – 200 × 10⁻⁶ /K |
| 18 | Thermal Stress (constrained) | $\sigma_T = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | E, α, ΔT as above | Pa | — |
| 19 | Izod / Charpy Impact Energy | $E_{\text{impact}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{A_{\text{notch}}}$ | A = notch cross-section | J/m | 20 – 1000 J/m |
| 20 | Fatigue Life (Basquin) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \, (2 N_f)^b$ | σ_f' = fatigue strength coeff., b = fatigue strength exponent | Pa | — |
| 21 | Stress Intensity Factor (Mode I) | $K_I = Y \, \sigma \, \sqrt{\pi \, a}$ | Y = geometry factor, a = crack length | Pa·m^½ | — |
| 22 | Fracture Toughness | $K_{IC} = Y \, \sigma_c \, \sqrt{\pi \, a_c}$ | subscript c = critical | Pa·m^½ | 1 – 5 MPa·m^½ (typical polymer) |
| 23 | Strain Energy Release Rate | $G_{IC} = \dfrac{K_{IC}^2}{E}$ (plane stress) | — | J/m² | 100 – 5000 J/m² |
| 24 | Dielectric Strength | $E_b = \dfrac{V_b}{t}$ | V_b = breakdown voltage, t = thickness | V/m or kV/mm | 10 – 40 kV/mm |
| 25 | Volume Resistivity | $\rho_v = \dfrac{R \, A}{l}$ | R = resistance, A = area, l = length | Ω·m | 10¹⁰ – 10¹⁸ Ω·m (insulators) |
| 26 | Water Absorption (24 h) | $WA\% = \dfrac{m_2 - m_1}{m_1} \times 100$ | m₁ = dry mass, m₂ = wet mass | % | 0.01 – 3.0 % |
| 27 | Mould Shrinkage | $S\% = \dfrac{L_{\text{mould}} - L_{\text{part}}}{L_{\text{mould}}} \times 100$ | — | % | 0.1 – 4.0 % |
| 28 | Rule of Mixtures (modulus of composite) | $E_c = E_f \, V_f + E_m \, (1 - V_f)$ | subscripts f = fibre, m = matrix, V = volume fraction | Pa | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall:** Many students confuse *engineering stress* (uses A₀) with *true stress* (uses instantaneous A). For polymers that neck heavily (e.g., LDPE, PP), the difference is huge. **Always state** which stress you are using.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations and design rules are not academic exercises — they directly drive decisions in:

- **Automotive industry:** Bumper beam, dashboard, fuel-line, headlamp housing.
- **Medical devices:** Syringe plungers (PE/PP), dialysis housings (PC), implant-grade PEEK.
- **Aerospace:** PAEK-based composites, PPS ducting, PTFE seals.
- **Packaging:** PET bottles (barrier + transparency), PP caps (creep + torque retention).
- **Electronics:** Epoxy PCB substrates (Tg, CTE match to copper), connector housings (LCP, PPS).
- **Piping:** HDPE pressure pipes (PE-RT, PE-100, long-term hydrostatic strength per ISO 4427).
- **Consumer goods:** Chair frames, helmet shells (ABS, PC, GF-PP).

In every case, the **functional design** defines the boundary conditions for these equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Allowable Stress from Ultimate Stress using Safety Factor

**Starting Principle (Newton's 2nd law on a bar under axial load):**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Step 1 — State the design philosophy**

In polymer product design, the working stress must always be **lower** than the failure stress, scaled by a multiplicative safety factor $n$:

$$n \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}}$$

Here $\sigma_{\text{failure}}$ may be $\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}$ (for brittle polymers like PS, PMMA) or $\sigma_{\text{yield}}$ (for ductile polymers like LDPE, PA).

**Step 2 — Rearrange for working (allowable) stress**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

Suppose a **polypropylene snap-fit** has ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u = 32$ MPa, and the design calls for $n = 5$ (typical for cyclic-loaded thermoplastics with uncertain load data).

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{32 \text{ MPa}}{5} = 6.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation**

The snap-fit cross-section must be sized so that under peak service load, the induced stress $\sigma_{\text{induced}} \le 6.4$ MPa. This is the **functional design constraint** that the detail-designer translates into wall thickness and rib geometry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cantilever Beam Deflection of a Polymer Spring

A common functional design problem: design a **PP cantilever spring** (e.g., for a battery contact in a TV remote).

**Step 1 — Define geometry and load**

Let the cantilever have:
- Length $L$ (m)
- Rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ (m × m)
- Tip load $F$ (N), acting vertically downward
- Material: PP, $E = 1.5 \text{ GPa} = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 2 — Second moment of area**

For a rectangular cross-section with bending about the neutral axis parallel to $b$:

$$I \;=\; \frac{b \, h^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Standard beam-theory result (Euler–Bernoulli)**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{F \, L^3}{3 \, E \, I}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute I**

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{F \, L^3}{3 \, E} \cdot \frac{12}{b \, h^3} \;=\; \frac{4 \, F \, L^3}{E \, b \, h^3}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for required thickness h**

Functional requirement: $\delta_{\text{tip}} \le 0.5$ mm under $F = 0.5$ N. Given $L = 25$ mm = 0.025 m, $b = 8$ mm = 0.008 m, $E = 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa.

$$h^3 \;=\; \frac{4 \, F \, L^3}{E \, b \, \delta_{\text{allow}}}$$

$$h^3 = \frac{4 \times 0.5 \times (0.025)^3}{1.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.008 \times 0.0005}$$

**Step 6 — Numerator**

$$4 \times 0.5 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-5} = 3.125 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 7 — Denominator**

$$1.5 \times 10^9 \times 0.008 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 6000$$

**Step 8 — Ratio**

$$h^3 = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{-5}}{6000} = 5.208 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 9 — Cube root**

$$h = (5.208 \times 10^{-9})^{1/3} = 1.735 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 1.74 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 10 — Design verdict**

A PP cantilever of 8 mm × 1.74 mm × 25 mm will deflect ≤ 0.5 mm under 0.5 N. This is a **functional-design-satisfied geometry**. The detail designer can then add draft angles, gate locations, and knit-line avoidance rules.

> [!NOTE]
> The above derivation is a **standard KTU board derivation**. The student is expected to write down Step 1 (geometry), Step 2 (I), Step 3 (δ formula), and proceed symbolically before plugging numbers.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Stress in a Constrained Polymer Pipe

A polymer pipe (HDPE) is clamped between two rigid flanges. The installation is at $T_1 = 25$ °C; the operating temperature is $T_2 = 60$ °C. Calculate the **thermal stress** generated if the pipe cannot expand.

**Step 1 — Free thermal strain**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \, \Delta T$$

For HDPE, $\alpha = 140 \times 10^{-6} \text{ K}^{-1}$ and $\Delta T = 60 - 25 = 35 \text{ K}$.

$$\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = 140 \times 10^{-6} \times 35 = 4.9 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 2 — If constrained, the same strain appears as mechanical strain in compression**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = -\varepsilon_{\text{th}}$$

The negative sign indicates compressive reaction from the flanges.

**Step 3 — Hooke's law**

$$\sigma_T = E \, \varepsilon_{\text{mech}} = -E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

For HDPE at 60 °C, the **secant modulus** drops to approximately $E = 0.25 \text{ GPa} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$ (short-term).

$$\sigma_T = -(2.5 \times 10^8) \times (140 \times 10^{-6}) \times 35$$

**Step 4 — Multiplication**

$$\sigma_T = -2.5 \times 10^8 \times 4.9 \times 10^{-3} = -1.225 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = -1.225 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation**

The compressive thermal stress is about **1.2 MPa**, well below the yield strength of HDPE (≈ 25 MPa). However, if the pipe were **frequently cycled**, thermal fatigue and creep-ratcheting would accumulate. This is why **expansion loops / bellows** are part of the functional design of long polymer pipelines.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Functional Requirement to Design Output

| Functional Requirement | Engineering Equation Used | Output Variable | Material Selection Lever | Manufacturing Lever |
|------------------------|---------------------------|-----------------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Carry 100 N axial load | $\sigma = F/A$ | Cross-section area | Tensile strength | — |
| Deflect < 1 mm under 5 N | $\delta = FL^3/(3EI)$ | Wall thickness, E, I | Modulus (fibre-reinforced) | Rib geometry |
| Survive 90 °C continuous | $\sigma_T = E\alpha\Delta T$ | CTE, modulus at T | High-Tg polymer (PPS, PEEK) | Annealing step |
| Insulate to 5 kV | $E_b = V_b/t$ | Wall thickness | Dielectric strength | — |
| Resist 30 % H₂SO₄ | Chemical compatibility tables | Material grade | PVDF, PTFE | Lining process |
| Transmit 80 % visible light | $T = I/I_0$ | Thickness, additives | PMMA, PC (clear) | Polish quality |
| UV-stable for 3 yr outdoor | UV-stabilizer package | Additive type, loading | HALS, UV absorbers | Co-extrusion cap layer |
| Snap-fit 50 cycles | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f'(2N_f)^b$ | Strain at hook | Toughened PA, POM | Living-hinge thickness |
| Creep < 1 % in 10 yr | Isochronous stress-strain | Stress level, T | Creep-resistant grade | — |
| Mass < 200 g | $\rho V$ | Volume, density | Foamed polymer, hollow rib | — |

This **matrix is the heart of functional design** — every row is one functional requirement and its full engineering solution chain.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Design Workflow in Polymer Product Development

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Identify Customer Need] --> B[Step2: Translate to Measurable Specifications via QFD]
    B --> C[Step3: Define Operating Conditions and Service Environment]
    C --> D[Step4: Apply Engineering Principles to Define Geometry and Material Class]
    D --> E[Step5: Apply Safety Factor and Design Allowables]
    E --> F[Step6: Validate via FEA Prototyping and Testing]
    F --> G{Design Meets All Functional Requirements}
    G -- Yes --> H[Release Functional Design Specification FDS Document]
    G -- No --> A
    H --> I[Hand Over to Configuration and Detail Design Stage]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Hierarchy of Design Stages (with Functional Design at the Apex)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Market Need] --> B1[Functional Design]
    B1 --> C1[Conceptual Design]
    C1 --> D1[Configuration Design]
    D1 --> E1[Detail Design]
    E1 --> F1[Tooling and Manufacturing Design]
    F1 --> G1[Production]
    B1 -.feedback.-> A1
    C1 -.feedback.-> B1
    D1 -.feedback.-> C1
    E1 -.feedback.-> D1
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Seven Pillars of Functional Design and their Interplay

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Mechanical Function] --> X[Integrated Functional Design Output]
    P2[Thermal Function] --> X
    P3[Electrical Function] --> X
    P4[Chemical Environmental Function] --> X
    P5[Optical Function] --> X
    P6[Aesthetic Ergonomic Function] --> X
    P7[Manufacturing Assembly Function] --> X
    X --> Y[Functional Design Specification FDS]
    Y --> Z[Material Selection Matrix]
    Y --> W[Geometry Draft]
    Y --> V[Process Selection]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Safety-Factor Selection Logic in Polymer Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Failure Mode] --> B{Failure Mode Type}
    B -- Brittle --> C[Use Ultimate Strength in Safety Factor]
    B -- Ductile --> D[Use Yield Strength in Safety Factor]
    B -- Creep --> E[Use Creep Rupture Strength at Design Life]
    B -- Fatigue --> F[Use Endurance Limit or Basquin Relation]
    C --> G[Apply Environmental Knockdown Factors]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Compute Allowable Stress]
    H --> I[Size Geometry]
```

### 4.5 Block Diagram — Functional Design Specification (FDS) Document Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FDS Document] --> B1[1 Product Identity and Intended Use]
    A --> B2[2 Functional Requirements Mechanical]
    A --> B3[3 Functional Requirements Thermal]
    A --> B4[4 Functional Requirements Electrical]
    A --> B5[5 Functional Requirements Chemical]
    A --> B6[6 Functional Requirements Optical]
    A --> B7[7 Functional Requirements Aesthetic Ergonomic]
    A --> B8[8 Service Environment and Lifetime]
    A --> B9[9 Applicable Standards ISO ASTM]
    A --> B10[10 Regulatory Requirements]
    A --> B11[11 Cost and Mass Budget]
    A --> B12[12 Test Plan and Acceptance Criteria]
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid block diagrams are used because physical drawings of, say, a moulded part's internal ribs are beyond the scope of a text-based schematic. The **functional flow** is faithfully represented instead.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**Define functional design. List any four functional requirements of a polymer product.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Functional design** is the engineering phase that translates the intended *purpose* of a polymer product into a set of **quantitative, measurable performance specifications** covering mechanical, thermal, electrical, chemical, optical, ergonomic, and manufacturing domains, against which the final design can be evaluated.

**Four functional requirements** (any four of the following are accepted):
1. **Mechanical** — the part must carry a specified load (e.g., ≥ 500 N) without yielding or fracturing.
2. **Thermal** — the part must operate continuously at a specified temperature (e.g., 100 °C) without softening, distorting, or oxidising.
3. **Electrical** — the part must provide a specified insulation resistance (e.g., ≥ 10¹² Ω) or dielectric strength (e.g., ≥ 20 kV/mm).
4. **Chemical/Environmental** — the part must resist specified chemicals (acids, fuels, oils) and weathering (UV, humidity) for a defined service life.
5. **Optical** — the part must transmit a specified fraction of light (e.g., ≥ 85 % transmittance) for a transparent cover.
6. **Aesthetic/Ergonomic** — the part must have a specified surface finish, colour, and feel.
7. **Manufacturing/Assembly** — the part must be mouldable, assemblable, and demouldable within a specified cycle time and yield.

---

#### **Question 2. (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Why are safety factors for polymer products typically higher than those used for metallic components? Justify with two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Safety factors for polymer products are typically in the range **n = 3 to 10**, compared to **n = 1.5 to 2.5** for metals, because:

1. **High scatter in polymer properties:** The coefficient of variation (CoV) of strength in polymers is typically **8 – 15 %**, while for steels it is only **3 – 5 %**. A larger safety factor is needed to absorb this statistical spread.
2. **Strong sensitivity to environment:** Polymer properties vary significantly with temperature, humidity, UV exposure, and chemical contact (e.g., Nylon loses ~50 % of its stiffness when wet; PC yellows under UV). This environmental knockdown must be compensated by a higher n.
3. **Viscoelastic / time-dependent behaviour:** Polymers creep, stress-relax, and fatigue under sustained or cyclic loading. Short-term test data must be extrapolated to multi-year service life, introducing uncertainty that demands a higher safety factor.
4. **Lower ductility and notch sensitivity:** Many polymers are notch-sensitive and fail in a brittle manner, leaving little warning before fracture. A higher n provides a safety margin against catastrophic failure.

*(Any two of the above reasons are sufficient for full 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below; the student answers ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Explain, with a suitable example, the **six-step methodology** of functional design of a polymer product. **(7 Marks)***

**(b)** *A polypropylene cantilever spring has length 30 mm, width 6 mm, and thickness 1.5 mm. It is loaded with a tip force of 0.8 N. Calculate the tip deflection. Given: E(PP) = 1.5 GPa. If the functional design requires the deflection to be **less than 1.5 mm**, is the current geometry acceptable? If not, suggest a geometry change (state any one). **(7 Marks)***

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **six-step methodology** of functional design is illustrated below with the example of an **automotive accelerator pedal** made of **glass-fibre reinforced PA 6,6**.

1. **Step 1 — Identify the Customer Need:**
   The customer (vehicle manufacturer) needs a *lightweight pedal* that transfers driver foot force to the throttle body, survives 10 lakh cycles without failure, and weighs < 400 g.

2. **Step 2 — Translate to Measurable Specifications (QFD):**
   - Foot load ≤ 800 N peak.
   - Fatigue life ≥ 1,000,000 cycles at 200 N RMS.
   - Deflection ≤ 5 mm at 500 N.
   - Operating temperature −40 °C to +85 °C.
   - Mass ≤ 400 g.
   - Standards: ISO 26262 (functional safety), FMVSS 124 (accelerator control).

3. **Step 3 — Define Service Environment:**
   - Engine bay temperature up to 85 °C; cold start at −40 °C.
   - Exposure to engine oil, coolant, road salt.
   - Constant vibration at 20–200 Hz.
   - UV exposure on driver-side footwell (low).

4. **Step 4 — Apply Engineering Principles to Define Geometry and Material Class:**
   - Stress analysis → I-beam or box-section rib geometry for high stiffness-to-mass ratio.
   - Material: 30 % glass-fibre reinforced PA 6,6 (high stiffness, fatigue resistance, oil resistance).
   - Wall thickness 3 mm, rib pitch 10 mm.

5. **Step 5 — Apply Safety Factor and Design Allowables:**
   - $\sigma_{\text{tensile, dry}}$ of GF-PA 6,6 = 180 MPa.
   - Environmental knockdown: −30 % (humidity + temperature).
   - Effective strength: 126 MPa.
   - Safety factor n = 5 (cyclic + variable environment).
   - $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = 126 / 5 = 25.2$ MPa.

6. **Step 6 — Validate via FEA, Prototype, Test:**
   - FEA stress map shows peak von-Mises stress = 18 MPa < 25.2 MPa. ✓
   - Bench test: 1.2 M cycles, no cracking. ✓
   - Deflection at 500 N = 3.8 mm < 5 mm. ✓
   - All functional requirements met → FDS released to detail design.

*(One mark for identifying each step correctly; one mark for the example integration.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** L = 30 mm = 0.030 m; b = 6 mm = 0.006 m; h = 1.5 mm = 0.0015 m; F = 0.8 N; E = 1.5 GPa = 1.5 × 10⁹ Pa.

**Step 1 — Second moment of area**

$$I = \frac{b \, h^3}{12} = \frac{0.006 \times (0.0015)^3}{12}$$

$$I = \frac{0.006 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-9}}{12} = \frac{2.025 \times 10^{-11}}{12} = 1.6875 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 2 — Cantilever tip deflection formula**

$$\delta = \frac{F \, L^3}{3 \, E \, I}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator**

$$F \, L^3 = 0.8 \times (0.030)^3 = 0.8 \times 2.7 \times 10^{-5} = 2.16 \times 10^{-5} \text{ N·m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Denominator**

$$3 \, E \, I = 3 \times 1.5 \times 10^9 \times 1.6875 \times 10^{-12} = 7.59375 \times 10^{-3} \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Step 5 — Deflection**

$$\delta = \frac{2.16 \times 10^{-5}}{7.59375 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.844 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.84 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict**

Since 2.84 mm > 1.5 mm, the **current geometry is NOT acceptable**.

**Step 7 — Suggested geometry change (any one):**

- **Option 1 — Increase thickness to h = 1.9 mm:**
  $I$ scales as $h^3$, so new $I = 1.6875 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.9/1.5)^3 = 1.6875 \times 10^{-12} \times 2.041 = 3.444 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$.
  New $\delta = 2.84 \times (1.5/1.9)^3 = 2.84 \times 0.491 = 1.39 \text{ mm}$ ✓.

- **Option 2 — Reduce length to L = 22 mm:**
  $\delta$ scales as $L^3$, so new $\delta = 2.84 \times (22/30)^3 = 2.84 \times 0.395 = 1.12 \text{ mm}$ ✓.

- **Option 3 — Use higher-modulus polymer** (e.g., POM, E = 3.0 GPa):
  $\delta$ scales as $1/E$, so new $\delta = 2.84 / 2 = 1.42 \text{ mm}$ ✓.

*(Full marks for explicit I calculation, deflection formula, numerical substitution, verdict, and one valid geometry change with numerical verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** *Discuss the **seven functional requirement domains** of a polymer product, giving one example requirement from each domain. **(7 Marks)***

**(b)** *An HDPE pipe is clamped between two rigid flanges. Installation temperature is 30 °C; the operating temperature is 70 °C. Compute the **thermal stress** developed in the pipe. Given: $\alpha$(HDPE) = 140 × 10⁻⁶ /K; E(HDPE at 70 °C) = 250 MPa. Comment on the safety of the design if the yield strength of HDPE is 22 MPa. Suggest one functional design improvement. **(7 Marks)***

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

| # | Domain | Example Functional Requirement | Typical Specification |
|---|--------|--------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical** | A safety helmet must absorb 50 J of impact energy. | Impact attenuation < 1500 g (peak), per IS 4151 / EN 1078. |
| 2 | **Thermal** | An under-hood connector housing must survive 130 °C continuous. | HDT ≥ 150 °C; material: PPS or PA 4,6. |
| 3 | **Electrical** | A switch-gear busbar support must withstand 30 kV. | Dielectric strength ≥ 20 kV/mm; material: BMC (bulk moulding compound) or epoxy. |
| 4 | **Chemical/Environmental** | A fuel-line quick connector must resist biodiesel B20. | No swelling > 5 % after 1000 h immersion; material: PA 12 or PVDF. |
| 5 | **Optical** | An automotive headlamp lens must transmit ≥ 90 % of light. | Haze < 1 %; material: PC with UV-stable hard-coat. |
| 6 | **Aesthetic/Ergonomic** | A toothbrush handle must have soft-touch grip. | Shore A 30–50; material: TPE overmould on PP. |
| 7 | **Manufacturing/Assembly** | A snap-fit clip must assemble in < 2 s with < 5 N insertion force. | Strain in hinge < 4 %; cycle time < 30 s; material: POM. |

*(One mark for naming each domain; one mark for a credible example per domain; the seventh (manufacturing) is sometimes given 0.5 marks extra for completeness.)*

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $T_1 = 30$ °C, $T_2 = 70$ °C, $\alpha = 140 \times 10^{-6} \text{ K}^{-1}$, $E = 250 \text{ MPa} = 2.5 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa}$, $\sigma_y = 22 \text{ MPa}$.

**Step 1 — Compute ΔT**

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 70 - 30 = 40 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Free thermal strain**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{th}} = \alpha \, \Delta T = 140 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 5.6 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3 — Constrained thermal stress (compressive)**

$$\sigma_T = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T = 2.5 \times 10^8 \times 5.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with yield strength**

$$\text{Safety factor} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_T} = \frac{22}{1.4} = 15.7$$

**Step 5 — Verdict**

The instantaneous thermal stress is well below yield — **safe for one-time heating**. However:

**Step 6 — Long-term concern**

HDPE undergoes **creep** at 70 °C. Over months, the constrained stress may not fully relax (since flange is rigid), and superimposed operating pressure stresses will accumulate. Cyclic thermal loading can cause **ratcheting** and eventual buckling.

**Step 7 — Functional design improvement (any one):**

- **Option 1 — Install an expansion loop or bellows** to decouple thermal expansion from the flange constraint.
- **Option 2 — Use a lower-CTE material** (e.g., glass-fibre reinforced HDPE, CTE ≈ 40 × 10⁻⁶ /K, reducing thermal strain by ~70 %).
- **Option 3 — Add a sliding support / guide** that allows axial movement while maintaining alignment.
- **Option 4 — Reduce operating temperature** by insulating the line.

*(Full marks: 3 for numerical computation, 2 for the safety comment, 2 for the improvement suggestion.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in Functional Design questions:**
> 1. **Writing "strong enough" or "durable"** — these are *qualitative*; the examiner expects *quantitative* numbers with units.
> 2. **Using the wrong safety-factor base** — some students compute n = σ_yield / σ_ultimate (a meaningless ratio). Use n = σ_ultimate / σ_working.
> 3. **Forgetting creep in long-life applications** — Polymers creep; metal-equivalent elastic stress analysis is **not** sufficient for a 10-year polymer design.
> 4. **Mixing up I = bh³/12 with I = πd⁴/64** — circular and rectangular sections have different formulas.
> 5. **Omitting units** — KTU board answers must include units at every numerical step.
> 6. **Not stating the failure criterion** — always say *yielding*, *fracture*, *excessive deflection*, *creep rupture*, etc.
> 7. **Ignoring the 3–10 safety factor** — applying a metal-style n = 1.5 will produce an unsafe, fragile polymer part.
> 8. **Confusing engineering stress with true stress** during necking — explicitly state the assumption.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Bullet-Point Summary — Functional Design (Module 1, PCPOT601)**

- **Functional design = translating customer need into quantified performance specs.**
- The output is the **Functional Design Specification (FDS)** document.
- **Seven domains** of functional design: **Mechanical, Thermal, Electrical, Chemical, Optical, Aesthetic/Ergonomic, Manufacturing/Assembly**.
- Every requirement is expressed as **Magnitude + Tolerance + Test Standard + Service Condition + Failure Criterion**.
- The **6-step methodology**: Identify Need → QFD Translation → Define Environment → Apply Engineering Principles → Apply Safety Factor → Validate.
- **Safety factors for polymers are 3–10** (vs 1.5–2.5 for metals), because of **high property scatter**, **environmental sensitivity**, and **viscoelasticity**.
- **Key equations** to memorize:
  - $\sigma = F/A_0$
  - $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$
  - $\delta = FL^3/(3EI)$ (cantilever)
  - $\delta = FL^3/(48EI)$ (simply supported, center load)
  - $I = bh^3/12$ (rectangle)
  - $n = \sigma_{\text{failure}}/\sigma_{\text{working}}$
  - $\sigma_T = E\alpha\Delta T$ (constrained thermal stress)
  - $K_{IC} = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$
  - $\sigma_a = \sigma_f'(2N_f)^b$ (Basquin fatigue)
- **Creep, stress relaxation, and fatigue** are time-dependent and must be explicitly designed for, not ignored.
- **WLF equation** governs time–temperature superposition above Tg.
- **Water absorption** degrades properties of hygroscopic polymers (PA, PC, PMMA, POM).
- **HDT and Tg** govern short-term and long-term thermal limits respectively.
- **CTE mismatch** with metals or ceramics can cause **delamination or warpage** — functional design must address this.
- **DFMEA** is the standard tool to identify and rank functional-design risks.
- **Iteration is mandatory** — FDS is updated after every FEA / prototype test.
- **Regulatory and standards compliance** (ASTM, ISO, BIS, SAE, FMVSS, IS) is a non-negotiable part of the FDS.
- **Cost and mass budgets** must appear in the FDS, not just performance specs.
- Functional design feeds into **conceptual → configuration → detail design** in that strict sequence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aesthetic Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aesthetic Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🎨 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Aesthetic Design** in the context of polymer product engineering is defined as the systematic and engineered application of visual, tactile, and sensory attributes to a polymer artefact such that the final product satisfies both functional requirements and the psychological/ emotional expectations of the end-user. It is the discipline that merges **Industrial Design**, **Color Science**, **Surface Engineering**, and **Ergonomics** into a single coherent product language.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (Course Outcome **CO1** of PCPOT601), aesthetic design is treated as an *integral sub-system* of the overall Product Design Specification (PDS) — not a cosmetic afterthought. It encompasses:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme - PCPOT601, Module 1):**
> "Aesthetic Design — elements and principles of design, colour, surface finish, texture, and their influence on consumer perception, market acceptability, and product life-cycle."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of aesthetic design as the **"clothing and grooming" of a polymer product**.

- A plain, injection-moulded polypropylene chair *functions* perfectly (it holds weight, resists moisture), but a version finished in matte textured black, with bevelled edges and a soft-touch elastomer grip will *sell* better and be perceived as a premium product.
- Aesthetics is to a polymer product what **attire, fragrance, and grooming** are to a person attending a job interview. The interview is the *function*; the attire is the *aesthetic*. Both are required for success.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:**
> Studies published by the Industrial Designers Society of America (IDSA) and the Colour Marketing Group indicate that **up to 60–80% of a consumer's first purchase decision** is influenced by visual aesthetics — colour, finish, and form — before any functional test is performed.

### 1.3 Key Metrics & Standards (Highlighted in Bold)

| Domain | Standard / Metric | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Colour Specification** | **RAL, Pantone, Munsell** | Universal colour communication |
| **Gloss Measurement** | **ASTM D523 / ISO 2813** (Gloss Units, GU) | Surface reflectance |
| **Surface Roughness** | **Ra (μm)** per **ISO 4287** | Tactile feel |
| **Colour Difference** | **CIELAB ΔE\*** (CIEDE2000) | Batch consistency |
| **Texture Classification** | **MTM / VDI 3400** | Mould surface reference |
| **Visual Inspection** | **ASTM D1729** | Standard lighting for colour judgement |

These metrics are the **language of quantification** that allows the polymer engineer to translate subjective aesthetic intent into measurable, verifiable specifications.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Aesthetic Design

Aesthetic design in polymer products rests on two structural pillars, each with measurable engineering sub-systems.

### Pillar A — The **Elements of Design** (the building blocks)
- **Line**: Edge definition, parting lines, draft angles.
- **Shape**: 2D outline (circle, polygon, organic).
- **Form**: 3D volume derived from shape.
- **Colour**: Hue, value, chroma — expressed numerically in the **CIELAB (L\*, a\*, b\*)** colour space.
- **Texture**: Visual (perceived) and tactile (measured) surface character.
- **Space**: Negative vs. positive volume — significant in transparent/semi-crystalline polymers.

### Pillar B — The **Principles of Design** (the rules of combination)
- **Balance** — symmetric / asymmetric / radial.
- **Proportion** — golden ratio (1 : 1.618), rule of thirds.
- **Emphasis / Focal Point** — a contrasting colour or surface zone that draws the eye.
- **Rhythm** — repetition of geometry, colour, or texture to create visual flow.
- **Unity / Harmony** — consistent visual language across the entire product family.

## 2.2 Colour Science for the Polymer Engineer

Colour is the single most powerful aesthetic variable. It is quantified using the **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** colour space, defined by the **CIE (Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage)** in 1976, and revised in **CIEDE2000**.

### Core Colour Coordinates

| Coordinate | Symbol | Range | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lightness | **L\*** | 0 → 100 | Black → White |
| Red–Green axis | **a\*** | −128 → +127 | Green → Red |
| Yellow–Blue axis | **b\*** | −128 → +127 | Blue → Yellow |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CIELAB and not RGB?**
> RGB is device-dependent (each camera/monitor sees a different "red"). CIELAB is a **perceptually uniform** space — equal numerical differences correspond to equal visual differences as perceived by the human eye. For a polymer engineer matching moulded parts to a master sample, CIELAB is the only acceptable system.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following equations form the **quantitative core** of aesthetic design and are highly testable.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$ | **CIE76** colour difference (simple Euclidean) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\Delta E_{00} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta L'}{k_L S_L}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\Delta C'}{k_C S_C}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\Delta H'}{k_H S_H}\right)^2 + R_T \dfrac{\Delta C' \Delta H'}{S_C S_H}}$ | **CIEDE2000** advanced colour difference | dimensionless |
| 3 | $G = \dfrac{I_s}{I_{ref}} \times 100$ | **Gloss** at specified geometry (60°/20°/85°) | **Gloss Units (GU)** |
| 4 | $Ra = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_0^L \vert z(x) \vert \, dx$ | **Arithmetic mean roughness** | **μm** |
| 5 | $H = 60^\circ \times \left[(a^*/b^*)\bmod 6\right]$ | **Hue angle** in CIELAB | degrees (°) |
| 6 | $C^* = \sqrt{(a^*)^2 + (b^*)^2}$ | **Chroma** (saturation) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Contrast Ratio} = \dfrac{Y_{over\,black}}{Y_{over\,white}}$ | Opacity of pigmented polymer (ASTM D2805) | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $\text{Part Line Visibility} \propto f(\mu, v, T_g)$ | Function of mould friction, draw speed, glass transition | qualitative |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Thresholds (Industry Standard):**
> - $\Delta E_{00} \le 1.0$ → visually indistinguishable (premium products).
> - $1.0 < \Delta E_{00} \le 2.0$ → acceptable for non-critical parts.
> - $\Delta E_{00} > 3.0$ → reject as **visible mismatch**.
> - **Gloss @ 60°**: Matte ≤ 10 GU; Satin 10–70 GU; Glossy > 70 GU.

## 2.4 Surface Finish Engineering

Surface finish is selected to satisfy both **functional** (release, scratch resistance, cleanability) and **aesthetic** (visual + tactile) goals.

| Finish Class | SPI / VDI Reference | Ra (μm) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mirror / Optical | SPI-A1 (VDI 3400 Ref. 30) | 0.025 – 0.05 | Lenses, display panels |
| High Gloss | SPI-A2 / A3 | 0.05 – 0.20 | Consumer electronics housing |
| Semi-Gloss / Satin | SPI-B1 / B2 | 0.20 – 0.80 | Automotive interior |
| Matte | SPI-C1 / C2 | 0.80 – 1.60 | Hand-held appliances |
| Textured | SPI-D1 / D3 | > 1.60 | Tool housings, anti-slip |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall (Common KTU Mistake):**
> A glossy mould finish (low Ra) on a polymer that is highly filled (e.g., 30 % glass fibre) will still *appear* dull because the fibres disrupt the surface. Always specify mould finish **with** the material grade.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Aesthetic design is not an "art class" topic in KTU — it has direct engineering consequences:

1. **Manufacturability (DFM)**: Sharp aesthetic corners must reconcile with **draft angles** (≥ 1°) for mould release.
2. **Assembly**: Hidden parting lines preserve aesthetic cleanliness.
3. **Material Selection**: Translucent aesthetics require **amorphous polymers (PC, PMMA, COC)**; deep colour saturation requires **engineering-grade masterbatch with proper dispersion**.
4. **Lifecycle**: Painted surfaces are a **recycling contaminant**; in-mould colour (mass pigmentation) is sustainable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the CIE76 Colour Difference (ΔE\*)

This derivation is frequently asked in KTU Module 1 as a **3-mark short derivation** and a **7-mark Part B sub-question**.

### Step 1 — Establish the Coordinate System

The CIELAB colour space assigns every perceivable colour a unique triplet $(L^*, a^*, b^*)$. Two samples, the **standard (master)** and the **batch (sample)**, each yield one triplet:

$$ \text{Standard} = (L^*_1, a^*_1, b^*_1) \qquad \text{Batch} = (L^*_2, a^*_2, b^*_2) $$

### Step 2 — Define Coordinate Differences

The component-wise difference between the two samples is:

$$ \Delta L^* = L^*_2 - L^*_1 $$

$$ \Delta a^* = a^*_2 - a^*_1 $$

$$ \Delta b^* = b^*_2 - b^*_1 $$

> **Logic**: Each axis measures an independent perceptual dimension — Lightness, Red–Green, Yellow–Blue. To find the *total* perceived difference, we must combine all three contributions.

### Step 3 — Apply the Euclidean Distance Principle

Because CIELAB is designed to be perceptually uniform, the total colour difference corresponds to the **straight-line (Euclidean) distance** between the two points in 3D colour space. By the Pythagorean theorem extended to 3D:

$$ \Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta x)^2 + (\Delta y)^2 + (\Delta z)^2} $$

Substituting the colour coordinates:

$$ \Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2} $$

### Step 4 — Final Expression

This is the **CIE76 colour difference equation** — simple, symmetric, and reproducible.

> [!NOTE]
> **CIE76 vs. CIEDE2000:**
> CIE76 *over-predicts* colour differences in the blue region and ignores perceptual non-uniformities. CIEDE2000 adds weighting functions $S_L, S_C, S_H$ and a rotation term $R_T$ to correct this. Use **CIEDE2000** for any production batch-acceptance decision.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A polymer production batch is measured in CIELAB as $(L^*, a^*, b^*) = (52.4, +25.1, -18.7)$. The master standard is $(L^*, a^*, b^*) = (50.0, +24.0, -20.0)$. Calculate the CIE76 colour difference $\Delta E^*$ and judge acceptability.

**Solution:**

### Step A — Compute the Differences

$$ \Delta L^* = 52.4 - 50.0 = +2.4 $$

$$ \Delta a^* = 25.1 - 24.0 = +1.1 $$

$$ \Delta b^* = -18.7 - (-20.0) = +1.3 $$

### Step B — Square Each Term

$$ (\Delta L^*)^2 = (2.4)^2 = 5.76 $$

$$ (\Delta a^*)^2 = (1.1)^2 = 1.21 $$

$$ (\Delta b^*)^2 = (1.3)^2 = 1.69 $$

### Step C — Sum the Squares

$$ \Sigma = 5.76 + 1.21 + 1.69 = 8.66 $$

### Step D — Take the Square Root

$$ \Delta E^* = \sqrt{8.66} = 2.943 $$

### Step E — Engineering Judgement

> $1.0 < \Delta E^* = 2.94 \le 3.0$ → **Borderline acceptable** for non-critical parts. For premium visible parts, this batch should be **rejected** and the masterbatch / moulding conditions re-verified.

**Valuation Note:** KTU examiners award the final mark only for the *engineering interpretation*, not just the numerical answer.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Hue Angle (H°)

The hue angle is essential when describing colour family (red, yellow, green, blue).

### Step 1 — The (a\*, b\*) Plane

$a^*$ and $b^*$ form a 2D Cartesian plane; hue is the **polar angle**.

### Step 2 — Apply the 2-Point Arctangent

$$ H = \arctan\left(\dfrac{b^*}{a^*}\right) $$

### Step 3 — Convert to the 0–360° Standard

$$ H = 60^\circ \times \left[\left(\dfrac{b^*}{a^*}\right) \bmod 6\right] \quad \text{(when } a^*>0\text{)} $$

For $a^*<0$, add $180^\circ$; for $a^*>0$ and $b^*<0$, add $360^\circ$.

> **Real-world mapping:**
> $H = 0°$ → Red, $90°$ → Yellow, $180°$ → Green, $270°$ → Blue.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Aesthetic Strategy

| Product Class | Recommended Colour Strategy | Recommended Finish | Special Considerations |
|---|---|---|---|
| Medical device | Pastel (light blue, mint) | Matte (anti-glare in OR) | Biocompatibility, sterilisation |
| Consumer electronics | Black, silver, signature colour | High gloss + soft-touch zones | Fingerprint visibility |
| Automotive interior | Warm neutrals (graphite, beige) | Satin / soft-touch | UV stability, scratch resistance |
| Toys | High chroma primaries | Glossy + textured grips | EN-71 safety, saliva resistance |
| Industrial enclosure | RAL 7035 (light grey), RAL 9005 (black) | Matte textured | Heat dissipation, hiding fingerprints |
| Luxury packaging | Metallic / pearlescent | Mirror polish + IML | Recyclability, brand value |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 🧩 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Aesthetic Design Workflow — Functional Architecture Flow

The following block diagram captures the engineering workflow from *design intent* to *verified aesthetic output*. This is the KTU-recommended thought process for aesthetic design of polymer products.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Market Need and User Persona] --> B[Define Aesthetic Brief: Colour, Finish, Form]
    B --> C[Translate to Quantified Specs: L a b, GU, Ra]
    C --> D[Select Polymer Grade and Masterbatch]
    D --> E[Select Mould Surface: SPI VDI Reference]
    E --> F[Prototype Tooling and Sample Moulding]
    F --> G[Instrumental Measurement: Spectrophotometer, Glossmeter, Profilometer]
    G --> H{Acceptance: Delta E within tolerance and Ra and GU within spec}
    H -- Yes --> I[Production Part Approval Process PPAP]
    H -- No --> J[Root Cause: Pigment Dispersion or Mould Condition or Material Batch]
    J --> D
    I --> K[Release to Market]
```

## 4.2 Colour Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Aesthetic Family] --> B{Translucent or Opaque}
    B -- Translucent --> C[Amorphous Polymer: PC, PMMA, COC]
    B -- Opaque --> D{Deep or Pastel Colour}
    C --> E[Low Pigment Load: 0.1 to 0.5 percent masterbatch]
    D -- Deep --> F[High Pigment Load: 1.0 to 3.0 percent plus opacifier TiO2]
    D -- Pastel --> G[Medium Pigment Load: 0.3 to 0.8 percent plus 1 to 3 percent TiO2]
    E --> H[Verify CIELAB and Haze per ASTM D1003]
    F --> I[Verify Delta E and Contrast Ratio per ASTM D2805]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Acceptance]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Elements vs. Principles — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Elements[Elements of Design]
        E1[Line]
        E2[Shape]
        E3[Form]
        E4[Colour]
        E5[Texture]
        E6[Space]
    end
    subgraph Principles[Principles of Design]
        P1[Balance]
        P2[Proportion]
        P3[Emphasis]
        P4[Rhythm]
        P5[Unity]
    end
    Elements --> Principles
    P1 --> Outcome[Harmonious Aesthetic Polymer Product]
    P2 --> Outcome
    P3 --> Outcome
    P4 --> Outcome
    P5 --> Outcome
```

## 4.4 Surface Finish vs. Polymer Compatibility Matrix (Textual Diagram)

```
                Mirror    High Gloss    Satin     Matte    Textured
                 Ra 0.05    Ra 0.2      Ra 0.5    Ra 1.0    Ra 2.0
                 GU 90      GU 80        GU 50     GU 20     GU 8
  PC              [OK]        [OK]         [OK]      [OK]     [OK]
  PMMA            [BEST]      [OK]         [OK]      [OK]     [OK]
  ABS             [NO]        [OK]         [BEST]    [OK]     [OK]
  PP              [NO]        [RISK]       [OK]      [BEST]   [OK]
  GF-PP 30        [NO]        [NO]         [RISK]    [OK]     [BEST]
  PA6             [NO]        [RISK]       [OK]      [BEST]   [OK]
  POM             [NO]        [OK]         [BEST]    [OK]     [OK]
```

> **Reading key:** `BEST` = ideal pairing; `OK` = acceptable; `RISK` = verify moulding conditions; `NO` = not recommended.
