# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define "Aesthetic Design" in the context of polymer products. List the six elements of design.**
*(3 Marks — CO1, K2: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
Aesthetic Design is the engineered integration of visual, tactile, and sensory attributes into a polymer product to satisfy both functional performance and the emotional/perceptual expectations of the end-user. It is a measurable discipline based on colour science, surface engineering, and industrial design principles.

The **six elements of design** are:

1. **Line** — edges, parting lines, geometric axes.
2. **Shape** — 2D outline.
3. **Form** — 3D volume.
4. **Colour** — quantified in CIELAB (L*, a*, b*).
5. **Texture** — visual and tactile surface character.
6. **Space** — positive and negative volume (especially in transparent polymers).

---

**Q2. What is meant by the term "Gloss" in polymer surface engineering? Name the standard test method and the standard unit.**
*(3 Marks — CO1, K1: Remember)*

**Model Answer:**
Gloss is the **specular reflectance** of a surface — the degree to which incident light is reflected at the mirror (equal) angle, as opposed to being scattered diffusely. High gloss indicates a smooth surface; low gloss (matte) indicates a textured or rough surface.

- **Standard Test Method:** **ASTM D523** (or ISO 2813).
- **Standard Unit:** **Gloss Units (GU)**, measured at 60° (general), 20° (high gloss), or 85° (matte) geometry.
- **Classification:** Matte ≤ 10 GU; Satin 10–70 GU; Glossy > 70 GU.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question, Modified for Module 1 Coverage]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a)** Explain the **CIELAB colour space** in detail. Define $L^*$, $a^*$, and $b^*$ with their numerical ranges and physical meaning. Derive the CIE76 colour difference equation $\Delta E^*$. *(7 Marks — CO1, K3: Apply)*

**(b)** A production batch of ABS housing is measured at $(L^*, a^*, b^*) = (45.0, -12.0, +22.0)$ while the master standard is $(40.0, -10.0, +25.0)$. Calculate $\Delta E^*$ and **justify** whether the batch is acceptable for a premium consumer product. State **two corrective actions** if rejected. *(7 Marks — CO1, K4: Analyse)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) The CIELAB Colour Space and Derivation of $\Delta E^*$:**

The CIELAB (or CIE L\*a\*b\*) colour space, defined by the **Commission Internationale de l'Éclairage** in 1976, is a **three-dimensional, perceptually uniform** colour model. Unlike the device-dependent RGB system, CIELAB represents colour as perceived by a standard human observer.

- **$L^*$ — Lightness (0 to 100):** $L^* = 0$ represents pure black; $L^* = 100$ represents a perfect white diffuser. It is the achromatic axis of the space.
- **$a^*$ — Red–Green axis (−128 to +127):** Positive $a^*$ shifts towards red; negative $a^*$ shifts towards green.
- **$b^*$ — Yellow–Blue axis (−128 to +127):** Positive $b^*$ shifts towards yellow; negative $b^*$ shifts towards blue.

**Derivation of CIE76 $\Delta E^*$:**
Let the standard sample be $(L^*_1, a^*_1, b^*_1)$ and the batch be $(L^*_2, a^*_2, b^*_2)$. The component-wise differences are $\Delta L^* = L^*_2 - L^*_1$, $\Delta a^* = a^*_2 - a^*_1$, $\Delta b^* = b^*_2 - b^*_1$. Because CIELAB is perceptually uniform, the total perceived colour difference equals the **Euclidean distance** in this 3D space. Applying the 3D extension of the Pythagorean theorem:

$$ \Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2} $$

**(b) Numerical Solution and Engineering Justification:**

**Step 1:** Compute differences.

$$ \Delta L^* = 45.0 - 40.0 = +5.0 $$

$$ \Delta a^* = -12.0 - (-10.0) = -2.0 $$

$$ \Delta b^* = 22.0 - 25.0 = -3.0 $$

**Step 2:** Square each term.

$$ (\Delta L^*)^2 = 25.0, \quad (\Delta a^*)^2 = 4.0, \quad (\Delta b^*)^2 = 9.0 $$

**Step 3:** Sum and take the square root.

$$ \Delta E^* = \sqrt{25.0 + 4.0 + 9.0} = \sqrt{38.0} = 6.164 $$

**Step 4:** Engineering judgement.

For a **premium consumer product**, the acceptable threshold is $\Delta E^* \le 1.0$. Since $\Delta E^* = 6.16$ is **far above** this limit, the batch is **rejected**.

**Two Corrective Actions:**
1. **Re-verify the masterbatch-to-polymer ratio** — a likely cause is incorrect pigment loading or poor dispersion, causing the colour to drift darker (higher $L^*$) and less yellow (lower $b^*$).
2. **Re-check moulding parameters** — excessive barrel temperature can thermally degrade the pigment; an unexpected shear rate can change gloss and apparent colour. A **Design of Experiments (DoE)** on temperature and injection speed is recommended.
3. *(Bonus credit)* Verify spectrophotometer calibration against a **certified white tile and black trap** before re-measurement.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a)** Discuss the **elements and principles of aesthetic design** as applied to polymer products. Give **two polymer-specific examples** for each. *(7 Marks — CO1, K2: Understand)*

**(b)** What are the various **surface finish classifications** used in injection moulding? How does **mould surface finish (SPI/VDI)** influence the final aesthetic appearance of a polymer part? *(7 Marks — CO1, K3: Apply)*

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Elements and Principles of Design:**

- **Line** → parting line concealment, hidden parting line in two-shot moulding of a toothbrush.
- **Shape** → rounded organic shape of a soft-drink bottle for grip and brand identity.
- **Form** → three-dimensional curvature of a motorcycle helmet visor.
- **Colour** → fluorescent safety yellow of a traffic cone (high visibility).
- **Texture** → leather-grain finish of an automotive steering wheel (soft-touch PVC/TPU).
- **Space** → negative space (transparency) of a PC drinking bottle showcasing the liquid.

**Principles:**
- **Balance** — symmetrical helmet design.
- **Proportion** — golden-ratio 1:1.618 used in smartphone bezels.
- **Emphasis** — contrasting colour logo on a black tool housing.
- **Rhythm** — repeating vent slots on a laptop chassis.
- **Unity** — consistent design language across a product family (e.g., Apple iPhone series).

**(b) Surface Finish Classification and Influence:**

Surface finishes in injection moulding are classified by:
1. **SPI Finish Standard** (Society of the Plastics Industry) — grades A1 to D3.
2. **VDI 3400** (Verein Deutscher Ingenieure) — numerical scale 12 to 45.
3. **Ra value** (μm) — direct measurement per ISO 4287.

| SPI Grade | VDI Ref. | Ra (μm) | Visual Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| A1, A2, A3 | 30, 33, 36 | 0.025 – 0.20 | High gloss to optical |
| B1, B2, B3 | 27, 24, 21 | 0.20 – 0.80 | Satin |
| C1, C2, C3 | 18, 15, 12 | 0.80 – 1.60 | Matte |
| D1, D2, D3 | 9, 6, 3 | > 1.60 | Textured / deep grain |

**Influence on Aesthetic Output:**
- A **glossy SPI-A1 mould** on amorphous **PMMA** yields a near-mirror finish suitable for display panels.
- A **textured SPI-D3 mould** on **30 % glass-filled PP** hides weld lines and fibre orientation marks — preferred for industrial enclosures.
- **Filling and fibre alignment** in semi-crystalline or fibre-reinforced polymers can partially override mould finish; the resulting Ra of the part is *not* identical to that of the mould cavity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Do not confuse CIELAB with RGB.** RGB is device-dependent; CIELAB is the KTU-required standard for polymer colour communication. Writing RGB hex codes in a colour-difference question = **0 marks** for the sub-question.
> 2. **Always state units.** Glossmeter readings without "GU" → mark deduction. Ra values without "μm" → mark deduction.
> 3. **For numerical questions, end with an engineering verdict.** Computing $\Delta E^* = 6.16$ without saying "the batch is **rejected** because it exceeds the threshold" loses the final 1–2 marks for *application*.
> 4. **Surface finish must be discussed in context of the polymer grade** — not as a standalone abstract list. A finish "good for ABS" is not always "good for filled PP".
> 5. **Memorise the threshold:** $\Delta E_{00} \le 1.0$ for premium; $\le 2.0$ for general; $> 3.0$ reject. This single line often determines the 14-mark valuation.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Aesthetic Design (Module 1, PCPOT601)**

- ✅ Aesthetic design is **measurable**, not subjective. Always specify in numbers (GU, Ra, ΔE, L\*a\*b\*).
- ✅ The **six elements** of design: **Line, Shape, Form, Colour, Texture, Space**.
- ✅ The **five principles** of design: **Balance, Proportion, Emphasis, Rhythm, Unity**.
- ✅ **CIELAB (L\*a\*b\*)** is the KTU standard colour space; L\* = 0–100; a\*, b\* = −128 to +127.
- ✅ **CIE76 equation**: $\Delta E^* = \sqrt{(\Delta L^*)^2 + (\Delta a^*)^2 + (\Delta b^*)^2}$.
- ✅ **Acceptance thresholds**: $\Delta E_{00} \le 1$ (premium); $\le 2$ (general); $> 3$ (reject).
- ✅ **Gloss** measured per **ASTM D523 / ISO 2813** in **Gloss Units (GU)** at 60° (standard geometry).
- ✅ **Surface roughness** measured per **ISO 4287** as **Ra in μm**.
- ✅ **SPI / VDI 3400** standards classify mould surface finish into gloss, satin, matte, and textured grades.
- ✅ **Glossy mould + filled polymer = visually dull part** — material grade interacts with mould finish.
- ✅ **Design for Manufacturing (DFM):** every aesthetic feature must coexist with ≥ 1° draft angle and uniform wall thickness.
- ✅ **In-mould decoration (IMD/IML)** and **mass pigmentation** are sustainable, recycling-friendly aesthetic strategies versus post-mould painting.
- ✅ **Aesthetic design serves market differentiation** — it directly influences the **first-purchase decision** of the consumer and is therefore a **commercial engineering variable**, not a luxury.

> **End of Notes — Aesthetic Design, Module 1, PCPOT601 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Fillers on Properties and Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Effect of Fillers on Properties and Performance # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **KTU Definition Box** > A **filler** is a relatively inert solid substance (organic or inorganic) added to a polymer matrix to modify its bulk properties, reduce cost, and/or enhance specific performance characteristics without significantly altering the fundamental chemistry of the base polymer. Fillers are distinguished from **reinforcing agents** by the magnitude of improvement they impart to mechanical strength. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fillers are classified as **additives** under the broader umbrella of **polymer compounding ingredients**. They occupy a critical position in the **filler–matrix interface** science and are characterized by: - **Particle size**: typically **0.1 µm to 100 µm** - **Aspect ratio (L/D)**: spherical (<10) vs fibrous (>10) - **Specific surface area (m²/g)**: governs matrix-filler interaction - **Oil absorption value (ml/100g)**: indicator of matrix demand - **Packing fraction (Φ)**: ratio of filler volume to total composite volume > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT601 Module 1)** > Fillers influence **mechanical (tensile, flexural, impact, hardness)**, **thermal (HDT, Tg, conductivity)**, **rheological (melt viscosity, Mooney)**, **electrical (conductivity, dielectric constant)**, **barrier (permeability)**, and **aesthetic (color, opacity)** properties. The dominant role is dictated by the **filler–polymer adhesion energy** and **filler geometry**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are making a **concrete slab** using cement (the polymer binder) and sand + gravel (the fillers). Without sand/gravel, plain cement cracks easily and is expensive. With sand, the mixture becomes: - **Stronger** (load-bearing aggregate), - **Cheaper** (sand is much less expensive than pure cement paste), - **Dimensionally stable** (less shrinkage on drying), - **More processable** (the wet mix flows more uniformly). In **polymers**, fillers play exactly this role: - **Calcium Carbonate (CaCO₃)** in PVC pipes = the "gravel" - **Carbon Black** in tires = the "reinforcing sand" that gives tear strength - **Glass fibers** in epoxy composites = the "steel rebar" > [!TIP] > **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** *A filler is to a polymer what ballast is to a ship — it adds weight, stability, and specific performance, but the polymer matrix still drives the overall chemistry.* ## 1.3 Why Use Fillers? (Engineering Drivers) | Driver | Engineering Reason | Typical Filler | |---|---|---| | **Cost reduction** | Replace expensive resin with cheap mineral | CaCO₃, talc | | **Stiffness enhancement** | Increase Young's modulus | Glass fiber, mica | | **Dimensional stability** | Reduce mold shrinkage & CTE | Talc, silica | | **Flame retardancy** | Promote char formation | ATH, Mg(OH)₂ | | **Electrical conductivity** | Create percolation paths | Carbon black, CNT | | **Barrier properties** | Tortuous path for permeants | Nano-clay, graphene | | **Aesthetics** | Color, opacity, texture | TiO₂, pigments | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Fillers (Hierarchical View) ``` Fillers ├── By Geometry │ ├── Particulate (L/D ≈ 1) → CaCO₃, silica, TiO₂ │ ├── Fibrous (L/D > 10) → Glass fiber, wollastonite │ ├── Platelet / Lamellar → Mica, talc, nano-clay │ └── Nanofillers (≤ 100 nm) → CNT, fumed silica, graphene │ ├── By Origin │ ├── Inorganic → Oxides, carbonates, silicates │ └── Organic → Cellulose, wood flour, starch │ ├── By Function │ ├── Inert / Extender → Whiting, clay │ ├── Reinforcing → Carbon black, fumed silica │ ├── Functional / Specialty → Conductive, magnetic, FR │ └── Coupling agent treated → Silane-coated glass, titanated CaCO₃ ``` ## 2.2 The Filler–Matrix Interface: The Heart of the Phenomenon > [!IMPORTANT] > All property modifications in filled polymers originate at the **interfacial region** (also called the **interphase**), a zone of altered polymer chain conformation and mobility surrounding each filler particle. The interface exhibits three distinct polymer populations: 1. **Bound polymer** — tightly adsorbed, immobile chains (~1–2 nm thick). 2. **Restricted mobility layer** — chains with reduced segmental motion (~5–10 nm thick). 3. **Bulk-like polymer** — matrix unaffected by the filler. This interphase is responsible for almost every property change we observe. ## 2.3 Effect on Mechanical Properties — The Core Reasoning ### A. Modulus (Stiffness) The modulus always **increases** with filler loading because the filler (typically a ceramic or metal oxide) has a modulus **10–100× higher** than the polymer. For rigid particles with perfect bonding: $$E_c = E_m \cdot (1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f)$$ (lower bound — Einstein equation for dilute, rigid spheres, where **Φ_f** = filler volume fraction). For non-spherical or high-aspect-ratio fillers, the modulus increase is **anisotropic** and amplified by shape factors. ### B. Tensile Strength - For **poorly bonded** particulates → tensile strength **decreases** (stress concentration). - For **well-bonded** particulates or fibrous fillers → tensile strength **increases**. - A maximum exists at an **optimum filler loading** beyond which agglomeration causes failure. ### C. Impact Strength - Generally **decreases** with rigid fillers because: - Stress concentration at particle poles initiates cracks. - Reduced polymer chain mobility inhibits plastic deformation. - **Exception:** Elastomeric fillers (rubber particles) **increase** impact strength via **crazing/shear yielding**. ### D. Hardness & Abrasion Resistance - Increase monotonically with filler content due to load-bearing by hard particles. ## 2.4 Effect on Thermal Properties | Property | Effect of Filler | Reason | |---|---|---| | **Glass Transition Temp (Tg)** | Slight increase (2–10 °C) | Restricted chain mobility at interface | | **Heat Deflection Temp (HDT)** | Substantial increase | Higher modulus at elevated T | | **Thermal Conductivity (k)** | Increases (esp. metal/ceramic fillers) | Phonon transport through filler network | | **Coeff. of Thermal Expansion (CTE)** | **Decreases** | Low-CTE filler dilutes polymer expansion | | **Flammability** | Decreases (for FR fillers) | Endothermic decomposition releases water/CO₂ | ## 2.5 Effect on Rheological & Processing Properties Melt viscosity **increases** with filler content. For spherical fillers (Einstein–Roscoe equation): $$\eta_c = \eta_0 \cdot (1 - \Phi_f / \Phi_m)^{-2.5}$$ where: - $\eta_c$ = composite viscosity, - $\eta_0$ = matrix viscosity, - $\Phi_f$ = filler volume fraction, - $\Phi_m$ = maximum packing fraction (~0.6–0.7 for spheres). > Processability implication: a filled compound requires **higher processing temperatures**, **higher injection pressure**, or **twin-screw compounding** to achieve acceptable flow. ## 2.6 Effect on Electrical Properties - **Insulating fillers** (SiO₂, Al₂O₃) → increase dielectric strength, reduce tracking. - **Conductive fillers** (carbon black, CNT, metal powders) → at the **percolation threshold (Φ_c)**, conductivity jumps by 10–12 orders of magnitude. **Percolation scaling law:** $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot (\Phi_f - \Phi_c)^t$$ where **t** ≈ 1.65 (3-D universal exponent), and $\Phi_c$ depends on filler aspect ratio (CNT: $\Phi_c \approx 0.001$; carbon black: $\Phi_c \approx 0.03$). ## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formula Table for KTU Board Exam** | # | Equation | Meaning / Use | Variables | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_c = E_m(1 + 2.5\Phi_f)$ | Einstein modulus (dilute) | $E_c$ composite modulus, $E_m$ matrix modulus, $\Phi_f$ filler volume fraction | $\Phi_f < 0.1$ | | 2 | $E_c = E_m(1 + 0.675\,S_f + 1.62\,S_f^2\,\Phi_f)$ | Guth–Gold (rod-like) | $S_f = L/D$ aspect ratio | $S_f > 1$ | | 3 | $\eta_c = \eta_0 (1 - \Phi_f/\Phi_m)^{-2.5}$ | Einstein–Roscoe viscosity | $\eta_c$ composite viscosity, $\eta_0$ matrix viscosity, $\Phi_m$ max packing | $\Phi_m \approx 0.64$ for spheres | | 4 | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f (\Phi_f - \Phi_c)^t$ | Percolation conductivity | $t \approx 1.65$, $\Phi_c$ percolation threshold | CNT: $10^{-3}$; CB: $3 \times 10^{-2}$ | | 5 | $k_c = k_m \dfrac{1 + 2\Phi_f (k_f - k_m)/(k_f + 2k_m)}{1 - \Phi_f (k_f - k_m)/(k_f + 2k_m)}$ | Maxwell–Eucken thermal conductivity | $k_c, k_m, k_f$ conductivities | — | | 6 | $P_c = P_m \cdot \dfrac{1 - \Phi_f}{1 + (\alpha/2)\,\Phi_f}$ | Nielsen barrier permeability | $\alpha = L/W$ platelet aspect ratio | $\alpha = 10 \Rightarrow P_c = 0.09\,P_m$ for $\Phi_f = 0.1$ | | 7 | $\tau = \tau_0 \cdot e^{(B\,\Phi_f)}$ | Mooney viscosity (high loadings) | $B \approx 2.5$, $\tau$ shear stress | — | | 8 | $V_f^{max} = \dfrac{1}{1 + (\rho_f / \rho_m)(W_f / W_m)^{-1}}$ | Loading limit (weight-to-volume conversion) | $\rho_f, \rho_m$ densities; $W_f/W_m$ weight ratio | Used in recipe design | > **Critical pipe-safe note:** In all equations above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes absolute value when needed (e.g., $\vert \Phi_f - \Phi_c \vert$), but is normally positive because $\Phi_f > \Phi_c$ post-percolation. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Einstein Equation for Modulus of Dilute Suspension ### Step 1 — Problem Setup Consider a single rigid spherical particle of modulus $E_f$ embedded in an infinite polymer matrix of modulus $E_m$. We assume: - The particle is much stiffer than the matrix ($E_f \gg E_m$), - Perfect interfacial adhesion (no slip), - Dilute loading ($\Phi_f \ll 1$) so that particle–particle interactions are negligible. Under an applied far-field tensile strain $\epsilon$, the strain energy stored in the composite $U_c$ must equal the strain energy stored in the matrix plus that stored in the filler, modified by the **stress concentration** around the particle. ### Step 2 — Strain Energy Without Filler For a unit volume of matrix: $$U_m = \tfrac{1}{2}\,E_m\,\epsilon^2$$ ### Step 3 — Effect of Inclusion (Energy Argument) Classical elasticity (Goodier, 1933) shows that a rigid sphere in a stressed matrix increases the stored energy by a factor that depends on the volume fraction. For a single sphere of volume $V_p$ in volume $V$: $$U_c = U_m \left[1 + \Phi_f \cdot \dfrac{5}{2}\right]$$ where $\Phi_f = V_p / V$. > **Why 5/2?** The numerical factor arises from integrating the deviatoric and dilatational stress concentrations around the sphere in an elastic continuum. The hydrostatic component contributes 1, and the deviatoric contributes 3/2, summing to 5/2. ### Step 4 — Equating to Effective Modulus By definition, $U_c = \tfrac{1}{2} E_c \epsilon^2$. Substituting: $$\tfrac{1}{2} E_c \epsilon^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} E_m \epsilon^2 \left(1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Final Einstein Result $$\boxed{\,E_c = E_m \left(1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f\right)\,}$$ **Limitation:** Valid only for $\Phi_f < 0.05$. For higher loadings, particle–particle interactions become significant and the **Guth–Gold equation** must be used. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Guth–Gold Equation (Non-Spherical / High Loading) ### Step 1 — Empirical Extension E. Guth (1945) and O. Gold extended Einstein's result to account for: - Particle **shape** (aspect ratio $S_f$), - Particle–particle **hydrodynamic and steric interactions** at higher $\Phi_f$. ### Step 2 — Starting Form The modulus of a composite with elongated fillers (rods, fibers) is: $$E_c = E_m \left(1 + 0.675\,S_f + 1.62\,S_f^2 \,\Phi_f\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Deriving the Shape Term For a prolate spheroid with semi-axes $a$ (long) and $b$ (short), $S_f = a/b$. The strain energy contribution per unit volume depends on the Eshelby tensor component $S_{1111}$: $$S_{1111} = \dfrac{1}{2(1-\nu)} \left[1 - 2\nu + \dfrac{3(S_f^2 - 1)}{(S_f^2 - 1)} - \dfrac{1}{2}\left(1 - \dfrac{3S_f^2}{S_f^2 - 1}\right) \ln\left(\dfrac{S_f + 1}{S_f - 1}\right)\right]$$ For $S_f \to \infty$, this reduces approximately to $S_{1111} \approx 0.675\,S_f$, which is the linear shape coefficient. For the quadratic coefficient $1.62\,S_f^2$, the derivation comes from the **second-order stress concentration** in two-particle interactions, where two aligned rods create an amplified stress zone whose volume scales as $S_f^2$. ### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Predict the modulus of a polypropylene composite (matrix $E_m = 1.5$ GPa) loaded with 20 vol% glass fibers having aspect ratio $S_f = 20$. **Step (a):** Compute the linear shape term: $0.675 \times 20 = 13.5$ **Step (b):** Compute the quadratic term: $1.62 \times 400 \times 0.20 = 129.6$ **Step (c):** Sum inside brackets: $1 + 13.5 + 129.6 = 144.1$ **Step (d):** Multiply by matrix modulus: $$E_c = 1.5 \times 144.1 \approx 216.15 \text{ GPa}$$ This is an upper bound; real values are lower due to fiber misalignment and imperfect bonding (typically 40–60% of this prediction). ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Einstein–Roscoe Viscosity Equation ### Step 1 — Starting Point For a dilute suspension of rigid spheres, Einstein showed: $$\eta_c = \eta_0 (1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f)$$ ### Step 2 — Extension to Concentrated Suspensions At high loadings, the spheres cannot move independently; they interact hydrodynamically. Roscoe (1952) recognized that the relative viscosity depends on how close the system is to **maximum packing** $\Phi_m$: $$\eta_c = \eta_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Phi_f}{\Phi_m}\right)^{-2.5}$$ ### Step 3 — Sanity Checks - At $\Phi_f = 0$: $\eta_c = \eta_0$ ✓ - As $\Phi_f \to \Phi_m$: $\eta_c \to \infty$ ✓ (jamming transition) - For $\Phi_f = 0.3$, $\Phi_m = 0.64$: $$\eta_c = \eta_0 (1 - 0.3/0.64)^{-2.5} = \eta_0 (0.531)^{-2.5} \approx \eta_0 \times 6.55$$ ### Step 4 — Physical Meaning The exponent $-2.5$ comes from the **intrinsic viscosity** $[\eta] = 2.5$ for rigid spheres. The denominator $(1 - \Phi_f/\Phi_m)$ is the **free volume** available for flow. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Percolation Threshold for Conductive Composites ### Step 1 — Definition **Percolation threshold** $\Phi_c$ is the filler volume fraction at which a continuous conductive pathway first forms across the sample. ### Step 2 — Geometric Probability For spherical fillers of diameter $d$ randomly placed in a 3-D matrix, the **excluded volume** concept gives: $$\Phi_c \approx \dfrac{1.4}{Z}$$ where $Z$ is the average number of contact points per particle (~6 for random close packing). Thus $\Phi_c \approx 0.23$ for spheres — but this is the *site* percolation; the *bond* threshold is lower. ### Step 3 — Effect of Aspect Ratio For fibers of length $L$ and diameter $d$, the percolation threshold scales as: $$\Phi_c \approx \dfrac{0.7}{S_f}$$ so for carbon nanotubes ($S_f \approx 1000$), $\Phi_c \approx 0.0007$ — explaining why CNTs make polymers conductive at very low loadings. ### Step 4 — Conductivity Above Percolation Once $\Phi_f > \Phi_c$: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot (\Phi_f - \Phi_c)^t, \quad t \approx 1.65 \text{ (3-D)}$$ Below percolation, $\sigma_c$ is essentially the polymer's intrinsic conductivity ($\sim 10^{-14}$ S/cm). --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Filler Classification & Effect Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Polymer Matrix] --> B{Add Filler} B --> C[Particulate] B --> D[Fibrous] B --> E[Platelet] B --> F[Nano] C --> C1[CaCO3 Silica] C --> C2[TiO2 Barium Sulphate] D --> D1[Glass Fiber] D --> D2[Carbon Fiber] E --> E1[Talc Mica] E --> E2[Nanoclay] F --> F1[CNT Fumed Silica] F --> F2[Graphene] C1 --> P1[Mechanical Modulus] C2 --> P2[Aesthetic Opacity] D1 --> P3[Reinforcement Strength] D2 --> P4[Thermal Stability] E1 --> P5[Dimensional Stability] E2 --> P6[Barrier Properties] F1 --> P7[Electrical Conductivity] F2 --> P8[Multifunctional Performance] P1 --> OUT[Modified Polymer Product] P2 --> OUT P3 --> OUT P4 --> OUT P5 --> OUT P6 --> OUT P7 --> OUT P8 --> OUT style A fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333 style OUT fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333 style B fill:#f4b084,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence — Filler–Matrix Interaction at Interface ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant M as Polymer Matrix participant I as Interphase Layer participant F as Filler Particle Note over M: Bulk chains free to move M->>I: Chain segments adsorb onto surface I->>F: Form bound polymer layer 1 to 2 nm Note over I: Restricted mobility shell 5 to 10 nm Note over F: Rigid, high-modulus substrate I-->>M: Constrains adjacent chain motion M->>F: Stress transfer via shear at interface F-->>M: Carries load, lowers strain Note over M: Net effect: increased modulus
increased Tg
increased viscosity ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR RAW[Raw Polymer Resin] --> MIX[Compounding Stage] FILLER[Filler
+ Coupling Agent] --> MIX MIX --> MELT[Melt Processing
Extrusion or Injection] MELT --> DISP[Dispersion and Distribution] DISP --> TEST[Property Testing] TEST --> MECH[Mechanical Tests] TEST --> THERM[Thermal Tests] TEST =>> RHEO[Rheological Tests] TEST --> ELEC[Electrical Tests] MECH --> DEC[Design Decision] THERM --> DEC RHEO --> DEC ELEC --> DEC DEC --> PROD[Final Polymer Product] style RAW fill:#ffe599 style FILLER fill:#b6d7a8 style PROD fill:#a4c2f4 style DEC fill:#f4cccc ``` > **Figure interpretation:** This diagram illustrates how the choice of filler type, combined with the polymer matrix, flows through compounding, processing, and testing stages before yielding a final product. Each testing domain (mechanical, thermal, rheological, electrical) provides feedback to the design decision, ensuring the filler selection is optimized for the target application. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define the term "filler" in the context of polymer compounding. Differentiate between an inert filler and a reinforcing filler with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** A filler is a solid particulate or fibrous substance added to a polymer matrix to improve specific properties, reduce cost, or modify processing behavior, without being a primary reactant. > > **Inert Filler (1 mark):** An inert filler acts mainly as a cost-reducing extender and has minimal effect on mechanical properties. *Example:* Calcium carbonate (whiting) in PVC. > > **Reinforcing Filler (1 mark):** A reinforcing filler significantly enhances mechanical properties such as tensile strength, modulus, and abrasion resistance. *Example:* Carbon black in natural rubber tyres. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What is the "percolation threshold" in conductive polymer composites? Mention the typical value for carbon black-filled polymers.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (2 marks):** The percolation threshold $\Phi_c$ is the critical filler volume fraction at which a continuous three-dimensional conductive network first forms inside the insulating polymer matrix, causing a sharp rise in electrical conductivity (often 8–12 orders of magnitude). > > **Typical value (1 mark):** For carbon black in typical thermoplastics/rubbers, $\Phi_c \approx 0.02$ to $0.05$ (2–5 vol%). --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` ### Question A **(a)** With the help of a suitable diagram, explain the **classification of fillers** based on geometry and function. Give two examples for each category. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **effect of fillers on the mechanical properties** (modulus, tensile strength, impact strength) of polymer composites. Use appropriate equations. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Classification of Fillers **Classification by geometry (3 marks):** | Geometry | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | Particulate (L/D ≈ 1) | Spherical or irregular particles | CaCO₃, silica | | Fibrous (L/D > 10) | Elongated rods or fibers | Glass fiber, carbon fiber | | Platelet / Lamellar | Plate-like, high aspect ratio in 2D | Talc, mica, nanoclay | | Nano (size < 100 nm) | Extremely fine, very high surface area | CNT, fumed silica | **Classification by function (2 marks):** | Function | Description | Example | |---|---|---| | Inert / Extender | Cost reduction, minimal reinforcement | Whiting, china clay | | Reinforcing | Major mechanical enhancement | Carbon black, fumed silica | | Functional / Specialty | Imparts a specific property (conductivity, FR, magnetism) | CNT, ATH, ferrite | | Coupling-agent treated | Surface-modified for adhesion | Silane-coated glass fiber | **Diagrammatic representation (2 marks):** Use a tree/flowchart showing the hierarchical classification (refer to Section 2.1 of these notes). #### Part (b) — Effect on Mechanical Properties **Effect on Modulus (3 marks):** The modulus always **increases** with filler content. For dilute spherical fillers, Einstein's equation applies: $$E_c = E_m (1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f)$$ For non-spherical fillers, the Guth–Gold equation is used: $$E_c = E_m (1 + 0.675\,S_f + 1.62\,S_f^2 \Phi_f)$$ **Effect on Tensile Strength (2 marks):** - **Poorly bonded fillers**: Tensile strength **decreases** because filler particles act as stress concentrators and crack initiation sites. - **Well-bonded fillers**: Tensile strength **increases** due to effective stress transfer across the interface. An optimum filler loading exists beyond which tensile strength drops due to agglomeration. **Effect on Impact Strength (2 marks):** Impact strength generally **decreases** with rigid fillers because: 1. Filler particles reduce polymer chain mobility and prevent plastic deformation. 2. Stress concentrations at particle-matrix interfaces initiate brittle cracks. Exception: **Elastomeric fillers** (e.g., rubber particles in HIPS, ABS) **improve impact strength** via crazing and shear yielding mechanisms. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` ### Question B **(a)** Derive the **Einstein equation** for the modulus of a polymer composite containing dilute rigid spherical fillers. State its assumptions and limitations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **effect of fillers on thermal and rheological properties** of polymers. Include appropriate equations in your answer. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### Part (a) — Derivation of Einstein Equation **Assumptions (2 marks):** 1. Filler particles are **rigid** ($E_f \gg E_m$) and **perfectly bonded** to the matrix. 2. Loading is **dilute** ($\Phi_f < 0.05$) so particle–particle interactions are negligible. 3. Filler geometry is **spherical** (L/D ≈ 1). 4. The matrix behaves as a **linear elastic continuum**. **Derivation (5 marks):** Step 1: Strain energy in unfilled matrix per unit volume is $U_m = \tfrac{1}{2} E_m \epsilon^2$. Step 2: A rigid sphere in a stressed matrix increases the local strain energy. For a single sphere of volume $V_p$ in total volume $V$, Goodier's elasticity solution gives: $$U_c = U_m \left[1 + \Phi_f \cdot \tfrac{5}{2}\right], \quad \text{where} \quad \Phi_f = \dfrac{V_p}{V}$$ Step 3: Equating to the composite modulus definition $U_c = \tfrac{1}{2} E_c \epsilon^2$: $$\tfrac{1}{2} E_c \epsilon^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} E_m \epsilon^2 (1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f)$$ Step 4: Simplifying yields the **Einstein equation**: $$\boxed{E_c = E_m (1 + 2.5\,\Phi_f)}$$ **Limitations:** - Only valid for $\Phi_f < 0.05$. - Assumes spherical geometry; fails for fibers, platelets. - Assumes perfect adhesion; real systems have imperfect bonding. #### Part (b) — Effect on Thermal and Rheological Properties **Effect on Thermal Properties (4 marks):** | Property | Effect | Mechanism | |---|---|---| | Glass Transition (Tg) | Slight increase (2–10 °C) | Restricted polymer chain mobility at interphase | | Heat Deflection Temp (HDT) | Substantial increase | Higher modulus retention at elevated T | | Thermal Conductivity (k) | Increases | Maxwell–Eucken: $k_c = k_m \dfrac{1 + 2\Phi_f(k_f - k_m)/(k_f + 2k_m)}{1 - \Phi_f(k_f - k_m)/(k_f + 2k_m)}$ | | CTE | Decreases | Low-CTE filler dilutes polymer expansion | | Flammability | Reduced (with FR fillers) | Endothermic dehydration releases water vapor | **Effect on Rheological Properties (3 marks):** Melt viscosity **increases** with filler loading. For spherical particles (Einstein–Roscoe): $$\eta_c = \eta_0 \left(1 - \dfrac{\Phi_f}{\Phi_m}\right)^{-2.5}$$ Implications for processing: - Higher injection/ extrusion pressures required. - Increased die-swell in some cases. - Need for processing aids or surface treatments to reduce viscosity. For high loadings, the **Mooney equation** is used: $$\eta_c = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\left(\dfrac{2.5\,\Phi_f}{1 - \Phi_f/\Phi_m}\right)$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Don't confuse "filler" with "reinforcement".** A filler is a generic additive; reinforcement is a function. Carbon black can be both. > 2. **Don't apply Einstein's equation beyond $\Phi_f = 0.05$.** Examiners will deduct marks if you use it for 30 vol% glass fiber. > 3. **Always state assumptions** before applying modulus equations (perfect bonding, dilute loading, spherical geometry). > 4. **For conductivity questions**, remember that below the percolation threshold, the composite behaves as an insulator — not a partial conductor. > 5. **Don't confuse "oil absorption" with "moisture content"** when discussing filler properties. > 6. **Units trap**: When using volume fraction, ensure densities are in consistent units (g/cm³ with phr / phr of weight). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Effect of Fillers)** - ✅ **Definition**: A filler is a solid additive used to modify polymer properties, reduce cost, or enhance performance. - ✅ **Classification by geometry**: Particulate, fibrous, platelet, nano. - ✅ **Classification by function**: Inert, reinforcing, functional/specialty, coupling-agent treated. - ✅ **Interphase concept**: Three polymer populations — bound, restricted, bulk. - ✅ **Einstein modulus equation**: $E_c = E_m(1 + 2.5\Phi_f)$; valid for $\Phi_f < 0.05$, spherical fillers. - ✅ **Guth–Gold equation**: For non-spherical / high aspect-ratio fillers; quadratic in $\Phi_f$ with $S_f^2$ term. - ✅ **Einstein–Roscoe viscosity**: $\eta_c = \eta_0 (1 - \Phi_f/\Phi_m)^{-2.5}$; diverges at $\Phi_m$. - ✅ **Percolation threshold**: $\Phi_c \approx 0.7/S_f$ for fibers; conductivity scales as $(\Phi_f - \Phi_c)^t$, $t \approx 1.65$. - ✅ **Mechanical effects**: Modulus ↑, hardness ↑, impact ↓ (rigid fillers), tensile strength depends on bonding. - ✅ **Thermal effects**: Tg ↑ slightly, HDT ↑ substantially, CTE ↓, thermal conductivity ↑. - ✅ **Rheological effects**: Viscosity ↑ with $\Phi_f$; processability decreases. - ✅ **Electrical effects**: Insulating fillers increase dielectric strength; conductive fillers create percolation networks. - ✅ **Coupling agents** (silanes, titanates): Improve filler–matrix adhesion, reduce viscosity, enhance mechanical strength. - ✅ **Critical pigment volume concentration (CPVC)**: Point at which matrix is insufficient to wet all filler; properties drop sharply above CPVC. - ✅ **Maximum packing fraction** $\Phi_m$: ~0.64 for random close-packed spheres; ~0.91 for hexagonal close packing. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Effect of Fillers on Properties and Performance**

topics.title: Long Term Property Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Long Term Property Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Long Term Property Analysis (LTPA)** in polymer engineering is defined as the systematic evaluation of the **time-dependent mechanical, thermal, and environmental response** of polymeric materials under sustained loading, constant strain, cyclic loading, or prolonged exposure to aggressive service conditions over extended durations (typically **100 hours to >10 years** of equivalent service life).

The discipline is governed by the fundamental premise that **polymers are viscoelastic materials** — they simultaneously exhibit characteristics of viscous liquids (flow under load) and elastic solids (instantaneous recovery). Unlike metals, whose short-term mechanical properties (yield strength, ultimate tensile strength) remain representative of long-term service behavior, polymer properties exhibit a strong **time-dependency**. Therefore, a polymer product designed using only short-term tensile data will almost certainly fail prematurely in service.

KTU 2024 scheme specifically categorizes long-term properties into four primary domains:

| # | Domain | Driving Parameter | Typical Duration |
|---|--------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Creep** | Constant Stress (σ = constant) | 10³ – 10⁵ hours |
| 2 | **Stress Relaxation** | Constant Strain (ε = constant) | 10² – 10⁴ hours |
| 3 | **Fatigue** | Cyclic Stress (σ = f(t)) | 10³ – 10⁷ cycles |
| 4 | **Aging/Weathering** | Environmental Exposure | Months – Years |

The **master reference standards** most commonly cited in KTU board questions and Indian industrial practice are:
- **ASTM D2990** — Standard Test Method for Tensile, Compressive, and Flexural Creep and Creep-Rupture of Plastics
- **ASTM D5279** — Standard Test Method for Plastics: Dynamic Mechanical Properties — In Torsion
- **ISO 899-1 / ISO 899-2** — Determination of Creep Behavior
- **ASTM D4329 / ASTM G154** — UV/Weathering Exposure

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Plastic Chair on the Veranda"**

Imagine a **plastic chair** placed on a sunlit veranda. A person of 75 kg sits on it for **6 hours every evening**. The chair looks perfectly fine on Day 1. But after **2 years**:

1. The legs have bent slightly outward — this is **CREEP** (the chair keeps deforming slowly under constant body weight).
2. If you stretch a rubber band around a stack of books and leave it for a week, the rubber band still holds the books, but the tension you had to apply initially has dropped — this is **STRESS RELAXATION** (constant deformation, decaying force).
3. The chair leg becomes brittle and snaps when you suddenly shift your weight — this is **AGING + EMBRITTLEMENT** (UV + oxidative degradation).
4. Repeated sitting down and standing up 10 times a day for 3 years → one leg cracks — this is **FATIGUE**.

**LTPA is the engineering discipline that predicts, quantifies, and designs against all four of these failure modes *before* the product reaches the consumer.**

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics Used Throughout LTPA:**

- **Creep Modulus (E_c(t))**: Defined as σ₀ / ε(t). Units: **MPa** or **GPa**. It is *not* a true elastic modulus but a time-dependent apparent stiffness.
- **Apparent Modulus**: The secant modulus calculated from the isochronous stress-strain curve at a chosen time.
- **Creep Compliance (J(t))**: Defined as ε(t) / σ₀. Units: **Pa⁻¹** or **1/GPa**. The reciprocal of creep modulus (for linear viscoelasticity).
- **Time-Temperature Shift Factor (a_T)**: A dimensionless ratio used to horizontally shift creep curves measured at different temperatures onto a single master curve.
- **WLF Constants**: C₁ = **17.44**, C₂ = **51.6 K** (universal near T_g). Reference temperature is typically T_g.
- **Activation Energy for Creep (Q_c)**: Typically **80 – 200 kJ/mol** for thermoplastics below T_g.

---

## 1.4 Why LTPA is Non-Negotiable for Polymers

| Property Class | Metal (e.g., Mild Steel) | Polymer (e.g., HDPE) |
|---|---|---|
| Short-term UTS predictability of long-term life | High (~95% accurate) | Poor (~30–50% accurate) |
| Creep at Room Temperature | Negligible | Significant (often >2% strain in 1 year) |
| Sensitivity to 10°C temperature rise | ~1% strength loss | **~10–15% strength loss** |
| Standard design lifetime data | Static tables available | Time-dependent curves mandatory |

This is why KTU 2024 scheme dedicates an entire module foundation to LTPA — without it, the design of any polymer pressure pipe, automotive bumper, or cable insulation is guesswork.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Long-Term Behavior — Operational Breakdown

### Pillar 1: CREEP (Constant Load, Increasing Strain)

When a constant stress σ₀ is instantaneously applied to a polymer specimen and held, the strain evolves as:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_{instantaneous} + \varepsilon_{delayed\,elastic} + \varepsilon_{viscous\,flow}$$

The four classical stages of creep strain vs. time on a logarithmic time axis:

1. **Primary (Transient) Creep** — Strain rate decreases with time; polymer chains undergo uncoiling and local rearrangement.
2. **Secondary (Steady-State) Creep** — Strain rate is approximately constant; chain slippage dominates.
3. **Tertiary Creep** — Strain rate accelerates; microvoid formation and crazing initiate.
4. **Rupture** — Specimen fails; defines the **stress rupture time (t_r)**.

The simplest one-dimensional constitutive model taught at KTU level is the **four-parameter Burgers model**, combining Maxwell and Kelvin-Voigt elements in series:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M} t$$

where the characteristic retardation time is:

$$\tau_K = \frac{\eta_K}{E_K}$$

### Pillar 2: STRESS RELAXATION (Constant Strain, Decreasing Stress)

A strain ε₀ is imposed instantaneously and held. The stress decays:

$$\sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 \cdot E(t)$$

where E(t) is the **relaxation modulus**. For a Maxwell element:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau_M}, \quad \tau_M = \frac{\eta_M}{E_M}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** Creep and Stress Relaxation are NOT the same measurement. Creep compliance J(t) and relaxation modulus E(t) are mathematically related via the convolution integral (Boltzmann Superposition Principle), but only for **linear viscoelastic** behavior:

$$\int_0^t E(t-\tau) J(\tau) \, d\tau = t$$

### Pillar 3: FATIGUE (Cyclic Loading)

Polymers fail under cyclic loads well below their static ultimate strength. The **S-N curve** (Stress vs. Number of cycles to failure) for polymers typically:
- Does **not** exhibit a true endurance limit like steels.
- Shows a continuously decreasing stress with cycles.
- Is highly sensitive to frequency (higher frequency → internal heating → faster failure).

### Pillar 4: AGING / ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION

Three principal aging pathways:
- **Physical Aging** — Free volume reduction below T_g; material becomes denser and more brittle over time.
- **Chemical Aging (Oxidation)** — Free-radical chain scission; accelerated by heat, oxygen, transition metal catalysts.
- **Weathering (UV + Moisture + Thermal Cycling)** — Photo-oxidative degradation of chromophores; surface microcracking.

---

## 2.2 The Isochronous and Isometric Stress-Strain Curves

These are the two most critical design aids in LTPA:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Isochronous Stress-Strain Curve (ISSC):** A plot of stress (y-axis) vs. strain (x-axis), where each curve corresponds to a fixed time of loading (e.g., 1 h, 10 h, 1000 h, 10000 h). Used by designers to pick allowable stress for a given strain and service life.

**Isometric Stress-Strain Curve (IMSC):** A plot of stress (y-axis) vs. time (x-axis), where each curve corresponds to a fixed strain. Used to determine how preload (e.g., bolt clamping force) decays with time.

Both curves are derivable from the same creep data using interpolation.

---

## 2.3 Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **THE most important concept** in KTU board questions on LTPA. Polymer creep data at different temperatures can be horizontally shifted along the log(time) axis to generate a single **master curve** at a chosen reference temperature T_ref.

The horizontal shift factor a_T is given by the **Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) equation** for temperatures T_g < T < T_g + 100°C:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T - T_{ref})}$$

For temperatures well below T_g, the **Arrhenius equation** is preferred:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{Q_c}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$$

where R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** and Q_c is the activation energy.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation you must memorize for a 14-mark LTPA question. All symbols follow ISO 1043 (Plastics — Symbols and Abbreviations).

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_c(t) = \sigma_0 \, / \, \varepsilon(t)$ | Creep Modulus | MPa |
| 2 | $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) \, / \, \sigma_0$ | Creep Compliance | GPa⁻¹ |
| 3 | $\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_K}(1-e^{-t/\tau_K}) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}t$ | Burgers 4-Param Model | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$ | Maxwell Stress Decay | MPa |
| 5 | $\tau_M = \eta_M / E_M$ | Maxwell Relaxation Time | s |
| 6 | $\tau_K = \eta_K / E_K$ | Kelvin Retardation Time | s |
| 7 | $\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-C_1(T-T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T-T_{ref})}$ | WLF Shift Factor | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{Q_c}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)$ | Arrhenius Shift Factor | dimensionless |
| 9 | $t_{ref} = a_T \cdot t_{measured}$ | Time-Temperature Equivalence | s |
| 10 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = \sigma_0 / \eta_M$ | Steady-State Creep Strain Rate | s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $E_{apparent} = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t_{design})$ | Apparent Modulus (from ISSC) | MPa |
| 12 | Larson-Miller: $LMP = \frac{T(K)}{1000}\left[C + \log_{10}(t_r)\right]$ | Creep Rupture Parameter | — |
| 13 | $\frac{\sigma_{long-term}}{\sigma_{short-term}} = k$ | Creep Strength Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 14 | $a_T = t_T / t_{T_{ref}}$ | Definition of Shift Factor | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** Temperatures in the WLF and Arrhenius equations MUST be in **Kelvin (K)**. Board examiners regularly deduct 1 mark if a student writes the Arrhenius equation with T in °C.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Pipe Design (HDPE, MDPE):** Designers use ISO 9080 and the Larson-Miller parameter to extrapolate 50-year creep rupture data from short-term tests (typically 1–2 years).
- **Automotive Interior Trim:** Creep compliance curves at 23°C, 50°C, and 80°C are used to predict sagging of dashboards after 10 years of service.
- **Cable Insulation:** Stress relaxation data determines the long-term radial clamping pressure of cable joints.
- **Geomembranes (LLDPE):** Stress cracking resistance (bent strip or NOTCH constant tensile load test per ASTM D5397) is a critical long-term performance metric.
- **Bolted Plastic Flanges:** Isometric curves determine the minimum retightening interval.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Burgers Model Creep Equation (Four-Parameter)

### 3.1.1 Model Topology

The **Burgers model** physically represents a polymer as a Maxwell element (spring E_M in series with dashpot η_M) connected in series with a Kelvin-Voigt element (spring E_K in parallel with dashpot η_K).

### 3.1.2 Setting Up the Strain Decomposition

Under a step stress σ₀ applied at t = 0, by **series additivity of strain**:

$$\varepsilon_{total}(t) = \varepsilon_{Maxwell}(t) + \varepsilon_{Kelvin-Voigt}(t)$$

### 3.1.3 Maxwell Element Strain Derivation

The Maxwell element obeys the differential equation:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_M = \frac{\dot{\sigma}_M}{E_M} + \frac{\sigma_M}{\eta_M}$$

For a step stress σ₀ (constant in time), $\dot{\sigma}_M = 0$, so:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_M = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M}$$

Integrating with initial condition ε_M(0) = σ₀/E_M (instantaneous spring stretch):

$$\varepsilon_M(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M} t$$

### 3.1.4 Kelvin-Voigt Element Strain Derivation

The Kelvin-Voigt element follows:

$$\sigma_0 = E_K \varepsilon_K(t) + \eta_K \dot{\varepsilon}_K(t)$$

Rearranging into a first-order ODE:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_K + \frac{E_K}{\eta_K}\varepsilon_K = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_K}$$

Defining retardation time τ_K = η_K / E_K:

$$\dot{\varepsilon}_K + \frac{\varepsilon_K}{\tau_K} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_K}$$

The integrating factor is e^(t/τ_K). Solving with initial condition ε_K(0) = 0:

$$\varepsilon_K(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)$$

### 3.1.5 Combining to Get Burgers Equation

Adding both elements:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{E_M}}_{\text{instantaneous elastic}} + \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{E_K}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}\right)}_{\text{retarded elastic}} + \underbrace{\frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M} t}_{\text{viscous flow}}$$

This is the standard Burgers 4-parameter creep equation.

### 3.1.6 Worked Numerical Example

> **Given:** A polypropylene specimen is subjected to σ₀ = 5 MPa. Model parameters: E_M = 1.5 GPa, E_K = 0.8 GPa, η_M = 400 GPa·s, η_K = 80 GPa·s. Find the strain at t = 100 s.

**Step 1 — Convert units to be consistent (MPa and seconds):**
- E_M = 1500 MPa
- E_K = 800 MPa
- η_M = 400,000 MPa·s
- η_K = 80,000 MPa·s

**Step 2 — Compute retardation time:**
$$\tau_K = \frac{\eta_K}{E_K} = \frac{80{,}000}{800} = 100 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute each strain component:**

$$\varepsilon_{instantaneous} = \frac{5}{1500} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_{retarded} = \frac{5}{800}\left(1 - e^{-100/100}\right) = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - e^{-1})$$

$$e^{-1} = 0.3679 \Rightarrow \varepsilon_{retarded} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6321 = 3.951 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\varepsilon_{viscous} = \frac{5}{400{,}000} \times 100 = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Total strain:**

$$\varepsilon(100) = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} + 3.951 \times 10^{-3} + 1.25 \times 10^{-3} = 8.534 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5 — Creep modulus at 100 s:**

$$E_c(100) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon(100)} = \frac{5}{8.534 \times 10^{-3}} = 585.9 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Interpretation:** Notice that even though the instantaneous elastic modulus is 1500 MPa, after just 100 seconds of sustained loading, the effective stiffness has dropped to **less than 40%** of its short-term value. This single example demonstrates why short-term tensile data is dangerously misleading for polymer design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of WLF Shift Factor (Brief)

Starting from the Doolittle free-volume equation for viscosity:

$$\eta = A \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where f is the fractional free volume and B ≈ 1. Assuming f increases linearly with temperature above T_g:

$$f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$$

Taking ratios of viscosities at T and T_ref, and using WLF's choice of T_ref = T_g:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \log_{10}\left(\frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}\right) = -\frac{B}{2.303\, f_g} \cdot \frac{T - T_g}{f_g/\alpha_f + (T - T_g)}$$

Matching with the WLF form gives the universal constants:

$$C_1 = \frac{B}{2.303\, f_g} = 17.44, \quad C_2 = \frac{f_g}{\alpha_f} = 51.6 \text{ K}$$

These are termed **"universal"** because they apply to nearly all amorphous polymers within roughly T_g < T < T_g + 100°C.

---

## 3.3 Worked WLF Numerical Example

> **Given:** Creep compliance of PVC is measured at 50°C. The reference temperature is 30°C. Determine the shift factor and the equivalent time at 30°C for a measurement taken at 50°C at t = 3600 s.

**Step 1 — Temperature difference:**
$$\Delta T = 50 - 30 = 20°C$$

**Step 2 — Apply WLF equation:**

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-17.44 \times 20}{51.6 + 20} = \frac{-348.8}{71.6} = -4.872$$

**Step 3 — Compute a_T:**

$$a_T = 10^{-4.872} = 1.342 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent time at reference (30°C):**

$$t_{ref} = a_T \times t_{measured} = 1.342 \times 10^{-5} \times 3600 = 0.0483 \text{ s}$$

> **Interpretation:** A 1-hour test at 50°C corresponds to only **0.048 seconds** of equivalent service time at 30°C. This shows that **elevated temperature dramatically accelerates creep** — this is the basis of accelerated life testing used to predict 50-year HDPE pipe behavior from <2 years of laboratory data.

---

## 3.4 Larson-Miller Parameter (Creep Rupture Extrapolation)

For stress rupture (time-to-failure at constant stress and temperature), the Larson-Miller parameter LMP is:

$$LMP = \frac{T(K)}{1000}\left[C + \log_{10}(t_r)\right]$$

where C is typically **20** for polymers. A higher LMP for a given stress means a more creep-resistant material.

> **Worked Example:** An acetal copolymer fails in 500 hours at 80°C under 35 MPa. Predict the time to failure at 50°C under the same stress.

$$\Delta LMP = \frac{T_1}{1000}\left[20 + \log_{10}(t_1)\right] = \frac{353}{1000}[20 + \log_{10}(500)]$$

$$\log_{10}(500) = 2.699$$

$$LMP_1 = 0.353 \times 22.699 = 8.013$$

Assuming same LMP at 50°C (323 K):

$$8.013 = 0.323 \times [20 + \log_{10}(t_2)]$$

$$20 + \log_{10}(t_2) = 24.808$$

$$\log_{10}(t_2) = 4.808 \Rightarrow t_2 = 64{,}270 \text{ hours} \approx 7.34 \text{ years}$$

> **Engineering Significance:** The part is predicted to survive **more than 14× longer** at the lower service temperature — confirming the strong temperature sensitivity of polymer long-term performance.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Four Mechanisms of Long-Term Failure — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[LTPA Input: Service Conditions] --> nodeB[Constant Stress Load]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Constant Strain Load]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Cyclic Load]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Environmental Exposure]
    
    nodeB --> nodeF[CREEP - Increasing Strain]
    nodeC --> nodeG[STRESS RELAXATION - Decreasing Stress]
    nodeD --> nodeH[FATIGUE - Cyclic Damage Accumulation]
    nodeE --> nodeI[AGING - Property Drift]
    
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Stage 1: Primary Transient]
    nodeF1 --> nodeF2[Stage 2: Secondary Steady]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3[Stage 3: Tertiary Acceleration]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4[Stage 4: Creep Rupture]
    
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Boltzmann Convolution Integral]
    nodeG1 --> nodeG2[Maxwell Element: Exponential Decay]
    nodeG2 --> nodeG3[Long Term Clamp Force Loss]
    
    nodeH --> nodeH1[SN Curve Construction]
    nodeH1 --> nodeH2[Hysteretic Heating]
    nodeH2 --> nodeH3[No True Endurance Limit]
    
    nodeI --> nodeI1[Physical Aging Free Volume Loss]
    nodeI --> nodeI2[Chemical Aging Oxidation]
    nodeI --> nodeI3[Weathering UV plus Moisture]
    
    nodeF4 --> nodeJ[Design Failure Mode Library]
    nodeG3 --> nodeJ
    nodeH3 --> nodeJ
    nodeI1 --> nodeJ
    nodeI2 --> nodeJ
    nodeI3 --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK[KTU Designer Output: Isochronous Curve Apparent Modulus Allowable Stress Creep Life]
```

## 4.2 Burgers Model — Mechanical Network Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStart[Stress Sigma0 Applied] --> nodeMaxwell
    nodeMaxwell[Maxwell Element E_M dashpot eta_M in series] --> nodeKelvin
    nodeKelvin[Kelvin Voigt Element E_K dashpot eta_K in parallel] --> nodeEnd[Total Strain epsilon]
    
    subgraph MaxwellBranch[Maxwell Element]
        nodeE1[Spring E_M] --- nodeD1[Viscosity eta_M]
    end
    
    subgraph KelvinBranch[Kelvin Voigt Element]
        nodeE2[Spring E_K] === nodeD2[Viscosity eta_K]
    end
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- **Maxwell branch** (top): Captures instantaneous elastic response + irreversible viscous flow.
- **Kelvin-Voigt branch** (bottom, parallel arrangement shown by `===`): Captures time-dependent retarded elasticity (recoverable on unloading).

## 4.3 Time-Temperature Superposition — Master Curve Construction

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeRaw[Raw Creep Data at T1 T2 T3 T4 T5] --> nodeShift[Apply WLF or Arrhenius Shift Factor a_T]
    nodeShift --> nodeMaster[Single Master Curve at T_ref]
    nodeMaster --> nodeExtrapolate[Extrapolate to Long Design Life]
    nodeExtrapolate --> nodeDesign[Design Allowable Stress at 50 year Service Life]
    
    nodeT1[Below T_g Arrhenius] -.-> nodeShift
    nodeT2[Above T_g WLF] -.-> nodeShift
    nodeT3[Near T_g Transition Zone] -.-> nodeShift
```

## 4.4 Isochronous vs Isometric Stress-Strain Curves — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeFamily[Family of Creep Curves Strain vs Time at various Stress levels] --> nodeIso
    nodeFamily --> nodeIsoM
    
    nodeIso[Isochronous Stress Strain Curve: Each curve is constant time] --> nodeIso1[1 hour curve]
    nodeIso --> nodeIso2[10 hour curve]
    nodeIso --> nodeIso3[1000 hour curve]
    nodeIso --> nodeIso4[100000 hour curve]
    
    nodeIsoM[Isometric Stress vs Time: Each curve is constant strain] --> nodeIsoM1[0.1 percent strain]
    nodeIsoM --> nodeIsoM2[0.5 percent strain]
    nodeIsoM --> nodeIsoM3[1 percent strain]
    
    nodeIso1 --> nodeDesignUse[Designer selects allowable stress for chosen strain and design life]
    nodeIso4 --> nodeDesignUse
    nodeIsoM2 --> nodeBoltDesign[Bolt preload loss determination]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for the exam:** The *Isochronous* approach answers the question **"What stress can I apply for 10 years if I want strain to stay below 1%?"** The *Isometric* approach answers **"After 5 years at a constant 1% strain, what residual stress remains to hold my joint together?"**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question A1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Define creep and state two polymers in which creep is critical for design.**

**Model Answer:**
Creep is the time-dependent continuous deformation of a material under a constant applied stress at constant temperature. For polymers, creep occurs because the long-chain molecular structure undergoes gradual uncoiling, chain slippage, and free-volume redistribution over time. Creep is particularly critical for:
1. **HDPE (High Density Polyethylene)** — used in pressure pipes and chemical tanks.
2. **Nylon 6,6** — used in automotive under-hood components and gears.

(1 mark for definition; 1 mark for polymer examples with application.)

### Question A2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**State the Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) equation and identify its range of validity.**

**Model Answer:**
The WLF equation is:

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_{ref})}{C_2 + (T - T_{ref})}$$

with universal constants **C₁ = 17.44** and **C₂ = 51.6 K** when T_ref is taken as the glass transition temperature T_g. The equation is valid for temperatures in the range **T_g to T_g + 100°C**. Outside this range (well below T_g), the Arrhenius equation is preferred.

(1 mark equation; 1 mark range.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer EITHER the full Question A OR the full Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks): Comprehensive Long-Term Property Problem

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`**

**(a)** A HDPE specimen is subjected to a constant tensile stress of 8 MPa at 25°C. The Burgers model parameters for this material at 25°C are:
E_M = 1200 MPa, E_K = 600 MPa, η_M = 5 × 10⁵ MPa·s, η_K = 3 × 10⁴ MPa·s.

Compute the strain at t = 50 s, 500 s, and 5000 s. Plot the trend and identify the dominant deformation mechanism at each time.

**(b)** The same HDPE is then tested at 60°C. Using the WLF equation with reference temperature 25°C and standard constants, compute the shift factor a_T and find the equivalent test duration at 25°C that corresponds to a 1000-hour creep test at 60°C. Comment on the significance for HDPE pressure pipe design.

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Compute retardation time τ_K:**

$$\tau_K = \frac{\eta_K}{E_K} = \frac{3 \times 10^4}{600} = 50 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute instantaneous, retarded (delayed elastic), and viscous components.**

The total Burgers equation:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E_M} + \frac{\sigma_0}{E_K}(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K}) + \frac{\sigma_0}{\eta_M} t$$

Common factors:
- σ₀/E_M = 8/1200 = **6.667 × 10⁻³**
- σ₀/E_K = 8/600 = **1.333 × 10⁻²**
- σ₀/η_M = 8 / (5 × 10⁵) = **1.6 × 10⁻⁵ s⁻¹**

**Step 3 — Evaluate at t = 50 s:**

$$e^{-50/50} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

$$\varepsilon(50) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 1.333 \times 10^{-2}(1 - 0.3679) + 1.6 \times 10^{-5}(50)$$

$$\varepsilon(50) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 8.428 \times 10^{-3} + 0.8 \times 10^{-3} = 1.589 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at t = 500 s:**

$$e^{-500/50} = e^{-10} = 4.54 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\varepsilon(500) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 1.333 \times 10^{-2}(1 - 4.54 \times 10^{-5}) + 1.6 \times 10^{-5}(500)$$

$$\varepsilon(500) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 1.332 \times 10^{-2} + 8.0 \times 10^{-3} = 2.799 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate at t = 5000 s:**

$$e^{-5000/50} = e^{-100} \approx 0 \text{ (effectively zero)}$$

$$\varepsilon(5000) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 1.333 \times 10^{-2}(1) + 1.6 \times 10^{-5}(5000)$$

$$\varepsilon(5000) = 6.667 \times 10^{-3} + 1.333 \times 10^{-2} + 8.0 \times 10^{-2} = 1.000 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Dominant Mechanism Analysis:**

| Time (s) | ε_instantaneous | ε_retarded | ε_viscous | Dominant Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50 | 6.67 × 10⁻³ | 8.43 × 10⁻³ | 0.8 × 10⁻³ | Retarded Elastic (chain uncoiling) |
| 500 | 6.67 × 10⁻³ | 13.32 × 10⁻³ | 8.0 × 10⁻³ | Mixed Retarded + Viscous |
| 5000 | 6.67 × 10⁻³ | 13.33 × 10⁻³ | 80.0 × 10⁻³ | Viscous Flow Dominates |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** For service life predictions of HDPE pipes (typically 50 years ≈ 1.6 × 10⁹ s), the viscous flow term completely dominates and ultimately determines long-term design strain. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Step 1 — Apply WLF equation with T_ref = 25°C, T = 60°C:**

$$\Delta T = 60 - 25 = 35°C$$

$$\log_{10}(a_T) = \frac{-17.44 \times 35}{51.6 + 35} = \frac{-610.4}{86.6} = -7.048$$

$$a_T = 10^{-7.048} = 8.954 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent time at 25°C for 1000-hour test at 60°C:**

First, convert 1000 hours to seconds:
$$t_{60°C} = 1000 \times 3600 = 3.6 \times 10^6 \text{ s}$$

Equivalent time at 25°C:
$$t_{25°C, equiv} = a_T \times t_{60°C} = 8.954 \times 10^{-8} \times 3.6 \times 10^6 = 0.322 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Significance for HDPE Pipe Design:**

> The above calculation demonstrates an enormously powerful design principle: a **1000-hour creep test at 60°C** is equivalent to only **0.322 seconds** of real service life at 25°C. The reciprocal interpretation is even more important for accelerated qualification testing:

To simulate **1 year (3.15 × 10⁷ s)** of HDPE service at 25°C, we would need:
$$t_{60°C\,required} = \frac{t_{25°C, desired}}{a_T} = \frac{3.15 \times 10^7}{8.954 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.52 \times 10^{14} \text{ s}$$

This impossibly long duration means that real-life extrapolation (over 50 years = 1.58 × 10⁹ s) requires **even higher test temperatures** or **multiple-temperature master curve fitting** using the ISO 9080 / ASTM D2837 protocol. This is the foundation of HDPE pipe pressure rating standards such as PE 100 and PE 80 designations.

**(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks): Alternative Choice — Stress Relaxation and Isochronous Curves

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`**

**(a)** Derive the stress relaxation response for a Maxwell element subjected to a step strain ε₀ at t = 0. Explain why a single Maxwell element is inadequate to describe long-term polymer behavior and how a Generalized Maxwell Model (Wiechert model) overcomes this.

**(b)** An ABS polymer exhibits the following tensile creep data at 23°C under a constant stress of 10 MPa:
- t = 1 h → ε = 0.0042
- t = 100 h → ε = 0.0061
- t = 1000 h → ε = 0.0085
- t = 10000 h → ε = 0.0118

Construct the **isochronous stress-strain curve** at 1000 hours by linear interpolation, assuming linear viscoelastic behavior holds up to 20 MPa. Determine the allowable design stress for a service life of 10000 h if the maximum allowable strain is 0.5%.

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Maxwell element stress relaxation derivation:**

A step strain ε₀ is applied at t = 0 and held constant. The Maxwell element consists of a spring E and a dashpot η in series. Under constant total strain:

$$\varepsilon_0 = \varepsilon_{spring} + \varepsilon_{dashpot} = \text{constant}$$

Differentiating:
$$\dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} + \dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = 0$$

From spring: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{spring} = \dot{\sigma}/E$
From dashpot: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \sigma/\eta$

Therefore:
$$\frac{\dot{\sigma}}{E} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0 \Rightarrow \dot{\sigma} + \frac{E}{\eta}\sigma = 0$$

Defining relaxation time τ = η/E:

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}$$

where σ₀ = E ε₀ is the initial stress.

**Step 2 — Why single Maxwell is inadequate:**

A single Maxwell element predicts that stress decays to **exactly zero** as t → ∞, which is physically incorrect for polymers that retain a finite equilibrium modulus (or at least a very slowly decaying modulus) over long times. Also, real polymers exhibit a **spectrum of relaxation times**, not a single one.

**Step 3 — Generalized Maxwell Model (Wiechert):**

A Generalized Maxwell Model consists of **N Maxwell elements in parallel**, each with its own modulus E_i and relaxation time τ_i. The total stress is:

$$\sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sum_{i=1}^{N} E_i \, e^{-t/\tau_i}$$

A Prony series fit of experimental data provides E_i and τ_i. This captures the broad distribution of molecular motion timescales — from local side-group rotations (τ ~ 10⁻⁶ s) to whole-chain reptation (τ ~ 10⁶ s).

**(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Step 1 — Creep compliance J(t) at each time:**

$$J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0 = \varepsilon(t)/10$$

| t (h) | ε | J(t) (GPa⁻¹) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.0042 | 4.20 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 100 | 0.0061 | 6.10 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 1000 | 0.0085 | 8.50 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 10000 | 0.0118 | 1.18 × 10⁻³ |

**Step 2 — Construct isochronous curve at 1000 h:**

For linear viscoelasticity, at fixed time t = 1000 h, the isochronous stress is proportional to strain:
$$\sigma = \varepsilon / J(1000) = \varepsilon / (8.50 \times 10^{-4})$$

Required reference points:
- At ε = 0.005 → σ = 5.88 MPa
- At ε = 0.0085 → σ = 10.00 MPa
- At ε = 0.012 → σ = 14.12 MPa

**Step 3 — Allowable stress for 10000 h at ε_max = 0.5% (0.005):**

Using J(10000 h) = 1.18 × 10⁻³ GPa⁻¹:

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\varepsilon_{max}}{J(10000)} = \frac{0.005}{1.18 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.24 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of linear viscoelastic limit (at 20 MPa):**

A stress of 20 MPa would produce strain at 1000 h:
$$\varepsilon = 20 \times 8.50 \times 10^{-4} = 0.017 = 1.7\%$$

This is below the typical linearity limit (~2%), so the assumption is acceptable.

> **Design Recommendation (1 mark):** The allowable stress for ABS at 10000 h service life and 0.5% strain limit is **4.24 MPa**, which is only ~42% of the short-term 10 MPa creep stress level. This reduction factor must be incorporated into the final product design safety factor. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in LTPA Questions (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Temperature Unit Error (-1 mark):** Using °C instead of K in the WLF or Arrhenius equations. Always convert: **T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15**.
> 2. **Forgetting to Specify Reference Temperature (-0.5 mark):** WLF shift factors are meaningless without explicitly stating T_ref.
> 3. **Confusing Creep Modulus with Elastic Modulus (-2 marks):** Creep modulus is **time-dependent**; elastic modulus is an instantaneous material constant.
> 4. **Mixing up Maxwell vs Kelvin-Voigt Response (-1 mark):** Maxwell gives exponential **stress decay** under constant strain; Kelvin-Voigt gives exponential **strain retardation** under constant stress.
> 5. **Not Drawing Creep Curve Stages in Descriptive Answers (-1 mark):** When asked to "explain creep," always include a labeled schematic showing Primary, Secondary, Tertiary, and Rupture stages.
> 6. **Larson-Miller Constant Value (-0.5 mark):** The standard constant C = 20 for polymers; using C = 8 (metals) will give wrong predictions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Long Term Property Analysis:**

- [ ] **Four Domains of LTPA**: Creep, Stress Relaxation, Fatigue, Aging.
- [ ] **Creep Stages**: Primary (decaying rate) → Secondary (constant rate) → Tertiary (accelerating) → Rupture.
- [ ] **Creep Modulus Definition**: $E_c(t) = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t)$, units MPa.
- [ ] **Creep Compliance Definition**: $J(t) = \varepsilon(t) / \sigma_0$, units GPa⁻¹; **Reciprocal only in linear regime**.
- [ ] **Burgers 4-Param Equation**: Must be memorized with all three strain components.
- [ ] **Maxwell Stress Decay**: $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau_M}$; describes stress relaxation.
- [ ] **Kelvin-Voigt Strain**: $\varepsilon(t) = (\sigma_0/E_K)(1 - e^{-t/\tau_K})$; describes retarded creep.
- [ ] **WLF Equation**: $\log_{10}(a_T) = -C_1(T-T_{ref})/[C_2 + (T-T_{ref})]$; C₁ = 17.44, C₂ = 51.6 K; valid **T_g to T_g + 100°C**.
- [ ] **Arrhenius**: Used **below T_g**; $\log_{10}(a_T) = (Q_c/2.303R)[1/T - 1/T_{ref}]$; **T in Kelvin**.
- [ ] **Isochronous Curves**: Constant time, varying stress-strain; for design selection.
- [ ] **Isometric Curves**: Constant strain, stress vs time; for preload loss (bolts, clamps).
- [ ] **Larson-Miller Parameter**: $LMP = (T/1000)[C + \log_{10}(t_r)]$; C = 20 for polymers; for creep rupture extrapolation.
- [ ] **Master Curve Construction**: Horizontally shift shorter-term/higher-temperature data using a_T to build long-term design curves.
- [ ] **No Endurance Limit**: Polymer S-N curves never become truly horizontal — fatigue must be designed against at all cycles.
- [ ] **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)**: A combined chemical + mechanical failure mode unique to polymers (e.g., HDPE in detergents); tested by ASTM D5397.
- [ ] **ASTM Standards to Remember**: D2990 (creep), D5279 (DMA torsion), D4329 (UV exposure), D5397 (ESC).

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Long Term Property Analysis**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Creep** is the **time-dependent, permanent (viscoelastic) deformation** exhibited by a polymeric material when it is subjected to a **constant load or constant stress** over a prolonged period, at a temperature typically below its glass transition temperature (T<sub>g</sub>) or melting point (T<sub>m</sub>).

In strict KTU board-examination terminology, creep is the progressive accumulation of **strain (ε)** in a polymer under a **sustained (static) stress (σ)** as a function of time. Unlike metals (where creep is significant only at high homologous temperatures, T/T<sub>m</sub> > 0.4), polymers exhibit pronounced creep at **room temperature**, because their amorphous regions remain far above their T<sub>g</sub> in practical service conditions.

The governing experimental relationship is:

$$
\varepsilon(t) \;=\; f\!\left(\sigma,\;T,\;t,\;\text{structure}\right)
$$

where **t** is the elapsed time under load and **T** is the absolute service temperature in **Kelvin (K)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey-on-a-Spoon" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a spoonful of thick honey held horizontally.**
> The moment you tilt the spoon, the honey begins to **flow slowly downward**, even though gravity is constant. After 1 second, it has moved a little; after 1 minute, much more; after 1 hour, it has nearly dripped off. The honey never broke or snapped — it simply *deformed over time*.

**This is exactly how a polymer creeps under load:**

- The **constant spoon-tilt** = the constant applied stress σ
- The **slow, progressive sliding downward** of the honey = the time-dependent strain ε(t)
- The **temperature of the room** = the service temperature T (higher T → faster creep, just like warm honey flows faster)

Unlike an elastic steel spring (which stretches instantly and stops), the polymer keeps deforming — **slowly, silently, and indefinitely**, until either the load is removed (recovery) or the part fails.

---

## 1.3 Why Creep Matters in Polymer Product Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Creep is the **single most important design-limiting factor** for polymers used in **load-bearing, long-service-life** applications. Examples in KTU-relevant industrial practice include:
> - **MDPE/HDPE pipes** carrying pressurized water/gas for 50-year service lives.
> - **Nylon gears and bearings** in automotive under-the-hood applications.
> - **PVC window profiles** holding glass under wind-load over decades.
> - **PTFE seals** in chemical plants under sustained bolt preload.

The standard design safety metric is the **Creep Modulus (E<sub>c</sub>)**, expressed in **MPa** or **GPa**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymers) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Applied stress | σ | 1 – 50 | MPa |
| Creep strain | ε(t) | 0.001 – 0.10 | dimensionless |
| Creep modulus | E<sub>c</sub>(t) | 0.1 – 5.0 | GPa |
| Service temperature | T | 293 – 393 | K |
| Glass transition temperature | T<sub>g</sub> | 200 – 420 | K |
| Creep rupture time | t<sub>r</sub> | 1 – 10⁶ | hours |
