# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Classical Stages of a Creep Curve

When a constant stress σ₀ is applied at time t = 0 and held indefinitely, the resulting strain ε(t) traces a characteristic **creep curve**. For KTU examination purposes, this curve is universally divided into **four distinct stages** (see Section 4 for the diagram):

### Stage I — Instantaneous Elastic Deformation (t = 0⁺)
- Occurs the **moment the load is applied**.
- Fully **recoverable** upon unloading (purely elastic response).
- Magnitude: ε₀ = σ₀ / E, where **E** is the Young's modulus measured at the loading rate.

### Stage II — Primary (Transient) Creep
- Strain rate **decreases** with time (strain hardening).
- Dominated by **chain uncoiling, bond rotation, and short-range segmental motion**.
- Mostly recoverable (anelastic) over time.

### Stage III — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep
- Strain rate is **constant** and reaches a minimum (or plateau).
- This is the **design-governing region** because it dominates long-term service life.
- Equilibrium between **strain-hardening** and **thermal-softening (recovery)** processes.

### Stage IV — Tertiary Creep (Pre-Rupture)
- Strain rate **accelerates** catastrophically.
- **Microvoid formation, craze development, and necking** dominate.
- Terminates in **creep rupture** at time t<sub>r</sub>.

---

## 2.2 The Isochronous and Isometric Curves — The Designer's Two Tools

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board questions frequently test the student's ability to **plot AND interpret** these two auxiliary curves, which are derived from a *family* of creep curves measured at different stress levels.

### (a) Isochronous Stress–Strain Curve (constant *time*)
- Plot **stress (σ)** on Y-axis vs **strain (ε)** on X-axis, each point read off the family of creep curves at the **same elapsed time** (e.g., 1000 hours).
- **At very short times**, the polymer behaves **stiff and glass-like** (steep slope).
- **At very long times**, the polymer behaves **soft and rubber-like** (shallow slope).
- **Design use:** Predict the strain after a fixed service time under a known stress.

### (b) Isometric Stress–Time Curve (constant *strain*)
- Plot **stress (σ)** on Y-axis vs **time (t)** on X-axis, each point read off the family of creep curves at the **same strain level**.
- The required sustaining stress **decays** with time — this is the **stress-relaxation analogue** of creep, plotted inversely.
- **Design use:** Determine the bolt-preload loss in a flanged plastic pipe joint over decades.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize the entries below — they form 80% of the numerical problems asked in KTU Module-1 questions.**

| # | Formula / Law | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{E} \;+\; \varepsilon_{cr}(t)$ | Total strain = elastic + creep strain | dimensionless |
| 2 | $E_{c}(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\varepsilon(t)}$ | **Creep modulus** (time-dependent effective modulus) | MPa |
| 3 | $\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \varepsilon_{0} \;+\; m\,t^{\,n}$ | **Findley's Power Law** (empirical) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{1}} \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{2}}\!\left[1-e^{-t/\tau}\right] \;+\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}}\,t$ | **Burgers 4-element model** (Maxwell ∥ Kelvin-Voigt) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\tau \;=\; \dfrac{\eta_{2}}{E_{2}}$ | **Retardation time** of Kelvin-Voigt unit | seconds |
| 6 | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}}$ | Steady-state creep strain rate | s⁻¹ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{\dot{\varepsilon}\,T_{0}^{2}}{T} \;=\; f(\sigma)$ | **Larson–Miller parameter** for rupture life | — |
| 8 | $\log t_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{A}{T} \;-\; B\,\log \sigma$ | **Stress-temperature superposition** | — |

> **Critical Notation Warning:** In every equation above, **σ₀** = constant applied stress, **ε₀** = instantaneous elastic strain, **m** = creep compliance coefficient, **n** = creep time exponent (typically 0.1 ≤ n ≤ 0.5 for polymers), **E₁, E₂** = spring constants, **η₂, η₃** = dashpot viscosities, **τ** = retardation time.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Creep in Polymers

| Factor | Effect on Creep Rate | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Temperature | **↑↑↑ Strongly increases** | Approaches T<sub>g</sub>; segmental mobility ↑ |
| ↑ Applied stress | ↑ Increases (often non-linearly) | More chain uncoiling per unit time |
| ↑ Molecular weight | ↓ Decreases | Longer entanglements resist slip |
| ↑ Crystallinity | ↓↓ Strongly decreases | Crystallites act as physical crosslinks |
| ↑ Crosslink density | ↓ Decreases | Restricts chain sliding |
| ↑ Filler content (e.g., glass fibre) | ↓↓ Strongly decreases | Load transferred to rigid filler |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | ↑↑ Strongly increases | Free volume ↑; T<sub>g</sub> ↓ |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Burgers 4-Element Model for Creep

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Burgers model** is the **most examination-relevant** mechanical analogue for polymer creep in the KTU 2024 scheme. It is constructed by **placing a Maxwell element in series with a Kelvin–Voigt element**.

### Step 1 — Model Construction
The Burgers model consists of **four elements**:

$$
\text{Spring } E_{1} \;\;—\;\; \text{Dashpot } \eta_{3} \;\;||\;\; \left(\text{Spring } E_{2} \;\;—\;\; \text{Dashpot } \eta_{2}\right)
$$

where the symbol **||** denotes the series connection of the Maxwell arm to the Kelvin–Voigt arm.

### Step 2 — Apply Series Strain Additivity
Because the Maxwell and Kelvin–Voigt units are in series, the **total strain is the sum of the strains of the two sub-units**:

$$
\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \varepsilon_{M}(t) \;+\; \varepsilon_{KV}(t)
$$

### Step 3 — Strain in the Maxwell Sub-unit
A Maxwell unit consists of a spring E₁ and dashpot η₃ in series. Under a constant stress σ₀ applied at t = 0:

- The spring gives an instantaneous strain: $\varepsilon_{spring} = \sigma_{0}/E_{1}$
- The dashpot flows at a constant rate: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{dashpot} = \sigma_{0}/\eta_{3}$

Therefore, the Maxwell strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_{M}(t) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{1}} \;+\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}}\,t
$$

### Step 4 — Strain in the Kelvin–Voigt Sub-unit
A Kelvin–Voigt unit has a spring E₂ and dashpot η₂ in **parallel**, so both experience the same strain ε<sub>KV</sub> and share the stress σ₀:

$$
\sigma_{0} \;=\; E_{2}\,\varepsilon_{KV}(t) \;+\; \eta_{2}\,\dot{\varepsilon}_{KV}(t)
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** in ε<sub>KV</sub>(t). Rearranging:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{KV} \;+\; \frac{E_{2}}{\eta_{2}}\,\varepsilon_{KV} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{2}}
$$

### Step 5 — Solve the ODE by Integrating Factor
The integrating factor is $\mu(t) = e^{E_{2}t/\eta_{2}}$. Multiply both sides:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left[\varepsilon_{KV}\,e^{E_{2}t/\eta_{2}}\right] \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{2}}\,e^{E_{2}t/\eta_{2}}
$$

Integrate both sides from 0 to t, applying the initial condition ε<sub>KV</sub>(0) = 0 (the Kelvin–Voigt spring cannot deform instantaneously because the parallel dashpot locks it):

$$
\varepsilon_{KV}(t)\,e^{E_{2}t/\eta_{2}} \;-\; 0 \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{2}}\!\left[\frac{\eta_{2}}{E_{2}}\!\left(e^{E_{2}t/\eta_{2}} - 1\right)\right]
$$

Solving:

$$
\varepsilon_{KV}(t) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{2}}\!\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right], \qquad \tau \;=\; \frac{\eta_{2}}{E_{2}}
$$

### Step 6 — Combine the Two Sub-units (Final Burgers Equation)

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{1}} \;+\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{2}}\!\left[1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right] \;+\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}}\,t\;}
$$

### Step 7 — Physical Interpretation of Each Term

| Term | Physical Meaning | Recoverable? |
|---|---|---|
| $\sigma_{0}/E_{1}$ | Instantaneous elastic deformation | ✅ Fully recoverable |
| $\dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{2}}\!\left[1-e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | Delayed viscoelastic (retarded) deformation | ✅ Time-delayed recoverable |
| $\dfrac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}}\,t$ | Viscous flow (irreversible) deformation | ❌ Permanent |

This single equation successfully reproduces **Stages I, II, and III** of the experimental creep curve, which is why it is the most celebrated model in viscoelasticity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Creep Modulus from Findley's Power Law

**Findley's empirical power law** is widely used in industrial design because it requires only **three material parameters** (ε₀, m, n):

$$
\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \varepsilon_{0} \;+\; m\,t^{\,n}
$$

### Step 1 — Identify ε₀ at t = 0⁺
At t = 0: ε(0) = ε₀ + m·(0)ⁿ = ε₀ → so **ε₀ is the instantaneous elastic strain**.

### Step 2 — Express the Creep Modulus
By definition: $E_{c}(t) = \sigma_{0}/\varepsilon(t)$.

Substitute Findley's law:

$$
E_{c}(t) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\varepsilon_{0} \;+\; m\,t^{\,n}}
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate at a Design Service Life t<sub>s</sub>
For a 50-year design life of an HDPE pipe: t<sub>s</sub> = 50 × 365 × 24 = 438,000 hours. Plug into the above to obtain E<sub>c</sub>(438,000 h) — this is the **long-term creep modulus** used to size the pipe wall thickness via the **ISO 9080** or **MRS (Minimum Required Strength)** classification.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Examination Style)

> **Problem:** A HDPE polymer exhibits the following Burgers model parameters at 23 °C: E₁ = 1.2 GPa, E₂ = 0.6 GPa, η₂ = 600 GPa·s, η₃ = 4500 GPa·s. A constant stress of σ₀ = 5 MPa is applied. Compute: (a) the instantaneous strain, (b) the strain at t = 100 s, (c) the steady-state strain rate, (d) the retardation time τ.

### Solution

**(a) Instantaneous strain** (t = 0⁺): Only the E₁ spring responds.

$$
\varepsilon(0) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{E_{1}} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa}}{1.2 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}} \;=\; 4.167 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**(b) Retardation time**:

$$
\tau \;=\; \frac{\eta_{2}}{E_{2}} \;=\; \frac{600 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa·s}}{0.6 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}} \;=\; 1000\,\text{s}
$$

**(c) Strain at t = 100 s**: Since t ≪ τ, the Kelvin–Voigt term is nearly inactive. Using $1 - e^{-100/1000} = 1 - e^{-0.1} \approx 0.0952$:

$$
\varepsilon(100) \;=\; \frac{5}{1200} \;+\; \frac{5}{600}\!\left[0.0952\right] \;+\; \frac{5}{4500}(100)
$$

Evaluate each term:

$$
\varepsilon(100) \;=\; 0.004167 \;+\; 0.000793 \;+\; 0.11111 \;=\; 0.11607
$$

**(d) Steady-state strain rate**:

$$
\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\eta_{3}} \;=\; \frac{5 \times 10^{6}}{4500 \times 10^{9}} \;=\; 1.111 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}^{-1}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Model?

| Model | Equation | Best Used When | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maxwell | $\varepsilon = \sigma/E + (\sigma/\eta)t$ | Stress relaxation problems | Predicts infinite creep; misses primary creep shape |
| Kelvin–Voigt | $\varepsilon = (\sigma/E)(1-e^{-t/\tau})$ | Primary creep only | No instantaneous strain; no permanent flow |
| **Burgers** | Full 4-element equation | **Primary + Secondary creep** (most polymers) | Does NOT predict tertiary creep / rupture |
| Findley Power Law | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 + mt^n$ | Empirical long-term design curves | No mechanistic insight; needs many tests |
| Norton-Bailey | $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = A\sigma^n \exp(-Q/RT)$ | High-temperature polymer creep | Empirical; requires activation energy Q |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define creep in polymers. List the four classical stages of a creep curve.**

**Model Answer:**
Creep is the **time-dependent viscoelastic deformation** of a polymer under a **constant applied stress** at constant temperature.

The four stages are:
1. **Stage I — Instantaneous elastic deformation** (occurs at t = 0⁺).
2. **Stage II — Primary (transient) creep** (decelerating strain rate).
3. **Stage III — Secondary (steady-state) creep** (constant strain rate).
4. **Stage IV — Tertiary creep** (accelerating strain rate leading to rupture).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between isochronous and isometric stress–strain curves.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Isochronous Curve | Isometric Curve |
|---|---|---|
| Variable held constant | **Time (t = constant)** | **Strain (ε = constant)** |
| Axes | σ vs ε | σ vs t |
| Family of curves needed | Creep curves at various σ | Creep curves at various σ |
| Designer uses it to find | Strain after a fixed service time | Stress decay needed to hold a fixed deformation |
| Typical KTU application | Predict pipe wall strain after 50 yrs | Predict bolt preload loss in a flanged joint |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per the **KTU 2024 ESE regulation**, Module-1 questions carry an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A — Full 14-Mark Question `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the **four stages of a creep curve** for a polymer under constant stress. Explain the molecular mechanisms responsible for each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **creep strain equation for the Burgers 4-element mechanical model**, identifying the physical meaning of each term and the corresponding material constants. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four stages are to be plotted with strain ε on the Y-axis and time t on the X-axis (log scale preferred for clarity).

| Stage | Time Range | Strain Rate | Molecular Mechanism | Recoverability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I — Instantaneous | t = 0⁺ | Infinite (slope) | Bond stretching | Fully recoverable |
| II — Primary | 0 < t < t₁ | Decreasing | Chain uncoiling + segmental rotation | Partly recoverable |
| III — Secondary | t₁ < t < t₂ | Minimum & constant | Viscous chain slip past entanglements | Irreversible |
| IV — Tertiary | t > t₂ | Accelerating | Microvoid growth, craze coalescence, necking | Irreversible → rupture at t<sub>r</sub> |

The sketch must clearly mark **ε₀** (instantaneous strain), the **inflection** between stages II and III, and the rupture point at **t<sub>r</sub>**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

The **complete derivation** is shown in **Section 3.1, Steps 1–6** above. The student must reproduce:

1. Series strain additivity: ε = ε<sub>M</sub> + ε<sub>KV</sub>
2. Maxwell strain: $\sigma_0/E_1 + (\sigma_0/\eta_3)t$
3. Kelvin–Voigt ODE: $\dot{\varepsilon}_{KV} + (E_2/\eta_2)\varepsilon_{KV} = \sigma_0/\eta_2$
4. Solution with initial condition ε<sub>KV</sub>(0) = 0
5. Final boxed Burgers equation with all three terms and the retardation time τ = η₂/E₂.

---

### Question B — Full 14-Mark Alternative Question `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **Findley Power Law** for polymer creep. State the equation, identify all parameters, and discuss its **industrial utility** in long-term product design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A polypropylene copolymer is subjected to a constant tensile stress of **σ₀ = 8 MPa**. Findley parameters at 23 °C are: **ε₀ = 0.004**, **m = 0.0015 h⁻ⁿ**, **n = 0.32**. Compute: (i) the strain after 1000 hours, (ii) the creep modulus at 1000 hours, (iii) the strain after 1 year (8760 hours), and (iv) the percentage increase in strain between 1000 h and 1 year. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Findley Power Law

**Equation:**
$$
\varepsilon(t) \;=\; \varepsilon_{0} \;+\; m\,t^{\,n}
$$

**Parameters:**

- ε₀ = instantaneous elastic strain (dimensionless)
- m = creep compliance coefficient (units of time⁻ⁿ)
- n = creep time exponent, typically **0.1 ≤ n ≤ 0.5** for polymers
- t = elapsed time under load

**Industrial Utility:**
- Used by PE pipe manufacturers to classify materials under **ISO 9080 / MRS system**.
- Requires only **three material constants** — obtained from short-term tests extrapolated via **Time-Temperature Superposition**.
- Forms the basis of **50-year design stress** predictions for HDPE pressure pipes.
- Preferred over Burgers in design offices because it is **empirical and conservative**.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**(i) Strain at t = 1000 h:**

$$
\varepsilon(1000) \;=\; 0.004 \;+\; 0.0015 \times (1000)^{0.32}
$$

Evaluate the power:

$$
1000^{0.32} \;=\; 10^{3 \times 0.32} \;=\; 10^{0.96} \;\approx\; 9.120
$$

Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon(1000) \;=\; 0.004 \;+\; 0.0015 \times 9.120 \;=\; 0.004 \;+\; 0.01368 \;=\; 0.01768
$$

**(ii) Creep modulus at 1000 h:**

$$
E_{c}(1000) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{0}}{\varepsilon(1000)} \;=\; \frac{8\,\text{MPa}}{0.01768} \;\approx\; 452.5\,\text{MPa}
$$

**(iii) Strain at t = 8760 h (1 year):**

$$
8760^{0.32} \;=\; e^{0.32 \ln(8760)} \;=\; e^{0.32 \times 9.078} \;=\; e^{2.905} \;\approx\; 18.27
$$

$$
\varepsilon(8760) \;=\; 0.004 \;+\; 0.0015 \times 18.27 \;=\; 0.004 \;+\; 0.02741 \;=\; 0.03141
$$

**(iv) Percentage increase:**

$$
\%\,\text{increase} \;=\; \frac{0.03141 - 0.01768}{0.01768} \times 100\% \;\approx\; 77.66\%
$$

The strain **increases by ~78 %** between 1000 h and 8760 h — a striking demonstration of why short-term laboratory data must never be used directly for design.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Creep Questions (verified by KTU board examiner patterns):**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition** ε<sub>KV</sub>(0) = 0 when solving the Kelvin–Voigt ODE. This loses **2 full marks** because the integrating factor integration then yields an incorrect constant of integration.
> 2. **Confusing creep modulus with Young's modulus.** Creep modulus is *time-dependent* and always smaller than E. Stating E<sub>c</sub> = E is a **1-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Mis-identifying Stage III vs Stage IV.** Some students confuse "secondary creep" (constant rate) with "tertiary creep" (accelerating rate). Examiners explicitly look for the keyword **"constant strain rate"** in Stage III.
> 4. **Missing units in Findley's power law.** The constant **m** has units of **h⁻ⁿ** or **s⁻ⁿ**, not dimensionless. Forgetting units loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Plotting creep curves on linear time axis** instead of **logarithmic time axis.** Examiners expect log-time plots because the primary creep region is otherwise compressed and invisible.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Creep" (Module 1)**

- ✅ Creep = **time-dependent strain under constant stress** at constant temperature.
- ✅ Four stages: **Instantaneous → Primary → Secondary → Tertiary → Rupture**.
- ✅ Stage II: decelerating rate (chain uncoiling); Stage III: constant rate (viscous flow); Stage IV: accelerating rate (crazing).
- ✅ **Creep modulus** $E_c(t) = \sigma_0 / \varepsilon(t)$ — decreases with time.
- ✅ **Isochronous** = constant time; **Isometric** = constant strain.
- ✅ **Findley's Power Law:** $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m\,t^n$ — empirical, design-friendly.
- ✅ **Burgers model:** $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0/E_1 + (\sigma_0/E_2)[1-e^{-t/\tau}] + (\sigma_0/\eta_3)\,t$ — three physical terms: elastic + retarded + viscous flow.
- ✅ **Retardation time:** $\tau = \eta_2/E_2$.
- ✅ **Steady-state creep rate:** $\dot{\varepsilon}_{ss} = \sigma_0 / \eta_3$.
- ✅ **Creep ↑ with** T, σ, plasticizer content, amorphous content, free volume.
- ✅ **Creep ↓ with** crystallinity, crosslink density, molecular weight, rigid filler content.
- ✅ **Time-Temperature Superposition (TTS)** enables long-term prediction from short-term high-T data using WLF shift factors.
- ✅ Industrial standards: **ISO 9080, MRS classification, Larson–Miller parameter** for creep rupture life.
- ✅ KTU-favourite derivation: **Burgers 4-element model** — memorize all six derivation steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress Relaxation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress Relaxation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Stress Relaxation in Polymeric Materials

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stress Relaxation** is defined as the time-dependent decrease in **stress** (internal restoring force) observed in a viscoelastic polymer specimen when it is **subjected to a constant, sustained strain (deformation)** held at a fixed temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim pattern):**
> *Stress relaxation is the gradual decay of stress with time in a polymer held at a constant strain, arising from the time-dependent molecular rearrangements of polymer chains, and is characterized by the relaxation modulus* $E(t) = \sigma(t) / \varepsilon_0$.

Mathematically, the **relaxation modulus** is expressed as:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$$

where:
- $E(t)$ is the **time-dependent relaxation modulus** (in **Pa**)
- $\sigma(t)$ is the **decaying stress** (in **Pa**)
- $\varepsilon_0$ is the **imposed constant strain** (dimensionless)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Band & Weight" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy (First-Read Friendly):**
> Imagine stretching a thick rubber band between two hooks and **locking the hooks in place** so the rubber band cannot return to its original length. When you first stretch it, you feel a strong pull-back force (high stress). But if you leave it stretched for hours, days, or weeks, you will notice that the pulling force becomes weaker and weaker, even though the band is still just as stretched. The *molecules inside* are slowly sliding past one another and rearranging, so the internal "push-back" relaxes with time. This is **stress relaxation** — the stress fades, but the strain does not.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard polymer-testing metrics govern stress relaxation analysis:

- **Relaxation Modulus** $E(t)$ — measured in **Pa** (or **MPa** for engineering polymers)
- **Relaxation Time** $\tau$ — measured in **seconds (s)**
- **Glass Transition Temperature** $T_g$ — measured in **°C** or **K**
- **Standard Test Temperature** — typically **23 °C (296 K)** as per **ASTM D5023 / ISO 6721**
- **Applied Strain Levels** — typically held between **0.1% and 1%** (linear viscoelastic region)

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners often test whether the student understands that stress relaxation occurs at **constant strain**, which is the *inverse* experimental setup of **creep** (which occurs at *constant stress*). Always remember: **Relaxation ↔ constant strain**, **Creep ↔ constant stress**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Molecular Mechanism

Stress relaxation originates from the **thermally activated segmental motion** of polymer chains. The stepwise logic is:

- **Step 1 — Instantaneous Elastic Response:** When a strain is suddenly imposed, the polymer responds instantly through **bond stretching** and **small-angle chain rotation** (a purely elastic response). This gives the **initial stress** $\sigma_0 = E \cdot \varepsilon_0$.
- **Step 2 — Time-Dependent Molecular Rearrangement:** With the strain held fixed, polymer chain segments begin to **uncoil, slip past entanglements, and reconfigure** to relieve the internal stress. The rate depends on **mobility**, which is governed by temperature relative to $T_g$.
- **Step 3 — Stress Decay:** As molecular segments continue to relax, the **internal restoring force drops monotonically**. After a sufficiently long time (theoretically infinite), stress approaches a limiting value — often **zero for a viscoelastic liquid** or a **finite equilibrium value** for a **crosslinked network / elastomer**.
- **Step 4 — Equilibrium:** At very long times, the system reaches a thermodynamic equilibrium where no further stress relaxation can occur.

## 2.2 Classification of Stress Relaxation Behaviour

Based on the polymer's physical state, stress relaxation is classified into:

| State of Polymer | Temperature Window | Stress Relaxation Behaviour | Equilibrium Stress |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Glass** | $T < T_g$ | Very slow, long relaxation time ($\tau \gg 10^6$ s) | High residual stress |
| **Rubbery / Leathery** | $T_g < T < T_m$ | Moderate, $\tau \approx 10^2$ to $10^4$ s | Approaches zero (uncrosslinked) |
| **Crosslinked Elastomer** | $T > T_g$, crosslinked | Decays to **non-zero equilibrium modulus** $E_e$ | $E_e > 0$ |
| **Viscous Flow** | $T > T_m$ (uncrosslinked) | Stress decays completely to zero | $\sigma(\infty) = 0$ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Relaxation Modulus (definition) | $E(t) = \sigma(t) \,/\, \varepsilon_0$ | $\sigma$ in Pa; $\varepsilon_0$ dimensionless; $E(t)$ in Pa | Master relationship |
| 2 | Maxwell Model Stress Decay | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp(-t/\tau)$ | $\sigma_0$ initial stress; $\tau$ relaxation time | Linear viscoelastic prediction |
| 3 | Maxwell Relaxation Time | $\tau = \eta / E$ | $\eta$ viscosity (Pa·s); $E$ modulus (Pa) | Characteristic decay time |
| 4 | Reduced/Shifted Variable (time) | $\log a_T$ — WLF shift factor | Dimensionless | Time-temperature superposition |
| 5 | Stress at any time $t$ | $\sigma(t) = E(t) \cdot \varepsilon_0$ | Pa | Direct stress calculation |
| 6 | Stress at $t = \tau$ | $\sigma(\tau) = \sigma_0 / e \approx 0.368 \, \sigma_0$ | Pa | Benchmark: 36.8% of initial stress |
| 7 | Multiple Maxwell (Prony Series) | $E(t) = E_\infty + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot \exp(-t/\tau_i)$ | $E_\infty$ equilibrium modulus | Real polymer behaviour |
| 8 | Temperature dependence of $\tau$ | $\tau(T) = \tau_0 \cdot \exp(\Delta H / RT)$ | $\Delta H$ activation energy | Arrhenius-type behaviour |
| 9 | Time-Temperature Superposition | $E(T,t) = E(T_{\text{ref}}, t \cdot a_T)$ | $a_T$ shift factor | Master curve generation |
| 10 | Logarithmic Decay Form | $\log \sigma(t) = \log \sigma_0 - (t \,/\, 2.303 \, \tau)$ | Dimensionless | Plotting & analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Note:** All absolute-value notations in this table use the LaTeX `\vert` command instead of the keyboard pipe `\|`, to prevent breaking the markdown table parser. KTU boards require clean, professional equations.

## 2.4 Why This Concept Matters in Real Engineering

Stress relaxation is not merely academic — it is a **design-critical phenomenon** in:

- **Bolted Polymer Flange Joints:** The torque pre-load decays with time; engineers must re-tighten or use locking washers.
- **Polymer Pipe Compression Fittings:** Stress decay can lead to **leakage** in plumbing systems using plastic pipes.
- **Gaskets and O-Rings:** Long-term sealing force decreases; design must account for the **residual stress** after 10–20 years.
- **Polymer Tires and Belts:** The tension in conveyor belts drops during operation, requiring **pre-tensioning margins**.
- **Post-Tensioned Polymer Tendons in Concrete:** Stress relaxation in FRP (fibre-reinforced polymer) tendons is a major design factor.
- **Biomedical Implants (UHMWPE hip liners):** Stress relaxation in vivo affects long-term joint performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maxwell Model Stress Relaxation Equation

We derive the **fundamental exponential decay law** for stress relaxation from first principles using the **Maxwell model** (a spring of modulus $E$ in series with a dashpot of viscosity $\eta$).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Constitutive equations of Maxwell elements.**
The **spring** obeys Hooke's law:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{spring}}$$

The **dashpot** obeys Newton's law of viscosity:
$$\sigma = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Strain additivity (series combination).**
For elements in series, the total strain is the sum:
$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_{\text{spring}} + \varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to time.**
Taking the time derivative of the strain-additivity equation:
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}}{dt} + \frac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the constitutive derivatives.**
From the spring equation: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{\text{spring}}}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E} \dfrac{d\sigma}{dt}$

From the dashpot equation: $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{\text{dashpot}}}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$

Therefore:
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

This is the **Maxwell constitutive differential equation**.

**Step 5 — Apply the stress relaxation boundary condition.**
In a stress relaxation experiment, the strain is held **constant**, so:
$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$$

Substituting this condition:
$$0 = \frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into a separable first-order ODE.**
$$\frac{1}{E} \frac{d\sigma}{dt} = -\frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, dt$$

**Step 7 — Define the relaxation time.**
Let $\tau = \dfrac{\eta}{E}$ (units: $\dfrac{\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{Pa}} = \text{s}$ — correct).

Then:
$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{dt}{\tau}$$

**Step 8 — Integrate both sides.**
Integrating from $0$ to $t$, with initial condition $\sigma(0) = \sigma_0$:

$$\int_{\sigma_0}^{\sigma(t)} \frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{1}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} \right) = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 9 — Exponentiate to obtain the final solution.**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\;}$$

**Step 10 — Substitute back the initial stress $\sigma_0 = E \varepsilon_0$.**

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = E \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)\;}$$

This is the **canonical KTU stress relaxation equation**. The relaxation modulus follows directly:

$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} = E \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Generalised Maxwell (Prony Series) Model

A single Maxwell element cannot capture the broad relaxation spectrum of real polymers. The generalisation uses **$n$ Maxwell elements in parallel**, each with modulus $E_i$ and relaxation time $\tau_i$.

**Step 1 — Stress additivity for parallel elements:**
$$\sigma(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \, \varepsilon_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_i}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Add an equilibrium spring for crosslinked polymers:**
$$\sigma(t) = E_\infty \, \varepsilon_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \, \varepsilon_0 \, \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_i}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Divide by $\varepsilon_0$ to obtain the relaxation modulus:**

$$\boxed{\;E(t) = E_\infty + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{t}{\tau_i}\right)\;}$$

This is the **Prony series representation** of the relaxation modulus — the gold standard for fitting experimental data.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Time Required for Stress to Fall to a Given Fraction

A common exam-style question: *"At what time will the stress fall to 10% of its initial value?"*

**Step 1 — Set up the equation:**
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp(-t/\tau) = 0.10 \cdot \sigma_0$$

**Step 2 — Cancel $\sigma_0$:**
$$\exp(-t/\tau) = 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm:**
$$-\frac{t}{\tau} = \ln(0.10) = -2.3026$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t$:**
$$t = 2.3026 \, \tau \approx 2.303 \, \tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalised result:** For stress to fall to any fraction $f$ of the initial value:
> $$t = -\tau \cdot \ln(f) = \tau \cdot \ln(1/f)$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Models

| Model Type | Stress Decay Function | Equilibrium Stress | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Single Maxwell** | $\sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$ | $\sigma(\infty) = 0$ | Qualitative understanding, simple problems |
| **Two-Element Maxwell** | $E_1 \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau_1} + E_2 \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau_2}$ | $\sigma(\infty) = 0$ | Two distinct molecular processes |
| **Prony Series (n elements)** | $E_\infty \varepsilon_0 + \sum E_i \varepsilon_0 e^{-t/\tau_i}$ | $E_\infty \varepsilon_0$ | Real polymer fitting, FEM analysis |
| **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** | $\sigma = E_R \varepsilon_0 \left[1 - \frac{E_R - E_U}{E_R} e^{-t/\tau}\right]$ | $E_R \varepsilon_0$ | Crosslinked elastomers |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse the **Standard Linear Solid (SLS)** model with the **Maxwell model**. The SLS has a *spring in parallel* with a Maxwell pair, leading to a **non-zero equilibrium stress**. The pure Maxwell model predicts **complete stress decay to zero**, which is *only valid for uncrosslinked linear polymers above $T_g$*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Maxwell Model — Spring-Dashpot Representation

This schematic shows the **mechanical analogue** of the Maxwell model, the foundational representation of linear viscoelastic stress relaxation behaviour.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fixed End]:::anchor -->|Connection Point| B((Node 1)):::node
    B -->|Spring E| C((Node 2)):::node
    C -->|Dashpot eta| D((Node 3)):::node
    D --> E[Movable End at strain epsilon0]:::anchor
    B -.applies.-> F[Stress sigma t]:::stress
    C -.transmits.-> F
    F -.measured.-> G[Relaxation Modulus E of t]:::output

    classDef anchor fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#1a252f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef node fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f6dad,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stress fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#a93226,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#27ae60,stroke:#1e8449,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Stress Relaxation Experimental Procedure Flow

The block-level architecture below maps the **complete workflow** of a stress relaxation test conducted on a Dynamic Mechanical Analyzer (DMA).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Prepare Specimen]:::step --> S2[Step 2: Mount in DMA Clamps]:::step
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Equilibrate at Test Temperature]:::step
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Constant Strain epsilon0]:::step
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Record Initial Stress sigma0 Instantly]:::step
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Monitor Stress Decay over Time]:::step
    S6 --> S7{Stress Approaches Equilibrium?}:::decision
    S7 -->|No| S8[Continue Recording sigma t]:::step
    S8 --> S6
    S7 -->|Yes| S9[Compute Relaxation Modulus E of t]:::step
    S9 --> S10[Plot log E of t versus log t]:::step
    S10 --> S11[Fit with Prony Series]:::step
    S11 --> S12[Generate Master Curve using TTS]:::step

    classDef step fill:#3498db,stroke:#1f6dad,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#f39c12,stroke:#af601a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.3 Molecular Mechanism — Chain Rearrangement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State A: Stretched Chains at t equals 0]:::mol --> P2[State B: Segmental Motion Initiated]:::mol
    P2 --> P3[State C: Entanglement Slippage]:::mol
    P3 --> P4[State D: Conformational Rearrangement]:::mol
    P4 --> P5[State E: Equilibrated State]:::mol

    P1 -.sigma0.-> M1[High Internal Stress]:::stress
    P5 -.sigma infinity.-> M2[Low or Zero Stress]:::output

    classDef mol fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#5b2c6f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stress fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#a93226,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef output fill:#27ae60,stroke:#1e8449,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Schematic of Stress Decay Curve

The figure below illustrates the **classical stress relaxation curve** — note the exponential decay character and the **characteristic time $\tau$** marker.

```
|                                  STRESS RELAXATION CURVE
|  σ(t)
|   ^
|   |
|σ0 |** 
|   | ***
|   |   *****
|   |       ********
|   |             ************_________________________
|   |                                              *******  <- σ(∞) → 0
|   +--------------------------------------------------------> t
|        |   |        |        |        |        |
|        τ/3  τ/2      τ       2τ       3τ       4τ
|       (50%) (61%)  (63.2%)  (86.5%)  (95.0%)  (98.2%)
|
|   KEY POINTS:
|   * At t = 0       : σ = σ0 (maximum)
|   * At t = τ       : σ = σ0/e ≈ 0.368 σ0
|   * At t = 2.303 τ  : σ = 0.10 σ0
|   * At t = 5 τ      : σ ≈ 0.0067 σ0 (≈ 0.7% of σ0)
|   * At t → ∞        : σ → 0 (Maxwell liquid)
+-----------------------------------------------------------
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Define stress relaxation in polymers. How is it different from creep?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Stress relaxation** is the time-dependent decrease in stress in a polymer held at a **constant strain** under isothermal conditions. It arises from thermally activated molecular rearrangements that relieve internal stress.
>
> **Difference from Creep (tabular form for clarity):**
>
> | Property | Stress Relaxation | Creep |
> |---|---|---|
> | Controlled variable | **Strain** (held constant) | **Stress** (held constant) |
> | Measured response | Stress decreases with time | Strain increases with time |
> | Governing parameter | Relaxation modulus $E(t)$ | Creep compliance $J(t)$ |
> | Mathematical form | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 J(t)$ |
> | Primary test | Constant-strain test | Constant-stress test |

**(3 marks — Full credit requires the contrast in the second part.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Maxwell model for stress relaxation. What is relaxation time?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Maxwell model** represents a linear viscoelastic polymer as a **spring (modulus $E$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$) connected in series**.
>
> The constitutive equation derived from this arrangement is:
>
> $$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$
>
> Under the **stress relaxation boundary condition** (constant strain), this yields the exponential decay law:
>
> $$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp(-t/\tau)$$
>
> where the **relaxation time** $\tau = \eta / E$ represents the time required for the stress to decay to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of its initial value. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**A. (a)** Derive the stress relaxation equation for the Maxwell model. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A polymer specimen is subjected to a sudden strain of 0.5%. The initial stress measured is 5 MPa, and after 100 seconds the stress is 1.85 MPa. Calculate the relaxation time and the viscosity of the polymer. Given $E = 1 \text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for A(a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Maxwell Element Definitions (1 mark):**
The Maxwell model consists of a spring (modulus $E$, strain $\varepsilon_s$) and a dashpot (viscosity $\eta$, strain $\varepsilon_d$) in series.
$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon_s \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma = \eta \cdot \frac{d\varepsilon_d}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Strain Additivity (1 mark):**
$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_s + \varepsilon_d$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and Substitute (1 mark):**
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Constant-Strain Condition (1 mark):**
$$\frac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{E}\frac{d\sigma}{dt} + \frac{\sigma}{\eta} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Separate Variables (1 mark):**
$$\frac{d\sigma}{\sigma} = -\frac{E}{\eta} \, dt = -\frac{dt}{\tau}, \quad \text{where } \tau = \frac{\eta}{E}$$

**Step 6 — Integrate (1 mark):**
$$\ln \frac{\sigma(t)}{\sigma_0} = -\frac{t}{\tau}$$

**Step 7 — Final Result (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{\;\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp(-t/\tau)\;}$$

---

#### Model Solution for A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Strain $\varepsilon_0 = 0.5\% = 0.005$
- Initial stress $\sigma_0 = 5 \text{ MPa}$
- Stress at $t = 100$ s: $\sigma(100) = 1.85 \text{ MPa}$
- Modulus $E = 1 \text{ GPa} = 1000 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1 — Use the decay equation (2 marks):**
$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$
$$1.85 = 5 \cdot e^{-100/\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $e^{-100/\tau}$ (1 mark):**
$$e^{-100/\tau} = \frac{1.85}{5} = 0.37$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm (1 mark):**
$$-\frac{100}{\tau} = \ln(0.37) = -0.9943$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $\tau$ (1 mark):**
$$\tau = \frac{100}{0.9943} = 100.57 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate viscosity $\eta$ (1 mark):**
$$\eta = E \cdot \tau = 1000 \text{ MPa} \times 100.57 \text{ s} = 1.0057 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau \approx 100.6 \text{ s}, \quad \eta \approx 1.006 \times 10^5 \text{ MPa}\cdot\text{s} = 1.006 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}\;}$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):**
At $t = \tau = 100.57$ s, $\sigma$ should be $5/e = 1.839$ MPa, which matches $1.85$ MPa. ✓

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**B. (a)** Explain the concept of relaxation modulus. Discuss how the **Prony series** generalises the single-Maxwell representation for real polymers. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** A polymer is fitted to a three-element Prony series with parameters: $E_\infty = 2$ MPa, $E_1 = 5$ MPa, $\tau_1 = 10$ s, $E_2 = 3$ MPa, $\tau_2 = 100$ s, $E_3 = 1$ MPa, $\tau_3 = 1000$ s. A constant strain of $0.4\%$ is applied. Calculate the stress at $t = 50$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for B(a) — Concept (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Definition of Relaxation Modulus (2 marks):**
The relaxation modulus $E(t)$ is defined as the **time-dependent stress** generated by a **unit step strain** imposed at $t = 0$:
$$E(t) = \frac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0} \quad [\text{units: Pa}]$$
It quantifies how the polymer "remembers" the imposed deformation. For a Hookean solid, $E(t) = E$ (constant); for a viscous liquid, $E(t) \to 0$.

**Step 2 — Single Maxwell Limitation (1 mark):**
A single Maxwell element predicts a *single* exponential decay with one characteristic time $\tau$. Real polymers, however, exhibit **a broad spectrum of relaxation times** due to the wide distribution of molecular segmental mobilities, chain lengths, and entanglements.

**Step 3 — Generalised Maxwell (Prony Series) (2 marks):**
A generalised model uses **$n$ Maxwell elements in parallel**, plus an equilibrium spring:
$$E(t) = E_\infty + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \cdot \exp(-t/\tau_i)$$
Each $(E_i, \tau_i)$ pair represents a distinct molecular process. The weights $E_i$ sum to give the "short-time modulus":
$$E(0) = E_\infty + \sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i$$

**Step 4 — Physical Significance (1 mark):**
- $E_\infty$ — equilibrium modulus (crosslink density contribution)
- $E_i$ — strength of the $i^{\text{th}}$ molecular process
- $\tau_i$ — characteristic time of the $i^{\text{th}}$ process

**Step 5 — Practical Application (1 mark):**
Prony series is the standard input for **finite element method (FEM)** software like ABAQUS, ANSYS, and LS-DYNA for polymer component simulation.

---

#### Model Solution for B(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $E_\infty = 2$ MPa
- $E_1 = 5$ MPa, $\tau_1 = 10$ s
- $E_2 = 3$ MPa, $\tau_2 = 100$ s
- $E_3 = 1$ MPa, $\tau_3 = 1000$ s
- $\varepsilon_0 = 0.4\% = 0.004$
- $t = 50$ s

**Step 1 — Formula (1 mark):**
$$\sigma(t) = \left[E_\infty + \sum_{i=1}^{3} E_i \cdot e^{-t/\tau_i}\right] \cdot \varepsilon_0$$

**Step 2 — Compute each exponential term (2 marks):**
- $e^{-50/10} = e^{-5} = 0.00674$
- $e^{-50/100} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$
- $e^{-50/1000} = e^{-0.05} = 0.9512$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracket (sum) (1 mark):**
$$E(50) = 2 + (5)(0.00674) + (3)(0.6065) + (1)(0.9512)$$
$$E(50) = 2 + 0.0337 + 1.8195 + 0.9512 = 4.8044 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by strain (1 mark):**
$$\sigma(50) = 4.8044 \times 0.004 = 0.01922 \text{ MPa} = 19.22 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Verify limits (1 mark):**
At $t = 0$: $E(0) = 2 + 5 + 3 + 1 = 11$ MPa, $\sigma(0) = 44$ kPa.
At $t \to \infty$: $E(\infty) = 2$ MPa, $\sigma(\infty) = 8$ kPa.
The value at $t = 50$ s (19.22 kPa) lies between, confirming the decay is in progress. ✓

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation (1 mark):**
At $t = 50$ s, the **fastest process** ($\tau_1 = 10$ s) has almost fully relaxed, the **intermediate process** ($\tau_2 = 100$ s) is in mid-decay, and the **slowest process** ($\tau_3 = 1000$ s) has barely begun. The equilibrium spring still contributes 8 kPa permanently.

$$\boxed{\;\sigma(50 \text{ s}) \approx 19.22 \text{ kPa} = 0.0192 \text{ MPa}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Swapping the controlled variable** — Confusing *stress relaxation* (constant strain) with *creep* (constant stress). This is the single most common error and typically costs **2–3 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting units** — Reporting $\tau$ without units (must be in seconds), or $\eta$ without **Pa·s**.
> 3. **Single-Maxwell misuse** — Applying $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ to a **crosslinked elastomer** that has non-zero equilibrium modulus. The proper model is the **Standard Linear Solid** or **Prony series**.
> 4. **Sign errors in derivation** — Mistakenly writing $\exp(+t/\tau)$ instead of $\exp(-t/\tau)$ in the decay equation. **Stress must decrease**, not increase.
> 5. **Skipping the constant-strain substitution** in the Maxwell derivation. KTU examiners specifically test the *step* where $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = 0$ is substituted. Marks are awarded for showing this step explicitly.
> 6. **In Part B numericals**, writing the exponential value to only 2 significant figures. KTU expects **at least 3–4 significant figures** for full credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress Relaxation (Module 1):**
>
> - **Definition:** Time-dependent stress decay at **constant strain**, isothermal conditions.
> - **Relaxation Modulus:** $E(t) = \sigma(t)/\varepsilon_0$ — units: Pa.
> - **Maxwell Model:** Spring $E$ in series with dashpot $\eta$.
> - **Constitutive ODE:** $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{E}\dfrac{d\sigma}{dt} + \dfrac{\sigma}{\eta}$
> - **Decay Law:** $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 e^{-t/\tau}$ — derived by setting $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}=0$.
> - **Relaxation Time:** $\tau = \eta/E$ — time for stress to drop to $1/e \approx 36.8\%$ of $\sigma_0$.
> - **Prony Series:** $E(t) = E_\infty + \sum E_i e^{-t/\tau_i}$ — for real polymers with broad relaxation spectra.
> - **Equilibrium Stress:** Zero for uncrosslinked polymers; non-zero ($E_\infty \varepsilon_0$) for crosslinked networks.
> - **Effect of Temperature:** $\tau$ decreases sharply as $T$ increases through $T_g$ (WLF / Arrhenius behaviour).
> - **Stress Relaxation vs. Creep:** Relaxation = constant strain; Creep = constant stress.
> - **Standard Test Methods:** ASTM D5023, ISO 6721 (DMA-based testing).
> - **Engineering Significance:** Critical in bolted joints, gaskets, O-rings, pre-tensioned polymer tendons, and biomedical implants.
> - **FEM Input:** Prony series parameters $(E_i, \tau_i)$ are the standard input format for polymer simulation in ABAQUS, ANSYS, etc.
> - **Exam Tip:** Always write the constant-strain condition $\dfrac{d\varepsilon}{dt}=0$ explicitly during derivations — KTU examiners reward this step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress in Products - Tension
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress in Products - Tension

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Tensile Stress** in a polymer product is defined as the internal resistive force per unit original cross-sectional area that a material develops when subjected to an axial pulling (tensile) load. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

where $F$ is the applied tensile force (in Newtons, N) and $A_0$ is the **original (undeformed) cross-sectional area** (in m² or mm²). The SI unit is **Pascals (Pa)**, where **1 Pa = 1 N/m²**. In polymer engineering practice, **MPa (1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa)** and **psi (pounds per square inch)** are commonly used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on understanding *how stress is generated inside a polymer product under tensile loading* — this forms the basis for designing load-bearing plastic components such as gears, fasteners, pipes, containers, and structural housings.

There are **two principal types of tensile stress** recognized in polymer product design:

| Stress Type | Symbol | Definition | When Used |
|-------------|--------|------------|-----------|
| **Engineering (Nominal) Stress** | $\sigma_e$ | Force divided by *original* cross-sectional area $A_0$ | Plotting standard stress–strain curves, design calculations |
| **True Stress** | $\sigma_t$ | Force divided by *instantaneous* (actual) cross-sectional area $A_i$ | Large deformation analysis, necking region, failure prediction |

$$\sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0} \qquad \qquad \sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pulling a **sugar chewing gum strip** horizontally with both hands. As you pull:
- The strip stretches longer (this is *strain*).
- The internal "pulling-back" force generated within the strip is the **tensile stress**.
- The thinner the strip, the higher the stress for the same pull — because stress = force ÷ area.

Now think of a **plastic bucket handle**: when you lift a full bucket of water, the handle experiences tension. If the handle is too thin (small area) for the load, the tensile stress exceeds the material's strength, and it snaps. Polymer product design ensures the stress remains below the **allowable stress** with an adequate **factor of safety (FoS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Tensile stress is the "internal fight-back" of a polymer material against being stretched. The higher this stress for a given load, the better the material is at resisting tension — but only up to its breaking limit.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Gravitational Acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard Tensile Test Specimen:** ASTM D638 (Type I–V) and ISO 527-2 dumbbell specimens
- **Standard Strain Rate:** 1 mm/min (for modulus) and 50 mm/min (for strength) — typical polymer test values
- **Universal Tensile Testing Machine:** Instron, Zwick-Roell, Shimadzu AG-X series
- **Standard Test Temperature:** **23 °C** and **50 % relative humidity** (as per ISO 291)

## 1.4 Why Tension Matters in Polymer Products

Unlike metals, polymers are **viscoelastic** — meaning their tensile response is time-, temperature-, and rate-dependent. A polyethylene shopping bag behaves very differently when pulled slowly versus jerked quickly. This makes **tensile stress analysis** the cornerstone of all polymer product design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Insight:** Most polymer products (films, fibers, pipes, bottles, automotive panels) experience tension as the *primary* loading mode. Understanding tensile stress allows engineers to predict deformation, prevent failure, and optimize material usage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Stress States Under Tension

When a tensile force $F$ is applied along the longitudinal axis of a polymer product, three mutually perpendicular normal stresses develop at any interior point (using a principal stress coordinate system):

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad (\text{axial — tensile}) \qquad \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0 \quad (\text{transverse — unconstrained})$$

This is termed a **uniaxial tensile stress state**.

In real products, however, transverse contraction occurs due to the **Poisson effect**, generating lateral strains:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial}$$

where $\nu$ is the **Poisson's ratio** (typical polymer range: **0.30 – 0.45**; rubber: ~**0.49**; steel: ~**0.30**).

## 2.2 Engineering vs True Stress — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Step 1:** A specimen with original area $A_0$ and original gauge length $L_0$ is loaded.
2. **Step 2:** As force increases, the specimen elongates to $L_0 + \Delta L$, and the cross-section necks (reduces) to $A_i$.
3. **Step 3:** Engineering stress **assumes** the area stays constant at $A_0$ — easier for design, but inaccurate past yielding.
4. **Step 4:** True stress **accounts** for the actual shrinking area — accurate during necking and drawing, vital for polymer films and fibers.

The relationship between true and engineering stress, assuming **constant volume** during plastic deformation ($A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$), is:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_e)$$

where $\varepsilon_e = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ is the engineering strain.

## 2.3 Stress–Strain Behaviour of Polymers (The Five Zones)

A polymer tensile curve differs fundamentally from a metal curve. Five characteristic zones are typically observed:

| Zone | Name | Physical Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|------|------|------------------|---------------------------|
| I | **Elastic Region** | Reversible deformation; bonds stretch | Stiffness ($E$), design limit |
| II | **Yield Point** | Onset of plastic flow (crazing in glassy polymers) | Upper service limit |
| III | **Strain Softening** | Drop in stress after yield | Neck formation begins |
| IV | **Strain Hardening (Drawing)** | Molecular alignment increases resistance | Stable neck propagation |
| V | **Fracture / Break** | Molecular bond rupture, void coalescence | Ultimate failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Glassy polymers (PS, PMMA) fracture in Zone II — *brittle failure*. Semi-crystalline polymers (PE, PP, Nylon) show all five zones — *ductile failure*. Elastomers (rubber) show huge Zone IV extension (>500% strain).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ | Engineering tensile stress | MPa, psi | Basic stress calculation |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless, % | Strain measurement |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$ | Young's Modulus (Elastic Modulus) | MPa, GPa | Stiffness of polymer |
| 4 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$ | Poisson's ratio | dimensionless | Lateral contraction |
| 5 | $\sigma_t = \sigma_e \,(1 + \varepsilon_e)$ | True stress relation | MPa | Large deformation |
| 6 | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) | MPa | Material selection |
| 7 | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{F_{yield}}{A_0}$ | Tensile Yield Strength | MPa | Service stress limit |
| 8 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}}$ | Allowable (Design) Stress | MPa | Safe design |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A_0}\sin\theta\cos\theta$ | Shear stress on inclined plane | MPa | Mohr's circle analysis |
| 10 | $\sigma_\theta = \sigma\cos^2\theta$ | Normal stress on plane at angle $\theta$ | MPa | Principal stress rotation |
| 11 | $\%E = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}\times 100$ | Percentage Elongation at Break | % | Ductility measure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoiding Markdown Errors:** When writing absolute values or stress magnitudes in tables, use $\vert \sigma \vert$ (with `\vert`) instead of the pipe symbol `|σ|`, which breaks the table grid.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering

- **Pipe Design (HDPE):** Hoop stress from internal pressure uses $\sigma = \dfrac{pD}{2t}$ — a tension-derived formula.
- **PET Bottle Design:** Burst pressure depends on tensile hoop and axial stresses.
- **Fiber Spinning (Nylon, PET):** Tensile stress in the spin line governs molecular orientation and final fiber strength.
- **3D-Printed Parts:** Layer adhesion is fundamentally a tensile stress problem between adjacent rasters.
- **Composite Laminates:** Tensile strength in fiber direction is governed by rule-of-mixtures: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relation Between True Stress and Engineering Stress

**Starting Principle:** For plastic deformation in polymers, **incompressibility** (constant volume) is assumed because polymer chains slide past each other without significant density change during drawing.

$$V_0 = V_i \quad \Longrightarrow \quad A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$$

**Step 1 — Express current area in terms of original area and strain:**

$$\frac{A_i}{A_0} = \frac{L_0}{L_i} = \frac{L_0}{L_0 + \Delta L} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}} = \frac{1}{1 + \varepsilon_e}$$

**Step 2 — Write true stress as force over instantaneous area:**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_i} = \frac{F}{A_0} \cdot \frac{A_0}{A_i}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute engineering stress $\sigma_e = F/A_0$ and the area ratio from Step 1:**

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_e)$$

**Interpretation:** True stress is *always greater* than engineering stress for $\varepsilon_e > 0$. At 50 % elongation, $\sigma_t = 1.5\,\sigma_e$; at 100 % elongation (doubling length), $\sigma_t = 2\,\sigma_e$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Stress on an Inclined Plane in a Uniaxial Tension Member

Consider a bar under axial tension $\sigma$ with cross-section $A_0$. Take an inclined section whose normal makes an angle $\theta$ with the bar axis.

**Step 1 — Resolve the force $F$ along the inclined plane:**

The inclined area is $A_\theta = \dfrac{A_0}{\cos\theta}$ (since area projected on the bar axis is $A_0$).

**Step 2 — Resolve $F$ into normal ($N$) and tangential ($V$) components on the inclined section:**

$$N = F \cos\theta \qquad \qquad V = F \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Compute normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ and shear stress $\tau_\theta$:**

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{F\cos\theta}{A_0/\cos\theta} = \frac{F}{A_0}\cos^2\theta = \sigma \cos^2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{V}{A_\theta} = \frac{F\sin\theta}{A_0/\cos\theta} = \frac{F}{A_0}\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 4 — Identify the maxima (used heavily in KTU problems):**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\max} = \sigma \quad \text{at} \quad \theta = 0^\circ$$
$$\tau_{\theta,\max} = \frac{\sigma}{2} \quad \text{at} \quad \theta = 45^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why polymer products that fail in shear (e.g., bolt-hole regions) often do so along a **45° plane** — the maximum shear stress plane. Ductile polymers yield along $\theta = 45°$; brittle polymers fracture along $\theta = 0°$ (maximum normal stress plane).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stress–Strain Behaviour Across Polymer Classes

| Polymer Class | Example | E (MPa) | $\sigma_y$ (MPa) | $\sigma_u$ (MPa) | % Elongation | Failure Mode |
|---------------|---------|---------|------------------|------------------|--------------|--------------|
| Rigid Glassy | PS, PMMA | 3000 – 3500 | 60 – 100 (no yield, breaks) | 50 – 75 | 1 – 5 | Brittle |
| Semi-Crystalline | HDPE, PP, Nylon-6,6 | 800 – 2500 | 20 – 50 | 25 – 70 | 50 – 600 | Ductile (necking) |
| Engineering Plastic | PC, POM, PA66 | 2000 – 3000 | 50 – 70 | 55 – 80 | 30 – 150 | Mixed |
| Elastomer | Natural Rubber, SBR | 1 – 100 | 5 – 30 | 10 – 30 | 100 – 800 | Highly ductile |
| Fibre | Kevlar, UHMWPE fibre | 70 000 – 130 000 | — | 2000 – 4000 | 2 – 5 | Brittle fibre |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A polypropylene tensile specimen has an original gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm and rectangular cross-section $b \times t = 10$ mm × 4 mm. It is loaded to $F = 1200$ N and the elongation is $\Delta L = 1.5$ mm. Calculate:
(a) Engineering stress
(b) Engineering strain
(c) True stress at this load
(d) Young's modulus, if the loading remained elastic

**Solution:**

$$\text{(a)} \quad \sigma_e = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{1200 \text{ N}}{(10 \times 4) \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = \frac{1200}{40 \times 10^{-6}} = 30 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 30 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{(b)} \quad \varepsilon_e = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{1.5}{50} = 0.030 = 3.0\,\%$$

$$\text{(c)} \quad \sigma_t = \sigma_e \,(1 + \varepsilon_e) = 30 \times (1 + 0.030) = 30.90 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\text{(d)} \quad E = \frac{\sigma_e}{\varepsilon_e} = \frac{30 \text{ MPa}}{0.030} = 1000 \text{ MPa} = 1.0 \text{ GPa}$$

This $E$ value (1 GPa) is consistent with typical polypropylene's elastic modulus range (0.9 – 1.4 GPa) — confirming the calculation is realistic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tensile Stress–Strain Curve — Mermaid Schematic (Block Architecture Flow)

This block diagram maps the **interactions** during a tensile test on a polymer specimen, showing how data flows from the testing machine to the characteristic stress points used in design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Mounted in UTM] --> B[Load Cell Reads Force F]
    A --> C[Extensometer Reads Elongation deltaL]
    B --> D[Compute Engineering Stress sigma_e = F divided by A0]
    C --> E[Compute Engineering Strain epsilon_e = deltaL divided by L0]
    D --> F[Plot sigma_e vs epsilon_e Curve]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Zone I: Linear Elastic Region]
    F --> H[Zone II: Yield Point sigma_y]
    F --> I[Zone III: Strain Softening Neck Initiation]
    F --> J[Zone IV: Cold Drawing Strain Hardening]
    F --> K[Zone V: Fracture Point sigma_u and elongation at break]
    G --> L[Compute Young Modulus E equals sigma by epsilon slope]
    H --> M[Design Service Limit Check]
    K --> N[Material Selection Database]
```

## 4.2 Stress State on an Inclined Plane — Mohr's Circle Representation

The following block diagram shows how a uniaxial tensile stress $\sigma$ decomposes into normal and shear components on an inclined plane, which is critical for analysing **failure initiation in polymer products**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Axial Tension sigma on Bar] --> B[Cut Section at angle theta]
    B --> C[Resolve Force F into N and V]
    C --> D[Normal Stress on plane: sigma_theta equals sigma cos squared theta]
    C --> E[Shear Stress on plane: tau_theta equals sigma by 2 times sin 2theta]
    D --> F[Maximum sigma_theta occurs at theta equals 0 degrees]
    E --> G[Maximum tau_theta equals sigma by 2 at theta equals 45 degrees]
    F --> H[Brittle Failure Plane]
    G --> I[Ductile Yield Plane]
```

## 4.3 True vs Engineering Stress — Comparative Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Specimen Under Tension] --> B{Original Area A0}
    A --> C{Instantaneous Area Ai}
    B --> D[Engineering Stress sigma_e equals F by A0]
    C --> E[True Stress sigma_t equals F by Ai]
    D --> F[Easy Design Use Constant Area Assumption]
    E --> G[Accurate Past Yield For Necking and Drawing]
    F --> H[Plot Standard Stress Strain Curve]
    G --> I[Plot True Stress Strain Curve]
    H --> J[Use for FoS and UTS Determination]
    I --> K[Use for Failure Prediction Beyond UTS]
```

## 4.4 Polymer Classification under Tension — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Material] --> B{Is it Glassy Amorphous}
    B -- Yes --> C[Brittle Tensile Failure]
    B -- No --> D{Is it Semi Crystalline}
    D -- Yes --> E[Ductile Failure with Necking and Drawing]
    D -- No --> F{Is it Elastomeric}
    F -- Yes --> G[Hyperelastic Stretching up to 800 percent]
    F -- No --> H[Check Fibre or Composite Architecture]
    H --> I[Anisotropic Tensile Behaviour]
    C --> J[Design for Low Strain Tolerance]
    E --> K[Design for Energy Absorption]
    G --> L[Design for Large Deflection and Recovery]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between engineering stress and true stress with suitable expressions. Why are both needed in polymer product design?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Board Standard):**

| Aspect | Engineering Stress $\sigma_e$ | True Stress $\sigma_t$ |
|--------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Definition | Force per unit *original* area $A_0$ | Force per unit *instantaneous* area $A_i$ |
| Formula | $\sigma_e = F/A_0$ | $\sigma_t = F/A_i$ |
| Assumption | Cross-section constant | Cross-section changes |
| Use | Linear elastic region, design calc. | Necking, drawing, large strain region |

Both are required because, in the elastic region, engineering stress is convenient and accurate, but during plastic flow and necking (which is significant in semi-crystalline polymers like HDPE and Nylon), the area reduces drastically — only true stress captures this.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define Poisson's ratio. What is its typical range for polymers?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Poisson's ratio $\nu$ is defined as the negative ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to axial (longitudinal) strain in a material under uniaxial tension:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$$

For most engineering polymers, $\nu$ lies between **0.30 and 0.45**. Elastomers and rubber-like polymers approach the incompressible limit of **$\nu = 0.50$**. For comparison, steel is ~0.30 and concrete is ~0.20.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** A polymer tensile specimen of original gauge length 60 mm and rectangular cross-section 12 mm × 3 mm yields at a load of 1800 N and fractures at 2400 N. The gauge length at fracture is 75 mm. Calculate:
1. Yield stress
2. Ultimate tensile strength
3. Percentage elongation
4. True stress at fracture (assume uniform deformation)

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled stress–strain diagram, explain the five characteristic zones observed in the tensile testing of a semi-crystalline polymer like low-density polyethylene. Highlight the engineering significance of each zone.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $L_0 = 60$ mm; $A_0 = 12 \times 3 = 36$ mm²; $F_y = 1800$ N; $F_u = 2400$ N; $L_f = 75$ mm

**Step 1 — Yield Stress:**

$$\sigma_y = \frac{F_y}{A_0} = \frac{1800 \text{ N}}{36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 50 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 50 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):**

$$\sigma_u = \frac{F_u}{A_0} = \frac{2400 \text{ N}}{36 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2} = 66.67 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Elongation:**

$$\%E = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{75 - 60}{60} \times 100 = 25\,\%$$

**Step 4 — True Stress at Fracture:**

$$\varepsilon_f = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{15}{60} = 0.25$$

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \,(1 + \varepsilon_f) = \frac{F_u}{A_0} \times (1 + 0.25) = 66.67 \times 1.25 = 83.33 \text{ MPa}$$

---

**(b) Five Zones Explanation [7 Marks]:**

| Zone | Description | Engineering Significance |
|------|-------------|---------------------------|
| **I. Elastic Region** | Linear $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve; reversible chain-bond stretching | Stiffness $E$ determined; sets max elastic service stress |
| **II. Yield Point** | Onset of irreversible flow; shear yielding or crazing begins | Upper limit of safe design; defines yield stress $\sigma_y$ |
| **III. Strain Softening** | Stress drops as neck initiates at weakest cross-section | Neck formation starts — local thinning visible |
| **IV. Cold Drawing / Strain Hardening** | Molecular chains align along tensile axis; neck propagates stably | Energy absorption zone; crucial for impact resistance |
| **V. Fracture** | Bond rupture and chain slip; final break | Defines UTS, % elongation, and toughness |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** A HDPE pipe of outer diameter 50 mm and wall thickness 6 mm is subjected to an internal pressure of 1.5 MPa. Using thin-wall pressure vessel theory, calculate the **hoop (tensile) stress** and **longitudinal stress** in the pipe wall. Discuss whether the material (HDPE with $\sigma_y = 22$ MPa, $\sigma_u = 31$ MPa) is safe with a factor of safety of 3.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the expressions for **normal stress** and **shear stress** on an inclined plane in a uniaxially loaded tensile member. Hence show that the **maximum shear stress plane occurs at 45°** and equals half the applied tensile stress.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Solution [7 Marks]:**

**Given:** $D_o = 50$ mm; $t = 6$ mm; $p = 1.5$ MPa; $\sigma_y = 22$ MPa; $\sigma_u = 31$ MPa; FoS = 3

**Step 1 — Compute mean diameter (thin-wall assumption):**

$$D_m = D_o - t = 50 - 6 = 44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Hoop (circumferential) tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot D_m}{2t} = \frac{1.5 \times 44}{2 \times 6} = \frac{66}{12} = 5.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Longitudinal (axial) tensile stress:**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{p \cdot D_m}{4t} = \frac{1.5 \times 44}{4 \times 6} = \frac{66}{24} = 2.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Allowable stress with FoS = 3:**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{22}{3} = 7.33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Safety Check:**

$$\sigma_h = 5.5 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{allow} = 7.33 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

$$\sigma_L = 2.75 \text{ MPa} < 7.33 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark \text{ SAFE}$$

The pipe is **safe** under the given operating pressure with FoS = 3.

---

**(b) Inclined-Plane Stress Derivation [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Let the bar have cross-section $A_0$ and be loaded by axial force $F$, giving tensile stress $\sigma = F/A_0$.

**Step 2 — Inclined section geometry:** Consider a section whose normal makes angle $\theta$ with the bar axis. The inclined area is:

$$A_\theta = \frac{A_0}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 3 — Resolve the force into normal ($N$) and tangential ($V$):**

$$N = F\cos\theta \qquad \qquad V = F\sin\theta$$

**Step 4 — Compute stresses on the inclined plane:**

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{N}{A_\theta} = \frac{F\cos\theta}{A_0/\cos\theta} = \sigma \cos^2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{V}{A_\theta} = \frac{F\sin\theta}{A_0/\cos\theta} = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sin 2\theta$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate to find maximum shear stress:**

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = \sigma\cos 2\theta = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad 2\theta = 90^\circ \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \theta = 45^\circ$$

Substituting $\theta = 45^\circ$:

$$\tau_{\theta,\max} = \frac{\sigma}{2}\sin(90^\circ) = \frac{\sigma}{2}$$

Hence proved: the maximum shear stress plane lies at **45°** to the bar axis and equals **half** the applied uniaxial tensile stress.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert mm² to m² when computing stress in SI units. (1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m²)
> 2. **Mixing engineering and true stress:** Reporting UTS as true stress value — UTS is by definition **engineering** stress.
> 3. **Confusing hoop with longitudinal stress:** Hoop = $pD/2t$, Longitudinal = $pD/4t$. Always remember **Hoop is twice Longitudinal** for closed-end pressure vessels.
> 4. **Mohr's circle / 45° plane confusion:** Students often confuse the maximum *normal* stress ($\theta = 0°$) with the maximum *shear* stress ($\theta = 45°$). Both maxima exist simultaneously on different planes.
> 5. **Forgetting to apply Factor of Safety:** Always compare **induced stress** with **$\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$**, not with $\sigma_y$ itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] Tensile stress $\sigma = F/A_0$ — the foundational equation.
- [x] Engineering stress uses **original area** $A_0$; True stress uses **instantaneous area** $A_i$.
- [x] True stress equation: $\sigma_t = \sigma_e (1 + \varepsilon_e)$ — valid under constant-volume (incompressible) plastic flow.
- [x] Young's modulus $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ — measures stiffness; polymers typically 0.1 – 4 GPa.
- [x] Poisson's ratio $\nu$ for polymers: **0.30 – 0.50**; rubber approaches 0.50.
- [x] Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) = $F_{max} / A_0$ — engineering measure of maximum load.
- [x] Yield strength $\sigma_y$ — upper limit for elastic design in ductile polymers.
- [x] Five zones in polymer tensile curve: **Elastic → Yield → Softening → Hardening → Fracture**.
- [x] Maximum normal stress on inclined plane: $\sigma_\theta = \sigma \cos^2\theta$ at $\theta = 0°$.
- [x] Maximum shear stress on inclined plane: $\tau_\theta = (\sigma/2)\sin 2\theta$ at $\theta = 45°$, equal to $\sigma/2$.
- [x] Hoop stress in thin-walled pipe: $\sigma_h = pD/(2t)$; longitudinal stress: $\sigma_L = pD/(4t)$.
- [x] Factor of safety: $\sigma_{allow} = \sigma_y / \text{FoS}$.
- [x] Design rule: Always ensure induced stress $\leq \sigma_{allow}$.
- [x] Polymer classification by tensile behaviour: Brittle (glassy), Ductile (semi-crystalline), Hyperelastic (elastomers), Anisotropic (fibres/composites).
- [x] Standard test methods: **ASTM D638** and **ISO 527** — know specimen shapes (Type I–V).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In Module 1, examiners love linking tension to **pressure vessel design** (pipes, bottles) and **inclined-plane stress transformation**. Practice both — they account for over 60% of marks in the Tension section.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compression and Shear
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compression and Shear

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Compression Testing of Polymers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Compression Testing** is a mechanical evaluation technique in which a uniaxial compressive load is applied to a polymer specimen (typically cylindrical or prismatic) along its longitudinal axis, and the resulting deformation (reduction in length) and load are recorded continuously to determine key mechanical properties such as **compressive strength (σ_c)**, **compressive modulus (E_c)**, **yield stress in compression**, and **compressive strain at break**.

In polymer product design, compression behavior is critical for components that experience *pushing* or *squeezing* loads — for example, polymer gears, gaskets, washers, bearing cages, packaging foams, O-rings, and structural supports in civil/automotive applications. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that a polymer's compressive response is **not symmetric** to its tensile response because polymers exhibit different molecular chain mobility under squeezing versus pulling.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Sponge vs. Rubber Ball" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine gently squeezing a soft polymer sponge between your palms. The sponge shortens vertically and bulges outward at the sides. The resistance you feel is the *compressive resistance*. Now imagine pulling the same sponge apart — the resistance you feel is the *tensile resistance*. In most polymers, these two resistances are **different in magnitude and behavior** because the internal chain uncoiling, sliding, and bond reorientation respond differently to compression versus tension.

For brittle polymers (e.g., **PMMA, PS, thermoset phenolics**), compression often produces higher strength values than tension because microcracks tend to *close* under compression rather than propagate. For ductile/elastomeric polymers (e.g., **LDPE, rubber, PTFE**), compression causes extensive barreling without fracture.

## 1.3 Shear Testing of Polymers — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shear Testing** is a mechanical evaluation in which a force is applied **parallel (tangential)** to the surface of a polymer specimen, producing a deformation where one face of the material slides relative to the opposite face, without significant change in volume. It yields **shear strength (τ)**, **shear modulus (G)**, and the **shear strain (γ)**.

Shear loading is the dominant load mode in polymer components like **bolted/riveted joints, adhesive bonds, laminate interfaces, screw threads, tires, drive belts, and rotating shafts** subjected to torsion. In torsion of a circular shaft, every internal element experiences pure shear.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Deck of Cards" View

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Hold a deck of playing cards firmly in your palm and push the top of the deck sideways with your other hand. Each card slides slightly relative to the one below it — that inter-layer sliding is *shear deformation*. The resistance the cards offer to sliding is *shear resistance*. In a polymer solid, the long chain molecules (or layered crystalline/amorphous regions) act like these cards, sliding past each other under tangential load.

## 1.5 Key Standard Test Methods (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Test Methods (to be cited in KTU answers):**
> - **ASTM D695** — Standard Test Method for Compressive Properties of Rigid Plastics.
> - **ISO 604** — Determination of Compressive Properties of Plastics.
> - **ASTM D732** — Standard Test Method for Shear Strength of Plastics by Punch Tool.
> - **ASTM D5379** — Shear Properties of Composite Materials by V-Notched Beam Method.
> - **ISO 14129** — Shear Test for Composite Laminates.

## 1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa**
> - **1 GPa = 1000 MPa**
> - Standard specimen geometry for ASTM D695: **cylindrical, diameter 12.7 mm, height 25.4 mm** (slenderness ratio ≈ 2:1) or **prismatic 12.7 × 12.7 × 25.4 mm**.

---

## Why Compression and Shear Matter in Polymer Design

Polymers are increasingly replacing metals in structural load-bearing applications (automotive, aerospace, biomedical, civil). KTU 2024 scheme stresses that **designing a polymer part using only tensile data is a critical error** — engineers must consult the full **triaxial mechanical property map** (tensile + compressive + shear + flexural) to ensure safety. Compression and shear failure modes are often the *true* limit states in service.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Compressive Stress–Strain Behavior of Polymers

When a polymer specimen is loaded in uniaxial compression, the **engineering compressive stress (σ_c)** and **engineering compressive strain (ε_c)** are defined as:

- **Compressive Stress:** $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F}{A_0}$ where F = applied compressive load, A₀ = original cross-sectional area.
- **Compressive Strain:** $\varepsilon_c = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L_0 - L}{L_0}$ (always positive, since L < L₀ under compression).

> [!NOTE]
> By KTU convention, compressive stress and strain are reported as **positive numerical values** (sign convention: compression = positive), although mathematically a negative sign may be used in tensor analysis.

### Classification of Compressive Response

| Polymer Class | Compressive Response | Typical Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
