| **Brittle (PS, PMMA, Phenolic)** | Linear elastic up to sudden fracture | Cracking, splitting, fragmentation |
| **Semi-ductile (ABS, Nylon, PC)** | Elastic → yield → plastic flow → strain hardening | Yielding + barreling |
| **Elastomeric (Rubber, TPE, Silicone)** | Large nonlinear elastic deformation | No fracture — recoverable strain > 100% |
| **Foamed polymers (PU foam, EPS)** | Three-region: linear → plateau → densification | Cell collapse, densification |

### Compressive Properties Extracted from the σ–ε Curve

1. **Compressive Modulus (E_c):** Slope of the initial linear portion of the stress–strain curve, $E_c = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_c}$ in the elastic region.
2. **Compressive Yield Strength (σ_c,y):** Stress at which the curve deviates from linearity (or shows a peak followed by plastic flow).
3. **Compressive Strength (σ_c,u):** Maximum stress the material can sustain; for brittle polymers, this equals the fracture stress.
4. **Compressive Strain at Break (ε_c,b):** Strain at which the specimen fractures or crumbles.
5. **Offset Yield Strength:** Stress at which a line parallel to the elastic slope, offset by a standard strain (typically 0.2% or 1%), intersects the curve.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Concept:** For most polymers, **E_c ≈ E_t (tensile modulus)** within 5–10% for isotropic, low-strain conditions. However, the **compressive strength is typically 1.2× to 1.5× higher than the tensile strength** for brittle polymers because compressive hydrostatic stress suppresses crack opening.

## 2.2 Engineering Significance of Compressive Testing in Polymer Design

- **Bearing pads and bridge bearings:** Must sustain high compressive loads over decades.
- **Polymer gears and cams:** Hertzian contact stresses are predominantly compressive at the tooth flank.
- **Crashworthiness (automotive bumpers, EPS foam cores):** Energy absorption in compression is a key design metric.
- **Packaging and cushioning:** Compression set (permanent deformation after long-term loading) determines service life.
- **Bioprosthetic implants (UHMWPE tibial inserts):** Compressive fatigue and creep determine implant longevity.

## 2.3 Shear Stress–Strain Behavior of Polymers

For a polymer element subjected to a tangential force F acting on area A:

- **Shear Stress:** $\tau = \dfrac{F}{A}$ (units: Pa, MPa, or psi).
- **Shear Strain (γ):** $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (for small angles), where θ is the angular deformation in radians.

### Shear Properties Extracted from the τ–γ Curve

1. **Shear Modulus (G):** Slope of the initial linear elastic region: $G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$.
2. **Shear Yield Strength (τ_y):** Stress at onset of plastic shear flow.
3. **Ultimate Shear Strength (τ_u):** Maximum shear stress before failure.
4. **Proportional Limit:** End of linear τ–γ relationship.

## 2.4 The Master Isotropic Elasticity Relationship — KTU High-Yield Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Equation of Isotropic Linear Elasticity (must be memorized for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> $$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$
>
> where **E** = Young's modulus, **G** = shear modulus, **ν** = Poisson's ratio.

This equation links tensile, shear, and lateral behavior. It is the **single most important equation** in elasticity for KTU exams because it allows determination of any one of (E, G, ν) from the other two.

### Derived Forms (KTU Frequently Asked)

$$G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$\nu = \dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$$

### Bounds on Poisson's Ratio for Isotropic Stable Materials

$$\;-1 < \nu < 0.5$$

- **ν ≈ 0.5** for incompressible elastomers (rubber).
- **ν ≈ 0.3 to 0.4** for most engineering thermoplastics.
- **ν ≈ 0.2 to 0.3** for rigid thermosets and composites.
- **ν ≈ 0.0** for cork (a natural cellular material).

## 2.5 Bulk Modulus vs. Compressive Modulus — KTU Conceptual Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Compressive Modulus (E_c)** refers to *uniaxial* compression along one axis. **Bulk Modulus (K)** refers to *hydrostatic* (equal pressure from all sides) compression. They are related by:
>
> $$K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$
>
> For most polymers with ν ≈ 0.35, K ≈ E/0.9 ≈ 1.11 E. Thus **bulk modulus is generally greater than Young's modulus**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_c = F/A_0$ | Compressive engineering stress | MPa | Direct calculation from load cell data |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_c = \Delta L / L_0$ | Compressive engineering strain | dimensionless | Extensometer output |
| 3 | $E_c = \sigma_c / \varepsilon_c$ | Compressive modulus (linear region) | MPa | Slope of initial σ–ε line |
| 4 | $\sigma_{c,0.2} = \sigma$ at 0.2% offset | Offset compressive yield strength | MPa | For ductile polymers (ABS, Nylon) |
| 5 | $\tau = F/A$ | Shear stress | MPa | Punch-tool (ASTM D732) test |
| 6 | $\gamma = \tan\theta$ | Shear strain | rad | Torsion test on polymer rod |
| 7 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Shear modulus | MPa | Slope of linear τ–γ region |
| 8 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Isotropic elastic relation | MPa | Connecting E, G, ν |
| 9 | $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Inverted form | MPa | Solve for G given E, ν |
| 10 | $\nu = E/(2G) - 1$ | Inverted form | dimensionless | Solve for ν given E, G |
| 11 | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | Bulk modulus | MPa | Hydrostatic compression |
| 12 | $U = \int \tau \cdot d\gamma$ | Strain energy density in shear | J/m³ | Energy absorption in shear |
| 13 | $W_c = \int_0^{\varepsilon_b} \sigma_c \, d\varepsilon_c$ | Work per unit volume in compression | J/m³ | Cushion/foam energy absorption |
| 14 | $\sigma_{c,ult} = P_{max}/A_0$ | Ultimate compressive strength | MPa | Failure criterion in design |
| 15 | $\tau_{ult} = P_{shear,max}/A$ | Ultimate shear strength | MPa | Bolt/adhesive joint design |

## 2.7 Practical Engineering Design Equations Using Shear and Compression Data

> [!NOTE]
> **For bolted polymer joints** (e.g., a polymer flange fastened by a steel bolt):
>
> $$\tau_{joint} = \dfrac{F_{bolt}}{\pi \cdot d \cdot t} \leq \dfrac{\tau_{ult}}{n}$$
>
> where d = bolt shank diameter, t = thickness of the thinnest plate, n = safety factor (typically 3–5 for polymers).

> [!NOTE]
> **For polymer column in compression** (Euler-buckling-relevant only for slender geometries; for stocky polymer pillars, material crushing governs):
>
> $$\sigma_{crush} = \dfrac{P_{crush}}{A} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{c,yield}}{n}$$
>
> For very slender polymer columns:
>
> $$P_{Euler} = \dfrac{\pi^2 E_c I}{L_e^2}$$
>
> where I = second moment of area, L_e = effective length based on end conditions.

## 2.8 Failure Theories Relevant to Compression + Shear States (KTU Advanced)

For a polymer element under combined normal and shear stress (e.g., oblique loading), the **maximum shear stress theory (Tresca)** and **distortion energy theory (von Mises)** are commonly applied.

- **Tresca (max shear stress):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} \leq \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$ (failure when max shear = yield shear/2).
- **Von Mises (distortion energy):** $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\sigma^2 + 3\tau^2} \leq \sigma_y$ for uniaxial σ with in-plane τ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For pure shear loading, von Mises reduces to $\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{3}\,\tau$, hence the **shear yield strength ≈ σ_y / √3** (≈ 0.577 σ_y) for von Mises materials. This is a frequently tested KTU result.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Isotropic Elastic Relation E = 2G(1 + ν)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU ESE Module 1. We derive it from the definitions of E, G, and ν applied to a small cubic element of an isotropic polymer.

### Setup

Consider a small cubic element of side L, made of an isotropic linear elastic polymer. Apply a **pure shear stress τ** on the top and bottom faces (and corresponding pair on the side faces, to maintain rotational equilibrium of the element).

The pure shear stress state produces two **principal stresses**:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

acting on planes oriented at 45° to the original cube faces (i.e., along the diagonals of the cube).

### Step 1 — Normal Strain Along the Principal Directions

By definition of Young's modulus, the strain along the σ₁ direction is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E} - \nu \dfrac{\sigma_2}{E}$$

Substitute σ₁ = +τ and σ₂ = −τ:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{+\tau}{E} - \nu \dfrac{(-\tau)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\tau}{E} + \dfrac{\nu \tau}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

### Step 2 — Engineering Shear Strain from Principal Strains

The **engineering shear strain γ** is the change in angle (originally 90°) between the two principal directions. If one diagonal of the cube elongates and the other shortens by amounts related to ε₁ and ε₂, the resulting angular change (in radians, for small strains) is:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$$

By symmetry of the shear stress state, ε₂ = −ε₁, so:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - (-\varepsilon_1) = 2\varepsilon_1$$

### Step 3 — Substitute ε₁

$$\gamma = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

$$\gamma = \dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Definition of Shear Modulus

By Hooke's law in shear:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma \quad \Rightarrow \quad \gamma = \dfrac{\tau}{G}$$

Equate the two expressions for γ:

$$\dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Cancel τ (non-zero) from both sides:

$$\dfrac{1}{G} = \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E}$$

Invert:

$$\boxed{\,G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}\,}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\,E = 2G(1+\nu)\,}$$

**Q.E.D. — the master isotropic elastic relation.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — KTU 3-Mark Style

> **Problem:** A polymer has Young's modulus E = 3.0 GPa and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.40. Calculate its shear modulus G.

**Solution:**

Given: E = 3.0 GPa = 3000 MPa, ν = 0.40.

Using the isotropic elastic relation:

$$G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

$$G = \dfrac{3000 \text{ MPa}}{2(1 + 0.40)}$$

$$G = \dfrac{3000}{2(1.40)} = \dfrac{3000}{2.80}$$

$$G \approx 1071.4 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.07 \text{ GPa}$$

**Answer:** G ≈ 1.07 GPa.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a typical KTU Part A 3-mark problem. Steps: (i) write formula, (ii) substitute, (iii) compute, (iv) state answer with units.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — KTU 7-Mark Style

> **Problem (Module 1, Part B typical):** A cylindrical polymer specimen of diameter 12.7 mm and height 25.4 mm is tested in compression per ASTM D695. The following load–displacement data were recorded (at room temperature). Determine (a) the compressive modulus E_c, (b) the 0.2% offset yield strength σ_c,y, and (c) the ultimate compressive strength σ_c,u.
>
> | Load F (N) | Displacement ΔL (mm) |
> |---|---|
> | 0 | 0.000 |
> | 500 | 0.030 |
> | 1000 | 0.061 |
> | 1500 | 0.092 |
> | 1800 | 0.110 |
> | 2000 | 0.122 |
> | 2100 | 0.150 (yield detected) |
> | 2150 | 0.230 |
> | 2200 | 0.380 |
> | 2100 | 0.610 (peak load) |
> | 1900 | 0.800 (failure load) |

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Geometry:**
Cross-sectional area: A₀ = π/4 × d² = π/4 × (12.7)² = 126.7 mm².
Original length: L₀ = 25.4 mm.

**Step (ii) — Convert (F, ΔL) to (σ, ε):**

| F (N) | ΔL (mm) | σ = F/A₀ (MPa) | ε = ΔL/L₀ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0.0000 |
| 500 | 0.030 | 3.95 | 0.00118 |
| 1000 | 0.061 | 7.89 | 0.00240 |
| 1500 | 0.092 | 11.84 | 0.00362 |
| 1800 | 0.110 | 14.21 | 0.00433 |
| 2000 | 0.122 | 15.79 | 0.00480 |
| 2100 | 0.150 | 16.58 | 0.00591 |
| 2150 | 0.230 | 16.97 | 0.00906 |
| 2200 | 0.380 | 17.36 | 0.01496 |
| 2100 | 0.610 | 16.58 | 0.02402 |
| 1900 | 0.800 | 14.99 | 0.03150 |

**Step (iii) — Compressive Modulus (linear region, ε < 0.005):**

Using first and fifth data points (linear elastic region):

$$E_c = \dfrac{\sigma_5 - \sigma_1}{\varepsilon_5 - \varepsilon_1} = \dfrac{14.21 - 0}{0.00433 - 0} = 3282 \text{ MPa}$$

**E_c ≈ 3.28 GPa** (typical of a medium-modulus engineering thermoplastic like polypropylene).

**Step (iv) — 0.2% offset yield strength:**

Construct a line parallel to the initial elastic slope, offset by ε = 0.002 (0.2%) on the strain axis:

Equation of offset line: σ = E_c × (ε − 0.002) = 3282 × (ε − 0.002).

The intersection of this offset line with the measured curve occurs approximately at:

$$3282 \times (\varepsilon - 0.002) = \sigma(\varepsilon)$$

By interpolation between the points (ε = 0.00480, σ = 15.79) and (ε = 0.00591, σ = 16.58):

At ε = ε_y, the offset line gives σ = 3282(ε_y − 0.002).

Trial: ε_y = 0.00591 → σ_offset = 3282 × 0.00391 = 12.84 MPa. Curve value = 16.58 MPa. Offset line is below curve → yield not yet reached at this strain.

Trial: ε_y = 0.00700 → σ_offset = 3282 × 0.005 = 16.41 MPa. Curve value (interpolated between 0.00591 and 0.00906) = 16.58 + (16.97 − 16.58) × (0.00700 − 0.00591)/(0.00906 − 0.00591) = 16.58 + 0.39 × 0.346 = 16.71 MPa. 

Set 3282(ε_y − 0.002) = curve value:
3282 ε_y − 6.564 = curve(ε_y).

Iterating:
ε_y ≈ 0.00725 → σ_offset = 16.78 MPa; curve ≈ 16.79 MPa ✓

**σ_c,0.2 ≈ 16.8 MPa.**

**Step (v) — Ultimate compressive strength:**

The peak load in the dataset is 2200 N (before the load drops to 2100 N).

$$\sigma_{c,u} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \dfrac{2200 \text{ N}}{126.7 \text{ mm}^2} = 17.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**σ_c,u ≈ 17.4 MPa.**

**Final Answers:**
- E_c ≈ **3.28 GPa**
- σ_c,0.2 ≈ **16.8 MPa**
- σ_c,u ≈ **17.4 MPa**

> [!NOTE]
> The peak (17.4 MPa) is slightly above the offset yield (16.8 MPa), indicating this polymer shows mild strain softening after yielding — typical of a semi-ductile polymer like **Nylon 6,6 or POM**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Shear via Punch Tool (ASTM D732)

> **Problem:** A polymer disk (diameter 50 mm, thickness 5 mm) is tested in shear using the punch-tool method (ASTM D732). The punch diameter is 25.4 mm. The maximum force recorded is 8.5 kN. Compute the **shear strength (τ_u)** of the polymer.

**Solution:**

In the ASTM D732 test, the punch shears out a cylindrical plug. The shear area is the **lateral cylindrical surface** of the plug:

$$A_{shear} = \pi \cdot d_{punch} \cdot t = \pi \times 25.4 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ mm}$$

$$A_{shear} = 3.1416 \times 25.4 \times 5 = 399.0 \text{ mm}^2$$

Shear strength:

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_{shear}} = \dfrac{8500 \text{ N}}{399.0 \text{ mm}^2} = 21.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**τ_u ≈ 21.3 MPa.**

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 4 — Torsion Test Deriving G

> **Problem:** A solid circular polymer shaft of diameter d = 20 mm and gauge length L = 200 mm is subjected to torque T = 12 N·m. The angle of twist over the gauge length is φ = 2.4° = 0.04189 rad. Compute the **shear modulus G**.

**Solution:**

Polar moment of inertia of a solid circular section:

$$J = \dfrac{\pi d^4}{32} = \dfrac{\pi (20)^4}{32} = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 160000}{32} = 15708 \text{ mm}^4$$

Surface shear stress at the outer surface (r = d/2 = 10 mm):

$$\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J} = \dfrac{12000 \text{ N·mm} \times 10 \text{ mm}}{15708 \text{ mm}^4} = 7.639 \text{ MPa}$$

Surface shear strain (at r = 10 mm):

$$\gamma = \dfrac{r \cdot \phi}{L} = \dfrac{10 \text{ mm} \times 0.04189 \text{ rad}}{200 \text{ mm}} = 0.002094 \text{ rad}$$

Shear modulus:

$$G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma} = \dfrac{7.639 \text{ MPa}}{0.002094} = 3648 \text{ MPa} \approx 3.65 \text{ GPa}$$

**G ≈ 3.65 GPa.**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 14-Mark Extension:** If a separate tensile test on the same polymer gives E = 9.0 GPa, verify the consistency using E = 2G(1+ν) → ν = E/(2G) − 1 = 9000/(2×3648) − 1 = 1.234 − 1 = 0.234. Since 0 < ν < 0.5, the result is physically admissible (ν ≈ 0.23 — typical of a rigid thermoset such as epoxy or phenolic).

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Compression vs. Shear Property Comparison

| Property / Aspect | **Compression Test** | **Shear Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Loading direction | Normal to face (axial push) | Tangential / parallel to face |
| Specimen geometry (typical) | Cylinder or prism (h/d ≈ 2) | Disk (punch), Iosipescu beam, or torsion rod |
| Strain definition | ε = ΔL/L₀ (linear) | γ = tan θ ≈ θ (angular) |
| Stress definition | σ = F/A₀ | τ = F/A |
| Modulus derived | E_c | G |
| Typical standard | **ASTM D695**, ISO 604 | **ASTM D732**, ASTM D5379 |
| Failure mode (brittle) | Axial splitting, fragmentation | Clean shear plug removal, edge shear |
| Failure mode (ductile) | Barreling, ductile flow | Plastic shear, drawing |
| Hydrostatic component | High (compressive hydrostatic stress) | Zero (pure shear is deviatoric) |
| Volume change | Tends to *decrease* | Essentially zero (small-strain incompressibility) |
| Common engineering use | Gears, bearings, foams, columns | Bolts, adhesives, rivets, shafts |
| Relation to other modes | Connects to E and K via ν | Connects to E via ν |

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Relationship Between E, G, K, and ν for an Isotropic Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young's Modulus E] --> EQ[Master Isotropic Elastic Equations]
    G[Shear Modulus G] --> EQ
    NU[Poisson's Ratio nu] --> EQ
    K[Bulk Modulus K] --> EQ
    EQ --> R1[E = 2G 1 + nu]
    EQ --> R2[G = E over 2 1 + nu]
    EQ --> R3[K = E over 3 1 minus 2 nu]
    EQ --> R4[nu = E over 2G minus 1]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows that **E, G, K, and ν form a closed system of four interrelated parameters** for an isotropic polymer. Knowing any two of (E, G, ν) uniquely determines the other two and also K.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define compressive strength of a polymer and state the standard test method used to determine it.**

**Model Answer:**
Compressive strength of a polymer is the maximum uniaxial compressive stress that a polymer specimen can sustain before failure (fracture for brittle polymers, or crushing/yielding for ductile polymers). Mathematically:

$$\sigma_{c,u} = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$

where P_max is the maximum compressive load and A₀ is the original cross-sectional area. The standard test method is **ASTM D695** (or ISO 604) which uses a cylindrical or prismatic specimen loaded between two flat platens at a controlled crosshead speed (typically 1.3 mm/min for rigid plastics). [3 marks]

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is shear modulus? How is it related to Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio for an isotropic polymer?**

**Model Answer:**
Shear modulus (G) is defined as the ratio of shear stress (τ) to shear strain (γ) within the linear elastic limit:

$$G = \dfrac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

For an isotropic polymer, the three elastic constants E, G, and ν are related by the **master isotropic elasticity equation**:

$$E = 2G(1+\nu) \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

This allows determination of G from tensile data alone, since E and ν are easily obtained from a standard tensile test. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question B1 – Choice A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the compressive stress–strain behavior of the three major classes of polymers — brittle, ductile, and elastomeric. Compare the compressive strength and modulus values with their tensile counterparts. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A polymer has E = 4.0 GPa and ν = 0.35. Calculate its shear modulus G, bulk modulus K, and the ratio K/G. Comment on the result. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Answer (a):

**Brittle polymers** (PS, PMMA, phenolic):
The σ–ε curve is essentially linear elastic up to a sudden fracture point. No plastic yielding. The compressive strength is significantly higher (≈ 1.3–1.5×) than tensile strength because compressive hydrostatic stress closes microcracks. E_c ≈ E_t.

**Ductile thermoplastics** (ABS, Nylon, PC):
The σ–ε curve shows: (i) initial linear elastic region, (ii) yield point, (iii) plastic flow region (often with barreling of the specimen), (iv) possible strain hardening. E_c ≈ E_t but the post-yield behavior differs sharply from tensile.

**Elastomers** (natural rubber, silicone, TPE):
The σ–ε curve is highly non-linear, J-shaped (low initial modulus, stiffening at high strain). Very large recoverable strains (up to 500–800%). Compressive modulus (E_c) and tensile modulus (E_t) are nearly equal only at infinitesimal strain; at finite strain they diverge due to the strain-stiffening effect of the underlying chain network.

**Comparison table:**

| Property | Brittle | Ductile | Elastomer |
|---|---|---|---|
| σ_c,u vs σ_t,u | σ_c > σ_t | σ_c ≈ σ_t (slightly higher) | σ_c ≈ σ_t |
| E_c vs E_t | E_c ≈ E_t | E_c ≈ E_t | E_c ≈ E_t (small strain) |
| Failure mode | Cracking/splitting | Yield + barreling | Recoverable compression |
| Specimen end condition | Sensitive | Very sensitive (friction at platen) | Lubrication critical |

> [!NOTE]
> For brittle polymers, platen friction prevents free lateral expansion, generating a triaxial state that elevates apparent strength (this is the "Bauschinger-like" effect reversed). For accurate E_c, lubrication of platen surfaces (e.g., PTFE film + grease) is recommended in ASTM D695.

**Sketch description (to be drawn in exam):**
A graph with ε on x-axis and σ on y-axis showing three curves: brittle (steep line, sudden drop), ductile (linear → yield → plateau → hardening), elastomer (J-shaped curve).

[7 marks awarded for: classification 2 marks, comparison 3 marks, sketch 2 marks]

#### Model Answer (b):

Given: E = 4.0 GPa, ν = 0.35.

**Shear Modulus:**

$$G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \dfrac{4.0}{2(1.35)} = \dfrac{4.0}{2.70} = 1.481 \text{ GPa}$$

**Bulk Modulus:**

$$K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \dfrac{4.0}{3(1 - 0.70)} = \dfrac{4.0}{3 \times 0.30} = \dfrac{4.0}{0.90} = 4.444 \text{ GPa}$$

**Ratio K/G:**

$$\dfrac{K}{G} = \dfrac{4.444}{1.481} = 3.00$$

**Comment:** The ratio K/G = 3.0 is consistent with the theoretical value K/G = (2/3) × (1+ν)/(1−2ν) = (2/3) × (1.35/0.30) = (2/3) × 4.5 = 3.0. This confirms the internal consistency of the four elastic constants. The fact that K > G > E/3 indicates the polymer resists volumetric compression (hydrostatic loading) more effectively than shear or uniaxial deformation, which is typical for glassy polymers with significant free volume. [7 marks: each sub-calculation 2 marks, final ratio 1 mark, comment 2 marks]

**Final Answers:**
- G ≈ 1.48 GPa
- K ≈ 4.44 GPa
- K/G = 3.00

---

### Question B1 – Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **ASTM D732 punch-tool shear test** for polymers. List its advantages and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A polymer disk of diameter 50 mm and thickness 4 mm is sheared in a punch-tool test (punch diameter 25.4 mm). The recorded peak load is 7.6 kN. Compute (i) the ultimate shear strength, (ii) the shear area, and (iii) the work done to failure if the load–displacement curve approximates a triangle with final displacement 1.2 mm. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Answer (a):

**Description of ASTM D732 Punch-Tool Shear Test:**

The ASTM D732 test uses a **punch-tool fixture** mounted on a universal testing machine. The specimen is a flat disk (typical 50 mm diameter, 1.6 to 12.7 mm thick). The fixture consists of:
1. A **supporting die** with a circular hole (clearance ~ 25.4 mm + small clearance).
2. A **cylindrical punch** of diameter 25.4 mm, perfectly aligned with the die hole.
3. The disk is clamped between the die and a clamping plate.
4. The punch moves downward at a fixed rate (typically 1.25 mm/min), shearing out a cylindrical plug from the disk.

**Diagram elements to be drawn:**
- Cross-section view showing the die, the disk, the punch, the sheared plug, and the direction of force.
- Indicate the shear area = π × d_punch × t.

**Advantages:**
- Simple specimen preparation (just a flat disk).
- Direct measurement of shear strength at ambient conditions.
- No specialized torsion equipment needed; uses standard UTM.
- Small specimen size, low material consumption.

**Limitations:**
- **Pure shear assumption is approximate** — the actual stress state at the punch edge includes bending and compressive components.
- Results are sensitive to **clearance between punch and die**; tight tolerance is required.
- Specimen thickness affects results: very thin disks buckle; very thick disks require high forces.
- Cannot measure shear modulus G directly; only ultimate shear strength.
- Edge-chipping and crack propagation may precede true shear, especially in brittle polymers.

[7 marks: diagram 2 marks, description 3 marks, advantages/limitations 2 marks]

#### Model Answer (b):

Given: D = 50 mm, t = 4 mm, d_punch = 25.4 mm, P_max = 7.6 kN = 7600 N, ΔL_final = 1.2 mm.

**(i) Shear Area:**

$$A_{shear} = \pi \cdot d_{punch} \cdot t = \pi \times 25.4 \times 4 = 319.2 \text{ mm}^2$$

**(ii) Ultimate Shear Strength:**

$$\tau_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_{shear}} = \dfrac{7600 \text{ N}}{319.2 \text{ mm}^2} = 23.81 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Work to Failure (triangular approximation):**

The work to failure is the area under the load–displacement curve. For a triangular area:

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} \times P_{max} \times \Delta L_{final}$$

$$W = \dfrac{1}{2} \times 7600 \text{ N} \times 1.2 \text{ mm} = 4560 \text{ N·mm} = 4.56 \text{ J}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) A_shear = **319.2 mm²**
- (ii) τ_u ≈ **23.8 MPa**
- (iii) W ≈ **4.56 J**

> [!NOTE]
> Strain energy per unit volume in shear: U = W / V_plug. The plug volume V_plug = A_punch × t = (π/4)(25.4)² × 4 = 2026.8 mm³. So U = 4560 / 2026.8 = 2.25 mJ/mm³ = 2.25 MJ/m³. This is the modulus of resilience in shear. [optional discussion line]

[7 marks: each sub-part 2 marks, units 1 mark]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Compression & Shear Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Sign convention confusion:** Students often report compressive strain as negative, but the KTU valuation key awards full marks only when **compressive stress and strain are reported as positive** magnitudes.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units:** Load in N and area in mm² gives stress in **MPa directly** (1 N/mm² = 1 MPa). Many students incorrectly convert to Pa and then forget to convert back to MPa, losing 1 mark.
>
> 3. **Shear area in ASTM D732 is the *lateral cylindrical* area, not the *cross-sectional* area of the punch.** A frequent error is to use A = π/4 × d², which is wrong for the punch-tool test. The correct formula is A = π × d_punch × t.
>
> 4. **Mixing compressive and tensile moduli:** E_c ≈ E_t only for *isotropic* polymers at small strains. For *fiber-reinforced polymers* (composites), E_c may differ significantly from E_t, and the simple E = 2G(1+ν) relation does **not** hold.
>
> 5. **Not stating the standard test method:** A 14-mark answer that omits the ASTM/ISO standard cited loses 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.
>
> 6. **Drawing the σ–ε curve without axes labels and units:** Always label x-axis as "Strain (mm/mm)" and y-axis as "Stress (MPa)" with values at key points marked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision points — Compression & Shear (Module 1)**
>
> ☐ **Master equation:** E = 2G(1+ν) — must be memorized; derives from pure shear principal stress analysis.
>
> ☐ **Compressive stress:** σ_c = F/A₀; **compressive strain:** ε_c = ΔL/L₀; **compressive modulus:** E_c = slope of initial linear σ–ε region.
>
> ☐ **0.2% offset yield strength** is used for ductile polymers that yield gradually; **fracture stress** is used for brittle polymers.
>
> ☐ **For most polymers, σ_c,u > σ_t,u (brittle) or σ_c,u ≈ σ_t,u (ductile)**; the difference arises from hydrostatic stress and crack-closure effects in compression.
>
> ☐ **Shear stress:** τ = F/A; **shear strain:** γ = tan θ; **shear modulus:** G = τ/γ.
>
> ☐ **Bulk modulus** K = E/[3(1−2ν)]; relates to hydrostatic (equal triaxial) compression.
>
> ☐ **Bounds on Poisson's ratio:** −1 < ν < 0.5; for stable isotropic polymers in service, 0.2 ≤ ν ≤ 0.45.
>
> ☐ **Standard test methods:** ASTM D695 (compression), ISO 604 (compression), ASTM D732 (punch shear), ASTM D5379 (V-notched beam shear), torsion test for G.
>
> ☐ **ASTM D732 shear area** is **lateral cylindrical**: A_shear = π × d_punch × t, **not** π/4 × d².
>
> ☐ **Tresca (max shear stress) criterion** for combined loading; **von Mises (distortion energy) criterion** for ductile polymer yield.
>
> ☐ **For pure shear under von Mises:** τ_y = σ_y/√3 ≈ 0.577 σ_y.
>
> ☐ **Elastomers** have ν ≈ 0.5, K → ∞ (incompressible), and exhibit J-shaped σ–ε curves.
>
> ☐ **Compressive strength increases** with decreasing slenderness ratio (h/d); standard specimen uses h/d = 2 to suppress buckling and approach pure compression.
>
> ☐ **Energy absorption in compression** is a critical design metric for foams, packaging, and crash-energy-management structures.
>
> ☐ **Platen lubrication** (PTFE + grease) is required in ASTM D695 to obtain true uniaxial compressive properties of ductile polymers.
>
> ☐ **Compression set** (permanent deformation after prolonged loading and unloading) is a key long-term property, distinct from instantaneous E_c and σ_c,u.
>
> ☐ **Strain rate sensitivity:** Polymers are viscoelastic — E_c and σ_c,u increase with strain rate. Always report test speed in KTU answers.
>
> ☐ **Temperature effect:** T_g (glass transition temperature) is the inflection point — below T_g the polymer is glassy and stiff, above T_g it is rubbery and compliant. Both compressive and shear moduli drop dramatically near T_g.
>
> ☐ **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the σ–ε (or τ–γ) curve when describing mechanical behavior — it earns 2 marks easily and demonstrates full conceptual understanding.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effect of Cyclic Loading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Effect of Cyclic Loading ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Cyclic Loading** refers to the application of a fluctuating, repetitive, or time-varying stress (or strain) on a polymer component over an extended period. In the context of polymer product design and testing, the **Effect of Cyclic Loading** describes the progressive, accumulated, and often localized structural damage that occurs in a polymeric material when subjected to such dynamic loading conditions below its ultimate static strength, ultimately leading to **fatigue failure**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > Cyclic loading induces **fatigue** in polymers — a process of progressive degradation of mechanical properties resulting in fracture after a finite number of cycles (N) at stress levels considerably lower than the static (monotonic) failure stress. Polymers exhibit a **viscoelastic** nature, meaning their response to cyclic loading is a hybrid of elastic solid and viscous fluid behavior. Consequently, the effect of cyclic loading on polymers is markedly different from that on metals and is governed by: - **Stress amplitude** ($\sigma_a$) - **Mean stress** ($\sigma_m$) - **Frequency** ($f$) - **Loading mode** (tension-tension, tension-compression, flexural, torsion) - **Environmental conditions** (temperature, humidity, chemical exposure) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Paper Clip Test > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box:** Imagine repeatedly bending a **paper clip** back and forth by even a small angle. Each bend is well below the force needed to snap it in a single pull. Yet, after perhaps 20–30 cycles, the wire gets hot at the bend, develops a tiny crack, and suddenly fractures. That visible crack initiation, the localized heating, and the eventual sudden break is precisely what happens inside a polymer gear, hinge, or living hinge under cyclic loading. For polymers, the analogy is even more dramatic because: 1. The **self-heating** (hysteretic heating) is much greater due to viscoelastic damping. 2. The fatigue strength is **frequency-dependent** — higher frequency = more heating = faster failure. 3. Failure often occurs via **internal heating** rather than just crack propagation. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | Fatigue Strength (Endurance Limit) | $\sigma_e$ | **MPa** | | Number of Cycles to Failure | $N_f$ | dimensionless cycles | | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a$ | **MPa** | | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m$ | **MPa** | | Stress Ratio | $R$ | dimensionless | | Test Frequency | $f$ | **Hz** | | Hysteretic Heat per Cycle | $W$ | **J/m³/cycle** | > [!NOTE] > **Key Board Distinction:** Unlike many metals, **most polymers do NOT possess a true endurance limit**. Their S-N curve continues to decline, meaning *every* cycle causes some damage, even at very low stresses. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The Five-Stage Damage Process When a polymer is subjected to cyclic loading, the damage evolves through these well-defined stages: - **Stage 1 — Viscoelastic Hysteresis Heating:** Each loading-unloading cycle dissipates energy as heat due to the polymer's internal friction between molecular chains. The energy lost per cycle is the area of the **hysteresis loop** in the stress-strain diagram. - **Stage 2 — Thermal Softening:** If heat generation exceeds heat dissipation (convection + conduction), the bulk temperature rises. Since polymer modulus decreases with temperature, the specimen gradually softens — accelerating damage. - **Stage 3 — Crazing and Microvoid Formation:** In glassy polymers (PS, PMMA, PC), tensile cycles initiate **crazes** — microcrack-like regions spanned by drawn fibrils. These are the precursors to fatigue cracks. - **Stage 4 — Crack Initiation:** Microvoids coalesce at stress concentrators (notches, fillers, surface scratches) forming a dominant fatigue crack of size $a_i$. - **Stage 5 — Crack Propagation to Fracture:** The crack grows according to the **Paris Law** until it reaches the critical size $a_c$, at which point catastrophic fracture occurs. ### 2.2 Why Polymers Behave Differently from Metals - **Energy dissipation mechanism:** Metals dissipate energy through plastic deformation (dislocation motion); polymers dissipate through chain segment rotation, slippage, and viscoelastic flow. - **Frequency sensitivity:** Metals are largely frequency-independent; polymers are strongly frequency-dependent. - **Temperature coupling:** Polymer fatigue is **strongly coupled to temperature rise**, creating a thermal-mechanical feedback loop absent in metals. - **Crazing vs. slip bands:** Polymers form crazes; metals form persistent slip bands. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM MANDATE:** The following equations constitute the core quantitative toolkit expected in the KTU 2024 ESE for Module 1. Master each one. | # | Equation / Concept | LaTeX Form | Boundary / Validity | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stress Amplitude | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ | Always defined | | 2 | Mean Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2}$ | Always defined | | 3 | Stress Ratio | $R = \dfrac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}}$ | $R = -1$ (fully reversed), $R = 0$ (pulsating) | | 4 | Basquin's Law (High-Cycle Fatigue) | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$ | $10^3 < N_f < 10^6$ for polymers | | 5 | Coffin–Manson (Low-Cycle Fatigue) | $\dfrac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' \cdot (2N_f)^c$ | $N_f < 10^3$ | | 6 | Paris Law (Crack Growth) | $\dfrac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m$ | Stage II propagation | | 7 | Goodman Relation | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_{uts}} = 1$ | Brittle polymers | | 8 | Soderberg Relation | $\dfrac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_e} + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\sigma_y} = 1$ | Ductile polymers | | 9 | Hysteresis Energy per Cycle | $W = \oint \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Area of hysteresis loop | | 10 | Steady-State Temperature Rise | $\Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{W \cdot f}{h \cdot (A/V)}$ | Adiabatic to steady-state | | 11 | Fatigue Notch Sensitivity | $q = \dfrac{K_f - 1}{K_t - 1}$ | $0 \le q \le 1$ | | 12 | Miner's Cumulative Damage | $D = \sum \dfrac{n_i}{N_i}$ | Failure when $D \ge 1$ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all written answers, never use the absolute value bar `|x|` inside inline math without escaping it as `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`, especially when constructing tables — Markdown interprets unescaped pipes as column separators. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Understanding cyclic loading effects is essential for designing: - **Polymer gears** in automotive and home appliances (millions of meshing cycles). - **Living hinges** in polypropylene containers (one-piece molded flexures). - **Heart valve leaflets** made from segmented polyurethane (billions of flex cycles over a human lifetime). - **Tyre components** (natural rubber, SBR) — high-frequency, high-strain cyclic loading. - **Vibration isolators** and **elastomeric bushings** in suspension systems. - **Surgical sutures and stents** in biomedical implants. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stress Amplitude and Mean Stress from Boundary Values **Problem Setup:** A polymer specimen experiences a maximum stress of +45 MPa and a minimum stress of -15 MPa during each cycle. Compute $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$, and $R$. $$ \begin{aligned} \sigma_{\max} &= +45 \text{ MPa} \\ \sigma_{\min} &= -15 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1 — Stress Amplitude:** This is half the algebraic range of the stress cycle. $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} $$ $$ \sigma_a = \frac{45 - (-15)}{2} = \frac{45 + 15}{2} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Mean Stress:** This is the arithmetic midpoint of the cycle. $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} $$ $$ \sigma_m = \frac{45 + (-15)}{2} = \frac{30}{2} = 15 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 3 — Stress Ratio:** A negative ratio indicates a fully or partially reversed loading cycle. $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{-15}{45} = -\frac{1}{3} \approx -0.333 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Since $R < 0$, this is a **reversed (tension-compression) cycle**, which is the most damaging mode for polymers because both crazing (in tension) and microbuckling (in compression) operate. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Basquin's Law for Polymer Fatigue Life **Starting Premise:** In the high-cycle fatigue regime (where deformation is essentially elastic), the relationship between stress amplitude and cycles to failure for a polymer follows a power law on log-log coordinates. $$ \sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b $$ where: - $\sigma_f'$ = fatigue strength coefficient (≈ monotonic tensile strength) - $b$ = fatigue strength exponent (negative, typically $-0.05$ to $-0.15$ for polymers) - $2N_f$ = reversals to failure **Step 1 — Linearize by Taking Logarithm (Base 10):** $$ \log(\sigma_a) = \log(\sigma_f') + b \cdot \log(2N_f) $$ This is the equation of a straight line: $y = c + bx$, where $y = \log(\sigma_a)$ and $x = \log(2N_f)$. **Step 2 — Determine Constants from Two Experimental Points:** Suppose a polymer test yields: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Point 1: } & N_{f1} = 10^3 \text{ cycles}, \quad \sigma_{a1} = 60 \text{ MPa} \\ \text{Point 2: } & N_{f2} = 10^6 \text{ cycles}, \quad \sigma_{a2} = 30 \text{ MPa} \end{aligned} $$ Compute slope: $$ b = \frac{\log(\sigma_{a2}) - \log(\sigma_{a1})}{\log(2N_{f2}) - \log(2N_{f1})} $$ $$ b = \frac{\log(30) - \log(60)}{\log(2 \times 10^6) - \log(2 \times 10^3)} $$ $$ b = \frac{1.4771 - 1.7782}{6.3010 - 3.3010} = \frac{-0.3011}{3.0000} = -0.1004 $$ **Step 3 — Compute Fatigue Strength Coefficient $\sigma_f'$:** $$ \sigma_f' = \frac{\sigma_{a1}}{(2N_{f1})^b} = \frac{60}{(2000)^{-0.1004}} $$ $$ (2000)^{-0.1004} = 10^{-0.1004 \times \log(2000)} = 10^{-0.1004 \times 3.3010} = 10^{-0.3314} = 0.4662 $$ $$ \sigma_f' = \frac{60}{0.4662} \approx 128.7 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 4 — Verify Using Point 2:** $$ \sigma_{a2} = 128.7 \times (2 \times 10^6)^{-0.1004} $$ $$ (2 \times 10^6)^{-0.1004} = 10^{-0.1004 \times 6.3010} = 10^{-0.6326} = 0.2328 $$ $$ \sigma_{a2} = 128.7 \times 0.2328 \approx 29.96 \text{ MPa} \approx 30 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark $$ **Step 5 — Predict Life at $\sigma_a = 45$ MPa:** $$ N_f = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{\sigma_a}{\sigma_f'} \right)^{1/b} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{45}{128.7} \right)^{1/(-0.1004)} $$ $$ \frac{45}{128.7} = 0.3497 $$ $$ 0.3497^{-9.960} = 10^{-9.960 \times \log(0.3497)} = 10^{-9.960 \times (-0.4564)} = 10^{4.546} = 35,170 $$ $$ N_f = \frac{35,170}{2} \approx 17,585 \text{ cycles} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Temperature Rise Due to Hysteretic Heating **Starting Premise:** In a polymer under cyclic loading at frequency $f$, each unit volume dissipates energy $W$ (J/m³) per cycle. The steady-state temperature rise occurs when heat generation equals heat loss to the surroundings. **Step 1 — Volumetric Heat Generation Rate:** $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = W \cdot f \quad \left[ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^3} \right] $$ **Step 2 — Volumetric Heat Loss Rate (Convective Cooling):** $$ \dot{Q}_{loss} = h \cdot \frac{A}{V} \cdot \Delta T \quad \left[ \frac{\text{W}}{\text{m}^3} \right] $$ where $h$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $A/V$ = surface-to-volume ratio (m⁻¹). **Step 3 — Apply Steady-State Energy Balance:** $$ W \cdot f = h \cdot \frac{A}{V} \cdot \Delta T_{ss} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\Delta T_{ss}$:** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{W \cdot f}{h \cdot (A/V)} $$ **Numerical Example:** $$ \begin{aligned} W &= 0.50 \text{ MJ/m}^3\text{/cycle} = 5.0 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^3\text{/cycle} \\ f &= 10 \text{ Hz} \\ h &= 25 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \\ A/V &= 200 \text{ m}^{-1} \text{ (typical for a tensile dumbbell)} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = 5.0 \times 10^5 \times 10 = 5.0 \times 10^6 \text{ W/m}^3 $$ $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{25 \times 200} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^6}{5000} = 1000 \text{ K} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Physical Inconsistency Note:** A 1000 K rise is physically impossible for a polymer — it would have melted or decomposed long before. This indicates that **the test is no longer in steady state**: the polymer is in a **runaway thermal failure** mode. In practice, designers must choose operating frequencies such that $\Delta T_{ss} \le T_g - T_{amb}$ for glassy polymers, or $T_m - T_{amb}$ for semi-crystalline polymers. **Corrected Realistic Example:** If $\Delta T_{ss,max} = 30$ K is permissible, solve for the maximum safe frequency: $$ f_{max} = \frac{h \cdot (A/V) \cdot \Delta T_{ss,max}}{W} = \frac{25 \times 200 \times 30}{5.0 \times 10^5} = \frac{150,000}{500,000} = 0.30 \text{ Hz} $$ This dramatic frequency reduction is why polymer fatigue tests must always report the test frequency. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Miner's Rule for Variable-Amplitude Loading **Starting Premise:** A polymer component operates under a two-step load spectrum: - Stage A: $n_A = 5000$ cycles at $\sigma_{a,A} = 50$ MPa - Stage B: $n_B = 20,000$ cycles at $\sigma_{a,B} = 35$ MPa The S-N relation is $\sigma_a = 128.7 \times (2N_f)^{-0.1004}$ MPa. **Step 1 — Compute Damage per Cycle:** $$ D = \sum_i \frac{n_i}{N_{f,i}} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $N_{f,A}$:** $$ 50 = 128.7 \times (2N_{f,A})^{-0.1004} $$ $$ (2N_{f,A})^{-0.1004} = \frac{50}{128.7} = 0.3885 $$ $$ -0.1004 \cdot \log(2N_{f,A}) = \log(0.3885) = -0.4105 $$ $$ \log(2N_{f,A}) = \frac{-0.4105}{-0.1004} = 4.089 $$ $$ 2N_{f,A} = 10^{4.089} = 12,270 \Rightarrow N_{f,A} = 6135 \text{ cycles} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $N_{f,B}$:** $$ 35 = 128.7 \times (2N_{f,B})^{-0.1004} $$ $$ (2N_{f,B})^{-0.1004} = \frac{35}{128.7} = 0.2720 $$ $$ -0.1004 \cdot \log(2N_{f,B}) = \log(0.2720) = -0.5650 $$ $$ \log(2N_{f,B}) = 5.628 \Rightarrow 2N_{f,B} = 10^{5.628} = 42,500 $$ $$ N_{f,B} = 21,250 \text{ cycles} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Cumulative Damage:** $$ D = \frac{n_A}{N_{f,A}} + \frac{n_B}{N_{f,B}} = \frac{5000}{6135} + \frac{20{,}000}{21{,}250} $$ $$ D = 0.815 + 0.941 = 1.756 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** Since $D = 1.756 > 1$, the component will **fail during Stage B**, after approximately $20{,}000 \times (1 - 0.815)/(0.941) = 20{,}000 \times 0.197 \approx 3{,}940$ additional cycles in Stage B. (For precise block-by-block tracking, Miner's rule is applied incrementally.) ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Polymer Fatigue Damage Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["CYCLIC LOAD APPLIED"] --> B["VISCOELASTIC RESPONSE"] B --> C["HYSTERESIS LOOP FORMS"] C --> D["MECHANICAL ENERGY DISSIPATED"] D --> E1["HEAT GENERATION"] D --> E2["MICROVOID FORMATION"] E1 --> F["TEMPERATURE RISES"] F --> G["MODULUS DECREASES"] G --> H["INCREASED STRAIN PER CYCLE"] H --> C E2 --> I["CRAZE INITIATION"] I --> J["CRACK NUCLEATION"] J --> K["PARIS LAW CRACK GROWTH"] K --> L["CRITICAL CRACK SIZE"] L --> M["CATASTROPHIC FRACTURE"] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic — Types of Stress Cycles ```mermaid flowchart LR T1["FULLY REVERSED
R EQUALS MINUS ONE"] --> S1["STRESS AMPLITUDE EQUALS PEAK
MEAN STRESS EQUALS ZERO
MOST DAMAGING"] T2["PULSATING TENSION
R EQUALS ZERO"] --> S2["STRESS RANGES FROM ZERO TO MAX
MEAN STRESS POSITIVE
COMMON IN GEAR TOOTH"] T3["PULSATING COMPRESSION
R EQUALS INFINITY"] --> S3["STRESS RANGES FROM MIN TO ZERO
LESS DAMAGING"] T4["ARBITRARY CYCLE
R BETWEEN MINUS ONE AND PLUS ONE"] --> S4["GENERAL ENGINEERING LOADING
GOODMAN OR SODERBERG APPLIED"] ``` ### 4.3 Schematic — Hysteresis Loop Geometry in Stress-Strain Coordinates ```mermaid flowchart TD P["STRESS STRAIN LOOP KEY POINTS"] --> Q1["POINT A: MAXIMUM STRESS
AND MAXIMUM STRAIN"] P --> Q2["POINT B: UNLOADING
STRAIN LAGS BEHIND STRESS"] P --> Q3["POINT C: MINIMUM STRESS
AND MINIMUM STRAIN"] P --> Q4["POINT D: RELOADING
CLOSES THE LOOP"] Q1 --> Q2 Q2 --> Q3 Q3 --> Q4 Q4 --> Q1 R["AREA ENCLOSED BY LOOP ABCD
EQUALS HYSTERESIS ENERGY W
PER UNIT VOLUME PER CYCLE"] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Influence Map of Design and Environmental Factors ```mermaid flowchart TD F["FATIGUE LIFE OF POLYMER"] --> F1["LOAD FACTORS"] F --> F2["MATERIAL FACTORS"] F --> F3["ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS"] F --> F4["GEOMETRIC FACTORS"] F1 --> F1A["STRESS AMPLITUDE"] F1 --> F1B["MEAN STRESS"] F1 --> F1C["FREQUENCY"] F1 --> F1D["LOADING MODE"] F2 --> F2A["MOLECULAR WEIGHT"] F2 --> F2B["DEGREE OF CRYSTALLINITY"] F2 --> F2C["FILLER CONTENT"] F2 --> F2D["CROSS LINK DENSITY"] F3 --> F3A["AMBIENT TEMPERATURE"] F3 --> F3B["HUMIDITY"] F3 --> F3C["CHEMICAL EXPOSURE"] F3 --> F3D["UV RADIATION"] F4 --> F4A["NOTCHES"] F4 --> F4B["SURFACE ROUGHNESS"] F4 --> F4C["WALL THICKNESS"] F4 --> F4D["GATE LOCATION"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` > **Define cyclic loading. Explain why polymers behave differently from metals under cyclic loading.** **Model Answer:** **Cyclic Loading:** Cyclic loading is the application of a fluctuating stress or strain on a material over time, characterized by parameters such as stress amplitude, mean stress, and frequency. **Why Polymers Differ from Metals:** | Aspect | Metals | Polymers | |---|---|---| | Energy dissipation mechanism | Plastic deformation via dislocations | Viscoelastic chain segment motion | | Frequency sensitivity | Negligible | Strong — heating depends on $f$ | | Endurance limit | Many steels show one | Almost no polymer shows one | | Damage form | Persistent slip bands, intrusions/extrusions | Crazing, microvoids, self-heating | | Temperature coupling | Weak | Strong thermal-mechanical feedback loop | **Key Differences (Memorize 3 Points):** 1. Polymers exhibit **strong frequency dependence** because their viscoelastic damping converts mechanical work to heat. 2. Polymers generally do **not** exhibit a true endurance limit — fatigue strength continues to drop with cycles. 3. The dominant damage mode in polymers is **hysteretic self-heating**, not just mechanical crack propagation as in metals. *(Self-contained, 3-mark depth.)* --- > **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` > **With the help of neat sketches, explain the meaning of stress amplitude and mean stress in cyclic loading.** **Model Answer:** Given a cyclic stress history with maximum stress $\sigma_{\max}$ and minimum stress $\sigma_{\min}$: **Stress Amplitude ($\sigma_a$):** The half-range of the stress cycle, representing the alternating (reversing) component. $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} $$ **Mean Stress ($\sigma_m$):** The arithmetic midpoint of the stress cycle, representing the static (DC) offset. $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} $$ **Stress Ratio ($R$):** $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}} $$ **Sketches (Verbal Description for Answer Sheet):** - Draw sinusoidal stress-time curves for **three cases**: (i) $R = -1$ (fully reversed), (ii) $R = 0$ (pulsating tension), (iii) $R = +1$ (static). - On each, mark $\sigma_{\max}$, $\sigma_{\min}$, draw horizontal line at $\sigma_m$, and double-arrow showing $\sigma_a$ above and below. *(Self-contained, 3-mark depth.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Question B.A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Explain the various stages of fatigue damage in a polymer subjected to cyclic loading. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A polymer component is subjected to cyclic loading between +60 MPa and +10 MPa. Using Basquin's law with $\sigma_f' = 150$ MPa and $b = -0.10$, determine the fatigue life $N_f$. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B.A(a) — 7 Marks The fatigue damage in polymers evolves through **five distinct stages**: **Stage 1: Viscoelastic Energy Dissipation (1 mark)** Under cyclic loading, the polymer's stress-strain response traces a closed loop called the **hysteresis loop**. The area enclosed equals the energy dissipated as heat per cycle per unit volume. This is unique to viscoelastic materials. **Stage 2: Thermal Softening (1 mark)** The dissipated heat raises the polymer's temperature. Since modulus $E$ decreases with temperature (especially as $T$ approaches $T_g$ for glassy polymers), the strain per cycle gradually increases — a positive feedback loop. **Stage 3: Crazing and Microvoid Formation (1 mark)** In glassy polymers, tensile half-cycles generate **crazes** — planar defects bridged by drawn fibrils. In semi-crystalline polymers, **microvoids** form between spherulites at the boundaries. **Stage 4: Crack Initiation (2 marks)** Microvoids and crazes coalesce at sites of stress concentration (notches, fillers, surface flaws). A dominant fatigue crack of initial size $a_i$ forms. Crack initiation accounts for ~90% of total fatigue life in most polymers. **Stage 5: Crack Propagation to Fracture (2 marks)** The crack grows per the **Paris Law**: $$ \frac{da}{dN} = C \cdot (\Delta K)^m $$ where $\Delta K = K_{\max} - K_{\min}$ is the stress intensity factor range. When $a$ reaches $a_c$, final fracture occurs. --- #### Model Solution for Question B.A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Compute $\sigma_a$ and $\sigma_m$ (1 mark):** $$ \sigma_a = \frac{\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{60 - 10}{2} = 25 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min}}{2} = \frac{60 + 10}{2} = 35 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Note Stress Ratio (1 mark):** $$ R = \frac{\sigma_{\min}}{\sigma_{\max}} = \frac{10}{60} = 0.167 $$ Since $0 < R < 1$, this is a **pulsating tensile** cycle with a positive mean stress — moderately damaging. **Step 3 — Apply Basquin's Law (2 marks):** $$ \sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b $$ $$ 25 = 150 \cdot (2N_f)^{-0.10} $$ $$ (2N_f)^{-0.10} = \frac{25}{150} = 0.1667 $$ $$ -0.10 \cdot \log(2N_f) = \log(0.1667) = -0.7782 $$ $$ \log(2N_f) = \frac{-0.7782}{-0.10} = 7.782 $$ $$ 2N_f = 10^{7.782} = 6.06 \times 10^7 $$ $$ \boxed{N_f = 3.03 \times 10^7 \text{ cycles}} $$ **Step 4 — Comment (2 marks):** A fatigue life of $\approx 30$ million cycles is in the **very high cycle fatigue (VHCF)** regime. For polymers, this is exceptional and suggests: - The stress amplitude is low enough to avoid runaway thermal failure. - The component would likely be tested at lower frequency (e.g., 1–5 Hz) to maintain isothermal conditions. - Real-world variable amplitude and environmental effects would reduce this life significantly. --- > **Question B.B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 / Model Paper]` **(a)** Discuss the factors affecting the fatigue behavior of polymers with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** During a polymer fatigue test, the hysteresis energy loss per cycle is $W = 0.4$ MJ/m³ at a frequency of 5 Hz. The specimen has surface-to-volume ratio $A/V = 250$ m⁻¹ and the convective heat transfer coefficient is $h = 30$ W/m²·K. Calculate the steady-state temperature rise. Discuss what happens if the test frequency is increased to 20 Hz. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B.B(a) — 7 Marks **Major factors affecting fatigue behavior of polymers:** **1. Stress Amplitude and Mean Stress (2 marks)** - Higher $\sigma_a$ → shorter life (Basquin's law). - Higher $\sigma_m$ (tensile) → shorter life due to acceleration of crazing. - Example: Polypropylene hinges fail much faster under fully reversed ($R = -1$) loading than under pulsating ($R = 0$) loading at the same amplitude. **2. Frequency of Loading (2 marks)** - Higher $f$ → more heat generated per unit time → higher $\Delta T$ → faster thermal softening. - Example: Nylon gears rated for 1 Hz operation may fail within hours at 50 Hz even at identical stress amplitude. **3. Temperature (1 mark)** - As ambient temperature $T \rightarrow T_g$ (for glassy polymers) or $T \rightarrow T_m$ (for semi-crystalline), fatigue strength drops dramatically. - Example: PMMA fatigue strength at 50°C is approximately 50% of its room temperature value. **4. Molecular Structure (1 mark)** - Higher molecular weight → longer fatigue life (more chain entanglements to resist disentanglement). - Cross-linking improves fatigue resistance (e.g., vulcanized rubber vs. uncured). - Crystallinity improves fatigue resistance in semi-crystalline polymers (e.g., HDPE > LDPE). **5. Environmental and Geometric Factors (1 mark)** - Humidity (e.g., Nylon absorbs moisture, lowering $T_g$ and reducing fatigue life). - Chemical exposure (stress cracking agents like detergents in PC). - Surface scratches, weld lines, and gates act as stress concentrators. - Example: Notched polystyrene specimens show fatigue strength ~40% lower than unnotched. --- #### Model Solution for Question B.B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Write Energy Balance Equation (1 mark):** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{W \cdot f}{h \cdot (A/V)} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values at $f = 5$ Hz (2 marks):** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{(0.4 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^3) \times 5 \text{ s}^{-1}}{30 \text{ W/m}^2\text{·K} \times 250 \text{ m}^{-1}} $$ $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{7500} = 266.7 \text{ K} $$ **Step 3 — Physical Interpretation at 5 Hz (1 mark):** A 266.7 K rise is well above the melting/decomposition temperature of most polymers. Therefore, the polymer has **not reached true steady state** — it is undergoing **thermal runaway failure** even at 5 Hz. **Step 4 — Recompute at $f = 20$ Hz (2 marks):** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{(0.4 \times 10^6) \times 20}{7500} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^6}{7500} = 1066.7 \text{ K} $$ **Step 5 — Discussion (1 mark):** Quadrupling the frequency quadruples the heat generation rate. At 20 Hz, the situation is far worse — the polymer would degrade chemically (oxidation, chain scission) within seconds, well before reaching any mechanical fatigue life. **Conclusion:** For this polymer, fatigue testing must be done either at very low frequency (≤ 1 Hz) or with active cooling to maintain isothermal conditions. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Cyclic Loading Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing stress amplitude with maximum stress.** Examiners strictly award partial credit only if $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min})/2$ is correctly identified. Writing $\sigma_a = \sigma_{\max}$ loses **1–2 marks** instantly. > > 2. **Ignoring the stress ratio $R$ in Basquin's law applications.** Basquin's law assumes zero mean stress. If $\sigma_m \neq 0$, students must first apply Goodman/Soderberg to find an equivalent fully-reversed stress amplitude before using Basquin. Skipping this loses **2–3 marks**. > > 3. **Not specifying units** for $N_f$ (cycles) and $\sigma_a$ (MPa). KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing units. > > 4. **Forgetting that polymers have no true endurance limit.** Writing "polymer fails after infinite cycles" or "polymer has an endurance limit like steel" is a **factual error** worth 1 mark deduction. > > 5. **In hysteresis calculations, treating the loop area as zero for purely elastic materials.** Polymers always show hysteresis; metals only do so past yield. Conflating these loses **1 mark**. > > 6. **In Miner's rule problems, forgetting that $D \ge 1$ predicts failure during a block, not at the end.** Always state explicitly which block and approximately which cycle failure occurs. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Effect of Cyclic Loading** **Core Concepts to Memorize:** - ☐ Cyclic loading = time-varying stress, characterized by $\sigma_a$, $\sigma_m$, $R$. - ☐ Fatigue = progressive damage at stresses below static strength. - ☐ Polymers are **viscoelastic** → unique cyclic response different from metals. **The Five Stages of Polymer Fatigue:** - ☐ Hysteresis heating → Thermal softening → Crazing → Crack initiation → Crack propagation. **Essential Equations (must solve on sight):** - ☐ $\sigma_a = (\sigma_{\max} - \sigma_{\min})/2$ - ☐ $\sigma_m = (\sigma_{\max} + \sigma_{\min})/2$ - ☐ $R = \sigma_{\min}/\sigma_{\max}$ - ☐ Basquin: $\sigma_a = \sigma_f' (2N_f)^b$ - ☐ Paris Law: $da/dN = C(\Delta K)^m$ - ☐ Goodman: $\sigma_a/\sigma_e + \sigma_m/\sigma_{uts} = 1$ - ☐ Steady-state temperature rise: $\Delta T_{ss} = Wf/(h \cdot A/V)$ - ☐ Miner's rule: $D = \sum n_i/N_i \ge 1$ means failure. **Key Differences from Metals (frequent ESE question):** - ☐ Polymers show **frequency-dependent** fatigue (metals do not). - ☐ Polymers have **no true endurance limit** (some steels do). - ☐ Polymer fatigue is dominated by **thermal-mechanical coupling**. **Practical Design Wisdom:** - ☐ Design polymer components to operate at the **lowest feasible frequency**. - ☐ Avoid sharp corners — use generous fillets to reduce stress concentration. - ☐ For dynamic seals, gears, and hinges, always consult manufacturer's **S-N data at the intended frequency**. **One-Line Memory Hook:** > *"Viscoelastic polymers eat cycles for breakfast — every cycle heats, softens, and slowly kills them; metals fatigue by cracking, polymers fatigue by heating."*

topics.title: Structural Design of Products Under Static and Dynamic Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Structural Design of Products Under Static and Dynamic Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Structural Design of Products Under Static and Dynamic Loads** is the engineering discipline concerned with proportioning the geometry, selecting the material, and determining the dimensions of a load-bearing polymer component such that it can safely transmit, resist, and absorb applied forces **without experiencing failure, excessive deformation, or loss of function** over its intended service life.

In the context of **Polymer Product Design**, this discipline is fundamentally more complex than its metallic counterpart because polymers are **viscoelastic materials** — their mechanical response is simultaneously:

- **Elastic** (instantaneous, recoverable, spring-like)
- **Viscous** (time-dependent, rate-sensitive, fluid-like)

Therefore, the response of a polymer to a given load depends not only on the **magnitude** of the load, but also on the **time of application**, the **rate of loading**, the **temperature**, and the **environmental history** of the material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Examinable):**
> *"Static loads are those whose magnitude, direction, and point of application remain constant with time, whereas dynamic loads vary with time and may be classified as cyclic (fatigue), impact (single high-rate pulse), or random (vibration). The design of polymer products must account for time-dependent viscoelastic phenomena such as creep, stress relaxation, and fatigue that do not exist in classical metallic design."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Slow-Motion Honey" Model

Imagine stretching two identical rubber bands horizontally:

- **Rubber Band A (Elastic Analogy)**: Pull it slowly. It stretches a little and returns to its original length when released. This is how a **steel spring** behaves — the response is independent of how fast you pull.

- **Rubber Band B (Polymer Analogy)**: Pull it with the **same force** but **very slowly over 10 hours**. It stretches **much more** than Rubber Band A and may **never fully return** to its original length. Now, pull an identical band **instantly** with a slightly higher force — it may **snap**.

The **same material** gives different responses based on **time and rate**. This is the essence of polymer structural design: a polymer does not have a single "strength" — it has an **entire family of strengths** indexed by time, temperature, and loading rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
> A plastic chair (polypropylene) supports a person sitting still (static load) for years with no problem. However, the **same chair**, when dropped from 1 meter (impact load), may fracture. A cable tie (nylon 6,6) holds a bundle tightly for months (**creep + stress relaxation**), yet can be snapped sharply (**impact**) with bare hands. **Identical polymer, different loading modes, dramatically different responses.**

---

## 1.3 Classification of Loads in Polymer Product Design

| Load Category | Definition | Typical Examples in Polymer Products | Time-Scale |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dead (Static) Load** | Constant force, no time variation | Self-weight of pipe, fluid pressure in a tank | Hours to Years |
| **Live (Static) Load** | Static but variable in magnitude between limits | Stack of books on a plastic shelf, water level in a bucket | Hours to Days |
| **Cyclic (Fatigue) Load** | Repeated stress cycles (tensile, compressive, or reversed) | Hinge of a flip phone, pedal of a bicycle, prosthetic limb joint | 10³ to 10⁷ cycles |
| **Impact Load** | Sudden, high-rate application of energy | Falling of a TV cabinet, car bumper crash, helmet strike | 10⁻³ to 10⁻¹ seconds |
| **Vibratory Load** | Sinusoidal or random oscillations | Dashboard in a car, washing machine agitator, pump impeller | 10¹ to 10⁵ Hz |
| **Creep Load** | Sustained constant load causing time-dependent deformation | Pipe carrying hot fluid, structural bracket, cable tie | Months to Decades |
| **Thermal Load** | Load generated by constrained thermal expansion | Polymer-metal joint in outdoor environment | Cyclic / Seasonal |

---

## 1.4 Why Polymer Design Differs from Metallic Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Critical Differences Every KTU Examiner Tests:**

1. **Time-Dependent Modulus**: For steel, $E = 210 \text{ GPa}$ (constant). For a polymer, the **effective modulus decreases with time** — a property described by the **creep modulus** $E_c(t)$.

2. **Strain-Rate Sensitivity**: Metals show minor rate effects; polymers may show a **5–10× change in yield strength** between slow and fast loading.

3. **Temperature Sensitivity**: A 10 °C rise can halve the creep life of a polymer. Polymers have a **Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)** above which the modulus drops by 2–3 orders of magnitude.

4. **Hysteresis and Damping**: Polymers dissipate energy as heat under cyclic loading, leading to **self-heating** and **thermal failure** — a failure mode that does not exist in metals.

---

## 1.5 Fundamental Design Objective

The designer's task is to ensure that for **every conceivable loading condition** experienced during the product's service life:

$$\sigma_{\text{applied}}(t, T, \dot{\varepsilon}, \text{environment}) \;\leq\; \frac{\sigma_{\text{failure}}(t, T, \dot{\varepsilon}, \text{environment})}{n}$$

where **n** is the **Factor of Safety**. The left side is what the load imposes; the right side is what the material can withstand. The challenge in polymers is that **both sides depend on time and temperature**.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Load Design — The Classical Foundation

### 2.1.1 Types of Static Stresses

A static load, although constant in time, can produce five fundamental stress states in a structural element:

**1. Tensile Stress (Normal Stress under Axial Pull)**

$$\sigma_t = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

where $F$ is the applied axial force and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area.

**2. Compressive Stress**

$$\sigma_c = \frac{F}{A_0} \quad (\text{magnitude only; sign convention negative})$$

**3. Shear Stress**

$$\tau = \frac{F_s}{A_s}$$

where $F_s$ is the tangential force and $A_s$ is the area parallel to the force.

**4. Bending (Flexural) Stress**

$$\sigma_b = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

where $M$ is the bending moment, $c$ is the distance from neutral axis, $I$ is the second moment of area, and $Z = I/c$ is the **section modulus**.

**5. Torsional (Shear due to Twist) Stress**

$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

where $T$ is the torque, $r$ is the radial distance, and $J$ is the polar moment of inertia.

### 2.1.2 Strain and Deformation

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad ; \quad \gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{y} \quad ; \quad \nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{transverse}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

where $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio** (typically **0.3–0.4** for most polymers; **0.5** for rubbers — meaning they are nearly **incompressible**).

### 2.1.3 The Generalized Hooke's Law for Isotropic Polymers

For a 3D stress state, the relationship between stress and strain in a linear elastic polymer is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} \quad ; \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G} \quad ; \quad \gamma_{xz} = \frac{\tau_{xz}}{G}$$

where the **shear modulus** $G$ is related to Young's modulus $E$ by:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often forget that the relationship $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ is valid **only for isotropic, linear-elastic materials**. Many polymers are **anisotropic** (especially fiber-reinforced composites) and require **5 or more independent elastic constants** described by the full **stiffness matrix $[C_{ij}]$**.

---

## 2.2 Failure Theories Applicable to Polymers (Static Design)

A designer must predict *when* a part will fail. The classical failure theories, originally derived for metals, are applied to polymers with **modifications**.

### 2.2.1 Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine's Theory)

Failure occurs when the maximum principal stress reaches the ultimate tensile strength.

$$\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{\text{uts}} \quad \text{AND} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq \sigma_{\text{ucs}}$$

where $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ are the **maximum and minimum principal stresses**, and $\sigma_{\text{uts}}$, $\sigma_{\text{ucs}}$ are the ultimate tensile and compressive strengths.

**Applicability to polymers:** Useful for **brittle polymers** (PS, PMMA, rigid PVC) that fail by **crazing and fracture** with little yielding.

### 2.2.2 Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca's Theory)

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} \leq \tau_{\text{yield}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{yield}}}{2}$$

**Applicability to polymers:** Best for **ductile polymers** (LDPE, PA, PP) that yield in shear.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises Criterion)

$$\sigma_{\text{vm}} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \sigma_{\text{yield}}$$

**Applicability to polymers:** Most accurate for **moderately ductile, isotropic polymers** undergoing multiaxial yielding.

### 2.2.4 Modified Failure Criteria for Polymers (KTU-Focused)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Polymers do not obey the constant-$\sigma_{\text{yield}}$ assumption. KTU examiners expect students to recognize that the **criterion is rate- and temperature-dependent**:

For a polymer under **multiaxial loading** at temperature $T$ and strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$:

$$\sigma_{\text{yield,eq}}(T, \dot{\varepsilon}) = \sigma_{y0} \cdot \left(\frac{\dot{\varepsilon}}{\dot{\varepsilon}_0}\right)^{m} \cdot \exp\left[\frac{Q}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $\sigma_{y0}$ = reference yield stress at reference rate $\dot{\varepsilon}_0$ and temperature $T_0$
- $m$ = **strain-rate sensitivity exponent** (typically 0.05–0.20 for polymers)
- $Q$ = **activation energy** for the yield process
- $R$ = universal gas constant

---

## 2.3 Stress Concentration — A Critical Concept for Polymers

Geometric discontinuities (holes, fillets, sharp corners, notches) cause local stress amplification. The **Stress Concentration Factor (SCT or $K_t$)** is defined as:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$$

For a plate of width $w$ containing a circular hole of diameter $d$ under uniaxial tension:

$$K_t \approx 3.00 - 3.13\left(\frac{d}{w}\right) + 3.66\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^2 - 1.53\left(\frac{d}{w}\right)^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Polymer-Specific Issue:** Polymers are *more* sensitive to stress concentrations than metals because of their **lower ductility** and **time-dependent crack growth**. The "notch sensitivity" of a polymer is often quantified by the **notch sensitivity factor $q$**, which relates $K_t$ (geometric) to $K_f$ (fatigue, the effective concentration factor):

$$K_f = 1 + q(K_t - 1) \quad ; \quad 0 \leq q \leq 1$$

- $q = 0$: material is **notch-insensitive** (rare in polymers)
- $q = 1$: material is **fully notch-sensitive** (typical for brittle polymers like PS)

---

## 2.4 Dynamic Load Design — The Time-Variable Domain

### 2.4.1 Creep — The Silent Killer of Polymer Products

**Creep** is the **time-dependent increase in strain** of a material under a **constant stress**. The classical creep curve for a polymer has **four regions**:

1. **Region I — Instantaneous Elastic Strain ($t = 0$)**: The polymer deforms immediately upon load application as if it were purely elastic.
2. **Region II — Primary (Transient) Creep**: Strain rate **decreases** with time; the material is hardening.
3. **Region III — Secondary (Steady-State) Creep**: Strain rate is **constant**; this is the longest region and dominates the design life.
4. **Region IV — Tertiary Creep**: Strain rate **accelerates**; microvoids coalesce; **rupture** is imminent.

The **Findley Power Law** is the most widely used empirical model in polymer design:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m \cdot t^n$$

where $\varepsilon_0$ is the **initial (time-independent) strain**, $m$ is the **creep coefficient**, and $n$ is the **creep exponent** (typically **0.1 to 0.5** for polymers).

The **creep modulus** (a time-dependent equivalent of Young's modulus) is defined as:

$$E_c(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon(t)} = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0 + m \cdot t^n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Design Practice:** When designing a plastic pipe that must carry a constant internal pressure for **20 years**, the engineer does **not** use the short-term $E$ from the datasheet. Instead, the designer uses the **20-year creep modulus** $E_c(20\text{ yr})$ and a **long-term strength** value called the **Hydrostatic Design Stress (HDS)**.

### 2.4.2 Stress Relaxation — The Hidden Dimension

**Stress relaxation** is the **time-dependent decrease in stress** under a **constant strain**. It is the complement of creep.

$$\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{t}{\tau}\right)$$

where $\tau$ is the **relaxation time** of the polymer.

**Design implication:** A pre-loaded plastic bolt in a joint **loses clamping force** over time due to stress relaxation. The designer must either:
- Use a **metal spring washer** (Belleville washer) to maintain load
- **Re-torque** periodically
- Use a polymer with **higher $T_g$** so that $T/T_g$ is lower (relaxation is slower)

### 2.4.3 Fatigue — Failure Under Cyclic Loading

**Fatigue** is the progressive, localized, permanent structural damage caused by **cyclic loading** at stress levels **below** the static ultimate strength.

The **S-N Curve (Wöhler Curve)** is the fundamental design tool:

- **S** = stress amplitude (or maximum stress)
- **N** = number of cycles to failure

For steels, there is a well-defined **endurance limit** (stress below which life is infinite). **For most polymers, NO endurance limit exists** — failure will eventually occur at any non-zero cyclic stress.

The **Basquin's Law** (high-cycle fatigue):

$$\sigma_a = \sigma_f' \cdot (2N_f)^b$$

where $\sigma_a$ is the stress amplitude, $\sigma_f'$ is the **fatigue strength coefficient**, $N_f$ is cycles to failure, and $b$ is the **fatigue strength exponent** (typically $-0.05$ to $-0.15$ for polymers).

The **Coffin-Manson Equation** (low-cycle, high-strain fatigue):

$$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon_p}{2} = \varepsilon_f' \cdot (2N_f)^c$$

where $\Delta\varepsilon_p / 2$ is the plastic strain amplitude, $\varepsilon_f'$ is the **fatigue ductility coefficient**, and $c$ is the **fatigue ductility exponent** (typically $-0.5$ to $-0.7$).

**Total strain-life equation (the unified form used in polymer fatigue design):**

$$\frac{\Delta \varepsilon}{2} = \frac{\sigma_f'}{E}(2N_f)^b + \varepsilon_f'(2N_f)^c$$

The first term dominates at **long lives** (elastic), the second at **short lives** (plastic).

### 2.4.4 Impact Loading — The High-Rate Domain

Impact loading is characterized by:
- Loading time: **milliseconds**
- Strain rate: **10² to 10⁴ s⁻¹**
- Stress response: **2–5× higher** than quasi-static strength

**Fracture mechanics approach:**

The **fracture toughness** $K_{Ic}$ describes a material's resistance to crack propagation. The condition for unstable fracture is:

$$K_I = Y \sigma \sqrt{\pi a} \geq K_{Ic}$$

where $K_I$ is the **stress intensity factor**, $Y$ is the geometry factor, $\sigma$ is the applied stress, and $a$ is the crack length.

For **ductile polymers** (PC, PA, POM), the fracture surface absorbs a lot of energy — high impact strength. For **brittle polymers** (PS, SAN, cured epoxy), cracks propagate with little energy absorption.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **notched Izod** and **notched Charpy** impact tests (per ASTM D256 and ISO 179) measure impact strength in **J/m** or **kJ/m²**. The notch is critical: it represents a real-world stress concentrator and **drastically reduces** the measured impact energy for notch-sensitive polymers.

---

## 2.5 The Master Curve Principle — Time-Temperature Superposition

Because polymers are viscoelastic, the **same effect** can be produced by either:
- Increasing the **time** under load at a fixed temperature, OR
- Increasing the **temperature** at a fixed loading time

This equivalence is the foundation of the **Time-Temperature Superposition Principle (TTSP)**, formalized by **Leadermann (1943)** and **Williams-Landel-Ferry (1955)**.

The **Williams-Landel-Ferry (WLF) equation** gives the horizontal shift factor $a_T$ needed to superpose curves at different temperatures:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_{\text{ref}})}{C_2 + (T - T_{\text{ref}})}$$

where **$C_1 = 17.44$** and **$C_2 = 51.6$ K** are universal constants **when $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$, these constants are universal for **all amorphous polymers**. The WLF equation is valid only for $T_g < T < T_g + 100 \text{ K}$. Above this range, the **Arrhenius equation** must be used.

The practical power of TTSP: a 10-year creep test can be performed in **a few hours** by testing at **elevated temperature** and shifting the data horizontally.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Case Studies Linking Theory to Practice (KTU 2024-Ready):**

1. **PVC Pressure Pipe (Static + Creep Design):** Designed for 50-year service using the **Hydrostatic Design Basis (HDB)** derived from long-term creep-rupture testing per ASTM D2837. The **Hydrostatic Design Stress (HDS) = HDB / 2.0** is the allowable working stress, incorporating a factor of safety of 2.0 for water service.

2. **Polypropylene Hinge (Dynamic/Fatigue Design):** A living hinge on a shampoo bottle cap is designed to survive 100,000+ flex cycles. The hinge thickness is **0.1–0.5 mm** with a sharp internal radius to control stress. Polypropylene is chosen because of its **high flexural fatigue life** when oriented.

3. **Nylon Cable Tie (Creep + Stress Relaxation Design):** The tie is pre-stretched to a specific strain; the design ensures that over 10 years, the **stress does not relax below the holding stress** of the bundle. Material: Nylon 6,6 (high $T_g \approx 50 °C$ to minimize relaxation at room temperature).

4. **Polycarbonate Safety Helmet (Impact Design):** Must absorb impact energy per EN 397. The shell thickness and foam liner are designed using **fracture mechanics** ($K_{Ic}$) and **energy absorption** ($\int F \, d\delta$) criteria. PC chosen for its high $K_{Ic} \approx 2.5$ MPa·m^0.5.

5. **Epoxy/Glass-Fiber Composite Leaf Spring (Static + Fatigue):** Replaces steel in automotive suspensions. Designed using **classical laminate theory (CLT)**, with fatigue analysis based on **S-N curves** of unidirectional laminae.
