# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Constitutive Law to Generalized Hooke's Law

The **isotropic linear elastic constitutive equation** in 3D can be derived from the strain energy density function. Starting with the assumption of a **strain energy density** $U$ that depends only on the three strain invariants:

$$U = U(I_1, I_2, I_3)$$

For **small deformations**, we expand to second order and obtain a quadratic form with **two independent constants** (Lamé constants $\lambda$ and $\mu$):

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\lambda (I_1)^2 + \mu I_2$$

where $I_1 = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ and $I_2 = \varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x$.

The stress is obtained by differentiating energy density with respect to strain:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{\partial U}{\partial \varepsilon_x} = \lambda I_1 + 2\mu \varepsilon_x$$

Substituting $I_1$ and the strain invariants:

$$\sigma_x = \lambda(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z) + 2\mu \varepsilon_x$$

Rearranging:

$$\sigma_x = (\lambda + 2\mu)\varepsilon_x + \lambda(\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

Identifying $\lambda + 2\mu = E(1-\nu)/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$ and $\lambda = E\nu/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$:

$$\sigma_x = \frac{E(1-\nu)}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\varepsilon_x + \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}(\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)$$

Solving for $\varepsilon_x$ in terms of stresses (inverting the stiffness matrix):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\sigma_x - \frac{\nu}{E}\sigma_y - \frac{\nu}{E}\sigma_z$$

Combining the three normal strain expressions:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

This is the **Generalized Hooke's Law** for isotropic linear-elastic polymers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Findley Power Law from Isochronous Stress-Strain Curves

The Findley power law $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m t^n$ can be motivated by an **isochronous stress-strain curve**. At a fixed time $t^*$, the stress-strain relationship is:

$$\sigma(\varepsilon, t^*) = \sigma_0 \cdot \left(\frac{\varepsilon(\varepsilon, t^*)}{\varepsilon_0(\sigma_0)}\right)$$

Assuming a **power-law dependence** of creep compliance $J(t) = \varepsilon(t)/\sigma_0$ on time:

$$J(t) = J_0 + J_1 \cdot t^n$$

where $J_0 = 1/E_0$ is the instantaneous compliance and $J_1$ is the creep compliance coefficient.

Multiplying both sides by $\sigma_0$:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \sigma_0 J_0 + \sigma_0 J_1 t^n$$

Defining $\varepsilon_0 = \sigma_0 J_0 = \sigma_0/E_0$ (instantaneous strain) and $m = \sigma_0 J_1$ (stress-dependent creep coefficient):

$$\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m t^n$$

This recovers the **Findley Power Law**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Units:** The creep coefficient $m$ has units of strain (it is implicitly a function of stress, so it scales with $\sigma_0$). The creep exponent $n$ is **dimensionless** and typically falls in the range 0.1 to 0.5 for most structural polymers.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Derivation of the WLF Equation

The WLF equation is derived from the **Doolittle equation** for free volume in polymers:

$$\eta = A \cdot \exp\left(\frac{B}{f}\right)$$

where $\eta$ is viscosity, $A$ and $B$ are constants, and $f$ is the **fractional free volume** ($f = f_g + \alpha_f (T - T_g)$), with $f_g$ being the free volume at $T_g$ and $\alpha_f$ the thermal expansion coefficient of free volume.

Defining the **shift factor** $a_T = \eta(T)/\eta(T_g)$ and assuming $\eta \propto t$ (time-temperature correspondence):

$$\log_{10} a_T = \log_{10}\left[\frac{\eta(T)}{\eta(T_g)}\right] = \frac{B}{2.303 f(T)} - \frac{B}{2.303 f_g}$$

Substituting $f(T) = f_g + \alpha_f(T - T_g)$ and rearranging:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{-B/[2.303 f_g] \cdot (T - T_g)}{f_g/\alpha_f + (T - T_g)}$$

Identifying $C_1 = B/(2.303 f_g)$ and $C_2 = f_g/\alpha_f$:

$$\log_{10} a_T = \frac{-C_1 (T - T_g)}{C_2 + (T - T_g)}$$

This is the **WLF equation** with $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$. With $f_g \approx 0.025$ and $\alpha_f \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$, one obtains the **universal constants** $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Solved Example — Step-by-Step (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Problem — Practice this entire solution before the exam.**

**Problem Statement:**
A nylon 6,6 tensile specimen has a circular cross-section of diameter $d = 12 \text{ mm}$ and a gauge length $L_0 = 50 \text{ mm}$. The specimen is subjected to an axial tensile load of $F = 5000 \text{ N}$. Young's modulus is $E = 2.8 \text{ GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio is $\nu = 0.39$.

**(a)** Calculate the engineering stress, engineering strain, and the lateral contraction of the specimen.

**(b)** If the same specimen is subjected to a sustained load of 4000 N for 1000 hours, and the Findley parameters are $\varepsilon_0 = 0.015$, $m = 0.004$, $n = 0.30$, calculate the total strain and the creep modulus at that time.

**(c)** Determine the factor of safety if the ultimate tensile strength is $\sigma_{\text{uts}} = 75 \text{ MPa}$.

### Solution — Part (a): Static Stress and Strain

**Step 1:** Calculate the original cross-sectional area:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.012)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 1.44 \times 10^{-4}}{4}$$

$$A_0 = 1.13097 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the engineering stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0} = \frac{5000}{1.13097 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.421 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 44.21 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the engineering strain using Hooke's law:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{44.21 \times 10^6}{2.8 \times 10^9} = 0.01579 = 1.579\%$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the lateral (transverse) strain using Poisson's ratio:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{trans}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon = -0.39 \times 0.01579 = -6.158 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the change in diameter:

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{\text{trans}} \times d_0 = -6.158 \times 10^{-3} \times 12 = -0.0739 \text{ mm}$$

The diameter contracts by approximately **0.074 mm**.

### Solution — Part (b): Creep Response at 1000 hours

**Step 1:** Calculate the new (lower) stress under the sustained load:

$$\sigma_{\text{sust}} = \frac{4000}{1.13097 \times 10^{-4}} = 3.537 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 35.37 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the instantaneous strain (time-independent component):

$$\varepsilon_0 = \frac{\sigma_{\text{sust}}}{E} = \frac{35.37 \times 10^6}{2.8 \times 10^9} = 0.01263$$

(Note: this is the value given in the problem; the close agreement is intentional.)

**Step 3:** Calculate the creep strain contribution at $t = 1000$ hours:

$$m \cdot t^n = 0.004 \times (1000)^{0.30} = 0.004 \times 7.943 = 0.03177$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the total strain:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}}(1000) = 0.01263 + 0.03177 = 0.0444 = 4.44\%$$

**Step 5:** Calculate the creep modulus at 1000 hours:

$$E_c(1000) = \frac{\sigma_{\text{sust}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{total}}} = \frac{35.37 \times 10^6}{0.0444} = 7.966 \times 10^8 \text{ Pa} \approx 797 \text{ MPa}$$

**Interpretation:** The polymer is **3.5 times softer** under long-term loading than its short-term modulus suggests ($E_c \approx 0.28 E$).

### Solution — Part (c): Factor of Safety

**Step 1:** Identify the working stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{working}} = 35.37 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the factor of safety formula:

$$n = \frac{\sigma_{\text{uts}}}{\sigma_{\text{working}}} = \frac{75}{35.37} = 2.12$$

**Step 3:** Verify against KTU guidelines. For long-term loading, the KTU-recommended **minimum** factor of safety is **2.0**. The calculated value of **2.12** is **acceptable**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** A common error is using the short-term modulus to compute strain and obtaining a higher safety factor. **Always use the long-term (creep-adjusted) modulus for sustained loads.** Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this oversight.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selecting the Right Design Approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this matrix to choose the correct design philosophy based on loading conditions:**

| Loading Type | Time Scale | Temperature Range | Design Approach | Key Equation | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Short-term Static** | $< 10^3$ s | $T < T_g - 50°C$ | Classical linear-elastic, factor of safety 1.5–2.0 | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Long-term Static (Creep)** | $10^3$–$10^9$ s | Any | Findley law + creep modulus + HDB | $E_c(t) = \sigma_0/[\varepsilon_0 + m t^n]$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Cyclic (Fatigue)** | $10^3$–$10^7$ cycles | Any | S-N curve, Basquin/Co-Manson | $\sigma_a = \sigma_f'(2N_f)^b$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Impact** | $10^{-3}$ s | Any | Fracture mechanics, $K_{Ic}$ | $K_{Ic} = Y\sigma\sqrt{\pi a}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Vibration** | $10^1$–$10^5$ Hz | Any | Modal analysis + damping | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | ⭐⭐ |
| **Stress Relaxation** | Hours to years | Near $T_g$ | Maxwell model, $a_T$ via WLF | $\sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \exp(-t/\tau)$ | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Thermo-mechanical** | Cyclic | Wide | TTSP + WLF/Arrhenius | $\log a_T$ equations | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q: Differentiate between static and dynamic loading. Why is the design of polymer products under dynamic loading more complex than under static loading?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Static Loading | Dynamic Loading |
|---|---|---|
| Time variation | Constant magnitude, direction, and point of application with time | Magnitude and/or direction vary with time |
| Examples | Weight of fluid in a tank, self-weight of pipe | Vibrations, impact, cyclic loads |
| Material response | Time-independent (for short durations) | Time-dependent — strain rate, hysteresis, and frequency matter |
| Polymer-specific issues | Minor (creep over long durations) | Significant — self-heating, fatigue, viscoelasticity |

The design of polymers under dynamic loading is more complex because of **(i) viscoelastic energy dissipation** (hysteresis losses converted to heat), **(ii) fatigue damage accumulation** at stresses well below the static strength, **(iii) rate-dependence of strength** (higher rates give higher apparent strength), and **(iv) the absence of an endurance limit** in most polymers — failure will eventually occur at any cyclic stress level. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q: Define stress concentration factor. Explain with a suitable example how it influences the design of a polymer component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **stress concentration factor (SCT or $K_t$)** is the ratio of the maximum local stress at a discontinuity to the nominal (average) stress in the surrounding material:

$$K_t = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{nominal}}}$$

**Example:** Consider a polyethylene (PE) tank with a circular hole of diameter 10 mm in a wall of width 50 mm. The theoretical $K_t$ for an infinite plate is 3.0, meaning the stress at the hole edge is **3× the average stress**. If the average stress is 5 MPa, the local stress is 15 MPa, which may exceed the long-term strength of PE.

**Design implication:** The designer must **(i)** use **generous fillet radii** at all corners, **(ii)** avoid **sharp internal corners**, **(iii)** position **holes away from high-stress regions**, and **(iv)** account for the **notch sensitivity** of the specific polymer, since brittle polymers (PS, acrylic) amplify the geometric $K_t$ more than ductile ones. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper PCPOT601]**

**Q: A polypropylene pipe of outer diameter 60 mm and wall thickness 6 mm is used to convey hot water at 70 °C under an internal pressure of 0.6 MPa.**

**(a) Calculate the hoop stress, longitudinal stress, and the maximum shear stress in the pipe wall. Identify the critical stress and check the factor of safety if the long-term strength of PP at 70 °C is 14 MPa. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the same pipe is subjected to a sustained internal pressure of 0.5 MPa at 70 °C for 5 years, and the Findley creep parameters are $\varepsilon_0 = 0.011$, $m = 0.0035$, $n = 0.32$, determine the total strain, the creep modulus, and the maximum allowable working pressure for a factor of safety of 2.5. (7 marks)**

#### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Identify pipe geometry.**

Mean diameter $D_m = D_o - t = 60 - 6 = 54 \text{ mm}$

Inner diameter $D_i = D_o - 2t = 60 - 12 = 48 \text{ mm}$

Wall thickness $t = 6 \text{ mm} = 0.006 \text{ m}$

Internal pressure $p = 0.6 \text{ MPa} = 0.6 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 2: Hoop (circumferential) stress.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p D_m}{2t} = \frac{0.6 \times 10^6 \times 0.054}{2 \times 0.006} = \frac{32400}{0.012}$$

$$\sigma_h = 2.70 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.70 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal (axial) stress.**

$$\sigma_L = \frac{p D_m}{4t} = \frac{\sigma_h}{2} = \frac{2.70}{2} = 1.35 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Maximum shear stress.**

For a thin-walled pipe under internal pressure (biaxial stress state with $\sigma_r \approx 0$):

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_r}{2} = \frac{2.70 - 0}{2} = 1.35 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5: Critical stress and factor of safety.**

The critical (maximum principal) stress is $\sigma_1 = \sigma_h = 2.70 \text{ MPa}$.

$$n = \frac{\sigma_{\text{long-term}}}{\sigma_h} = \frac{14}{2.70} = 5.19$$

The factor of safety of **5.19** is well above the typical minimum of 2.0 — the design is conservative. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Calculate the new hoop stress at $p = 0.5$ MPa.**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{0.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.054}{2 \times 0.006} = 2.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Calculate total strain at $t = 5 \text{ years} = 5 \times 8760 = 43800 \text{ hours}$.**

$$m \cdot t^n = 0.0035 \times (43800)^{0.32}$$

Calculate $(43800)^{0.32}$:

$$\log_{10}(43800) = 4.641 \quad ; \quad 0.32 \times 4.641 = 1.485 \quad ; \quad 10^{1.485} = 30.55$$

$$m \cdot t^n = 0.0035 \times 30.55 = 0.1069$$

Total strain:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = 0.011 + 0.1069 = 0.1179 = 11.79\%$$

**Step 3: Calculate the creep modulus at 5 years.**

$$E_c(5 \text{ yr}) = \frac{\sigma_h}{\varepsilon_{\text{total}}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^6}{0.1179} = 1.908 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 19.08 \text{ MPa}$$

(For reference, the short-term $E$ of PP is ~1.5 GPa. The 5-year modulus is **~1.3%** of the short-term value — a dramatic reduction due to creep at 70 °C, which is close to the $T_g$ of polypropylene.)

**Step 4: Maximum allowable working pressure for $n = 2.5$.**

$$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{long-term}}}{n} = \frac{14}{2.5} = 5.6 \text{ MPa}$$

Rearranging the hoop stress equation:

$$p_{\max} = \frac{2t \sigma_{\text{allow}}}{D_m} = \frac{2 \times 0.006 \times 5.6 \times 10^6}{0.054}$$

$$p_{\max} = 1.244 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 1.244 \text{ MPa}$$

**The maximum allowable working pressure is 1.24 MPa for a 5-year design life with a safety factor of 2.5. (7 marks)**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper PCPOT601]**

**Q: A medium-density polyethylene (MDPE) chemical storage tank of rectangular cross-section is 1.2 m long, 0.8 m wide, and 0.6 m deep. The wall thickness is 8 mm. The tank is filled with a liquid of density 1100 kg/m³.**

**(a) Treating the long wall as a simply supported plate with hydrostatic pressure, calculate the maximum bending stress at the bottom of the wall. Given: $E = 0.8$ GPa, Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.42$, allowable deflection = L/200. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the tank experiences temperature cycling from 25 °C to 55 °C daily and is subjected to a sustained load for 10 years, compute the creep strain using the Findley law with $\varepsilon_0 = 0.008$, $m = 0.0028$, $n = 0.28$, and discuss the implication for design life. (7 marks)**

#### Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1: Hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the wall.**

$$p = \rho g h = 1100 \times 9.81 \times 0.6 = 6474.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 6.475 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2: Treat long wall as a simply supported plate (1.2 m × 0.6 m height).**

The wall is a rectangular plate with $a = 1.2$ m (length), $b = 0.6$ m (height). Since $a/b = 2.0 > 1.0$, the long-wall bending formula applies (one-way bending, bending primarily along the short dimension 0.6 m).

**Step 3: Maximum bending moment for a simply supported plate strip (1 m wide).**

The maximum bending moment at mid-span of a simply supported beam (unit width) under uniformly distributed load $p$ is:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{p L^2}{8} = \frac{6474.6 \times (0.6)^2}{8} = \frac{6474.6 \times 0.36}{8}$$

$$M_{\max} = 291.36 \text{ N·m/m}$$

**Step 4: Section modulus of the wall (per unit width).**

$$Z = \frac{t^2}{6} = \frac{(0.008)^2}{6} = \frac{6.4 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.067 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{m}$$

**Step 5: Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{291.36}{1.067 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.731 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} = 27.31 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6: Allowable deflection check.**

Allowable deflection $\delta_{\text{allow}} = L/200 = 600/200 = 3 \text{ mm}$.

Actual deflection of a simply supported beam under UDL:

$$\delta = \frac{5 p L^4}{384 E I}$$

where $I = t^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ for a plate (accounting for Poisson's effect):

$$I = \frac{(0.008)^3}{12(1-0.42^2)} = \frac{5.12 \times 10^{-7}}{12 \times 0.8236} = 5.182 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^4/\text{m}$$

$$\delta = \frac{5 \times 6474.6 \times (0.6)^4}{384 \times 0.8 \times 10^9 \times 5.182 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{4197.2}{15920} = 0.2637 \text{ m}$$

This is much greater than 3 mm. **The wall is under-designed for stiffness.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Many students forget to use the **plate moment of inertia** $I_{\text{plate}} = t^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ which accounts for the constraint against lateral contraction (Poisson's effect). Using the simple beam formula $I = t^3/12$ underestimates the deflection by a factor of $(1-\nu^2)$. Always include this correction for **plate bending** problems.

The actual stress of 27.3 MPa must be compared with the long-term allowable stress of MDPE (typically ~10 MPa for water service at 25 °C). The stress is too high. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1: Time conversion.**

$$t = 10 \text{ years} = 10 \times 365.25 \times 24 = 87660 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Calculate $t^n$.**

$$t^n = (87660)^{0.28}$$

$$\log_{10}(87660) = 4.943 \quad ; \quad 0.28 \times 4.943 = 1.384 \quad ; \quad 10^{1.384} = 24.23$$

$$m \cdot t^n = 0.0028 \times 24.23 = 0.0679$$

**Step 3: Total creep strain.**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{total}} = \varepsilon_0 + m t^n = 0.008 + 0.0679 = 0.0759 = 7.59\%$$

**Step 4: Discussion of design implications.**

A strain of 7.6% at 10 years is **significant** for MDPE. The yield strain of MDPE is typically 10–15%. The tank is operating at **roughly 50–75% of its yield strain**, which leaves a very small margin for:

- Any additional stress from **temperature cycling** (25–55 °C, close to the $T_g$ of MDPE ~ -20 °C, so the polymer is well above $T_g$ in the **rubbery regime** — high creep rate)
- **Chemical absorption** that may plasticize the polymer
- **UV degradation** if the tank is outdoors
- **Stress concentration** at fittings and corners

**Recommendation:** The designer should **(i)** increase the wall thickness to 12–15 mm, **(ii)** add **external ribs** to reduce the unsupported span, **(iii)** use a **fiber-reinforced** MDPE or upgrade to **HDPE** (higher $T_g$, better creep resistance), and **(iv)** apply a **factor of safety of at least 3.0** for chemical tanks. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Five Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each:**

1. **Using short-term modulus for long-term design.** Always state whether you are using $E$ (short-term) or $E_c(t)$ (creep-adjusted) and justify the choice. The examiner is checking for this awareness explicitly.

2. **Forgetting the $1-\nu^2$ factor in plate bending.** This is a **2-mark deduction** in many KTU answer keys. Memorize: $I_{\text{plate}} = t^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$.

3. **Choosing the wrong failure criterion.** Don't apply **von Mises** to a brittle polymer like polystyrene — use **Maximum Normal Stress Theory** instead. Always state which criterion is appropriate **and why**.

4. **Mixing units.** MPa vs Pa, mm vs m, hours vs seconds. A single unit mismatch can derail an entire numerical answer. State units at every step.

5. **Ignoring stress concentrations.** A calculation that gives a perfect safety factor of 3.0 is meaningless if the design has a sharp 90° internal corner that produces $K_t = 5$. Always include a note on **fillet radii and edge geometry** in design answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and revise 24 hours before the exam:**

- [ ] **Static design** uses short-term modulus $E$ and a factor of safety of 1.5–2.0 for non-critical applications.
- [ ] **Long-term static design** uses creep modulus $E_c(t)$ from the Findley law: $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 + m t^n$.
- [ ] **The Findley parameters $m$ and $n$ are temperature- and stress-dependent** — always specify the conditions.
- [ ] **Factor of safety for long-term polymer design** should be **at least 2.0**, preferably **3.0** for critical applications.
- [ ] **Failure theories:** Use **Max Normal Stress** for brittle polymers (PS, PMMA, EP); use **Tresca or von Mises** for ductile polymers (LDPE, PA, PC).
- [ ] **Stress concentration factor** $K_t$ quantifies the stress amplification at geometric discontinuities. **Always specify a fillet radius** of at least 0.5× the wall thickness in polymer parts.
- [ ] **Fatigue** in polymers has **no endurance limit** — every cyclic stress amplitude eventually causes failure.
- [ ] **S-N curves** are the primary design tool for fatigue; **Basquin's law** applies at high cycles, **Coffin-Manson** at low cycles.
- [ ] **Impact strength** is measured by **Izod** (ASTM D256) or **Charpy** (ISO 179); results are strongly **notch-dependent**.
- [ ] **Creep rupture** is the long-term failure mode for sustained loading; **stress relaxation** is the loss of preload in joints.
- [ ] **TTSP (Time-Temperature Superposition)** uses the **WLF equation** with universal constants $C_1 = 17.44$ and $C_2 = 51.6$ K at $T_{\text{ref}} = T_g$.
- [ ] **Plate bending** uses $I = t^3/[12(1-\nu^2)]$ to account for the Poisson constraint — **not** the simple beam formula.
- [ ] **Hoop stress** in a thin-walled pipe is **twice** the longitudinal stress: $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$.
- [ ] **Glass transition temperature $T_g$** is the single most important polymer design parameter — keep service temperature **at least 20 °C below $T_g$** for structural applications.
- [ ] **For fatigue design of polymers**, use a derating factor of **0.3–0.5** on the static strength to obtain the allowable cyclic stress amplitude.
- [ ] **Strain-rate sensitivity exponent $m$** for polymers is typically 0.05–0.20 — much higher than metals (~0.01).

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — Structural Design of Products Under Static and Dynamic Loads**

*Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCPOT601 — Polymer Products — Design & Testing.*

topics.title: Design Features of Products
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design Features of Products

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Design Features of Products** in the context of polymer engineering refer to the engineered geometric, functional, and structural attributes deliberately integrated into a plastic component to satisfy mechanical performance, manufacturability, aesthetic appeal, ergonomic comfort, assembly compatibility, and economic feasibility. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT601), these features form the bridge between conceptual product ideation and the actual moulded or fabricated part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> Design features are the specific physical and geometric characteristics (such as wall thickness, ribs, bosses, draft, radii, snap-fits, and living hinges) deliberately incorporated into a polymer product to optimize its functional, structural, and manufacturing performance while minimizing cost and defects.

The major design features that govern polymer products are classified as:

1. **Wall Thickness & Uniformity**
2. **Draft Angle (Taper)**
3. **Radii and Fillets**
4. **Ribs**
5. **Bosses**
6. **Gussets**
7. **Holes and Coring**
8. **Snap Fits**
9. **Living Hinges**
10. **Surface Texture and Finish**
11. **Tolerances and Shrinkage Allowances**
12. **Parting Line and Gate Location**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Plastic Bucket" Metaphor**
> Imagine designing a simple plastic bucket. Have you ever wondered why the bottom of the bucket is *thicker* than the sides? Or why the handle has those curved notches where it meets the rim? Every one of these is a **deliberate design feature**. If you moulded a bucket with a uniform 3 mm wall everywhere, it would warp, sink, waste resin, and crack. The thicker bottom gives rigidity; the ribs (vertical lines on the bucket's outer side) add stiffness without adding mass; the curved fillet at the corner eliminates a stress concentration that would otherwise cause cracking under load. In short, **design features are the "silent engineers" of every plastic product** — you never see them in marketing brochures, but they dictate whether the product lasts 6 months or 16 years.

### 1.3 Key Physical Standards & Metrics

- **Standard Draft Angle Range:** $1°$ to $2°$ (general), up to $5°$ (deep draw), $\pm 0.5°$ tolerance.
- **Minimum Wall Thickness Reference (Polystyrene):** **0.8 mm** (thin-wall design), **1.5–3.0 mm** (general purpose).
- **Recommended Fillet Radius:** **0.4 × wall thickness** to **0.6 × wall thickness**.
- **Shrinkage Allowance Range:** **0.001–0.020** (i.e., 0.1 % – 2.0 %) depending on polymer.
- **Standard Rib Thickness Ratio:** **0.5 × nominal wall thickness** to **0.75 × nominal wall thickness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT601 – Module 2):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to *describe, identify, and justify* the inclusion of ribs, bosses, gussets, draft, radii, holes, and snap fits in moulded polymer products. Memorize both the **purpose** AND the **dimensional rule-of-thumb** for each feature.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Wall Thickness — The Master Variable

Wall thickness is the single most influential design parameter in any injection-moulded or extruded polymer product. It governs:

- **Crystallinity & Shrinkage:** Non-uniform walls cool at different rates → differential shrinkage → warpage.
- **Cycle Time:** Thick sections prolong cooling → reduced productivity.
- **Sink Marks:** Thick spots on the surface of a moulded part where the underlying material has shrunk are called **sink marks**.
- **Voids:** Internal vacuum pockets formed in thick sections.
- **Mechanical Performance:** Stiffness scales with the cube of thickness ($I \propto t^3$).

**Wall Thickness Selection Rule:**

$$t_{\text{optimal}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I_{\text{allowable}}}}$$

For a beam in flexure under load $F$ over span $L$ with Young's modulus $E$ and allowable deflection $I_{\text{allowable}}$.

### 2.2 Draft Angle (Taper)

Draft is the intentional taper on a mould wall parallel to the mould-opening direction. It enables part ejection without scuffing, dragging, or deformation.

**Draft Calculation Formula:**

$$D = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

where:
- $D$ = total draft displacement at depth $h$
- $h$ = depth of cavity in the direction of draw
- $\theta$ = draft angle (typically $1°$ to $2°$)

For $\theta = 1°$ and $h = 50$ mm:

$$D = 50 \cdot \tan(1°) = 50 \cdot 0.01745 = 0.8725 \text{ mm}$$

### 2.3 Ribs — Stiffness Without Mass

Ribs are vertical wall extensions used to increase the moment of inertia ($I$) of a part without increasing wall thickness. A common KTU question is: *"Why are ribs made thinner than the nominal wall?"*

**Answer:** To prevent **sink marks**. If a rib's base is thicker than the wall, the material pool at the rib root cools slowly, pulls in, and forms a visible sink on the opposite (cosmetic) surface.

**Rib Stiffness Gain:**

For a flat plate of width $b$ and thickness $t$ reinforced by $n$ ribs of height $h_r$ and thickness $t_r$:

$$I_{\text{reinforced}} = I_{\text{plate}} + n \cdot I_{\text{rib}} \approx \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} + n \cdot \frac{t_r \cdot h_r^3}{12}$$

Since $h_r$ can be **5–10×** $t_r$, rib contribution dominates.

### 2.4 Bosses — Locating & Fastening Features

A **boss** is a cylindrical protrusion designed to receive a self-tapping screw, a threaded insert, or a locating pin. Bosses must incorporate:
- A **core pin hole** to form the internal diameter.
- A **draft angle** identical to the part.
- **Ribs radiating outward** to the surrounding wall (typically **3 ribs at 120°**) to provide support and prevent boss deflection during screw insertion.

### 2.5 Gussets — Triangular Reinforcements

Gussets are flat, triangular webs used at the junction of two perpendicular walls (e.g., the corner of a box, the foot of a chair leg). They increase load-bearing capacity and reduce stress concentration at the corner.

**Rule of Thumb:** Gusset thickness = **0.6 × to 0.8 ×** the nominal wall thickness.

### 2.6 Radii and Fillets

Sharp internal corners are **stress magnifiers** and **flow restrictors**. The stress concentration factor for a sharp internal corner can be **> 3**. Adding a fillet reduces $K_t$ dramatically.

**Recommended Fillet Radius:**

$$R_{\text{fillet}} = 0.4 \cdot t \quad \text{to} \quad 0.6 \cdot t$$

where $t$ is the nominal wall thickness.

For an external corner, a radius of **$1.5 \cdot t$** is preferred to maintain uniform wall thickness at the corner junction.

### 2.7 Snap Fits

Snap fits are integral plastic features that allow rapid, fastener-free assembly. Two principal types:

- **Cantilever Snap Fit:** A flexible beam deflects and engages a hook.
- **Annular (Torus) Snap Fit:** A ring snaps over a groove (e.g., pen caps, bottle closures).

**Cantilever Deflection Equation (KTU High-Yield):**

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

$$\delta_{\text{max allowable}} \approx 0.05 \cdot L \quad \text{(5\% of length)}$$

Strain at the base of the cantilever:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \cdot t \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^2}$$

For amorphous polymers, the allowable strain is **5 % – 8 %**; for glass-filled or highly crystalline polymers, only **1 % – 2 %**.

### 2.8 Living Hinges

A living hinge is an extremely thin section of plastic (typically **0.25–0.5 mm**) that acts as a flexure, allowing two rigid sections of the same moulded part to bend repeatedly (e.g., shampoo bottle flip caps, tool cases, ketchup dispenser lids).

**Critical Rule:** Living hinges work ONLY with **semi-crystalline polymers** like **Polypropylene (PP)** and **Polyethylene (PE)** because their molecular chains align during flexure, increasing flex life. They do NOT work with amorphous polymers (PS, PC, ABS).

**Hinge Thickness Formula:**

$$t_{\text{hinge}} = 0.25 \text{ mm (minimum)} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.5 \text{ mm (typical)}$$

The minimum thickness is dictated by mould flow and is gated by the *mouldability* constraint.

### 2.9 Holes and Coring

Holes in polymer parts are formed by **core pins**. The recommended core pin diameter $d$ must satisfy:

$$d_{\min} \geq 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(general)} \quad d_{\min} \geq 0.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(precision moulding)}$$

For deep holes (depth-to-diameter ratio $L/d > 4$), a **slight draft of 0.5° to 1°** per side is mandatory to prevent core pin deflection.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Design Feature | Governing Equation | Recommended Range / Rule | Primary Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall Thickness | $t_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt[3]{FL^3 / 3EI}$ | $0.8 - 3.0$ mm (PS) | Uniformity prevents warp/sink |
| 2 | Draft Angle | $D = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$ | $1° - 2°$ standard, up to $5°$ deep | Ejection without scuffing |
| 3 | Fillet Radius | $R = 0.4t - 0.6t$ | Internal: $0.4t - 0.6t$ | Reduces $K_t$, improves flow |
| 4 | Rib Thickness | $t_r = 0.5t - 0.75t$ | 50–75% of wall | Stiffness without sink |
| 5 | Rib Height | $h_r \leq 3t$ | Max $3 \times$ wall | Prevents filling issues |
| 6 | Gusset Thickness | $t_g = 0.6t - 0.8t$ | 60–80% of wall | Corner reinforcement |
| 7 | Boss Ribs | 3 ribs at $120°$ | Tri-symmetric | Boss support |
| 8 | Cantilever Deflection | $\delta = FL^3 / 3EI$ | $\leq 5\%$ of $L$ | Snap-fit travel |
| 9 | Snap Strain | $\varepsilon = 3t\delta / 2L^2$ | $\leq 5\% - 8\%$ (amorphous) | Flexure durability |
| 10 | Living Hinge | $t_h = 0.25 - 0.5$ mm | PP/PE only | Repeated flexure |
| 11 | Hole Diameter | $d \geq 1.0$ mm | $L/d \leq 4$ for straight | Avoid core pin deflection |
| 12 | Shrinkage | $S = (L_m - L_p) / L_m$ | $0.001 - 0.020$ (0.1%–2%) | Dimensional compensation |
| 13 | Stiffness | $I = bt^3 / 12$ | $I \propto t^3$ | Bending resistance |
| 14 | Parting Line | Single, planar | Avoid cosmetic surfaces | Mould separation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> These features directly translate to **mould cost**, **cycle time**, **scrap rate**, and **field warranty claims**. For example, omitting a $0.5$ mm radius on a corner can reduce mould life by **40 %** (flash formation) and increase field failure by **300 %** (crack initiation). Mastery of these rules is non-negotiable for any polymer product designer.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Snap-Fit Deflection (Board-Favourite Question)

**Problem Statement:** A cantilever snap-fit beam is moulded from Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) with Young's modulus $E = 2.3$ GPa. The beam has length $L = 30$ mm, width $b = 6$ mm, and thickness $t = 2$ mm. The required engagement deflection is $\delta = 1.0$ mm. Verify whether the beam will yield in flexure and compute the maximum strain at the beam base.

**Step 1 — State the governing beam equation.**

For a cantilever beam loaded transversely at its free end by force $F$:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

**Step 2 — Express the second moment of area.**

For a rectangular cross-section, bending about the weak axis:

$$I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}$$

Substituting numerical values:

$$I = \frac{6 \times 2^3}{12} = \frac{6 \times 8}{12} = \frac{48}{12} = 4.0 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the engagement force $F$.**

Rearranging the deflection equation:

$$F = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \delta}{L^3}$$

Insert the values. Convert $E$ to N/mm² first:

$$E = 2.3 \text{ GPa} = 2300 \text{ MPa} = 2300 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

Then:

$$F = \frac{3 \times 2300 \times 4.0 \times 1.0}{30^3} = \frac{3 \times 2300 \times 4.0}{27000} = \frac{27600}{27000} = 1.0222 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum bending stress.**

The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end of the cantilever:

$$M_{\max} = F \cdot L = 1.0222 \times 30 = 30.666 \text{ N·mm}$$

The maximum bending stress (at the outer fibre, $c = t/2$):

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{30.666 \times 1.0}{4.0} = 7.667 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 7.667 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum strain at the base.**

$$\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{E} = \frac{7.667}{2300} = 0.003333 = 0.333\%$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check using the strain formula.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \cdot t \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^2} = \frac{3 \times 2.0 \times 1.0}{2 \times 30^2} = \frac{6}{1800} = 0.003333$$

The two methods agree exactly. Since $0.333\% \ll 5\%$ (allowable for ABS), the snap-fit design is **safe**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Rib Stiffness Enhancement of a Flat Plate

**Problem Statement:** A flat PP plate of width $b = 100$ mm, length $L = 200$ mm, and wall thickness $t = 3$ mm is reinforced with $n = 4$ ribs of height $h_r = 12$ mm and thickness $t_r = 2$ mm. Compute the ratio of stiffened-to-unreinforced second moment of area.

**Step 1 — Unreinforced plate $I$.**

$$I_{\text{plate}} = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 3^3}{12} = \frac{100 \times 27}{12} = \frac{2700}{12} = 225 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Single rib $I$.**

$$I_{\text{rib}} = \frac{t_r \cdot h_r^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 12^3}{12} = \frac{2 \times 1728}{12} = \frac{3456}{12} = 288 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 3 — Total reinforced $I$ (parallel-axis approximation, ribs on neutral axis).**

$$I_{\text{total}} = I_{\text{plate}} + n \cdot I_{\text{rib}} = 225 + 4 \times 288 = 225 + 1152 = 1377 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 4 — Compute the stiffness enhancement ratio.**

$$\eta = \frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_{\text{plate}}} = \frac{1377}{225} = 6.12$$

**Conclusion:** Adding four ribs (with a mere $2$ mm extra material at each rib location) increased the bending stiffness **6.12-fold**. This is the designer's "free lunch" — added stiffness without adding bulk.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Draft for a Deep Draw Part

**Problem Statement:** A rectangular container has a wall section of height $h = 100$ mm that must be drawn out of a mould. Determine the draft displacement $D$ at the bottom of the draw for the minimum recommended draft angle of $1°$ and the typical recommended angle of $2°$.

**Step 1 — Apply the draft equation.**

$$D = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate for $\theta = 1°$.**

$$D_1 = 100 \cdot \tan(1°) = 100 \times 0.017455 = 1.7455 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate for $\theta = 2°$.**

$$D_2 = 100 \cdot \tan(2°) = 100 \times 0.034921 = 3.4921 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result.**

A 2° draft doubles the side-wall taper. While $1°$ is acceptable for low-friction materials (e.g., PP, HDPE), $2°$ is preferred for high-friction materials (e.g., ABS, PC) and for surfaces that need a **consistent gloss** after ejection.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Design Pitfalls (KTU Repeated)

| Pitfall | Symptom in Moulded Part | Correct Design Action |
|---|---|---|
| Uniform thick wall throughout | Sink marks, voids, long cycle | Use ribs (thinner than wall) |
| Sharp internal corner | Cracks, weld lines, high stress | Add $R = 0.4t - 0.6t$ fillet |
| Boss flush with wall | Boss cracks under screw torque | Add 3 supporting ribs at $120°$ |
| Living hinge in ABS/PC | Cracks after a few cycles | Switch to PP/PE; orient hinge to flow |
| Hole with $L/d > 6$ | Core pin deflects, hole off-centre | Add $0.5° - 1°$ draft to core |
| Draft angle $= 0°$ | Part drags, scuffs, breaks on ejection | Use $1° - 2°$ minimum |
| Rib base = full wall thickness | Sink marks on cosmetic face | Reduce rib base to $0.5t - 0.75t$ |
| Sharp external corner | Thin-out, warpage, stress | Add radius $\approx 1.5t$ |

> When KTU presents a cross-sectional view of a moulded part and asks you to identify design features, look for these give-away signatures:
> 1. **Asymmetric walls at a corner** → likely a rib or boss.
> 2. **Small cylindrical protrusion with a center hole** → boss.
> 3. **Ultra-thin strip between two thick blocks** → living hinge.
> 4. **Tapered walls** → draft angle evidence.
> 5. **Curved internal corner** → fillet.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**List any six design features that must be considered while designing a polymer product for injection moulding. State the purpose of each in one line.**

**Model Answer (Board-Expected):**

| # | Design Feature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall thickness uniformity | Prevents warpage, sink marks, and voids |
| 2 | Draft angle (1° – 2°) | Enables smooth part ejection from mould |
| 3 | Radii and fillets ($0.4t - 0.6t$) | Reduces stress concentration; improves melt flow |
| 4 | Ribs (thickness $0.5t - 0.75t$) | Increases stiffness without increasing wall thickness |
| 5 | Bosses (with 3 supporting ribs) | Locating/fastening features with adequate support |
| 6 | Snap fits or living hinges | Enables fastener-free assembly |
| 7 | Hole coring ($L/d \leq 4$) | Prevents core pin deflection |
| 8 | Tolerances & shrinkage | Compensates for thermal contraction |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Explain why ribs in a polymer product are made thinner than the nominal wall thickness. What is the recommended maximum rib height?**

**Model Answer:**

Ribs are deliberately made **thinner than the wall (typically $0.5t - 0.75t$)** to avoid **sink marks** on the cosmetic surface opposite the rib root. When a rib base equals the full wall thickness, the material pool at the root cools more slowly than the surrounding wall, contracts more, and pulls the surface inward, forming a visible sink. A thinner rib allows the rib material to cool at a rate similar to the wall, eliminating the differential shrinkage.

The **recommended maximum rib height** is **$3 \times$ the nominal wall thickness** ($h_r \leq 3t$). Exceeding this ratio leads to filling problems, short shots, and excessive moulded-in stress.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Always state the numerical rule-of-thumb**, not just the qualitative purpose. Examiners award 1 mark for the *exact numerical range* (e.g., $0.5t - 0.75t$).
> 2. **Draw a labelled sketch** wherever the question mentions "with a neat diagram". A missing sketch costs 2–3 marks.
> 3. **For snap-fit numerical problems, ALWAYS verify the strain** against the allowable strain — students routinely skip this and lose 3 marks.
> 4. **Mention material-specific exceptions** (e.g., living hinges work only with PP/PE) — this is a favourite 2-mark differentiator.

---

**Part B – Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Describe the design guidelines for **wall thickness, ribs, and bosses** in polymer products. For each, give the recommended dimensional rule-of-thumb and justify with the underlying reason.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A **cantilever snap-fit** is to be designed in ABS ($E = 2.3$ GPa). The beam length $L = 40$ mm, width $b = 8$ mm, and thickness $t = 2.5$ mm. If the engagement deflection required is $\delta = 1.2$ mm, calculate the engagement force and verify that the maximum strain in the beam is within the allowable limit for ABS.

---

**Model Solution — Part B, Question A:**

**(a) Design Guidelines for Wall Thickness, Ribs, and Bosses (7 Marks)**

**1. Wall Thickness:**

The wall thickness should be **as uniform as possible** throughout the part. Recommended range for general-purpose polymers is **$1.5 - 3.0$ mm**. Uniform walls cool at the same rate, minimizing differential shrinkage. Non-uniform walls cause:
- **Sink marks** at thick spots,
- **Voids** inside thick sections,
- **Warpage** due to differential cooling,
- **Internal stresses** that reduce long-term performance.

The wall thickness must be calculated based on the bending stiffness requirement:

$$t_{\text{opt}} = \sqrt[3]{\frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I_{\text{allowable}}}}$$

**(Marks: 2)**

**2. Ribs:**

Ribs are vertical projections from a wall that increase the moment of inertia $I$ **without increasing wall thickness**. Design rules:

- **Rib thickness** $= 0.5t - 0.75t$ (where $t$ is the wall).
- **Rib height** $\leq 3t$.
- **Spacing between ribs** $\geq 2 \times$ rib thickness.
- **Fillet at rib root** $= 0.4t - 0.6t$.
- Ribs should be **drafted by $0.5° - 1°$ per side** for ejection.
- For a circular boss pattern, use **3 ribs at $120°$** for symmetric support.

**Why thinner than the wall?** To avoid sink marks. The thinner rib base cools at a rate close to the surrounding wall, eliminating differential shrinkage.

**(Marks: 3)**

**3. Bosses:**

A boss is a cylindrical protrusion used to mount screws, threaded inserts, or locating pins.

Design rules:
- **Boss outer diameter** $\approx 2 \times$ core hole diameter.
- **Boss wall thickness** $\approx 0.6 \times$ nominal wall.
- **Add 3 ribs** at $120°$ from boss to surrounding wall.
- **Add fillet** of $0.4t$ at boss base.
- If a self-tapping screw is used, the **core hole diameter** should be **$\approx 0.8 \times$ the screw root diameter** for thermoplastics.

**(Marks: 2)**

---

**(b) Cantilever Snap-Fit Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $E = 2.3$ GPa $= 2300$ MPa $= 2300$ N/mm², $L = 40$ mm, $b = 8$ mm, $t = 2.5$ mm, $\delta = 1.2$ mm.

**Step 1 — Second moment of area $I$.**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times (2.5)^3}{12} = \frac{8 \times 15.625}{12} = \frac{125}{12} = 10.4167 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 2 — Engagement force $F$.**

$$F = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \delta}{L^3} = \frac{3 \times 2300 \times 10.4167 \times 1.2}{(40)^3}$$

$$F = \frac{3 \times 2300 \times 10.4167 \times 1.2}{64000} = \frac{86250.012}{64000} = 1.3477 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum strain at the base.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{3 \cdot t \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^2} = \frac{3 \times 2.5 \times 1.2}{2 \times 40^2} = \frac{9}{3200} = 0.0028125 = 0.281\%$$

**Step 4 — Verification against allowable strain.**

For amorphous ABS, the allowable strain is $\varepsilon_{\text{allowable}} = 5\% - 8\%$.

Since $0.281\% \ll 5\%$, the snap-fit is **well within the safe limit**.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The engagement force is approximately **1.35 N**, and the maximum strain is **0.28 %**, which is well below the 5 % allowable limit for ABS. The design is **safe and efficient**.

**(Marks: 7)**

---

**Part B – Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With neat sketches, explain the design of **draft angles, fillets, and radii** in polymer products. Mention the recommended ranges and explain why each feature is essential.

**(b) (7 Marks)** A **polypropylene living hinge** must connect two rigid walls of total span $80$ mm. The moulded part will be flexed about $30$ times in its service life. Justify whether PP is an appropriate material for the living hinge, state the recommended hinge thickness, and explain the **molecular orientation mechanism** that gives a living hinge its flex life.

---

**Model Solution — Part B, Question B:**

**(a) Draft Angles, Fillets, and Radii (7 Marks)**

**1. Draft Angles:**

A draft angle is the slight taper applied to mould walls parallel to the mould-opening direction. Without draft, the part drags against the mould wall during ejection, causing scuffing, deformation, or breakage.

- **Recommended draft angle:** $1° - 2°$ for most polymers, up to $5°$ for deep-draw parts and textured surfaces.
- **Draft displacement at depth $h$:** $D = h \cdot \tan(\theta)$.
- For $h = 100$ mm, $\theta = 1°$: $D = 1.745$ mm.

**Materials with low friction** (PP, HDPE, PA) need only $0.5° - 1°$. **Materials with high friction** (PC, PMMA, ABS) need $2°$ or more.

**(Marks: 2)**

**2. Fillets (Internal Radii):**

A fillet is a curved transition at an internal corner. Sharp internal corners are stress magnifiers with a stress concentration factor $K_t > 3$.

- **Recommended fillet radius:** $R = 0.4t - 0.6t$ (where $t$ is the wall thickness).
- For $t = 3$ mm, $R = 1.2 - 1.8$ mm.

Fillet benefits:
- Reduces $K_t$, improving fatigue life.
- Improves melt flow into the corner (avoids weld lines).
- Reduces notch sensitivity at the corner.

**(Marks: 2)**

**3. External Radii:**

External corners need a radius of $\approx 1.5t$ to maintain **uniform wall thickness** at the corner junction. A sharp external corner creates a thin-out region, leading to warpage and stress concentration.

**(Marks: 1)**

**4. Sketches:**

> *(For exam: Draw three cross-sectional views showing (a) draft taper on a vertical wall, (b) a filleted internal corner, and (c) a radiused external corner with maintained wall thickness.)*

**(Marks: 2)**

---

**(b) Polypropylene Living Hinge Design (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Justify material choice.**

PP is the **gold standard** for living hinges because it is a **semi-crystalline polymer** with a **flexible, linear molecular chain** (low $T_g \approx -20°C$). When the hinge is flexed in service, the crystalline lamellae break up and the molecular chains **align along the hinge line**. This strain-induced orientation **strengthens the hinge** rather than degrading it. Amorphous polymers (PS, PC, ABS) cannot undergo this alignment and fail by **crazing and cracking** after a few cycles.

For only 30 flex cycles, even un-oriented PP will perform well; but the orientation effect gives a **20–100× improvement** in flex life.

**Step 2 — Recommended hinge thickness.**

$$t_{\text{hinge}} = 0.25 \text{ mm (absolute minimum)} \quad \text{to} \quad 0.5 \text{ mm (typical recommended)}$$

For a wall thickness $t = 2.5$ mm (typical for a bottle flip cap), the hinge thickness is **$\approx 0.3$ mm**, i.e., $\approx 12\%$ of the wall thickness.

**Step 3 — Molecular orientation mechanism.**

When the PP melt flows through the thin hinge section during moulding, the **long molecular chains align** in the flow direction. On cooling, the hinge is essentially **unidirectional-fibre-reinforced** by its own molecules. On first flex, the oriented chains lock into a "hinge memory." Subsequent flexing distributes strain over the aligned chains rather than fracturing random amorphous regions.

**Step 4 — Design considerations.**

- The hinge must be gated so that the **flow direction is perpendicular to the hinge axis** (chains align across the hinge line).
- The hinge section must be **uniformly thin** — any thick spot becomes a stress raiser.
- The two rigid sections meeting the hinge should have a **fillet of $0.4t - 0.6t$** at the hinge root to prevent crack initiation.

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

PP is an appropriate material; the recommended hinge thickness is **$0.3 - 0.5$ mm**; the molecular orientation mechanism provides the high flex life expected of a living hinge.

**(Marks: 7)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ☐ **Wall thickness uniformity is the master rule** — non-uniform walls cause 80% of moulding defects.
- ☐ **Draft angle of $1° - 2°$** is mandatory on all walls parallel to mould opening.
- ☐ **Fillet radius = $0.4t - 0.6t$** (internal) and **external radius = $1.5t$** to maintain uniform wall.
- ☐ **Rib thickness = $0.5t - 0.75t$**; **rib height $\leq 3t$**; **gap between ribs $\geq 2 \times t_r$**.
- ☐ **Bosses need 3 supporting ribs at $120°$** and a base fillet of $0.4t$.
- ☐ **Gussets are triangular corner reinforcements** with thickness $0.6t - 0.8t$.
- ☐ **Snap-fit strain** $\varepsilon = 3t\delta / 2L^2$ — must be $\leq 5\%$ for amorphous, $\leq 1\% - 2\%$ for glass-filled.
- ☐ **Living hinges work ONLY with PP/PE** — hinge thickness $0.25 - 0.5$ mm.
- ☐ **Holes: $L/d \leq 4$**; minimum diameter $1.0$ mm; add $0.5° - 1°$ core draft for deep holes.
- ☐ **Shrinkage allowance $0.001 - 0.020$** (0.1%–2%) — depends on polymer crystallinity.
- ☐ **Parting line should avoid cosmetic surfaces** and be located at the maximum cross-section.
- ☐ **Stiffness scales with $t^3$**, not $t$ — a 50% thickness increase = $\approx 3.4 \times$ stiffness.
- ☐ **For every design feature, memorize THREE things: Purpose, Numerical Rule, Failure Mode if Violated.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Examiner Tip:**
> In KTU board exams, always pair your answer with a **labelled sketch** (worth 2 marks alone) and a **defended numerical value** (worth 2–3 marks). A qualitative answer without numbers is treated as a "partial answer" and capped at 60% marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inside and Outside Corners
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inside and Outside Corners

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **polymer product design and molding**, an **inside corner** (also called an *internal corner*, *concave corner*, or *fillet*) is the location where two walls or surfaces of a molded part meet, forming a concave intersection from the perspective of the part's exterior. Geometrically, it is the re-entrant angle region of the molded component.

An **outside corner** (also called an *external corner*, *convex corner*, or *round*) is the location where two walls or surfaces meet forming a convex intersection — the outer projection of the part.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):**
> Corner design is one of the most critical aspects of polymer product design because corners govern **stress distribution**, **melt flow behavior**, **mold release (drafting)**, **warpage**, and **tool life**. The 2024 KTU scheme specifically tests corner design rules under Design-for-Manufacturing (DFM) principles.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

**Think of a plastic bucket used to carry water:**
- The **inside corner** of the bucket is where the side wall meets the base — like the inside corner of a cardboard box where the bottom flaps fold up.
- The **outside corner** is the outside of the same junction — like the pointed edge of the box.

If the inside corner is **sharp (zero radius)**, the polymer melt has difficulty flowing into the corner, leading to **voids, air traps, and incomplete filling**. Sharp outside corners form **stress concentration points**, much like a paper tear always begins at the corner because the corner concentrates the pulling force onto a tiny area.

To avoid this, designers add a small **fillet radius** (rounded curve) to inside corners, and a matching larger **round** to outside corners. A useful rule of thumb:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Corner Golden Rule (KTU Board Favourite):**
> - **Inside corner radius:** $R_i \geq 0.5 \times t$ where $t$ is the nominal wall thickness.
> - **Outside corner radius:** $R_o = R_i + t$
> This guarantees **uniform wall thickness**, which prevents **sink marks**, **weld lines**, and **differential shrinkage** during cooling.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Minimum recommended $R_i$ for amorphous polymers: **0.5 mm – 1.0 mm**
- Minimum recommended $R_i$ for semi-crystalline polymers: **1.0 mm – 1.5 mm**
- Minimum recommended $R_i$ for glass-fibre reinforced polymers: **1.5 mm – 2.0 mm**
- Standard wall thickness range for general purpose polymers: **1.0 mm – 4.0 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Corners in Polymer Parts

| Corner Type | Geometric Form | Design Concern | Molding Concern |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Sharp Inside Corner** ($R_i = 0$) | $90°$ re-entrant edge | Stress singularity | Voids, air traps |
| **Rounded Inside Corner** ($R_i > 0$) | Concave fillet | Stress concentration factor $K_t$ | Better melt flow |
| **Sharp Outside Corner** ($R_o = 0$) | Sharp convex edge | Stress raiser on external surface | Tool wear, flash |
| **Rounded Outside Corner** ($R_o > 0$) | Convex round | Distributes surface stress | Better ejection |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic for Inside Corners

1. **Step 1 — Identify the nominal wall thickness** $t$ of the part.
2. **Step 2 — Select the inside corner radius** $R_i$ based on:
   - Material flow characteristics
   - Filler content (glass, carbon fibre increases minimum)
   - Mechanical stress level
3. **Step 3 — Compute the outside corner radius** as $R_o = R_i + t$ to **maintain uniform wall thickness** through the corner.
4. **Step 4 — Verify sink mark potential** — the corner should not act as a thick section. The effective wall thickness at the corner must not exceed $1.25 \times t$ at the side walls.
5. **Step 5 — Apply draft angle** ($\geq 1°$ for most thermoplastics) to both side walls near the corner to facilitate ejection.

## 2.3 Stress Concentration Theory at Corners

When a sharp corner is loaded, the stress lines bunch together at the corner tip, creating a localized stress peak much higher than the nominal stress. For a **rounded inside corner** under bending or tensile loading, the **theoretical stress concentration factor** can be approximated using the **Inglis-Neuber relationship** for a notch of root radius $\rho$:

$$K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}}$$

where:
- $K_t$ = elastic stress concentration factor
- $a$ = half-length of the notch / characteristic dimension
- $\rho$ = radius of the inside corner (= $R_i$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As $R_i \to 0$ (perfectly sharp corner), $\rho \to 0$ and $K_t \to \infty$ — explaining why sharp corners always initiate cracks first in fatigue testing.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|----------------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $R_i \geq 0.5 \, t$ | Inside radius vs wall thickness | mm | Minimum fillet design |
| 2 | $R_o = R_i + t$ | Outside corner radius | mm | Maintain uniform wall |
| 3 | $t_{\text{corner}} \leq 1.25 \, t$ | Max effective thickness at corner | mm | Sink mark prevention |
| 4 | $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ | Stress concentration factor | dimensionless | Sharp vs rounded corner analysis |
| 5 | $\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{nom}}$ | Peak stress at corner | MPa | Failure prediction |
| 6 | Draft $\geq 1°$ (amorphous), $\geq 1.5°$ (semi-crystalline) | Ejection taper | degrees | Mould release |

> Note: The vertical bars in expressions such as $\vert K_t \vert$ and $\vert \sigma \vert$ must be rendered using `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Inside and outside corner design rules are applied universally in:
- **Injection moulded housings** (mobile phone cases, AC outer bodies)
- **Blow-moulded containers** (fuel tanks, water bottles)
- **Thermoformed trays**
- **Compression moulded FRP components**

In the **automotive industry**, corner design is a primary criterion in FEA (Finite Element Analysis) validation. A poorly designed corner can cause failure under cyclic loading far below the material's ultimate tensile strength, leading to warranty claims and recalls.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Wall Thickness at a Rounded Inside Corner

### Problem Statement
A polypropylene injection moulded part has a nominal wall thickness $t = 3$ mm and an inside corner radius $R_i$. Find the maximum allowable inside corner radius such that the effective wall thickness at the corner does not exceed $1.25 \, t$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**

Consider two walls of thickness $t$ meeting at an inside corner with radius $R_i$. The effective wall thickness at the corner $t_{\text{corner}}$ is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{corner}} &= R_i + t
\end{aligned}
$$

This represents the **maximum material path** through the corner (centreline of the wall on both sides plus the corner radius). If both walls have thickness $t$, the material travels a horizontal distance $R_i$ through the curve and the full thickness $t$ across each wall.

**Step 2 — Apply the Sink Mark Constraint**

To prevent sink marks, the KTU/DFM rule states:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{corner}} &\leq 1.25 \, t \\
R_i + t &\leq 1.25 \, t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Maximum $R_i$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i + t &\leq 1.25 \, t \\
R_i &\leq 1.25 \, t - t \\
R_i &\leq 0.25 \, t
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $t = 3$ mm**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_i &\leq 0.25 \times 3 \\
R_i &\leq 0.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the Design Rule**

The minimum recommended inside radius is $R_i \geq 0.5 \, t = 1.5$ mm. Since $0.75$ mm $< 1.5$ mm, the sink mark constraint is **automatically satisfied** if the designer follows the minimum radius rule. Therefore the binding design limit is **the minimum**, not the maximum — confirming the conservative nature of KTU corner design philosophy.

## 3.2 Derivation: Stress Concentration Factor Comparison

### Problem Statement
Compare the stress concentration factor $K_t$ at a sharp inside corner ($\rho = 0.05$ mm) versus a properly filleted corner ($\rho = 1.5$ mm) for a part with characteristic notch dimension $a = 5$ mm under nominal tensile stress $\sigma_{\text{nom}} = 30$ MPa.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute $K_t$ for the sharp corner ($\rho = 0.05$ mm)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{t,\text{sharp}} &= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{0.05}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{100} \\
&= 1 + 2 \times 10 \\
&= 21
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_t$ for the filleted corner ($\rho = 1.5$ mm)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{t,\text{round}} &= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{5}{1.5}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{3.333} \\
&= 1 + 2 \times 1.826 \\
&= 4.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Peak Stresses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max,\text{sharp}} &= K_{t,\text{sharp}} \times \sigma_{\text{nom}} = 21 \times 30 = 630 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_{\max,\text{round}} &= K_{t,\text{round}} \times \sigma_{\text{nom}} = 4.65 \times 30 = 139.5 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

The sharp corner produces a **peak stress more than 4.5 times higher** than the filleted corner for the same applied load. For polypropylene (tensile yield $\approx 30$–35 MPa), the sharp corner **would fail catastrophically** under nominal service loads, while the filleted corner remains well within elastic limits.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Corner Design Decision Table

| Application Scenario | Recommended $R_i$ | Recommended $R_o$ | Reasoning |
|----------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Thin-wall PP housing ($t = 1$ mm) | 0.5 mm | 1.5 mm | Minimum for melt flow |
| Standard ABS enclosure ($t = 2.5$ mm) | 1.25 mm | 3.75 mm | $R_i = 0.5t$, $R_o = R_i + t$ |
| Glass-filled Nylon gear ($t = 4$ mm) | 2.0 mm | 6.0 mm | Fibre orientation needs larger radius |
| HDPE bottle base ($t = 3$ mm) | 1.5 mm | 4.5 mm | Semi-crystalline, high shrinkage |
| Snap-fit cantilever (PC, $t = 2$ mm) | 0.5 mm | 2.5 mm | Stress relief at the bend root |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Corner Design Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: New Polymer Part Design"] --> B{"Determine Nominal Wall Thickness t?"}
    B -- Known --> C["Select Material Type"]
    B -- Unknown --> D["Perform Wall Thickness Optimization"]
    C --> E{"Amorphous or Semi-crystalline?"}
    E -- Amorphous --> F["Set Minimum R_i = 0.5 mm"]
    E -- Semi-crystalline --> G["Set Minimum R_i = 1.0 mm"]
    E -- Glass-filled --> H["Set Minimum R_i = 1.5 mm"]
    F --> I["Compute R_i = 0.5 multiplied by t"]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J["Compute R_o = R_i + t"]
    J --> K{"Check Sink Mark Constraint t_corner less than 1.25 t?"}
    K -- Yes --> L["Apply Draft Angle at least 1 degree"]
    K -- No --> M["Reduce R_i to meet sink rule"]
    M --> I
    L --> N["Verify Stress Concentration via FEA"]
    N --> O{"Peak Stress less than Allowable?"}
    O -- Yes --> P["Finalize Corner Geometry"]
    O -- No --> Q["Increase R_i and Re-iterate"]
    Q --> I
    P --> R["End: Release to Tool Design"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Corner Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Part["Molded Polymer Part"]
        W1["Wall 1 (thickness t)"]
        W2["Wall 2 (thickness t)"]
        IC["Inside Corner Radius R_i"]
        OC["Outside Corner Radius R_o = R_i + t"]
        DA["Draft Angle 1 to 1.5 deg"]
    end
    subgraph Tooling["Injection Mold Tool"]
        Core["Core Side Cavity"]
        Cavity["Cavity Side Insert"]
        Eject["Ejector Pin Region"]
    end
    subgraph Process["Processing Effects"]
        Flow["Melt Flow into Corner"]
        Cool["Differential Cooling"]
        Shrink["Shrinkage and Warpage"]
    end
    W1 --- IC
    W2 --- IC
    IC --- OC
    W1 --- DA
    W2 --- DA
    Core --- Cavity
    Cavity --- Eject
    IC -. affects .-> Flow
    OC -. affects .-> Cool
    DA -. affects .-> Eject
    Shrink -. driven by .-> IC
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Inside vs Outside Corner Geometry (ASCII Visualisation)

```
  OUTSIDE CORNER (Convex)
  
          _______
         /        <-- R_o (outside radius)
        /
        |  WALL 1
        |
        |       WALL 2
        \________
                \
                 \_______
                 
  ══════════════════════════════════════
  
  INSIDE CORNER (Concave)
  
        |         |
        |         |
        |         | WALL 1
   _____|         |_____
  /                     \
 (   R_i  <-- corner     )   WALL 2
  \_____________________/
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define an inside corner and an outside corner in polymer product design. Why are sharp inside corners avoided in injection moulded parts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Inside corner (1 mark):** The concave junction where two walls of a moulded part meet on the interior of the part. The local re-entrant geometry is described by the inside corner radius $R_i$.
- **Outside corner (1 mark):** The convex junction on the exterior of the part, defined by the outside corner radius $R_o$.
- **Reasons to avoid sharp inside corners (1 mark):**
  1. Sharp corners act as **stress raisers** ($K_t \to \infty$ as $R_i \to 0$), initiating cracks.
  2. They cause **melt flow stagnation**, producing voids and short shots.
  3. They create **thick sections** that shrink non-uniformly, causing sink marks and warpage.
  4. Sharp corners induce **differential cooling**, leading to residual stresses.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the KTU design rule relating inside corner radius ($R_i$) and outside corner radius ($R_o$) to wall thickness ($t$). Why is this rule critical for uniform wall thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Rule (2 marks):** $R_i \geq 0.5 \, t$ and $R_o = R_i + t$.
- **Criticality (1 mark):** The relation $R_o = R_i + t$ ensures that the effective wall thickness at the corner equals the nominal wall thickness of the adjoining walls, preventing the formation of a thick "bubble" that would sink, warp, or hold residual stress during cooling.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the difference between inside and outside corners in polymer moulded parts. Discuss the effect of corner geometry on **(i)** melt flow, **(ii)** stress concentration, and **(iii)** ejection of the part. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene part has a nominal wall thickness of 3 mm. The designer proposes an inside corner radius of 2 mm. Calculate:
   (i) the required outside corner radius,
   (ii) the effective wall thickness at the corner,
   (iii) whether the sink mark constraint ($t_{\text{corner}} \leq 1.25 \, t$) is satisfied. Comment on the suitability of the proposed design. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Corner Geometry & Effects (7 marks)**

| Aspect | Inside Corner ($R_i$) | Outside Corner ($R_o$) |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Sketch description** | Concave, re-entrant | Convex, projecting |
| **Melt flow (1.5 m)** | Sharp $R_i$ causes flow stagnation and voids. Larger $R_i$ promotes smooth laminar flow. | Sharp $R_o$ creates air-trap regions near cavity walls. Larger $R_o$ smooths flow front. |
| **Stress concentration (2 m)** | Acts as a notch — peak stress occurs at the **inside** of the bend. $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$. Reducing this requires increasing $R_i$. | Outside corner carries **compressive** peak stress under bending; less critical but sharp $R_o$ causes surface cracks and tool wear. |
| **Ejection (1.5 m)** | Affects material shrinkage direction. | Sharp $R_o$ causes drag on cavity wall; rounded $R_o$ reduces ejection force and prevents surface damage. Draft angle $\geq 1°$ must be added to both walls emerging from the corner. |

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given:** $t = 3$ mm, $R_i = 2$ mm.

**(i) Outside corner radius**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o &= R_i + t \\
R_o &= 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Effective wall thickness at the corner**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{corner}} &= R_i + t \\
t_{\text{corner}} &= 2 + 3 = 5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Sink mark constraint check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Allowable } t_{\text{corner}} &= 1.25 \times t = 1.25 \times 3 = 3.75 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Actual } t_{\text{corner}} &= 5 \text{ mm} \\
5 \text{ mm} &> 3.75 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \text{Constraint VIOLATED}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The proposed design **fails the sink mark constraint** because the inside corner radius (2 mm) exceeds the maximum permissible $R_i = 0.25 \, t = 0.75$ mm. The corrected design should reduce $R_i$ to at most **0.75 mm** (and ideally use the minimum recommended value of $R_i = 0.5 \, t = 1.5$ mm) and accordingly set $R_o = 1.5 + 3 = 4.5$ mm. The redesign ensures uniform wall thickness and prevents sink marks.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative choice for Question A)*
**(a)** With the help of a labelled diagram, derive the relationship between inside corner radius ($R_i$), outside corner radius ($R_o$), and wall thickness ($t$). Explain how this relationship maintains uniform wall thickness at corners. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An ABS enclosure has a sharp inside corner ($\rho = 0.1$ mm) at a section where the notch characteristic length is $a = 4$ mm. The part is subjected to a nominal tensile stress of 25 MPa.
   (i) Calculate the stress concentration factor $K_t$ for the sharp corner.
   (ii) The design is modified to a filleted corner with $R_i = 2$ mm. Compute the new $K_t$ and compare the peak stresses in both cases.
   (iii) State two design modifications you would recommend to further reduce stress at the corner. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $R_o = R_i + t$ (7 marks)**

**Diagram description (2 marks):** Draw a top view showing two walls of thickness $t$ meeting at an inside corner of radius $R_i$. The material path through the corner is traced along the centreline of each wall.

**Geometric reasoning (3 marks):** The path of the polymer melt (and the corresponding wall cross-section) through the inside corner comprises:
- The horizontal travel through the curved portion = $R_i$
- The full thickness of the wall = $t$

Hence the effective wall thickness at the corner is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{corner}} &= R_i + t
\end{aligned}
$$

For the wall thickness to be **continuous** (no step change from straight wall to corner), the outside corner radius $R_o$ must be:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_o &= R_i + t
\end{aligned}
$$

This ensures that the **outer fibre** of the wall follows a continuous arc, and the **inner fibre** follows a parallel arc offset by the wall thickness $t$.

**Why uniform wall thickness matters (2 marks):**
1. **Even cooling** — uniform thickness means uniform cooling rate, which minimises differential shrinkage and warpage.
2. **No sink marks** — thick sections at corners would shrink more than thin walls, leaving visible depressions on the cosmetic surface.
3. **Predictable mould shrinkage** — designers can apply a single shrinkage factor rather than multiple localised corrections.

**(b) Stress Analysis (7 marks)**

**Given:** $\rho_{\text{sharp}} = 0.1$ mm, $\rho_{\text{round}} = 2$ mm, $a = 4$ mm, $\sigma_{\text{nom}} = 25$ MPa.

**(i) $K_t$ for sharp corner (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{t,\text{sharp}} &= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho_{\text{sharp}}}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{4}{0.1}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{40} \\
&= 1 + 2 \times 6.32 \\
&= 13.65
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) $K_t$ for filleted corner and comparison (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_{t,\text{round}} &= 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{4}{2}} \\
&= 1 + 2\sqrt{2} \\
&= 1 + 2 \times 1.414 \\
&= 3.83
\end{aligned}
$$

Peak stresses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\max,\text{sharp}} &= K_{t,\text{sharp}} \times \sigma_{\text{nom}} = 13.65 \times 25 = 341.3 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_{\max,\text{round}} &= K_{t,\text{round}} \times \sigma_{\text{nom}} = 3.83 \times 25 = 95.7 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Reduction in peak stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reduction} &= 341.3 - 95.7 = 245.6 \text{ MPa} \\
\text{Percentage reduction} &= \dfrac{245.6}{341.3} \times 100 \approx 72\%
\end{aligned}
$$

The filleted corner reduces the peak stress by approximately **72 %**, bringing it well below the ABS tensile strength (~40 MPa allowable with safety factor), confirming the design's viability.

**(iii) Two further design modifications (2 marks):**
1. **Increase the inside corner radius further** to $R_i = 4$ mm (or to $R_i = t = 4$ mm) to bring $K_t$ closer to 1.
2. **Use a stress-relieving fillet profile** — a non-circular (elliptical or "fat" fillet) that reduces stress more efficiently per unit radius increase.
3. **Add a local reinforcement rib** near the corner to redistribute load away from the high-stress region.
4. **Use a material with higher fracture toughness** (e.g., PC/ABS blend instead of plain ABS).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students frequently lose 2–3 marks because they:
> - Confuse $R_i$ (inside radius) with $R_o$ (outside radius) when writing the formula $R_o = R_i + t$.
> - Forget to verify the **sink mark constraint** $t_{\text{corner}} \leq 1.25 t$ after calculating $R_i$.
> - Use the wrong equation for $K_t$ — KTU expects the Inglis/Neuber approximation $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$, not the full Peterson charts unless the question explicitly provides them.
> - Forget to attach **units (mm, MPa)** in the final numerical answer.
> - Skip the **comment / interpretation step** that examiners specifically look for at the end of the solution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inside and Outside Corners**
>
> - **Inside corner radius** $R_i$ must be **at least $0.5 \times t$** to allow proper melt flow and avoid stress singularities.
> - **Outside corner radius** $R_o = R_i + t$ guarantees **uniform wall thickness** through the corner.
> - **Maximum $R_i$** is constrained by the **sink mark rule**: $t_{\text{corner}} = R_i + t \leq 1.25 \, t$, giving $R_i \leq 0.25 \, t$.
> - **Sharp corners produce infinite $K_t$**; rounding reduces $K_t$ per $K_t = 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$.
> - **Draft angle** of $\geq 1°$ (amorphous) or $\geq 1.5°$ (semi-crystalline) must accompany corner design for ejection.
> - **Glass-filled polymers** require **larger radii** (1.5–2.0 mm minimum) due to fibre orientation effects.
> - **Semi-crystalline polymers** require **larger radii** than amorphous polymers because of higher shrinkage.
> - Always **verify the sink mark constraint** after choosing $R_i$.
> - Always **compute peak stress** via $\sigma_{\max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{\text{nom}}$ and compare to allowable design stress.
> - In KTU board answers, **sketch the corner** before solving — examiners award 1–2 marks for correct diagrams.
> - **Mention real-world examples** (bottles, gears, enclosures) wherever applicable to score the application-level marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wall Thickness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wall Thickness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Product Design (PCPOT601 – KTU 2024 Scheme)**, **Wall Thickness** is defined as the perpendicular distance measured between the two opposing inner and outer surfaces of a hollow or semi-hollow molded/formed polymeric article. It is the single most critical geometric parameter that simultaneously governs the **mechanical performance, dimensional accuracy, processability, thermal behavior, mold economics, and aesthetic quality** of any injection-molded, blow-molded, rotomolded, or thermoformed plastic product.

Mathematically, for any uniform-walled article:

$$t = \frac{V}{A_s}$$

where $t$ is the nominal wall thickness, $V$ is the internal moldable volume of the cavity, and $A_s$ is the internal surface area of the cavity. The units are universally expressed in **millimeters (mm)** in the KTU/Indian industry context.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Design Considerations):**
> Wall thickness is categorized as a **Primary Design Parameter** because nearly every other design feature (ribs, bosses, draft, radii, sink marks, weld lines, cycle time) is either a *direct consequence* of the chosen wall thickness or must be *designed around* it. Under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, mastering wall thickness directly addresses **CO2: Apply design principles to develop polymer products**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Overview)

Imagine you are blowing a **soap bubble** with a straw. The soap film is your "wall." If the film is thin, the bubble is delicate, deforms easily, wobbles in the slightest breeze, and pops quickly. If the film is thick, the bubble is heavy, sluggish, may not even form a sphere, takes forever to dry, and sags downward.

A **polymer part behaves identically**:

| Soap Bubble Scenario | Polymer Product Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Thin film (low thickness) | Sink marks, warpage, low stiffness, short shots |
| Thick film (high thickness) | Long cycle time, voids, residual stress, sinkage |
| Uniform film everywhere | Predictable shrinkage, no warpage, balanced flow |
| Sudden thick-to-thin change | Stress concentration, weld line weakness, hesitation |

**The Engineering Takeaway:** Like the bubble, polymer walls must be **uniform, balanced, and just thick enough** to perform their structural function — no more, no less. Over-designing thickness is just as bad as under-designing it, because every extra 0.5 mm of wall thickness can increase cycle time by ~10–15% and part weight by 15–25%.

---

## 1.3 The Three Universal "Golden Rules" of Wall Thickness

> [!NOTE]
> These three rules are universally tested in KTU examinations and are cited in standard references such as *Malloy – Plastic Part Design for Injection Molding* and *Becker – Plastic Product Design*.

**Rule 1 — Uniformity:**
Maintain as **uniform a wall thickness as possible** throughout the entire part. Maximum permissible variation is typically **± 0.10 mm to ± 0.25 mm** for precision parts.

**Rule 2 — Thinnest Practical Section:**
