> **KTU Exam Tip:** The **single most-cited number** in board questions is **1° per side** as the default minimum for unreinforced thermoplastics. Memorise this.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown — Why Draft Angles Work

1. **Shrinkage-induced radial pressure:** As the molten polymer cools, it contracts around the mold core, generating a normal (radial) pressure $P_r$ on the core surface.
2. **Friction at ejection:** The frictional resistance to ejection is:
$$F_f = \mu \cdot P_r \cdot A_s$$
where $\mu$ = coefficient of friction polymer-on-steel ($\approx 0.20$–$0.35$), and $A_s$ = surface contact area.
3. **Taper breaks the vacuum and reduces contact area:** A draft of $\alpha$ reduces the effective contact area by a factor of $\cos(\alpha)$ and creates a small gap that allows air to bleed in, breaking the partial vacuum.
4. **Ejector force available:** Modern ejector systems provide 2–5 kN of force; the draft keeps the required force well below this limit.

### 2.2 Types of Draft in Molds

- **External draft:** Applied to the **outside** walls of a part that are formed by the **cavity** side. Draft is added so the part is larger at the parting line.
- **Internal draft:** Applied to the **inside** walls formed by the **core**. Draft is added so the core is larger at the parting line and smaller at the deep end (so the part releases from the core).
- **Dual draft (split-draft):** Draft applied in **two directions** along the same wall — common in threaded cores and deep ribs.
- **Reverse draft / Undercut:** Deliberate *negative* angle that requires side-action cams, slides, or collapsible cores (a separate, advanced topic).

### 2.3 Factors Governing the Magnitude of Draft Angle

> [!NOTE]
> **The "SLESS-PT" Mnemonic (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **S**hrinkage rate of polymer
> - **L**ength/depth of the wall
> - **E**jection method (mechanical pin, sleeve, stripper plate)
> - **S**urface finish (SPI polish grade)
> - **S**tiffness/modulus of the material
> - **P**arting line geometry
> - **T**exture depth

| # | Governing Factor | Effect on Draft Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Higher material **shrinkage** | ↑ increase draft |
| 2 | Greater **wall depth** $L$ | ↑ increase draft (linearly) |
| 3 | **Stripper-plate** ejection (vs. pin) | ↓ can reduce draft by ~50 % |
| 4 | **Textured** surface (MT/VDI 3400) | ↑ add 1° per 0.025 mm of texture |
| 5 | **High-modulus** (GF-filled, rigid) material | ↓ can use 0.5° minimum |
| 6 | **Soft / flexible** polymer (TPE) | ↑ use 2° or more |
| 7 | **Tight tolerance** on a mating face | ↓ minimize draft on that face only |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ | Draft allowance at parting line | mm |
| 2 | $D_s = L \cdot \tan(\alpha) \cdot (1+S)$ | Draft compensated for shrinkage | mm |
| 3 | $\alpha_{\text{min}} = 0.5^{\circ}$ | Minimum for rigid, smooth, unreinforced | ° |
| 4 | $\alpha_{\text{default}} = 1^{\circ}$ | Industry standard default | ° |
| 5 | $\alpha_{\text{text}} = \alpha_{\text{base}} + 1^{\circ}/0.025\,\text{mm}$ | Texture-adjusted draft | ° |
| 6 | $\alpha_{\text{TPE}} = 2^{\circ}\,\text{to}\,3^{\circ}$ | Soft elastomer range | ° |
| 7 | $F_{\text{eject}} \geq \mu \cdot P_r \cdot A_s \cdot \cos(\alpha)$ | Required ejection force | N |
| 8 | $L_{\text{max\_no\_DA}} \approx 50\,\text{mm}$ | Approximate safe depth without draft (PP/PE) | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All values above are engineering-rule-of-thumb**, not absolute laws. KTU expects the **default $1°$** and the **shrinkage-compensation form** $D_s$ as the two highest-weight concepts.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive interior trim:** Draft ensures Class-A surface parts release without sink marks or drag lines.
- **Medical syringes (PP/PE):** Draft on the internal bore allows the core pin to retract without scoring the lumen.
- **Threaded closures (PET bottle preforms):** Drafts of $0.5°$ to $1°$ are built into thread flanks to allow unscrewing ejection.
- **Consumer electronics housings:** Side-action slides are avoided wherever possible by allowing sufficient draft — reducing mold cost by **30 %–50 %**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Draft Allowance from Wall Depth and Angle

**Statement:** For a wall of depth $L$ inclined at an angle $\alpha$ to the ejection axis, derive the horizontal draft allowance $D$ at the parting line.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** Consider a right-triangle where:
- The vertical leg (along the mold-opening axis) = $L$
- The included angle between the slanted wall and the vertical = $\alpha$
- The horizontal leg (at the parting line) = $D$

**Step 2 — Apply the tangent function.** By definition, in a right-triangle:

$$\tan(\alpha) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{D}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to solve for $D$.**

$$D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — For small angles (engineering rule of thumb).** Since $\alpha$ is typically $\leq 3°$, use the small-angle approximation $\tan(\alpha) \approx \alpha \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{180}$:

$$D \approx L \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{\pi}{180}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check.** Take $L = 100$ mm and $\alpha = 1°$:

$$D = 100 \cdot \tan(1^{\circ}) = 100 \cdot 0.01745 = 1.745\,\text{mm}$$

This is consistent with the industry rule of thumb "**1° draft gives roughly 0.0175 mm per mm of depth**".

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shrinkage-Compensated Draft

**Statement:** The cavity is machined to a *scaled-up* size of factor $(1+S)$ to compensate for mould-shrinkage $S$ (expressed as a fraction, e.g., 0.020 for 2 %). Show that the compensated draft allowance becomes:

$$D_s = L \cdot \tan(\alpha) \cdot (1+S)$$

**Step 1 — Uncompensated draft at nominal scale.** The design draft gives a linear offset:

$$D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 2 — Apply linear scaling to all cavity dimensions.** To pre-shrink the cavity (so the cooled part matches the nominal drawing), every linear dimension — including the draft offset — is multiplied by $(1+S)$.

**Step 3 — Write the compensated offset.**

$$D_s = (1+S) \cdot L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** PP with $S = 0.020$ (2 %), wall depth $L = 50$ mm, $\alpha = 1.5°$:

$$D_s = (1+0.020) \cdot 50 \cdot \tan(1.5^{\circ})$$

$$D_s = 1.020 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.02618 = 1.335\,\text{mm}$$

(Compared to $D = 1.309$ mm uncompensated — a difference of $0.026$ mm, which is small but critical for tight-tolerance parts.)

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Texture-Adjusted Draft

**Statement:** The SPI/VDA surface-finish standard adds **1° of draft for every 0.025 mm (1 thou) of texture depth** to prevent the ejecting part from scuffing against the cavity texture. Derive the total required draft.

**Step 1 — Base draft from material selection.**

$$\alpha_{\text{base}} = 1^{\circ}\,\text{(for rigid, smooth, unreinforced)}$$

**Step 2 — Texture contribution.** Let $R_t$ be the maximum peak-to-valley texture depth in mm. The texture-induced additional draft is:

$$\alpha_{\text{texture}} = \frac{R_t}{0.025\,\text{mm}} \cdot 1^{\circ}$$

**Step 3 — Total draft.**

$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = \alpha_{\text{base}} + \alpha_{\text{texture}} = 1^{\circ} + \frac{R_t}{0.025}\,(^{\circ})$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.** A part with SPI-MT-11010 finish (texture depth $\approx 0.020$ mm) on PP ($S = 0.02$):

$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = 1^{\circ} + \frac{0.020}{0.025} \cdot 1^{\circ} = 1^{\circ} + 0.8^{\circ} = 1.8^{\circ}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Flow

| Question | Decision Rule | Resulting Draft |
|---|---|---|
| Is the material rigid amorphous (PS, ABS)? | Use minimum | **0.5° – 1°** |
| Is the material semi-crystalline (PA, POM)? | Add 0.5° for shrinkage grip | **1° – 2°** |
| Is the material glass-filled (>15 % GF)? | Stiffer flow, lower draft | **0.75° – 1°** |
| Is the material TPE / TPU? | Soft, high friction | **2° – 3°** |
| Is the surface polished (SPI-A1)? | No texture contribution | base draft only |
| Is the surface textured (MT/VDI)? | Add $1°/0.025$ mm | base + texture |
| Is the wall depth >100 mm? | Add extra to prevent scuffing | base × 1.5 |
| Is the part dimensionally critical at the parting face? | Draft on other faces only | selective draft |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Draft Angle Geometry — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of how draft angle is generated within a two-plate injection mould, showing the cavity/core, the ejector system, and the resulting draft on the molded part.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MOLD BASE - FIXED SIDE"] --> B["CAVITY PLATE"]
    B --> C["CAVITY INSERT - DRAFT ON OUTSIDE WALLS"]
    D["MOLD BASE - MOVING SIDE"] --> E["CORE PLATE"]
    E --> F["CORE INSERT - DRAFT ON INSIDE WALLS"]
    F --> G["EJECTOR PINS"]
    G --> H["PART RELEASE PLANE"]
    C --> I["MOLDED PART - FRUSTUM SHAPE"]
    F --> I
    I --> J["DRAFT ANGLE ALPHA ON EACH WALL"]
    I --> K["WALL DEPTH L ALONG EJECTION AXIS"]
    J --> L["D = L x TAN ALPHA AT PARTING LINE"]
    K --> L
    L --> M["EJECTION SUCCESS - NO DRAG LINES"]
```

### 4.2 Draft Application — Cavity vs Core Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["CAVITY SIDE - EXTERNAL DRAFT"]
        A1["Wall larger at parting line"] --> A2["Draft opens OUTWARD"]
    end
    subgraph B["CORE SIDE - INTERNAL DRAFT"]
        B1["Core larger at parting line"] --> B2["Draft opens INWARD ON PART"]
    end
    A2 --> C["PART RELEASES OUT OF CAVITY FIRST"]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D["EJECTOR PINS PUSH PART OFF CORE"]
```

### 4.3 Process Flow — Draft Angle Selection in Product Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Start - Define Part Geometry"] --> S2["Identify Wall Depth L"]
    S2 --> S3["Select Base Draft from Material Table"]
    S3 --> S4{"Is Surface Textured?"}
    S4 -- YES --> S5["Add 1 degree per 0.025 mm texture"]
    S4 -- NO --> S6["Keep base draft"]
    S5 --> S7{"Is Wall Depth more than 100 mm?"}
    S6 --> S7
    S7 -- YES --> S8["Increase draft by factor 1.5"]
    S7 -- NO --> S9["Compute draft allowance D = L x tan alpha"]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10["Apply shrinkage compensation factor 1 + S"]
    S10 --> S11["Final D = L x tan alpha x 1 + S"]
    S11 --> S12["Encode in 2D drawing and 3D model"]
    S12 --> S13["End - Pass to Tool Design"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams render as **functional flow architecture**. In an actual injection-mould cross-section, the cavity and core inserts are shown as two mirror-tapered blocks forming the part cavity, with the draft angle drawn as a slight wedge (typically exaggerated to 5°–10° on engineering drawings for visibility, even when the real value is 1°).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[Q-A1] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> **Define draft angle. Why is it provided on the walls of injection-moulded plastic parts?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Draft angle is the intentional taper provided on the vertical walls of a moulded plastic part, measured in degrees from the nominal wall orientation. It is provided to (i) facilitate smooth ejection of the cooled part from the mould cavity and core, (ii) prevent damage to the part surface such as drag lines and scratches, (iii) reduce wear on the mould surfaces, and (iv) shorten cycle time by lowering the required ejection force. A minimum of **1°** is recommended for rigid thermoplastics.

> **[Q-A2] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **List any four factors that influence the magnitude of draft angle required for a moulded plastic component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four major factors are:
1. **Type and grade of plastic** (shrinkage rate, stiffness, filler content).
2. **Wall depth** of the feature (deeper walls need larger draft).
3. **Surface finish** of the mould (textured surfaces need more draft than polished ones).
4. **Method of ejection** (stripper-plate ejection allows smaller draft than ejector pins).

*(Alternative 4th factor: part geometry / wall thickness uniformity / parting line location.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[Q-B1A] [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Choice A]** **(14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **concept of draft angle** in injection-moulded plastic parts. Differentiate between **internal and external draft** with reference to the cavity and core. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A polypropylene (PP) container has a vertical wall of **depth 80 mm**. If the recommended draft is **1.5° per side**, calculate: (i) the **draft allowance** at the parting line, and (ii) the **shrinkage-compensated draft allowance** for PP (shrinkage $S = 0.020$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Concept (3 marks):** Draft angle is the taper applied to walls parallel to the direction of mould opening. It ensures that as the mould opens, the part separates cleanly without binding. The **draft allowance** $D$ at the parting line is $D = L \tan(\alpha)$.
- **External draft on cavity (2 marks):** Walls formed by the cavity insert are tapered so the part is *larger at the parting line* and *smaller at the bottom*. Draft opens outward; the cavity wall leans inward into the steel block.
- **Internal draft on core (2 marks):** Walls formed by the core insert are tapered in the *opposite* sense — the core is *larger at the parting line* and *smaller at the deep end*, so the part releases from the core as it shrinks onto the core.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Given: $L = 80$ mm, $\alpha = 1.5^{\circ}$, $S = 0.020$ (i.e. 2 %).

**(i) Draft allowance at the parting line (3 marks):**

$$D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 80 \cdot \tan(1.5^{\circ})$$

$$D = 80 \cdot 0.02618 = 2.094\,\text{mm} \approx 2.10\,\text{mm}$$

**(ii) Shrinkage-compensated draft allowance (4 marks):**

$$D_s = (1+S) \cdot L \cdot \tan(\alpha) = (1+0.020) \cdot 2.094$$

$$D_s = 1.020 \cdot 2.094 = 2.136\,\text{mm} \approx 2.14\,\text{mm}$$

**Conclusion:** The mould cavity/core must be cut such that the parting-line offset is $2.14$ mm (slightly larger than the nominal $2.10$ mm) to ensure the *cooled* part has exactly $2.10$ mm of draft for clean ejection.

> **[Q-B1B] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Choice B]** **(14 Marks)**
>
> **(a)** Discuss the **factors affecting the magnitude of draft angle** for injection-moulded plastic parts. Provide typical draft values for: (i) rigid amorphous, (ii) semi-crystalline, and (iii) TPE materials. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 150 mm deep moulded box in ABS (shrinkage $S = 0.005$) has a textured surface with peak-to-valley depth $R_t = 0.050$ mm. Determine the **total recommended draft angle** and the **draft allowance at the parting line**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**
- **Five factors (5 marks, 1 mark each):**
  1. **Material shrinkage** — higher shrinkage → higher draft.
  2. **Wall depth** — deeper walls need more draft.
  3. **Surface texture** — textured mould surfaces need more draft.
  4. **Ejection method** — stripper plates allow smaller drafts than pins.
  5. **Part stiffness** — soft materials (TPE) need more draft; rigid materials (GF-filled) need less.

- **Typical values (2 marks):**
  - (i) Rigid amorphous (PS, ABS, PC) → **0.5° to 1°**
  - (ii) Semi-crystalline (PP, PE, PA) → **1° to 2°**
  - (iii) TPE / TPU → **2° to 3°**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**
Given: $L = 150$ mm, $S = 0.005$ (negligible here for draft calculation), $R_t = 0.050$ mm, base material ABS (rigid amorphous → base draft $= 1°$).

**(i) Texture contribution (3 marks):**

$$\alpha_{\text{texture}} = \frac{R_t}{0.025\,\text{mm}} \cdot 1^{\circ} = \frac{0.050}{0.025} \cdot 1^{\circ} = 2.0^{\circ}$$

**(ii) Total draft angle (2 marks):**

$$\alpha_{\text{total}} = \alpha_{\text{base}} + \alpha_{\text{texture}} = 1.0^{\circ} + 2.0^{\circ} = 3.0^{\circ}$$

**(iii) Draft allowance at parting line (2 marks):**

$$D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha_{\text{total}}) = 150 \cdot \tan(3^{\circ})$$

$$D = 150 \cdot 0.05241 = 7.86\,\text{mm}$$

**Conclusion:** A 150 mm deep textured ABS box requires a total draft of $3^{\circ}$ and a parting-line offset of approximately $7.86$ mm. The shrinkage correction is small ($D_s = 7.86 \times 1.005 = 7.90$ mm) and often ignored in standard practice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Draft-Angle Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion in the small-angle formula.** If you use $\tan(1^{\circ})$ in a calculator set to **radian mode**, you will get $\tan(1) = 1.557$ instead of $\tan(1^{\circ}) = 0.01745$. This will cost you **3 marks** instantly. **Always verify the calculator is in DEG mode.**
> 2. **Confusing draft angle (α) with draft allowance (D).** They are *not* the same; one is in degrees, the other in millimetres. Many students write "$D = 1.5°$" — a unit error that loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring the texture adjustment.** A question that mentions "textured surface" almost always expects the **$1°/0.025\,\text{mm}$** rule. Skipping it costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting shrinkage compensation.** When the polymer is named (PP, PA, ABS), the examiner expects the $(1+S)$ factor. Not applying it costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Drawing the taper in the wrong direction on a sketch.** On a *cavity wall*, the taper must open outward (part larger at the parting line). On a *core wall*, the taper opens inward into the part. Reversed sketch → **−1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Fire Revision Checklist — "DR-AFT-5":**
> - **D**efinition: deliberate taper on walls parallel to mould-opening axis (°).
> - **R**ule of thumb default: **1° per side** for rigid thermoplastics.
> - **A**llowance formula: $D = L \cdot \tan(\alpha)$ — units of length.
> - **F**ormula for shrinkage compensation: multiply by $(1+S)$.
> - **T**exture rule: add **1° per 0.025 mm** of texture depth.
> - **5 factors** that increase draft: ↑ shrinkage, ↑ wall depth, ↑ texture depth, soft material, pin ejection.
> - **Cavity** draft opens outward; **core** draft opens inward on the part.
> - Reverse draft = **undercut** → requires side-action / cam / split cavity.
> - Draft reduces **ejection force** and **mould wear**, and **eliminates drag lines**.
> - Deep walls (>$100$ mm) demand more draft than the standard 1°.

> **Most-Asked Values (memorise):**
> - $\tan(0.5^{\circ}) \approx 0.00873$
> - $\tan(1.0^{\circ}) \approx 0.01745$
> - $\tan(1.5^{\circ}) \approx 0.02618$
> - $\tan(2.0^{\circ}) \approx 0.03492$
> - $\tan(3.0^{\circ}) \approx 0.05241$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weld Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Weld Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Weld Lines in Injection Molding

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weld Line (Definition):** A weld line is a visible surface or internal defect formed in an injection-molded polymer part when two (or more) separate melt flow fronts, which have been split by a mold feature (such as a hole, boss, insert, or change in wall thickness), rejoin and fuse together during the cavity filling stage.

**Meld Line vs. Weld Line — The Critical Distinction:**

| Feature | Weld Line | Meld Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Formation Cause** | Melt fronts meet after being separated by a solid obstruction (pin, hole, insert) | Melt fronts meet without any obstruction in the flow path (e.g., two gates meeting head-on) |
| **Temperature at Meeting** | Both fronts are partially cooled at the obstruction → poorer fusion | Higher residual temperature retained → generally better fusion |
| **Strength** | Significantly weaker (often **40–90%** reduction vs. base material) | Moderately weaker (typically **10–30%** reduction) |
| **Visual Appearance** | Deeper V-notch on surface | Shallower, smoother |

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 KTU Board Focus:** Examiners frequently ask students to (a) differentiate between weld and meld lines, (b) draw schematics showing their formation, and (c) suggest design remedies. Memorize the comparison table above.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are pouring two streams of hot melted chocolate onto a plate from opposite sides of a small cookie. The two streams approach the cookie, split around it (because the cookie is a solid obstacle), and must meet again on the far side. By the time they meet, both streams have cooled a bit, and where they "kiss" you can clearly see a seam — a thin, weaker line in the chocolate. The chocolate snaps most easily right along that seam.

**This seam is exactly what a weld line is in a polymer part:**
- The **mold cavity insert/hole** = the cookie (the obstruction)
- The **two melt flow fronts** = the two streams of chocolate
- The **seam where they meet** = the weld line
- The **weaker joint** = the reduced mechanical strength region

**Real-world impact:** A weld line on a plastic gear tooth, a snap-fit clip, or a structural bracket acts as a pre-existing crack — failure will initiate there under cyclic or impact loading, even though the rest of the part may be perfectly molded.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Typical weld line strength reduction:** **40 – 90 %** of base material tensile strength for semi-crystalline polymers; **10 – 30 %** for amorphous polymers.
- **Surface notch depth (V-notch):** typically **5 – 50 µm** deep depending on molding conditions.
- **Standard test method:** ASTM D638 (tensile), ISO 294-4 (weld line test specimen).
- **Key molding parameter:** Mold surface temperature — must remain above the polymer's **Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT)** near the weld region to ensure adequate fusion.
- **Acceptable Knit Line Strength Ratio (KLSR):** KLSR ≥ 0.8 is generally considered acceptable for structural parts.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Formation Mechanism (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The formation of a weld line follows a **5-stage chronology**. Examiners award marks for stating these stages in correct order.

### Stage 1 — Flow Splitting
When the advancing polymer melt encounters a geometric obstruction (a hole for a fastener, a metallic insert, a core pin, or a boss cavity), the flow stream divides into **two or more sub-fronts** that must flow around the obstacle.

### Stage 2 — Independent Cooling
Each separated sub-front travels independently along a longer path, losing heat to the cold mold walls. By the time they re-converge, the **interfacial temperature has dropped significantly** — often well below the polymer's melting/softening point for semi-crystalline grades.

### Stage 3 — Convergence & Contact
The two sub-fronts meet head-on (weld) or tangentially (meld) at a location downstream of the obstruction. Because they are advancing in **opposing normal directions**, the polymer chains cannot interdiffuse across the interface — they can only "kiss" surface-to-surface.

### Stage 4 — Chain Interdiffusion (or Lack Thereof)
At the meeting plane, molecular chain segments from each side attempt to interdiffuse (a process governed by **reptation theory** — de Gennes, 1971). However, the limited contact time, low interfacial temperature, and absence of flow-induced chain orientation severely restrict entanglement. The result is a **weak interfacial bond**.

### Stage 5 — V-Notch Formation
The volumetric shrinkage of the polymer during cooling pulls material away from the meeting line, opening a **V-shaped surface notch**. This notch acts as a **stress concentrator** (stress concentration factor Kt typically **2.5 – 4.0**) and is the primary cause of premature failure.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Knit Line Strength Ratio (KLSR)** | $\text{KLSR} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{bulk}}$ | Ratio of weld-line tensile strength to bulk tensile strength. Target $\geq 0.8$ for structural parts. |
| 2 | **Weld Line Strength Reduction (%)** | $\Delta \sigma \, (\%) = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{bulk}}\right) \times 100$ | Percentage drop in tensile strength due to weld. |
| 3 | **Stress Concentration Factor (V-notch)** | $K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}}$ | Where $a$ = notch depth, $\rho$ = notch root radius. For sharp weld notches, $K_t \approx 3$–$4$. |
| 4 | **Reptation Time (de Gennes)** | $t_{rep} \propto M^3$ | Time needed for a polymer chain to diffuse across the interface. $M$ = molecular weight. Higher $M$ → longer weld healing time → weaker weld. |
| 5 | **Critical Healing Time** | $t_{heal} \geq t_{rep}$ | Minimum contact time under temperature $T > T_g$ (amorphous) or $T > T_m$ (semi-crystalline) for chain interdiffusion. |
| 6 | **Temperature Drop Across Obstruction** | $\Delta T = T_{melt,0} - T_{interface}$ | Should be minimized by raising mold temperature and reducing obstruction width. |
| 7 | **Flow Front Velocity (Poiseuille approx.)** | $v_f = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot h^2}{12 \cdot \eta \cdot L}$ | Higher velocity → shorter cooling time at obstruction → stronger weld. |
| 8 | **Gate Number Effect** | $\sigma_{weld} \propto \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{N_{gates}}}$ | Empirical — more gates produce more weld lines and progressively lower local strength. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention:** All temperatures in **°C** or **K**; pressures in **MPa**; strengths in **MPa**; times in **seconds**; velocities in **mm/s**.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility in Real-World Design

- **Automotive:** Weld lines in bumper fascias, instrument panel carriers, and door handles are designed to lie in **non-load-bearing, non-visible regions** to avoid both cosmetic and mechanical failure.
- **Medical Devices:** Weld lines in syringe barrels and IV components must be characterized per **ISO 8537** because they can affect **burst pressure** and **biological safety**.
- **Electronics:** Weld lines near insert-molded electrical contacts can cause **poor sealing**, leading to moisture ingress and dielectric failure.
- **Packaging:** Thin-wall containers with multiple gates show weld lines that may **leak under drop-test** conditions — a critical issue per ISTA transport testing standards.

---

## 2.4 Factors Affecting Weld Line Strength

| Category | Factor | Effect on Weld Strength |
|---|---|---|
| **Material** | Molecular weight (MW) | ↓ MW → ↑ weld strength (better chain mobility) |
| | Filler content (glass fiber) | Fibers do not cross the weld plane → severe strength loss (50–70%) |
| | Crystallinity | Semi-crystalline polymers lose more strength than amorphous |
| **Processing** | Mold surface temperature | ↑ T_mold → ↑ weld strength (delays interfacial freezing) |
| | Melt temperature | ↑ T_melt → ↑ weld strength (more thermal energy for interdiffusion) |
| | Injection velocity | ↑ v → ↑ weld strength (shorter cooling window) |
| | Packing pressure | ↑ P_pack → ↑ weld strength (compresses V-notch) |
| **Design** | Obstruction width | ↓ Width → ↑ weld strength (less cooling) |
| | Number of gates | ↓ N_gates → fewer weld lines |
| | Weld line orientation | Weld perpendicular to load = catastrophic; weld parallel = tolerable |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Knit Line Strength Ratio (KLSR) Framework

We derive the fundamental relationship governing weld line strength from first principles of diffusion and thermal cooling.

### Step 1 — Define the Interfacial Bonding Condition

For two polymer melt fronts meeting, the weld strength depends on the **depth of polymer chain interdiffusion** across the interface, governed by the **reptation model**:

$$ \xi(t) = a \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{t_{rep}}\right)^{1/4} $$

where:
- $\xi(t)$ = average chain displacement across the interface at time $t$
- $a$ = monomer segment length (≈ 0.5 nm for PE, 0.6 nm for PP)
- $t_{rep}$ = reptation time
- $t$ = contact time at temperature $T > T_g$ or $T_m$

### Step 2 — Establish the Reptation Time Scaling

de Gennes showed that the reptation time scales with molecular weight cubed:

$$ t_{rep} = t_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{M}{M_0}\right)^3 \cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right] $$

**Reasoning:** Longer chains take exponentially longer to disentangle and diffuse. Higher temperature reduces $t_{rep}$ exponentially via the Arrhenius-type term.

### Step 3 — Apply the Critical Healing Condition

The weld achieves bulk strength only when $\xi(t) \geq R_g$ (radius of gyration of the polymer chain):

$$ \xi(t_{heal}) = R_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{heal} \geq t_{rep} \cdot \left(\dfrac{R_g}{a}\right)^{4} $$

This gives the **minimum contact time** required for full weld healing.

### Step 4 — Relate Contact Time to Cooling

During filling, the interfacial temperature drops according to a 1-D heat conduction solution (Stefan approximation). The contact time available before the interface solidifies is:

$$ t_{contact} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot c_p \cdot h^2}{k_{mold}} \cdot f(T_{melt}, T_{mold}, T_g) $$

where $h$ is the local part thickness, $k_{mold}$ is mold thermal conductivity, and $f$ is a dimensionless temperature function.

### Step 5 — Define the Strength Ratio

The weld line strength ratio is therefore:

$$ \text{KLSR} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{bulk}} = \min\!\left\{1, \ \dfrac{\xi(t_{contact})}{R_g}\right\} $$

**Interpretation:**
- If $t_{contact} \geq t_{heal}$ → $\xi \geq R_g$ → **KLSR ≈ 1.0** (perfect weld — strong as bulk)
- If $t_{contact} < t_{heal}$ → $\xi < R_g$ → **KLSR < 1.0** (weak weld)

### Step 6 — Express KLSR in a Practical Engineering Form

Empirically calibrated for typical injection-molded polymers:

$$ \text{KLSR} \approx \min\!\left\{1, \ K_0 \cdot (T_{mold} - T_g) \cdot v_{inj}^{0.3} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\Delta H_{fusion}}{R \cdot T_{melt}}\right)\right\} $$

This is the **engineer's working equation** — it shows that increasing mold temperature, injection velocity, or melt temperature all monotonically increase weld strength, while increasing the heat of fusion (semi-crystalline polymers) decreases it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A polypropylene (PP) gear is injection-molded with a metallic insert at the center. The weld line forms diametrically opposite the gate. Given:
> - Bulk tensile strength: $\sigma_{bulk}$ = **35 MPa**
> - Weld tensile strength (measured per ASTM D638): $\sigma_{weld}$ = **14 MPa**
> - V-notch depth: $a$ = **25 µm**, root radius: $\rho$ = **5 µm**

**Find:** (a) Knit Line Strength Ratio, (b) Percentage strength reduction, (c) Approximate stress concentration factor.

**Solution:**

**(a) KLSR:**

$$ \text{KLSR} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{bulk}} = \dfrac{14}{35} = 0.40 $$

**(b) Percentage Reduction:**

$$ \Delta \sigma \, (\%) = (1 - 0.40) \times 100 = 60\% $$

This is typical for filled or semi-crystalline polymers.

**(c) Stress Concentration Factor:**

$$ K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{25}{5}} = 1 + 2\sqrt{5} = 1 + 2(2.236) = 5.47 $$

**Effective localized stress** when the part carries a working stress $\sigma_{applied}$ = 10 MPa:

$$ \sigma_{max} = K_t \cdot \sigma_{applied} = 5.47 \times 10 = 54.7 \text{ MPa} $$

Since **54.7 MPa > 35 MPa (bulk strength)**, the gear will **fail at the weld line** even though the bulk material is safe. This is why weld line redesign is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Symptom | Root Cause | Design Action | Processing Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Weld on visible surface | Two gates placed on cosmetic face | Move gates to non-visible side | — |
| Weld on snap-fit cantilever | Insert or hole in load path | Relocate insert away from hinge | — |
| Weld in pressure-bearing wall | Hole through pressure surface | Use coring from opposite side | — |
| V-notch visible | Low mold temperature | — | Raise $T_{mold}$ above HDT |
| Brittle weld in glass-filled PP | Fibers don't bridge weld plane | Use unfilled grade locally; or gate strategy | — |
| Weld on cosmetic surface | Meld line from fan-gate split | Change gate to single film gate | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Weld Line vs. Meld Line Formation (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Polymer Melt Injection Start"]
    A1["Melt Enters Cavity"]
    A2["Obstruction Present in Flow Path"]
    A3["Flow Splits Around Obstruction"]
    A4["Two Sub Fronts Cool Independently"]
    A5["Sub Fronts Rejoin Downstream"]
    A6["Weld Line Forms V Notch"]
    A7["Reduced Local Strength KLSR less than 0.8"]

    B0["Two Gates Active Simultaneously"]
    B1["Two Fronts Advance Toward Each Other"]
    B2["No Obstruction in Path"]
    B3["Fronts Meet Head On"]
    B4["Meld Line Forms Shallow Seam"]
    B5["Moderate Strength Reduction KLSR around 0.9"]

    A0 --> A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> A4 --> A5 --> A6 --> A7
    A0 --> B0 --> B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Weld Line Mitigation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CAUSE["ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS"]
        C1["Identify Obstructions Holes Inserts"]
        C2["Identify Multi Gate Configurations"]
        C3["Map Weld Line Locations via Moldflow"]
    end

    subgraph FIX["MITIGATION LAYERS"]
        D1["DESIGN FIX Relocate Holes Single Gate Wall Thickness Uniformity"]
        D2["MATERIAL FIX Lower MW Grade Unfilled Resin Coupling Agents"]
        D3["PROCESS FIX Higher Mold Temp Higher Melt Temp Higher Injection Speed Higher Pack Pressure"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["VERIFICATION"]
        E1["Tensile Test ASTM D638 Weld Specimen"]
        E2["KLSR greater than 0.8 Pass"]
        E3["KLSR less than 0.8 Re iterate"]
    end

    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C3 --> D2
    C1 --> D3
    C2 --> D3
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> D1
    E3 --> D2
    E3 --> D3
```

## 4.3 Weld Line Geometry Schematic (ASCII Block)

```
            GATE
              |
              v
    ===============================
              |  (flow splits at insert)
        ______|______
       /             \
      |   [INS]       |
       \_____________/
              |  (fronts rejoin below insert)
              X  <-- WELD LINE (V-notch, weak)
              |
    ===============================
              |
              v
         CAVITY END
```

**Legend:**
- `[INS]` = metallic/mold insert (obstruction)
- `X` = weld line location
- `=` = mold cavity wall
- `|` = flow direction

## 4.4 Stress Concentration at V-Notch (Schematic)

```
   Bulk Material  |   Weld Region
   -----------    |   -----------
       |          |       \
       |          |        \____  <-- V-notch root (high stress)
       |          |        /
       |          |       /
   -----------    |   -----------
                   ↑
              Weld plane
   (chains meet here but don't interdiffuse fully)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Differentiate between a weld line and a meld line in injection-molded plastic parts. State any **two** distinguishing features.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Weld Line | Meld Line |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Forms when melt fronts are **separated by a solid obstruction** (pin, hole, insert) and rejoin downstream | Forms when melt fronts meet without any obstruction (e.g., from two gates) |
| 2 | Fronts are **partially cooled** at the obstruction → significant strength loss (40–90%) | Higher residual temperature retained → only mild strength loss (10–30%) |
| 3 | Visible deep V-notch on the surface | Shallower, smoother seam |

*(3 marks — Examiner expects both definitions and at least two contrasting points)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List **any three** processing parameters that can be adjusted to improve the strength of a weld line.

**Model Answer:**
1. **Increase mold surface temperature** — keeps the interface above $T_g$ (or $T_m$) longer, allowing more chain interdiffusion.
2. **Increase melt temperature** — raises the thermal energy at the interface, reducing $t_{rep}$.
3. **Increase injection velocity** — shortens the time the melt spends in contact with cold mold walls before converging.

*(3 marks — one mark per correctly stated parameter with brief justification)*

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** 
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the mechanism of formation of weld lines in injection-molded plastic parts. Discuss any **four** design guidelines to minimize weld lines. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** A polypropylene part is molded with a weld line. The bulk tensile strength is **32 MPa** and the weld line tensile strength is **12 MPa**. The V-notch at the weld has a depth of **20 µm** and a root radius of **4 µm**. Calculate: (i) Knit Line Strength Ratio, (ii) Percentage strength reduction, and (iii) approximate stress concentration factor. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Mechanism (4 marks):**
1. Melt flows into the cavity and encounters an obstruction (hole/insert).
2. Flow splits into two or more sub-fronts that travel independently.
3. Each sub-front cools as it flows along the cavity wall.
4. Sub-fronts re-converge downstream and meet head-on, forming a weld line.
5. Polymer chains cannot interdiffuse fully across the interface due to low temperature and short contact time, producing a weak bond with a V-notch.

**Design Guidelines — any four (3 marks, ~0.75 each):**
1. **Avoid holes/inserts in load-bearing or cosmetic regions** — place them in non-critical zones.
2. **Use single-gate configurations** wherever possible to eliminate weld lines altogether.
3. **Maintain uniform wall thickness** — abrupt thickness changes promote weld formation.
4. **Position gates so that weld lines lie parallel to the principal stress direction**, not perpendicular.
5. **Round off all flow obstructions** — sharp corners promote cold slugs and weak welds.
6. **Use sequential or cascade gating** to control the meeting point of melt fronts.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Knit Line Strength Ratio:**

$$ \text{KLSR} = \dfrac{\sigma_{weld}}{\sigma_{bulk}} = \dfrac{12}{32} = 0.375 $$

**(ii) Percentage Strength Reduction:**

$$ \Delta \sigma \, (\%) = (1 - 0.375) \times 100 = 62.5\% $$

**(iii) Stress Concentration Factor:**

$$ K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{a}{\rho}} = 1 + 2\sqrt{\dfrac{20}{4}} = 1 + 2\sqrt{5} = 1 + 4.472 = 5.47 $$

The high $K_t$ value (~5.5) confirms that the weld line will act as a crack initiator under any applied tensile load.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> **(a)** What are the factors affecting weld line strength in injection-molded parts? Discuss material, processing, and design factors. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain with a neat diagram the **reptation theory** of polymer chain interdiffusion across a weld interface. How does this theory explain why high-molecular-weight polymers show weaker weld lines? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):** *(Categorized enumeration — full 7 marks)*

**Material Factors:**
- Molecular weight (lower MW → stronger weld due to faster diffusion).
- Filler content (glass/carbon fibers don't cross the weld plane → severe strength loss).
- Polymer type (semi-crystalline > amorphous in weld strength loss due to sharper melting transition).
- Additives such as coupling agents can improve interfacial bonding.

**Processing Factors:**
- Mold temperature (higher → stronger weld).
- Melt temperature (higher → stronger weld).
- Injection velocity (higher → stronger weld).
- Packing/holding pressure (higher → compresses V-notch → stronger weld).
- Cooling time (shorter → less interfacial crystallization → better for amorphous, mixed for semi-crystalline).

**Design Factors:**
- Number and position of gates (fewer gates → fewer welds).
- Wall thickness uniformity (uniform → fewer welds).
- Geometry around obstructions (rounded → better flow → stronger welds).
- Location of inserts/holes (placed in non-load regions → avoids critical welds).

**Model Solution (b):**

**Reptation Theory (3 marks):**
- Proposed by **de Gennes (1971)**, a polymer chain trapped in a "tube" formed by surrounding chains can only move by **snake-like (reptation) motion** along its own contour.
- After weld formation, chain segments from each side must **diffuse across the interface** by reptation.
- The characteristic time required is the **reptation time $t_{rep}$**, which scales as $t_{rep} \propto M^3$, where $M$ is molecular weight.

**Why high MW → weaker welds (4 marks):**
1. Longer chains take **cubically longer** to diffuse across the interface.
2. During injection molding, the contact time at the weld is fixed (typically 0.1–2 seconds).
3. For high-MW grades, $t_{rep}$ exceeds this contact window → chains don't fully cross.
4. Result: incomplete chain entanglement → weak interfacial bond → KLSR drops sharply.
5. Practical remedy: use **lower-MW injection grades** for parts with critical welds, or **broaden MW distribution** to ensure a fraction of short chains can bridge the interface quickly.
6. Adding a small fraction of low-MW "chain mobilizer" or **lubricant/wax** in the formulation can also improve weld strength without sacrificing bulk mechanical properties.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students write *"reptation is the time for a polymer to melt"* — this is **WRONG**. Reptation is the snake-like motion of a single chain within its entanglement tube. Mark loss: **2 marks**.
> - Students often forget the **cubic dependence** $t_{rep} \propto M^3$ — examiners specifically test this scaling. Mark loss: **1 mark**.
> - In numerical problems, forgetting to **square-root** the $a/\rho$ ratio in $K_t$ is a common error. Mark loss: **1 mark**.
> - Always state **units** ($MPa$, $°C$, $s$) in the final answer — KTU 2024 scheme deducts **0.5 marks per missing unit**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Weld Lines (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Weld line = defect where two melt fronts separated by an obstruction rejoin.
- **Meld Line vs. Weld Line:** Weld = obstruction present (weaker, 40–90% loss); Meld = no obstruction (milder, 10–30% loss).
- **5-Stage Formation:** Flow split → Independent cooling → Convergence → Limited interdiffusion → V-notch.
- **KLSR Formula:** $\text{KLSR} = \sigma_{weld}/\sigma_{bulk}$ — Target $\geq$ **0.8** for structural parts.
- **Strength Reduction Formula:** $\Delta\sigma\,(\%) = (1 - \text{KLSR}) \times 100$.
- **Stress Concentration (V-notch):** $K_t \approx 1 + 2\sqrt{a/\rho}$ — Typical weld $K_t \approx 3$ to $5.5$.
- **Reptation Time Scaling:** $t_{rep} \propto M^3$ — higher MW → longer healing time → weaker weld.
- **Critical Healing Condition:** $t_{contact} \geq t_{heal} \geq t_{rep}$ at $T > T_g$ (amorphous) or $T > T_m$ (semi-crystalline).
- **Design Remedies:** Single gate, uniform wall thickness, relocated inserts, rounded obstructions, gate placement parallel to load.
- **Processing Remedies:** Raise $T_{mold}$, raise $T_{melt}$, increase $v_{inj}$, increase $P_{pack}$.
- **Material Remedies:** Lower MW grade, unfilled resin in critical regions, coupling agents, low-MW chain mobilizers.
- **Semi-crystalline vs. Amorphous:** Semi-crystalline polymers (PP, PA, POM) lose more weld strength than amorphous polymers (PS, ABS, PC).
- **Glass-Fiber Reinforced:** Weld strength drops 50–70% because fibers cannot cross the weld plane — design gating to avoid welds in critical regions.
- **Standard Tests:** ASTM D638 (tensile), ISO 294-4 (weld line specimen geometry).
- **Moldflow Simulation:** Always run a fill analysis to predict weld line locations before tooling commitment — a KTU-recommended industry practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Size and Location
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gate Size and Location

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gate**: A gate in polymer injection molding is a precisely engineered narrow constriction (orifice) at the entry point of the mold cavity that controls the flow rate, volumetric filling, and packing of molten polymer into the cavity. It is the smallest cross-section in the flow path between the runner system and the mold cavity.

The **gate** is the terminal restriction of the runner system that connects to the mold cavity. It is the point where:
- The polymer melt transitions from runner to cavity
- The shear rate is deliberately maximized to generate frictional heating (reducing melt viscosity locally)
- The melt is "frozen off" (gate freeze) at the end of the packing phase to separate the part from the runner system

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a gate is needed in injection molding:**
> 1. **Flow Control** – Regulates the volumetric flow rate into the cavity
> 2. **Weld Line Management** – Position can minimize or relocate weld lines
> 3. **Aesthetic Function** – Can be hidden on non-visible surfaces
> 4. **Degating** – Allows easy separation of part from runner
> 5. **Shear Heating** – Increases melt temperature locally to fill thin sections
> 6. **Pack/Hold Phase Seal** – Freezes off to prevent backflow during packing

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine a **water tap connected to a hose pipe watering your garden**. The tap (gate) controls how much water flows into the pipe (cavity). If you fully open the tap, water rushes in chaotically — this represents a **gate that is too large**, leading to flash, warpage, and poor packing. If you barely open it, the water trickles and cannot reach the far end — this represents a **gate that is too small**, leading to short shots, excessive shear heating, and material degradation. The **perfect gate** is like a tap opened just enough to deliver a steady, controlled stream that fills the entire garden evenly — analogous to optimal melt front advancement.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Standard — Industry-Recognized Design Values:**
> - **Shear rate at gate** $\dot{\gamma}_g$ : **10,000 – 50,000 s⁻¹** (typical design range)
> - **Gate thickness** $h_g$ : Typically **0.5 – 1.5 mm** for most engineering polymers
> - **Gate width** $w_g$ : Usually **3 – 10 mm** depending on part wall thickness
> - **Land length** $L_g$ : Generally **0.5 – 1.5 mm** (short land preferred for many thermoplastics)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Gates

Gates are classified based on **geometry**, **location**, and **method of degating**.

### 2.1.1 Based on Geometry

| Gate Type | Geometry Feature | Typical Application |
|-----------|-----------------|---------------------|
| **Sprue Gate** | Direct, conical entry from nozzle to cavity | Simple single-cavity molds, large parts |
| **Edge Gate (Side Gate)** | Rectangular cross-section at cavity edge | General purpose, most common |
| **Fan Gate** | Wide, gradually tapering fan-shaped entry | Flat, thin parts requiring uniform flow |
| **Tab Gate** | A small thick tab attached before gate | Helps prevent jetting, used for cosmetic parts |
| **Pin Gate (Tunnel Gate)** | Cylindrical gate machined at angle to parting line | Three-plate molds, multi-cavity molds |
| **Submarine Gate** | Pin gate whose runner is submerged below parting line | Automatic degating, concealed gates |
| **Diaphragm Gate** | Circular gate around a central core | Tube-like parts, cylindrical products |
| **Ring Gate** | Annular ring around a central core | Cylindrical hollow parts |

### 2.1.2 Based on Degating Method

1. **Cold Runner Gates** — Material solidifies in runner (trimmed manually or automatically)
2. **Hot Runner Gates** — Runner kept molten; gate is a heated orifice (no scrap)

## 2.2 Gate Location Rules — Design Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Favourite — "7 Golden Rules of Gate Location":**

1. **Thick-to-Thin Rule**: Always gate into the **thickest section** of the part. Material flows from thick to thin, ensuring proper packing of thick areas.
2. **Weld Line Avoidance**: Position gate to **minimize weld lines** on cosmetic/functional surfaces.
3. **Symmetry**: For multi-cavity or symmetric parts, gates should be placed **symmetrically** for balanced filling.
4. **Flow Length Optimization**: Place gate such that the **flow length is minimized** (ideally L/t ≤ 150–200 for thermoplastics).
5. **Avoid Cores and Inserts**: Gates should not impinge directly on cores, which can cause erosion and weld lines.
6. **Sink Mark Prevention**: For thick sections, place gate such that **packing can compensate** for shrinkage.
7. **Aesthetic Consideration**: Gate location should ideally be on **non-visible surfaces** (hidden edges, parting lines).

## 2.3 Gate Sizing — Engineering Principles

The gate is sized to satisfy **two competing constraints**:
- **Shear rate must be high enough** (≥ 10,000 s⁻¹) to ensure the melt reaches the cavity walls without premature freeze-off.
- **Shear rate must not be excessive** (≤ 50,000 s⁻¹ for most polymers; ≤ 100,000 s⁻¹ for fiber-filled or heat-sensitive grades) to avoid degradation.

### 2.3.1 Shear Rate at the Gate

For a **rectangular edge gate** (width $w_g$, thickness $h_g$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{6Q}{w_g \cdot h_g^2}$$

For a **circular pin gate** (diameter $d_g$):

$$\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}$$

> Where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (m³/s)

### 2.3.2 Gate Thickness (Edge Gate)

$$h_g = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.0) \cdot t_{wall}$$

> Where $t_{wall}$ = nominal wall thickness of the part

### 2.3.3 Gate Width (Edge Gate)

$$w_g = (3 \text{ to } 10) \cdot h_g$$

### 2.3.4 Gate Land Length

$$L_g = (0.5 \text{ to } 1.5) \text{ mm (short)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why land length is kept short:** A long land creates excessive pressure drop and shear heating, increasing the risk of material degradation. However, a very short land (< 0.5 mm) makes machining difficult and creates a weak gate that may break prematurely.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Shear rate — Edge gate | $\dot{\gamma}_g = \dfrac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}$ | s⁻¹ | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 2 | Shear rate — Pin gate | $\dot{\gamma}_g = \dfrac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}$ | s⁻¹ | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 3 | Edge gate thickness | $h_g = (0.5\text{–}1.0)\,t_{wall}$ | mm | KTU standard |
| 4 | Edge gate width | $w_g = (3\text{–}10)\,h_g$ | mm | KTU standard |
| 5 | Pin gate diameter | $d_g = (0.5\text{–}0.8)\,t_{wall}$ | mm | For three-plate molds |
| 6 | Flow length / thickness | $L/t \leq 150\text{–}200$ | — | Practical limit for thermoplastics |
| 7 | Pressure drop across gate | $\Delta P_g = \dfrac{12 \eta Q L_g}{w_g h_g^3}$ | Pa | For rectangular gate (Hagen-Poiseuille) |
| 8 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = \dfrac{V_{part}}{t_{fill}}$ | m³/s | $V_{part}$ = part volume, $t_{fill}$ = fill time |
| 9 | Recommended shear rate range | $\dot{\gamma}_g = 10{,}000\text{–}50{,}000$ | s⁻¹ | For most engineering polymers |
| 10 | Gate freeze time (approx.) | $t_{freeze} = \dfrac{h_g^2}{\pi^2 \alpha}\, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8(T_m-T_w)}{\pi (T_e-T_w)}\right)$ | s | Stefan's equation for solidification |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Application Insight:**
> - **Automotive**: Fan gates are used for dashboard panels to ensure uniform flow across large surface areas.
> - **Medical Devices**: Submarine gates are preferred for syringe barrels to allow automatic degating without leaving witness marks.
> - **Packaging**: Hot runner systems with pinpoint gates (pin gates) are standard for thin-walled container caps.
> - **Electronics**: Diaphragm gates are used for connector housings to eliminate weld lines around pins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Shear Rate at a Rectangular Edge Gate

### Starting Concept
Consider a rectangular slit of width $w_g$ and gap $h_g$. The flow through this gap can be modeled using the **plane Poiseuille flow** assumption (Newtonian approximation).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Velocity Profile**
For fully developed flow between two parallel plates separated by distance $h_g$, the velocity profile is parabolic:

$$
u(y) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{h_g}\right)^2\right]
$$

> Where $y$ is measured from the centerline and $u_{max}$ is the centerline velocity.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate**
Integrating the velocity profile across the gap and across the width:

$$
Q = w_g \int_{-h_g/2}^{+h_g/2} u(y)\, dy
$$

$$
Q = w_g \cdot u_{max} \int_{-h_g/2}^{+h_g/2} \left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{h_g}\right)^2\right] dy
$$

$$
Q = w_g \cdot u_{max} \cdot \left[y - \frac{4y^3}{3 h_g^2}\right]_{-h_g/2}^{+h_g/2}
$$

$$
Q = w_g \cdot u_{max} \cdot \left[\frac{2h_g}{3}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Centerline Velocity**

$$
u_{max} = \frac{3Q}{2 w_g h_g}
$$

**Step 4 — Wall Shear Rate**
The wall shear rate is the magnitude of the velocity gradient at the wall ($y = \pm h_g/2$):

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \left|\frac{du}{dy}\right|_{y=\pm h_g/2} = \frac{4 u_{max}}{h_g}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $u_{max}$**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4}{h_g} \cdot \frac{3Q}{2 w_g h_g}
$$

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Result:**
> $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shear Rate at a Circular Pin Gate

### Starting Concept
Apply **Hagen-Poiseuille flow** through a cylindrical pipe of diameter $d_g$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Velocity Profile (Hagen-Poiseuille)**

$$
u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]
$$

> Where $R = d_g/2$ and $r$ is the radial coordinate.

**Step 2 — Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$
Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = 2\pi u_{max} \int_0^R \left[r - \frac{r^3}{R^2}\right] dr
$$

$$
Q = 2\pi u_{max} \left[\frac{R^2}{2} - \frac{R^2}{4}\right]
$$

$$
Q = 2\pi u_{max} \cdot \frac{R^2}{4} = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{max}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $u_{max}$**

$$
u_{max} = \frac{2Q}{\pi R^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Wall Shear Rate**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \left|\frac{du}{dr}\right|_{r=R} = \frac{2 u_{max}}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{2}{R} \cdot \frac{2Q}{\pi R^2} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}
$$

**Step 6 — Replace $R = d_g/2$**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_w = \frac{4Q}{\pi \left(\frac{d_g}{2}\right)^3} = \frac{4Q}{\pi} \cdot \frac{8}{d_g^3}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Result:**
> $$\boxed{\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Edge Gate Sizing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Typical Numerical Problem Format**

**Given:**
- Part volume: $V_{part} = 60 \text{ cm}^3$
- Fill time: $t_{fill} = 1.5 \text{ s}$
- Wall thickness: $t_{wall} = 3 \text{ mm}$
- Material: Polypropylene (recommended $\dot{\gamma}_g$ = 30,000 s⁻¹)

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{part}}{t_{fill}} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3}{1.5 \text{ s}} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Gate Thickness**

$$
h_g = 0.7 \cdot t_{wall} = 0.7 \cdot 3 = 2.1 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for Gate Width using Shear Rate Equation**

Rearranging $\dot{\gamma}_g = \dfrac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}$:

$$
w_g = \frac{6Q}{\dot{\gamma}_g \cdot h_g^2}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
w_g = \frac{6 \times (4 \times 10^{-5})}{30{,}000 \times (2.1 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
w_g = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{30{,}000 \times 4.41 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
w_g = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-4}}{1.323 \times 10^{-1}} = 1.81 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{w_g \approx 1.81 \text{ mm}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Check**: This width is very narrow. Realistic edge gate widths are ≥ 3 mm. Therefore, for this part, a **pin gate** or **fan gate** would be more appropriate than a conventional edge gate.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pin Gate Sizing

**Given:**
- $Q = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Target $\dot{\gamma}_g$ = 30,000 s⁻¹
- $t_{wall}$ = 3 mm

**Step 1 — Pin Gate Diameter from Shear Rate Equation**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}
$$

$$
d_g^3 = \frac{32Q}{\pi \dot{\gamma}_g}
$$

$$
d_g^3 = \frac{32 \times 4 \times 10^{-5}}{\pi \times 30{,}000}
$$

$$
d_g^3 = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-3}}{9.4248 \times 10^4} = 1.358 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3
$$

$$
d_g = (1.358 \times 10^{-8})^{1/3} = 2.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{d_g \approx 2.38 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 2 — Validate against Wall Thickness Rule**

$$
d_g = 0.79 \cdot t_{wall} = 0.79 \cdot 3 = 2.37 \text{ mm} \;\checkmark
$$

> Both methods agree — pin gate diameter ≈ **2.4 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Gate Type Classification — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gate Classification] --> B[Based on Geometry]
    A --> C[Based on Degating Method]

    B --> B1[Edge Gate - Rectangular]
    B --> B2[Pin Gate - Cylindrical]
    B --> B3[Submarine Gate - Tunnel Type]
    B --> B4[Fan Gate - Tapered Wide]
    B --> B5[Diaphragm Gate - Circular]
    B --> B6[Ring Gate - Annular]

    C --> C1[Cold Runner - Manual/Automatic Trim]
    C --> C2[Hot Runner - Heated Nozzle System]

    B1 --> D1[General purpose parts]
    B2 --> D2[Three plate molds, small parts]
    B3 --> D3[Automatic degating needed]
    B4 --> D4[Flat thin-walled parts]
    B5 --> D5[Tubular parts]
    B6 --> D6[Hollow cylindrical parts]
```

## 4.2 Gate Location Decision Logic — Mermaid Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start - Identify Part Geometry] --> Q1{Is part symmetric?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2[Place gate on axis of symmetry]
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Identify thickest section]
    Q3 --> Q4{Is thick section accessible?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> GATE[Gate placed in thick section]
    Q4 -- No --> Q5[Relocate to nearest thick region]

    GATE --> Q6{Does placement create weld lines on visible surface?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[Reposition or use multiple gates]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8[Verify flow length L divided by t]

    Q8 --> Q9{Is L over t less than 200?}
    Q9 -- Yes --> OK[Design Accepted]
    Q9 -- No --> Q10[Use multiple gates or hot runner]

    OK --> FINAL[Finalize gate size using shear rate formula]
```

## 4.3 Flow Path Architecture — Injection Molding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Heated Barrel] --> S[Sprue - Conical Channel]
    S --> R[Runner System - Primary]
    R --> SG[Secondary Runner if Multi-Cavity]
    SG --> G[Gate - Smallest Cross Section]
    G --> C[Mold Cavity - Final Part Shape]

    C -. Packing Phase .-> P[Gate Freezes Off]
    P -. Ejection .-> E[Part Separated from Runner]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Rectangular Edge Gate Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TITLE[Rectangular Edge Gate - Cross Section View]
    TITLE --> L1[Land Length Lg = 0.5 to 1.5 mm]
    TITLE --> W1[Gate Width wg = 3 to 10 mm]
    TITLE --> H1[Gate Thickness hg = 0.5 to 1.0 times wall thickness]
    TITLE --> F1[Flow Direction from Runner to Cavity]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define a gate in injection molding. List any four functions of a gate.*

**Model Answer:**

A gate is the small, restricted opening at the entrance of the mold cavity that controls the flow of molten polymer from the runner system into the cavity.

**Four functions of a gate:**
1. Controls the **volumetric flow rate** of melt into the cavity.
2. Generates **localized shear heating** to reduce melt viscosity, aiding cavity filling.
3. Provides a **freeze-off point** at the end of the packing phase to prevent backflow.
4. Determines the **location of weld lines and knit lines** by influencing flow patterns.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *State the "Golden Rules" of gate location in injection molding.*

**Model Answer:**

The Golden Rules of Gate Location are:
1. **Gate into the thickest section** of the part (thick-to-thin rule) to ensure proper packing.
2. **Minimize weld lines** on cosmetic or functional surfaces.
3. **Maintain symmetry** for balanced filling in symmetric/multi-cavity molds.
4. **Keep flow length to thickness ratio (L/t)** within 150–200 for thermoplastics.
5. **Avoid impingement on cores** to reduce wear and weld lines.
6. **Place on non-visible surfaces** for aesthetic reasons.
7. **Allow adequate packing** to prevent sink marks in thick areas.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *With neat sketches, classify the different types of gates used in injection molding. Discuss in detail the factors governing the selection of gate location for a polymer component.*

#### Part (a) — Classification of Gates (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

Gates can be classified based on **geometry** and **method of degating**.

**Based on Geometry:**

1. **Sprue Gate**: Direct, tapered entry from the machine nozzle to the cavity. Used for single-cavity molds with large parts. Leaves a large witness mark.

2. **Edge Gate (Side Gate)**: Most common. Rectangular cross-section cut at the cavity edge. Easy to machine, easy to degate.

3. **Pin Gate**: Small cylindrical gate machined at an angle to the parting line. Used in three-plate molds. Leaves a small, almost invisible witness mark.

4. **Submarine Gate (Tunnel Gate)**: Pin gate whose runner is buried below the parting line. Allows automatic degating during ejection.

5. **Fan Gate**: Wide, gradually tapering fan-shaped entry. Provides uniform flow front across wide parts like panels.

6. **Tab Gate**: A small thickened tab between the runner and cavity. Reduces jetting by providing a buffer zone.

7. **Diaphragm Gate**: Circular gate surrounding a central core. Used for hollow cylindrical parts; eliminates weld lines.

8. **Ring Gate**: Annular ring around a core. Similar use to diaphragm gate but for longer cylindrical parts.

**Based on Degating Method:**

- **Cold Runner Gates** — Material solidifies in runner; runner is scrap; gates trimmed manually or automatically.
- **Hot Runner Gates** — Runner is kept molten using heated manifolds; no scrap; gate is a heated orifice.

#### Part (b) — Factors Governing Gate Location (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

The selection of gate location depends on the following factors:

1. **Wall Thickness Variation**: The thick-to-thin rule mandates gating at the thickest section. This ensures the molten polymer can flow from thick to thin sections without premature freeze-off and provides adequate packing to compensate for shrinkage in thick areas.

2. **Weld Line and Knit Line Control**: Weld lines form where two flow fronts meet. Gate location determines the position of these lines. Critical cosmetic and functional surfaces should be free of weld lines by placing the gate such that flow fronts do not converge on them.

3. **Flow Length**: The flow length to thickness ratio (L/t) should ideally be ≤ 150–200 for thermoplastics. Beyond this, the melt may freeze before filling the cavity. Multiple gates may be required for long parts.

4. **Symmetry**: For symmetric parts or multi-cavity molds, gates should be placed symmetrically to ensure balanced and uniform filling.

5. **Sink Marks and Voids**: In thick sections, improper gating leads to sink marks due to volumetric shrinkage. Proper gate placement ensures sufficient packing pressure reaches these areas.

6. **Aesthetic Considerations**: Gates should ideally be located on non-visible surfaces or hidden along parting lines to minimize visual defects.

7. **Core and Insert Avoidance**: Direct impingement of melt on cores or inserts causes erosion and may create weld lines. Gates should be positioned to avoid direct jetting on such features.

8. **Ease of Degating**: Gates should be located where automatic or manual trimming can be performed easily without damaging the part.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse weld lines with melt fronts**. Weld lines form where flow fronts meet; flow fronts are the leading edges of the advancing melt.
> - Do **NOT** claim that fan gates are used for thick parts — they are for **flat, thin-walled parts**.
> - In multi-cavity molds, **symmetric placement of gates is mandatory**, not optional.

---

### Question B — Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]** *Explain the engineering principles governing gate sizing in injection molding. Derive the expressions for shear rate at (i) a rectangular edge gate and (ii) a circular pin gate. An injection molding machine fills a 50 cm³ part in 1.2 seconds through an edge gate of thickness 1.5 mm. Calculate the required gate width if the recommended shear rate is 25,000 s⁻¹. State whether an alternative gate type would be more appropriate and justify.*

#### Part (a) — Gate Sizing Principles + Derivations (7 Marks)

**Answer:**

**Engineering Principles of Gate Sizing:**

Gate sizing balances two competing requirements:
- **Minimum Shear Rate** (~10,000 s⁻¹): Must be high enough to maintain melt fluidity and prevent premature freezing.
- **Maximum Shear Rate** (~50,000 s⁻¹): Must not be so high that it degrades the polymer or causes excessive flash.

The shear rate depends on the **volumetric flow rate** and the **gate geometry**. Two key geometric gate types are analyzed:

**(i) Shear Rate at a Rectangular Edge Gate:**

Consider flow through a slit of width $w_g$ and thickness $h_g$. Using plane Poiseuille flow:

$$
u(y) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{2y}{h_g}\right)^2\right]
$$

Integrating across the gap and width:

$$
Q = w_g \cdot u_{max} \cdot \frac{2h_g}{3}
$$

Solving:

$$
u_{max} = \frac{3Q}{2 w_g h_g}
$$

Wall shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{4 u_{max}}{h_g} = \frac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}
$$

**(ii) Shear Rate at a Circular Pin Gate:**

For Hagen-Poiseuille flow in a circular pipe of diameter $d_g$:

$$
u(r) = u_{max}\left[1 - \left(\frac{r}{R}\right)^2\right]
$$

Integrating:

$$
Q = \frac{\pi R^2 u_{max}}{2} \Rightarrow u_{max} = \frac{2Q}{\pi R^2}
$$

Wall shear rate:

$$
\dot{\gamma}_g = \frac{2 u_{max}}{R} = \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3} = \frac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Calculation + Recommendation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $V_{part} = 50 \text{ cm}^3 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$
- $t_{fill} = 1.2 \text{ s}$
- $h_g = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $\dot{\gamma}_g = 25{,}000 \text{ s}^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Rate**

$$
Q = \frac{V_{part}}{t_{fill}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{1.2} = 4.167 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Gate Width from Shear Rate Equation**

Rearranging:

$$
w_g = \frac{6Q}{\dot{\gamma}_g \cdot h_g^2}
$$

Substituting:

$$
w_g = \frac{6 \times 4.167 \times 10^{-5}}{25{,}000 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
w_g = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{25{,}000 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
w_g = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.625 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.44 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
$$

$$
\boxed{w_g \approx 4.44 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Recommendation**

The calculated width of **4.44 mm** is within the standard edge gate range (3–10 mm), so the edge gate is dimensionally acceptable. However:

- The **fill time of 1.2 s for 50 cm³** is relatively fast, suggesting high injection velocity.
- For such a part, a **fan gate** would be more appropriate if the part is flat and wide, as it provides uniform flow front.
- Alternatively, a **pin gate** (diameter ≈ 2 mm) would be more compact and provide better automatic degating in a multi-cavity mold.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students forget to **convert cm³ to m³** and lose 1–2 marks immediately.
> - The **fill time must be in seconds** and the gate dimensions in **meters** for SI consistency.
> - Always **state the units** clearly in your final answer — KTU examiners deduct marks for unitless final results.
> - Do not blindly recommend a hot runner — the question does not specify production volume.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Gate Size and Location":**

- ✅ **Gate definition**: Smallest cross-section between runner and cavity.
- ✅ **Primary function**: Control volumetric flow and provide freeze-off.
- ✅ **Recommended shear rate**: 10,000 – 50,000 s⁻¹.
- ✅ **Edge gate shear rate formula**: $\dot{\gamma}_g = \dfrac{6Q}{w_g h_g^2}$
- ✅ **Pin gate shear rate formula**: $\dot{\gamma}_g = \dfrac{32Q}{\pi d_g^3}$
- ✅ **Edge gate thickness**: $h_g = (0.5\text{–}1.0) \times t_{wall}$
- ✅ **Edge gate width**: $w_g = (3\text{–}10) \times h_g$
- ✅ **Pin gate diameter**: $d_g = (0.5\text{–}0.8) \times t_{wall}$
- ✅ **Land length**: 0.5 – 1.5 mm (short preferred).
- ✅ **Flow length limit**: L/t ≤ 150–200 for thermoplastics.
- ✅ **Golden Rule #1**: Gate into the thickest section (thick-to-thin).
- ✅ **Weld lines** form where flow fronts meet — gate placement controls their position.
- ✅ **Fan gate**: For wide, thin flat parts.
- ✅ **Submarine gate**: For automatic degating (buried runner).
- ✅ **Diaphragm/Ring gate**: For tubular/hollow parts.
- ✅ **Hot runner**: Eliminates scrap; used in high-volume production.
- ✅ **Gate freeze-off** seals the cavity during packing phase — prevents backflow.
- ✅ **Volumetric flow rate**: $Q = V_{part}/t_{fill}$ — always convert cm³ to m³.
- ✅ **SI consistency**: All dimensions in meters when using SI formulas.
- ✅ **Pressure drop across gate**: $\Delta P_g = \dfrac{12 \eta Q L_g}{w_g h_g^3}$ (rectangular).
- ✅ **Sink marks** result from inadequate packing of thick sections — fix via proper gate location.
- ✅ **Symmetric gate placement** is mandatory for multi-cavity molds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Polymer Product Design**, a **Tolerance** is defined as the permissible limit or limits of variation in a specified physical, dimensional, or mechanical property of a moulded polymer part, relative to its nominal (target) design value. Tolerances account for inevitable variations introduced during the polymer processing cycle (injection moulding, extrusion, blow moulding, compression moulding), material behaviour (shrinkage, warpage, anisotropy), tooling wear, and measurement uncertainty.

The **KTU 2024 Scheme** emphasizes tolerance design as a critical link between **Design Intent**, **Material Selection**, and **Manufacturability**, ensuring that the moulded part performs its intended function reliably within a mass-production environment while remaining economically feasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):**
> Tolerances govern **dimensional accuracy**, **functional fit**, **assembly interchangeability**, and **aesthetic conformance** of polymer products. Under-tight tolerance specification escalates tooling cost and rejection rates, while over-loose tolerances compromise functional performance.

The formal mathematical representation of a tolerance band is:

$$
T = D_{max} - D_{min}
$$

where $T$ is the total tolerance, $D_{max}$ is the upper dimensional limit, and $D_{min}$ is the lower dimensional limit. The nominal dimension $D_{nom}$ lies centrally (for symmetric tolerances) or asymmetrically (for unilateral tolerances) within this band.

## 1.2 Categorical Classification of Tolerances

Polymer product tolerances are broadly classified into the following functional categories:

| **Category** | **Purpose** | **Typical Magnitude** |
|--------------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Commercial Tolerance** | General dimensional conformance for non-critical features | Wide (loose) |
| **Functional Tolerance** | Ensures mating, sealing, or mechanical operation | Tight |
| **Aesthetic Tolerance** | Controls visual appearance, sink marks, flash | Moderate to Tight |
| **Process Tolerance** | Imposed by limitations of moulding process | Process-dependent |
| **Tooling Tolerance** | Set by mould machining accuracy | Very Tight |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Sieve Metaphor":**
> Imagine you are baking cookies using a plastic cookie cutter. The cutter has a nominal diameter of **50 mm**, but due to the heat of the oven (analogous to moulding temperature), the plastic cookie expands or contracts slightly. You cannot guarantee every cookie is exactly 50.000 mm. Instead, you decide: *"Any cookie between 49 mm and 51 mm is acceptable."* That ±1 mm range is your **tolerance band**. If you insisted on ±0.01 mm tolerance (sub-millimetre accuracy), you would discard most cookies — that is exactly what happens in injection moulding when tolerances are over-specified: rejection rates skyrocket and per-piece cost balloons.

Now apply this to a polymer **snap-fit clip** on a mobile phone housing. The clip must engage reliably with the mating housing part thousands of times in production. A tolerance of ±0.1 mm might be acceptable for an aesthetic feature, but the snap-fit hook engagement requires ±0.05 mm or tighter — because if the hook is too thick, it won't flex; if too thin, it will fracture. This **functional necessity** dictates tolerance selection.

## 1.4 Why Tolerances Matter in Polymer Design

Polymers introduce unique tolerance challenges not encountered in metals:

- **High Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CLTE):** Plastics expand 5–10× more than metals per °C.
- **Shrinkage:** Thermoplastics shrink **0.2% – 4%** upon cooling from mould temperature to room temperature (amorphous polymers: 0.2–0.7%; semi-crystalline: 1.0–4.0%).
- **Anisotropy:** Fibre-reinforced or filled polymers shrink differently along flow vs. transverse directions.
- **Moisture Absorption:** Nylon (PA) absorbs up to 9% water by weight, causing post-moulding dimensional drift.
- **Viscoelastic Behaviour:** Creep and relaxation alter dimensions under sustained load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:**
> A common student error is treating polymer tolerances the same as metal tolerances. Examiners often award full marks only when students explicitly mention **shrinkage allowance**, **mould shrinkage compensation**, and **process capability (Cpk)** in their tolerance design answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Components of a Dimensional Callout

Every tolerance specification on a polymer part drawing contains the following logical components:

1. **Nominal Dimension ($D_{nom}$):** The target theoretical value.
2. **Upper Deviation (+e):** Permissible increase above nominal.
3. **Lower Deviation (-e):** Permissible decrease below nominal.
4. **Tolerance Zone ($T = (+e) - (-e)$):** Total allowable variation.
5. **Datum References:** Geometric features used as origin for measurements.

For example, a dimension specified as **$50.00^{+0.20}_{-0.10}$ mm** means:
- $D_{max} = 50.20$ mm
- $D_{min} = 49.90$ mm
- Total tolerance $T = 0.30$ mm
- Asymmetric — biased towards the lower side (common for male shafts to ensure clearance fit).

## 2.2 Types of Tolerance Systems

### 2.2.1 Unilateral Tolerance
The tolerance band lies entirely on **one side** of the nominal dimension. Used when a critical functional feature (e.g., minimum wall thickness for strength) must be maintained.

$$
D = D_{nom} + 0.10 / -0.00 \text{ mm}
$$

### 2.2.2 Bilateral Tolerance (Symmetric)
Tolerance is distributed **equally** on both sides of nominal.

$$
D = D_{nom} \pm e
$$

### 2.2.3 Bilateral Tolerance (Asymmetric)
Tolerance is **unequally distributed** — common in polymer moulding to compensate for differential shrinkage behaviour.

$$
D = D_{nom}^{+0.15}_{-0.05} \text{ mm}
$$

### 2.2.4 Limit Dimensioning
Direct specification of upper and lower limits without reference to nominal — common in ISO drawings.

$$
D_{min} = 49.90 \text{ mm}, \quad D_{max} = 50.10 \text{ mm}
$$

## 2.3 ISO Tolerance Grades for Plastics (ISO 20457 / DIN 16750)

The **ISO Tolerance System** applies standardized tolerance grades to plastic parts. The commonly referenced grades in KTU syllabus context are:

| **Tolerance Grade** | **Application Class** | **Typical Use Case** |
|----------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Class A (Fine)** | High-precision technical parts | Optical lenses, gears |
| **Class B (Medium)** | Engineering-grade assemblies | Snap fits, threaded inserts |
| **Class C (Coarse)** | Non-critical dimensions | Aesthetic housings, ribs |
| **Class D (Very Coarse)** | Cosmetic-only features | Internal ribs, gate vestiges |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Standards **ISO 20457:2018** and **DIN 16750** are the primary references for plastic part dimensional tolerances. ISO 20457 provides 4 tolerance classes (TF, TM, TG, TK) based on processing method.

## 2.4 Mould Shrinkage Compensation (The Heart of Polymer Tolerance Design)

Because thermoplastics shrink as they cool in the mould, the **mould cavity must be oversized** by a precise shrinkage factor. This is called **mould shrinkage allowance** or **shrinkage compensation**.

The fundamental relationship is:

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)
$$

where:
- $D_{cavity}$ = Mould cavity dimension (the dimension machined into the tool steel)
- $D_{part}$ = Final desired plastic part dimension at room temperature (23°C, 50% RH)
- $S$ = Volumetric or linear shrinkage fraction (e.g., 0.015 for 1.5% shrinkage)

### 2.5 Typical Shrinkage Values (For Quick Reference)

| **Polymer** | **Shrinkage Range (%)** | **Notes** |
|-------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | 1.0 – 3.0 | Semi-crystalline, high shrinkage |
| **Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS)** | 0.3 – 0.8 | Amorphous, moderate |
| **Polycarbonate (PC)** | 0.5 – 0.7 | Amorphous, low |
| **Nylon 6,6 (PA66)** | 1.0 – 2.5 (dry), 1.5 – 2.0 (conditioned) | Moisture-sensitive |
| **Polyethylene (HDPE)** | 1.5 – 4.0 | Semi-crystalline, very high |
| **Polystyrene (PS)** | 0.2 – 0.6 | Amorphous, low |
| **POM (Acetal)** | 1.8 – 3.0 | Highly crystalline |

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Engineering Use** |
