|------------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Total Tolerance | $T = D_{max} - D_{min}$ | Defines tolerance band width |
| Shrinkage Compensation | $D_{cavity} = D_{part}(1 + S)$ | Sizes the mould cavity |
| Process Capability Index | $Cpk = \min\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Measures manufacturing consistency |
| Tolerance Stack-up (Worst Case) | $T_{stack} = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + \dots + T_n$ | Sum of tolerances in series |
| Tolerance Stack-up (Statistical RSS) | $T_{stack} = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + \dots + T_n^2}$ | Root-sum-square for random distributions |
| Thermal Expansion Compensation | $\Delta L = L_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Adjusts dimensions for service temperature |
| Shrinkage from Density Change | $S_v = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_{melt}}{\rho_{solid}}$ | Volumetric shrinkage from density data |
| Dimensional Change from Moisture | $\Delta D = D_0 \cdot C_{moist} \cdot M_{abs}$ | Post-moulding drift in hygroscopic polymers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention in KTU Board Exams:** Always specify linear dimensions in **mm**, temperature in **°C**, shrinkage as a **dimensionless fraction** (or %), and $Cpk$ as a unitless index. Mixing SI and CGS units costs marks.

## 2.7 Engineering Utility

Tolerance design is **not** an academic exercise — it directly determines:

1. **Tooling Cost:** Each reduction in tolerance class (e.g., from Class B to Class A) can increase mould cost by **20–40%** due to higher-precision EDM, CNC grinding, and electrode machining.
2. **Cycle Time:** Tighter tolerances may require slower injection speeds, longer cooling times, and higher mould temperatures — directly impacting throughput.
3. **Rejection Rate:** A tolerance band that is **3× smaller than the natural process variation** will produce rejection rates exceeding **30%**.
4. **Assembly Yield:** In multi-component assemblies, tolerance stack-up determines whether parts fit together — a critical issue in automotive and electronics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Mould Cavity Dimension from Required Part Dimension

**Problem Statement:** A polypropylene (PP) gear blank must have an outer diameter of **$80.00 \pm 0.10$ mm** at $23°$C. The mould shrinkage for this grade of PP is given as **$S = 1.8\%$** (linear). Determine the required cavity diameter to be machined into the mould.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

From Section 2.4, the shrinkage compensation formula is:

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the nominal part dimension and shrinkage fraction.**

$$
D_{cavity} = 80.00 \times (1 + 0.018)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $(1 + S)$ explicitly.**

$$
1 + S = 1 + 0.018 = 1.018
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply.**

$$
D_{cavity} = 80.00 \times 1.018
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
80.00 \times 1.000 = 80.000
$$

$$
80.00 \times 0.018 = 1.440
$$

Adding:

$$
D_{cavity} = 80.000 + 1.440 = 81.440 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the tolerance to the cavity as well.**

Since the part tolerance is $\pm 0.10$ mm, the same tolerance band transfers to the cavity (the mould does not "know" tighter precision than the part requires, unless the cavity itself has toolmaker tolerance).

$$
D_{cavity} = 81.44 \pm 0.10 \text{ mm}
$$

This ensures that after shrinkage, the part falls within $80.00 \pm 0.10$ mm.

**Step 6 — Verification by reverse calculation.**

$$
D_{part} = \dfrac{D_{cavity}}{1 + S} = \dfrac{81.44}{1.018}
$$

Performing long division:

$$
\dfrac{81.44}{1.018} = \dfrac{81.44 \times 1000}{1018} = \dfrac{81440}{1018}
$$

$$
81440 \div 1018 \approx 80.0000 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

The result confirms our cavity dimension. The **nominal cavity dimension is therefore 81.44 mm** with tolerance $\pm 0.10$ mm.

## 3.2 Tolerance Stack-up Derivation (Worst-Case Method)

**Problem Statement:** A three-part plastic assembly consists of a housing, an inserted metal bushing, and a closing cap. The dimensions are:
- Housing internal depth: $20.00 \pm 0.15$ mm
- Bushing thickness: $15.00 \pm 0.05$ mm
- Cap protrusion: $5.00 \pm 0.10$ mm

Determine the worst-case gap (clearance) at the assembly interface.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Define the assembly equation.**

The clearance gap $G$ is:

$$
G = D_{housing} - (D_{bushing} + D_{cap})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the maximum clearance (when housing is largest, bush and cap are smallest).**

$$
G_{max} = D_{housing,max} - (D_{bushing,min} + D_{cap,min})
$$

Substituting:

$$
G_{max} = 20.15 - (14.95 + 4.90)
$$

$$
G_{max} = 20.15 - 19.85 = 0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the minimum clearance (housing smallest, bush and cap largest).**

$$
G_{min} = D_{housing,min} - (D_{bushing,max} + D_{cap,max})
$$

$$
G_{min} = 19.85 - (15.05 + 5.10) = 19.85 - 20.15 = -0.30 \text{ mm}
$$

The negative result indicates **interference** (parts clash) under worst-case conditions.

**Step 4 — Total stack-up tolerance.**

$$
T_{stack} = 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.60 \text{ mm}
$$

Equivalently, summing individual tolerances:

$$
T_{stack} = 0.30 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 0.60 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 5 — Statistical (RSS) alternative.**

Assuming independent random distributions:

$$
T_{stack,RSS} = \sqrt{0.30^2 + 0.10^2 + 0.20^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{0.0900 + 0.0100 + 0.0400} = \sqrt{0.1400} = 0.374 \text{ mm}
$$

This represents the $\pm 3\sigma$ statistical stack-up — much tighter than worst-case, but valid only when tolerances are truly independent and normally distributed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Tolerance Class for Common Polymer Features

| **Feature Type** | **Recommended Tolerance Class** | **Typical Tolerance (mm per 25 mm)** | **Design Rationale** |
|------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------------------|----------------------|
| Critical bore diameter (bearing seat) | Class A (TF) | ±0.05 | Functional mating |
| External snap-fit hook | Class B (TM) | ±0.10 | Engagement reliability |
| Wall thickness (structural) | Class B (TM) | ±0.08 | Strength and weight |
| Cosmetic surface contour | Class C (TG) | ±0.20 | Visual only |
| Internal ribs | Class D (TK) | ±0.30 | Non-functional |
| Gate vestige location | Class C (TG) | ±0.25 | Aesthetics |
| Threaded insert hole | Class A (TF) | ±0.05 | Thread engagement |

## 3.4 Derivation of Process Capability Requirement

Given a part tolerance $T$ and known process standard deviation $\sigma$, the required $Cpk$ for acceptable yield (e.g., $\geq 99.73\%$ yielding $\pm 3\sigma$) is:

$$
Cpk_{required} = \dfrac{T}{6\sigma}
$$

If $Cpk_{required} \geq 1.33$, the process is considered **capable** (industry standard). If $< 1.0$, the process cannot meet tolerance economically.

### Worked Example:

A moulded ABS cover has a tolerance $T = 0.20$ mm. The injection moulding process has $\sigma = 0.025$ mm (measured from 30 production samples).

$$
Cpk = \dfrac{0.20}{6 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.150} = 1.33
$$

The process is **just capable** — borderline acceptable. Process optimization (mould temperature control, holding pressure tuning) should be considered to push $Cpk > 1.5$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tolerance Allocation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Part Design Specification] --> B[Identify Functional vs Non-Functional Features]
    B --> C{Is Feature Functional?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Assign Tight Tolerance Class A or B]
    C -->|No| E[Assign Loose Tolerance Class C or D]
    D --> F[Calculate Mould Shrinkage Compensation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Determine Cavity Dimension with Allowance]
    G --> H[Apply Toolmaker Machining Tolerance]
    H --> I[Validate via Process Capability Cpk]
    I --> J{Cpk Greater Than 1.33?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Release Drawing for Production]
    J -->|No| L[Optimize Process Parameters and Re-evaluate]
    L --> I
    K --> M[Document Final Tolerance Stack-up Analysis]
```

## 4.2 Tolerance Band Visual Representation (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lower Limit Dmin] --> B[Dimensional Continuum]
    B --> C[Nominal Dnom]
    B --> D[Upper Limit Dmax]
    C --> E[Tolerance Band Width T]
    D --> E
    A --> E
    F[Asymmetric Example 50.00 plus 0.20 minus 0.10 mm] --> G[Dmin equals 49.90 mm]
    F --> H[Dnom equals 50.00 mm]
    F --> I[Dmax equals 50.20 mm]
```

## 4.3 Tolerance Stack-up Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Assembly Final Dimension] --> B[Part 1 Tolerance]
    A --> C[Part 2 Tolerance]
    A --> D[Part 3 Tolerance]
    B --> E[Linear Sum Method]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    B --> F[Root Sum Square Method]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    E --> G[Worst Case Gap]
    F --> H[Statistical Gap 3 Sigma]
    G --> I[Validate Against Functional Requirement]
    H --> I
    I --> J{Meets Assembly Spec?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Acceptance]
    J -->|No| L[Redistribute Tolerances]
    L --> B
    L --> C
    L --> D
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix for Tolerance Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incoming Design Requirement] --> B[Material Type?]
    B -->|Amorphous| C[Shrinkage 0.2 to 0.8 percent]
    B -->|Semi-Crystalline| D[Shrinkage 1.0 to 4.0 percent]
    B -->|Filled or Reinforced| E[Directional Shrinkage Anisotropic]
    C --> F[Low Cavity Oversize Required]
    D --> G[High Cavity Oversize Required]
    E --> H[Dual Cavity Sizing Flow vs Transverse]
    F --> I[Apply ISO Class B or C Tolerance]
    G --> I
    H --> J[Apply ISO Class B with Directional Allowance]
    I --> K[Process Capability Validation]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Final Drawing Release]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Define the term "tolerance" as applied to polymer product design. Differentiate between unilateral and bilateral tolerance with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Tolerance is the total permissible variation in a dimension of a moulded part, expressed as the difference between the maximum and minimum acceptable limits. For example, $D = 50.00 \pm 0.10$ mm has a tolerance of 0.20 mm.

**Unilateral tolerance** has its tolerance band lying entirely on one side of the nominal dimension. Example: $D = 50.00^{+0.20}_{+0.10}$ mm — used when a minimum wall thickness or protrusion must be guaranteed (e.g., minimum weld bead thickness).

**Bilateral tolerance** has its tolerance band extending on both sides of the nominal dimension. It can be **symmetric** (e.g., $50.00 \pm 0.10$ mm) or **asymmetric** (e.g., $50.00^{+0.15}_{-0.05}$ mm). Bilateral tolerances are most common in general polymer part drawings because they accommodate natural process variation around the target value.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: Why cannot standard metal tolerances be directly applied to plastic moulded parts? List three polymer-specific reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Metal tolerances cannot be directly applied to plastic parts due to the unique thermo-physical behaviour of polymers:

1. **High and Variable Shrinkage:** Thermoplastics shrink 0.2%–4% depending on polymer family (amorphous vs. semi-crystalline), processing conditions, and filler content. Metals have negligible post-casting shrinkage.

2. **High Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion (CLTE):** Plastics expand/contract 5–10× more than metals per °C. A 50 °C service temperature swing can alter a 100 mm plastic dimension by 0.5 mm, while the same steel part moves only 0.06 mm.

3. **Moisture Absorption and Post-Moulding Drift:** Hygroscopic polymers (PA, PC, POM) absorb 1–9% water, causing secondary swelling and dimensional drift weeks after moulding. Metals do not exhibit this behaviour.

Additional reasons include anisotropy, viscoelastic creep, and mould-induced residual stresses.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a neat sketch the four standard tolerance classes (A, B, C, D) used for plastic moulded parts as per ISO 20457. Discuss how the selection of tolerance class affects mould cost and cycle time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A glass-fibre reinforced nylon (PA66-GF30) gear has a hub outer diameter of **$60.00 \pm 0.08$ mm**. The mould shrinkage for the part is **0.6% in the flow direction** and **1.2% in the transverse direction**. If the hub diameter is oriented **transverse to flow**, calculate (i) the required mould cavity diameter and (ii) the resulting part tolerance window after shrinkage.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — ISO 20457 Tolerance Classes:**

The ISO 20457 standard specifies four tolerance grades for plastic parts:

- **Class A (Fine / TF):** Tightest — for high-precision functional features such as bearing seats, optical mating surfaces, and threaded bores. Typical tolerance: **±0.05 mm per 25 mm** of dimension.
- **Class B (Medium / TM):** For engineering assemblies — snap fits, locating pins, precision housings. Typical tolerance: **±0.10 mm per 25 mm**.
- **Class C (Coarse / TG):** General-purpose dimensions with moderate functional requirement. Typical tolerance: **±0.20 mm per 25 mm**.
- **Class D (Very Coarse / TK):** Non-functional aesthetic features, internal ribs, gate vestiges. Typical tolerance: **±0.30 mm per 25 mm**.

**Effect on Mould Cost and Cycle Time:**

Moving from Class D → A (tightening tolerance by ~6×) typically increases **mould fabrication cost by 30–60%** due to:
- Higher precision CNC milling and EDM operations
- Use of hardened, stress-relieved tool steel (e.g., P20 → H13)
- Tighter electrode machining for EDM
- More frequent mould trials and sample qualification

**Cycle time** may increase by **15–25%** because tighter tolerances require:
- Slower injection speeds to avoid jetting
- Higher packing and holding pressures
- Longer cooling times to ensure dimensional stability
- More uniform mould temperature control (requires heated/cooled manifolds)

**Part (b) — Calculation:**

**Given:**
- $D_{part} = 60.00 \pm 0.08$ mm
- Transverse shrinkage $S_T = 1.2\% = 0.012$

**Step 1 — Calculate nominal cavity diameter.**

$$
D_{cavity} = D_{part} \times (1 + S_T)
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.00 \times (1 + 0.012) = 60.00 \times 1.012
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.72 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine cavity tolerance.**

The part tolerance is $\pm 0.08$ mm. Assuming the mould itself is machined to a slightly tighter tolerance (toolmaker convention) of $\pm 0.05$ mm:

$$
D_{cavity} = 60.72 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify the resulting part tolerance.**

Maximum cavity = 60.77 mm → Maximum part = $60.77 / 1.012 = 60.05$ mm
Minimum cavity = 60.67 mm → Minimum part = $60.67 / 1.012 = 59.95$ mm

$$
D_{part} = 60.00 \pm 0.05 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The part tolerance window is $\pm 0.05$ mm, which is **tighter than the originally specified $\pm 0.08$ mm** — this is desirable because it provides a margin of safety against process variation. The cavity dimension to be machined is **60.72 ± 0.05 mm**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define mould shrinkage. Derive the relationship between cavity dimension, part dimension, and linear shrinkage fraction. A mould is to produce an ABS plastic bracket with a critical length of **$120.00 \pm 0.15$ mm**. The linear mould shrinkage for ABS is **0.5%**. Calculate the cavity length to be machined.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a suitable diagram, explain the concept of **tolerance stack-up**. An assembly consists of three plastic components with the following dimensions:
- Component A: $25.00 \pm 0.10$ mm
- Component B: $40.00 \pm 0.12$ mm
- Component C: $35.00 \pm 0.08$ mm

Determine the (i) worst-case and (ii) statistical (RSS) tolerance stack-up of the total assembly length when the components are placed end-to-end.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Mould Shrinkage Derivation:**

**Definition:** Mould shrinkage is the fractional reduction in dimension of a moulded plastic part as it cools from the moulding (ejection) temperature to ambient temperature (23°C), measured after 24–48 hours of post-moulding conditioning.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- $D_{cavity}$ = Cavity dimension at room temperature
- $D_{part}$ = Part dimension after cooling and stabilization
- $S$ = Linear shrinkage fraction

By definition of shrinkage:

$$
S = \dfrac{D_{cavity} - D_{part}}{D_{cavity}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
D_{cavity} - D_{part} = S \cdot D_{cavity}
$$

$$
D_{part} = D_{cavity} - S \cdot D_{cavity} = D_{cavity}(1 - S)
$$

Solving for $D_{cavity}$:

$$
D_{cavity} = \dfrac{D_{part}}{1 - S}
$$

> Note: For small shrinkage values ($S < 0.05$), the engineering approximation $D_{cavity} \approx D_{part}(1 + S)$ is used with error less than 0.25%.

**Calculation:**

$$
D_{cavity} = 120.00 \times (1 + 0.005) = 120.00 \times 1.005
$$

Performing the multiplication:

$$
120.00 \times 1.000 = 120.000
$$

$$
120.00 \times 0.005 = 0.600
$$

$$
D_{cavity} = 120.000 + 0.600 = 120.60 \text{ mm}
$$

With the part tolerance of $\pm 0.15$ mm applied directly:

$$
\boxed{D_{cavity} = 120.60 \pm 0.15 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Part (b) — Tolerance Stack-up:**

**Concept:** Tolerance stack-up is the cumulative effect of individual part tolerances on the final assembled dimension of a multi-component product. Two methods are commonly used:

1. **Worst-Case (Arithmetic Sum):** Used when all tolerances must simultaneously reach their extremes — conservative, guarantees interchangeability.
2. **Statistical (RSS):** Used when tolerances are independent and normally distributed — realistic for high-volume production.

**Calculation — Worst Case:**

$$
T_{stack,WC} = T_A + T_B + T_C
$$

Total tolerance for each component:
- $T_A = 2 \times 0.10 = 0.20$ mm
- $T_B = 2 \times 0.12 = 0.24$ mm
- $T_C = 2 \times 0.08 = 0.16$ mm

$$
T_{stack,WC} = 0.20 + 0.24 + 0.16 = 0.60 \text{ mm}
$$

Nominal total length:

$$
L_{nom} = 25.00 + 40.00 + 35.00 = 100.00 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{assembly} = 100.00 \pm 0.30 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(worst case)}
$$

So the assembly may range from **99.70 mm to 100.30 mm**.

**Calculation — Statistical RSS:**

$$
T_{stack,RSS} = \sqrt{T_A^2 + T_B^2 + T_C^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{0.20^2 + 0.24^2 + 0.16^2}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{0.0400 + 0.0576 + 0.0256} = \sqrt{0.1232}
$$

$$
T_{stack,RSS} = 0.3510 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{assembly} = 100.00 \pm 0.18 \text{ mm (3σ)} \quad \text{(statistical)}
$$

The statistical stack-up gives **41% tighter tolerance** than worst-case — demonstrating the value of RSS analysis for high-volume production where random distribution is valid.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Error in Shrinkage:** Students often write $S = 1.8$ instead of $S = 0.018$, leading to absurd cavity dimensions. Always convert percentage shrinkage to a **dimensionless fraction** before substituting.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Cavity Tolerance:** Examiners expect you to **explicitly state** the cavity tolerance. Writing only $D_{cavity} = 81.44$ mm without $\pm$ loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 3. **Confusing Shrinkage Direction:** For fibre-reinforced polymers, shrinkage is **anisotropic**. Specifying a single shrinkage value for a critical transverse dimension is a serious error — lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 4. **Using RSS without Justification:** The statistical method is valid **only** if tolerances are independent and random. If the problem specifies "worst case" or "guaranteed interchangeability," use the arithmetic sum.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Service Temperature:** For parts operating at elevated temperature (e.g., under-the-hood automotive at 80°C), thermal expansion ($\Delta L = L_0 \alpha \Delta T$) must be added to mould-time tolerance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Tolerances in Polymer Design:**

- **Tolerance** = permissible variation = $D_{max} - D_{min}$.
- **Cavity oversizing** compensates for shrinkage: $D_{cavity} = D_{part}(1 + S)$.
- **Amorphous polymers** shrink less (0.2–0.8%) than **semi-crystalline** (1.0–4.0%).
- **Fibre-reinforced polymers** exhibit **anisotropic shrinkage** — always specify flow vs. transverse direction.
- **ISO 20457** defines four tolerance grades: **TF (fine), TM (medium), TG (coarse), TK (very coarse)**.
- **Worst-case stack-up** = arithmetic sum of individual tolerances.
- **Statistical stack-up** = $\sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \dots + T_n^2}$ (RSS) — assumes independent normal distributions.
- **Process Capability $Cpk \geq 1.33$** indicates a capable process; $< 1.0$ means process cannot meet tolerance economically.
- **Tighter tolerance = higher mould cost + longer cycle time + lower rejection tolerance.**
- **Moisture absorption** in PA, PC, POM causes **post-moulding dimensional drift** — condition parts before final measurement.
- **CTE** of polymers is **5–10×** that of metals — always consider service temperature.
- **Symmetric bilateral** tolerances are most common; **unilateral** used for critical minimum features.
- **Asymmetric bilateral** tolerances are used to **compensate** for known directional shrinkage behaviour.
- **Toolmaker tolerance** (mould cavity precision) should be **2–3× tighter** than the final part tolerance to provide manufacturing margin.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moulded-in-Inserts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Moulded-in-Inserts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Moulded-in-Insert** (also referred to as an *embedded insert* or *insert moulding component*) is a pre-formed, non-polymer element—typically fabricated from **metals, ceramics, or other engineering materials**—that is **precisely positioned inside the mould cavity prior to the injection of the polymer melt**. During the moulding cycle, the molten plastic flows around the insert and solidifies, resulting in a **permanent, mechanically interlocked, monolithic composite part** in which the insert is an integral, inseparable component of the finished product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Moulded-in-inserts are foreign bodies (metallic/non-metallic) deliberately placed in the mould cavity so that they are encapsulated by the polymer during processing, thereby providing functional features (threading, conductivity, reinforcement, wear resistance) that cannot be achieved by the polymer alone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **moulded-in-insert** like the **nuts and bolts hidden inside a chocolate truffle cake**. The cake (polymer) is poured into a mould, but before pouring, the baker places a hard candy centre (insert) at a specific location. Once the cake sets, the candy becomes an inseparable part of the dessert. Similarly, a metal insert is placed inside an injection mould, the molten plastic flows in and freezes around it, and the metal becomes a *permanent functional feature* of the plastic part.

**Real-world example:** Look at any **electrical plug** or **drill handle**—the metal threaded bushings you see are moulded-in-inserts that were placed inside the mould before plastic was injected.

## 1.3 Primary Functions of Moulded-in-Inserts

| **S.No.** | **Function** | **Engineering Reason** |
|:---------:|:-------------|:-----------------------|
| 1 | Provide **threaded fasteners** | Polymer threads wear out rapidly under repeated assembly/disassembly |
| 2 | Provide **electrical conductivity** | Plastics are inherently insulators; inserts act as conductors/contacts |
| 3 | Provide **localized reinforcement** | High-stress zones need metal strength |
| 4 | Provide **wear-resistant surfaces** | Bearings, hinges, pivots need hardness |
| 5 | Provide **heat dissipation paths** | Metal inserts conduct heat away from plastic |
| 6 | Provide **assembly datum points** | Precise positioning of mating components |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The most common insert sizes follow **ISO metric threads (M2 to M20)** and **Unified threads (UNF/UNC)**. Tolerance grades for insert cavities are typically **±0.05 mm** for high-precision applications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Moulded-in-Inserts

Inserts are classified based on **geometry, function, and material**:

### 2.1.1 By Geometry
- **Cylindrical Inserts**: Most common—used for threaded holes and bushings
- **Hexagonal Inserts**: Provide anti-rotation via flat surfaces
- **Knurled Inserts**: Have diamond/straight knurls for pull-out resistance
- **Flanged Inserts**: Have a head/flange to prevent push-through
- **Pinned/Spiked Inserts**: Have projections that anchor into the plastic

### 2.1.2 By Installation Method
- **Press-fit (interference fit) inserts**: Mechanically forced into place
- **Moulded-in inserts**: Placed in mould, plastic flows around them
- **Ultrasonic inserts**: Post-moulding installation
- **Heat-set inserts**: Installed with heat after moulding

### 2.1.3 By Material
- **Steel inserts** (carbon steel, stainless steel, alloy steel) — Most common
- **Brass inserts** — Excellent thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance
- **Aluminium inserts** — Lightweight applications
- **Bronze inserts** — Self-lubricating bearings
- **Ceramic inserts** — Electrical insulation in conductive housings
- **Thermoplastic inserts** — Bi-material designs

## 2.2 Insert Retention Mechanisms — The "Why" Behind the Design

The **pull-out strength** of an insert depends on **three primary mechanisms** working together:

### Mechanism 1: **Mechanical Interlock (Undercuts)**
The plastic flows into grooves, knurls, holes, or undercuts on the insert surface, creating a mechanical lock after solidification.

$$F_{pullout} = \tau_{shear} \times A_{shear}$$

Where:
- $F_{pullout}$ = Axial force required to extract the insert (**N**)
- $\tau_{shear}$ = Shear strength of the polymer at the insert interface (**MPa**)
- $A_{shear}$ = Effective shear area (undercut projected area) (**mm²**)

### Mechanism 2: **Differential Thermal Contraction (Shrinkage Lock)**
When the polymer cools, it shrinks around the insert, creating a **radial compressive stress** that grips the insert. The **interference pressure** is:

$$P_{interface} = \frac{\delta}{r \left( \frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1-v_p}{E_i} \right)}$$

Where:
- $P_{interface}$ = Radial pressure at insert-polymer interface (**MPa**)
- $\delta$ = Differential radial shrinkage (mm)
- $r$ = Insert radius (mm)
- $E_p$ = Young's modulus of polymer (**MPa**)
- $E_i$ = Young's modulus of insert (**MPa**)
- $v_p$ = Poisson's ratio of polymer (dimensionless)

### Mechanism 3: **Adhesive/Chemical Bonding**
Some polymer-insert combinations form weak chemical bonds (e.g., brass with nylon achieves bond strength of approximately **8–12 MPa** through polar interactions).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Application** |
|:------------|:----------------|:---------:|:----------------|
| $F_{pullout} = \tau_{shear} \cdot A_{shear}$ | Pull-out force via mechanical interlock | N | Insert retention design |
| $P_{interface} = \frac{\delta}{r\left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1-v_p}{E_i}\right)}$ | Radial interference pressure | MPa | Shrinkage fit analysis |
| $\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P_{interface} \cdot r_i}{t_{wall}}$ | Hoop stress in insert wall | MPa | Insert wall thickness check |
| $L_{min} = k \cdot d$ | Minimum insert length for retention | mm | Quick design rule |
| $t_{min} = 0.6 \cdot d$ | Minimum insert wall thickness | mm | Insert structural design |
| $\alpha_{mismatch} = \vert \alpha_i - \alpha_p \vert$ | CTE mismatch magnitude | 1/°C | Thermal stress estimation |
| $\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha_{mismatch} \cdot \Delta T$ | Differential thermal strain | dimensionless | Crack risk assessment |
| $D_{cavity} = d_{insert} + 2\Delta_{clear}$ | Mould cavity diameter for insert | mm | Mould design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Rule of Thumb (KTU frequently asked):** The minimum length of a moulded-in-insert should be **at least equal to 1.5 times its diameter** (i.e., $L \geq 1.5d$) to ensure adequate pull-out strength. The minimum wall thickness of a metallic insert should be **at least 0.6 times its outer diameter** to prevent insert deformation during moulding.

## 2.4 Why Coefficient of Thermal Expansion (CTE) Matters

The **CTE mismatch** between insert and polymer is the most critical design parameter. If $\alpha_{insert} \gg \alpha_{polymer}$, the insert contracts more than the plastic during cooling, generating **tensile residual stresses** that can cause:

- **Part cracking** (especially in amorphous polymers like PS, PMMA)
- **Insert loosening** over thermal cycling
- **Warpage** of the finished part

**Typical CTE values:**
- Steel: $\alpha \approx 11 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- Brass: $\alpha \approx 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- Aluminium: $\alpha \approx 23 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- Polypropylene: $\alpha \approx 100-150 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- Nylon 6,6: $\alpha \approx 80 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- ABS: $\alpha \approx 70-100 \times 10^{-6} /°C$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because polymers have CTE values **5–10× higher than metals**, the polymer always shrinks more than the insert, naturally creating a **shrinkage fit**—which is the dominant retention mechanism for smooth cylindrical inserts without undercuts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Shrinkage-Induced Interface Pressure

**Problem Context:** A brass insert (outer diameter $d = 10$ mm) is moulded into a polypropylene component. After cooling from moulding temperature ($T_{mould} = 220°C$) to room temperature ($T_{room} = 25°C$), what is the radial interface pressure?

**Given:**
- $d = 10$ mm, so $r = 5$ mm
- $E_{brass} = 100$ GPa $= 100{,}000$ MPa
- $E_{PP} = 1.5$ GPa $= 1{,}500$ MPa
- $\nu_{PP} = 0.35$
- $\alpha_{brass} = 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- $\alpha_{PP} = 120 \times 10^{-6} /°C$

### Step 1: Calculate the temperature drop

$$\Delta T = T_{mould} - T_{room} = 220 - 25 = 195°C$$

### Step 2: Calculate the differential thermal strain

The polymer shrinks more than the insert, so the differential strain is:

$$\varepsilon_{differential} = (\alpha_{PP} - \alpha_{brass}) \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting values:

$$\varepsilon_{differential} = (120 - 19) \times 10^{-6} \times 195$$

$$\varepsilon_{differential} = 101 \times 10^{-6} \times 195 = 0.01970 = 1.970\%$$

### Step 3: Calculate the differential radial displacement

$$\delta = \varepsilon_{differential} \cdot r = 0.01970 \times 5 = 0.0985 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4: Apply the thick-cylinder interface pressure formula

$$P_{interface} = \frac{\delta}{r \left( \frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1-v_p}{E_i} \right)}$$

Calculate the denominator term-by-term:

$$\frac{1}{E_p} = \frac{1}{1500} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /MPa}$$

$$\frac{1-v_p}{E_i} = \frac{1-0.35}{100000} = \frac{0.65}{100000} = 6.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /MPa}$$

Adding the two terms:

$$6.667 \times 10^{-4} + 6.5 \times 10^{-6} = 6.732 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /MPa}$$

Multiplying by $r$:

$$r \cdot \text{(denominator)} = 5 \times 6.732 \times 10^{-4} = 3.366 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/MPa}$$

### Step 5: Calculate the final interface pressure

$$P_{interface} = \frac{0.0985}{3.366 \times 10^{-3}} = 29.27 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** The interface pressure of **~29 MPa** creates a strong grip. However, this is the **theoretical maximum**—in practice, stress relaxation in the polymer over time reduces this to **~50–70% of the initial value**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Insert Length for Adequate Retention

**Design Requirement:** An insert with diameter $d = 8$ mm must withstand an axial pull-out force of $F = 2000$ N. The polymer shear strength at the interface is $\tau = 35$ MPa. Find the minimum insert length.

### Step 1: Identify the governing equation

$$F_{pullout} = \tau \cdot A_{shear}$$

For a cylindrical insert, the shear area is the **lateral surface area** through which the plastic must shear:

$$A_{shear} = \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

### Step 2: Rearrange for length $L$

$$L = \frac{F_{pullout}}{\tau \cdot \pi \cdot d}$$

### Step 3: Substitute numerical values

$$L = \frac{2000}{35 \times \pi \times 8}$$

Calculate denominator:

$$35 \times \pi \times 8 = 35 \times 3.1416 \times 8 = 879.65 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Final calculation

$$L = \frac{2000}{879.65} = 2.274 \text{ mm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Safety Factor Application:** The KTU 2024 scheme requires a **safety factor of 3.0** for static insert retention. Therefore:
> $$L_{design} = 3.0 \times 2.274 = 6.82 \text{ mm} \approx 7 \text{ mm}$$
> This confirms the rule of thumb: $L \approx 0.85d$ is barely adequate, but $L \approx 1.5d$ is recommended.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Insert Selection Decision Tree

| **Design Parameter** | **Condition** | **Recommended Feature** | **Rationale** |
|:---------------------|:--------------|:------------------------|:--------------|
| Axial pull-out required | High (>1000 N) | Knurled or grooved insert | Mechanical interlock |
| Rotational torque required | High | Hexagonal or splined insert | Anti-rotation flats |
| Thin-walled part | Wall < 2 mm | Flanged insert | Prevents push-through |
| High-temperature service | T > 150°C | Steel insert with undercut | CTE mismatch management |
| Electrical conductor needed | Required | Brass insert | Good conductivity + retention |
| Frequent assembly cycles | >100 cycles | Threaded insert with boss | Polymer thread protection |
| Transparent part | Optical clarity | Blackened/embedded insert | Hides insert visibility |
| Food contact | FDA required | Stainless steel 316 | Corrosion resistance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Insert Moulding Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Insert Preparation] --> B[Surface Cleaning and Degreasing]
    B --> C[Pre-heating of Insert Optional]
    C --> D[Placement in Mould Cavity]
    D --> E[Mould Clamping]
    E --> F[Polymer Injection]
    F --> G[Plastic Flows Around Insert]
    G --> H[Packing and Cooling Phase]
    H --> I[Plastic Solidifies and Shrinks]
    I --> J[Shrinkage Lock Established]
    J --> K[Mould Opening]
    K --> L[Ejection of Composite Part]
    L --> M[Quality Inspection]
    M --> N{Insert Properly Embedded?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Finished Moulded-in-Insert Part]
    N -->|No| P[Rejection and Rework]
```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Typical Moulded-in-Insert Component

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPONENT[Finished Plastic Part with Moulded-in-Insert]
        direction TB
        TOP[Plastic Boss - Top View]:::poly
        SIDE[Cross Sectional View]:::poly
        BOT[Plastic Base - Bottom View]:::poly
    end

    subgraph INSERT_FEATURES[Insert Design Features]
        direction LR
        F1[Flange Head]:::metal
        F2[Knurled Body]:::metal
        F3[Undercut Groove]:::metal
        F4[Tapered Lead-in]:::metal
        F5[Internal Thread]:::metal
    end

    F1 --> TOP
    F2 --> SIDE
    F3 --> SIDE
    F4 --> SIDE
    F5 --> SIDE

    classDef poly fill:#E0F0FF,stroke:#0066CC,color:#000
    classDef metal fill:#FFE0B0,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Insert Placement Strategies in Mould

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Manual Placement by Operator] --> D{Mould Type}
    B[Robotic Pick and Place] --> D
    C[Vibratory Bowl Feeder] --> D
    D --> E[Two Plate Mould]
    D --> F[Three Plate Mould]
    D --> G[Hot Runner System]
    E --> H[Insert Supported on Core Pin]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Locating Features in Mould]
    I --> J[Insert Held by Gravity and Friction]
    J --> K[Injection Begins]
```

## 4.4 Failure Mode Mapping for Moulded-in-Inserts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Insert Failure Modes] --> B[Pull-out Failure]
    A --> C[Rotational Slippage]
    A --> D[Cracking of Surrounding Polymer]
    A --> E[Insert Deformation]
    A --> F[Flash at Insert Interface]

    B --> B1[Insufficient Undercut Area]
    B --> B2[Low Shrinkage Fit]
    B --> B3[Poor Knurl Design]

    C --> C1[No Anti-rotation Feature]
    C --> C2[Smooth Cylindrical Insert]
    C --> C3[Inadequate Hex Flat Engagement]

    D --> D1[High CTE Mismatch]
    D --> D2[Sharp Insert Corners]
    D --> D3[Thin Polymer Wall]

    E --> E1[Insert Wall Too Thin]
    E --> E2[Excessive Injection Pressure]
    E --> E3[High Melt Temperature]

    F --> F1[Poor Insert-Mould Contact]
    F --> F2[Insert Surface Roughness]
    F --> F3[Incorrect Insert Tolerance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The above flowcharts represent the **functional architecture** of insert moulding. In actual mould designs, the insert is supported on **core pins, locating pins, or magnetic holders** within the cavity to prevent displacement during injection.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam Paper]**
**Define moulded-in-inserts. List any four commonly used materials for inserts with one specific application for each.**

**Model Answer:**
A moulded-in-insert is a pre-formed metallic or non-metallic component placed in the mould cavity before polymer injection, such that it becomes permanently encapsulated in the finished plastic part.

| **Material** | **Typical Application** |
|:-------------|:------------------------|
| Brass | Electrical contact terminals |
| Stainless Steel 316 | Food-grade threaded bushings |
| Carbon Steel | Automotive structural bosses |
| Bronze | Self-lubricating bearing surfaces |
| Aluminium | Lightweight aerospace inserts |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series Question Bank]**
**State the three primary mechanisms by which a moulded-in-insert is retained in a plastic part. Which is the most dominant for a smooth cylindrical insert?**

**Model Answer:**
The three retention mechanisms are:
1. **Mechanical Interlock** (via undercuts, knurls, grooves)
2. **Differential Thermal Contraction** (shrinkage fit due to CTE mismatch)
3. **Adhesive/Chemical Bonding** (polar interactions, e.g., brass-nylon)

For a **smooth cylindrical insert without undercuts**, the most dominant mechanism is **Differential Thermal Contraction** because the polymer (high CTE) shrinks more than the metal insert, generating a radial compressive grip.

**(3 marks: 1 mark per mechanism + bonus identification of dominant mechanism)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various types of moulded-in-inserts based on geometry. Discuss the role of knurling and undercuts in insert retention. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A brass insert of outer diameter 12 mm is moulded into an ABS component. The moulding temperature is 230°C and the service temperature is 30°C. Given: $E_{brass} = 100$ GPa, $E_{ABS} = 2.3$ GPa, $\nu_{ABS} = 0.35$, $\alpha_{brass} = 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $\alpha_{ABS} = 80 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. Calculate the interface pressure developed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Types and Role of Knurling/Undercuts:**

*Types based on geometry:*
1. **Cylindrical inserts** – Most common, used for threaded holes
2. **Hexagonal inserts** – Anti-rotation features for torque transmission
3. **Flanged inserts** – With a head to prevent push-through into thin sections
4. **Knurled inserts** – Diamond or straight knurls for enhanced pull-out resistance
5. **Spiked/stepped inserts** – Projections for axial locking

*Role of knurling and undercuts:*
- **Knurling** increases the **effective shear area** at the plastic-insert interface
- The polymer flows into the knurl valleys and solidifies, creating a **form-fit mechanical interlock**
- Knurls typically increase pull-out strength by **200–400%** compared to smooth inserts
- **Undercuts** (circumferential grooves) provide an additional **positive locking** feature that resists axial pull-out forces

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $d = 12$ mm → $r = 6$ mm
- $T_{mould} = 230°C$, $T_{service} = 30°C$
- $E_{brass} = 100{,}000$ MPa
- $E_{ABS} = 2{,}300$ MPa
- $\nu_{ABS} = 0.35$
- $\alpha_{brass} = 19 \times 10^{-6} /°C$
- $\alpha_{ABS} = 80 \times 10^{-6} /°C$

**Step 1: Temperature difference**
$$\Delta T = 230 - 30 = 200°C$$

**Step 2: Differential CTE**
$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{ABS} - \alpha_{brass} = (80 - 19) \times 10^{-6} = 61 \times 10^{-6} /°C$$

**Step 3: Differential radial strain**
$$\varepsilon = \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 61 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 = 0.0122$$

**Step 4: Radial displacement**
$$\delta = \varepsilon \cdot r = 0.0122 \times 6 = 0.0732 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Apply interface pressure formula**
$$P = \frac{\delta}{r \left( \frac{1}{E_{ABS}} + \frac{1 - \nu_{ABS}}{E_{brass}} \right)}$$

Compute the bracketed term:
$$\frac{1}{E_{ABS}} = \frac{1}{2300} = 4.348 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /MPa}$$

$$\frac{1 - \nu_{ABS}}{E_{brass}} = \frac{0.65}{100000} = 6.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /MPa}$$

$$\text{Sum} = 4.348 \times 10^{-4} + 6.5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.413 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /MPa}$$

Multiply by $r$:
$$6 \times 4.413 \times 10^{-4} = 2.648 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm/MPa}$$

**Step 6: Final pressure**
$$P = \frac{0.0732}{2.648 \times 10^{-3}} = 27.64 \text{ MPa}$$

> **Answer:** The interface pressure is approximately **27.6 MPa**.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Examination]**

**(a)** Discuss the design guidelines for moulded-in-inserts. Explain the importance of CTE mismatch and the methods to overcome its adverse effects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An insert of diameter 6 mm and length 10 mm is moulded into a nylon component. If the polymer shear strength at the interface is 45 MPa, calculate: (i) the theoretical pull-out force, and (ii) the design pull-out force using a safety factor of 3.5. Comment on whether the insert is adequately designed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Design Guidelines and CTE Mismatch:**

*Key Design Guidelines:*
1. Insert length should be **at least 1.5 times the diameter** for adequate retention
2. Minimum wall thickness of metallic insert: **0.6 × outer diameter**
3. Provide **draft angles** on the insert for easy mould release (1°–2°)
4. **Avoid sharp corners** on inserts—use radii of at least **0.5 mm** to prevent stress concentration
5. Provide **flange or head** for thin-walled plastic sections
6. **Pre-heat inserts** to 80–120°C to reduce thermal shock and surface condensation

*CTE Mismatch Importance:*
- Polymer CTE is typically **5–10× higher** than metal
- High mismatch causes **residual stresses**, **cracking** in brittle polymers, and **interface debonding**
- Excessive mismatch can also cause **warpage** of the part

*Methods to Overcome Adverse Effects:*
1. **Material selection**: Use inserts with CTE closer to the polymer (e.g., brass vs. aluminium)
2. **Pre-heating inserts** to reduce the temperature gradient during moulding
3. **Use of compliant intermediate layers** (elastomeric coatings)
4. **Optimize insert geometry**: Use gradual transitions instead of sharp corners
5. **Select low-shrinkage polymers** (e.g., filled nylon vs. unfilled PP)
6. **Annealing** of finished parts to relieve residual stresses

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $d = 6$ mm
- $L = 10$ mm
- $\tau = 45$ MPa
- Safety factor $SF = 3.5$

**Part (i): Theoretical Pull-out Force**

$$F_{pullout} = \tau \cdot A_{shear} = \tau \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L$$

Calculate shear area:
$$A_{shear} = \pi \times 6 \times 10 = 188.5 \text{ mm}^2$$

Calculate force:
$$F_{theoretical} = 45 \times 188.5 = 8482.3 \text{ N} \approx 8.48 \text{ kN}$$

**Part (ii): Design Pull-out Force**

$$F_{design} = \frac{F_{theoretical}}{SF} = \frac{8482.3}{3.5} = 2423.5 \text{ N} \approx 2.42 \text{ kN}$$

**Comment on Adequacy:**

Length-to-diameter ratio: $L/d = 10/6 = 1.67$

Since $L/d = 1.67 > 1.5$ (the KTU recommended minimum), the insert geometry is **adequately designed** based on retention length criterion. The design pull-out force of **2.42 kN** is sufficient for most medium-duty assembly applications.

> **Final Answer:** $F_{theoretical} = 8.48$ kN; $F_{design} = 2.42$ kN. Insert is **adequately designed**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing radius with diameter** in the pressure formula — examiners specifically test whether you use $r$ or $d$ correctly.
> 2. **Forgetting the Poisson term** $\nu_p$ — losing 1 mark instantly.
> 3. **Wrong direction of CTE difference** — always subtract the smaller from the larger CTE; sign errors yield negative pressures.
> 4. **Ignoring safety factors** in design questions — KTU 2024 scheme mandates a **factor of safety between 2.5 and 4.0** for insert retention.
> 5. **Not mentioning the cooling rate effect** — slow cooling reduces residual stresses and improves retention.
> 6. **Confusing press-fit inserts with moulded-in inserts** — they are categorically different installation methods.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Moulded-in-Inserts**

- **Definition**: Pre-formed non-polymer component placed in mould cavity before injection → permanently encapsulated in finished part.
- **Primary Functions**: Threading, electrical conductivity, reinforcement, wear resistance, heat dissipation, assembly datums.
- **Common Materials**: Steel (structural), brass (electrical), aluminium (lightweight), bronze (bearings), stainless steel (food/medical).
- **Three Retention Mechanisms**:
  1. Mechanical interlock (undercuts, knurls)
  2. Differential thermal contraction (shrinkage fit)
  3. Chemical/adhesive bonding
- **Design Rule of Thumb**: $L \geq 1.5d$ for insert length; $t_{wall} \geq 0.6d$ for insert wall thickness.
- **CTE Hierarchy**: Polymer (70–150 × 10⁻⁶/°C) >> Brass/Aluminium >> Steel (11 × 10⁻⁶/°C).
- **Key Formula for Pressure**: $P = \dfrac{\delta}{r\left(\frac{1}{E_p} + \frac{1-\nu_p}{E_i}\right)}$
- **Key Formula for Pull-out Force**: $F = \tau \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot L$
- **Safety Factor**: Use 2.5 to 4.0 for static insert retention (KTU standard is 3.0).
- **Anti-rotation**: Use hex flats, splines, or non-circular cross-sections.
- **Pre-heating**: Recommended at 80–120°C to reduce thermal shock and prevent surface moisture.
- **Common Failure Modes**: Pull-out, rotational slippage, polymer cracking, insert deformation, flash at interface.
- **Applications**: Electrical plugs, automotive parts, drill housings, appliance knobs, aerospace components, medical devices.
- **Mould Design**: Inserts are supported on core pins, locating pins, or magnetic holders to prevent displacement during injection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Plastic Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **plastic thread** (also termed a polymer thread or molded polymer thread) is a continuous helical ridge of uniform cross-section formed on the internal or external cylindrical surface of a polymer component, designed to mate with a corresponding thread on a mating part (metal, polymer, or composite) to provide a secure, removable, or permanent mechanical fastening. Plastic threads are typically classified as **molded-in threads**, **machined threads**, **insert threads**, **self-tapping threads**, and **ultrasonically or heat-staked threads**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with product design, plastic threads are treated as integral or secondary functional features that transform a simple molded article into a **load-bearing fastening element**, governed by the rules of ISO metric thread profiles (ISO 261, ISO 965) or specialized profiles such as **buttress, ACME, or trapezoidal** when high axial load transfer is required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPOT601 - Module 2):** Plastic threads form the bridge between *materials selection* and *product design for assembly (DFA)*. Every design decision (thread profile, pitch, lead, boss wall thickness, core diameter, and mold draft) directly controls the **demoldability, mechanical strength, and assembly torque** of the final product.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are fastening a wooden lid onto a glass jar. With a glass jar, you cannot cut threads directly into the glass — it would shatter. So you either glue a metal ring onto the jar, or pour molten plastic into a thread-shaped cavity to form a thread directly. Plastic threads work on exactly this principle. **Plastic is not metal** — it is viscoelastic, anisotropic after molding, and weaker in shear. So the *design approach* must respect these limits:

- If the thread is **strong enough** and **production volume is high**, you mold the thread directly into the part (cheap, fast, but limited precision).
- If the thread is **subjected to repeated assembly/disassembly** or **high torque**, you use a **metal insert** embedded in a plastic boss (more expensive, but very durable).
- If the part is a **soft plastic like PP or PE**, you let a metal screw **cut its own thread** (self-tapping screw) during assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Plastic Thread Design (KTU Mnemonic — "P-F-M"):**
> 1. **P**rofile (shape — V, buttress, ACME, square)
> 2. **F**unction (permanent vs. removable, torque level, cycle count)
> 3. **M**anufacturing route (molded-in, machined, insert, self-tap)

### 1.3 Classification of Plastic Threads
Plastic threads are broadly classified into **five engineering categories** recognized in KTU PCPOT601 syllabus:

1. **Molded-in Threads** — Formed during the primary molding operation (injection/compression molding) using threaded cores or side-action cavities.
2. **Machined Threads** — Cut into a fully molded plastic blank using taps (internal) or dies (external) on a lathe or tapping machine.
3. **Inserted Threads (Threaded Inserts)** — Pre-formed metal or polymer inserts (brass, stainless steel, heat-set) molded-in or post-molded into a boss.
4. **Self-Tapping / Self-Forming Threads** — Created during assembly when a hardened screw is driven into a pilot hole in the plastic; the screw displaces plastic to form mating threads.
5. **Ultrasonic / Heat-Staked Threads** — Metal inserts pressed into the plastic via ultrasonic vibration or thermal softening (also called **heat-set inserts**).

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used
The following metrics (highlighted in **bold**) are universally applied in plastic thread design per ISO and KTU reference texts:

- **Pitch (P)** — axial distance between adjacent thread crests, in **mm**.
- **Major Diameter (D)** — outermost diameter of an external thread / innermost diameter of an internal thread, in **mm**.
- **Minor Diameter (D₁)** — root diameter; critical for **boss wall thickness** calculation.
- **Threads per unit length (n)** — number of threads per inch (TPI) for inch-based systems or threads per cm in metric.
- **Engagement Length (Lₑ)** — length of thread overlap between internal and external threads, ≥ **1.5 × D** for full mating strength.
- **Stripping Torque (Tₛ)** — torque at which the internal plastic thread fails in shear, expressed in **N·m**.
- **Driving Torque (T_d)** — torque required to install the screw/insert, ≤ **0.6 × Tₛ** to ensure assembly safety.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Design Logic for Plastic Threads
Designing a plastic threaded joint involves a sequential decision tree. The KTU 2024 Scheme outcome demands that students internalize this sequence and justify each step:

**Step 1 — Service Condition Analysis**
- Is the joint **permanent** (one-time assembly) or **removable** (multiple cycles)?
- What is the expected **axial load** (tensile/compressive)?
- What is the **environmental exposure** (temperature, chemicals, UV)?
- What is the **assembly torque** budget?

**Step 2 — Thread Type Selection**
- **Molded-in threads** → high-volume, low-cost, limited precision.
- **Machined threads** → low-volume, high-precision, expensive.
- **Insert threads** → repeated assembly, high strength required.
- **Self-tapping threads** → soft thermoplastics (PP, PE), single-assembly.

**Step 3 — Pitch and Diameter Selection**
- Use **coarse pitch** (e.g., M6 × 1.0 rather than M6 × 0.75) for plastic — coarse threads have larger root area, more resistant to stripping.
- Standard coarse metric threads per **ISO 261** are preferred.

**Step 4 — Boss and Wall Thickness Design**
The boss (the cylindrical projection of plastic surrounding the hole) must be thick enough to withstand the **radial expansion forces** during screw insertion. The standard empirical rule used in industry (and adopted by KTU valuation) is:

- **Boss outer diameter (D_b)** ≈ **2.0 × Major diameter (D)** of the screw.
- **Boss wall thickness (t_w)** ≈ **0.5 × D** (minimum).
- **Pilot hole diameter** for self-tapping screws ≈ **(0.8 to 0.85) × D**.

**Step 5 — Draft Angle and Demolding**
Because molded threads are on internal cylindrical surfaces, the core pin must be unscrewed from the molded part. This requires either:
- **Undercut design with collapsible cores**, or
- **Reduced draft with multi-start threads**, or
- **Thread undercut removed and replaced with a smooth hole** (most common).

For external threads on plastic, a draft of **0.5° to 1°** is added to allow demolding without thread damage.

**Step 6 — Verification of Stripping Strength**
Calculate the **stripping torque** and confirm that **driving torque < 60% of stripping torque**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbol / Equation | Units | Validity / Boundary |
|---|---------------------|-------------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Pitch (axial distance per thread) | $P = \dfrac{1}{n}$ | mm | $P \geq 0.5$ mm for molded threads |
| 2 | Stripping Torque (internal plastic thread) | $T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | N·m | Valid for $L_e / D \leq 2.5$ |
| 3 | Engagement Length (minimum) | $L_e \geq 1.5 \cdot D$ | mm | KTU standard |
| 4 | Boss Outer Diameter | $D_b \approx 2.0 \cdot D$ | mm | Empirical rule |
| 5 | Boss Wall Thickness | $t_w \approx 0.5 \cdot D$ | mm | Minimum guideline |
| 6 | Allowable Shear Stress (plastic, ~30% of yield) | $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \cdot \sigma_y$ | MPa | For thermoplastics |
| 7 | Driving Torque Safety Factor | $T_d \leq 0.6 \cdot T_s$ | — | KTU design rule |
| 8 | Pilot Hole Diameter (self-tap) | $d_{hole} = (0.80 \text{ to } 0.85) \cdot D$ | mm | For self-tapping screws |
| 9 | Core Diameter (metric ISO) | $D_1 = D - 1.0825 \cdot P$ | mm | ISO 965 basic profile |
| 10 | Tensile Stress Area | $A_s = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(\dfrac{D + D_1}{2}\right)^2$ | mm² | ISO 898 |
| 11 | Tightening Torque (Mischke equation) | $T = K \cdot F \cdot d$ | N·m | $K \approx 0.2$ for plastic |
| 12 | Preload Force | $F = \sigma_{pre} \cdot A_s$ | N | $\sigma_{pre} \leq 0.75 \cdot \sigma_y$ |
| 13 | Boss Radial Hoop Stress | $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{p \cdot r}{t_w}$ | MPa | Thin-wall cylinder approximation |
| 14 | Stripping Force (axial pull-out) | $F_s = \pi \cdot D_1 \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$ | N | Internal thread shear failure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention Used in the Table:** The symbol $\vert$ for absolute value has been deliberately written using $\vert$ LaTeX command in any tabular cell where needed, so that the markdown pipeline does not break the column structure. The vertical bar **inside an equation environment** is still rendered correctly because LaTeX processes it separately.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plastic threads are encountered in virtually every polymer product engineered for assembly:

- **Automotive:** Dashboard mounts, bumper fasteners, headlamp adjuster screws.
- **Electronics:** Mobile phone back covers (self-tap into polycarbonate/ABS).
- **Plumbing:** PVC pipe threads (tapered NPT/BSP variants), PE fittings.
- **Consumer goods:** Bottle caps (PP threads on PET bottles), appliance housings.
- **Medical:** Syringe luer locks, IV drip chambers, inhaler mouthpieces.
- **Aerospace interiors:** PA66 molded-in threads for panel retention.

In each application, the engineering challenge is **balancing manufacturability, strength, cost, and assembly ergonomics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stripping Torque Equation for Internal Plastic Threads

The **stripping torque** $T_s$ is the torque at which the internal plastic thread shears along the cylindrical surface defined by the **mean thread diameter**. We derive this step-by-step in standard KTU board-exam style.

**Starting principle:** When a screw is tightened, the helical thread surface on the plastic is subjected to a shear stress $\tau$. The resisting torque equals the shear force acting at radius $r_m$ (mean thread radius) integrated over the engagement length.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry**

Let $D$ = nominal major diameter of the screw, $D_1$ = minor (root) diameter of the screw (= tap drill diameter + minor thread depth). The **mean thread diameter** is:

$$D_m = \frac{D + D_1}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express the shear force on the helical surface**

The cylindrical surface area of the engaged plastic thread (shear plane) is approximately the circumference times the engagement length:

$$A_{shear} = \pi \cdot D_m \cdot L_e$$

**Step 3 — Write the shear force**

The shear force resisting axial stripping is:

$$F_s = \tau_{allow} \cdot A_{shear} = \pi \cdot D_m \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$$

**Step 4 — Convert shear force to torque**

The torque required to overcome this shear force, applied at the mean radius $D_m / 2$, is:

$$T_s = F_s \cdot \frac{D_m}{2}$$

Substituting $F_s$:

$$T_s = \pi \cdot D_m \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow} \cdot \frac{D_m}{2} = \frac{\pi \cdot D_m^{\,2} \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $D_m$ using $D$ and $D_1$**

Since $D_m = (D + D_1) / 2$, we get:

$$D_m^{\,2} = \left(\frac{D + D_1}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{(D + D_1)^2}{4}$$

Substituting back:

$$T_s = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{(D + D_1)^2}{4} \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow} = \frac{\pi \cdot (D + D_1)^2 \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}}{8}$$

**Step 6 — Final compact KTU form (commonly quoted)**

In most engineering handbooks, the formula is simplified by replacing $D_m$ with $D$ (since $D_m \approx D$ for coarse threads), yielding the practical equation:

$$\boxed{T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}}$$

This is the equation that appears in the KTU Formula Sheet.

**Step 7 — Apply boundary conditions**

- $L_e \leq 2.5 \cdot D$ — beyond this, screw failure (not thread stripping) governs.
- $\tau_{allow} \approx 0.30 \cdot \sigma_y$ for unreinforced thermoplastics.
- For glass-filled plastics, $\tau_{allow}$ may rise by 40–60%.

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

**Problem (typical 7-mark sub-part):** A steel screw of M6 (ISO metric coarse, pitch 1.0 mm) is to be molded into a glass-filled polyamide (PA66 + 30% GF) boss. The engagement length is 12 mm. The yield strength of the plastic is $\sigma_y = 110$ MPa. Calculate the **stripping torque** and recommend whether a driving torque of 4.5 N·m is safe.

**Given:**
- $D = 6$ mm, $P = 1.0$ mm, $L_e = 12$ mm
- $\sigma_y = 110$ MPa

**Step A — Compute $D_1$ using ISO 965 basic profile:**

$$D_1 = D - 1.0825 \cdot P = 6 - 1.0825 \cdot 1.0 = 4.9175 \text{ mm}$$

**Step B — Compute allowable shear stress (30% of yield):**

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \cdot \sigma_y = 0.30 \cdot 110 = 33 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step C — Apply the stripping torque equation:**

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow} = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot 6 \cdot 12 \cdot 33$$

Let us expand this:

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 6 \cdot 12 \cdot 33$$

$$T_s = 1.5708 \cdot 6 \cdot 12 \cdot 33$$

$$T_s = 1.5708 \cdot 72 \cdot 33$$

$$T_s = 113.0976 \cdot 33$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 3732.22 \text{ N·mm} = 3.73 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step D — Safety check on driving torque:**

$$T_d = 4.5 \text{ N·m} > 0.6 \cdot T_s = 0.6 \cdot 3.73 = 2.24 \text{ N·m}$$

**Conclusion:** The driving torque of 4.5 N·m **exceeds the safe limit**. The plastic thread will strip. The designer must either:
1. Increase the boss outer diameter.
2. Increase engagement length.
3. Switch to a metal insert (brass heat-set insert).
4. Use a smaller screw size.

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Selection of Plastic Thread Type

| Design Requirement | Molded-in | Machined | Insert | Self-Tap |
|--------------------|:---------:|:--------:|:------:|:--------:|
| Low unit cost (high volume) | ★★★ | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| High mechanical strength | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Repeated assembly cycles | ★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| Tight dimensional tolerance | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Suitable for soft plastics (PP/PE) | ★★ | ★★ | ★★★ | ★★★ |
| Suitable for glass-filled plastics | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| Demoldability / mold complexity | ★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★ |
| Corrosion resistance | ★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway:** For KTU board answers, whenever a question asks *"which thread type would you recommend?"*, follow this 3-step rule:
> 1. State the **functional requirement** (strength / cycles / cost).
> 2. Match it to the **best-suited thread type** using the matrix above.
> 3. Justify with **one quantitative reason** (e.g., engagement length, cost per piece, cycle count).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Plastic Thread Design Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Service Conditions] --> B{Assembly Type?}
    B -->|Permanent| C[Use Molded-in Thread]
    B -->|Removable, High Strength| D[Use Metal Insert]
    B -->|Single Assembly, Soft Plastic| E[Use Self-Tapping Screw]
    B -->|High Precision| F[Use Machined Thread]
    C --> G[Select Pitch and Diameter]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Design Boss Geometry]
    H --> I[Calculate Stripping Torque Ts]
    I --> J{Driving Td less than 0.6 Ts?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Accept Design]
    J -->|No| L[Modify: Increase Boss or Use Insert]
    L --> I
    K --> M[End: Final Thread Specification]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Plastic Thread Types and Their Manufacturing Routes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MT[Molded Threads]
        MT1[Injection Molded]
        MT2[Compression Molded]
    end
    subgraph IT[Insert Threads]
        IT1[Brass Insert]
        IT2[Stainless Steel Insert]
        IT3[Heat Set Insert]
    end
    subgraph ST[Self Tapping Threads]
        ST1[Type AB Screw]
        ST2[Type B Screw]
        ST3[Thread Forming Screw]
    end
    subgraph MACHT[Machined Threads]
        MACHT1[Tapped Internal]
        MACHT2[Died External]
    end
    MT --> APP[Application: Consumer Goods, Caps, Housings]
    IT --> APP
    ST --> APP
    MACHT --> APP
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Boss Geometry Design Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Major Diameter D] --> P2[Boss Outer Diameter Db = 2.0 D]
    P1 --> P3[Boss Wall Thickness tw = 0.5 D]
    P1 --> P4[Pilot Hole Diameter d_hole = 0.80 to 0.85 D]
    P1 --> P5[Engagement Length Le ≥ 1.5 D]
    P2 --> OUT[Final Boss Geometry]
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
    P5 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic — Internal Plastic Thread with Boss

A typical molded-in internal thread in a boss appears as follows (described schematically since Mermaid cannot render a true cross-section with thread pitch):

| Layer / Component | Description |
|-------------------|-------------|
| **Outer boss wall** | Cylindrical plastic wall; outer diameter $D_b \approx 2D$. |
| **Engagement zone** | Length $L_e$ where threads interlock with mating screw. |
| **Thread root** | Minor diameter $D_1$ — smallest inside diameter of the thread. |
| **Thread crest** | Major diameter $D$ — largest inside diameter of the thread. |
| **Draft angle** | $0.5°$ to $1°$ taper on the core pin to enable demolding. |
| **Undercut relief** | Smooth hole section beyond the threaded region to release the core. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing Interpretation Tip:** When KTU examination asks to "sketch a typical plastic boss with internal thread", draw a sectioned view showing: (1) the boss outline, (2) the helical thread profile inside, (3) the engagement length labeled $L_e$, (4) the boss outer diameter $D_b$, and (5) the wall thickness $t_w$. This 5-label sketch earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "stripping torque" of a plastic internal thread. Mention one factor that affects it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **stripping torque** of a plastic internal thread is the maximum tightening torque at which the plastic thread shears and fails in shear, allowing the screw to be pulled out without further rotation. It is the torque at which the helical thread surface fractures along its mean diameter.

$$\text{Factor affecting it: Engagement length } (L_e), \text{ screw diameter } (D), \text{ or material shear strength } (\tau_{allow}).$$

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Differentiate between a self-tapping screw and a self-forming screw used in plastic assemblies.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Self-Tapping Screw | Self-Forming Screw |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Action | Cuts a thread by **removing** plastic chips | **Displaces** plastic to form mating thread |
| Hole prep | Requires a pilot hole of correct diameter | Uses an undersized pilot hole |
| Suitable for | Ductile plastics like PP, PE | Ductile plastics like PA, ABS |
| Cycle count | Limited (cuts weaken surrounding material) | Better thread reuse |

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Explain the different **types of plastic threads** used in polymer product design, with a sketch and labeled diagram for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a plastic boss for an M8 steel screw (ISO metric coarse, pitch 1.25 mm) molded into an unreinforced polycarbonate (PC) part with engagement length 15 mm. Yield strength of PC is 60 MPa. Calculate the **stripping torque** and check whether a driving torque of 5 N·m is safe. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Discuss five types of plastic threads:
1. **Molded-in threads** — formed during injection molding; cost-effective for high-volume parts; require collapsible cores or thread undercut for internal threads; draft angle of 0.5°–1° on external threads; common in bottle caps, enclosures.
2. **Machined threads** — cut by taps (internal) or dies (external) on finished molded parts; expensive, used for prototypes, low volumes, and precision parts; tolerances ±0.05 mm achievable.
3. **Inserted threads (threaded inserts)** — pre-formed metal (brass, stainless steel) or polymer inserts placed in the mold or post-molded; high strength, repeatable use; heat-set inserts melt into the boss using ultrasonic or thermal energy; widely used in automotive.
4. **Self-tapping threads** — screw cuts its own thread during assembly; pilot hole 0.80–0.85 × D; one-time assembly typical; suitable for soft plastics like PP/PE.
5. **Ultrasonic / heat-staked threads** — metal inserts embedded using ultrasonic vibration (~20–40 kHz) which locally melts the plastic; strong joint; cost-effective for medium volumes.

*(Examiner's tip: A labeled sketch of a molded-in thread boss + an insert thread + a self-tap cross-section will earn full marks.)*

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $D = 8$ mm, $P = 1.25$ mm, $L_e = 15$ mm, $\sigma_y = 60$ MPa, $T_d = 5$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Minor diameter:**

$$D_1 = D - 1.0825 \cdot P = 8 - 1.0825 \cdot 1.25 = 8 - 1.353 = 6.647 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable shear stress:**

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \cdot \sigma_y = 0.30 \cdot 60 = 18 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Stripping torque:**

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$$

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 8 \cdot 15 \cdot 18$$

$$T_s = 1.5708 \cdot 120 \cdot 18$$

$$T_s = 188.496 \cdot 18$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 3392.92 \text{ N·mm} = 3.39 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Safety check:**

$$0.6 \cdot T_s = 0.6 \cdot 3.39 = 2.04 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_d = 5 \text{ N·m} > 2.04 \text{ N·m}$, the design is **unsafe**. The plastic thread will strip.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**
- Use **brass heat-set insert** to increase thread strength by 2–3×.
- Or **increase engagement length** to ~25 mm.
- Or **use glass-filled PC** with $\sigma_y = 90$ MPa.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Using $D_m$ (mean diameter) instead of $D$ in the simplified formula $T_s = 0.5 \pi D L_e \tau$. The simplified form uses $D$, not $D_m$. Both are accepted, but state which one you are using.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert N·mm to N·m in the final answer (1 N·m = 1000 N·mm).
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not comparing $T_d$ with $0.6 \cdot T_s$ and concluding "unsafe" — students often forget to convert units and write $T_s$ directly in N·m without the factor of 1000 conversion.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **design guidelines for a plastic boss** used to receive a metal screw. List at least six guidelines with justifications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** An M10 × 1.5 mm steel screw is to be driven into a glass-fiber reinforced polyamide (PA66 + 30% GF) boss with engagement length 20 mm. Yield strength of the plastic is 165 MPa. Determine the **stripping torque** and recommend whether the design is safe for a driving torque of 18 N·m. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Design guidelines for a plastic boss:
1. **Boss outer diameter ≥ 2 × screw diameter** — to distribute radial expansion stress and avoid boss cracking.
2. **Boss wall thickness ≥ 0.5 × screw diameter** — to provide adequate shear resistance and prevent thread stripping.
3. **Coarse pitch threads preferred** — coarse threads have larger root cross-section, more resistant to stripping.
4. **Pilot hole diameter = 0.80–0.85 × D** for self-tapping screws — too small = boss cracking; too large = thread stripping under low torque.
5. **Engagement length ≥ 1.5 × D** — to develop full thread strength; values up to 2.5 × D give diminishing returns.
6. **Provide a draft angle of 0.5°–1°** on external features for demoldability.
7. **Use a generous fillet radius** at the boss base (≥ 0.4 × wall thickness) to reduce stress concentration.
8. **Keep boss away from part edges** (minimum distance ≥ 2 × D) to avoid edge cracking.
9. **Use metal inserts for repeated assembly** — plastic threads wear out after 5–10 cycles.
10. **Avoid sharp corners** at the boss root — use radius ≥ 0.25 mm.

**(b) Numerical solution:**

Given: $D = 10$ mm, $P = 1.5$ mm, $L_e = 20$ mm, $\sigma_y = 165$ MPa, $T_d = 18$ N·m.

**Step 1 — Minor diameter:**

$$D_1 = D - 1.0825 \cdot P = 10 - 1.0825 \cdot 1.5 = 10 - 1.624 = 8.376 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Allowable shear stress (30% rule):**

$$\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \cdot 165 = 49.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Stripping torque:**

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$$

$$T_s = 0.5 \cdot 3.1416 \cdot 10 \cdot 20 \cdot 49.5$$

$$T_s = 1.5708 \cdot 200 \cdot 49.5$$

$$T_s = 314.16 \cdot 49.5$$

$$\boxed{T_s = 15{,}550.92 \text{ N·mm} = 15.55 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 4 — Safety check:**

$$0.6 \cdot T_s = 0.6 \cdot 15.55 = 9.33 \text{ N·m}$$

Since $T_d = 18 \text{ N·m} > 9.33 \text{ N·m}$, the design is **unsafe**. The plastic thread will strip before the screw is fully tightened.

**Step 5 — Recommendation:**
- Reduce driving torque to ≤ 9 N·m, or
- Increase engagement length to 35 mm, or
- Use a brass heat-set insert to raise stripping torque 2–3×.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Using $\sigma_y$ directly in the shear equation instead of $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \sigma_y$. This will overestimate $T_s$ by ~3.3×.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Using $P$ instead of $1.0825 P$ in $D_1$ calculation — ISO metric threads use $1.0825 P$ for the basic profile, not $P$.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Not stating the conclusion — KTU examiners explicitly look for a one-line conclusion like *"The design is unsafe because $T_d > 0.6 T_s$"* to award the final mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Plastic threads** fall into **five categories**: molded-in, machined, insert, self-tapping, and ultrasonic/heat-set.
- **Coarse pitch** threads are preferred over fine pitch because they have larger root cross-section and resist stripping.
- **Stripping torque** formula (KTU high-yield): $T_s = 0.5 \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot L_e \cdot \tau_{allow}$ where $\tau_{allow} = 0.30 \sigma_y$.
- **Safety rule:** Driving torque $T_d \leq 0.6 \cdot T_s$. If violated, the thread will strip.
- **Boss design rules:** Outer diameter $\approx 2D$, wall thickness $\approx 0.5D$, engagement length $\geq 1.5D$, draft angle $0.5°$–$1°$.
- **Self-tapping screws** need a pilot hole of $(0.80 \text{ to } 0.85) \times D$.
- **Metal inserts (brass, stainless)** are mandatory for high-cycle applications (>10 assembly cycles).
- **Minor diameter** of an ISO metric thread: $D_1 = D - 1.0825 P$.
- **Glass-fiber reinforcement** increases $\sigma_y$ and $\tau_{allow}$ by 40–60% compared to unfilled plastic.
- **Always state the conclusion** explicitly in numerical answers — KTU board examiners reserve 1 mark for the safety conclusion.
- **Convert N·mm to N·m** by dividing by 1000 in the final answer; this is the most common unit-conversion error.
- **For KTU 14-mark questions**, always split into (a) descriptive part (7 marks) and (b) numerical part (7 marks) — and answer both sub-parts in order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blind Holes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blind Holes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Blind Hole** is a cylindrical or non-cylindrical cavity machined or molded into a plastic component that **does not pass entirely through the parent material**. In the context of polymer product design and injection molding, blind holes are formed by **stationary or side-action cores** that protrude into the mold cavity from one face of the part, leaving a closed end (bottom) inside the component.

By KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature used in PCPOT601, blind holes fall under the broader category of **Internal Features in Plastic Part Design**, and their feasibility is dictated by the **Depth-to-Diameter (L/D) ratio**, **draft requirements**, **mold construction**, and **processing parameters** of the chosen polymer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Definition (Board Exam Favourite):**
> *A blind hole is a non-through hole whose depth is intentionally less than the full thickness of the workpiece, with the bottom wall of the hole being integral (formed from the same molded material) rather than open.*

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine pushing your finger into a lump of wet clay from one side — you create a cavity that has an opening at the top but a sealed bottom. That cavity is essentially a **blind hole**.

In daily life:
- The **recessed slot in a screwdriver handle** where the metal shaft inserts → blind hole concept.
- The **counterbore (screw recess) on a plastic cover** that holds a screw head without letting it pass through → another blind hole variant.
- The **cavity inside a plastic gear hub** that accepts a shaft but doesn't allow it to exit → classic engineering blind hole.

The core idea: **the hole has only one opening and one closed end**.

## 1.3 Classification of Blind Holes (Polymer Design)

For design clarity, blind holes in plastic parts are classified into three categories:

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Standard Blind Hole** | Simple cylindrical bore parallel to mold opening direction | Bosses, mounting recesses, fastener seats |
| **Off-Axis Blind Hole** | Hole axis not parallel to mold opening | Requires side-action core |
| **Stepped/Tapered Blind Hole** | Variable diameter or conical blind recess | Countersinks, draft-locked fasteners |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 2 of PCPOT601 specifically covers *Design of internal features in plastic parts*. Blind holes constitute a **direct Part-A (3 marks) and Part-B (14 marks) frequent topic** because they integrate molding science with product functionality.

---

## 1.4 Distinction: Blind Hole vs. Through Hole

| Parameter | Blind Hole | Through Hole |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| Geometry | One open end + one closed end | Two open ends |
| Mold Core Type | Single-ended (cantilever) | Two-ended (supported) |
| L/D Limitation | **Strict (≤ 4:1 typical)** | Relaxed (≤ 10:1) |
| Bottom Wall | **Mandatory** | None |
| Cooling of Core | Difficult (only one end exposed) | Easier (both ends accessible) |
| Cost | Higher (side-actions if off-axis) | Lower |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Whenever a question mentions *"minimum wall thickness at hole bottom"* or *"core deflection"*, the answer lies in **blind hole design rules**, not through hole design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Blind Holes Are a Design Challenge

Unlike through holes (which are supported at **both ends** by core pins passing between two mold halves), blind holes are formed by **cantilevered cores** that protrude into only one mold half. This creates four fundamental engineering challenges:

1. **Core Deflection** — The unsupported end of the core bends under injection pressure.
2. **Heat Extraction** — Cooling fluid cannot easily reach the closed end of the core.
3. **Demolding** — Plastic shrinks onto the core, generating frictional lock.
4. **Venting** — Air trapped at the closed end of the cavity must escape.

## 2.2 The Six Governing Design Rules (KTU High-Yield)

### Rule 1 — Depth-to-Diameter (L/D) Ratio

The maximum recommended L/D for a blind hole molded parallel to the parting line is:

$$\frac{L}{D} \leq 4$$

For **engineering thermoplastics** (ABS, PC, PA), this ratio is **relaxed to 5:1** with proper venting.
