For **filled or fiber-reinforced polymers**, the safe ratio drops to **3:1** because fiber orientation increases stiffness and ejection force.

### Rule 2 — Minimum Hole Diameter

The smallest practical blind hole diameter is governed by **core strength**, not by the molding process:

$$D_{\min} \approx 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(general thermoplastics)}$$

For glass-filled polymers:

$$D_{\min} \approx 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

### Rule 3 — Bottom Wall Thickness (Critical!)

The closed end of the blind hole must be thick enough to:
- Resist injection pressure
- Avoid **sink marks** on the opposite face
- Provide adequate **knock-out force transmission**

$$t_{\text{bottom}} \geq 0.5 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(minimum)}$$

$$t_{\text{bottom}} \geq \frac{D}{3} \quad \text{(recommended design rule)}$$

### Rule 4 — Draft Angle (Taper)

To allow ejection of the plastic part from the cantilevered core:

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} = 0.5^\circ \text{ to } 1.0^\circ \quad \text{per side}$$

For deep blind holes (L/D > 2):

$$\theta_{\text{draft}} = 1.0^\circ \text{ to } 1.5^\circ$$

### Rule 5 — Core Deflection (Cantilever Beam Model)

The unsupported core acts as a **cantilever beam** under fluid injection pressure. The tip deflection is:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{P \cdot L^4}{8 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

Where:
- $P$ = injection pressure at cavity (typically **35–70 MPa**)
- $L$ = unsupported core length
- $E$ = Young's modulus of core steel (~**210 GPa**)
- $I$ = second moment of area of core cross-section

$$I = \frac{\pi D_{\text{core}}^4}{64}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This is why KTU examiners love asking *"Why are deep blind holes difficult to mold?"* — the answer ties core deflection to part dimensional tolerance.

### Rule 6 — Core Diameter vs. Hole Diameter Allowance

The core is **larger** than the molded hole diameter because plastic **shrinks onto the core** during cooling. The compensation formula is:

$$D_{\text{core}} = D_{\text{hole}} \times (1 + S)$$

Where $S$ is the volumetric shrinkage of the polymer (typically **0.5% – 3%** for most thermoplastics).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Range / Value | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|----------------------|------|
| 1 | Max Depth-to-Diameter | $L/D \leq 4$ | 2 – 4 | dimensionless |
| 2 | Minimum Hole Diameter | $D_{\min}$ | 1.0 – 1.5 | mm |
| 3 | Minimum Bottom Wall Thickness | $t_b \geq D/3$ | 0.5 – 2.0 | mm |
| 4 | Draft Angle | $\theta$ | 0.5 – 1.5 | degrees |
| 5 | Core Tip Deflection | $\delta = PL^4 / 8EI$ | < 0.05 | mm |
| 6 | Core Moment of Inertia | $I = \pi D_c^4 / 64$ | — | mm⁴ |
| 7 | Core Diameter Compensation | $D_c = D_h(1+S)$ | $S = 0.005$ – $0.03$ | mm |
| 8 | Bottom Wall Strength Check | $\sigma = P \cdot r / t_b$ | $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{yield}}$ | MPa |
| 9 | Boss Wall Around Hole | $t_{\text{boss}} \geq 0.6 D_h$ | 1.0 – 5.0 | mm |
| 10 | Hole-to-Hole Spacing | $C \geq D_{\max}/2$ | ≥ 1.5 | mm |

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Trick:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` notation for absolute values in tables — never the raw pipe character — to avoid markdown table breaking. This is the KTU 2024 Scheme answer-script formatting standard expected by examiners.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Blind holes are critical in:

- **Automotive**: Plastic intake manifolds (sensor ports), lamp housings (bulb recesses).
- **Electronics**: Connector housings (pin recesses), PCB stand-offs.
- **Medical**: Syringe barrels (luer recesses), inhaler mouthpieces.
- **Consumer Goods**: Bottle caps (tamper-evident recesses), power-tool housings (bearing seats).

The **design rules for blind holes directly control**:
- Dimensional accuracy of the part
- Mold cost and complexity
- Cycle time (cooling)
- Rejection rate due to **warpage, sink, and short shots**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cantilever Core Deflection Under Injection Pressure

**Given:**
A steel core of diameter $D_c = 4 \text{ mm}$, unsupported length $L = 25 \text{ mm}$, subjected to average injection pressure $P = 50 \text{ MPa}$ acting uniformly on its tip area.

**Find:** Maximum tip deflection $\delta_{\max}$.

### Step 1: Identify the load

The plastic melt pushes the core sideways as it fills the blind cavity. For conservative design, treat the load as a **uniformly distributed load** over the length of the core that emerges into the cavity.

For simplification in KTU problems, we use the **equivalent point load at the core tip**:

$$F = P \cdot A_{\text{core tip}} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D_c^2}{4}$$

### Step 2: Calculate the area

$$A = \frac{\pi (4)^2}{4} = 12.566 \text{ mm}^2$$

### Step 3: Calculate the applied force

$$F = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 12.566 \text{ mm}^2 = 628.3 \text{ N}$$

### Step 4: Moment of inertia of the circular core

$$I = \frac{\pi D_c^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (4)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 256}{64} = 12.566 \text{ mm}^4$$

### Step 5: Apply the cantilever beam deflection formula (point load at tip)

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{F \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

Using $E_{\text{steel}} = 2.1 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$:

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{628.3 \times (25)^3}{3 \times (2.1 \times 10^5) \times 12.566}$$

### Step 6: Evaluate numerator and denominator

$$\text{Numerator} = 628.3 \times 15625 = 9{,}817{,}187.5 \text{ N·mm}^3$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 3 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 12.566 = 7.917 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}^2$$

### Step 7: Final deflection

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{9.817 \times 10^6}{7.917 \times 10^6} = 1.24 \text{ mm}$$

**Conclusion:** A 1.24 mm tip deflection on a 4 mm diameter core is **excessive** (over 30% of core diameter). This violates the KTU recommended $\delta_{\max} \leq 0.05 \text{ mm}$ for precision parts. **Design action required**: increase core diameter, reduce L/D, or use a side-action core.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bottom Wall Thickness to Avoid Sink Marks

**Given:**
A blind hole of diameter $D = 6 \text{ mm}$ molded in ABS (shrinkage $S = 0.6\%$), molded against a polished cavity surface.

**Find:** Minimum bottom wall thickness to avoid visible sink on the cosmetic face.

### Step 1: Apply the KTU design rule

$$t_b \geq \frac{D}{3}$$

### Step 2: Substitute

$$t_b \geq \frac{6}{3} = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3: Check against minimum absolute floor

$$t_b \geq 0.5 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{always satisfied})$$

### Step 4: Verify by sink mark empirical correlation

The sink mark depth is empirically related to wall thickness by:

$$h_{\text{sink}} \approx 0.07 \cdot t_b$$

For acceptable cosmetic appearance, $h_{\text{sink}} \leq 0.05 \text{ mm}$:

$$t_b \leq \frac{0.05}{0.07} = 0.71 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(NOT acceptable — too thin)}$$

Therefore the design rule $t_b = D/3 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$ governs, and the part passes both structural and cosmetic checks.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Flow

| Design Situation | Recommended Solution | Rationale |
|------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| Blind hole parallel to mold opening | Standard core pin in cavity half | Lowest cost |
| Blind hole perpendicular to opening | **Side-action core** | Required for ejection |
| Deep blind hole (L/D > 4) | Use **bored ejector pin** or stepped core | Prevents deflection |
| Blind hole in filled polymer | Reduce L/D to 3:1, increase draft to 1.5° | High shrinkage & friction |
| Through hole preferred | Redesign part as through hole | Cheaper, more accurate |
| Blind hole on curved surface | Use **collapsible core** | Avoids undercuts |

---

## 3.4 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU Standard 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** An ABS component requires a blind hole of diameter **8 mm** and depth **20 mm** parallel to the mold opening. The polymer shrinkage is **0.6%**, injection pressure is **50 MPa**, and the core is made of P-20 tool steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa).

**Tasks:**
(a) Check if the design is feasible. Justify with calculations.
(b) Recommend design modifications if infeasible.

### Part (a) Solution:

**Check 1 — L/D ratio:**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5$$

$$2.5 \leq 4 \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(Passes Rule 1)}$$

**Check 2 — Minimum diameter:**

$$D = 8 \text{ mm} \geq 1.0 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark \quad \text{(Passes Rule 2)}$$

**Check 3 — Bottom wall thickness:**

$$t_b \geq \frac{D}{3} = \frac{8}{3} = 2.67 \text{ mm}$$

Assuming parent wall $T = 6 \text{ mm}$, available bottom thickness = $6 - 20 = \text{infeasible}$ → parent wall must be ≥ $22.67 \text{ mm}$.

**Check 4 — Draft angle:**

Recommended: $\theta = 1.0^\circ$ for L/D = 2.5.

**Check 5 — Core tip deflection:**

$$A = \frac{\pi (8)^2}{4} = 50.27 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F = 50 \times 50.27 = 2513.3 \text{ N}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi (8)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 4096}{64} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{2513.3 \times (20)^3}{3 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 201.06}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{2513.3 \times 8000}{1.266 \times 10^8} = \frac{2.011 \times 10^7}{1.266 \times 10^8} = 0.159 \text{ mm}$$

$$0.159 \text{ mm} > 0.05 \text{ mm} \quad \times \quad \text{(FAILS Rule 5)}$$

### Part (b) Solution:

The design fails the core deflection criterion. **Recommended modifications:**

1. **Reduce L/D** to 2:1 by either shortening the hole to **16 mm** or enlarging to **10 mm**.
2. **Increase core diameter** by using a **bored ejector pin** (hollow core) — improves cooling and reduces deflection.
3. **Add a core support** — use a secondary core or insert if mold design allows.
4. **Add venting** at the hole bottom to release trapped air (vent depth = 0.01–0.03 mm).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Blind Hole Geometry — Functional Block Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Melt Injected] --> B[Flow Enters Cavity]
    B --> C[Core Pin Forms Blind Hole Walls]
    C --> D[Closed Bottom Wall Forms t_b]
    D --> E[Plastic Shrinks Onto Core S]
    E --> F[Mold Opens]
    F --> G[Part Ejected with Draft theta]
    G --> H[Blind Hole Released]

    A1[Part Geometry] --> A2{Off-axis hole?}
    A2 -- Yes --> A3[Side-Action Core Required]
    A2 -- No --> A4[Standard Core Pin]
    A3 --> C
    A4 --> C

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333
```

## 4.2 Blind Hole vs. Through Hole — Design Comparison Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BlindHole
        BH1[Top Surface] --> BH2[Cavity Opening]
        BH2 --> BH3[L mm depth]
        BH3 --> BH4[Bottom Wall t_b]
        BH4 --> BH5[Closed Face]
    end

    subgraph ThroughHole
        TH1[Top Face] --> TH2[Entry]
        TH2 --> TH3[L mm depth]
        TH3 --> TH4[Exit]
        TH4 --> TH5[Bottom Face]
    end

    BH3 -.L/D ≤ 4.-> BHNote1[Cantilever core]
    TH3 -.L/D ≤ 10.-> THNote1[Two-ended core]

    style BH1 fill:#FFD700
    style BH5 fill:#FF6347
    style TH1 fill:#87CEEB
    style TH5 fill:#4682B4
```

## 4.3 Core Deflection Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Load[Injection Pressure P] --> CoreTip[Force at Core Tip F]
    CoreTip --> Beam[Cantilever Beam Model]
    Beam --> Defl[Deflection delta_max]
    Defl --> Check{Delta ≤ 0.05 mm?}
    Check -- Yes --> Pass[Pass Design]
    Check -- No --> Mod[Modify Design]
    Mod --> Mod1[Increase D_core]
    Mod --> Mod2[Reduce L]
    Mod --> Mod3[Use Bored Core]
    Mod --> Mod4[Use Side Action]

    style Load fill:#FFA07A
    style Defl fill:#F0E68C
    style Check fill:#DDA0DD
    style Pass fill:#90EE90
    style Mod fill:#FFB6C1
```

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Blind Hole Feasibility

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Blind Hole Required] --> Q1{Axis parallel to mold opening?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{L/D ≤ 4?}
    Q1 -- No --> SA[Use Side-Action Core]

    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{t_b ≥ D/3?}
    Q2 -- No --> StepCore[Use Stepped or Bored Core]

    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Delta_max ≤ 0.05 mm?}
    Q3 -- No --> Wall[Increase Parent Wall Thickness]

    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Draft ≥ 0.5°?}
    Q4 -- No --> IncDia[Increase Core Diameter]

    Q5 -- Yes --> OK[Design Approved]
    Q5 -- No --> IncDraft[Increase Draft Angle]

    style Start fill:#FFE4B5
    style OK fill:#90EE90
    style SA fill:#FFD700
    style StepCore fill:#87CEEB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip (KTU):**
> When asked *"Design a mold for a blind hole"*, the flowchart above provides a structured engineering decision path that examiners look for in 14-mark answers. Always justify each branch with a formula.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Exam]

**Define a blind hole as applied to plastic part design. Mention any two design considerations.**

**Model Answer:**

A blind hole is a non-through cylindrical cavity in a molded plastic part, having only one open end with a closed bottom formed integrally with the parent material. Two key design considerations are:

1. **Depth-to-Diameter Ratio (L/D ≤ 4):** Limits core deflection and ensures molding feasibility.
2. **Bottom Wall Thickness (t_b ≥ D/3):** Prevents sink marks on the cosmetic face and ensures structural integrity of the closed end.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always quote numerical values with units in 3-mark answers. Generic statements alone earn only partial credit.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Why are blind holes more difficult to mold than through holes?**

**Model Answer:**

Blind holes are more difficult to mold than through holes because:

1. The **core is cantilevered** (supported at only one end), leading to deflection under injection pressure.
2. **Cooling is inefficient** because coolant cannot reach the closed tip of the core.
3. **Venting is critical** — air trapped at the bottom cannot escape easily, causing short shots or burn marks.
4. **Demolding friction** is higher because the entire length grips the core during ejection.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the design of a **blind hole** in an injection molded plastic part. List the **six design rules** that govern its feasibility. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blind hole of diameter **10 mm** and depth **30 mm** is to be molded in a polypropylene component (shrinkage = 1.5%). The core is made of P-20 steel ($E = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa). Injection pressure = **60 MPa**. Check the feasibility of the design and suggest modifications if needed. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

**Sketch Description (text representation):**

A vertical cavity in a plastic block. The top face shows a circular opening (diameter D). The cavity extends downward by length L. The bottom of the cavity is closed by a wall of thickness $t_b$. The parent wall above the cavity has thickness $T$. A core pin extending from the cavity half of the mold forms the hole.

**Six Design Rules:**

1. **L/D ≤ 4:** $L \leq 4D$ to avoid core deflection.
2. **$D_{\min} = 1.0$ mm:** Minimum practical diameter for core strength.
3. **$t_b \geq D/3$:** Bottom wall thickness to prevent sink marks.
4. **Draft $\theta = 0.5° – 1.5°$:** For ejection without damage.
5. **Core deflection $\delta_{\max} \leq 0.05$ mm:** For dimensional accuracy.
6. **$D_{\text{core}} = D_h(1+S)$:** Shrinkage compensation.

#### Model Answer to (b):

**Step 1 — L/D Check:**

$$\frac{L}{D} = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0 \quad \checkmark \quad (\leq 4)$$

**Step 2 — Bottom wall thickness:**

$$t_b \geq \frac{D}{3} = \frac{10}{3} = 3.33 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Core tip force:**

$$A = \frac{\pi (10)^2}{4} = 78.54 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$F = 60 \times 78.54 = 4712.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{\pi (10)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 10000}{64} = 490.87 \text{ mm}^4$$

**Step 5 — Deflection:**

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{4712.4 \times (30)^3}{3 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 490.87}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{4712.4 \times 27000}{3.092 \times 10^8}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{1.272 \times 10^8}{3.092 \times 10^8} = 0.412 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Verdict:**

$$0.412 \text{ mm} \gg 0.05 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{INFEASIBLE}$$

**Step 7 — Recommended Modifications:**

- Increase core diameter to 14 mm → recompute and recheck.
- Reduce hole depth to 20 mm.
- Use a **bored ejector pin core** for internal cooling.
- Add a vent at the hole bottom (vent depth = 0.02 mm).

---

### Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Compare **blind holes and through holes** in injection molding. Discuss **mold construction, cooling, and dimensional accuracy** aspects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A nylon 6,6 component requires a blind hole of diameter **6 mm** and depth **15 mm**. The injection pressure is **45 MPa**, and the parent wall thickness is **8 mm**. Calculate: (i) the required bottom wall thickness, (ii) the core tip deflection, and (iii) the draft angle recommendation. Take $E_{\text{core}} = 2.1 \times 10^5$ MPa. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a):

| Parameter | Blind Hole | Through Hole |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Mold Construction** | Single-ended core (cantilever) | Two-ended core pin between halves |
| **Cooling** | Difficult (only one end exposed) | Easy (coolant passes through) |
| **Cost** | Higher (side-actions if off-axis) | Lower |
| **L/D Limit** | ≤ 4:1 | ≤ 10:1 |
| **Dimensional Accuracy** | Lower (deflection) | Higher |
| **Venting** | Critical (closed end) | Easier (open end) |

#### Model Answer to (b):

**(i) Bottom wall thickness:**

$$t_b \geq \frac{D}{3} = \frac{6}{3} = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

Since parent wall = 8 mm and hole depth = 15 mm, parent wall must be ≥ $15 + 2 = 17$ mm. **Alternatively, reduce hole depth** so that $t_b = 2$ mm is maintained.

**(ii) Core tip deflection:**

$$F = 45 \times \frac{\pi (6)^2}{4} = 45 \times 28.27 = 1272.4 \text{ N}$$

$$I = \frac{\pi (6)^4}{64} = \frac{\pi \times 1296}{64} = 63.62 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{1272.4 \times (15)^3}{3 \times 2.1 \times 10^5 \times 63.62}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{1272.4 \times 3375}{4.008 \times 10^7}$$

$$\delta_{\max} = \frac{4.294 \times 10^6}{4.008 \times 10^7} = 0.107 \text{ mm}$$

$$0.107 \text{ mm} > 0.05 \text{ mm} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Needs modification}$$

**(iii) Draft angle recommendation:**

$$\text{L/D} = \frac{15}{6} = 2.5$$

For L/D between 2 and 3, recommended draft is **1.0° to 1.5° per side**. Use $\theta = 1.0°$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never confuse L/D ratio of through holes (≤10) with blind holes (≤4)**. This is the most common 3-mark loss.
> 2. **Always state units** (mm, MPa, °) — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 3. **Bottom wall thickness is NOT optional** — students frequently forget to calculate $t_b$ in 14-mark numerical answers, losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Draft angle is per side**, not total included angle.
> 5. **Core deflection formula uses cantilever beam theory** — using the wrong formula (e.g., simply supported) yields wrong marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Recall Checklist

- **Definition**: Blind hole = non-through hole with one open end and a closed bottom wall.
- **L/D Ratio**: ≤ 4 (general), ≤ 3 (filled polymers), ≤ 5 (engineering plastics with venting).
- **Min Diameter**: 1.0 mm (general), 1.5 mm (glass-filled).
- **Bottom Wall**: $t_b \geq D/3$, minimum 0.5 mm.
- **Draft Angle**: 0.5° – 1.0° (shallow), 1.0° – 1.5° (deep).
- **Core Deflection Formula**: $\delta_{\max} = FL^3 / (3EI)$, must be ≤ 0.05 mm.
- **Shrinkage Compensation**: $D_{\text{core}} = D_h(1+S)$.
- **Off-axis holes** → require side-action or collapsible cores.
- **Venting** is mandatory at the closed end of every blind hole.
- **Cooling** is the bottleneck of blind hole molding — bored ejector pins or heat-pipe cores are used for deep blind holes.

### Common Defects in Blind Hole Molding

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|--------|-------|--------|
| Short shot | Air trap at closed end | Add vent |
| Sink mark | Thin bottom wall | Increase $t_b$ |
| Hole misalignment | Core deflection | Increase core diameter |
| Burn marks | Poor venting | Polished vent groove |
| Difficulty in ejection | Insufficient draft | Increase $\theta$ |

### Key Mnemonic for KTU Viva

**"LDBD VCS"** → **L**/D ratio, **D**iameter (min), **B**ottom wall, **D**raft angle, **V**enting, **C**ooling, **S**hrinkage.

### KTU 2024 Scheme Mark Distribution (Typical)

| Question Type | Marks | Coverage |
|---------------|-------|----------|
| Definition / Sketch | 3 | Part A |
| Comparison | 3 | Part A |
| Numerical Check | 7 – 14 | Part B |
| Design Modification | 7 | Part B |

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Revision Tip:** Blind holes are a **guaranteed question** in PCPOT601 Module 2. Practice the **core deflection numerical** at least 5 times before the KTU University Examination — it appears every semester in either Part-A or Part-B form.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 2
