<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔖 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **polymer product design and injection moulding**, an **Undercut** is defined as any feature, projection, recess, or geometric interruption on the internal or external surface of a moulded plastic part that **prevents the part from being ejected in a single, straight-line mould opening motion** along the primary parting line axis.

Undercuts are features that physically interlock with the corresponding mould cavity or core, thereby obstructing the linear demoulding path. They are typically classified as either:

- **External Undercut** – A projection or protrusion on the outer surface of the part (e.g., a side boss, flange lip, or threaded boss).
- **Internal Undercut** – A recess or inward projection on the inner surface of the part (e.g., internal threads, internal snap-fit lug, or an interior lip).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Undercuts are considered one of the most critical mould-design decisions in **Part Design for Manufacturing (DFM)**. They directly influence **mould cost**, **cycle time**, **part ejection mechanism**, and **tool complexity**. Board questions frequently test students on *classification*, *handling methods*, and *design trade-offs*.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **plastic drinking mug with a handle** (like a classic coffee cup).

- The **cylindrical body** of the mug is a *simple* shape — you can pull the mould apart vertically and the cup comes out cleanly.
- However, the **handle** is shaped like a "D" or a "C" that loops outward. If you try to pull the mould straight up, the **handle physically locks** onto the steel core that formed it.

That "locked" feeling — where a feature prevents straight-line release — is exactly what an **undercut** is in moulding terminology. The mould designer must now decide:
- Use a **side-action cam** (a moving piece that swings out of the way),
- Use a **collapsible core** (a core that folds inward before ejection),
- Or **hand-load inserts** (an operator removes the part manually before opening the mould).

This same principle applies to bottle caps, snap-fit closures, threaded containers, electrical connector housings, and toys with interlocking parts.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions

| Parameter | Typical Value / Range | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Acceptable **external undercut depth** (without side action) | ≤ **0.5 mm to 1.0 mm** | Often stripped with a stripper plate |
| Acceptable **internal undercut depth** (without collapsible core) | ≤ **0.5 mm** | Limited by part stiffness |
| Standard **draft angle** for unreinforced polymers | **0.5° – 1.0°** | Per side, KTU typical value |
| Standard **draft angle** for reinforced polymers | **1.0° – 2.0°** | Higher friction against steel |
| Maximum economical undercut (with side action) | **5–10% of part depth** | Beyond this, mould cost escalates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** "If a feature cannot be released by simple straight-line mould opening, it is an undercut — period." The mould cost can increase by **30%–200%** for each undercut that requires a side-action or split-cavity.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔖 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Why Undercuts Exist & How They Are Treated

### A. Causes / Sources of Undercuts in a Plastic Part

1. **Functional Design Requirements**
   - Snap-fit clips (assembly without fasteners)
   - Threaded features (screw caps, fasteners)
   - Hinges (living hinges, piano hinges)
   - Luer-lock connectors (medical devices)
   - Bottle handles and flanges

2. **Aesthetic / Branding Features**
   - Embossed logos
   - Recessed trademark areas
   - Surface texture changes

3. **Assembly Features**
   - Bayonet locks
   - Press-fit bosses
   - Hook-and-loop style interlocks

4. **Manufacturing Limitations**
   - Parting line mismatch
   - Wall-thickness variation causing sink marks (not strictly undercut but related)

---

### B. Methods to Handle Undercuts (in Order of Increasing Cost)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know the following hierarchy of undercut-handling techniques:

#### **Method 1 — Redesign (Eliminate the Undercut)**
- Modify the part geometry so it can be moulded with straight-line ejection.
- *Example:* Replace a thread with a snap-fit boss; remove a small lip by adding a chamfer.
- **Cost impact:** **Lowest**. Always the first choice in DFM.

#### **Method 2 — Hand-Loaded Inserts / Loose Cores**
- A separate steel piece is placed in the mould by the operator for each shot.
- Used for very deep or complex internal undercuts (e.g., internal threads).
- **Disadvantage:** Slows cycle time, increases labour cost.

#### **Method 3 — Stripper-Plate Ejection (for shallow external undercuts)**
- A **stripper plate** pushes the part off the core after mould opening.
- Suitable for **shallow, flexible** undercuts (depth ≤ 1 mm).
- The part deforms slightly during stripping, then springs back.
- *Example:* A flexible polymer latch on a small consumer enclosure.

#### **Method 4 — Side-Action Cores (Angular Slides)**
- A **side core** moves perpendicular to the main mould-opening direction, retracting clear of the undercut before the part is ejected.
- Actuated by **angle pins (cam pins)**, hydraulic cylinders, or pneumatic cylinders.
- Most common method for **moderate external undercuts**.

#### **Method 5 — Collapsible Cores (for internal undercuts)**
- The core is built in segments that **collapse inward** (radially) before ejection.
- Used for **internal threads** on bottle caps, internal undercuts on tube fittings.
- Complex and expensive; reserved for high-volume production.

#### **Method 6 — Split Moulds / Multi-Part Cavities**
- The mould itself splits into two or more pieces along a secondary parting line.
- Used for very complex parts (e.g., pipe elbows, threaded bottle preforms).
- Most expensive, but necessary for **deep internal undercuts**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Use / Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Draft angle (general) | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h}{d}\right)$ | **degrees** | Minimum draft for clean release |
| 2 | Stripper stripping distance | $S_s = d_u + (2 \text{ to } 5)\,\text{mm}$ | **mm** | Travel of stripper plate past undercut depth |
| 3 | Stripper force (perimeter strip) | $F_s = p \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_y$ | **N** | $p$ = perimeter of undercut, $\mu \approx 0.25$–$0.45$ (steel–polymer), $\sigma_y$ = compressive yield stress at ejection temperature |
| 4 | Side-action core retraction distance | $L_r = d_u + s$ | **mm** | Must clear undercut depth $d_u$ plus safety $s \geq 2\,\text{mm}$ |
| 5 | Side-action actuation force (angle pin) | $F_c = F_s \cdot \tan(\alpha - \phi)$ | **N** | $\alpha$ = angle-pin angle (typically 15°–25°), $\phi$ = friction angle (≈ 8°–12°) |
| 6 | Maximum allowable undercut depth (flexible strip) | $d_u \leq \dfrac{E \cdot \delta^2}{3 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot L}$ | **mm** | When part flexes to release; depends on modulus $E$, deflection $\delta$, length $L$ |
| 7 | Undercut cost premium factor | $C_{total} = C_{base} \cdot (1 + n \cdot k)$ | **₹ / $** | $n$ = number of undercuts, $k \approx 0.3$–$2.0$ per undercut |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (Friction Coefficient μ):** For *most polymer–steel pairs* at ejection temperature, use **μ ≈ 0.20 to 0.45**. Reinforced and glass-filled polymers sit on the higher end.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Dashboard components, air-vent louvers, and snap-fit bezels all contain undercuts; they are handled by **side-action slides** operating at 15°–25° cam angles.
- **Medical Devices:** Luer-lock syringes, IV connectors, and threaded vials rely on **collapsible cores** for internal threads.
- **Packaging:** PET bottle preforms are moulded in **split moulds** because of the deep internal thread undercut at the finish.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Phone chargers, USB housings, and snap-fit enclosures use **flexible polymer deflection** to release small undercuts without any mould complexity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔖 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Side-Action Cam Actuation Force

When an undercut must be released by a **side-action core**, the side core is retracted by an **angle pin (cam pin)** as the mould opens. We need to find the force the angle pin must overcome.

### Given
- $F_s$ = total stripping / frictional holding force on the side core (**N**)
- $\alpha$ = angle-pin angle with the parting line (**degrees**)
- $\phi$ = friction angle between pin and slide (**degrees**), where $\mu = \tan\phi$
- $F_c$ = horizontal cam force component that must be overcome (**N**)

### Step 1 — Geometry of the Angle-Pin Mechanism

As the mould opens by a vertical distance $y$, the pin lifts the side core horizontally by:

$$x = y \cdot \tan \alpha$$

Differentiating once with respect to time gives the velocity ratio:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} = \frac{dy}{dt} \cdot \tan \alpha$$

### Step 2 — Force Decomposition along the Pin

The stripping force $F_s$ acts **horizontally** (resisting side-core motion). The angle pin pushes the slide along a direction inclined at $\alpha$ to the parting line. We resolve the **resisting force** along the pin axis.

The component of $F_s$ **along the pin direction** (which the pin must overcome directly) is:

$$F_{\text{axial}} = F_s \cdot \cos \alpha$$

### Step 3 — Inclusion of Pin-to-Slide Friction

As the slide moves along the pin, sliding friction $\mu$ acts *opposing* the motion. The normal force between pin and slide is $F_s \cdot \sin\alpha$, so friction is:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot F_s \cdot \sin \alpha = F_s \cdot \sin \alpha \cdot \tan \phi$$

### Step 4 — Total Force the Mould-Opening Stroke Must Exert

Summing axial and friction components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_c &= F_s \cdot \cos \alpha \;+\; F_s \cdot \sin \alpha \cdot \tan \phi \\[4pt]
    &= F_s \cdot \bigl(\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \cdot \tan \phi \bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos \alpha + \sin \alpha \cdot \tan \phi = \dfrac{\cos(\alpha - \phi)}{\cos \phi}$, we obtain the **canonical KTU form**:

$$
\boxed{\,F_c \;=\; F_s \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\alpha - \phi)}{\cos \phi}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The horizontal cam force $F_c$ is what the **mould-opening hydraulic cylinder** must supply. If $\alpha$ is too small, $F_c$ becomes enormous; if $\alpha$ is too large, the pin lifts the slide too quickly and may jam. The **optimum angle pin range is 15°–25°**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Stripper Force on an External Undercut

A **rectangular box** is moulded in ABS. It has an external undercut of depth $d_u = 0.8\,\text{mm}$ running around its perimeter.

**Given data:**

- Perimeter of undercut $p = 240\,\text{mm} = 0.240\,\text{m}$
- Coefficient of friction (ABS on steel at 80 °C) $\mu = 0.30$
- Compressive yield stress of ABS at ejection $\sigma_y = 35\,\text{MPa} = 35 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}^2$

**Find:** Stripper force $F_s$.

### Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= p \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_y \\[4pt]
    &= 0.240 \;\text{m} \;\times\; 0.30 \;\times\; 35 \times 10^6\;\text{N/m}^2 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.240 \times 0.30 \times 35 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
    &= 2.52 \times 10^6 \;\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait — for a **line contact** (the undercut is a thin edge), the contact area is $p \cdot w$, where $w$ is the contact width (typically 1–3 mm for a sharp edge). Let $w = 1\,\text{mm} = 0.001\,\text{m}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= p \cdot w \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_y \\[4pt]
    &= 0.240 \;\times\; 0.001 \;\times\; 0.30 \;\times\; 35 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.240 \times 0.001 \times 0.30 \times 35 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
    &= 2{,}520\;\text{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $F_s \approx 2.52\;\text{kN}$ — this is the stripper-plate force required.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Takeaway:** If the same undercut were deeper (say 2 mm) and the material stiffer (glass-filled nylon, $\sigma_y \approx 80\,\text{MPa}$), the force could exceed **15 kN**, which often *exceeds* what a stripper plate can deliver — **forcing the designer to switch to a side-action slide**.

---

## 3.3 Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Undercut Solution

| Undercut Type | Depth | Material Stiffness | Recommended Method | Cost Impact |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Shallow external | ≤ 0.5 mm | Flexible (PP, PE) | Stripper plate | **Low** |
| Shallow external | ≤ 1.0 mm | Rigid (ABS, PC) | Stripper plate or hand-load | **Low–Medium** |
| Moderate external | 1–5 mm | Any | Side-action core (angle pin) | **Medium** |
| Deep external | > 5 mm | Any | Split mould / hydraulic side action | **High** |
| Internal, shallow | ≤ 0.5 mm | Flexible | Slight deflection during ejection | **Low** |
| Internal, deep | > 1 mm | Any | Collapsible core / unscrewing mechanism | **Very High** |
| Internal threads | Any | Any | Unscrewing device (mechanical / hydraulic / motorised) | **High** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔖 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Undercut Classification & Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Plastic Part Geometry] --> B{Does it release in straight-line mould opening?}
    B -- YES --> C[Simple Two-Plate Mould]
    B -- NO --> D[Undercut Detected]
    D --> E{Internal or External?}
    E -- EXTERNAL --> F[External Undercut]
    E -- INTERNAL --> G[Internal Undercut]
    F --> H{Depth d_u?}
    H -- d_u less than 0.5 mm --> I1[Stripper Plate Ejection]
    H -- d_u 0.5 to 5 mm --> I2[Side-Action Core Angle Pin]
    H -- d_u greater than 5 mm --> I3[Split Mould or Hydraulic Slide]
    G --> J{Material Flexibility?}
    J -- Flexible Polymer --> K1[Hand-Loaded Insert or Deflection Release]
    J -- Rigid Polymer --> K2[Collapsible Core or Unscrewing Mechanism]
    K2 --> K3{Threaded Feature?}
    K3 -- YES --> K4[Unscrewing Device Motor / Hydraulic]
    K3 -- NO --> K5[Collapsible Core Segments]
    I1 --> Z1[Mould Cost LOW]
    I2 --> Z2[Mould Cost MEDIUM]
    I3 --> Z3[Mould Cost HIGH]
    K1 --> Z2
    K4 --> Z3
    K5 --> Z3
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Side-Action Cam Mechanism (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MOULD[MOULD BASE PLATES]
        A1[Fixed Half A Plate] --- A2[Cavity Insert]
        B1[Moving Half B Plate] --- B2[Core Insert]
    end
    B2 --- C1[Main Mould Opening Force F_o]
    B2 --- C2[Side Action Slide Block]
    C2 --- C3[Undercut Forming Tip]
    C2 --- C4[Angle Pin Cam]
    C4 -->|Angle alpha 15 to 25 deg| C2
    C2 --- C5[Retainer Pin]
    C2 --- C6[Side Core Lock Bolt]
    A1 --- D1[Parting Line]
    B1 --- D1
    C3 -. forms undercut feature .-> C7[Moulded Plastic Part]
    C7 --- D2[Stripper Plate Ejector]
    B1 --- D2
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cost vs. Undercut Complexity Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Zero Undercut] -->|Baseline Cost| Q1[Single Two Plate Mould Rs 1.0 Lakh]
    P2[One External Undercut] --> Q2[Side Action Added Rs 1.4 Lakh]
    P3[Two External Undercuts] --> Q3[Two Side Actions Rs 1.9 Lakh]
    P4[Internal Thread] --> Q4[Unscrewing Device Rs 2.8 Lakh]
    P5[Collapsible Core] --> Q5[Collapsible Mechanism Rs 4.0 Lakh Plus]
    P6[Multiple Internal Deep Undercuts] --> Q6[Split Mould Complex Rs 6.0 Lakh Plus]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each box represents a *mould component* or *design decision*. The arrows show the *flow of mould-opening motion* and the *direction of side-core retraction*. The double-line arrows on the right represent *cost escalation* with increasing undercut complexity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔖 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an undercut in plastic part design. Differentiate between internal and external undercuts with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **undercut** is any feature on a moulded plastic part — internal or external — that prevents the part from being ejected in a single, straight-line mould opening motion along the parting line direction.

| Type | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **External Undercut** | A projection or protrusion on the *outside* surface of the part that locks onto the cavity steel. | A side handle on a plastic mug; a thread on the outside of a bottle neck. |
| **Internal Undercut** | A recess or inward projection on the *inside* surface that locks onto the core steel. | Internal thread of a screw cap; an internal snap-fit lug. |

*(1 mark: definition; 1 mark: external explanation + example; 1 mark: internal explanation + example.)*

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four methods commonly employed to mould parts with undercuts.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks; 1 mark for correct classification / explanation):**

1. **Redesign of the part** to eliminate the undercut.
2. **Hand-loaded loose inserts** placed in the mould by the operator.
3. **Stripper plate** ejection for shallow flexible external undercuts.
4. **Side-action cores** (angle-pin actuated slides) for moderate external undercuts.
5. **Collapsible cores** for deep internal undercuts.
6. **Split moulds** for very complex geometries.
7. **Unscrewing mechanisms** (mechanical, hydraulic, or motorised) for internal/external threads.

*(Any four correctly listed — full 3 marks.)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: (a) carries **7 marks** and (b) carries **7 marks**.

---

### **🔷 Question B — Option A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the different **methods used to handle external undercuts** in injection mould design. Compare stripper-plate ejection and side-action cores in terms of undercut depth, cost, and part quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rectangular ABS enclosure has an **external undercut of depth 1.2 mm** running along a perimeter of **320 mm**. The contact width of the undercut is **1.5 mm**. The coefficient of friction between ABS and mould steel at ejection temperature is **0.35**, and the compressive yield stress of ABS at 80 °C is **32 MPa**.
Calculate:
- (i) The **stripper force** required if a stripper plate is used. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Comment on whether a **stripper plate is feasible** or whether a **side-action slide** is recommended. Justify. **(4 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Methods to Handle External Undercuts** *(7 marks)*

The following methods are used, in increasing order of cost and complexity:

1. **Redesign (1 mark)** — Modify the part to eliminate the undercut. The cheapest and most reliable DFM approach. Example: change a side handle to a recessed grip.

2. **Hand-Loaded Inserts (1 mark)** — Operator manually places a steel insert in the mould to form the undercut. Slow but flexible. Used for prototypes and very low volumes.

3. **Stripper Plate Ejection (2 marks)** — A flat plate pushes the part off the core after mould opening. Suitable when:
   - Undercut depth ≤ 0.5–1.0 mm
   - Polymer is flexible enough to deform slightly (e.g., PP, LDPE, flexible ABS blends)
   - Volume is high and labour cost must be minimised
   - Part must be free of witness lines from slides.

4. **Side-Action Cores (2 marks)** — A slide block moves perpendicular to mould opening, actuated by an angle pin (cam), hydraulic cylinder, or pneumatic cylinder. The slide retracts clear of the undercut before the part is ejected. Used when:
   - Undercut depth > 1 mm
   - Polymer is rigid (PC, PMMA, glass-filled nylon)
   - High dimensional accuracy is required
   - Higher mould cost is justified by volume.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Parameter | Stripper Plate | Side-Action Core |
|---|---|---|
| Max. undercut depth | ≤ 1 mm | 5–10 mm (or more) |
| Cost | Low | Medium–High |
| Cycle time | Faster | Slower (slide motion) |
| Surface quality | Excellent (no slide marks) | May show witness line |
| Suitable polymers | Flexible | Rigid or flexible |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution** *(7 marks)*

**Given:**
- $d_u = 1.2\,\text{mm}$ (only for feasibility check)
- $p = 320\,\text{mm} = 0.320\,\text{m}$
- $w = 1.5\,\text{mm} = 0.0015\,\text{m}$
- $\mu = 0.35$
- $\sigma_y = 32\,\text{MPa} = 32 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m}^2$

**(i) Stripper Force** *(3 marks)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_s &= p \cdot w \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_y \\[4pt]
    &= 0.320 \;\text{m} \;\times\; 0.0015\;\text{m} \;\times\; 0.35 \;\times\; 32 \times 10^6\;\text{N/m}^2 \\[4pt]
    &= 0.320 \times 0.0015 \times 0.35 \times 32 \times 10^6 \\[4pt]
    &= 5{,}376\;\text{N} \;\approx\; 5.38\;\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**$F_s \approx 5.38\;\text{kN}$** *(Full 3 marks for correct numerical evaluation with units.)*

**(ii) Feasibility Comment** *(4 marks)*

A stripper-plate force of **5.38 kN** is technically achievable in a standard mould, but several concerns arise:

- The undercut depth is **1.2 mm**, which is at the **upper limit** of stripper-plate capability for a *rigid* polymer like ABS.
- Stripper plates typically require the part to flex momentarily; ABS does not flex as much as PP or PE, raising the risk of **part damage, white stress marks, or cracking** at the undercut edge.
- The contact width is only 1.5 mm, concentrating the stress and increasing the probability of **ejector-pin push-through** failure.

**Conclusion (4 marks distributed as 2 for reasoning + 2 for recommendation):**
- A **stripper plate is borderline feasible** for very high-volume production where mould cost is critical.
- For a robust, defect-free solution, a **side-action slide (angle pin cam)** is **strongly recommended** because:
  - It can handle the 1.2 mm undercut reliably.
  - It avoids the high localised stress on the ABS undercut.
  - Cycle time penalty is acceptable for medium-to-high volumes.

---

### **🔷 Question B — Option B (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What are **collapsible cores**? Explain with a neat sketch how they are used to mould parts with **internal undercuts**. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a force diagram, derive the expression for the **side-action cam force** required to release an undercut, and explain how the **angle-pin angle α** affects this force. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution Outline**

**(a) Collapsible Cores** *(7 marks)*

- **Definition (2 marks):** A collapsible core is a mould component made of multiple segments (typically 3, 4, 6, or 8 segments) that **retract radially inward** when actuated, allowing an internal undercut to be released before ejection.
- **Operating Principle (2 marks):** A central pull rod, hydraulic actuator, or cam moves the segments together once the mould opens; the segments collapse inward, freeing the part's internal undercut. The part is then ejected normally.
- **Application (1 mark):** Internal threads on bottle caps, internal undercuts on tube elbows, internal snap-fit features.
- **Advantages (1 mark):** Solves otherwise impossible internal-undercut geometries; preserves part concentricity.
- **Limitations (1 mark):** Expensive, complex, sensitive to wear, limited to relatively round cross-sections, longer cycle time.

**(b) Side-Action Cam Force Derivation** *(7 marks)*

- Diagram and force resolution (2 marks).
- Component of stripping force along the pin axis: $F_s \cos\alpha$ (2 marks).
- Friction component: $\mu F_s \sin\alpha = F_s \sin\alpha \tan\phi$ (1 mark).
- Final expression:
$$
F_c = F_s \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\alpha - \phi)}{\cos \phi} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
$$
- Effect of α: A small α gives large $F_c$ (hard to actuate); a large α lifts the slide too fast and risks jamming. **Optimum α = 15°–25°** for most KTU problems (1 mark).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on Undercut Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing internal and external undercuts** — A *thread on the outside* of a bottle neck is an **external** undercut; the same thread on the *inside* of a cap is **internal**. Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for misclassification.
> 2. **Forgetting the friction term** in the cam force derivation. The correct answer always has the $\tan\phi$ term. Writing only $F_c = F_s \cos\alpha$ loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Using the wrong units** in the stripper force calculation: $\sigma_y$ must be in **Pa (N/m²)** and all lengths in **metres**, not mm.
> 4. **Not justifying the recommendation** in numerical problems. A number without a "yes/no" recommendation with reasoning typically gets **partial credit only**.
> 5. **Missing the "redesign" option** in the methods list. *Always list redesign first* — examiners specifically look for the DFM philosophy.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ An **undercut** = any feature preventing **straight-line mould release**.
- ✅ Two broad types: **Internal** (locks on the core) and **External** (locks on the cavity).
- ✅ Always consider **redesign first** — it is the cheapest, fastest, and most reliable solution.
- ✅ **Stripper plate** suits **shallow (≤ 1 mm), flexible-polymer** external undercuts.
- ✅ **Side-action slides** handle **moderate (1–5 mm) external undercuts** in rigid polymers; use cam angle α = 15°–25°.
- ✅ **Collapsible cores** are the go-to for **internal undercuts** in round parts (e.g., internal threads).
- ✅ **Split moulds** are reserved for **deep, complex, multi-direction** undercuts — most expensive.
- ✅ **Unscrewing devices** (mechanical / hydraulic / motorised) are mandatory for **moulded-in threads**.
- ✅ Stripper force formula: $F_s = p \cdot w \cdot \mu \cdot \sigma_y$ — remember **all SI units**.
- ✅ Cam force formula: $F_c = F_s \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\alpha - \phi)}{\cos \phi}$ — always include the **friction term** $\tan\phi$.
- ✅ Cost premium per undercut: typically **30%–200%** of base mould cost; reinforce the **DFM argument** in your answer.
- ✅ For a 14-mark question, **always include a sketch, a table, and a justified recommendation** — examiners reward structured, visual answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hinges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hinges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **hinge** in polymer product design is a localized, flexible region of a molded plastic part that allows controlled rotation or angular movement between two rigid sections of the same component. In the context of KTU's PCPOT601 syllabus, the term specifically refers to **integral (living) hinges** — flexible sections molded as a continuous, monolithic part with the parent product, eliminating the need for mechanical fasteners, pins, or assembled metal hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A living hinge is a thin, flexible web of polymer material — typically between **0.25 mm and 0.5 mm** thick — that acts as a rotational axis, molded in a single shot with the two rigid halves of the product. It functions through controlled **molecular orientation** and **strain-induced crystallization** in the hinge region.

The two rigid flanking sections are termed the **hinge leaves** (or **flaps**), while the thin flexible section is the **knuckle** or **web**. When the hinge is flexed for the first time (the "first-cycle" or "activation" flex), the polymer chains in the highly oriented knuckle region align perpendicular to the hinge axis, dramatically increasing the flex life.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **book cover**. The thick cardboard pages are rigid, but the central spine is a thin strip of material that lets you open and close the book thousands of times without breaking. A polymer living hinge is exactly that — but instead of glued paper, it is a single piece of injection-molded plastic where the **designer deliberately made one tiny strip very thin** so it bends preferentially.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world examples you can relate to:**
> - The **flip-top cap** of a shampoo bottle (polypropylene hinge)
> - The lid of a **clamshell food container**
> - The cap of a **marker pen / felt-tip pen**
> - The **eyeglass case** clasp
> - The **toilet seat** (often polypropylene with a living hinge)
> - The **USB cap** on a flash drive

The genius of a living hinge is that there are **no separate parts to assemble, no screws, no springs** — just one molded piece. This dramatically reduces manufacturing cost, eliminates assembly errors, and improves reliability.

## 1.3 Why Polymers Are Uniquely Suited

Metals fatigue and crack at a bend point; polymers can undergo **millions of flex cycles** if designed correctly. The mechanism is:

1. During injection molding, polymer molecules in the thin hinge region undergo **high shear flow**.
2. This causes **molecular orientation** parallel to the flow direction.
3. On first flex, the thin section yields plastically, and chains reorient **perpendicular to the hinge axis**.
4. This new orientation, combined with strain-induced crystallization (especially in **polypropylene**), gives the hinge extraordinary fatigue resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for exams:** Polypropylene (PP) is the *gold standard* material for living hinges because its **tacticity** (semi-crystalline structure) allows the strain-induced crystallization that gives exceptional flex life. Polyethylene and POM are also used.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used

| Metric | Typical Value | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hinge thickness (t) | **0.25 – 0.5** | mm | Thin web dimensions |
| Hinge width (b) | Equal to rigid wall thickness, usually 1 – 3 mm | mm | Span perpendicular to bend |
| Flex life (N) | **> 10⁶ cycles** for PP | cycles | Number of bends before failure |
| Minimum bend radius | 0.5 mm | mm | Geometric constraint |
| Bending strain | 100 – 200 % | % | Strain at outer fiber |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Living Hinge

A properly designed living hinge consists of three zones, all molded simultaneously:

1. **Knuckle (web):** The thin central strip. This is the actual flexing element. Typical thickness **0.25–0.5 mm**.
2. **Transition zones (tapered sections):** Smooth tapers connecting the thin knuckle to the thicker rigid leaves. They prevent stress concentration at the rigid-to-flexible boundary.
3. **Hinge leaves (flaps):** The rigid parent parts on either side.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The taper is critical.** A sudden thickness change (step) acts as a **stress concentrator** and is the most common cause of hinge failure. The taper ratio (T) is typically **2:1 to 4:1** (length:thickness change).

## 2.2 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Flexing

When the user opens the product (e.g., flips a shampoo cap), the knuckle experiences:

- **Tensile stress** on the outer fiber
- **Compressive stress** on the inner fiber
- **Neutral axis** at the mid-thickness

The first bend aligns polymer chains perpendicular to the hinge axis, producing:
- **Strain-induced crystallization** (especially in PP)
- High **molecular orientation** in the favorable direction
- Increased modulus along the chain direction
- Improved fatigue resistance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why one-time activation matters:** Initial flexing at 90° is often called the "activation" of the hinge. Some designs include a **pre-flex** in the mold, but in most production parts the consumer does it on first use.

## 2.3 Design Parameters (Structured Logic)

### 2.3.1 Hinge Thickness (t)
- **Smaller t** → Lower bending stress, higher flex life
- **Larger t** → More robust, but stiffer
- KTU exam formula: typically solve for **t** given applied load and desired stress

### 2.3.2 Hinge Width (b)
- Should match the wall thickness of the rigid part on both sides
- Wider hinges distribute stress and increase flex life linearly

### 2.3.3 Taper Design
- Taper length (L_t) ≥ **2 × (t_rigid − t_hinge)**
- Smooth, continuous taper preferred over stepped geometry

### 2.3.4 Surface Finish
- Hinge region should be **matte / non-polished** for higher flex life
- Highly polished mold surfaces can reduce hinge life (paradoxical: better surface ≠ better hinge)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the most important section for KTU Board Exam preparation. Memorize the highlighted formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending stress (max) in hinge | $\sigma = \dfrac{6FL}{b t^2}$ | F = applied force, L = moment arm, b = hinge width, t = hinge thickness | MPa (N/mm²) | Cantilever beam at hinge |
| 2 | Bending moment | $M = F \cdot L$ | F, L as above | N·mm | Moment at hinge root |
| 3 | Section modulus (rectangle) | $Z = \dfrac{b t^2}{6}$ | b, t as above | mm³ | Geometric property |
| 4 | Strain at outer fiber | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ | t = thickness, R = bend radius | dimensionless | Determines flex life |
| 5 | Stress from strain | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | E = flexural modulus, ε = strain | MPa | Linear elastic regime |
| 6 | Required hinge thickness | $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6FL}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$ | All as above; σ_allow = allowable stress | mm | Design (solve for t) |
| 7 | Minimum taper length | $L_t \geq 2(t_{rigid} - t_{hinge})$ | t_rigid, t_hinge | mm | Geometry check |
| 8 | Flex life (Coffin-Manson type) | $N_f = C \cdot \varepsilon^{-n}$ | N_f = cycles to fail, C, n = material constants | cycles | Fatigue estimate |
| 9 | Hinge width = wall thickness | $b \approx t_{wall}$ | t_wall = adjacent wall thickness | mm | Standard rule |
| 10 | Gap-free closure gap | $g \leq 0.05\,\text{mm}$ | — | mm | Tolerancing |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Living hinges are central to:
- **Packaging industry** (flip-top caps, blister packs, clamshells)
- **Consumer electronics** (laptop hinges in some designs, USB covers)
- **Medical devices** (single-use sterile enclosures)
- **Automotive** (cup holder covers, small compartment lids)
- **Toys** (transformer figures, action figure packaging)

The economic value: a single living hinge **eliminates 2–4 parts, 2–4 assembly steps, and reduces labor cost by 30–60 %** in the affected subassembly. This is why almost every shampoo bottle, ketchup cap, and Tic-Tac box uses one.

## 2.6 Failure Modes (the "How It Goes Wrong")

- **Stress whitening:** Microscopic void formation, cosmetic
- **Hinge tearing:** Crack propagation from poor taper or contamination
- **Permanent set:** Loss of closure (hinge doesn't return to closed position) — caused by creep at high temperature
- **Cold drawing:** Excessive thinning at the bend
- **Material selection error:** Using amorphous polymer (PS, ABS) gives very low flex life

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bending Stress at the Hinge

**Starting scenario:** A hinge is modeled as a cantilever beam of length L (moment arm) and rectangular cross-section b × t. A transverse force F is applied at the free end.

**Step 1 — Identify the maximum bending moment.**
The maximum bending moment occurs at the **root of the hinge** (fixed end), where the cantilever meets the rigid leaf. The free-body diagram gives:

$$M_{max} = F \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Write the section modulus for a rectangular cross-section.**
For a rectangle of width b and thickness t, the moment of inertia about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber.**
For a symmetric rectangle bending about its mid-plane:

$$c = \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the flexure formula (Euler-Bernoulli beam theory).**

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions for M, c, and I.**

$$\sigma = \frac{(F L) \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{b t^3}{12}}$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the algebra.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F L \cdot t \cdot 12}{2 \cdot b t^3}$$

$$\sigma = \frac{12 \, F L}{2 \, b \, t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{6 F L}{b \, t^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Units check:** F [N], L [mm], b [mm], t [mm] → σ has units of **N/mm² = MPa**. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Solving for Hinge Thickness (Design Equation)

In KTU board problems, the question typically gives F, L, b, and an allowable stress σ_allow, asking for the **minimum hinge thickness**.

**Step 1 — Start from the bending stress equation.**

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate t².**

$$t^2 = \frac{6 F L}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 F L}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (illustrative).**
Suppose F = 5 N, L = 30 mm, b = 2 mm, σ_allow = 25 MPa:

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 5 \times 30}{2 \times 25}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{900}{50}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{18}$$

$$t \approx 4.24 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity check for KTU problems:** The hinge thickness you calculate is almost always **less than 1 mm** for typical consumer products. If your answer comes out to several mm, you have likely made a units error or used a non-polymer allowable stress (metal values are much higher).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bending Strain at the Outer Fiber

**Step 1 — Define the geometry of bending.** When a beam of thickness t is bent to a radius R, the outer fiber lies on a circle of radius (R + t/2), while the inner fiber is at (R − t/2).

**Step 2 — Express the arc length of the outer fiber.**
Original length of neutral axis fiber:

$$L_0 = R \cdot \theta$$

Length of outer fiber (after bending):

$$L_{outer} = \left(R + \frac{t}{2}\right) \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — Engineering strain is the change in length divided by original length.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{L_{outer} - L_0}{L_0} = \frac{\left(R + \dfrac{t}{2}\right)\theta - R\theta}{R\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\dfrac{t}{2} \cdot \theta}{R \cdot \theta}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical use:** This formula is used to verify whether the strain stays below the **yield strain** of the polymer. For PP, ε_yield ≈ 10–15 %, so for t = 0.4 mm the minimum bend radius is R_min ≈ t/(2 × 0.10) = 2.0 mm.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Hinge Width from Wall Thickness Equivalence

The hinge web must carry the bending moment without separating from the rigid leaves. The design rule is:

$$b = t_{wall}$$

**Reasoning:** The wall thickness of the rigid leaves governs the local cross-sectional area. If the hinge width matches the wall thickness, the moment of inertia is continuous across the geometry, preventing a stress jump.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination tip:** If a problem provides a wall thickness t_wall = 1.5 mm, you can directly use **b = 1.5 mm** in all subsequent equations without further justification. KTU examiners award full credit for this standard practice.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree

| Step | Question to Ask | What to Look Up | What to Compute |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | What is the function of the hinge? | — | Identify open angle (180°, 90°, 270°) |
| 2 | What force is required? | Product specifications | F [N] |
| 3 | What is the moment arm? | Geometry | L [mm] |
| 4 | What material? | Allowable stress table | σ_allow [MPa] |
| 5 | What is the wall thickness? | Mold design | t_wall → b [mm] |
| 6 | Calculate t | Equation #6 above | t [mm] |
| 7 | Verify t is moldable | Mold-flow analysis | 0.25 mm ≤ t ≤ 0.5 mm ideally |
| 8 | Check flex life | Fatigue curve | N_f [cycles] |
| 9 | Design taper | Equation #7 | L_t [mm] |
| 10 | Add fillet radii | Stress concentration | r [mm] |

## 3.6 Illustrative Worked Example

**Problem (typical KTU style):**
A flip-top cap of a shampoo bottle is made of polypropylene. The moment arm from the hinge to the cap center of mass is 35 mm. A consumer applies a force of 3 N to open the cap. The wall thickness of the rigid parts is 1.2 mm. The allowable flex stress of PP at the hinge is 15 MPa. Calculate the minimum hinge thickness.

**Solution:**

Given:
- F = 3 N
- L = 35 mm
- b = t_wall = 1.2 mm
- σ_allow = 15 MPa = 15 N/mm²

**Step 1 — Write the design equation.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 F L}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 3 \times 35}{1.2 \times 15}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{630}{18}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{35}$$

$$t \approx 5.92 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This answer is too large!** A 5.92 mm hinge is not a living hinge — it would be a rigid joint. The KTU board exam interpretation: this design would **fail as a living hinge**; the material stress allowable is too low, OR the wall thickness assumption is wrong, OR a **living hinge is not feasible** for this geometry and a separate mechanical hinge should be used.

**Re-interpretation for a feasible living hinge:** If we target t = 0.4 mm, what allowable stress is needed?

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2} = \frac{6 \times 3 \times 35}{1.2 \times 0.4^2}$$

$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{630}{0.192}$$

$$\sigma_{allow} \approx 3281 \text{ MPa}$$

This confirms that **the stress at the hinge will inevitably exceed the polymer's elastic limit on the first flex** — and that is acceptable for a living hinge, which operates in the **plastic deformation regime** by design. Hence the design is feasible. The "allowable stress" used in the linear-elastic derivation is actually a **stress-to-yield check**; the hinge is *designed to yield plastically on the first flex* and then operate elastically on subsequent cycles.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hinge Design Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Product Function Definition] --> B[Identify Opening Force F]
    B --> C[Measure Moment Arm L]
    C --> D[Select Polymer Material]
    D --> E[Determine Allowable Stress sigma_allow]
    E --> F[Set Hinge Width b equals Wall Thickness]
    F --> G[Calculate Hinge Thickness t]
    G --> H{Is t between 0.25 and 0.5 mm?}
    H -->|Yes| I[Design Tapered Transition Zones]
    H -->|No| J[Re-evaluate Force or Material]
    I --> K[Set Minimum Bend Radius R_min]
    K --> L[Run Mold Flow Simulation]
    L --> M{Is Flex Life above 10^5 cycles?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Finalize Tooling and Release]
    M -->|No| O[Adjust Material or Geometry]
    O --> G
    J --> G
    N --> P[Production Approval]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Living Hinge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rigid Leaf 1] -->|Taper 1| B[Thin Knuckle Web]
    B -->|Taper 2| C[Rigid Leaf 2]
    B --> D[Outer Fiber in Tension]
    B --> E[Inner Fiber in Compression]
    B --> F[Neutral Axis at Mid-Thickness]
    D --> G[First Flex Orients Molecules]
    E --> G
    G --> H[Strain Induced Crystallization]
    H --> I[High Flex Life above 10^6 cycles]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Failure Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hinge Failure Observed] --> B{Type of Failure}
    B -->|Crack at root| C[Check Taper Length L_t]
    C -->|L_t too short| D[Increase Taper Length]
    C -->|L_t adequate| E[Check Material Selection]
    E -->|Amorphous polymer| F[Switch to PP or POM]
    B -->|Permanent set| G[Check Operating Temperature]
    G -->|Above T_g| H[Use Higher Heat Material]
    B -->|Stress whitening| I[Acceptable for Living Hinge]
    B -->|Tearing| J[Reduce Hinge Thickness]
    J --> K[Recalculate Flex Life]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hinge Type Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Hinges] --> B[Living Integral Hinge]
    A --> C[Film Hinge]
    A --> D[Tapered Beam Hinge]
    A --> E[Hybrid Hinge]
    B --> B1[Single Piece, Molded Together]
    B1 --> B2[Thin Web 0.25 to 0.5 mm]
    C --> C1[Very Thin, less than 0.1 mm]
    C1 --> C2[Used in Disposable Packaging]
    D --> D1[Thicker than Living]
    D1 --> D2[Repeated Heavy Duty Cycles]
    E --> E1[Living Hinge plus Spring Element]
    E1 --> E2[Used in Lids and Closures]
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description (Text-Based Drawing)

A typical living hinge cross-section looks like this (you can sketch this in the exam):

```
       ___________________              ___________________
      |                   |            |                   |
      |   RIGID LEAF 1    |            |   RIGID LEAF 2    |
      |   (thick wall)    |            |   (thick wall)    |
      |                   |            |                   |
      |___________________|            |___________________|
                       \              /
                        \            /        ← Tapered transitions
                         \__________/
                         |          |         ← Thin knuckle (web)
                         |   t=0.4  |
                         |__________|         ← Hinge axis
```

- The **thickness t** at the knuckle is typically 0.25–0.5 mm
- The **taper length** on each side is approximately 2× the thickness difference
- The **rigid leaves** are 1.0–3.0 mm thick depending on product
- The **width b** (into the page) matches the wall thickness of the rigid leaves

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam tip:** Even if you cannot draw perfect pictures in the exam, a clearly labeled **schematic cross-section** with dimensions and labels for "rigid leaf," "knuckle," and "taper" will earn full marks. KTU examiners value **clear labeling** over artistic skill.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a living hinge. List any two polymers commonly used for living hinges and explain why.**

**Model Answer:**

A **living hinge** (or integral hinge) is a thin, flexible web of polymer molded as a continuous, monolithic part of an injection-molded product, allowing controlled rotation between two rigid sections without any separate hinge hardware.

Two commonly used polymers:
1. **Polypropylene (PP):** Most widely used because of its **semi-crystalline nature** and ability to undergo **strain-induced crystallization** on first flex, which dramatically extends flex life to **over 10⁶ cycles**.
2. **Polyoxymethylene (POM / Acetal):** Used for high-precision, high-cycle hinges because of its high modulus, low friction, and excellent fatigue resistance, especially in engineering applications.

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark answer checklist:** Definition (1 mark) + two polymers with one-line reasons (2 marks). Avoid long explanations — KTU Part A expects crisp answers.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the typical thickness range for the knuckle of a living hinge? State the role of the tapered transition zones.**

**Model Answer:**

The **knuckle (web) thickness** of a typical living hinge is in the range of **0.25 mm to 0.5 mm**. Thinner hinges (≈ 0.25 mm) give longer flex life but are harder to mold reliably; thicker hinges (≈ 0.5 mm) are easier to fill but reduce flex life.

**Role of the tapered transition zones:**
- They provide a **gradual change in cross-section** from the rigid leaf thickness (1–3 mm) to the thin knuckle.
- They **reduce stress concentration** at the rigid-flexible boundary.
- They distribute strain smoothly, preventing crack initiation at the geometric step.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Writing the knuckle thickness as "0.25 to 5 mm" — examiners mark this wrong. **Memorize the 0.25–0.5 mm range.**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**A flip-top bottle cap is molded in polypropylene. The cap must withstand a consumer-applied opening force of 4 N at a moment arm of 25 mm from the hinge. The wall thickness of the rigid parts is 1.5 mm and the allowable flex stress of PP is 20 MPa.**

**(a)** Derive the formula for the maximum bending stress at the knuckle of a living hinge. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using this formula, calculate the required hinge thickness and verify whether it is feasible as a living hinge. Suggest a corrected design if needed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Assumption:** Model the hinge as a cantilever beam of rectangular cross-section b × t, length L (moment arm), with transverse force F at the free end.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending moment at the root of the cantilever.**

$$M_{max} = F \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Moment of inertia of the rectangular cross-section.**

$$I = \frac{b t^3}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber.**

$$c = \frac{t}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Euler-Bernoulli flexure formula.**

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the three expressions.**

$$\sigma = \frac{(F L) \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{2}\right)}{\dfrac{b t^3}{12}}$$

**Step 6 — Algebraic simplification.**

$$\sigma = \frac{F L \cdot t \cdot 12}{2 \cdot b t^3} = \frac{12 F L}{2 b t^2} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **7-mark marking scheme:** Free-body diagram (2 marks), moment expression (1 mark), flexure formula application (2 marks), algebraic simplification (2 marks).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** F = 4 N, L = 25 mm, b = 1.5 mm, σ_allow = 20 N/mm².

**Step 1 — Rearrange the formula to solve for t.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 F L}{b \cdot \sigma_{allow}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{6 \times 4 \times 25}{1.5 \times 20}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{\frac{600}{30}}$$

$$t = \sqrt{20}$$

$$t \approx 4.47 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Verify feasibility.**

A thickness of 4.47 mm **does not qualify as a living hinge** (which requires 0.25–0.5 mm). At this thickness, the part would behave as a **rigid joint**, and the consumer would not be able to open the cap.

**Step 4 — Corrected design approach.**

The proper engineering interpretation is that **the calculated t is the thickness at which the stress would equal σ_allow**, but a living hinge is **designed to operate in the plastic deformation regime**. Therefore, the design should:

1. **Set the knuckle thickness to the standard living-hinge value of t = 0.4 mm.**
2. **Accept that on the first flex, the outer fibers will yield plastically.**
3. **Verify that the strain at the outer fiber does not cause tearing.**

**Step 5 — Verify the resulting strain.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2 R_{min}}$$

For PP with t = 0.4 mm and R_min = 1.5 mm (typical mold radius):

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.4}{2 \times 1.5} = 0.133 = 13.3\%$$

This is within the strain-to-failure range of PP (≈ 200 %), so **the design is feasible**.

$$\boxed{t_{design} = 0.4 \text{ mm} \text{ (feasible as a living hinge)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on this question type:**
> 1. **Writing t = 4.47 mm as the final answer without commenting on feasibility** — KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this. You MUST discuss the implications.
> 2. **Forgetting to include units** in intermediate steps — marks lost for unit errors.
> 3. **Mixing up F, L, b, t variables** in the substitution step — students sometimes swap numerator and denominator.
> 4. **Not converting σ_allow to consistent units** — given σ_allow = 20 MPa, ensure you use N/mm² (= 20) and not Pa (= 20 × 10⁶).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain the design principles of living hinges in polymer products. Discuss the role of taper length, surface finish, and material selection with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene living hinge is 0.35 mm thick and 2.0 mm wide. During opening, the user applies a force of 2 N at a moment arm of 40 mm. Calculate the maximum bending stress and the strain at the outer fiber, assuming a bend radius of 1.5 mm. Also calculate the minimum required taper length if the rigid wall thickness is 1.5 mm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. The Living Hinge Concept:** A living hinge is a thin, integral flexure molded in a single piece with the parent part. It replaces a mechanical hinge and eliminates assembly cost. Common in flip-top caps, pen caps, clamshells, and spectacle cases.

**2. Design Principles:**

**(i) Minimum thickness with adequate strength.** The knuckle should be **0.25–0.5 mm**. Thinner is better for flex life, thicker is easier to mold. The exact value is solved from $\sigma = 6FL/(bt^2)$.

**(ii) Tapered transitions.** The transition from the rigid leaf (1–3 mm) to the knuckle (0.25–0.5 mm) must be **smooth and tapered**, not stepped. The taper length is:

$$L_t \geq 2(t_{rigid} - t_{hinge})$$

For example, if the rigid wall is 2 mm and hinge is 0.4 mm, then L_t ≥ 2 × 1.6 = 3.2 mm. A typical industrial design uses **L_t ≈ 1–2 mm** on each side, giving a total transition zone of 2–4 mm.

**(iii) Material selection.** Polypropylene is preferred for its **strain-induced crystallization** behavior, giving flex life of **over 10⁶ cycles**. POM is used for engineering-grade hinges. Amorphous polymers (PS, ABS, PC) are **not suitable** for living hinges because they do not strain-crystallize.

**(iv) Surface finish.** The mold surface at the hinge region should be **matte (rough)**, not polished. A polished surface produces a thin skin of highly oriented polymer that is prone to stress cracking. EDM-textured or sandblasted mold surfaces give longer flex life.

**(v) Flow direction during molding.** Polymer melt should flow **perpendicular to the hinge axis**. This causes the chains to align along the hinge length, the direction in which tensile and compressive stresses act. Incorrect flow direction severely reduces flex life.

**(vi) Bend radius.** The minimum bend radius at the knuckle should be **at least 0.5 mm** to avoid stress concentration.

> [!TIP]
> **7-mark marking scheme:** Definition (1 mark) + 4–5 design principles explained with examples (5–6 marks).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** t = 0.35 mm, b = 2.0 mm, F = 2 N, L = 40 mm, R = 1.5 mm, t_rigid = 1.5 mm.

**Step 1 — Maximum bending stress.**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 F L}{b t^2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \times 2 \times 40}{2.0 \times 0.35^2}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{480}{2.0 \times 0.1225}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{480}{0.245}$$

$$\sigma_{max} \approx 1959.2 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.96 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This calculated stress is far above the elastic limit of PP (≈ 30 MPa). This is **expected** for a living hinge on the first flex — the outer fibers yield plastically. Subsequent cycles operate at much lower stress due to molecular reorientation.

**Step 2 — Strain at the outer fiber.**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{t}{2 R}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{0.35}{2 \times 1.5} = \frac{0.35}{3.0}$$

$$\varepsilon \approx 0.1167 = 11.67\%$$

This is within the strain-to-failure of PP (≈ 100–200 %), so the design is feasible.

**Step 3 — Minimum taper length.**

$$L_t \geq 2 \cdot (t_{rigid} - t_{hinge})$$

$$L_t \geq 2 \cdot (1.5 - 0.35)$$

$$L_t \geq 2 \cdot 1.15$$

$$L_t \geq 2.30 \text{ mm}$$

**Final results:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 1.96 \text{ GPa (first-flex yield stress)}}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 11.67\% \text{ (within PP limits)}}$$

$$\boxed{L_{t,min} = 2.30 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students lose marks on Question B:**
> 1. **Reporting σ = 1.96 GPa without interpretation** — the examiner expects you to state that this is above the elastic limit and that **plastic deformation is expected and acceptable** for a living hinge.
> 2. **Confusing strain at outer fiber (ε = t/2R) with strain at neutral axis (ε = 0)** — students sometimes write ε = t/R or ε = t/(2πR); the correct formula is ε = t/(2R).
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply the taper length factor by 2** — some students give L_t = 1.15 mm, forgetting the formula has an explicit factor of 2.
> 4. **Mixing up rigid wall thickness and hinge thickness** — be careful which value goes where in the taper equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU exam revision — read this section just before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Definition:** A living hinge is a thin, flexible web (0.25–0.5 mm) molded integrally with the parent polymer part, allowing rotation between two rigid sections without separate hardware.

- [ ] **Material of choice:** **Polypropylene (PP)** is the gold standard due to strain-induced crystallization. POM is the engineering-grade alternative. Avoid amorphous polymers.

- [ ] **Knuckle thickness range:** **0.25 mm to 0.5 mm** (memorize this exact range).

- [ ] **Hinge width = adjacent wall thickness:** $b = t_{wall}$ is the standard design rule.

- [ ] **Master formula:** $\sigma = \dfrac{6 F L}{b t^2}$ — derive this in any question that asks for derivation.

- [ ] **Solve for thickness:** $t = \sqrt{\dfrac{6 F L}{b \sigma_{allow}}}$ — always verify the answer lies in the 0.25–0.5 mm range for a feasible living hinge.

- [ ] **Strain at outer fiber:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{t}{2R}$ — used to verify the design stays within the polymer's strain-to-failure.

- [ ] **Taper length rule:** $L_t \geq 2 \cdot (t_{rigid} - t_{hinge})$ — taper on **both sides** of the knuckle.

- [ ] **Mold surface at hinge:** Should be **matte / rough**, not polished, to maximize flex life.

- [ ] **Melt flow direction:** Must be **perpendicular to the hinge axis** for proper chain orientation.

- [ ] **Typical flex life:** **> 10⁶ cycles** for PP living hinges; lower for other materials.

- [ ] **First flex activates the hinge** — chains reorient and strain-crystallize, increasing flex life.

- [ ] **Operating regime:** Living hinges are designed to **yield plastically on the first flex** — do not interpret high calculated stress as design failure.

- [ ] **Examples to cite in answers:** Shampoo flip-top caps, pen caps, clamshell packaging, spectacle cases, toilet seats, USB drive covers.

- [ ] **Failure modes to know:** Stress whitening (cosmetic, acceptable), permanent set (creep, high temperature), tearing (poor design/material), and cold drawing (excessive thinning).

- [ ] **Units to use:** F [N], L [mm], b [mm], t [mm], σ [MPa = N/mm²]. Always carry units in the calculation.

- [ ] **When asked to "design a hinge":** Follow the 10-step decision tree (Section 3.5) in order. Examiners award marks for systematic methodology, not just the final number.

- [ ] **Examiner's pet topics:** Derivation of $\sigma = 6FL/(bt^2)$, calculation of $t$ from a numerical problem, taper length formula, and discussion of material choice for living hinges. Prepare these thoroughly.

- [ ] **Common confusions to avoid:**
  - "Allowable stress" for living hinges is a **first-yield check**, not a service-stress limit.
  - The **flex life equation** $N_f = C \varepsilon^{-n}$ is the Coffin-Manson form, used for fatigue estimation; you do not need to derive it, just cite and use it.
  - **Living hinge ≠ mechanical hinge.** A mechanical hinge has a pin, knuckle, and barrel; a living hinge has none of these.

- [ ] **One-line exam answer you can write at the start of any hinge question:**
  *"A living hinge is a thin, integral flexure of polymer (typically 0.25–0.5 mm thick) molded as one piece with the parent product, replacing the need for separate hinge hardware. Polypropylene is the most common material due to strain-induced crystallization, which provides flex life exceeding 10⁶ cycles."*

> [!TIP]
> **Final advice from the KTU Examiner's perspective:** When you see a hinge design problem, **always derive the formula first** (it earns easy marks), then **substitute numerical values with units**, then **comment on the feasibility of the answer**. Students who skip the feasibility discussion lose 2–3 marks per question. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education** — meaning the examiner wants to see that you understand *why* the design works, not just that you can plug numbers into a formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Functional Surfaces and Lettering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Functional Surfaces and Lettering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Functional Surfaces

A **functional surface** in engineering drawing and product design refers to any surface of a part or component that is not a datum feature but is essential to the product's operational performance. These surfaces directly participate in fulfilling the mechanical, tribological, sealing, locating, or load-bearing functions of the assembly. According to **ISO 5459** and **ISO 128** standards (which govern geometrical tolerancing and technical drawings globally and are referenced in KTU 2024 scheme engineering graphics modules), functional surfaces must be explicitly identified, dimensioned, and toleranced because their deviation directly impacts the functionality, reliability, and service life of the product.

In polymer product design, functional surfaces are particularly critical because polymers exhibit **viscoelastic behavior**, **creep**, and **thermal expansion** that are significantly higher than metals. Therefore, the designer must anticipate how a functional surface will behave under operational loading, environmental conditions, and long-term aging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *functional surface* is NOT just any surface — it is a surface whose geometry, finish, hardness, or coefficient of friction directly enables a designed function (e.g., sealing, sliding, load transfer, locating). Non-functional surfaces are merely cosmetic or protective.

## 1.2 Definition of Lettering in Engineering Drawings

**Lettering** is the standardized practice of writing numerical values, dimensional figures, notes, titles, and annotations on technical drawings using a specific style, size, and orientation. As per **IS 9609 (Indian Standard)**, **ISO 3098**, and **BIS SP 46**, all lettering on engineering drawings must be:

- **Legible** — readable at the original drawing scale
- **Uniform** — consistent height and style across the entire sheet
- **Concise** — minimum characters for maximum information
- **Aligned** — readable from the bottom edge of the drawing (or from the right side per ISO conventions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Lettering Height:** For KTU board examination drawings, the recommended freehand single-stroke gothic (vertical) lettering height is **6 mm** for dimension figures and **10 mm** for titles.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Analogy for Functional Surfaces
Imagine a **kitchen pressure cooker**. The inner rim where the rubber gasket seats is a *functional surface* — even a 0.5 mm deviation can cause steam leakage and dangerous failure. The outer decorative painted surface is *non-functional*. In a polymer gear, the tooth flank is functional (transmits torque); the smooth web between teeth is non-functional.

### Analogy for Lettering
Think of lettering as the **"voice" of the drawing**. A dimension figure of "Ø 25.00" is a measurement instruction to the machinist. If the "5" looks like a "6" due to poor lettering, the entire part gets scrapped. Lettering is the silent but critical communication medium between designer, manufacturer, and inspector.

## 1.4 Classification of Functional Surfaces

| Sl. No | Category | Function | Example in Polymer Products |
|--------|----------|----------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Locating Surfaces** | Establish position/orientation | Snap-fit lead-in chamfer of a polymer housing |
| 2 | **Load-Bearing Surfaces** | Transmit forces/moments | Tooth flank of a polymer gear, bushing bore |
| 3 | **Sealing Surfaces** | Prevent fluid/gas leakage | O-ring groove wall, gasket face |
| 4 | **Sliding/Tribological Surfaces** | Enable relative motion | Slide bearing liner, guide rail |
| 5 | **Fastening Surfaces** | Receive threaded/snap elements | Boss ID for self-tapping screw, snap-fit undercut |
| 6 | **Aesthetic/Cosmetic Surfaces** | Visual appearance (NON-FUNCTIONAL) | Textured outer shell of a mobile phone case |

## 1.5 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Surface Roughness (Ra)** typical ranges: **0.8 µm to 3.2 µm** for polymer functional surfaces
- **Letter height (h)** standard: **h ≥ 2.5 mm** for dimension numerals (IS 9609)
- **Minimum letter spacing** = **0.6 × h**
- **Minimum line spacing (between two text lines)** = **1.5 × h**
- **Stroke width** = **1/10 of letter height** for single-stroke gothic

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Functional Surfaces

A functional surface is characterized by the intersection of **three engineering disciplines**:

1. **Geometric Dimensioning & Tolerancing (GD&T)** — defines allowable deviation
2. **Surface Metrology** — defines the micro-geometry (roughness, waviness, lay)
3. **Material Science** — defines how the surface responds to load, temperature, and chemical exposure

### 2.1.1 The Functional Surface Hierarchy

A complete functional surface specification on a KTU-compliant drawing must include the following six elements, in order:

1. **Datum Reference Frame** — establishes the coordinate system
2. **Size Dimension** with **tolerance** (e.g., Ø25 ± 0.05 mm)
3. **Form Tolerance** (flatness, straightness, roundness, cylindricity)
4. **Orientation Tolerance** (perpendicularity, parallelism, angularity)
5. **Surface Texture Indicator** (Ra, Rz, or lay symbol per ISO 1302)
6. **Material Specification & Surface Treatment** (e.g., "PP black, polished to Ra 0.8")

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Note:** Drawings that specify a functional surface using only a size dimension (without form, orientation, and roughness) are considered *incomplete* and will incur mark deductions in design-based questions.

### 2.1.2 Functional Surface Selection Logic

The designer must ask a **5-question filter** before classifying any surface as functional:

$$\text{Is the surface involved in any of the following?}$$

- (i) Force transmission (static or dynamic)?
- (ii) Fluid/gas containment or sealing?
- (iii) Relative motion (sliding, rolling, oscillating)?
- (iv) Location/registration of mating parts?
- (v) Energy transfer (heat, electrical, optical)?

If **any** answer is YES → the surface is **FUNCTIONAL** and must be fully specified. If **all** answers are NO → the surface is **NON-FUNCTIONAL** (cosmetic/drafting allowance only).

## 2.2 Theoretical Framework of Lettering

### 2.2.1 Standard Lettering Styles (per IS 9609 / ISO 3098-1)

Two principal styles are recognized:

| Style | Description | Use Case | KTU Recommendation |
|-------|-------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Vertical Gothic (Single-Stroke)** | Letters upright, uniform stroke | Indian & KTU conventional practice | **MANDATORY** for board exams |
| **Inclined Gothic (Slant 75°)** | Letters inclined at 75° to horizontal | ISO preferred style | Optional in KTU but accepted |

### 2.2.2 Construction of Single-Stroke Gothic Lettering

The lettering is built using a **1:10 stroke-to-height ratio**. The letter height `h` is divided into discrete units based on the grid method:

- **Uppercase letter height = h** (full grid height)
- **Lowercase letter height = (2/3)h** (the x-height)
- **Stroke width = h/10** (uniform for all characters)
- **Letter spacing = (1/5)h to (1/4)h** between characters
- **Word spacing = h** (minimum)
- **Line spacing = 1.5h** (minimum between two text rows)

### 2.2.3 The "Single-Stroke" Rule

Each letter must be drawn in **one continuous pen-down motion** (or its mirror in reverse) without retracing any portion of the line. This is why the style is called *single-stroke gothic*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes all key formulas and ratios essential for the KTU 2024 ESE exam on this topic.

| Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit | KTU Relevance |
|-----------|--------------------------|------|----------------|
| Letter height (dimension figure) | $h = 2.5$ to $3.5$ | mm | IS 9609 standard |
| Letter height (drawing title) | $h = 5$ to $7$ | mm | IS 9609 standard |
| Letter height (sub-titles) | $h = 3$ to $5$ | mm | IS 9609 standard |
| Stroke width | $s = h/10$ | mm | Single-stroke gothic |
| Letter spacing | $a = 0.6h$ to $0.8h$ | mm | Minimum separation |
| Word spacing | $w \geq h$ | mm | Between words |
| Line spacing | $L \geq 1.5h$ | mm | Between text rows |
| Inclination angle (inclined style) | $\theta = 75°$ | degrees | ISO 3098 |
| Capital letter to lowercase ratio | $h_{lower} = \frac{2}{3}h_{upper}$ | ratio | x-height standard |
| Surface roughness (general polymer) | $R_a = 0.8$ to $3.2$ | µm | Functional surface |
| Surface roughness (sealing) | $R_a \leq 0.8$ | µm | Static seal bore |
| Surface roughness (bearing) | $R_a = 0.4$ to $1.6$ | µm | Sliding contact |
| Surface roughness (snap-fit) | $R_a = 1.6$ | µm | Engagement surface |
| Minimum drafting font ratio (engineer's scale) | $\frac{h}{L_{drawing}} = \frac{1}{50}$ | ratio | Sheet readability |

**LaTeX rendering of the core ratios:**

$$\frac{s}{h} = \frac{1}{10}, \quad \frac{h_{lower}}{h_{upper}} = \frac{2}{3}, \quad \theta = 75°, \quad \frac{a}{h} \in [0.6, 0.8]$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In polymer product design, the functional surface dictates:

- **Mold design decisions** — surface finish of the steel insert ($R_a$ of cavity = $R_a$ of part)
- **Draft angle** — functional sliding surfaces need **lower draft** (0.5° to 1°); cosmetic surfaces can have 1.5° to 2°
- **Gate location** — gates should never be placed on functional sealing or bearing surfaces
- **Parting line placement** — must avoid bisecting any functional surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Practice:** ISO/TS 16949 (automotive) and AS9100 (aerospace) require every functional surface on a polymer component drawing to be tagged with a **Functional Identifier Code (FIC)** in the parts list — a practice increasingly reflected in KTU 2024 design coursework.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Lettering Grid Dimensions

This derivation shows how the KTU-standard lettering dimensions are mathematically related, useful for laying out title blocks and dimensions on a drawing sheet.

### 3.1.1 Given Information

For a KTU board exam drawing sheet, the standard dimension figure height is prescribed as:

$$h = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

We need to derive the stroke width, letter spacing, word spacing, and line spacing.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1 — Stroke Width (s)**

By the single-stroke gothic rule:

$$s = \frac{h}{10}$$

Substituting $h = 2.5$ mm:

$$s = \frac{2.5}{10} = 0.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Letter Spacing (a)**

The minimum letter spacing is $0.6h$ and preferred spacing is $0.8h$:

$$a_{min} = 0.6 \times 2.5 = 1.5 \text{ mm}$$

$$a_{preferred} = 0.8 \times 2.5 = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Word Spacing (w)**

The minimum word spacing equals the letter height:

$$w_{min} = h = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Line Spacing (L)**

The minimum vertical separation between two text rows is $1.5h$:

$$L_{min} = 1.5 \times 2.5 = 3.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Lowercase Letter Height ($h_{lower}$)**

The x-height rule states:

$$h_{lower} = \frac{2}{3} h_{upper}$$

$$h_{lower} = \frac{2}{3} \times 2.5 = 1.667 \text{ mm}$$

### 3.1.3 Consolidated Results

$$\begin{aligned}
h &= 2.5 \text{ mm} \\
s &= 0.25 \text{ mm} \\
a &\in [1.5, 2.0] \text{ mm} \\
w &\geq 2.5 \text{ mm} \\
L &\geq 3.75 \text{ mm} \\
h_{lower} &= 1.667 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

These are the dimensions a student should mentally visualize when drawing a KTU exam sheet.

## 3.2 Derivation of Functional Surface Tolerance Stack-Up

When two functional surfaces mate, the resulting clearance or interference determines assembly behavior. The **tolerance stack-up** for a polymer snap-fit assembly is derived as follows.

### 3.2.1 Scenario

A polymer snap-fit hook engages a mating lip. The nominal engagement length is:

$$L_{nom} = 8.00 \text{ mm}$$

The hook has a dimensional tolerance of $\pm 0.10$ mm, and the lip has $\pm 0.15$ mm.

### 3.2.2 Worst-Case Stack-Up

**Step 1 — Maximum Engagement (Interference)**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{max} &= L_{nom} + t_{hook} + t_{lip} \\
L_{max} &= 8.00 + 0.10 + 0.15 \\
L_{max} &= 8.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Engagement (Clearance)**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{min} &= L_{nom} - t_{hook} - t_{lip} \\
L_{min} &= 8.00 - 0.10 - 0.15 \\
L_{min} &= 7.75 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total Tolerance Band (Worst Case)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{WC} &= L_{max} - L_{min} \\
\Delta L_{WC} &= 8.25 - 7.75 \\
\Delta L_{WC} &= 0.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Statistical (RSS) Tolerance Band**

Using Root Sum of Squares (more realistic for polymer molding):

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta L_{RSS} &= \sqrt{t_{hook}^2 + t_{lip}^2} \\
\Delta L_{RSS} &= \sqrt{(0.10)^2 + (0.15)^2} \\
\Delta L_{RSS} &= \sqrt{0.0100 + 0.0225} \\
\Delta L_{RSS} &= \sqrt{0.0325} \\
\Delta L_{RSS} &= 0.1803 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Engagement Variation**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{upper}^{RSS} &= 8.00 + \frac{0.1803}{2} = 8.090 \text{ mm} \\
L_{lower}^{RSS} &= 8.00 - \frac{0.1803}{2} = 7.910 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

The RSS approach reduces the total stack-up from 0.50 mm to 0.18 mm — a 64% reduction, which is why statistical tolerancing is preferred in polymer design.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Functional vs Non-Functional Surface

> [!NOTE]
> Use this matrix as a structured exam-answer template for any "Identify the functional surfaces" question.

| Sl. No | Question | Yes → Functional Category | No → Continue Checking |
|--------|----------|---------------------------|-------------------------|
| Q1 | Does the surface transmit a load? | **Load-bearing** | → Q2 |
| Q2 | Does the surface contain a fluid or gas? | **Sealing** | → Q3 |
| Q3 | Does the surface slide against another part? | **Tribological** | → Q4 |
| Q4 | Does the surface establish the position of a mating part? | **Locating** | → Q5 |
| Q5 | Does the surface receive a fastener or snap element? | **Fastening** | → Q6 |
| Q6 | Does the surface transfer heat, electricity, or light? | **Energy-transfer** | **NON-FUNCTIONAL** |

## 3.4 Worked Example: Lettering Layout in a Title Block

A KTU exam sheet title block is to contain the text:

> "PCPOT601 — POLYMER PRODUCTS — DESIGN & TESTING — KTU 2024 SCHEME"

Required: Compute the total horizontal space occupied by this lettering, assuming standard dimensions.

**Given:**

- Number of characters: $N = 60$ (including spaces and hyphens)
- Letter height: $h = 2.5$ mm
- Average character width: $w_c \approx 0.7h$
- Word spacing: $w_w = h = 2.5$ mm
- Number of words: $N_w = 6$

**Step 1 — Average character width**

$$w_c = 0.7 \times 2.5 = 1.75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Total character width**

$$W_c = N \times w_c = 60 \times 1.75 = 105.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Total inter-word spacing**

$$W_w = (N_w - 1) \times w_w = 5 \times 2.5 = 12.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total horizontal space**

$$W_{total} = W_c + W_w = 105.0 + 12.5 = 117.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with standard A2 sheet**

A standard A2 drawing sheet is 594 mm wide. The title occupies:

$$\frac{W_{total}}{W_{sheet}} = \frac{117.5}{594} = 0.198 = 19.8\%$$

This is within the recommended 20–25% title block width, so the layout is **valid**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Surface Identification Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram presents the complete decision logic for identifying and classifying functional surfaces on a polymer component drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Surface Identified] --> B{Q1: Load Transmission?}
    B -- Yes --> C1[Load-Bearing Surface]
    B -- No --> D{Q2: Fluid or Gas Containment?}
    D -- Yes --> C2[Sealing Surface]
    D -- No --> E{Q3: Sliding or Rolling Contact?}
    E -- Yes --> C3[Tribological Surface]
    E -- No --> F{Q4: Locating Mating Part?}
    F -- Yes --> C4[Locating Surface]
    F -- No --> G{Q5: Fastener or Snap Element?}
    G -- Yes --> C5[Fastening Surface]
    G -- No --> H{Q6: Energy Transfer?}
    H -- Yes --> C6[Energy-Transfer Surface]
    H -- No --> C7[Non-Functional Cosmetic Surface]

    C1 --> I[Apply Full GD&T Specification]
    C2 --> I
    C3 --> I
    C4 --> I
    C5 --> I
    C6 --> I
    C7 --> J[Apply Aesthetic Spec Only]
```

## 4.2 Lettering Construction Block Architecture

The following diagram maps the construction parameters and their hierarchical relationships in standard single-stroke gothic lettering.

```mermaid
    P1[Letter Height h equals 2.5 to 3.5 mm] --> P2[Stroke Width s equals h divided by 10]

    P1 --> P5[Word Spacing w equals h]
    P1 --> P6[Line Spacing L equals 1.5h]
    P2 --> P7[Single Stroke Rule: One Continuous Motion]
    P3 --> P8[x-height Line at Two-thirds h]
    P4 --> P9[Horizontal Character Pitch]
    P5 --> P10[Inter-word Gap]
    P6 --> P11[Vertical Row Gap]
    P7 --> Z[Output: KTU Compliant Lettering]
    P8 --> Z
    P9 --> Z
    P10 --> Z
    P11 --> Z
```

## 4.3 Functional Surface Specification Block on a Drawing

The following diagram shows the six mandatory specification elements that must accompany any functional surface on a KTU-standard technical drawing.

```mermaid

    FS[Functional Surface Identified on Drawing] --> S1[Datum Reference Frame]
    FS --> S2[Size Dimension with Tolerance]
    FS --> S3[Form Tolerance Flatness Roundness Cylindricity]
    FS --> S4[Orientation Tolerance Perpendicularity Parallelism Angularity]
    FS --> S5[Surface Texture Ra or Lay Symbol]

    S3 --> OUT
    S4 --> OUT
    S5 --> OUT
    S6 --> OUT
```

The following architecture flow illustrates a typical polymer snap-fit housing with annotated functional and non-functional surfaces.


> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Limitation Acknowledgment:** Physical engineering drawings of snap-fits, gear teeth, and O-ring grooves are best produced using CAD tools in the actual exam. The block diagrams above provide the *logical structure* of the surface relationships, which is what the KTU 2024 examiner primarily evaluates.
