<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define a functional surface. Give two examples from polymer product design.**

**Model Answer:**

A *functional surface* is any surface of an engineering component that is essential to the product's operational function, such as load transmission, sealing, locating, sliding, or fastening. Unlike cosmetic surfaces, functional surfaces must be explicitly dimensioned, toleranced, and surface-finish specified because their geometric deviation directly affects product performance and reliability.

*Examples in polymer products:*
1. The **inner bore of a polymer bushing** — a tribological surface that supports a rotating shaft and must maintain a controlled $R_a$ of 0.8 µm to 1.6 µm.
2. The **engagement face of a snap-fit hook** — a fastening surface whose angle and undercut depth govern the assembly force and retention strength.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse "functional" with "machined" or "visible". A functional surface is defined by its **role**, not by its manufacturing process. Many functional polymer surfaces are produced as-molded (no machining). Marks are lost if the student equates functional with machined.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State the standard proportions (height, stroke width, letter spacing) used in single-stroke gothic lettering as per IS 9609.**

**Model Answer:**

According to **IS 9609** (Indian Standard on lettering on technical drawings), the standard proportions for single-stroke gothic lettering are:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Letter height } h &= \text{chosen (typically 2.5 to 3.5 mm for dimension figures)} \\
\text{Stroke width } s &= \frac{h}{10} \\
\text{Letter spacing } a &= 0.6h \text{ to } 0.8h \\
\text{Word spacing } w &\geq h \\
\text{Line spacing } L &\geq 1.5h
\end{aligned}$$

For inclined gothic lettering, the inclination angle is **75°** to the horizontal, and lowercase (x-height) letters are drawn at **2/3 of the uppercase height**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [Internal Choice Option A — KTU University Exam, May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the procedure to identify functional surfaces on a polymer product drawing, with reference to ISO 5459 and IS 9609 standards. List the categories of functional surfaces.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A polymer bearing housing has a 25.00 mm bore with tolerance ±0.05 mm, which mates with a steel shaft of 24.95 mm with tolerance ±0.03 mm. Calculate (i) the maximum clearance, (ii) the minimum clearance, and (iii) the statistical (RSS) clearance. Comment on the suitability of using worst-case versus statistical tolerance for polymer components.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define functional surface** (1 mark): As per ISO 5459, a functional surface is one whose geometric deviation affects the performance of the product.

**Step 2 — Identification procedure** (3 marks):

1. Identify all surfaces on the component drawing.
2. Apply the 6-question filter (load, fluid, motion, locating, fastening, energy).
3. Mark functional surfaces with a "Functional Identifier" and a leader line in the drawing.
4. Specify each functional surface with the 6 mandatory elements (datum, size+tolerance, form, orientation, surface texture, material).
5. Cross-reference with the parts list using a Functional Identifier Code (FIC).

**Step 3 — Categories of functional surfaces** (3 marks):

1. Load-bearing surfaces
2. Sealing surfaces
3. Sliding/tribological surfaces
4. Locating surfaces
5. Fastening surfaces
6. Energy-transfer surfaces

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- Bore: $D_{bore} = 25.00 \pm 0.05$ mm
- Shaft: $D_{shaft} = 24.95 \pm 0.03$ mm
- Nominal clearance: $C_{nom} = D_{bore} - D_{shaft} = 25.00 - 24.95 = 0.05$ mm

**(i) Maximum Clearance** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{max} &= C_{nom} + t_{bore} + t_{shaft} \\
C_{max} &= 0.05 + 0.05 + 0.03 \\
C_{max} &= 0.13 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Minimum Clearance** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= C_{nom} - t_{bore} - t_{shaft} \\
C_{min} &= 0.05 - 0.05 - 0.03 \\
C_{min} &= -0.03 \text{ mm (INTERFERENCE)}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $C_{min} < 0$, the assembly will suffer interference at the worst-case combination.

**(iii) Statistical (RSS) Clearance** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{C} &= \sqrt{t_{bore}^2 + t_{shaft}^2} \\
\sigma_{C} &= \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (0.03)^2} \\
\sigma_{C} &= \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.0009} \\
\sigma_{C} &= \sqrt{0.0034} \\
\sigma_{C} &= 0.0583 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{RSS,upper} &= C_{nom} + 3\sigma_{C} = 0.05 + 0.175 = 0.225 \text{ mm} \\
C_{RSS,lower} &= C_{nom} - 3\sigma_{C} = 0.05 - 0.175 = -0.125 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on suitability** (1 mark):

Worst-case analysis is overly conservative and shows interference. Statistical analysis shows that approximately 99.74% (within ±3σ) of assemblies will have a clearance between −0.125 mm and +0.225 mm. For polymer components, **statistical tolerancing is preferred** because (i) polymer dimensions are statistically distributed due to molding process variation, and (ii) the interference in a small percentage of parts can be tolerated by the polymer's elastic compliance, unlike metals. The designer should ensure the **minimum process clearance is positive** by adjusting nominal dimensions or tightening tolerances.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark question):** Many students forget to compute the **interference indication** when $C_{min} < 0$. Marks are lost if the answer simply states $C_{min}$ without commenting on the physical implication (interference fit, assembly failure). Always cross-check the sign of $C_{min}$.

---

### Question B [Internal Choice Option B — KTU University Exam, May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the principles of lettering on engineering drawings as per IS 9609 and ISO 3098. Compare vertical gothic and inclined gothic lettering styles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A title block on a KTU exam sheet must accommodate the following text in 3 lines:
- Line 1: "POLYMER GEAR DESIGN" (18 characters)
- Line 2: "MODULE 2 ASSIGNMENT" (19 characters)
- Line 3: "KTU 2024 SCHEME" (15 characters)
Compute the total vertical space required for the three lines if the letter height is 3 mm. Comment on whether the design complies with the standard minimum line spacing.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Purpose of lettering** (1 mark): Lettering provides the textual communication on a drawing — dimensions, notes, titles, tolerances, materials — and must be legible, uniform, and standardized.

**Step 2 — Principles of IS 9609** (2 marks):

- Single-stroke gothic style
- Stroke width = h/10
- Letter spacing = 0.6h to 0.8h
- Line spacing ≥ 1.5h
- Word spacing ≥ h
- Vertical letters are the default; inclined letters at 75° are also permitted

**Step 3 — Principles of ISO 3098** (1 mark): ISO 3098 specifies an internationally harmonized lettering system, supports both Latin and non-Latin scripts, and is the modern preferred standard.

**Step 4 — Comparison Table** (3 marks):

| Feature | Vertical Gothic | Inclined Gothic |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Orientation | Upright (0°) | Inclined at 75° |
| Ease of freehand execution | Easier for right-handers | Slightly harder |
| Standard reference | IS 9609 (Indian) | ISO 3098-1 (International) |
| KTU board exam preference | **Mandatory default** | Accepted alternative |
| Aesthetic appearance | Formal, rigid | Slightly elegant |
| Speed of execution | Moderate | Moderate |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- Letter height: $h = 3$ mm
- Number of lines: $n = 3$
- Standard minimum line spacing: $L_{min} = 1.5h$

**Step 1 — Standard minimum line spacing** (2 marks)

$$L_{min} = 1.5 \times 3 = 4.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Total vertical space** (3 marks)

For 3 lines of text, the total vertical space occupied is the sum of three letter heights plus two inter-line gaps:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{total} &= n \times h + (n - 1) \times L_{min} \\
V_{total} &= 3 \times 3 + 2 \times 4.5 \\
V_{total} &= 9 + 9 \\
V_{total} &= 18.0 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Line-by-line breakdown** (1 mark)

| Line | Text | Character Count | Letter Height (mm) |
|------|------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | POLYMER GEAR DESIGN | 18 | 3.0 |
| 2 | MODULE 2 ASSIGNMENT | 19 | 3.0 |
| 3 | KTU 2024 SCHEME | 15 | 3.0 |

**Step 4 — Compliance check** (1 mark)

The design **complies** with IS 9609 because:
- Each line uses the prescribed letter height of 3 mm (within the 2.5–3.5 mm range).
- The inter-line spacing of 4.5 mm equals the standard minimum $1.5h$.
- Total vertical space (18 mm) is reasonable for a title block in an A2 sheet (594 × 420 mm).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (14-mark question):** A common mistake is to compute the total vertical space as $3 \times h = 9$ mm, **forgetting the gaps between lines**. The total space is always $n \times h + (n-1) \times L$. Forgetting the inter-line gaps costs 2–3 marks.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — General Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Functional Surfaces & Lettering Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "functional" with "machined" or "visible"** — many polymer functional surfaces are as-molded, not machined.
> 2. **Incomplete surface specification** — listing only a size dimension without form, orientation, and roughness is considered incomplete.
> 3. **Wrong sign convention in tolerance stack-up** — students often write $C_{min}$ as a positive number even when the calculation yields interference.
> 4. **Stroke width errors in lettering** — drawing strokes that are 1/5 or 1/8 of $h$ instead of the correct 1/10.
> 5. **Forgetting the (n-1) line-gap rule** — total vertical space = $n \times h + (n-1) \times 1.5h$, not just $n \times h$.
> 6. **Using the vertical pipe `|` inside LaTeX equations in answer sheets** — break the line and use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value notation.
> 7. **Ignoring lowercase x-height** — drawing lowercase letters at the same height as uppercase violates IS 9609.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional Surface:** A surface essential to the product's function (load, seal, slide, locate, fasten, energy-transfer).
- **Non-Functional Surface:** A purely cosmetic or structural-drafting surface that does not affect product performance.
- **The 6-Question Filter:** Load, Fluid, Motion, Locate, Fasten, Energy — any "Yes" makes a surface functional.
- **The 6 Mandatory Specifications** for a functional surface: Datum, Size+Tolerance, Form, Orientation, Surface Texture, Material.
- **IS 9609 / ISO 3098** are the governing standards for lettering.
- **Single-stroke gothic** is the KTU-mandatory style.
- **Stroke width = h/10**, **Letter spacing = 0.6h to 0.8h**, **Word spacing ≥ h**, **Line spacing ≥ 1.5h**.
- **Lowercase x-height = (2/3) × uppercase height**.
- **Inclined gothic angle = 75°** to the horizontal.
- **Polymer-specific consideration:** Functional surfaces should be located away from gate marks, weld lines, and ejector-pin witness marks.
- **Surface roughness (Ra) for typical polymer functional surfaces:** 0.8–3.2 µm.
- **Tolerance stack-up:** Always compute both worst-case and statistical (RSS) values for polymer assemblies and comment on interference vs. clearance.
- **Statistical tolerancing** reduces the total stack-up significantly (often by 50–70%) and is preferred for polymer molded parts.
- **Lettering on title blocks** must occupy 20–25% of the sheet width for readability.
- **Minimum letter height** for dimension figures is 2.5 mm (IS 9609) — never go below this.
- **Functional surface identification** is a **process**, not a single step — apply the 6-question filter rigorously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Snap Fitting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Snap Fitting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Snap Fit** is an integral, permanent or separable mechanical fastening system used to join two or more components, where at least one component is manufactured from a polymeric material that undergoes **elastic deformation** during assembly and recovers its original shape to produce a **mechanical interlock** that resists disassembly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Polymer Product Design Context):** A snap fit is a *one-piece* or *two-piece* fastening joint that utilizes the inherent viscoelastic flexibility of the polymer to deflect a protruding element (hook, bead, or lug) over a mating undercut, generating a holding force through stored elastic strain energy, without the use of additional fasteners (screws, rivets, adhesives).

Snap fits are classified under **Mechanical Joining Methods** in polymer product design and are widely employed in consumer electronics (mobile phone housings, battery compartments), automotive interiors (trim panels, glove boxes), toys (LEGO bricks), and medical devices.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine snapping together the lid of a **plastic food container (like a Tupperware box)**. When you press the lid down, you feel the four tabs on the sides **flex outward** momentarily, and as soon as they pass over the rim of the container, they **spring back** into a groove with a satisfying "click." That momentary flexing is *elastic deformation*, and the click is the *mechanical interlock*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - A **snap fit = a clever use of polymer flexibility**. Instead of using a metal screw or glue, the plastic part itself bends a little bit during assembly, then "remembers" its original shape.
> - The polymer behaves like a **spring** during assembly and like a **rigid latch** once snapped.
> - The **maximum strain** experienced during this flex must NEVER exceed the polymer's *elastic strain limit*, otherwise the snap will permanently deform or break.

## 1.3 Why Snap Fits Are Critical in Polymer Engineering

| Attribute | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| **Cost** | Eliminates secondary fastening operations (screwing, welding, gluing) |
| **Assembly Speed** | Reduces assembly time by up to **80%** in high-volume production |
| **Disassembly** | Allows easy end-of-life recycling (circular economy compliance) |
| **Weight** | Reduces overall product mass vs. metallic fasteners |
| **Design Freedom** | Enables complex aesthetic enclosures without visible fixings |

## 1.4 Key Design Metrics (Bold Standards)

The three primary metrics that govern every snap fit design are:

- **Deflection (δ)** — measured in **mm**, the displacement at the snap's tip during assembly
- **Maximum Fiber Strain (ε)** — *dimensionless ratio*, must remain below the polymer's *strain limit*
- **Assembly Force (F_assembly)** — measured in **Newtons (N)**, the force required to engage the snap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-designed snap fit has **ε < ε_allowable**, where ε_allowable is typically **25–50%** of the polymer's *strain-at-break* value (a standard industry safety factor of 2–4×).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Snap Fits

Snap fits are broadly classified into **three geometric categories**, each with distinct stress states:

### 2.1.1 Cantilever Snap Fit (Most Common)

A projecting beam (the *cantilever*) carries a **hook or bead** at its free end. During assembly, the hook deflects over the mating undercut.

- **Loading Mode:** Transverse bending (beam bending).
- **Stress State:** Maximum bending stress at the *root* (fixed end).
- **Typical Applications:** Battery covers, enclosure lids, clip-on panels.
- **Equation Type:** Linear elastic beam theory.

### 2.1.2 Annular (Torsional) Snap Fit

A cylindrical ring with an inward or outward bead engages a mating groove. The ring is circumferentially stretched or compressed.

- **Loading Mode:** Pure hoop stress / circumferential tension.
- **Stress State:** Uniform around the ring (for thin-walled rings).
- **Typical Applications:** Bottle caps, pen barrels, connector couplings.
- **Equation Type:** Thin-walled pressure vessel theory.

### 2.1.3 Cylindrical Snap Fit (Compression Snap)

A cylindrical protrusion is press-fit into a slightly smaller bore. The interference generates radial compression and a friction-based hold.

- **Loading Mode:** Radial compression / interference fit.
- **Stress State:** Hoop and radial stresses (Lamé's equations).
- **Typical Applications:** Plug-in electronic connectors, dowel pins.

> [!NOTE]
> The **cantilever snap** dominates KTU examination questions because it requires application of *beam deflection* and *bending strain* — fundamental topics of strength of materials.

## 2.2 Cantilever Beam Mechanics — The Foundation

The cantilever beam is the building block of every projecting snap fit. Consider a rectangular cantilever of:

- Length: $L$ (mm)
- Width: $b$ (mm)
- Thickness: $h$ (mm)
- Modulus of Elasticity: $E$ (MPa)
- Applied transverse load at free end: $F$ (N)

### Key Cantilever Equations

**1. Tip Deflection** (force applied at free end):

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area: $I = \dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$

**2. Bending Moment at Root** (max moment location):

$$M_{max} = F \cdot L$$

**3. Maximum Bending Stress** (at the root, outer fiber):

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}}$$

where $c = h/2$ (distance to outer fiber).

**4. Maximum Fiber Strain** (at the root, outer fiber):

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^{2}}$$

**5. Stiffness (Spring Constant)** of the cantilever:

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$$

**6. Strain expressed directly via tip deflection**:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot \delta \cdot h}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$$

This is the **MOST IMPORTANT** snap-fit equation for KTU — it lets you check strain directly from the geometric deflection.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Second Moment of Area (rectangular beam) | $I$ | $\dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | Use *b* = width, *h* = thickness |
| 2 | Tip Deflection | $\delta$ | $\dfrac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$ | mm | Force at free end |
| 3 | Max Bending Moment | $M_{max}$ | $F \cdot L$ | N·mm | At root of cantilever |
| 4 | Max Bending Stress | $\sigma_{max}$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Outer fiber, at root |
| 5 | Max Fiber Strain | $\varepsilon_{max}$ | $\dfrac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{E \cdot b \cdot h^{2}}$ | dimensionless | Compare to allowable |
| 6 | Strain via Deflection | $\varepsilon_{max}$ | $\dfrac{3 \cdot \delta \cdot h}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$ | dimensionless | ⭐ KTU Favorite |
| 7 | Cantilever Stiffness | $k$ | $\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$ | N/mm | Spring rate |
| 8 | Assembly Force | $F_{asm}$ | $\dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3} \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$ | N | Combines (5) and (7) |
| 9 | Hoop Stress (annular) | $\sigma_{h}$ | $\dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | MPa | $P$ = interference pressure |
| 10 | Allowable Strain Factor | SF | $\varepsilon_{allow} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{break}}{SF}$ | dimensionless | SF = 2 to 4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** When a numerical snap-fit problem is asked, examiners almost always expect you to compute $\varepsilon_{max}$ using equation (6) and compare it with $\varepsilon_{allowable}$. Memorize this equation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics:** Apple MacBook casings, Samsung phone batteries — cantilever snaps allow tool-free battery swap.
- **Automotive:** Instrument cluster lenses snapped into dashboards; reduces screw inventory.
- **Medical:** Single-use diagnostic cartridges that snap into analyzers — tolerances of ±0.05 mm.
- **Toys:** LEGO studs — geometric interference fit requiring micron-level precision.

## 2.5 Undercut Geometry & Engagement Mechanics

The **undercut (U)** is the radial distance the hook must travel over to lock. During assembly, the cantilever deflects by an amount equal to (or slightly greater than) the undercut.

The **holding force (F_hold)** after engagement is calculated using the *stress relaxation* over time, accounting for viscoelastic creep. For short-term static loads:

$$F_{hold} = \frac{\sigma_{hold} \cdot b \cdot h^{2}}{6 \cdot L}$$

where $\sigma_{hold}$ is the design stress (typically 25–50% of yield strength).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Cantilever Snap Fit Maximum Strain

**Objective:** Derive the expression for maximum fiber strain in a rectangular cantilever snap fit in terms of deflection (δ) and geometry (L, h).

### Step 1 — Define Geometry and Loading

Consider a rectangular cantilever of length *L*, width *b*, thickness *h*, fixed at one end (the *root*) and free at the other (carrying the *hook*). A transverse assembly force *F* is applied at the free end, producing a tip deflection δ.

### Step 2 — Express Deflection of a Cantilever with End Load

For a cantilever beam loaded transversely at the free end:

$$\delta = \frac{F \cdot L^{3}}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}$$

Rearranging for *F*:

$$F = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot \delta}{L^{3}}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Moment of Inertia

For a rectangular cross-section, $I = \dfrac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12}$ :

$$F = \frac{3 \cdot E}{L^{3}} \cdot \frac{b \cdot h^{3}}{12} \cdot \delta$$

$$F = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3} \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$$

This is the **stiffness equation** $F = k \cdot \delta$ where $k = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$.

### Step 4 — Compute the Bending Stress at the Root

The maximum bending moment at the root is:

$$M_{max} = F \cdot L$$

The maximum bending stress at the outer fiber (distance *c = h/2* from neutral axis) is:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{F \cdot L \cdot (h/2)}{(b \cdot h^{3}/12)}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{F \cdot L \cdot h \cdot 12}{2 \cdot b \cdot h^{3}} = \frac{6 \cdot F \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute F from Step 3 into Stress Equation

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot L}{b \cdot h^{2}} \cdot \left( \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3} \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^{3}} \right)$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot E \cdot b \cdot h^{3} \cdot L \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^{3} \cdot b \cdot h^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6 \cdot E \cdot h \cdot \delta}{4 \cdot L^{2}}$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot E \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$$

### Step 6 — Convert Stress to Strain via Hooke's Law

For linear elastic polymer behavior within the design range:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \frac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$$

This is the **final governing equation** for maximum fiber strain in a rectangular cantilever snap fit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Form:**
> $$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}}$$
> This equation is *independent* of the modulus *E* — which means the *geometry alone* dictates the strain, not the material choice! This is a profound insight in snap-fit design.

### Step 7 — Derive Stiffness in Terms of Geometry Only

From Step 3:

$$k = \frac{F}{\delta} = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$$

This shows that *thickness h* has a **cubic influence** on stiffness — tripling the thickness makes the snap **27× stiffer**, while tripling the length reduces stiffness by **27×**.

### Step 8 — Assembly Force Calculation

Combining stiffness with the required undercut deflection:

$$F_{assembly} = k \cdot \delta = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}} \cdot \delta$$

### Step 3.9 — Design Verification Step

After computing $\varepsilon_{max}$, verify against allowable:

$$\varepsilon_{max} \leq \varepsilon_{allowable} = \frac{\varepsilon_{break}}{SF}$$

If $\varepsilon_{max} > \varepsilon_{allowable}$, the snap will fail — redesign by:

- Increasing *L* (longer beam → less strain) ⭐ Best option
- Decreasing *h* (thinner beam → less strain)
- Reducing undercut δ (smaller deflection → less strain)
- Choosing a more flexible polymer (higher $\varepsilon_{break}$)

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A cantilever snap fit is to be designed in **Polypropylene (PP)** with the following data:

- Length, $L = 25$ mm
- Width, $b = 8$ mm
- Thickness, $h = 2.5$ mm
- Modulus, $E = 1500$ MPa
- Undercut (deflection), $\delta = 1.5$ mm
- Strain-at-break of PP, $\varepsilon_{break} = 0.12$ (i.e., 12%)
- Safety factor, $SF = 3$

**Required:**
(a) Calculate the maximum fiber strain at the root.
(b) Determine if the design is safe.
(c) Calculate the assembly force.

### Solution:

**(a) Maximum Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot (2.5) \cdot (1.5)}{2 \cdot (25)^{2}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{11.25}{1250} = 0.009$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = 0.009 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 0.9\%}$$

**(b) Allowable Strain Check:**

$$\varepsilon_{allowable} = \frac{\varepsilon_{break}}{SF} = \frac{0.12}{3} = 0.04 \;\; (4\%)$$

Since $\varepsilon_{max} = 0.009 < 0.04 = \varepsilon_{allowable}$, **the design is SAFE** ✅.

**(c) Assembly Force:**

$$F_{assembly} = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}} \cdot \delta$$

$$F_{assembly} = \frac{1500 \cdot 8 \cdot (2.5)^{3}}{4 \cdot (25)^{3}} \cdot 1.5$$

$$F_{assembly} = \frac{1500 \cdot 8 \cdot 15.625}{4 \cdot 15625} \cdot 1.5$$

$$F_{assembly} = \frac{187500}{62500} \cdot 1.5$$

$$F_{assembly} = 3.0 \cdot 1.5 = 4.5 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{assembly} = 4.5 \;\; \text{N}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An assembly force of just 4.5 N is ideal for a hand-assembled consumer product — typically the acceptable range is **5–20 N** for finger assembly. If the result is too high (>30 N), the snap becomes difficult to engage; if too low (<2 N), it will not hold securely.

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define snap fitting. List any THREE advantages of snap fits over conventional fasteners.**

**Model Answer:**

A **snap fit** is an integral mechanical fastening system that uses the inherent *elastic flexibility* of a polymer to deflect a projecting element (hook, bead, or lug) over a mating undercut during assembly, and then allows it to recover elastically, producing a mechanical interlock that resists disassembly — without using screws, adhesives, or additional fasteners.

**Three Advantages:**

1. **Reduced Assembly Time** — Snap fits engage in a single linear or rotational motion, eliminating the need for multiple fastening operations (screwing, riveting, or welding).
2. **Lower Total Cost** — Fewer components (no screws or inserts), no secondary operation, and easier automation of assembly.
3. **Easy Disassembly for Recycling** — Supports circular-economy and end-of-life disassembly requirements (e.g., WEEE Directive compliance).

*(Word limit: ~80–100 words | 3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for advantages)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between cantilever and annular snap fits with respect to (a) loading mode and (b) stress state.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Cantilever Snap Fit | Annular Snap Fit |
|---|---|---|
| **Loading Mode** | Transverse bending — the hook is at the free end of a projecting beam | Circumferential (hoop) tension or compression — the entire ring stretches or contracts |
| **Stress State** | Non-uniform bending stress; maximum at the *root* (fixed end), zero at the tip | Uniform hoop stress distributed around the circumference (for thin-walled rings) |
| **Governing Equation** | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{3 h \delta}{2 L^{2}}$ | $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$ |
| **Example** | Mobile phone battery cover | Bottle cap, pen barrel |

*(3 marks: 1.5 each for the two features)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, students answer ONE full question (14 marks) per module, choosing between two alternatives. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**A cantilever snap fit is to be designed for a polypropylene (PP) enclosure. The design specifications are:**

- Length, $L = 30$ mm
- Width, $b = 6$ mm
- Thickness, $h = 2$ mm
- Modulus, $E = 1400$ MPa
- Undercut (deflection), $\delta = 1.2$ mm
- Strain-at-break of PP, $\varepsilon_{break} = 0.10$
- Design safety factor, $SF = 4$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the maximum fiber strain at the root of the cantilever and verify whether the design is safe.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the governing strain equation for a cantilever snap fit:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the given values:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot (2) \cdot (1.2)}{2 \cdot (30)^{2}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{7.2}{1800}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = 0.004 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 0.4\%}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the allowable strain:

$$\varepsilon_{allowable} = \frac{\varepsilon_{break}}{SF} = \frac{0.10}{4} = 0.025 \;\; (2.5\%)$$

**Step 4:** Compare:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = 0.004 \;\; < \;\; \varepsilon_{allowable} = 0.025$$

**Conclusion:** The design is **SAFE** with a significant margin. The strain is only **16%** of the allowable limit, indicating the snap could be made more compact or could be in a stiffer polymer.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Determine the assembly force and the cantilever stiffness. Comment on whether the assembly force is suitable for manual (finger) assembly.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the cantilever stiffness:

$$k = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$$

$$k = \frac{1400 \cdot 6 \cdot (2)^{3}}{4 \cdot (30)^{3}}$$

$$k = \frac{1400 \cdot 6 \cdot 8}{4 \cdot 27000}$$

$$k = \frac{67200}{108000}$$

$$\boxed{k = 0.622 \;\; \text{N/mm}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the assembly force:

$$F_{assembly} = k \cdot \delta = 0.622 \cdot 1.2 = 0.747 \;\; \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{assembly} \approx 0.75 \;\; \text{N}}$$

**Step 3:** Ergonomic Comment:

The acceptable range for manual finger assembly is typically **5 N to 20 N**. A force of **0.75 N** is **too low** — the snap will engage too easily, leading to:
- **Loose feel** during handling
- **Risk of accidental disengagement** during shipping or use
- **Insufficient holding force** in service

**Recommendation:** The design should be modified by:
- **Increasing the undercut δ** to ~3 mm (raises $F$ to ~1.9 N — still low but better), OR
- **Increasing thickness h** to 3 mm — this would make $F$ scale by $(h_{new}/h_{old})^{3} = (3/2)^{3} = 3.375×$, raising $F$ to ~2.5 N, OR
- **Selecting a stiffer polymer** like PA66 ($E \approx 3000$ MPa) which doubles $F$ to ~1.5 N.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**A snap-fit hook for a battery cover is manufactured from Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS). Design data:**

- Cantilever length, $L = 20$ mm
- Width, $b = 5$ mm
- Thickness, $h = 1.8$ mm
- Modulus, $E = 2300$ MPa
- Strain-at-break, $\varepsilon_{break} = 0.06$
- Safety factor, $SF = 2.5$
- Required undercut (deflection), $\delta = 1.0$ mm

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Calculate the maximum fiber strain at the root and the assembly force required for engagement.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Maximum Fiber Strain:

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}} = \frac{3 \cdot (1.8) \cdot (1.0)}{2 \cdot (20)^{2}}$$

$$\varepsilon_{max} = \frac{5.4}{800} = 0.00675$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{max} = 0.675\%}$$

**Step 2:** Allowable Strain Check:

$$\varepsilon_{allowable} = \frac{0.06}{2.5} = 0.024 \;\; (2.4\%)$$

Since $0.00675 < 0.024$, **design is safe** ✅.

**Step 3:** Cantilever Stiffness:

$$k = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}} = \frac{2300 \cdot 5 \cdot (1.8)^{3}}{4 \cdot (20)^{3}}$$

$$k = \frac{2300 \cdot 5 \cdot 5.832}{4 \cdot 8000} = \frac{67068}{32000}$$

$$\boxed{k = 2.096 \;\; \text{N/mm}}$$

**Step 4:** Assembly Force:

$$F_{assembly} = k \cdot \delta = 2.096 \cdot 1.0$$

$$\boxed{F_{assembly} \approx 2.1 \;\; \text{N}}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation:

An assembly force of 2.1 N is on the lower side of the ergonomic range (5–20 N). The snap will engage easily with the thumb; suitable for compact consumer electronics.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the design is to be changed so that the assembly force is increased to a target value of 8 N (with the same material), and the deflection is increased to 1.5 mm, determine the new required thickness *h* while keeping length L and width b unchanged. Verify that the new strain remains safe.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the required stiffness for $F_{target} = 8$ N and $\delta_{new} = 1.5$ mm:

$$k_{required} = \frac{F_{target}}{\delta_{new}} = \frac{8}{1.5} = 5.333 \;\; \text{N/mm}$$

**Step 2:** Solve the stiffness equation for new thickness *h*:

$$k = \frac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$$

$$h^{3} = \frac{4 \cdot L^{3} \cdot k}{E \cdot b}$$

$$h^{3} = \frac{4 \cdot (20)^{3} \cdot 5.333}{2300 \cdot 5} = \frac{4 \cdot 8000 \cdot 5.333}{11500}$$

$$h^{3} = \frac{170656}{11500} = 14.84$$

$$h = \sqrt[3]{14.84} = 2.45 \;\; \text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{h_{new} \approx 2.45 \;\; \text{mm}}$$

**Step 3:** Verify the new strain with $h_{new} = 2.45$ mm and $\delta_{new} = 1.5$ mm:

$$\varepsilon_{max,new} = \frac{3 \cdot h_{new} \cdot \delta_{new}}{2 \cdot L^{2}} = \frac{3 \cdot 2.45 \cdot 1.5}{2 \cdot 400}$$

$$\varepsilon_{max,new} = \frac{11.025}{800} = 0.01378 \;\; (1.38\%)$$

**Step 4:** Check against allowable:

$$\varepsilon_{allowable} = 0.024 \;\; (2.4\%)$$

Since $0.01378 < 0.024$, the **new design is SAFE** ✅.

**Step 5:** Comment:

Increasing thickness from 1.8 mm to 2.45 mm (a 36% increase) produces a **2.54× rise in stiffness** (cube relationship), which raises the assembly force from 2.1 N to the target 8 N while still keeping the strain well below the limit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in Snap-Fit Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong formula for strain:** Using $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{6 F L}{E b h^{2}}$ instead of the much simpler *deflection-based* form $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{3 h \delta}{2 L^{2}}$. The latter is *geometry-only* and is the **KTU-preferred form**. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the safety factor:** Comparing $\varepsilon_{max}$ directly with $\varepsilon_{break}$ without dividing by SF. Always write: $\varepsilon_{allowable} = \varepsilon_{break} / SF$. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing *N/mm²* with *MPa* inconsistently. **1 MPa = 1 N/mm²** — they are the same unit. (Loss: 1 mark)
>
> 4. **Confusing thickness *h* with width *b*:** In the cantilever formula, $h$ is the dimension *in the direction of bending* (i.e., the thickness perpendicular to the load). Many students plug width where thickness should go. (Loss: 3–4 marks)
>
> 5. **Skipping the ergonomic interpretation:** A numerical answer of just "F = 4.5 N" without stating whether it is suitable for finger assembly will lose 1 mark. Always add a concluding sentence.
>
> 6. **Not stating the type of snap fit:** Always begin your answer by explicitly naming the type (cantilever, annular, cylindrical) and the loading mode.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist — Snap Fits**

- ✅ A **snap fit** uses the *elastic deformation* of a polymer to create a *mechanical interlock* without separate fasteners.

- ✅ **Three main types:** *Cantilever* (bending), *Annular* (hoop), *Cylindrical* (radial compression).

- ✅ **Master Equation ⭐:** $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{3 \cdot h \cdot \delta}{2 \cdot L^{2}}$ — independent of modulus *E*; depends only on geometry!

- ✅ **Stiffness Equation:** $k = \dfrac{E \cdot b \cdot h^{3}}{4 \cdot L^{3}}$ — thickness $h$ has a **cubic** effect; length $L$ has an **inverse-cubic** effect.

- ✅ **Assembly Force:** $F_{asm} = k \cdot \delta$ — target range for finger assembly is **5 N to 20 N**.

- ✅ **Design Rule:** $\varepsilon_{max} \leq \dfrac{\varepsilon_{break}}{SF}$, with typical $SF = 2$ to $4$.

- ✅ **Failure Modes:** *Yielding* (strain exceeds elastic limit), *Fatigue* (cyclic loading), *Stress Relaxation* (long-term holding force decay), *Creep* (under sustained load).

- ✅ **Best Design Lever:** Increase $L$ to reduce strain (linear in $L^{2}$); avoid making $h$ thinner than 0.8 mm (mold-flow issues).

- ✅ **Mold Draft Angle:** Always include **0.5° to 1°** draft on snap-fit side walls for ejector clearance — a *manufacturing* must.

- ✅ **Knit/Weld Line Avoidance:** Place knit lines *away from the snap-fit root* (the high-stress zone) by adjusting gate location.

- ✅ **Material Selection Heuristic:**
  - *Polypropylene (PP)* — low cost, good flexibility (ε_break ≈ 10–15%)
  - *Acetal (POM)* — high stiffness, low friction (ε_break ≈ 5–8%)
  - *Nylon 6,6 (PA66)* — high temperature resistance (ε_break ≈ 6–10%)
  - *ABS* — balanced properties (ε_break ≈ 5–8%)
  - *Polycarbonate (PC)* — high impact, transparent applications (ε_break ≈ 6–10%)

- ✅ **Strain is a pure number** — always express it as a *decimal* (0.04) OR as a *percentage* (4%), and be consistent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Welding** is defined as a **fabrication process** in which two thermoplastic components are joined together by the application of heat, pressure, and/or time, with or without the use of a filler material, to create a permanent molecular bond across the interface. The weld zone is formed when the polymer chains at the joint interface are brought into a viscoelastic state, allowing chain interdiffusion across the boundary, followed by re-solidification under controlled cooling.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCPOT601 – Polymer Products Design & Testing*, welding is treated as a primary **secondary processing technique** (also called a **joining/assembly process**) for thermoplastic polymers, distinct from mechanical fastening and adhesive bonding.

### 1.1.1 Underlying Molecular Mechanism

Welding in thermoplastics is fundamentally a **diffusion-controlled process**. The microscopic bonding arises through three sequential molecular events at the interface:

1. **Surface Rearrangement** – Polymer chains align themselves across the freshly contacted interface.
2. **Surface Approximation** – The two surfaces are brought within a critical reptation distance (typically 1–10 nm).
3. **Chain Interdiffusion (Reptation)** – Following the **de Gennes reptation theory**, polymer chains snake across the interface, entangling with chains on the opposite side. This forms the **interphase**, whose strength increases with welding time and temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Insight:** Unlike metal welding (which involves metallurgical fusion and phase changes), polymer welding involves *physical* flow and diffusion — no melting point in the metallurgical sense, but a transition through the **rubbery/viscoelastic plateau** above the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)** for amorphous polymers or above the **melting temperature ($T_m$)** for semi-crystalline polymers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🧠 Real-World Analogy: The "Chocolate Bar Bonding"**
> Imagine two identical chocolate bars. If you simply press them together at room temperature, they fall apart — because the surface molecules haven't gained enough mobility to interlock. Now, if you warm the joint slightly (just enough to soften, not melt) and press them together firmly, the softened chocolate molecules from each bar intermingle and, upon cooling, become **chemically indistinguishable** at the interface. The two bars are now one continuous solid. Polymer welding is essentially this exact phenomenon — but executed with surgical precision on engineering plastics like **HDPE, PP, PVC, ABS, PC, and PA**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used throughout polymer welding engineering:

- **Weld Temperature ($T_w$):** Typically **$T_g$ + 30 °C to 100 °C** for amorphous polymers; **$T_m$ + 10 °C to 50 °C** for semi-crystalline polymers.
- **Weld Pressure ($P_w$):** Generally in the range of **0.1 MPa to 5 MPa** depending on technique.
- **Weld Time ($t_w$):** From milliseconds (ultrasonic) to several minutes (hot gas).
- **Heat Affected Zone (HAZ):** The region adjacent to the weld where thermal degradation, oxidation, or crystallinity changes occur.
- **Weld Factor ($\eta_w$):** Defined as:
  $$\eta_w = \frac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base material}}}$$
  where $\sigma_{\text{weld}}$ is the strength of the welded joint and $\sigma_{\text{base material}}$ is the parent material strength. A weld factor of **1.0** indicates a perfect weld; commercial welds typically achieve **0.7 to 0.95**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The concept of **weld factor** is a frequently examined 3-mark question and is critical to understanding joint efficiency.

### 1.4 Classification of Polymer Welding Techniques

Polymer welding methods are broadly classified into three categories based on the **heat generation mechanism**:

| Category | Heat Source | Typical Methods |
|---|---|---|
| **External Heating** | External hot tool / hot gas | Hot Plate, Hot Gas, Extrusion |
| **Internal (Frictional) Heating** | Mechanical motion → heat | Ultrasonic, Vibration, Spin, Friction, Orbital |
| **Electromagnetic Heating** | EM field interaction | Induction, Radio Frequency (RF/Dielectric), Laser, Microwave |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Each Major Welding Method

### 2.1.1 Hot Gas Welding (Hot Air Welding)

**Principle:** A stream of **hot inert gas** (typically air or nitrogen at **200–400 °C**) is directed at the joint area between two polymer parts. Simultaneously, a filler rod of the same polymer is fed into the V-groove joint. The heat softens both the parent material and the filler, allowing them to fuse.

**Process Steps:**
1. Joint preparation (V-groove, typically **60° included angle**).
2. Pre-heating of joint area with hot gas.
3. Feeding filler rod under pressure into the molten groove.
4. Solidification under ambient cooling.

**Key Parameters:**
- Gas temperature: **200–400 °C**
- Gas flow rate: **20–60 L/min**
- Welding speed: **5–30 cm/min**
- Filler rod diameter: **3–5 mm**
- Suitable for: **PE, PP, PVC, PVDF**

> [!NOTE]
> **Application Note:** Hot gas welding is widely used for **fabricating large chemical tanks, ducting, and field repairs** of thermoplastic pipes and linings.

### 2.1.2 Hot Plate (Heated Tool) Welding

**Principle:** A precisely temperature-controlled **metallic hot plate** is used to simultaneously heat the two joining surfaces. Once melted, the plate is removed and the parts are pressed together under controlled pressure.

**Process Sequence (4 Stages):**
1. **Match Phase:** Parts are pressed against the hot plate ($P_m \approx 0.1$–$0.5$ MPa).
2. **Heat Soak Phase:** Pressure reduced; surface reaches melt temperature.
3. **Changeover Phase:** Hot plate retracted; parts held apart to minimize cooling.
4. **Forge/Join Phase:** Parts pressed together ($P_f \approx 0.1$–$0.5$ MPa); weld consolidates.

**Key Parameter:** The **Heat Soak Time (HST)** is critical and is empirically related to:
$$\text{HST} \propto \frac{L}{k(T_w - T_s)}$$
where $L$ = wall thickness, $k$ = thermal conductivity, $T_w$ = plate temperature, $T_s$ = surface temperature required for fusion.

### 2.1.3 Ultrasonic Welding

**Principle:** High-frequency (**20–40 kHz**) mechanical vibrations are coupled into the parts through a **sonotrode (horn)**. The cyclic shear at the interface generates frictional heat, melting the polymer. Pressure is maintained during the brief **hold time** to consolidate the weld.

**Key Components:**
- **Piezoelectric Transducer** (converts electrical → mechanical vibration)
- **Booster** (amplifies amplitude)
- **Sonotrode/Horn** (couples vibration to the part, $f = 20$–$40$ kHz, amplitude $= 15$–$100\ \mu m$)
- **Anvil/Fixture**

**Three Mechanisms of Heat Generation:**
1. **Surface Friction** (initial heating)
2. **Viscoelastic Hysteresis** (in amorphous polymers)
3. **Interfacial Shear**

**Heat Generation Equation:**
$$Q = \pi \cdot f \cdot \mu \cdot F_n \cdot A$$
where $f$ = frequency, $\mu$ = coefficient of friction, $F_n$ = normal force, $A$ = amplitude.

**Suitable for:** Small to medium **thermoplastic parts** — **ABS, PP, PS, PA, PC**. Standard method in the **automotive, medical device, and packaging** industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Director:** For rigid amorphous plastics, an **energy director** (a small triangular ridge, typically $0.25$–$0.5$ mm high) is molded onto one part. It concentrates the initial melting and initiates the weld.

### 2.1.4 Vibration Welding

**Principle:** One part is vibrated against the other at **low frequency ($f = 120$–$240$ Hz)** under pressure. Friction generates heat at the interface.

**Two Variants:**
- **Linear Vibration Welding:** Relative motion is linear (amplitude **0.5–2 mm**).
- **Orbital Vibration Welding:** Relative motion is orbital; better for irregular joints.

**Process Phases:**
1. **Solid Friction Phase** ($t_1$) — initial Coulomb friction heating.
2. **Transient Phase** — melting begins; friction drops.
3. **Steady-State Phase** — constant melt film thickness.
4. **Solidification/Hold Phase** — vibration stops; weld solidifies under pressure.

**Total Weld Time Equation:**
$$t_{\text{total}} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_{\text{hold}}$$

### 2.1.5 Spin Welding

**Principle:** One part is rotated at high speed relative to the stationary part. Friction generates heat; rotation is stopped and pressure consolidates the joint.

**Key Parameters:**
- Rotational speed: **1000–4000 RPM**
- Axial pressure: **0.5–2 MPa**
- Suitable for **circular cross-section joints**.

### 2.1.6 Laser Welding

**Principle:** A **laser beam (typically diode laser, $\lambda = 800$–$1000$ nm)** is transmitted through a transparent upper part and absorbed by the lower (carbon-black-filled or IR-absorbing) part, generating localized heat.

**Advantages:** Non-contact, precise, fast (**< 1 s**), clean (no flash).
**Key Parameter:** The **transmission rate of the upper part** must exceed **~30%**, while the lower part must absorb strongly at the laser wavelength.

### 2.1.7 Induction Welding

**Principle:** An **electromagnetic coil** induces eddy currents in a conductive insert (e.g., a metal mesh or carbon-loaded thermoplastic layer) embedded between the two parts. The insert heats and melts the surrounding polymer.

**Frequency:** Typically **1–10 MHz**.
**Suitable for:** Bonding **continuous fiber-reinforced thermoplastics** in aerospace.

### 2.1.8 Radio Frequency (RF/Dielectric) Welding

**Principle:** The parts are placed between two electrodes; an alternating electric field (**27.12 MHz**, the ISM band) causes **dielectric heating** in polar polymers.

**Suitable for:** **PVC, PU, PA** (polar polymers). Commonly used for **medical bags, inflatable products, tarpaulins**.

### 2.1.9 Friction Stir Welding (FSW)

**Principle:** Adapted from metals, a **rotating non-consumable tool** is plunged into the joint line and traverses along it. Frictional and deformation heat softens the polymer and mechanically mixes it to form a solid-state weld.

**Suitable for:** **PE, PP, PA, PEEK** plates.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every equation, parameter, and unit you will need to solve KTU 2024 Scheme numerical and theoretical questions on Welding.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_w = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}}$ | Weld Factor (Joint Efficiency) | Dimensionless (0–1) |
| 2 | $Q = \pi \cdot f \cdot \mu \cdot F_n \cdot A$ | Heat generated per cycle (Ultrasonic) | Joules |
| 3 | $\text{HST} \propto \dfrac{L}{k(T_w - T_s)}$ | Heat Soak Time proportionality | seconds |
| 4 | $\eta_w = 1 - \dfrac{d_h}{L}$ | Empirical weld factor (hole/cavity defect) | Dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\text{ultrasonic}} = F_n \cdot v = F_n \cdot 2\pi f A$ | Ultrasonic power input | Watts |
| 6 | $t_{\text{weld}} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3 + t_h$ | Total vibration weld time | seconds |
| 7 | $T_w = T_m + (10 \text{ to } 50)\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Weld temperature (semi-crystalline) | °C |
| 8 | $T_w = T_g + (30 \text{ to } 100)\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | Weld temperature (amorphous) | °C |
| 9 | $A_{\text{horn}} = A_{\text{transducer}} \cdot \dfrac{d_{\text{transducer}}}{d_{\text{horn}}}$ | Horn amplification ratio | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\sigma_{\text{peel}} = \dfrac{F}{b}$ | Peel strength of weld | N/mm |
| 11 | $\sigma_{\text{shear}} = \dfrac{F}{A_j}$ | Shear strength of weld | MPa |
| 12 | $V_{\text{collapse}} = \Delta h \cdot A_j$ | Weld collapse volume | mm³ |
| 13 | $T_{\text{interface}}(t) = T_s + (T_w - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\dfrac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$ | 1-D transient conduction at interface | °C |
| 14 | $W_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot s_{\text{relative}}$ | Frictional work input | Joules |
| 15 | $\text{Weld quality} \propto \sqrt{D \cdot t_w}$ | Reptation diffusion-controlled strength | – |

**Notation Key:**
- $\sigma$ = Stress (MPa), $f$ = Frequency (Hz), $\mu$ = Coefficient of friction, $F_n$ = Normal force (N), $A$ = Amplitude ($\mu$m)
- $L$ = Wall thickness (mm), $k$ = Thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $\alpha$ = Thermal diffusivity (m²/s)
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient (m²/s), $t_w$ = Weld time (s)

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility and Real-World Applications

| Industry | Typical Welding Method | Components Joined |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Ultrasonic, Vibration, Hot Plate | Dashboards, bumpers, air intake manifolds, battery housings |
| **Medical Devices** | Ultrasonic, Laser, RF | Blood filters, IV components, surgical instrument housings |
| **Packaging** | Ultrasonic, Impulse Heat Sealing | Blister packs, food packaging, pouches |
| **Chemical Processing** | Hot Gas, Extrusion Welding | Storage tanks, pipe linings, ducting |
| **Aerospace** | Induction, Friction Stir | Composite panels, ducts, structural components |
| **Consumer Electronics** | Laser, Ultrasonic | Smartphone housings, laptop shells |
| **Plumbing** | Socket Fusion, Butt Fusion | HDPE/PP pipes for water and gas distribution |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Weld Factor Equation

We begin with the fundamental definition of joint efficiency.

**Step 1 — Define the load-bearing area of the welded joint and base material.**

For a single-lap or butt weld configuration, the maximum sustainable tensile load is the strength multiplied by the cross-sectional area:

$$F_{\max} = \sigma \cdot A$$

**Step 2 — Write the expressions for the welded and base specimens.**

$$F_{\text{weld}} = \sigma_{\text{weld}} \cdot A_{\text{weld}}$$
$$F_{\text{base}} = \sigma_{\text{base}} \cdot A_{\text{base}}$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio (this is the formal definition of weld factor).**

$$\eta_w = \frac{F_{\text{weld}}}{F_{\text{base}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify (assuming identical cross-sectional areas, $A_{\text{weld}} = A_{\text{base}}$).**

$$\eta_w = \frac{\sigma_{\text{weld}} \cdot A_{\text{base}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}} \cdot A_{\text{base}}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\eta_w = \frac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}}}$$

> This is dimensionless and lies in the closed interval **[0, 1]**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Heat Generated in Ultrasonic Welding

We derive the instantaneous heat generation rate at the weld interface.

**Step 1 — A vibrating horn tip oscillates sinusoidally.**

The displacement of the horn tip is given by:

$$x(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$$

where $A$ is the peak amplitude and $f$ is the frequency.

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain velocity.**

$$v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = 2\pi f A \cdot \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 — The peak velocity is therefore $v_{\text{peak}} = 2\pi f A$.**

**Step 4 — The instantaneous frictional force at the interface.**

The Coulomb friction model gives:

$$F_{\text{friction}}(t) = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot \text{sgn}\!\left(v(t)\right)$$

**Step 5 — Instantaneous power dissipated as heat.**

$$P(t) = F_{\text{friction}}(t) \cdot v(t) = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot \vert v(t) \vert$$

**Step 6 — Time-average the absolute value over one cycle.**

Since the time-average of $\vert \cos(2\pi f t) \vert$ over a complete period is $\frac{2}{\pi}$:

$$\langle P \rangle = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot \langle \vert v(t) \vert \rangle = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot \frac{2}{\pi} \cdot 2\pi f A$$

**Step 7 — Simplify.**

$$\boxed{\langle P \rangle = 4 \mu F_n f A}$$

**Step 8 — Energy per cycle.**

$$Q_{\text{cycle}} = \langle P \rangle \cdot \frac{1}{f} = 4 \mu F_n A$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If your exam question gives $\pi$ instead of $4$ in the formula, the derivation assumes a **signed** (not absolute) averaging. For KTU purposes, both forms are acceptable; just state your assumption clearly.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Heat-Soak Time (HST) for Hot Plate Welding

**Step 1 — The 1-D transient heat conduction equation at a semi-infinite interface.**

$$T(x,t) = T_s + (T_w - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{x}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

where $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ is thermal diffusivity.

**Step 2 — We require the surface ($x = 0$) to reach the fusion temperature $T_f$.**

$$T_f = T_s + (T_w - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}(0) = T_s$$

This is the initial condition — we need a non-zero surface displacement $x$ to define a melt layer of thickness $\delta$. We require:

$$T(\delta, t_h) = T_f$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $t_h$.**

$$T_f - T_s = (T_w - T_s) \cdot \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}}\right)$$

$$\text{erf}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}}\right) = \frac{T_f - T_s}{T_w - T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Invert the error function.**

$$\frac{\delta}{2\sqrt{\alpha t_h}} = \text{erf}^{-1}\!\left(\frac{T_f - T_s}{T_w - T_s}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $t_h$ (Heat Soak Time).**

$$\boxed{t_h = \frac{\delta^2}{4 \alpha \cdot \left[\text{erf}^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{T_f - T_s}{T_w - T_s}\right)\right]^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Practical approximation.**

For small melt-layer thickness $\delta$, the result reduces to:

$$t_h \approx \frac{L^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{T_w - T_s}{T_w - T_f}\right)$$

where $L$ is the wall thickness. This is the form most often quoted in textbooks.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:** Two HDPE sheets (each 4 mm thick) are joined by hot plate welding. The plate temperature is $T_w = 220\ ^\circ$C, ambient temperature is $T_s = 25\ ^\circ$C, and fusion temperature is $T_f = 140\ ^\circ$C. The thermal diffusivity of HDPE is $\alpha = 0.18 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s. Estimate the heat-soak time required to form a melt layer of thickness $\delta = 0.2$ mm at the interface.

**Step 1 — Calculate the argument of the inverse error function.**

$$\frac{T_f - T_s}{T_w - T_s} = \frac{140 - 25}{220 - 25} = \frac{115}{195} = 0.5897$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the inverse error function.**

$$\text{erf}^{-1}(0.5897) \approx 0.589$$

(From standard tables, since $\text{erf}(0.589) \approx 0.589$.)

**Step 3 — Apply the formula.**

$$t_h = \frac{\delta^2}{4 \alpha \cdot [\text{erf}^{-1}(\cdot)]^2}$$

Convert $\delta = 0.2$ mm $= 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$t_h = \frac{(0.2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4 \times 0.18 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.589)^2}$$

$$t_h = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{4 \times 0.18 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.347}$$

$$t_h = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{2.498 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$t_h \approx 0.160\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

A melt layer of **0.2 mm** forms in approximately **0.16 seconds** at the interface — explaining why hot plate welding of HDPE is a fast, production-suitable process.

> [!NOTE]
> In real industrial practice, an additional **changeover time** (~1–3 s) is added to account for the hot plate retraction phase, during which the melt begins to cool. The total cycle therefore becomes **$t_{\text{total}} = t_h + t_{\text{changeover}} + t_{\text{forge}}$**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Welding Methods

| Method | Cycle Time | Material Suitability | Joint Strength ($\eta_w$) | Typical Applications | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Plate | 5–60 s | All thermoplastics | **0.85–0.95** | Pipes, tanks, automotive | Medium |
| Hot Gas | 1–10 min | PE, PP, PVC | **0.6–0.8** | Field repairs, tanks | Low |
| Ultrasonic | 0.1–2 s | Amorphous thermoplastics | **0.7–0.9** | Small parts, packaging | Medium |
| Vibration | 1–10 s | Most thermoplastics | **0.85–0.95** | Large irregular parts | High |
| Spin | 0.5–3 s | Circular joints | **0.85–0.95** | Filters, bottles | Medium |
| Laser | < 1 s | Transparent + absorbing pairs | **0.9–0.98** | Electronics, medical | High |
| Induction | 1–10 s | Composites with inserts | **0.85–0.95** | Aerospace composites | High |
| RF/Dielectric | 1–10 s | Polar polymers | **0.8–0.95** | PVC/PU film products | Medium |
| Friction Stir | 10–60 s | Plates of PE, PP, PEEK | **0.7–0.9** | Plates, structural | High |
| Solvent | Hours | Amorphous, solvent-weldable | **0.8–0.95** | PVC pipes, acrylics | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of an Ultrasonic Welding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psu["PSU - 20 kHz Power Supply"]
    pz["Piezoelectric Transducer"]
    bst["Booster - Amplifier"]
    snt["Sonotrode Horn - Coupler"]
    jnt["Weld Joint Interface"]
    partA["Upper Polymer Part"]
    partB["Lower Polymer Part"]
    anv["Anvil - Fixture"]
    ctrl["PLC - Process Controller"]

    psu --> pz
    pz --> bst
    bst --> snt
    snt --> jnt
    partA --> jnt
    jnt --> partB
    anv --> partB
    ctrl -.-> psu
    ctrl -.-> snt
    jnt -.-> ctrl
    ctrl --> out["Weld Quality Data Logger"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram captures the **signal and force flow** from the power supply to the joint. The dashed lines indicate **control feedback loops**, which are essential for closed-loop welding systems.

---

## 4.2 Hot Plate Welding - Process State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Load
    Load --> MatchPhase
    MatchPhase --> HeatSoakPhase
    HeatSoakPhase --> ChangeoverPhase
    ChangeoverPhase --> ForgePhase
    ForgePhase --> HoldPhase
    HoldPhase --> Unload
    Unload --> [*]

    note right of MatchPhase: Parts contact plate; pressure Pm
    note right of HeatSoakPhase: Melt layer grows; thickness delta
    note right of ChangeoverPhase: Plate retracts; melt cools
    note right of ForgePhase: Parts pressed; pressure Pf
    note right of HoldPhase: Cooling under pressure
```

---

## 4.3 Vibration Welding - Process Phase Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Weld Start - t equals 0"]
    phase1["Phase 1 - Solid Friction - Heat builds via Coulomb friction"]
    phase2["Phase 2 - Transient - Melt initiates; friction drops"]
    phase3["Phase 3 - Steady State - Constant melt film"]
    phase4["Phase 4 - Solidification Hold - Vibration stops"]
    end["Weld Complete"]

    start --> phase1
    phase1 --> phase2
    phase2 --> phase3
    phase3 --> phase4
    phase4 --> end

    p1["Parameter: t1 typically 0.1 to 0.5 s"]
    p2["Parameter: t2 typically 0.2 to 1 s"]
    p3["Parameter: t3 typically 0.5 to 3 s"]
    p4["Parameter: th typically 0.5 to 2 s"]

    phase1 -.- p1
    phase2 -.- p2
    phase3 -.- p3
    phase4 -.- p4
```

---

## 4.4 Classification Tree of Polymer Welding Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Polymer Welding Methods"]

    root --> ext["External Heating Category"]
    root --> fric["Frictional Heating Category"]
    root --> em["Electromagnetic Heating Category"]

    ext --> hp["Hot Plate Welding"]
    ext --> hg["Hot Gas Welding"]
    ext --> ex["Extrusion Welding"]

    fric --> us["Ultrasonic Welding"]
    fric --> vib["Vibration Welding"]
    fric --> sp["Spin Welding"]
    fric --> fsw["Friction Stir Welding"]

    em --> ind["Induction Welding"]
    em --> rf["RF Dielectric Welding"]
    em --> las["Laser Welding"]
    em --> mw["Microwave Welding"]
```

---

## 4.5 Bond Strength vs. Weld Time - Reptation Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Time t equals 0 - No Contact"]
    B["Time t1 - Surface Contact Established"]
    C["Time t2 - Chain End Migration Begins"]
    D["Time tR - Reptation Time Achieved"]
    E["Time t greater than tR - Full Strength Plateau"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E

    sg["Sigma Weld vs tW - Square Root Growth"]
    pl["Sigma Base - Parent Material Strength"]

    E -.-> sg
    sg -.-> pl
    pl -.- note["Weld factor eta approaches 1 over time"]

    style sg fill:#FFE5B4
    style pl fill:#C1E1C1
    style note fill:#F0F0F0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The figure illustrates the **de Gennes reptation model** prediction: weld strength grows with the square root of weld time, $\sigma_{\text{weld}} \propto \sqrt{t_w}$, until it plateaus at the parent material strength. The characteristic time scale is the **reptation time $t_R$**, which decreases exponentially with temperature above $T_g$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Weld Factor" in polymer welding. What is its significance in evaluating welded joints?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **weld factor ($\eta_w$)** is defined as the ratio of the strength of the welded joint to the strength of the base (parent) material:

$$\eta_w = \frac{\sigma_{\text{weld}}}{\sigma_{\text{base}}}$$

It is **dimensionless** and lies between 0 and 1. A weld factor of **1.0** indicates a weld as strong as the parent material (ideal weld); typical industrial welds achieve **0.7 to 0.95**. The weld factor is significant because it provides a direct measure of **joint efficiency**, enabling engineers to compare welding methods, optimize process parameters, and verify structural adequacy in design. *(3 marks)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Hot Plate Welding and Ultrasonic Welding of thermoplastics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hot Plate Welding | Ultrasonic Welding |
|---|---|---|
| Heat Source | Externally heated metallic plate | High-frequency mechanical vibration |
| Cycle Time | 5–60 s | 0.1–2 s |
| Heat Generation | Conduction from plate | Friction + viscoelastic hysteresis |
| Joint Type | Large planar or pipe joints | Small to medium parts |
| Energy Director | Not required | Often required |

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **Ultrasonic Welding** process for thermoplastics. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the role of the **sonotrode**, **booster**, **transducer**, and **energy director**.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Ultrasonic welding is a **frictional welding** technique in which high-frequency mechanical vibrations ($f = 20$–$40$ kHz) are used to generate localized heat at the joint interface, melting and fusing thermoplastic parts without external heat input.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** The ultrasonic generator converts **50 Hz mains** electrical energy into **high-frequency electrical signal**, which is fed to a **piezoelectric transducer**. The transducer converts this electrical signal into **mechanical vibration** at the ultrasonic frequency. A **booster** amplifies (or attenuates) the vibration amplitude, which is then transmitted to the part through the **sonotrode (horn)**. Under clamping pressure, the vibrating surface rubs against the stationary mating part, generating heat through (i) surface friction and (ii) viscoelastic hysteresis. The polymer melts at the interface; the melt is squeezed out as a flash, and on solidification a strong weld forms.

**Role of Components (3 marks):**
- **Piezoelectric Transducer:** Converts electrical → mechanical (vibratory) energy using piezoelectric crystals.
- **Booster:** Mechanically amplifies the vibration amplitude (typical gain: 1:1 to 1:3).
- **Sonotrode/Horn:** Designed to deliver vibrations to a specific joint geometry. Made from **aluminum or titanium**, it is tuned to resonate at the operating frequency.
- **Energy Director:** A small molded triangular ridge ($0.25$–$0.5$ mm height) on the upper part that **concentrates** the initial energy input and ensures consistent, rapid melting initiation.

**Applications (1 mark):** Used widely in **automotive (interior trim), medical devices, packaging, and consumer electronics** for joining small thermoplastic parts.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **defects** encountered in polymer welds. How are they tested?

**Model Answer:**

**Common Weld Defects (4 marks):**

1. **Lack of Fusion (Cold Weld):** Insufficient heat or pressure; weld factor drops to 0.3–0.6. Common in hot gas and ultrasonic welding.
2. **Voids and Porosity:** Trapped air or volatile degradation products at the weld interface.
3. **Flash (Squeeze-out):** Excessive melt is extruded from the joint, leaving a flash bead. Indicates over-melting or excessive pressure.
4. **Thermal Degradation:** Polymer overheating causes chain scission, oxidation, and discoloration. Common in PVC and PEEK.
5. **Weld-Line Displacement:** Mismatch of weld path leading to stress concentration.
6. **Crystallinity Variation (in semi-crystalline polymers):** Rapid cooling creates low-crystallinity weld zones, weakening the joint.
7. **Contamination:** Oil, dust, or mold release agent at the interface prevents fusion.

**Testing Methods (3 marks):**

- **Destructive Testing:**
  - **Tensile Test (ASTM D638):** Measures weld strength in pure tension.
  - **Lap Shear Test (ASTM D3163):** Measures shear strength of lap joints.
  - **Peel Test (ASTM D1876):** Measures resistance to peel forces.
  - **Impact Test (ASTM D256):** Measures toughness of the weld zone.
- **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):**
  - **Ultrasonic C-Scan:** Detects voids and lack of fusion.
  - **X-ray Radiography:** Reveals internal voids and inclusions.
  - **Visual Inspection:** Detects surface defects and flash.
  - **Leak Testing (Bubble Test or Pressure Test):** For fluid containers.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Vibration Welding** process. Compare **linear vibration welding** and **orbital vibration welding** with reference to their suitability, motion, and applications.

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Vibration welding is a **frictional welding process** in which one of the two parts is vibrated against the other under pressure at low frequency ($f = 120$–$240$ Hz, amplitude $= 0.5$–$2$ mm). The resulting frictional heat melts the polymer interface, and consolidation under pressure forms the weld.

**Process Stages (2 marks):**
1. **Solid Friction Phase:** Initial contact at asperities; rapid heating.
2. **Transition Phase:** Asperities melt; friction coefficient drops.
3. **Steady-State Phase:** Constant melt film thickness; controlled collapse.
4. **Solidification Phase:** Vibration stops; pressure maintained; weld solidifies.

**Comparison (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Linear Vibration | Orbital Vibration |
|---|---|---|
| Motion Type | Linear (one direction) | Orbital (closed-loop elliptical) |
| Frequency | 120–240 Hz | 100–240 Hz |
| Amplitude | 0.5–2 mm | 0.5–3 mm |
| Joint Geometry | Flat or slightly curved, rectangular | Complex, irregular, 3D |
| Pressure Distribution | May be uneven at corners | Uniform |
| Cycle Time | 1–5 s | 2–10 s |
| Application | Auto dashboards, bumpers | Complex 3D parts, manifolds |

**Advantages (1 mark):** Suitable for **large irregular parts**; high weld factor (**0.85–0.95**); no special surface preparation; works with filled and reinforced thermoplastics.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

**(b) [7 Marks]** Describe **Laser Welding** and **Induction Welding** of polymers. List the advantages and limitations of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Laser Welding (3.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** A diode laser beam ($\lambda = 800$–$1000$ nm) is directed at the joint. The **upper (transparent) part** transmits the beam; the **lower (absorbing) part**, typically filled with carbon black or a near-infrared absorber, converts the beam to heat, melting the interface.
- **Process Variants:** Transmission welding (most common), contour welding, simultaneous welding, mask welding.
- **Advantages:** Non-contact, high precision (sub-mm), no flash/particulate, cycle time < 1 s, suitable for medical/electronics.
- **Limitations:** Requires optical access; one part must be transparent and the other absorbing; high capital cost.

**Induction Welding (3.5 marks):**
- **Principle:** A high-frequency alternating current (typically 1–10 MHz) flows through a coil, generating an alternating magnetic field. A conductive insert (metal mesh, carbon-loaded polymer) embedded at the joint absorbs the energy via **eddy current losses**, heating and melting the surrounding polymer.
- **Process Variants:** Implant induction welding (mesh insert), and direct induction welding (carbon-filled thermoplastics).
- **Advantages:** Fast, localized heating, suitable for **continuous fiber-reinforced thermoplastics** (aerospace), repeatable.
- **Limitations:** Requires conductive insert; insert cost; limited to specific joint designs.

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse $T_g$ and $T_m$.** Amorphous polymers weld above $T_g$; semi-crystalline polymers weld above $T_m$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Always specify units** ($^\circ$C, MPa, Hz). KTU answer scripts without units often receive partial credit only.
> 3. **Reptation vs. simple melting:** Do not state that welding occurs due to "melting" alone. The correct answer involves **interfacial chain diffusion (reptation)** — a 1-mark distinction that examiners prize.
> 4. **Energy Director** is often omitted by students. Always mention it in ultrasonic welding answers.
> 5. **Weld Factor** should be expressed as a **ratio or percentage**, not as an absolute stress value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Welding (Module 2)**

- ✅ **Welding = molecular-level fusion** via chain interdiffusion (reptation), not just melting.
- ✅ **Weld Factor** $\eta_w = \sigma_{\text{weld}} / \sigma_{\text{base}}$; ideal = 1.0; typical = 0.7–0.95.
- ✅ **Three Categories:** External Heating, Frictional Heating, Electromagnetic Heating.
- ✅ **Amorphous polymers** weld above $T_g$; **semi-crystalline** above $T_m$.
- ✅ **Hot Plate Welding** has 4 stages: Match → Heat Soak → Changeover → Forge.
- ✅ **Ultrasonic Welding** frequency = 20–40 kHz; amplitude = 15–100 $\mu$m; uses **energy director**.
- ✅ **Vibration Welding** frequency = 120–240 Hz; 4 phases of melting; suitable for large irregular parts.
- ✅ **Spin Welding** for circular joints; rotation = 1000–4000 RPM.
- ✅ **Laser Welding** needs transparent upper + absorbing lower part; uses diode laser $\lambda \approx 808$–$940$ nm.
- ✅ **Induction Welding** uses eddy current heating of an embedded insert.
- ✅ **RF Welding** frequency = 27.12 MHz; works only for **polar polymers** (PVC, PA).
- ✅ **Friction Stir Welding** is solid-state; uses a non-consumable rotating tool.
- ✅ **Heat generation in ultrasonic welding:** $Q = \pi \mu F_n f A$ per cycle (commonly $4\mu F_n A$ using full-cycle averaging).
- ✅ **Heat Soak Time** in hot plate welding: $t_h \propto L^2 / [\alpha \ln(\Delta T)]$.
- ✅ **Common defects:** Lack of fusion, voids, flash, thermal degradation, crystallinity variation.
- ✅ **NDT methods:** Ultrasonic C-scan, X-ray radiography, leak testing.
- ✅ **Weld strength grows as $\sigma \propto \sqrt{D \cdot t_w}$** per reptation theory.
- ✅ **Industrial applications:** Automotive (hot plate, ultrasonic), medical (laser, RF), aerospace (induction, FSW), packaging (ultrasonic).
- ✅ **Cycle time:** Ultrasonic < Vibration < Hot Plate < Hot Gas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles of Plastic Products - Gears
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Principles of Plastic Products - Gears

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **plastic gear** is a power-transmission gear manufactured predominantly from polymeric (thermoplastic or thermoset) materials, in which the load-bearing tooth geometry, hub, and web are produced as a single integrated part through molding (injection, compression, or extrusion) or machining from a polymer stock. Per KTU 2024 Scheme Module-3 syllabus, the **design of plastic gears** is treated as a specialized case of **machine-element design** in which classical metal-gear equations (Lewis, Buckingham, AGMA) are modified to account for the **viscoelastic, temperature-sensitive, and moisture-sensitive** behavior of polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Plastic gears are gear elements in which polymeric materials replace conventional metals. Their design requires modification of standard gear equations because polymer properties (modulus, strength, fatigue endurance, thermal expansion) are significantly different from metals and are functions of temperature, strain-rate, and environmental conditions.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wooden comb** (representing a metal gear) versus a **soft plastic comb** (representing a plastic gear). Both have teeth, but:
- The wooden comb has **stiff, sharp, brittle teeth** — they can carry high instantaneous load but break suddenly if overloaded.
- The plastic comb's teeth are **flexible, forgiving, and damp vibrations** — they can bend slightly under load, absorb shock, run quietly, but will deform permanently if pushed too far.

This single analogy captures the entire engineering philosophy behind plastic gear design: **flexibility is a feature, but deformation is a limit**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** In metal gears, the limiting factor is usually **fatigue failure** (sudden). In plastic gears, the limiting factor is usually **progressive deformation** (gradual wear, creep, and tooth deflection).

### 1.3 Advantages and Limitations of Plastic Gears

| **Property** | **Plastic Gear** | **Metal Gear** |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | **1.0–1.6 g/cm³** (lightweight) | 7.8 g/cm³ (steel) |
| **Modulus of Elasticity, E** | **1–5 GPa** (POM, Nylon) | 200 GPa (steel) |
| **Self-Lubrication** | **Yes** (intrinsic low friction) | No (requires external lubrication) |
| **Corrosion Resistance** | **Excellent** | Poor (requires plating/coating) |
| **Noise Level (dB)** | **Low** (60–70 dB typical) | High (80–95 dB typical) |
| **Cost per Part (high volume)** | **Low** (single molding step) | High (machining + finishing) |
| **Operating Temperature** | **–40 °C to +200 °C** (material-dependent) | Up to +500 °C |
| **Power Transmission Capacity** | Low to medium | High |
| **Dimensional Stability** | Sensitive to moisture/temp | Excellent |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Student Mistake:** Treating plastic gear design as a direct substitution of metal equations. **This is wrong.** The entire derivation must be re-visited because **E (modulus)**, **strength**, and **failure mode** are fundamentally different.

### 1.4 Common Plastic Materials for Gears

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Yield KTU Material Table:**

- **POM (Acetal / Delrin®)** — *Workhorse material.* E ≈ 3 GPa, σ_t ≈ 70 MPa, low friction, low moisture absorption (~0.2%).
- **Nylon 6 / 66 (PA)** — High toughness, good wear, but **moisture absorption up to 9%** (dimensional change).
- **Polycarbonate (PC)** — High impact strength, transparent, used in instrumentation gears.
- **PBT / PET** — Good dimensional stability, used in automotive.
- **PPS (Polyphenylene Sulphide)** — High-temp (up to 200 °C), chemical resistant.
- **PEEK** — Premium polymer for aerospace/medical, E ≈ 3.6 GPa, σ_t ≈ 100 MPa.
- **Glass-Fiber Reinforced variants** — POM + 30% GF increases E to ~10 GPa, σ_t to ~130 MPa.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Plastic Gear Design Differs from Metal Gear Design

The classical metal-gear design theory (Lewis, 1893) was developed assuming:
1. Elastic, homogeneous, isotropic material → Polymer is **viscoelastic** and **non-linear**.
2. Constant modulus → Polymer modulus varies with **temperature**, **strain-rate**, and **moisture**.
3. Tooth acts as a cantilever beam → Polymer tooth **deforms under load sharing** differently.
4. Failure by fatigue cracking → Polymer failure is by **wear, creep, and progressive tooth deflection**.

Therefore, **every term in the Lewis and Buckingham equations must be re-interpreted**.

### 2.2 Design Procedure for Plastic Spur Gears (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Define Requirements**
Determine transmitted power **P (kW)**, pinion speed **N (rpm)**, service life, operating temperature, environment (humidity, chemicals), and gear ratio.

**Step 2 — Select Material**
Choose based on E, σ_allowable, wear resistance, thermal limit, and cost.

**Step 3 — Establish Geometry**
Choose module **m**, number of pinion teeth **z₁**, gear teeth **z₂**, face width **F**, pressure angle **φ** (typically 20° for plastic gears, sometimes 14.5° for low-noise applications).

**Step 4 — Bending Stress Analysis (Modified Lewis)**
Calculate **σ_b** and compare with allowable bending stress σ_b,allow.

**Step 5 — Wear / Surface Fatigue Analysis (Modified Buckingham)**
Calculate **σ_w** and compare with allowable wear stress σ_w,allow.

**Step 6 — Tooth Deflection Check**
Calculate **δ** (tooth tip deflection) and compare with **0.1 × m** (acceptable limit).
