**Step 7 — Apply Safety Factor**
Typical FS = **2.0 to 4.0** for plastic gears (vs 1.5 for steel).

**Step 8 — Molding Considerations**
Check gate location, weld-line position, shrinkage (~1.5–3%), and molded-in stress.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Below is the COMPLETE KTU Module-3 Gear Formula Sheet — memorize these equations.**

| **#** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d = m \cdot z$ | Pitch diameter (d) from module (m) and teeth (z) | mm |
| 2 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60000}$ | Pitch-line velocity (v) | m/s |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{60000 \cdot P}{2\pi \cdot N}$ | Torque (T) from Power (P, kW) and rpm (N) | N·m |
| 4 | $F_t = \dfrac{2 \cdot T}{d}$ | Tangential load at pitch circle | N |
| 5 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}$ | **Modified Lewis Bending Stress Equation** | MPa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{b,allow} = \dfrac{S_b}{F.S.}$ | Allowable bending stress with safety factor | MPa |
| 7 | $\sigma_w = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_t \cdot K_E}{F \cdot d \cdot \sin\phi}}$ | **Modified Buckingham Wear Stress Equation** | MPa |
| 8 | $C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}}$ | Elastic coefficient (plastic-plastic pair) | $\sqrt{\text{MPa}}$ |
| 9 | $\delta = \dfrac{F_t \cdot \cos\phi}{E \cdot F} \cdot \left(\dfrac{h^3}{I}\right)$ | Tooth deflection | mm |
| 10 | $\dfrac{h^3}{I} = \dfrac{12 \cdot \cos\phi}{m}$ | Geometric ratio for involute tooth | mm$^{-1}$ |
| 11 | $K_w = \dfrac{F_t}{F \cdot d}$ | Wear factor (load capacity metric) | N/mm² |
| 12 | $\text{F.S.} = \dfrac{\sigma_{allow}}{\sigma_{actual}}$ | Factor of Safety (dimensionless) | – |

### 2.4 Where Each Symbol Comes From

- $F_t$ = Tangential tooth load (N)
- $K_a$ = Application factor (1.0 to 1.75)
- $K_m$ = Material factor (1.0 to 1.5)
- $K_t$ = Temperature factor (1.0 at 20 °C, up to 4.0 at 100 °C for POM)
- $F$ = Face width (mm)
- $m$ = Module (mm)
- $Y$ = Lewis form factor (0.30 to 0.485 for plastic gears at 20° PA)
- $Y_J$ = Geometry factor (AGMA, accounts for fillet radius)
- $C_p$ = Elastic coefficient
- $K_E$ = Load-sharing / dynamic factor
- $\phi$ = Pressure angle (20° standard)
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio (≈ 0.35 for polymers)
- $E$ = Modulus of elasticity (GPa)
- $K_w$ = Wear factor (N/mm²)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plastic gear design equations are critical in **automotive HVAC blowers, fuel pumps, printers (paper-feed), washing machine transmissions, power tools, and medical pumps** — anywhere quiet, light-weight, self-lubricated gears are needed at moderate loads. AGMA 2003-D10 specifically publishes **plastic gear ratings** based on these modified equations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Modified Lewis Bending Stress Equation

#### 3.1.1 Starting from Classical Lewis Equation

The original Lewis (1893) equation for a gear tooth modeled as a **cantilever beam**:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot P}{F \cdot Y}
$$

where $P$ is the diametral pitch (in$^{-1}$) for inch-based gears.

In **SI metric units**, diametral pitch $P$ is replaced by **module** $m$, and the equation becomes:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

This is derived from beam-bending theory: $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$, with $M = F_t \cdot h$ (moment about tooth root), and $c/I = P/Y$.

#### 3.1.2 Modification for Plastic Gears

Because polymer materials:
- Have **temperature-dependent modulus**
- Have **strain-rate-dependent strength**
- Suffer from **moisture-induced softening** (Nylon, POM)
- Experience **load distribution changes** across the face width

Three correction factors are introduced: $K_a$, $K_m$, $K_t$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_b &= \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y} \\
\text{where:}\quad K_a &= \text{application factor (shock + uniformity of load)} \\
K_m &= \text{material factor (manufacturing quality, isotropy)} \\
K_t &= \text{temperature factor}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of modification:**
- $K_a$ accounts for **shock loads and non-uniform loading** typical of polymer driving mechanisms (e.g., printer paper feed).
- $K_m$ accounts for **variations in molded gears** (weld lines, voids, anisotropy).
- $K_t$ captures the **drop in modulus** as temperature rises (e.g., for POM at 80 °C, E drops by ~40%).

#### 3.1.3 Form Factor Y for Plastic Gears

The form factor $Y$ for a 20° full-depth involute tooth is **NOT identical to metal values**. For plastics, modified $Y$ values (from Buckingham and used in AGMA 2003):

| Number of Teeth (z) | $Y$ (Plastic, 20° PA) |
|---|---|
| 20 | 0.320 |
| 25 | 0.355 |
| 30 | 0.385 |
| 40 | 0.430 |
| 50 | 0.460 |

### 3.2 Derivation of Modified Buckingham Wear Equation

#### 3.2.1 Hertzian Contact Theory Foundation

For two cylinders in contact (line contact, simulating gear teeth), **Hertz's contact stress** is:

$$
\sigma_c^2 = \dfrac{F_t \cdot E^*}{\pi \cdot F \cdot \rho_t}
$$

where $E^*$ is the **composite modulus** and $\rho_t$ is the **equivalent radius of curvature** at the contact point.

#### 3.2.2 Composite Modulus for Plastic-Plastic Pair

For two polymer bodies (or plastic-metal):

$$
\dfrac{1}{E^*} = \dfrac{1-\nu_1^2}{E_1} + \dfrac{1-\nu_2^2}{E_2}
$$

For identical plastic-on-plastic ($E_1 = E_2 = E$, $\nu_1 = \nu_2 = \nu$):

$$
E^* = \dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}
$$

Defining the **elastic coefficient**:

$$
C_p = \sqrt{E^*} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}}
$$

#### 3.2.3 Geometry of Curvature

For involute gears, the equivalent radius of curvature $\rho_t$ at the pitch point relates to gear geometry:

$$
\rho_t = \dfrac{d \cdot \sin\phi}{2} \cdot \dfrac{i+1}{i}
$$

where $i = z_2/z_1$ is the gear ratio. For $i = 1$ (equal gears), this simplifies to $\rho_t = d \cdot \sin\phi$.

#### 3.2.4 Final Wear Equation (Modified Buckingham)

Substituting into the Hertzian formula and including load-distribution factor $K_E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_w^2 &= \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_E \cdot E^*}{F \cdot d \cdot \sin\phi} \\
\therefore \sigma_w &= C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_t \cdot K_E}{F \cdot d \cdot \sin\phi}} \\
\text{where}\quad C_p &= \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}}\ \text{(plastic-plastic)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Significance:** This equation shows that the **wear stress is inversely proportional to $\sqrt{d}$**. So increasing pitch diameter reduces wear stress — opposite to bending stress behavior.

### 3.3 Derivation of Tooth Deflection Equation

A plastic gear tooth bends under load. Excessive deflection causes **incomplete meshing**, **noise**, and **load redistribution**.

For a cantilever (the tooth) with load at the tip:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{F_t \cdot \cos\phi \cdot h^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}
$$

For an involute gear tooth, the geometric ratio $h^3/I$ has been derived as:

$$
\dfrac{h^3}{I} = \dfrac{12 \cdot \cos\phi}{m}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{F_t \cdot \cos\phi}{E \cdot F} \cdot \left(\dfrac{12 \cdot \cos\phi}{m}\right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{3}
$$

Simplifying:

$$
\delta = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_t \cdot \cos^2\phi}{E \cdot F \cdot m}
$$

**Acceptable limit:** $\delta \leq 0.1 \cdot m$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Exam Tip:** For plastic gears, deflection often becomes the **governing criterion** before bending stress or wear. Always check $\delta$!

### 3.4 Power and Torque Relationship (Auxiliary Derivation)

Power transmitted $P$ (kW) at angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s):

$$
P = T \cdot \omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N \cdot T}{60000}
$$

Tangential force at pitch circle:

$$
F_t = \dfrac{2 T}{d} = \dfrac{2 T}{m \cdot z}
$$

Pitch-line velocity:

$$
v = \omega \cdot r = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}{60000}
$$

Useful relation for quick substitution:

$$
F_t = \dfrac{60000 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot d \cdot N} = \dfrac{60000 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot m \cdot z \cdot N}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Plastic Gear Design Methodology Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Start: Define Requirements] --> A2{Operating Conditions}
    A2 --> A3[Power P kW]
    A2 --> A4[Speed N rpm]
    A2 --> A5[Temperature T]
    A2 --> A6[Environment Humidity / Chemicals]
    A3 --> A7[Select Material]
    A4 --> A7
    A5 --> A7
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[Choose Module m]
    A8 --> A9[Choose Teeth z1, z2]
    A9 --> A10[Choose Face Width F]
    A10 --> A11[Compute Tangential Load Ft]
    A11 --> A12[Compute Modified Lewis Bending Stress]
    A11 --> A13[Compute Modified Buckingham Wear Stress]
    A11 --> A14[Compute Tooth Deflection]
    A12 --> A15{Sigma B Sigma B Allowable}
    A13 --> A16{Sigma W Sigma W Allowable}
    A14 --> A17{Delta Less Than 0 Point 1 m}
    A15 -->|No| A18[Redesign: Increase m or F]
    A16 -->|No| A18
    A17 -->|No| A18
    A18 --> A12
    A15 -->|Yes| A19[Design Acceptable]
    A16 -->|Yes| A19
    A17 -->|Yes| A19
    A19 --> A20[Check Molding: Gate, Weld Line, Shrinkage]
    A20 --> A21[Final Plastic Gear Design]
```

### 4.2 Gear Geometry Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Pinion z1 Module m] --> B2[Pitch Diameter d1 equals m times z1]
    B3[Pitch Point] --> B4[Line of Action]
    B5[Pressure Angle phi equals 20 deg] --> B4
    B6[Gear z2] --> B7[Pitch Diameter d2 equals m times z2]
    B2 --> B3
    B7 --> B3
    B3 --> B8[Forces]
    B8 --> B9[Ft Tangential Load]
    B8 --> B10[Fr Radial Load equals Ft tan phi]
    B9 --> B11[Lewis Bending Stress]
    B9 --> B12[Buckingham Wear Stress]
    B9 --> B13[Tooth Deflection]
```

### 4.3 Plastic Gear Anatomy (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Plastic Gear Assembly] --> C2[Tooth Region]
    C1 --> C3[Hub Region]
    C1 --> C4[Web Region]
    C2 --> C5[Tip Circle]
    C2 --> C6[Pitch Circle]
    C2 --> C7[Root Circle]
    C2 --> C8[Involute Profile 20 deg PA]
    C3 --> C9[Boss Design]
    C3 --> C10[Keyway / Spline / D-Shaft]
    C4 --> C11[Plain Web]
    C4 --> C12[Lightening Holes]
    C4 --> C13[Ribbed Web]
```

### 4.4 Failure Mode Hierarchy in Plastic Gears

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Plastic Gear Under Load] --> D2{Limiting Mode}
    D2 -->|Low Load| D3[Tooth Bending Sigma B]
    D2 -->|Medium Load| D4[Tooth Wear Sigma W]
    D2 -->|High Load| D5[Tooth Deflection Delta]
    D2 -->|Sustained Load| D6[Creep Deformation]
    D2 -->|Cyclic Load| D7[Fatigue Cracking]
    D2 -->|High Temperature| D8[Thermal Softening]
    D3 --> D9[Root Cracking]
    D4 --> D10[Pitting and Abrasive Wear]
    D5 --> D11[Incomplete Meshing Noise]
    D6 --> D12[Permanent Tooth Bending]
    D7 --> D13[Progressive Tooth Loss]
    D8 --> D14[Material Flow and Deformation]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Reading the Diagram:** Unlike metal gears where fatigue dominates, plastic gear failure starts with **wear and deflection** and progressively moves to bending and creep. This is the *failure cascade* unique to polymers.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**  
> *List any six advantages of plastic gears over metallic gears.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Self-lubricating** — no external lubrication required, ideal for food and medical applications.
2. **Lightweight** — density 1.0–1.6 g/cm³ versus 7.8 g/cm³ for steel.
3. **Corrosion-resistant** — unaffected by water, oils, and many chemicals.
4. **Noise reduction** — operates at 60–70 dB versus 80–95 dB for steel.
5. **Cost-effective** — single-step injection molding versus multi-step machining.
6. **Shock absorption** — viscoelastic damping absorbs vibration and impact loads.
7. *(Bonus point)* Complex hub/web geometry can be molded as one piece.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**  
> *Write a short note on the modifications required in the Lewis equation for plastic gear design.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The classical Lewis equation $\sigma_b = F_t / (F \cdot m \cdot Y)$ assumes elastic, temperature-independent material. For plastic gears, three correction factors are introduced:

1. **Application factor $K_a$** — accounts for shock loads and load uniformity (1.0–1.75).
2. **Material factor $K_m$** — accounts for molded part variability, weld lines, voids (1.0–1.5).
3. **Temperature factor $K_t$** — accounts for drop in modulus at elevated temperature (1.0 at 20 °C, up to 4.0 at 100 °C).

The modified equation becomes:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

Additionally, the form factor $Y$ uses polymer-specific values (e.g., 0.385 for z = 30 teeth at 20° PA) rather than metal values.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

> **Module 3 Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE of the following.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A (a)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> *Explain in detail the various factors to be considered while designing plastic gears. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Factor 1: Mechanical Loading Requirements (2 Marks)**
The designer must establish transmitted power P (kW), pinion speed N (rpm), torque T, and expected service life. From this, tangential force $F_t$ at the pitch circle is computed: $F_t = 2T/d$. Plastic gears typically operate at **pitch-line velocities below 10 m/s** for safety.

**Factor 2: Material Selection (2 Marks)**
Material is chosen based on:
- **Modulus (E)** — affects deflection and stress.
- **Tensile strength** — sets bending limit.
- **Fatigue endurance** — sets life limit.
- **Temperature resistance** — POM (≤100 °C), PEEK (≤250 °C).
- **Moisture absorption** — Nylon absorbs 9%, POM only 0.2%.
- **Wear resistance** — POM and UHMWPE are excellent.
- Common choices: **POM (Delrin)**, **Nylon 66**, **PC**, **PEEK**, and glass-filled composites.

**Factor 3: Environmental Conditions (1 Mark)**
Operating temperature, humidity, chemical exposure, and UV radiation all influence dimensional stability and mechanical properties. Glass-filled grades are preferred for high-humidity conditions.

**Factor 4: Tooth Geometry (1 Mark)**
Standard 20° pressure angle involute profile. Module $m$ and number of teeth $z$ must satisfy:
- **No undercutting:** $z_1 \geq 17$ for 20° PA.
- **Avoid too many teeth:** increasing $z$ raises pitch-line velocity and wear.
- Face width $F$ typically 6m to 10m.

**Factor 5: Molding Considerations (1 Mark)**
- Gate location should be at the **web/rim** (not at the tooth tip) to avoid weld lines on load-bearing tooth flanks.
- Shrinkage compensation: 1.5–3% depending on material.
- Molded-in stress must be minimized by uniform cooling.

---

> **Q.A (b)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> *A plastic spur gear made of acetal (POM) has the following specifications:*
> *Number of teeth z = 30, module m = 2 mm, face width F = 12 mm,*
> *speed N = 1200 rpm, transmitted power P = 2.0 kW.*
> *Material data: E = 3.0 GPa, ν = 0.35, allowable bending stress σ_b,allow = 50 MPa,*
> *allowable wear stress σ_w,allow = 85 MPa, Y = 0.385 (for z = 30 at 20° PA),*
> *K_a = 1.25, K_m = 1.10, K_t = 1.20, K_E = 1.0.*
> *Check the gear for: (i) bending stress, (ii) wear stress, (iii) tooth deflection. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
$z = 30$, $m = 2 \text{ mm}$, $F = 12 \text{ mm}$, $N = 1200 \text{ rpm}$, $P = 2.0 \text{ kW}$
$E = 3.0 \text{ GPa} = 3000 \text{ MPa}$, $\nu = 0.35$, $\phi = 20°$, $Y = 0.385$
$K_a = 1.25$, $K_m = 1.10$, $K_t = 1.20$, $K_E = 1.0$
$\sigma_{b,allow} = 50 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_{w,allow} = 85 \text{ MPa}$

**Step 1: Pitch Diameter**

$$
d = m \cdot z = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2: Tangential Load**

$$
F_t = \dfrac{60000 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot d \cdot N} = \dfrac{60000 \times 2.0}{\pi \times 60 \times 1200}
$$

$$
F_t = \dfrac{120000}{226195} = 0.5305 \text{ kN} = 530.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3: Modified Lewis Bending Stress**

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{530.5 \times 1.25 \times 1.10 \times 1.20}{12 \times 2 \times 0.385}
$$

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{530.5 \times 1.65}{9.24} = \dfrac{875.3}{9.24} = 94.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Comparison:** $\sigma_b = 94.7 \text{ MPa} > \sigma_{b,allow} = 50 \text{ MPa}$
**Bending check FAILS** — design must be modified (increase F or m).

*(For teaching demonstration, we proceed to the next checks.)*

**Step 4: Modified Buckingham Wear Stress**

$$
C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3000}{2(1 - 0.35^2)}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3000}{2 \times 0.8775}}
$$

$$
C_p = \sqrt{1709.4} = 41.35 \ \sqrt{\text{MPa}}
$$

$$
\sigma_w = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_t \cdot K_E}{F \cdot d \cdot \sin\phi}}
$$

$$
\sigma_w = 41.35 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{530.5 \times 1.0}{12 \times 60 \times \sin 20°}}
$$

$$
\sigma_w = 41.35 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{530.5}{246.2}} = 41.35 \times \sqrt{2.155}
$$

$$
\sigma_w = 41.35 \times 1.468 = 60.7 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Comparison:** $\sigma_w = 60.7 \text{ MPa} < \sigma_{w,allow} = 85 \text{ MPa}$
**Wear check PASSES.**

**Step 5: Tooth Deflection**

$$
\delta = \dfrac{4 \cdot F_t \cdot \cos^2\phi}{E \cdot F \cdot m} = \dfrac{4 \times 530.5 \times (\cos 20°)^2}{3000 \times 12 \times 2}
$$

$$
\delta = \dfrac{4 \times 530.5 \times 0.8830}{72000} = \dfrac{1873.4}{72000} = 0.026 \text{ mm}
$$

**Acceptable limit:** $0.1 \times m = 0.1 \times 2 = 0.2 \text{ mm}$

**Comparison:** $\delta = 0.026 \text{ mm} < 0.2 \text{ mm}$
**Deflection check PASSES.**

**Conclusion:** Bending stress fails; **redesign required** by increasing face width to F = 25 mm or module to m = 3 mm. Wear and deflection are within limits.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q.B (a)** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**  
> *Discuss the material selection criteria for plastic gears with examples of commonly used materials. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Criterion 1: Mechanical Strength (2 Marks)**
The gear must transmit the required torque without bending or wear failure. Tensile strength $\sigma_t$ and fatigue endurance limit $\sigma_e$ are key. Example: **POM** has $\sigma_t = 70$ MPa, while **glass-filled POM (30% GF)** has $\sigma_t = 130$ MPa.

**Criterion 2: Modulus of Elasticity (1 Mark)**
Higher E reduces tooth deflection. POM: 3 GPa; Glass-filled PA66: 10 GPa. For high-precision motion-control gears (e.g., printers), high E is critical.

**Criterion 3: Temperature Resistance (1 Mark)**
Operating temperature determines the material class. POM is limited to ~100 °C; PPS to 200 °C; PEEK to 250 °C. Under-the-hood automotive gears need PPS or PEEK.

**Criterion 4: Moisture Absorption (1 Mark)**
Nylon 6 absorbs ~9% water, causing 1–3% dimensional change and modulus drop. POM absorbs only 0.2%, making it dimensionally stable. For humid environments, **POM or PBT** is preferred over Nylon.

**Criterion 5: Wear Resistance and Friction (1 Mark)**
For low-friction, self-lubricated gears, **POM, UHMWPE, and PTFE-filled compounds** are chosen. For high-speed dry operation, **PEEK** or **PPS** are preferred.

**Criterion 6: Molding Characteristics (1 Mark)**
The material must flow uniformly to fill thin tooth sections. POM and PA have good melt flow; PEEK requires high molding temperatures (400 °C). For complex gears with thin webs, POM is preferred.

**Common Material Examples Summary:**

| Application | Material |
|---|---|
| Office printer gears | POM (Delrin® 100) |
| Automotive HVAC | Glass-filled PA66 |
| Medical infusion pumps | POM, PC |
| Aerospace actuators | PEEK |
| Food-grade conveyors | UHMWPE, POM |

---

> **Q.B (b)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**  
> *Derive the modified Lewis equation for plastic gear design and explain each term in detail. (7 Marks)*

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Starting Equation (Classical Lewis, 1893)**
For a gear tooth modeled as a cantilever beam loaded at the tip:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot P}{F \cdot Y}
$$

where $P$ = diametral pitch, $F$ = face width, $Y$ = Lewis form factor.

**Step 2: Conversion to SI Metric System**
Replacing diametral pitch with module:

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

**Step 3: Introduction of Polymer-Specific Correction Factors**

Because polymer properties depend on **environment, manufacturing, and temperature**, three correction factors are introduced:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K_a &= \text{application factor} \quad (1.0\text{–}1.75) \\
K_m &= \text{material factor} \quad (1.0\text{–}1.5) \\
K_t &= \text{temperature factor} \quad (1.0\text{–}4.0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Final Modified Lewis Equation**

$$
\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}
$$

**Explanation of Each Term (4 Marks):**

| Term | Description | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| $F_t$ | Tangential tooth load at pitch circle | Calculated |
| $K_a$ | Application factor — accounts for shock loads, non-uniform loading (e.g., printer paper feed) | 1.0–1.75 |
| $K_m$ | Material factor — accounts for molded-gear variability (voids, weld lines, anisotropy) | 1.0–1.5 |
| $K_t$ | Temperature factor — accounts for modulus drop with temperature | 1.0 (20 °C) – 4.0 (100 °C for POM) |
| $F$ | Face width of the gear | 6m–10m typically |
| $m$ | Module of the gear | 0.5–5 mm typically |
| $Y$ | Lewis form factor (modified for plastics, depends on teeth count) | 0.30–0.485 |

**Significance:** The modified equation explicitly captures the **multi-parameter sensitivity of polymers**, making it suitable for **AGMA-style plastic gear rating** and **reliability-based design**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Most Common Mistakes):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting correction factors $K_a$, $K_m$, $K_t$** — Examiner expects to see ALL three in modified Lewis equation. Skipping any → **lose 1–2 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. **Using metal form factor $Y$ values** — Plastics use lower $Y$ values (e.g., 0.385 for z = 30). Using metallic values (0.485) → **wrong bending stress**.
> 3. **Mixing up wear and bending stress equations** — Wear uses **$C_p$ and $\sqrt{}$**, bending uses **linear $F_t$**.
> 4. **Not converting units consistently** — E in GPa must be converted to MPa before substituting. Mixed units → **arithmetic error**.
> 5. **Ignoring tooth deflection check** — Examiner often allocates **1–2 marks** specifically for deflection, even if bending passes.
> 6. **Not stating the governing equation origin** — Always mention "Modified Lewis" and "Modified Buckingham" — not just "Lewis" or "Hertz". **–2 marks if missed.**
> 7. **Skipping the "design recommendation" step** — If bending fails, conclude with **"Redesign: increase F or m"**. A bare "FAIL" without redesign → **–1 mark**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Plastic Gears**

- [x] Plastic gears are **not** designed using metal-gear equations directly; **modified Lewis + modified Buckingham** are mandatory.
- [x] The **modified Lewis equation** is: $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_t \cdot K_a \cdot K_m \cdot K_t}{F \cdot m \cdot Y}$ — memorize it.
- [x] The **modified Buckingham equation** is: $\sigma_w = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{F_t \cdot K_E}{F \cdot d \cdot \sin\phi}}$ — memorize it.
- [x] The **elastic coefficient** for plastic-plastic gear pair: $C_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{2(1-\nu^2)}}$ — note $\nu \approx 0.35$ for polymers.
- [x] **Tooth deflection limit:** $\delta \leq 0.1 \cdot m$ — this often governs the design.
- [x] **Pitch diameter:** $d = m \cdot z$; **tangential load:** $F_t = \dfrac{60000 \cdot P}{\pi \cdot d \cdot N}$.
- [x] Standard **pressure angle** for plastic gears: **20°** (sometimes 14.5° for low-noise).
- [x] **Typical factor of safety** for plastic gears: **2.0 to 4.0** (vs 1.5 for steel).
- [x] Common materials: **POM (Delrin), Nylon 6/66, PC, PBT, PPS, PEEK**, glass-filled variants.
- [x] **Failure hierarchy:** wear → deflection → bending → creep → fatigue.
- [x] **POM** is the workhorse gear material (E ≈ 3 GPa, low moisture, low friction).
- [x] **Nylon** absorbs up to 9% moisture — avoid in humid precision applications.
- [x] **Glass-filled** materials increase modulus but may reduce wear resistance (abrasive glass fibers).
- [x] **Standard references:** AGMA 2003-D10 (Plastic Gears), VDI 2736 (German plastic gear guideline).
- [x] For KTU numericals, always **end with a conclusion**: "Design PASSES" or "Redesign required".
- [x] Always **quote units** in final answer (MPa, mm, N) — bare numbers → ½ mark penalty.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Bearings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plastic Bearings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plastic Bearing (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A **plastic bearing** is a machine element designed to constrain relative motion between two moving parts (rotational, linear, or oscillating) to a desired degree of freedom, in which the load-bearing and/or sliding surfaces are manufactured predominantly from **polymeric materials** — thermoplastics, thermosets, or polymer-matrix composites — either as a self-lubricating solid, as a lubricant-retaining porous structure, or as a liner bonded to a metallic backing.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCPOT601 – Polymer Products: Design & Testing**, plastic bearings are classified as a **tribological polymer product**, where the engineering goal is to manage the four interlinked tribological variables: **Load (P)**, **Speed (V)**, **Temperature (T)**, and **Wear rate (k)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Soap-Bar Slider"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a metal skewer rotating inside a metal sleeve — it squeaks, it heats up, and without oil it welds itself to the sleeve within minutes. Now swap the metal sleeve for a **nylon bush** or a **PTFE-lined sleeve**. The skewer now rotates silently, generates far less heat, and even works dry. That nylon/PTFE bush is a plastic bearing. The polymer acts as a *built-in lubricant* because its long, flexible molecular chains shear easily over the counterface, creating a thin, low-shear-strength transfer film that prevents metal-to-metal contact — exactly the same trick a thin film of oil performs in a metal bearing.

This analogy captures the three signature traits of plastic bearings:
1. **Self-lubrication** (no external oil needed in many designs)
2. **Low coefficient of friction** (typically $\mu = 0.05$ to $0.4$ for polymers vs. $0.5+$ for dry steel-on-steel)
3. **Conformability** — polymers creep slightly under load, distributing contact pressure over a larger area than rigid metals.

## 1.3 Why Plastic Bearings? — The Engineering Rationale

A KTU examiner will always look for the *justification* before the *description*. Plastic bearings are specified over metal bearings when one or more of the following is critical:

| # | Engineering Requirement | Why Polymer Wins |
|---|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Corrosion resistance** (chemicals, water, salt) | Polymers are chemically inert; metals rust |
| 2 | **Dry / boundary lubrication** (food, vacuum, clean rooms) | Polymers have built-in transfer-film lubrication |
| 3 | **Noise dampening** (consumer appliances, instruments) | Polymer's viscoelasticity absorbs vibration |
| 4 | **Lightweight** (aerospace, automotive) | Polymer density = **900–1500 kg/m³** vs steel **7850 kg/m³** |
| 5 | **Electrical insulation** (motors, instruments) | Polymers are dielectrics |
| 6 | **Low cost at high volume** (injection mouldable) | One moulding vs multi-step metal machining |
| 7 | **Shock/impact tolerance** | Polymers yield plastically, don't chip |
| 8 | **Compliance with FDA / EU food contact** | Specific grades (UHMWPE, POM, PEEK) are food-grade |

## 1.4 Classification of Plastic Bearings (Syllabus Mapping)

Plastic bearings are classified by **geometry**, **motion type**, and **construction**:

- **By Motion Type:**
  - **Journal / Sleeve bearings** → rotational (radial load)
  - **Thrust bearings** → axial load
  - **Linear bearings** → sliding motion
  - **Spherical / Rod-end bearings** → oscillating/misaligned motion

- **By Construction:**
  - **All-plastic bearings** (e.g., pure nylon bush)
  - **Plastic-lined metal bearings** (e.g., PTFE or phenolic liner on steel backing)
  - **Reinforced-plastic bearings** (e.g., glass-fibre filled PA 66)
  - **Plastic-caged rolling element bearings** (polymer retainer for steel balls)
  - **Filament-wound composite bearings** (PTFE/glass fibre over-wound)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic is positioned in **Module 3**, which deals with design-oriented polymer products. Expect questions that link *material selection* → *load-speed envelope* → *failure mode* — the classic polymer-bearings design triangle.

## 1.5 Standard Engineering Metrics Used Throughout This Module

The following bolded terms appear in every KTU question on this topic. Memorize them as **vocabulary**:

- **PV limit** — the maximum product of bearing pressure (P) and sliding velocity (V) a material can sustain without failure. **Units:** MPa·m/s (or psi·fpm in older texts).
- **K factor (wear factor)** — intrinsic wear resistance of the polymer. **Units:** mm³/N·m.
- **Coefficient of friction (μ)** — ratio of frictional force to normal load. **Dimensionless.**
- **Limiting PV** — PV at which the material undergoes *uncontrolled* thermal runaway.
- **Specific wear rate** — volume lost per unit sliding distance per unit load.
- **Conformability** — ability of the polymer to deform plastically and match the counterface geometry under load.
- **Embeddability** — ability to absorb hard foreign particles into the polymer matrix, preventing them from abrading the counterface.
- **Thermal conductivity (k)** — for most unfilled polymers lies in the range **0.2 – 0.5 W/(m·K)** (very low!), which is why heat dissipation is the #1 design challenge.
- **Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE)** — for polymers, typically **50 – 150 × 10⁻⁶ /K**, an order of magnitude higher than steel (~12 × 10⁻⁶ /K).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tribological System of a Plastic Bearing

A plastic bearing is not a "nylon ring." It is a **tribo-system** — an interaction zone where the following six variables must be balanced simultaneously. KTU examiners love asking students to identify which variable is the limiting one in a given scenario.

1. **Contact pressure (P)** — the load per unit projected area.
2. **Sliding velocity (V)** — the relative speed at the interface.
3. **Interface temperature (T)** — generated by frictional heating and removed by conduction/convection.
4. **Friction coefficient (μ)** — the system's "tax" on motion.
5. **Wear rate** — material lost per unit time/distance.
6. **Lubrication state** — boundary, mixed, hydrodynamic, or dry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pearl:** If you can articulate how changing *one* of these six variables affects the other five, you can answer virtually any Part-B question on plastic bearings. This is the **systems-thinking skill** KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework is testing.

## 2.2 The PV Concept — Heart of Polymer Bearing Design

The **PV product** is the single most important design parameter for plastic bearings. It combines the two most damaging operating variables — pressure and velocity — into one number that the material's manufacturer publishes as a **limiting PV**.

$$\text{PV} = P \times V$$

Where:
- $P$ = bearing pressure in **MPa** (or psi)
- $V$ = sliding velocity in **m/s** (or fpm)

**Physical meaning of PV:**
- Pressure alone melts the polymer (high stationary load on a small area).
- Velocity alone heats the polymer by friction.
- Their **product** captures *both* the mechanical and thermal load on the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of PV as the "**stress-power density**" at the sliding interface. If you exceed the limiting PV, the polymer cannot conduct heat away fast enough — the interface temperature rises, the polymer softens, friction increases, more heat is generated, and the system spirals into **thermal runaway** (uncontrolled melting and seizure).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> The following table is the **minimum set of equations** you must know for any KTU question on this topic. Commit to memory.

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|-------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $PV = P \times V$ | $P$ = MPa, $V$ = m/s | Stress-power density at the sliding interface |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{W}{L \cdot D}$ | $W$ = load (N), $L$ = bearing length (mm), $D$ = shaft dia. (mm) | Bearing pressure for a journal bearing |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | $D$ = m, $N$ = rpm | Sliding velocity at the journal surface |
| 4 | $T_{f} = \mu \cdot W \cdot r$ | $T_f$ = N·m, $\mu$ = dimensionless, $r$ = shaft radius (m) | Friction torque in a journal bearing |
| 5 | $Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V$ | $Q$ = W (watts) | Frictional heat generation rate |
| 6 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{Q}{h \cdot A}$ | $h$ = W/(m²·K), $A$ = m² | Interface temperature rise |
| 7 | $V_{w} = K \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t$ | $V_w$ = mm³, $K$ = mm³/(N·m), $t$ = s | **Archard's wear law** adapted for polymers |
| 8 | $W_{s} = \dfrac{V_{w}}{P \cdot V \cdot t}$ | $W_s$ = mm³/(N·m) | Specific wear rate (wear coefficient) |
| 9 | $\delta_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot L$ | $\alpha$ = 1/K, $L$ = m | Thermal expansion / clearance change |
| 10 | $C_{min} = C_{nom} - \delta_{th}$ | $C$ = clearance (mm) | Design clearance after thermal expansion |
| 11 | $h_{min} = k_{film} \cdot \dfrac{\mu \cdot V}{P}$ | $h_{min}$ = m | Minimum lubricant film thickness (Hamrock-Dowson simplified) |
| 12 | $K_{compound} = \dfrac{\sum f_i \cdot k_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $f_i$ = volume fraction, $k_i$ = conductivity | Thermal conductivity of filled polymer (rule of mixtures) |
| 13 | $\sigma_{creep} = \sigma_0 \cdot t^{-n}$ | $\sigma$ = MPa, $t$ = s, $n$ = creep exponent | Polymer creep (power-law approximation) |
| 14 | $\eta_{eff} = \eta_0 \cdot e^{E_a / (R \cdot T)}$ | $E_a$ = J/mol, $T$ = K | Arrhenius viscosity for melt-flow during PV overload |

**Critical Note on Units:** KTU examiners may give $P$ in psi and $V$ in fpm (foot-per-minute). Always convert to SI (MPa, m/s) **first**. Conversion: $1 \text{ psi·fpm} = 0.0002237 \text{ MPa·m/s}$.

## 2.4 Material Selection — The Four Polymer Families for Bearings

### 2.4.1 Thermoplastics for Bearings

| Polymer | Abbrev. | Max Continuous T (°C) | Limiting PV (MPa·m/s) | μ (dry) | Best Use |
|---------|---------|------------------------|------------------------|---------|----------|
| **Polyamide (Nylon) 6, 66** | PA | 80 – 100 | 0.4 – 0.9 | 0.2 – 0.4 | General purpose, low-load bushings |
| **Polyoxymethylene (Acetal)** | POM | 100 | 0.6 – 1.0 | 0.15 – 0.35 | Precision gears, fuel-system bearings |
| **Polytetrafluoroethylene** | PTFE | 260 | 0.4 – 0.6 (filled) | 0.04 – 0.10 | Dry, high-temp, chemical-resistant |
| **Ultra-High Molecular Weight PE** | UHMWPE | 80 | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.10 – 0.20 | Food, medical (orthopaedic) |
| **Polyetheretherketone** | PEEK | 250 | 1.0 – 1.5 (filled) | 0.25 – 0.40 | Aerospace, high-temp, high-PV |
| **Polyimide (Vespel)** | PI | 300 | 1.5 – 2.5 | 0.20 – 0.30 | Extreme temperature (jet engines) |

### 2.4.2 Thermosets (Composite Bearings)

- **Phenolic resin + cotton/PTFE fabric liners** — used in rubber-hinge bearings, aircraft control-surface bearings, high-load oscillating joints.
- **Epoxy + glass/carbon fibre + PTFE/MoS₂** — used in helicopter rotor bearings, marine stern tubes.
- **Polyester + glass fibre + graphite** — large industrial rolls and kingpin bushings.

### 2.4.3 Polymer-Caged Rolling Element Bearings

Even in *steel* ball or roller bearings, the **cage (retainer)** that spaces the rolling elements is increasingly made of **glass-fibre reinforced PA 66 (nylon)** or **PEEK** because:
- Lower mass → lower centrifugal stress at high rpm
- Self-lubricating → less cage-to-race friction
- Quieter operation
- Operates briefly even under lubricant starvation (fail-safe)

### 2.4.4 Filled / Reinforced Polymers for Bearings

Pure polymers rarely meet industrial PV limits. They are almost always **filled** with:
- **PTFE fillers** → reduce friction, improve dry running
- **Graphite** → improve thermal conductivity, reduce wear
- **MoS₂ (Molybdenum disulphide)** → solid lubricant, high-temperature
- **Glass fibre** → improve strength, creep resistance, dimensional stability
- **Carbon fibre** → improve strength + thermal conductivity + reduce CTE
- **Bronze / steel powder** → improve thermal conductivity dramatically (used in PTFE-based DU-bushing style)

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the PV Concept

The PV concept is the *design contract* between the bearing user and the bearing manufacturer. It is the single number on a material datasheet that tells the designer: *"If you operate at or below this PV, the bearing will perform to its published wear life."*

In the field, this enables:
- **Rapid material screening** during product development.
- **Trade-off analysis:** if a designer wants to *double the speed*, the allowable pressure is *halved* (and vice versa), all else being equal.
- **Failure diagnosis:** if a bearing has failed, the operating PV (computed from the failed application) is compared to the material's published limiting PV. If the operating PV > limiting PV, the failure is a *design* error; if operating PV < limiting PV but the bearing still failed, the failure is a *material* or *lubrication* issue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The PV concept is the *only* way to answer "design a plastic bearing for the following load and speed" questions in the exam. Memorize equation (1), (2), and (3) in the cheat sheet and practice computing $P$, $V$, and $PV$ from a stated load, shaft diameter, bearing length, and rotational speed.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Examples

## 3.1 Derivation of Bearing Pressure for a Journal Bearing

Consider a cylindrical plastic bush (length $L$, internal diameter $D$) carrying a radial load $W$ on a steel shaft. The **projected area** over which this load is supported is:

$$A_{proj} = L \cdot D$$

By definition, the **bearing pressure** $P$ is the load per unit projected area:

$$P = \frac{W}{A_{proj}} = \frac{W}{L \cdot D}$$

**Validity check (units):** $W$ in Newtons [N], $L$ in metres [m], $D$ in metres [m].

$$\text{Units of } P = \frac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^2} = \text{Pa} \; (\text{SI})$$

To convert to **MPa** (the unit KTU uses for PV limit):

$$P \text{ [MPa]} = \frac{W \text{ [N]}}{L \text{ [mm]} \cdot D \text{ [mm]}}$$

because $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ MPa}$.

## 3.2 Derivation of Sliding Velocity for a Journal Bearing

The shaft surface travels one circumference per revolution. If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute:

- Distance per revolution = $\pi \cdot D$
- Distance per minute = $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$
- Distance per second (sliding velocity):

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$$

**Validity check (units):** $D$ in metres, $N$ in rpm.

$$\text{Units of } V = \frac{\text{m} \cdot \text{rev/min}}{\text{s/min}} = \text{m/s} \; (\text{SI})$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Frictional Heat Generation

The friction force at the journal interface is:

$$F_f = \mu \cdot W$$

The power dissipated as heat (energy per unit time) is the friction force times the sliding velocity:

$$Q = F_f \cdot V = \mu \cdot W \cdot V$$

**Validity check (units):** $\mu$ is dimensionless, $W$ in N, $V$ in m/s.

$$\text{Units of } Q = \text{N} \cdot \text{m/s} = \text{J/s} = \text{W}$$

This is the *frictional heat input*. It must be **removed by conduction through the bearing housing and shaft, and by convection to ambient air**. The interface temperature is found from a thermal balance:

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{h \cdot A_{surface}}$$

where $h$ is the convective heat-transfer coefficient and $A_{surface}$ is the bearing's external surface area exposed to ambient.

## 3.4 Derivation of Thermal Clearance Compensation

Plastics have a coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) roughly **10× greater** than steel. When a plastic bush heats up during operation, its internal diameter *grows* more than the steel shaft. The designer must account for this.

The thermal expansion of the bearing's diameter:

$$\Delta D = \alpha_p \cdot D \cdot \Delta T$$

where $\alpha_p$ is the CTE of the polymer (≈ 80 × 10⁻⁶ /K for nylon) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature rise of the polymer above the assembly temperature.

The shaft also expands, but by a much smaller amount:

$$\Delta D_{shaft} = \alpha_{steel} \cdot D \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot D \cdot \Delta T$$

The **net change in radial clearance** is:

$$\Delta C = \frac{\Delta D - \Delta D_{shaft}}{2} = \frac{(\alpha_p - \alpha_{steel}) \cdot D \cdot \Delta T}{2}$$

The designer must size the **initial clearance** $C_{nom}$ at room temperature such that, after thermal expansion, the **operating clearance** $C_{op}$ remains positive (so the shaft doesn't seize):

$$C_{nom} \geq C_{op,min} + \Delta C$$

## 3.5 Worked Example #1 — PV Computation for a Nylon 66 Bushing

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A steel shaft of diameter **50 mm** is supported in a **nylon 66 bushing** of length **40 mm**. The shaft carries a radial load of **3000 N** and rotates at **240 rpm**. The application is in a textile mill with ambient temperature 30 °C and convective cooling coefficient $h = 25 \text{ W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)}$. The coefficient of friction between nylon 66 and steel (dry, lightly lubricated) is $\mu = 0.25$.

Calculate: (i) Bearing pressure, (ii) Sliding velocity, (iii) PV product, (iv) Frictional heat generated, (v) Interface temperature rise, and (vi) State if the material is safe. Published limiting PV for dry nylon 66 = **0.6 MPa·m/s**.

### Step 1 — Bearing Pressure $P$

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{3000 \text{ N}}{40 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm}} = \frac{3000}{2000} = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Sliding Velocity $V$

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.050 \text{ m} \times 240}{60}$$

$$V = \frac{37.699 \times 0.050}{60} = \frac{1.8850}{60} = 0.03142 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 3 — PV Product

$$PV = P \times V = 1.5 \text{ MPa} \times 0.03142 \text{ m/s} = 0.0471 \text{ MPa·m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Decision: Material Safe?

The computed PV ($0.0471$) is **13× lower** than the limiting PV of dry nylon 66 ($0.6$).

**Conclusion:** The material is *safe on a PV basis*. However, this is only a *first-pass* check — we must also verify the thermal and wear life in the next steps.

### Step 5 — Frictional Heat Generation $Q$

$$Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V = 0.25 \times 3000 \text{ N} \times 0.03142 \text{ m/s}$$

$$Q = 23.565 \text{ W}$$

### Step 6 — Interface Temperature Rise $\Delta T$

External surface area of the bushing (cylindrical, ignoring end caps):

$$A_{ext} = \pi \cdot D_o \cdot L$$

Assuming outer diameter $D_o = 70$ mm = 0.070 m:

$$A_{ext} = \pi \times 0.070 \times 0.040 = 0.00880 \text{ m}^2$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{h \cdot A_{ext}} = \frac{23.565}{25 \times 0.00880} = \frac{23.565}{0.2199} = 107.1 \text{ °C}$$

### Step 7 — Critical Realisation: Thermal Failure

The interface would rise by **107 °C above ambient**, giving an interface temperature of approximately **30 + 107 = 137 °C**. This is *above* the maximum continuous service temperature of nylon 66 (≈ 100 °C).

**Conclusion:** Even though the PV is comfortably within the material's published limit, the polymer will *overheat* because of poor heat dissipation (low thermal conductivity, no oil cooling). The designer must:
- Reduce load or speed,
- Add forced-air or water cooling,
- Switch to a filled nylon (e.g., glass-filled PA 66, or MoS₂-filled),
- Or switch to a higher-temperature polymer (POM, PEEK).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pearl:** This is the *exact* kind of "design trap" examiners plant in 14-mark problems. The student who only checks PV says "safe"; the student who also checks thermal dissipation catches the real failure mode. Always check *both*.

## 3.6 Worked Example #2 — Thermal Clearance Sizing for a POM Bushing

**Problem Statement:**
A polyoxymethylene (POM/acetal) bushing is to be assembled on a steel shaft at an ambient temperature of **25 °C**. The bushing's internal diameter is **$D = 30$ mm**, and the application requires the *operating clearance* to be at least **0.05 mm** at a steady-state operating temperature of **80 °C**.

Material data:
- CTE of POM: $\alpha_{POM} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$
- CTE of steel: $\alpha_{steel} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /K}$

Calculate the **minimum nominal (room-temperature) clearance** to avoid seizure at 80 °C.

### Step 1 — Temperature Rise $\Delta T$

$$\Delta T = 80 - 25 = 55 \text{ K}$$

### Step 2 — Thermal Growth of POM Bore

$$\Delta D_{POM} = \alpha_{POM} \cdot D \cdot \Delta T = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times 30 \times 55$$

$$\Delta D_{POM} = 0.165 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 3 — Thermal Growth of Steel Shaft

$$\Delta D_{steel} = \alpha_{steel} \cdot D \cdot \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 30 \times 55$$

$$\Delta D_{steel} = 0.0198 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 4 — Net Change in Diametrical Clearance

$$\Delta (\text{dia. clearance}) = \Delta D_{POM} - \Delta D_{steel} = 0.165 - 0.0198 = 0.1452 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Change in Radial Clearance

Since clearance is radial, divide by 2:

$$\Delta C = \frac{0.1452}{2} = 0.0726 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6 — Required Nominal Clearance

For the operating clearance to be at least 0.05 mm at 80 °C:

$$C_{nom} = C_{op,min} + \Delta C = 0.05 + 0.0726 = 0.1226 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The bushing must be designed with a **nominal radial clearance of at least 0.12 mm** at room temperature. Otherwise, the POM will swell against the shaft at operating temperature and seize.

## 3.7 Worked Example #3 — Archard Wear Life Prediction

**Problem Statement:**
A PTFE + 25 % glass fibre + 5 % MoS₂ composite bushing is operating under:
- Load: $W = 5000$ N
- Sliding velocity: $V = 0.5$ m/s
- Bearing area: $L \times D = 60 \times 50 = 3000$ mm²
- Required service life: $t_{life} = 5000$ hours
- Published wear factor for this composite: $K = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^3\text{/N·m}$

Predict the radial wear depth after 5000 hours of operation.

### Step 1 — Bearing Pressure

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{5000}{60 \times 50} = \frac{5000}{3000} = 1.667 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 2 — Total Sliding Distance

$$s = V \times t_{life} = 0.5 \text{ m/s} \times (5000 \times 3600) \text{ s}$$

$$s = 0.5 \times 18\,000\,000 = 9 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}$$

### Step 3 — Wear Volume (Archard's Law, polymer-adapted)

$$V_w = K \cdot P \cdot V \cdot t = (4.0 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.667 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2) \times 0.5 \times (18 \times 10^{6} \text{ s})$$

$$V_w = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.667 \times 10^{6} \times 0.5 \times 18 \times 10^{6}$$

Computing the product step by step:

$$V_w = 4.0 \times 1.667 \times 0.5 \times 18 \times 10^{-6+6+6} = 4.0 \times 1.667 \times 0.5 \times 18 \times 10^{6}$$

$$V_w = 4.0 \times 1.667 = 6.668; \quad 6.668 \times 0.5 = 3.334; \quad 3.334 \times 18 = 60.01$$

$$\therefore V_w \approx 60.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ mm}^3 = 60\,000 \text{ cm}^3$$

(That seems high — let me verify by using the standard form. The polymer-adapted Archard equation is often written as $V_w = K \cdot F_n \cdot s$, where $F_n = W$ and $s$ is sliding distance.)

**Re-derivation using the more direct form:**

$$V_w = K \cdot W \cdot s = (4.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}) \times 5000 \text{ N} \times (9 \times 10^{6} \text{ m})$$

$$V_w = 4.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 5000 \times 9 \times 10^{6} = 4.0 \times 5000 \times 9 \times 10^{0}$$

$$V_w = 180\,000 \text{ mm}^3 = 180 \text{ cm}^3$$

### Step 4 — Radial Wear Depth

Assuming uniform wear over the bearing surface area $A = 3000$ mm²:

$$\text{Wear depth} = \frac{V_w}{A} = \frac{180\,000 \text{ mm}^3}{3000 \text{ mm}^2} = 60 \text{ mm}$$

This is **catastrophic** — the bearing would wear through 60 mm of material, more than the bearing's own length! The design is **not viable** for 5000 hours. Either the $K$ value is too high for the actual composite grade (consult supplier), or operating conditions must be relaxed, or a different material chosen.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** The $K$ values in datasheets span a **huge** range — PTFE alone has $K \approx 1000 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}$, while glass-fibre-filled PEEK has $K \approx 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}^3/\text{N·m}$. Always use the *grade-specific* $K$ value, not generic values.
