# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper Set A)]

**"Define the term 'PV limit' in the context of plastic bearing design. Why is it a critical design parameter?"**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

The **PV limit** is the maximum permissible product of bearing pressure ($P$, in MPa) and sliding velocity ($V$, in m/s) that a polymer bearing material can sustain without undergoing thermal runaway, excessive wear, or seizure. It is the *single design contract* that links mechanical load to thermal dissipation. Polymers fail catastrophically above the PV limit because their low thermal conductivity cannot remove the frictional heat generated at high $P \times V$, causing the interface temperature to rise, the polymer to soften, friction to increase, and the system to spiral into a melt-down failure. The PV limit is therefore *the* primary design constraint for plastic bearings, taking precedence over individual $P$ or $V$ considerations.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper Set B)]

**"List any four advantages of plastic bearings over metallic bearings. For each, name a specific application where the advantage is exploited."**

**Model Answer (≈ 90 words):**

1. **Self-lubrication** — Nylon 66 bushings in domestic fan motors (no oil, no maintenance).
2. **Corrosion resistance** — PTFE-lined bearings in chemical pumps handling acids.
3. **Light weight** — PEEK cages in aerospace turbine ball bearings.
4. **Noise dampening** — POM gears in washing machines (quieter than steel).
5. **Electrical insulation** — PA 66 bushes in small motor armatures.
6. **FDA compliance** — UHMWPE bearings in food-processing conveyor rollers.

(Any four with matched applications earn full 3 marks; KTU awards 0.5 marks for advantage, 0.25 marks for application.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Rule:** The student answers *one* of two alternatives. Each alternative has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The model answers below are written to a length matching the mark allocation.

---

### ⭐ Question A. [KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Paper 1]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of the following four polymer families in plastic bearing design, with at least one specific grade example for each: (i) Polyamides, (ii) PTFE, (iii) PEEK, (iv) Phenolic composites.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 40 mm diameter steel shaft runs at 300 rpm in a polyamide (nylon 66) bushing of length 50 mm. The radial load is 2000 N. The coefficient of friction is 0.30. The external surface area available for convection is 0.012 m² and the convective heat-transfer coefficient is 20 W/(m²·K). Compute (i) bearing pressure, (ii) sliding velocity, (iii) PV product, and (iv) interface temperature rise. The published limiting PV of nylon 66 is 0.6 MPa·m/s and the maximum service temperature is 100 °C. Ambient temperature is 30 °C. Comment on the suitability of the design.

#### Model Answer to (a) — 7 Marks

**Polyamides (PA 6, PA 66, PA 12)** *(≈ 1.5 marks)*
- Most widely used thermoplastic bearings — workhorse of the industry.
- **Grades:** Nylon 6 (tough, absorbs water → dimensionally unstable), Nylon 66 (higher melting point, harder), Nylon 12 (lowest water absorption, dimensionally stable).
- **Mechanism:** PA forms a thin transfer film on the steel counterface; the contact then becomes PA-on-PA, which is low-friction and low-wear.
- **Limitations:** Low limiting PV (0.4 – 0.9 MPa·m/s), high water absorption (changes dimensions and properties), creeps under sustained load.
- **Typical use:** Door hinges, washing-machine bearings, small gear bushings.

**PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene)** *(≈ 1.5 marks)*
- **Lowest coefficient of friction** of any solid polymer: $\mu = 0.04$ – $0.10$ dry.
- **Limiting PV:** 0.4 – 0.6 MPa·m/s *unfilled*, but rises to 1.5+ MPa·m/s with bronze, glass, or carbon fillers.
- **Mechanism:** PTFE's highly linear, helical molecular chain slides over itself with very low shear strength, producing a self-lubricating transfer film.
- **Drawbacks:** Poor thermal conductivity → poor heat removal, very high cold-flow (creep) under load.
- **Typical use:** PTFE-lined steel-backed composite bearings (DU-bush type) in automotive shock absorbers, aircraft control rods.

**PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** *(≈ 1.5 marks)*
- **High-performance thermoplastic:** service temperature up to **250 °C**, limiting PV 1.0 – 1.5 MPa·m/s (filled grades).
- **Mechanism:** Semi-crystalline with strong inter-chain bonding; resists creep, wear, and chemical attack.
- **Grades:** Unfilled PEEK (FDA-grade, medical), glass-fibre-filled (higher strength, lower CTE), carbon-fibre-filled (highest PV, ESD-safe).
- **Drawback:** Expensive (~ 10× the cost of nylon).
- **Typical use:** Aerospace control-surface bearings, semiconductor wafer-handling robots, oil-and-gas downhole tools.

**Phenolic Composites (Thermoset)** *(≈ 1.5 marks)*
- **Phenolic resin + cotton fabric, glass fabric, or PTFE fabric** laminated and cured.
- **Mechanism:** A woven fabric liner bonded to a steel backing — the fabric holds lubricant (grease or oil) in its weave, providing a reservoir for long-life, low-maintenance operation under oscillating loads.
- **Limiting PV:** up to 1.5 – 2.0 MPa·m/s for the rubber-bonded variant.
- **Typical use:** Automotive rubber-hinge bearings, king-pin bushings, helicopter rotor bearings, ship rudder bearings.
- **Drawback:** Cannot be remoulded; limited to bearing geometry (mostly cylindrical sleeve form).

#### Model Answer to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 40$ mm = 0.040 m, $N = 300$ rpm, $L = 50$ mm, $W = 2000$ N, $\mu = 0.30$, $A_{ext} = 0.012$ m², $h = 20$ W/(m²·K), ambient $T_\infty = 30$ °C, $T_{max} = 100$ °C, $PV_{lim} = 0.6$ MPa·m/s.

**(i) Bearing Pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{2000}{50 \times 40} = \frac{2000}{2000} = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Sliding Velocity**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.040 \times 300}{60} = \frac{37.699}{60} = 0.6283 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) PV Product**

$$PV = P \times V = 1.0 \times 0.6283 = 0.6283 \text{ MPa·m/s}$$

**Comparison with limit:** $PV_{operating} = 0.6283 > PV_{lim} = 0.6$ MPa·m/s.

The design **exceeds the PV limit by ~ 4.7 %**. This is borderline — within the safety margin of well-vented designs, the material will survive, but it has no design headroom.

**(iv) Frictional Heat & Interface Temperature**

$$Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V = 0.30 \times 2000 \times 0.6283 = 377.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{h \cdot A_{ext}} = \frac{377.0}{20 \times 0.012} = \frac{377.0}{0.24} = 1570.8 \text{ °C}$$

**This is physically impossible** — it indicates that the assumed convection conditions are *grossly insufficient* to remove the frictional heat. In reality, the polymer surface would melt, transfer-film would thicken, the shaft would seize, and the design would fail.

**Comments on Design Suitability** *(≈ 1.5 marks for this section)*
- The design is **NOT suitable**. The interface temperature rise (1570 °C, unphysical) and the operating PV (0.628 vs. limit 0.6) both indicate failure.
- **Remedies:** (a) Reduce load to 1800 N or less, (b) reduce speed to ~ 280 rpm, (c) switch to a higher-PV material (filled PA 66 with MoS₂, or POM, or PEEK), (d) add forced-air cooling, (e) use oil-impregnated bushing for internal cooling.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers in Question A(b):**
> 1. **Unit conversion mistake:** Failing to convert $D$ from mm to m when computing $V$. This gives a 12.5× error in $V$ and cascades through the entire solution (lose 3–4 marks).
> 2. **Forgetting to compare PV with the limit:** Many students compute $PV$ but stop there. The *comparison* is the answer to the question (lose 1 mark).
> 3. **Not commenting on physical meaning of unphysical temperature rise:** If $\Delta T$ comes out impossibly high, the correct response is to identify that the design is inadequate and propose a remedy, not to ignore it (lose 1–2 marks).
> 4. **Confusing bearing pressure $P$ with the PV product's $P$** — both are pressure, but the bearing pressure $P = W/(LD)$ is what you multiply by $V$ to get PV. Don't use contact stress from Hertzian contact.

---

### ⭐ Question B. [KTU University Exam — Module 3, Model Paper 2] **(Alternative to Question A)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the four principal *failure modes* observed in plastic bearings in service. For each, identify the root cause and a preventive design measure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A PTFE + 15 % glass fibre + 5 % graphite composite bearing is to be designed for a mixer application. The shaft diameter is 60 mm, length 80 mm, load 4500 N, and rotational speed 100 rpm. Compute (i) bearing pressure, (ii) sliding velocity, (iii) PV product, and (iv) state if the operating PV is within the published limit of 1.2 MPa·m/s for this composite. If the operating PV is acceptable, calculate the frictional heat generated assuming $\mu = 0.20$.

#### Model Answer to (a) — 7 Marks

**Failure Mode 1: Thermal Runaway (Melt-down Seizure)** *(≈ 1.75 marks)*
- **Root cause:** Operating PV exceeds the material's limiting PV, OR heat dissipation is inadequate for the heat generated. Interface temperature rises above the polymer's softening point (Tg for amorphous, Tm for semi-crystalline).
- **Mechanism:** T↑ → μ↑ → Q↑ → T↑↑ (positive feedback loop).
- **Prevention:** Stay within PV limit; design adequate thermal path; add cooling; choose higher-temperature polymer (PEEK, PI).

**Failure Mode 2: Excessive Wear (Loss of Clearance, Shaft Misalignment)** *(≈ 1.75 marks)*
- **Root cause:** Material's specific wear rate $K$ is too high for the application, OR abrasive contamination, OR dry running under marginal PV.
- **Mechanism:** Material erodes at the contact; clearance widens; shaft begins to vibrate; bearing may "whistle" and ultimately fail by fatigue.
- **Prevention:** Choose low-wear grade (filled PTFE, CF-PEEK); add hard-face coating to shaft; improve sealing; use lubricant where possible.

**Failure Mode 3: Creep (Cold Flow) under Sustained Load** *(≈ 1.75 marks)*
- **Root cause:** Sustained static load exceeds the polymer's creep resistance. Common in PA 6/66 above 50 % of short-term compressive strength at temperatures above 60 °C.
- **Mechanism:** Viscoelastic flow of polymer chains; bearing length shortens or radial wall thins; clearance closes; seizure.
- **Prevention:** Use creep-resistant grades (glass-fibre-filled PA, POM, PPS); reduce sustained stress to < 25 % of short-term strength; design for the steady-state creep strain, not the instantaneous strain.

**Failure Mode 4: Swelling / Softening by Fluid Absorption** *(≈ 1.75 marks)*
- **Root cause:** Polymer absorbs water, oil, or process fluid → plasticization → softening → dimensional change (swelling) and drop in modulus and Tg.
- **Mechanism:** Particularly severe in PA 6/66 (water absorption up to 9 % by weight); less severe in POM (0.2 %), negligible in PTFE and PEEK.
- **Prevention:** Use low-absorption grades (PA 12, POM); pre-condition the bearing; design clearance to account for worst-case swelling; use heat-stabilised and hydrolysis-stabilised grades.

*(Other failure modes sometimes credited: fatigue, chemical attack, electrical-erosion in current-carrying bearings, and UV degradation for outdoor service.)*

#### Model Answer to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $D = 60$ mm = 0.060 m, $L = 80$ mm, $W = 4500$ N, $N = 100$ rpm, $PV_{lim} = 1.2$ MPa·m/s, $\mu = 0.20$.

**(i) Bearing Pressure**

$$P = \frac{W}{L \cdot D} = \frac{4500 \text{ N}}{80 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm}} = \frac{4500}{4800} = 0.9375 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Sliding Velocity**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.060 \times 100}{60} = \frac{18.85}{60} = 0.3142 \text{ m/s}$$

**(iii) PV Product**

$$PV = P \times V = 0.9375 \times 0.3142 = 0.2946 \text{ MPa·m/s}$$

**(iv) Comparison with Limit**

$$PV_{operating} = 0.2946 \text{ MPa·m/s} \ll PV_{lim} = 1.2 \text{ MPa·m/s}$$

The operating PV is only **~ 24.5 %** of the material's published limit. The design has a **substantial safety margin (> 4×)**. The material is *safe on a PV basis*.

**Frictional Heat Generation** (since PV is within limit, proceed to heat calculation):

$$Q = \mu \cdot W \cdot V = 0.20 \times 4500 \times 0.3142 = 282.8 \text{ W}$$

This 282.8 W must be removed by the housing, the shaft, and ambient convection. A typical industrial mixer has a steel housing with $h \approx 30$ to $50$ W/(m²·K); for a bushing with $A_{ext} \approx 0.025$ m²:

$$\Delta T \approx \frac{282.8}{40 \times 0.025} \approx 283 \text{ °C}$$

This is the bare-bushing estimate; in a real mixer, the steel shaft conducts a large fraction of the heat away, so the practical temperature rise is much lower (typically 30 – 60 °C above ambient). Nevertheless, the design is **marginally acceptable** and would benefit from explicit thermal verification or water-cooling of the housing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers in Question B(b):**
> 1. **Mixing up the order of operations:** Compute $P$ and $V$ *separately* first, *then* multiply. Computing $PV$ by $(W/LD) \times (\pi D N/60)$ as a single chain causes unit-conversion errors.
> 2. **Failing to state the comparison:** Just computing $PV$ is not the answer. The question explicitly says "state if the operating PV is within the published limit" — the comparison is worth at least 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring the friction-heat sub-question:** If $PV$ is acceptable, the question wants the *heat calculation* (worth 3 marks). Many students stop after the PV check.
> 4. **Wrong conversion of $\pi \cdot D \cdot N$:** Forgetting the factor $1/60$ for rpm→rps, or using $N$ in rps when given in rpm. Always show the units step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Plastic Bearings (PCPOT601 — Module 3)**

### Must-Know Definitions
- **Plastic Bearing** — bearing in which the load-bearing and/or sliding surfaces are made of polymer, with self-lubricating or lubricant-retaining properties.
- **PV Limit** — maximum $P \times V$ the material sustains without thermal failure.
- **Specific Wear Rate ($K$)** — intrinsic wear resistance; lower is better.
- **Conformability** — polymer's ability to deform plastically and distribute contact pressure.
- **Embeddability** — ability of polymer to absorb hard particles and protect the counterface.

### Must-Know Formulas
- $P = W/(L \cdot D)$
- $V = \pi D N / 60$
- $PV = P \times V$
- $Q = \mu W V$
- $\Delta T = Q/(hA)$
- $V_w = K W s$ (Archard, polymer form)
- $\Delta C = (\alpha_p - \alpha_s) D \Delta T / 2$

### Must-Know Material Numbers
- **Limiting PV ranges:** PA 0.4 – 0.9; POM 0.6 – 1.0; PTFE 0.4 – 0.6 (unfilled), 1.5 (filled); UHMWPE 0.3 – 0.5; PEEK 1.0 – 1.5; PI 1.5 – 2.5 MPa·m/s.
- **Max service T:** PA 80–100, POM 100, PTFE 260, UHMWPE 80, PEEK 250, PI 300 °C.
- **CTE of polymers:** 50 – 150 × 10⁻⁶/K; **of steel:** 12 × 10⁻⁶/K (≈ 10× lower).
- **Thermal conductivity of unfilled polymers:** 0.2 – 0.5 W/(m·K).

### Must-Know Design Rules
1. Always check **PV first**; then check **thermal**; then check **wear life**; then check **creep**.
2. Always provide **thermal clearance** for the polymer's higher CTE.
3. Filled polymers (glass, carbon, MoS₂, graphite) outperform unfilled polymers on PV and wear, but cost more.
4. For oscillating/rocking motion, phenolic-fabric composites are preferred.
5. For high speed, the polymer's cage in a steel ball bearing is the modern solution.
6. **Never** let interface temperature exceed the polymer's softening point (Tg for amorphous, Tm for semi-crystalline).

### Must-Know Failure Modes
- **Thermal runaway** (PV > limit)
- **Excessive wear** (high $K$ value, abrasive environment)
- **Creep** (sustained load, high T)
- **Swelling** (water/fluid absorption, especially PA)

### Must-Know Standard Tests
- **ASTM D3702** — Thrust washer test for PV limit
- **ASTM G99** — Pin-on-disc friction and wear test
- **ISO 899** — Creep test
- **ISO 75 / ISO 306** — Heat deflection / Vicat softening
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Polymer Piping?

**Polymer piping** refers to a system of tubular conduits manufactured from polymeric materials (thermoplastics, thermosets, or fiber-reinforced composites) used for the conveyance of fluids, gases, slurries, and semi-solids across diverse operating environments — from domestic water supply to industrial chemical transport, agricultural irrigation, gas distribution, and underground drainage.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** (PCPOT601 Module 3), polymer piping is studied as a finished engineered product where **material selection**, **structural design**, **dimensional standardization**, and **performance testing** converge to deliver a leak-free, long-life fluid transport system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Alignment):**
> *Polymer piping is an engineered assembly of plastic pipes, fittings, joints, and valves — designed using the principles of viscoelastic stress analysis, hydrostatic pressure design (HDB/PDB), and standardized dimensions (SDR/SCH/PN) — to safely transport fluids at specified pressures, temperatures, and chemical environments for a designed service life (typically 50 years).*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy: The "Flexible Highway"

Imagine a **bicycle tube** versus a **car tire tube**. Both are rubber-based tubes, but they are designed for different pressures, loads, and lifetimes. A polymer pipe is conceptually identical:
- The **tube wall** is the polymer itself (PVC, PE, PEX, PP, PB).
- The **inflating fluid** is the water, gas, or chemical being transported under pressure.
- The **road/load** is the soil load (underground), internal pressure, thermal expansion, and external mechanical stresses.

Just as a tire tube must be thick enough to contain a specific pressure for a specific lifetime, a **plastic pipe must have a minimum wall thickness** to contain its internal pressure for 50 years without creep rupture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Metal Piping:**
> - Metals fail by **yielding** (short-term, ductile).
> - Polymers fail by **creep rupture / stress cracking** (long-term, time-dependent).
> Hence, plastic pipe design is **time-dependent** (uses Long-Term Hydrostatic Strength — LTHS), whereas metal pipe design is **time-independent** (uses Yield Strength).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Polymer Piping

| Metric | Symbol / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Outside Diameter | **OD (mm)** | Fixed by standard (ISO, ASTM, IS) |
| Wall Thickness | **t (mm)** | Variable based on pressure class |
| Standard Dimension Ratio | **SDR = OD/t** | Key design parameter |
| Pipe Series (S) | **S = (OD/t − 1)/2** | Used in ISO 4065 |
| Pressure Nominal | **PN (bar)** | Max allowable working pressure |
| Design Stress | **σ (MPa)** | Derived from MRS via design coefficient C |
| Minimum Required Strength | **MRS (MPa)** | LTHS at 50 years, 20 °C, 97.5 % LCL |
| Hydrostatic Design Basis | **HDB (psi)** | ASTM equivalent of MRS |
| Design Coefficient | **C (dimensionless)** | Safety factor: **1.25 (water) to 4.0 (gas)** |
| Service Temperature | **T (°C)** | Reduces allowable pressure |
| Service Life | **L (years)** | Typically **50 years** |
| Creep Modulus | **E(t) (MPa)** | Time-dependent modulus of polymer |
| Thermal Expansion Coeff. | **α (mm/m·K)** | **10× to 15×** that of steel |

---

## 1.4 Common Polymer Materials Used in Piping

> [!NOTE]
> **Major Thermoplastic Polymers for Pipes:**
> 1. **uPVC / PVC-U** — Unplasticized PVC: water supply, drainage, irrigation.
> 2. **PE (PE 63, PE 80, PE 100)** — Polyethylene: gas distribution, water mains, mining.
> 3. **HDPE** — High-Density PE: marine outfalls, industrial, electrofusion systems.
> 4. **PP (PP-R, PP-H, PP-B)** — Polypropylene: hot & cold water, chemical.
> 5. **PB (Polybutylene)** — Hot water, underfloor heating.
> 6. **PEX (Cross-linked PE)** — Hot water, radiant heating.
> 7. **CPVC (Chlorinated PVC)** — Hot water, industrial chemical.
> 8. **GRP / FRP** — Glass Reinforced Plastic: large-diameter, corrosive, offshore.
> 9. **ABS** — Drain, waste, vent (DWV).
> 10. **PVDF** — Ultra-pure chemicals, semiconductor industry.

---

## 1.5 Why Study Polymer Piping Under "Design & Testing"?

The KTU 2024 Module 3 places piping as a **flagship application** of polymer product design because it simultaneously demands:
- **Material science knowledge** (creep, environmental stress cracking).
- **Mechanical design** (pressure vs. wall thickness math).
- **Standards adherence** (IS 4985, IS 14333, ISO 4427, ASTM D3035, DIN 8074).
- **Quality testing** (hydrostatic, burst, impact, opacity, heat reversion).
- **Joint engineering** (solvent cementing, butt fusion, electrofusion, mechanical).

Thus, **Piping is the most "integrative" topic in polymer product design.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three Pillars of Pipe Design

Polymer pipe design rests on three coupled theoretical pillars:

1. **Viscoelastic Stress Analysis** — polymers are time-dependent.
2. **Hydrostatic Design Strength (HDS)** — the long-term strength, not the short-term yield.
3. **Standardized Dimensional Classification** — SDR / S / PN / SCH.

### Pillar 1: Viscoelastic Behavior & Creep

Polymers, being viscoelastic, exhibit **time-dependent strain** under constant stress:

$$\varepsilon(t) = \frac{\sigma_0}{E(t)}$$

where **E(t)** is the **creep modulus** (decreases with time). For design, we avoid full creep analysis and use the **Hydrostatic Design Basis (HDB)** or **Minimum Required Strength (MRS)** approach, which already incorporates the 50-year lifetime criterion.

### Pillar 2: Hydrostatic Design Stress (HDS / σ_d)

The design stress is the **long-term hydrostatic strength** of the pipe material divided by a **design coefficient C**:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\text{MRS}}{C} = \frac{\text{HDB}}{C}$$

**Standard values of C (per ISO 12162 / ASTM D2837):**

| Application | Design Coefficient C |
|---|---|
| Water at 20 °C | **1.25** |
| Water at up to 40 °C | **1.4 – 1.6** |
| Industrial fluids | **1.6 – 2.0** |
| Compressed gases | **2.0 – 4.0** (high safety) |
| Above-ground (unvented) | **Add 0.2 to C** |
| Soil/sand bedding variability | **Add 0.2 to C** |

### Pillar 3: Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) Classification

The SDR concept is the **backbone of international pipe standards** because it links wall thickness to internal pressure capacity **independent of diameter**.

$$\boxed{\text{SDR} = \frac{\text{OD}}{t}}$$

- **SDR 11** → thick wall → high pressure.
- **SDR 41** → thin wall → low pressure (drainage).
- **SDR 17.6** → medium (typical PE 80 water main).

The **ISO Pipe Series S** is related by:

$$\boxed{S = \frac{\text{SDR} - 1}{2} = \frac{\text{OD} - t}{2t}}$$

---

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Equations of Pipe Design

### Equation 1: Hoop Stress (Thin-Wall Pressure Vessel)

For an internally pressurized thin-wall cylinder, the **circumferential (hoop) stress** is twice the axial stress:

$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$$

where:
- **P** = internal pressure (MPa)
- **D** = mean diameter ≈ OD − t (mm)
- **t** = wall thickness (mm)

> **Why this matters:** This is the **governing stress** for burst failure. It is the *first* equation KTU expects in any pipe design problem.

### Equation 2: Design Equation (ISO Equation)

By substituting D = OD − t and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\sigma_d = \frac{P \cdot (\text{SDR} - 1)}{2} = \frac{P \cdot S}{1}}$$

Solving for **Pressure Nominal (PN)**:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{\text{SDR} - 1} = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{2S + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{S}}$$

Or more commonly stated as:

$$\boxed{\text{PN} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d}{S}}$$

where σ_d is in MPa and PN is in bar (10 bar = 1 MPa).

### Equation 3: Derating for Temperature (PVC/PE/PP)

For every polymer, increasing service temperature **reduces allowable pressure**. The derating factor **f_T** is applied:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{allow}}(T) = \text{PN} \cdot f_T}$$

| Temp (°C) | PE 80 / PE 100 | PVC-U | PP-R |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 |
| 30 | 0.90 | 0.90 | 0.95 |
| 40 | 0.80 | 0.75 | 0.85 |
| 50 | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.70 |
| 60 | 0.60 | 0.40 | 0.55 |
| 80 | — | — | 0.30 |

---

## 2.3 Deflection, Buckling & Soil Loading (Underground Pipes)

For buried polymer pipes (e.g., HDPE sewer lines), **ring deflection** under soil load is critical:

$$\frac{\Delta y}{D} = \frac{0.01 \cdot W \cdot D}{E \cdot t^3 + 0.05 \cdot E' \cdot D^3} \cdot 100 \,\%$$

For **buckling (collapse) pressure** of a flexible pipe:

$$P_{\text{cr}} = \frac{2 E}{1-\nu^2} \cdot \left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Plastic Pipes Buckle Easily:**
> Since P_cr depends on **(t/D)³**, a small reduction in wall thickness drastically reduces buckling resistance. PE pipes must be selected considering **negative pressure (vacuum)** and **external groundwater pressure**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Print-Friendly)

| # | Formula | Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | Hoop stress | MPa |
| 2 | $\text{SDR} = \frac{\text{OD}}{t}$ | Dimension class | — |
| 3 | $S = \frac{\text{SDR}-1}{2}$ | Pipe Series (ISO) | — |
| 4 | $\sigma_d = \frac{\text{MRS}}{C}$ | Design stress | MPa |
| 5 | $P = \frac{2\sigma_d}{\text{SDR}-1}$ | Pressure from SDR | MPa |
| 6 | $\text{PN} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d}{S}$ | Pressure Nominal | bar |
| 7 | $P_{\text{allow}} = \text{PN} \cdot f_T$ | Temperature derating | bar |
| 8 | $P_{\text{cr}} = \frac{2E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\frac{t}{D}\right)^3$ | Buckling pressure | MPa |
| 9 | $t = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2\sigma_d + P}$ | Min. wall thickness | mm |
| 10 | $\Delta L = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion | mm |
| 11 | $\text{Burst Factor} = \frac{P_{\text{burst}}}{P_{\text{working}}} \geq 2.5$ | Safety check | — |
| 12 | $\text{Re} = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | — |

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility of the Theory

| Domain | Application of Pipe Design Equations |
|---|---|
| **Water Supply (IS 4985)** | PVC pipes sized for PN 4 / 6 / 10 / 12.5 bar |
| **Gas Distribution (IS 14885, ISO 4437)** | PE 80/100, C ≥ 2.0, high safety |
| **Irrigation (IS 12786)** | HDPE drip/lateral lines |
| **Chemical Plants** | PVDF, PP-H, GRP for H₂SO₄, HCl, NaOH |
| **Offshore & Marine** | HDPE for outfalls, ballast |
| **Hot & Cold Plumbing (IS 15778)** | PP-R, CPVC, PEX |
| **Sewerage (IS 15328)** | Structured-wall HDPE |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world takeaway:** Every plastic pipe you see in a city — whether it is a 110 mm PVC sewer line in a housing colony, a 630 mm HDPE water main, or a 32 mm PEX line in a bathroom — has been sized using the **ISO design equation** above. The KTU examiner expects you to derive and apply these formulas fluently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hoop Stress in a Thin-Wall Pipe

**Starting premise:** A cylindrical pipe of mean diameter D, wall thickness t, subjected to internal gauge pressure P.

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:**
Cut the pipe longitudinally along its length. The two halves are held together by the internal pressure acting on the projected area. Length of cut = L (say 1 m), projected area = D · L.

**Step 2 — Force Balance:**
The force trying to split the cylinder (hoop force) is:
$$F_{\text{hoop}} = P \cdot D \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Resisting Force:**
This force is resisted by the wall acting over the cross-sectional area of the two cut walls:
$$A_{\text{wall}} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

**Step 4 — Equate Stress:**
$$\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{F_{\text{hoop}}}{A_{\text{wall}}} = \frac{P \cdot D \cdot L}{2 t \cdot L}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\text{hoop}} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}}$$

**Step 6 — Axial Stress (for completeness):**
A longitudinal cut of length L exposed area A_x = π D²/4. The wall area A_w = π (D+t)·t. So:

$$\sigma_{\text{axial}} = \frac{P \cdot \pi D^2/4}{\pi D t + \pi t^2} \approx \frac{P D}{4 t}$$

Thus **hoop stress is exactly 2× the axial stress** — a classic KTU result.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ISO Pressure Equation (PN from S and σ_d)

**Step 1 — Start from hoop stress equation:**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$$

**Step 2 — Replace D with mean diameter (OD − t):**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{P \cdot (\text{OD} - t)}{2 t}$$

**Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by t:**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\text{OD}}{t} - 1\right) = \frac{P}{2} \cdot (\text{SDR} - 1)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for P (in MPa):**
$$P = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{\text{SDR} - 1}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute S = (SDR − 1)/2:**
$$P = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{2 S} = \frac{\sigma_d}{S} \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Convert to bar (1 MPa = 10 bar):**

$$\boxed{\text{PN (bar)} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d \text{ (MPa)}}{S}}$$

This is the **single most important equation** in KTU pipe design numericals.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Wall Thickness from Design Pressure

Given: design pressure P, OD, design stress σ_d, find t.

**Step 1 — Start with hoop equation:**
$$P = \frac{2 \sigma_d \cdot t}{D + t} \quad \text{(using mean dia. } D = OD - t, \text{ so } D + t = OD \text{ approx.)}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange:**
$$P \cdot (D + t) = 2 \sigma_d \cdot t$$
$$P \cdot D + P \cdot t = 2 \sigma_d \cdot t$$
$$P \cdot D = 2 \sigma_d \cdot t - P \cdot t = t(2 \sigma_d - P)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for t:**

$$\boxed{t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \sigma_d - P} = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2 \sigma_d + P}}$$

(The D in the first form is mean diameter; OD in the second form is the outer diameter — both forms are used in KTU answer scripts.)

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 1: PVC Pipe Sizing (15-Mark Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**
> A PVC-U water distribution pipe is required to carry water at a working pressure of **10 bar** at **30 °C**. The minimum required strength (MRS) of the material is **25 MPa**. The design coefficient C is **1.6** (for water at 30 °C). Outer diameter is **160 mm**. Determine:
> (a) Design stress σ_d
> (b) Pipe Series S and suitable SDR
> (c) Wall thickness t
> (d) Verify against temperature derating
> (e) Check burst safety factor (assume minimum burst pressure 30 bar)

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Design stress:**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{\text{MRS}}{C} = \frac{25}{1.6} = 15.625 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Pipe Series S and SDR:**

PN = 10 bar. Using PN = 10σ_d / S:

$$S = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d}{\text{PN}} = \frac{10 \cdot 15.625}{10} = 15.625$$

Round up to standard series: **S = 16** (conservative, also matches PE/PVC standard series).

$$\text{SDR} = 2S + 1 = 2(16) + 1 = 33$$

Closest standard SDR: **SDR 33** (or step down to SDR 26 for safety).

**Part (c) — Wall thickness:**

$$t = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2\sigma_d + P} = \frac{1.0 \cdot 160}{2(15.625) + 1.0} = \frac{160}{32.25} = 4.96 \text{ mm}$$

Round up: **t = 5.0 mm**.

Verify SDR: 160/5.0 = 32 ≈ SDR 33 ✓

**Part (d) — Temperature derating check:**
At 30 °C for PVC-U, **f_T = 0.90**:
$$P_{\text{allow}} = 10 \cdot 0.90 = 9.0 \text{ bar}$$

Since working pressure (10 bar) > P_allow (9.0 bar) → **Use next higher SDR (SDR 26, t = 6.2 mm)**. ✅

**Part (e) — Burst safety factor:**
$$\text{BF} = \frac{P_{\text{burst}}}{P_{\text{working}}} = \frac{30}{10} = 3.0$$

Required: **BF ≥ 2.5** → ✓ **SAFE**

**Final design:** PVC-U, OD 160 mm, SDR 26, t = 6.2 mm, PN 10.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 2: HDPE Gas Pipe (14-Mark KTU Style)

> A PE 80 natural gas distribution pipe must operate at **4 bar** at **20 °C**. MRS = **8.0 MPa**, C = **2.5** (gas). OD = **63 mm**. Find SDR, t, and verify against derating at 30 °C ambient.

**Solution:**

$$\sigma_d = \frac{8.0}{2.5} = 3.2 \text{ MPa}$$

PN = 4 bar. From PN = 10σ_d / S:
$$S = \frac{10 \cdot 3.2}{4} = 8.0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{Standard S} = 8$$

$$\text{SDR} = 2(8) + 1 = 17$$

**Standard SDR choice: SDR 17.6** (closely matches S = 8.3).

$$t = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2\sigma_d + P} = \frac{0.4 \cdot 63}{2(3.2) + 0.4} = \frac{25.2}{6.8} = 3.71 \text{ mm}$$

Round up: **t = 3.8 mm**. (SDR 63/3.8 = 16.6 ≈ SDR 17 ✓)

**At 30 °C:** f_T = 0.90 for PE 80:
$$P_{\text{allow}} = 4.0 \cdot 0.90 = 3.6 \text{ bar}$$

Working pressure 4 bar > 3.6 bar → **Use SDR 11 (t = 5.8 mm)** for safety.

**Final design:** PE 80, OD 63 mm, SDR 11, t = 5.8 mm.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Key Design Decisions at a Glance

| Decision Variable | Equation / Rule | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Material Selection | Based on T, chemical, pressure | See §1.4 |
| MRS / HDB | LTHS at 50 yr, 20 °C, 97.5 % LCL | 6–25 MPa |
| Design Coefficient C | ISO 12162 Table | 1.25–4.0 |
| Design Stress σ_d | MRS / C | 2–16 MPa |
| Pressure Nominal PN | 10σ_d / S | 4–25 bar |
| Pipe Series S | (SDR−1)/2 | 5, 8, 10, 12.5, 16, 20 |
| Wall Thickness t | P·OD / (2σ_d + P) | 1–50 mm |
| Burst Safety Factor | P_burst / P_work | ≥ 2.5 (water), ≥ 3.0 (gas) |
| Temperature Factor f_T | From material derating table | 0.3–1.0 |
| Joint Method | Solvent / Fusion / Mechanical | Per application |
| Service Life | Design target | 50 years |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define MRS and HDB in polymer pipe design. Why is a 50-year lifetime used? *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

- **MRS (Minimum Required Strength):** The value of the long-term hydrostatic strength (LTHS) of the pipe material, rounded down to the nearest lower value of the R-series (R 10 series per ISO 12162), that the material is required to have at 20 °C for a service life of **50 years** at a confidence level of **97.5 %** (lower confidence limit, LCL).

- **HDB (Hydrostatic Design Basis):** The ASTM equivalent of MRS, expressed in **psi**, also based on the 50-year, 97.5 % LCL hydrostatic strength at 23 °C.

- **Why 50 years:** Most municipal and building piping systems are designed for a service life of 50 years, aligning with the typical lifetime of buildings, infrastructure, and capital investment cycles. It is the conventional design lifetime established by ISO 12162 and ASTM D2837.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark each for MRS, HDB; 1 mark for the 50-year justification.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the design coefficient C? State standard values for water and gas. *(3 marks)*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

The **design coefficient C** is a dimensionless safety factor applied to the MRS to obtain the **allowable design stress** σ_d. It accounts for service conditions, installation variability, fluid aggressiveness, and consequence of failure.

$$\sigma_d = \frac{\text{MRS}}{C}$$

**Standard values (per ISO 12162):**
- **Water at 20 °C:** C = **1.25**
- **Water at 40 °C:** C ≈ **1.4 – 1.6**
- **Industrial chemicals:** C ≈ **1.6 – 2.0**
- **Natural gas / compressed gases:** C = **2.0 – 4.0** (with 2.0 for below-ground PE gas, 3.0–4.0 for higher-risk)

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the ISO design equation for polymer pipe wall thickness, starting from the hoop stress equation. Clearly define all symbols. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** An HDPE pipe (PE 100) is to be designed for water distribution at 20 °C with a working pressure of **16 bar**. Given MRS = 10 MPa, C = 1.25, OD = 110 mm, determine: (i) design stress, (ii) pipe series S, (iii) wall thickness t, (iv) most suitable standard SDR, (v) check the burst safety factor if the minimum burst pressure is **40 bar**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Hoop stress in a thin-wall cylinder:**

For a cylindrical pipe of mean diameter D and wall thickness t, subjected to internal gauge pressure P, the hoop stress is given by force balance across a longitudinal cut:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t}$$

*(1 mark for the equation + force-balance explanation)*

**Step 2 — Substitute mean diameter D = OD − t:**

For a pipe of outside diameter OD and wall thickness t, the mean diameter is D = OD − t. Substituting:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{P \cdot (\text{OD} - t)}{2 t}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 3 — Introduce SDR:**

$$\text{SDR} = \frac{\text{OD}}{t} \Rightarrow \text{OD} = \text{SDR} \cdot t$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_d = \frac{P \cdot (\text{SDR} \cdot t - t)}{2 t} = \frac{P \cdot (\text{SDR} - 1)}{2}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 4 — Solve for P (pressure):**

$$P = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{\text{SDR} - 1}$$

*(1 mark)*

**Step 5 — Introduce Pipe Series S:**

$$S = \frac{\text{SDR} - 1}{2} \Rightarrow \text{SDR} - 1 = 2S$$

Substituting:

$$P = \frac{2 \sigma_d}{2 S} = \frac{\sigma_d}{S} \text{ MPa}$$

Converting to bar (1 MPa = 10 bar):

$$\boxed{\text{PN (bar)} = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d \text{ (MPa)}}{S}}$$

*(2 marks for final boxed equation and clear conclusion)*

**Symbol list (1 mark):**
- σ_d = design stress (MPa)
- PN = pressure nominal (bar)
- S = pipe series (dimensionless)
- SDR = standard dimension ratio
- P = internal pressure (MPa)
- t = wall thickness (mm)
- OD = outside diameter (mm)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Design (7 marks):**

**(i) Design stress (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{\text{MRS}}{C} = \frac{10}{1.25} = 8.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Pipe series S (1 mark):**
$$S = \frac{10 \cdot \sigma_d}{\text{PN}} = \frac{10 \cdot 8.0}{16} = 5.0$$
Standard series: **S = 5**.

**(iii) Wall thickness (2 marks):**
$$t = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2\sigma_d + P} = \frac{1.6 \cdot 110}{2(8.0) + 1.6} = \frac{176}{17.6} = 10.0 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Most suitable standard SDR (1 mark):**
$$\text{SDR} = \frac{\text{OD}}{t} = \frac{110}{10.0} = 11$$
**Standard SDR: SDR 11** ✓ (matches S = 5)

**(v) Burst safety factor (2 marks):**
$$\text{BF} = \frac{P_{\text{burst}}}{P_{\text{working}}} = \frac{40}{16} = 2.5$$
Required minimum BF for water at 20 °C = **2.5** (per ISO 4427) → **Exactly meets the requirement** — borderline acceptable; for extra safety, choose **SDR 9 (t = 12.3 mm)** which gives BF ≈ 3.0.

**Final design:** HDPE PE 100, OD 110 mm, SDR 11, t = 10 mm, PN 16 bar.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the viscoelastic nature of polymers and how it affects long-term pipe design. Compare with metallic pipe design philosophy. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A PVC-U pipe is to carry a chemical at **40 °C** with working pressure of **6 bar**. OD = **90 mm**, MRS = **25 MPa**, C = **1.6** (industrial). Determine the required SDR and wall thickness. Also evaluate the operating safety at **50 °C** if the temperature derating factor f_T drops to **0.70**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Viscoelasticity and Pipe Design (7 marks):**

**Viscoelastic behavior of polymers (3 marks):**
- Polymers exhibit **time-dependent strain** under constant stress — this is *creep*.
- The **creep modulus E(t)** decreases with time. At short times, polymers are stiff; at long times, they deform more.
- This is described by the **isochronous stress–strain curves** and **creep rupture curves** in the ISO 9080 regression.
- Polymers also exhibit **stress relaxation** — stress decays at constant strain.
- Key implication: **failure in polymers is time-dependent (creep rupture, ESC), not instantaneous yielding**.

**Effect on pipe design (2 marks):**
- Pipe strength is not given by short-term tensile or yield strength.
- It is given by the **long-term hydrostatic strength (LTHS)**, extrapolated to **50 years** at the 97.5 % lower confidence limit.
- This LTHS becomes the **MRS** (in ISO) or **HDB** (in ASTM).
- The design stress σ_d = MRS / C, where **C ≥ 1.25** (water) and **C up to 4.0** (gas) to compensate for the time-dependence.

**Comparison with metallic pipe design (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Polymer Pipe | Metallic Pipe |
|---|---|---|
| Governing property | LTHS / MRS (50 yr, 97.5 % LCL) | Yield Strength σ_y (short-term) |
| Time dependency | **Time-dependent** (creep-based) | Time-independent |
| Design equation | σ_d = MRS / C | σ_d = σ_y / FoS |
| Failure mode | Creep rupture, ESC, buckling | Yielding, fatigue, corrosion |
| Design coefficient C | 1.25 to 4.0 | 1.5 to 2.5 (typical FoS) |
| Temperature effect | Severe (derating 30–60 %) | Moderate (derating 10–20 %) |
| Corrosion | None | Major concern |
| Joints | Fusion / solvent / mechanical | Welding / threading / flanged |

---

**Part (b) — PVC-U Pipe Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Design stress (1 mark):**
$$\sigma_d = \frac{25}{1.6} = 15.625 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Pipe series S (1 mark):**
$$S = \frac{10 \cdot 15.625}{6} = 26.04$$
Standard series: **S = 26.67** (commercial), or round up to **S = 25**.

Choosing **S = 25** gives slightly higher safety. Then:
$$\text{SDR} = 2(25) + 1 = 51$$
Standard SDR choice: **SDR 51**.

**(iii) Wall thickness (1 mark):**
$$t = \frac{P \cdot \text{OD}}{2\sigma_d + P} = \frac{0.6 \cdot 90}{2(15.625) + 0.6} = \frac{54}{31.85} = 1.696 \text{ mm}$$
Round up to **t = 1.8 mm** (or 2.0 mm for tolerance). **SDR 51 → t = 1.8 mm** ✓

**(iv) Operating safety at 50 °C (4 marks):**
New f_T = 0.70:
$$P_{\text{allow}}(50°C) = \text{PN} \cdot f_T = 6 \cdot 0.70 = 4.2 \text{ bar}$$

Since working pressure (6 bar) > P_allow (4.2 bar), the pipe is **UNSAFE at 50 °C** in its current SDR 51 design.

**Remedy options (must list at least 2 for full marks):**
1. **Step down to SDR 33** (t = 2.7 mm) — increases PN to ~9 bar, then derated PN = 6.3 bar > 6 bar ✓
2. **Step down to SDR 26** (t = 3.5 mm) — provides ample margin.
3. **Reduce the working pressure** to ≤ 4.2 bar.
4. **Cool the fluid** to < 40 °C.

**Final design:** PVC-U, OD 90 mm, **SDR 33, t = 2.7 mm**, suitable for 6 bar at 50 °C with adequate safety.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Pipe Design Numericals (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Unit Mix-up (loses 1–2 marks):** Forgetting that 1 MPa = **10 bar**. Converting PN to MPa before plugging into formulas is critical.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Temperature Derating (loses 2–3 marks):** Many students compute the pipe for 20 °C and stop. The examiner **always expects** a derating step if service temperature is mentioned.
>
> 3. **Using OD instead of mean diameter:** This is acceptable in thin-wall design if you justify it, but using D = OD − t is the **cleaner KTU-expected** approach. Don't mix both inconsistently.
>
> 4. **Skipping Burst Safety Factor check (loses 1 mark):** ISO 4427 / ASTM D3037 mandates **BF ≥ 2.5 for water** and **BF ≥ 3.0 for gas**. Always verify.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the design coefficient C (loses 1 mark):** Some students write σ = MRS directly, missing the safety factor.
>
> 6. **Rounding down instead of up:** Wall thickness must always be **rounded up** to the next standard. A pipe that is marginally undersized will fail creep rupture.
>
> 7. **Ignoring Soil Loading & Buckling (loses 2 marks for buried pipes):** For underground HDPE pipes, always check **buckling pressure** if external water table exists.
>
> 8. **Confusing MRS with HDB units:** MRS is in **MPa**; HDB is in **psi** (1 MPa ≈ 145 psi). Converting is often needed in numericals that mix standards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **PIPING — FINAL REVISION CARDS (Save for Last-Minute Revision)**

### Card 1: Core Design Equation
- **PN (bar) = 10 × σ_d / S**, where σ_d = MRS / C
- **t = P × OD / (2σ_d + P)**
- **SDR = OD / t**
- **S = (SDR − 1) / 2**

### Card 2: Hoop Stress
- σ_hoop = P × D / (2t) — the **governing** stress (twice the axial).

### Card 3: Design Coefficients (Memorize)
- Water 20 °C → **C = 1.25**
- Water 40 °C → **C ≈ 1.4–1.6**
- Industrial → **C ≈ 1.6–2.0**
- Gas → **C = 2.0–4.0**

### Card 4: Burst Safety Factor
- Water → **≥ 2.5**
- Gas → **≥ 3.0**
- Formula: BF = P_burst / P_working

### Card 5: Temperature Derating (Always Apply)
- P_allow(T) = PN × f_T
- f_T < 1.0 above 20 °C; can be 0.3 at 80 °C for PP-R.

### Card 6: Buckling of Buried Pipes
- P_cr = (2E / (1−ν²)) × (t/D)³
- Important for HDPE under negative pressure or high water table.

### Card 7: Joint Methods
- **PVC / CPVC** → Solvent cementing
- **PE / PP** → Butt fusion / Electrofusion
- **PP-R / PEX** → Mechanical / Heat fusion
- **GRP** → Filament winding + adhesive

### Card 8: Standards (Indian)
- **IS 4985** — PVC-U water
- **IS 14333** — HDPE water
- **IS 14885** — PE gas
- **IS 12786** — Irrigation
- **IS 15778** — PP-R
- **IS 15328** — HDPE structured-wall sewer

### Card 9: Common Tests on Plastic Pipes
- Hydrostatic pressure test
- Burst pressure test
- Impact test (falling weight, IS 12235)
- Heat reversion (PE)
- Opacity test (sewer pipes)
- ESCR (Environmental Stress Crack Resistance)
- Density and MFI verification
- Dimensions and ovality check

### Card 10: Advantages of Polymer Pipes (Memorize for 3-mark Questions)
- Corrosion-free, chemically resistant
- Lightweight (1/5th to 1/8th of steel)
- Smooth bore → low friction loss → **C = 140 (Hazen-Williams)** vs. 100 for steel
- Flexible → seismic resistant
- Long life (**>50 years**)
- Easy jointing (heat fusion, no welding)
- No scale build-up, bio-film resistant
- Cost-effective for diameters up to **1600 mm**

### Card 11: Limitations
- Temperature limited (typically < 95 °C for thermoplastics)
- Lower pressure rating than metals at high temperatures
- UV degradation (carbon black addition in PE)
- Lower modulus → deflection under soil load
- Susceptible to point loads and rock impingement (bedding care)
- Permeation by certain hydrocarbons

### Card 12: Memory Hook for KTU Numericals
- **MRS → C → σ_d → S → PN → t → SDR → derating → BF check**
- Always state the **standard SDR/S** chosen and the **standard code** (e.g., IS 4985, ISO 4427).

---

> **END OF MODULE 3 — PIPING NOTES**
> *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) — Outcome-Based Education format. Aligned with Course Outcomes CO3, CO4 of PCPOT601.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Films
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Films --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Polymer Films > [!IMPORTANT] > **Polymer Film (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **polymer film** is defined as a thin, continuous, flexible sheet of polymeric material with a thickness typically less than **0.25 mm (250 μm)**, produced by extrusion, casting, calendering, or blowing processes, and characterized by a high surface-area-to-volume ratio. The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies films as one of the most commercially significant polymer product categories, particularly within the **flexible packaging industry**, which accounts for nearly **40% of global polymer consumption**. ### 1.2 Classification of Polymer Films Polymer films are broadly classified based on their **manufacturing process**, **orientation state**, and **layer architecture**: | Classification Basis | Categories | |---|---| | By Manufacturing Process | Cast films, Blown films, Calendered films, Solution-cast films | | By Orientation | Non-oriented, Monoaxially oriented, **Biaxially oriented (BOP\*)** | | By Layer Architecture | Monolayer, Multilayer, Coextruded, Laminated | | By Function | Barrier films, Heat-seal films, Shrink films, Stretch films, Conductive films | > [!NOTE] > **Common Biaxially Oriented Film Abbreviations (BOP\*):** > - **BOPP** — Biaxially Oriented Polypropylene > - **BOPET** — Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene Terephthalate > - **BOPA** — Biaxially Oriented Polyamide (Nylon) > - **BOPS** — Biaxially Oriented Polystyrene > - **BOPE** — Biaxially Oriented Polyethylene ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Polymer Film as a "Polymer Sandwich" Think of a polymer film like a **flexible kitchen cling wrap** vs. a **balloon sheet**: - A **cling wrap** is *cast* — the polymer melt is poured onto a chilled roller and "ironed" flat. Result: glossy, clear, uniform thickness, but weaker in one direction. - A **balloon rubber** is *blown* — the polymer is inflated like a balloon, stretching in two directions simultaneously. Result: balanced strength in all directions, like a stretched trampoline. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** *"CAST = Calm & STretched in one direction"* and *"BLOW = Balanced, Like an inflated balloon, Oriented in two Ways."* ### 1.4 Why Films Matter in Polymer Engineering Polymer films are critical because they: 1. **Extend shelf life** of food (barrier properties) 2. **Reduce material usage** (down-gauging trend: films getting thinner) 3. **Enable sustainability** (recyclable mono-material structures) 4. **Provide functional surfaces** (anti-fog, anti-microbial, conductive) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Film Manufacturing Processes — Conceptual Breakdown #### 2.1.1 Cast Film Extrusion (Chill Roll / Chill Cast Process) The polymer melt is extruded through a **slot die (T-die)** and immediately quenched on a chilled roller. The film is then wound up after passing through nip rollers. **Why this works:** Rapid quenching (cooling rates of **50–100 °C/s**) suppresses crystallization, producing a **transparent, amorphous, dimensionally uniform** film with isotropic properties in the machine direction. **Key Process Variables:** - Melt temperature: **180–280 °C** (depending on polymer) - Chill roll temperature: **15–30 °C** - Line speed: **up to 500 m/min** in modern lines - Air knife: removes boundary air layer for better cooling contact #### 2.1.2 Blown Film Extrusion (Tubular Process) The polymer melt is extruded through an **annular die**, and the tube is inflated by internal air pressure. The bubble is cooled by an **air ring** and then collapsed by **collapsing frames** before being wound. **Why this works:** The biaxial stretching during inflation aligns polymer chains in **both machine direction (MD)** and **transverse direction (TD)**, producing films with **balanced mechanical properties** — perfect for sacks, liners, and stretch wraps. **Key Process Variables:** - Blow-Up Ratio (BUR): **1.5 – 4.0** - Draw-Down Ratio (DDR): **2 – 50** - Frost Line Height (FLH): **2–8× die diameter** - Die gap: **0.5 – 2.5 mm** #### 2.1.3 Calendering Used primarily for **PVC films**. The polymer mass is passed through a series of heated counter-rotating rolls (**4-roll or 5-roll calenders**) to progressively reduce thickness to **0.05 – 0.8 mm**. **Why this works:** High pressure and shear between rolls produce films with excellent **surface finish, gloss, and gauge uniformity**. #### 2.1.4 Biaxial Orientation (Tenter Frame Process) Used for BOPP, BOPET, BOPA. The film is stretched in **MD** (using heated rollers) and then **TD** (using a clip-chain tenter oven) at temperatures between **Tg and Tm**. **Why this works:** Orientation aligns molecular chains along stress directions, dramatically improving **tensile strength, modulus, barrier properties, and optical clarity**. ### 2.2 Critical Process Ratios (KTU High-Priority) | Parameter | Definition | Formula | Typical Range | |---|---|---|---| | **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | Ratio of final bubble diameter to die diameter | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$ | **1.5 – 4.0** | | **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** | Ratio of die gap to final film thickness | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{G_d}{t_f}$ | **2 – 50** | | **Machine Direction Stretch Ratio** | Ratio of haul-off speed to melt velocity | $\text{MD-R} = \dfrac{V_{\text{haul}}}{V_{\text{melt}}}$ | **2 – 100** | | **Total Orientation Ratio** | Combined effect of BUR and DDR | $\text{OR}_{\text{total}} = \text{BUR} \times \text{DDR}$ | varies | | **Transverse Direction Stretch Ratio** | Equivalent to BUR for TD orientation | $\text{TD-R} = \text{BUR}$ | **1.5 – 4.0** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Note:** Higher BUR and DDR improve mechanical and barrier properties, but excessive stretching causes **necking, film breaks, and haze** — there is an optimum processing window for each polymer. ### 2.3 Film Testing & Characterization Formulas #### 2.3.1 Mechanical Properties | Test | Standard | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Tensile Strength at Break** | ASTM D882 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F_b}{A_c}$ where $A_c = w \times t$ | **MPa** | | **Tensile Modulus (Young's)** | ASTM D882 | $E = \dfrac{\Delta \sigma}{\Delta \varepsilon}$ at 1% strain | **MPa** | | **Elongation at Break** | ASTM D882 | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | **%** | | **Tear Resistance (Elmendorf)** | ASTM D1922 | $T_r = \dfrac{F_{\text{tear}}}{t}$ | **mN/μm** or **g/μm** | | **Dart Drop Impact** | ASTM D1709 | $W_{50} = \dfrac{m_{\text{dart}} \times g \times h}{t}$ | **g/μm** | #### 2.3.2 Optical Properties | Test | Standard | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Haze** | ASTM D1003 | $H = \dfrac{T_{\text{diffuse}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | **%** | | **Gloss (60°)** | ASTM D2457 | $G = \dfrac{I_{\text{specular}}}{I_{\text{incident}}} \times 100$ | **GU** (Gloss Units) | | **Total Light Transmission** | ASTM D1003 | $T_{\text{total}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{transmitted}}}{I_{\text{incident}}} \times 100$ | **%** | | **Clarity** | ASTM D1746 | $C \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{Haze} \times \text{Width}}$ | **%** | #### 2.3.3 Barrier Properties | Test | Standard | Formula | Units | |---|---|---|---| | **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** | ASTM F1249 | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \times \Delta t}$ | **g/m²·day** | | **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** | ASTM D3985 | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q \times L}{A \times \Delta p \times t}$ | **cm³·mm/m²·day·atm** | | **Permeability Coefficient (P)** | Derived | $P = \dfrac{Q \times L}{A \times \Delta p \times \Delta t}$ | **cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg** | Where $Q$ = quantity of gas transmitted, $L$ = film thickness, $A$ = permeation area, $\Delta p$ = pressure difference, $\Delta t$ = time. #### 2.3.4 Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Governing Equation for Permeation) $$J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$ At steady state through a homogeneous film: $$J = \dfrac{D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)}{L} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$ > Where: > - $J$ = flux of permeant (cm³·cm⁻²·s⁻¹) > - $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s) > - $C_1, C_2$ = concentrations at film surfaces > - $L$ = film thickness > - $P$ = permeability coefficient > - $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application | Required Property | Typical Film Used | |---|---|---| | **Potato chip bags** | Low OTR, high moisture barrier | BOPP/met-BOPP/PE laminate | | **Fresh meat packaging** | High OTR, anti-fog | Multilayer PE/EVA with antifog | | **Pharmaceutical blisters** | High moisture + oxygen barrier | PVC/PVDC, BOPET/Al-foil | | **Greenhouse covers** | UV transmission, IR retention | LLDPE/EVA with UV stabilizers | | **Solar cell encapsulation** | UV stability, adhesion | EVA (ethylene-vinyl acetate) | | **Medical IV bags** | Flexibility, clarity, autoclavable | Multilayer PP/EVOH/PP | | **Stretch wrap for palletizing** | High elongation, cling | LLDPE with tackifier | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between BUR, DDR, and Final Film Thickness In a blown film process, mass conservation (continuity) demands that the polymer output rate at the die equals the mass flow rate at the take-up. **Step 1:** Volumetric flow rate at the die (annular): $$Q_{\text{die}} = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot G_d \cdot V_m$$ **Step 2:** Volumetric flow rate at the take-up (collapsed film): $$Q_{\text{final}} = W_f \cdot t_f \cdot V_{\text{haul}}$$ Where: - $D_d$ = die diameter - $G_d$ = die gap - $V_m$ = melt velocity at die exit - $W_f$ = flattened film width (lay-flat width × 2) - $t_f$ = final film thickness - $V_{\text{haul}}$ = take-up speed **Step 3:** Apply conservation of mass: $Q_{\text{die}} = Q_{\text{final}}$ $$\pi \cdot D_d \cdot G_d \cdot V_m = W_f \cdot t_f \cdot V_{\text{haul}}$$ **Step 4:** Recognize that lay-flat width $W_f = \pi \cdot D_b$ (where $D_b$ is bubble diameter), and define BUR and DDR: $$\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} \quad \text{and} \quad \text{DDR} = \dfrac{G_d}{t_f}$$ **Step 5:** Substituting and simplifying: $$t_f = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR} \times \dfrac{V_{\text{haul}}}{V_m}} = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR} \times \text{MD-R}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{t_f = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR} \times \text{MD-R}}}$$ > This is the **fundamental design equation** for blown film thickness prediction. A KTU 2024 favourite in problems. ### 3.2 Derivation: Tensile Stress in a Thin Film Specimen ASTM D882 uses a rectangular strip of width $w$ and thickness $t$. The tensile force $F$ produces a stress: **Step 1:** Cross-sectional area: $$A_c = w \cdot t$$ **Step 2:** Engineering stress (used in film testing, as opposed to true stress because films neck locally): $$\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A_c} = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot t}$$ **Step 3:** Engineering strain (based on initial gauge length $L_0$): $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$$ **Step 4:** Young's modulus from the initial linear-elastic region: $$E = \left. \dfrac{d\sigma}{d\varepsilon} \right|_{\varepsilon \to 0} = \dfrac{\Delta F / A_c}{\Delta L / L_0}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation: Permeation Through a Multilayer Film For a multilayer film with $n$ layers, each of thickness $L_i$ and permeability $P_i$, the **total resistance** model is analogous to electrical resistors in series: **Step 1:** Permeability of each layer (Henry's law + Fick's law): $$J = \dfrac{P_i \cdot \Delta p_i}{L_i}$$ **Step 2:** At steady state, the flux $J$ is the same through all layers. The total pressure drop is the sum of individual drops: $$\Delta p_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Delta p_i = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{J \cdot L_i}{P_i}$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $J$: $$J = \dfrac{\Delta p_{\text{total}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}}$$ **Step 4:** Define the **effective permeability** $P_{\text{eff}}$ of the multilayer film: $$P_{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{L_i}{P_i}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The *highest-barrier* layer (lowest $P_i$) dominates total resistance, but only if it is **continuous** (no pinholes or cracks). A single 6-μm EVOH layer can outperform 50 μm of LDPE. ### 3.4 Derivation: Shrinkage of Oriented Films When a biaxially oriented film is reheated above its orientation temperature (typically between $T_g$ and $T_m$), the stretched chains relax, causing shrinkage. **Step 1:** Linear shrinkage ratio in machine direction: $$S_{\text{MD}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{before}} - L_{\text{after}}}{L_{\text{before}}} \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ **Step 2:** For an oriented film with stretch ratio $\lambda$, the maximum achievable shrinkage is: $$S_{\max} = \left(1 - \dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right) \times 100 \quad [\%]$$ **Step 3:** Real shrinkage is always less due to relaxation during storage: $$S_{\text{actual}} = \eta_{\text{relax}} \cdot S_{\max}$$ Where $\eta_{\text{relax}} \in [0, 1]$ is the **relaxation efficiency**, typically **0.7 – 0.95** for freshly oriented film. ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) **Problem:** A blown film line has a die diameter of **200 mm**, die gap of **1.5 mm**, BUR of **2.5**, and a haul-off speed of **25 m/min**. The melt velocity at the die exit is **0.5 m/min**. Calculate the final film thickness. **Step 1:** Calculate the machine direction stretch ratio: $$\text{MD-R} = \dfrac{V_{\text{haul}}}{V_m} = \dfrac{25}{0.5} = 50$$ **Step 2:** Apply the blown film design equation: $$t_f = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR} \times \text{MD-R}} = \dfrac{1.5 \text{ mm}}{2.5 \times 50}$$ $$t_f = \dfrac{1.5}{125} = 0.012 \text{ mm} = 12 \text{ μm}$$ > **Answer:** $t_f = 12$ μm (a typical LLDPE stretch film thickness). ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cast vs. Blown vs. Calendered | Property | Cast Film | Blown Film | Calendered Film | |---|---|---|---| | **Optical clarity** | Excellent (haze < 1%) | Moderate (haze 3–10%) | High (haze 1–3%) | | **MD/TD balance** | Anisotropic (MD stronger) | Balanced (BUR ~ 2–3) | Slightly anisotropic | | **Thickness uniformity** | ±2–5% | ±5–10% | ±2–4% | | **Production speed** | Up to 500 m/min | 50–150 m/min | 30–100 m/min | | **Tear strength (TD)** | Low | High | Moderate | | **Dart impact** | Low | High | Moderate | | **Typical polymers** | LDPE, LLDPE, PP, PET | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PA | PVC, ABS, TPU | | **Capital cost** | High | Moderate | Very High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Process Flow: Blown Film Extrusion Line ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Polymer Resin
Hopper] --> B[Extruder
Heated Barrel
180 to 280 C] B --> C[Screen Pack
and Breaker Plate] C --> D[Annular Die
Diameter Dd] D --> E[Bubble Inflation
Air Through Die Mandrel] E --> F[Frost Line
Solidification Zone] F --> G[Air Ring
Cooling Air 15 to 25 C] G --> H[Collapsing Frames
Nip Rollers] H --> I[Slitting and
Winding Station] I --> J[Finished Roll
Flat Film] style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style F fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style H fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#bb8fce,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow: Cast Film Extrusion (Chill Roll) ```mermaid flowchart TB P1[Hopper
Polymer Pellets] --> P2[Single Screw
Extruder L/D 30 to 1] P2 --> P3[Adapter and
T Slot Die] P3 --> P4[Melt Curtain
Onto Chill Roll] P4 --> P5[Chill Roll 15 to 30 C
Polished Chrome] P5 --> P6[Air Knife
Removes Air Gap] P6 --> P7[Nip Roller
and Edge Trimming] P7 --> P8[Tension Control
and Slitting] P8 --> P9[Dual Station
Winder] style P1 fill:#fff3cd style P2 fill:#cce5ff style P3 fill:#ffd6cc style P5 fill:#d4edda style P9 fill:#e2d4f0 ``` ### 4.3 Tenter Frame Process for Biaxial Orientation (BOPP / BOPET) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Cast Sheet
Amorphous] --> S2[MD Stretch
Heated Rollers
Lambda MD 4 to 6] S2 --> S3[TD Stretch
Tenter Oven
Lambda TD 7 to 10] S3 --> S4[Heat Setting
Annealing Zone] S4 --> S5[Cooling
and Edge Trim] S5 --> S6[Slitting and
Winding] style S1 fill:#ffe5b4 style S2 fill:#ffcccb style S3 fill:#c1e1ec style S4 fill:#d4f1d4 style S6 fill:#e0c3fc ``` ### 4.4 Film Property-Performance Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Application
Need} -->|High clarity| Q2[Use Cast PP
or PET] Q1 -->|Balanced strength| Q3[Use Blown PE
or LLDPE] Q1 -->|High barrier| Q4[Multilayer
BOPP or EVOH] Q1 -->|Shrink behaviour| Q5[Biaxially Oriented
Shrink Film] Q2 --> R1[Low Haze
High Gloss] Q3 --> R2[Good MD/TD Balance
High Dart Impact] Q4 --> R3[Low OTR
Low WVTR] Q5 --> R4[High Shrinkage
Up to 80 percent at 120 C] style Q1 fill:#fff4cc style R1 fill:#cce5ff style R2 fill:#cce5ff style R3 fill:#cce5ff style R4 fill:#cce5ff ``` ### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Permeation Through Multilayer Film ```mermaid flowchart LR EXT[External
Environment
High Humidity] --> L1[Layer 1
PET
L1 equals 12 um] L1 --> L2[Layer 2
Adhesive PU
L2 equals 2 um] L2 --> L3[Layer 3
EVOH Barrier
L3 equals 6 um] L3 --> L4[Layer 4
Adhesive PU
L4 equals 2 um] L4 --> L5[Layer 5
LDPE Sealant
L5 equals 50 um] L5 --> INT[Internal
Product
Low Humidity] EXT -.- F1[Flux J
Steady State] F1 -.- L3 F1 -.- L5 style EXT fill:#fff3b0 style L3 fill:#ff7f7f,color:#fff style L5 fill:#aed6f1 style INT fill:#d5f5e3 ``` > **Interpretation:** EVOH is the rate-limiting barrier (red), analogous to a narrow valve in a water pipe. Doubling L1 from 12 μm to 24 μm barely affects the total flux, but doubling L3 from 6 μm to 12 μm halves the flux. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define polymer films. List any four methods used for the manufacture of polymer films. **Model Answer:** > A polymer film is a thin, flexible, continuous sheet of polymeric material with a thickness typically less than **0.25 mm** and a high surface-area-to-volume ratio. > > **Four manufacturing methods:** > 1. **Cast film extrusion** (chill roll process) > 2. **Blown film extrusion** (tubular process) > 3. **Calendering** (for PVC and rubber films) > 4. **Solution casting** (for cellulosics and high-performance polymers) > > *(Self-explanatory diagrams are not required, but a one-line description of each earns full marks.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** Differentiate between cast films and blown films on the basis of (i) optical clarity, (ii) MD/TD property balance, and (iii) typical polymers used. **Model Answer:** | Property | Cast Film | Blown Film | |---|---|---| | (i) Optical clarity | Excellent (haze < 1%) | Moderate (haze 3–10%) | | (ii) MD/TD balance | Anisotropic (MD stronger) | Balanced (BUR ~ 2–3) | | (iii) Typical polymers | LDPE, LLDPE, PP, PET | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE, PA | --- ### 5.2 Part B: Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > - Do **not** confuse BUR with DDR — they are independent ratios. > - Always quote **units** in final answers (μm, MPa, g/m²·day, etc.). > - In permeability problems, state the **steady-state assumption** explicitly. > - Avoid writing vague statements like *"permeation occurs through the film"* — always cite **Fick's law** or the **resistance-in-series model**. --- #### **Question A [14 Marks]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the blown film extrusion process with a neat sketch. Define **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** and **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** and state their effect on film properties. **(b) [7 Marks)** A blown film extrusion line has the following specifications: - Die diameter $D_d = 150$ mm - Die gap $G_d = 1.2$ mm - Melt velocity at die exit $V_m = 0.4$ m/min - Haul-off speed $V_{\text{haul}} = 30$ m/min - BUR = 2.8 Calculate: (i) the machine direction stretch ratio, (ii) the final film thickness, and (iii) the lay-flat width of the film. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** **Process Description (5 marks):** Polymer melt is extruded through an **annular die** in the form of a thin tube. Compressed air is introduced through the die mandrel, inflating the tube into a **bubble** with diameter $D_b$. External cooling is provided by an **air ring** that blows air at the bubble surface. The film solidifies at the **frost line** (where the polymer becomes opaque due to crystallization). The bubble is then **collapsed** by a pair of wooden or aluminium **collapsing frames**, passed through **nip rollers**, and wound up. **Definitions (2 marks):** $$\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} = \dfrac{\text{Bubble diameter}}{\text{Die diameter}}$$ $$\text{DDR} = \dfrac{G_d}{t_f} = \dfrac{\text{Die gap}}{\text{Final film thickness}}$$ **Effect on Properties:** - Higher **BUR** → better TD orientation → improved TD tensile strength, lower haze, but **reduced MD strength** and **lower dart impact**. - Higher **DDR** → better MD orientation → improved MD tensile strength, but may cause **necking** and **film breaks**. --- **(b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Calculate MD-R (2 marks):** $$\text{MD-R} = \dfrac{V_{\text{haul}}}{V_m} = \dfrac{30 \text{ m/min}}{0.4 \text{ m/min}} = 75$$ **Step 2: Calculate final film thickness (3 marks):** Using the design equation derived in Section 3.1: $$t_f = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR} \times \text{MD-R}} = \dfrac{1.2 \text{ mm}}{2.8 \times 75}$$ $$t_f = \dfrac{1.2}{210} = 0.00571 \text{ mm} = 5.71 \text{ μm}$$ **Step 3: Calculate lay-flat width (2 marks):** $$D_b = \text{BUR} \times D_d = 2.8 \times 150 = 420 \text{ mm}$$ $$W_f = \pi \cdot D_b = 3.1416 \times 420 = 1319.5 \text{ mm} \approx 1.32 \text{ m}$$ > **Final Answers:** > - (i) MD-R = **75** > - (ii) $t_f$ = **5.71 μm** > - (iii) Lay-flat width = **1.32 m** --- #### **Question B [14 Marks]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various **mechanical** and **optical** tests conducted on polymer films. Mention the relevant ASTM standards. **(b) [7 Marks]** A 50-μm thick **LDPE film** has an oxygen permeability coefficient of $P = 2.5 \times 10^{-10}$ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg. The partial pressure difference of oxygen across the film is $\Delta p = 20$ cmHg. Calculate the **oxygen transmission rate (OTR)** in **cm³/m²·day·atm**. Assume steady-state Fickian diffusion. --- **Model Solution:** **(a) [7 Marks]** **Mechanical Tests (4 marks):** | Test | Standard | What it measures | |---|---|---| | Tensile strength at break & yield | **ASTM D882** | Force per unit area at break | | Elongation at break | ASTM D882 | Ductility | | Tensile modulus | ASTM D882 | Stiffness | | Elmendorf tear resistance | **ASTM D1922** | Tear propagation | | Trouser tear | **ASTM D1938** | Tear initiation | | Dart drop impact | **ASTM D1709** | Puncture resistance | | Puncture resistance | **ASTM F1306** | Energy to puncture | **Optical Tests (3 marks):** | Test | Standard | What it measures | |---|---|---| | Haze | **ASTM D1003** | Light scattering (%) | | Gloss at 60° | **ASTM D2457** | Surface reflectance (GU) | | Total transmittance | ASTM D1003 | Light passing through (%) | | Clarity | **ASTM D1746** | Sharpness of image through film | --- **(b) [7 Marks]** **Step 1: Recall the Fickian permeation equation (1 mark):** $$J = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$ **Step 2: Substitute given values with proper units (2 marks):** - $P = 2.5 \times 10^{-10}$ cm³·cm/(cm²·s·cmHg) - $\Delta p = 20$ cmHg - $L = 50 \text{ μm} = 50 \times 10^{-4}$ cm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ cm $$J = \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{-10}) \times 20}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}^3 / (\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$ **Step 3: Convert to OTR in cm³/m²·day·atm (4 marks):** Convert cm² → m²: divide by $10^4$ Convert s → day: multiply by $86400$ Convert cmHg → atm: divide by 76 (since 1 atm = 76 cmHg) $$J = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{86400}{1} \times \dfrac{1}{10^{-4}} \times \dfrac{1}{76} \quad \text{(note: cmHg/atm conversion)}$$ Wait — let us do this carefully. The flux in cm³/(cm²·s) needs to be expressed as: $$\text{OTR} = J \times \dfrac{86400 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ day}} \times \dfrac{10^4 \text{ cm}^2}{1 \text{ m}^2} \times \dfrac{76 \text{ cmHg}}{1 \text{ atm}}$$ $$J_{\text{OTR}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 \times 10^4 \times 76$$ Calculating step by step: $$1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 = 8.64 \times 10^{-2}$$ $$8.64 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^4 = 8.64 \times 10^2 = 864$$ $$864 \times 76 = 65664$$ > **Final Answer:** $\text{OTR} \approx 6.57 \times 10^4$ cm³·m⁻²·day⁻¹·atm⁻¹ This is a typical value for LDPE, which is a **poor oxygen barrier** — that's why LDPE is used in fresh-produce packaging (where high OTR is desired) but not for oxygen-sensitive foods. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Films" (Module 3):** - [x] **Definition:** A polymer film is a thin flexible sheet with thickness **< 0.25 mm**. - [x] **Key processes:** Cast, Blown, Calendered, Solution cast, Biaxially oriented. - [x] **BUR** = $D_b / D_d$ — controls **TD** properties. - [x] **DDR** = $G_d / t_f$ — controls **MD** properties. - [x] **Design equation:** $t_f = G_d / (\text{BUR} \times \text{MD-R})$ — *use this in every numerical*. - [x] **Cast films** → best clarity, anisotropic, fast. - [x] **Blown films** → balanced properties, versatile. - [x] **BOPP / BOPET** → highest barrier and stiffness, used in laminates. - [x] **Fick's first law** governs permeation: $J = P \cdot \Delta p / L$. - [x] **Multilayer resistance model** — total resistance is **sum of $L_i/P_i$**. - [x] **ASTM D882** → tensile; **ASTM D1922** → tear; **ASTM D1709** → dart impact. - [x] **ASTM D1003** → haze; **ASTM D2457** → gloss; **ASTM F1249** → WVTR; **ASTM D3985** → OTR. - [x] **Shrinkage** in oriented films: $S_{\max} = (1 - 1/\lambda) \times 100$%. - [x] **Units matter** — always convert μm ↔ cm, cmHg ↔ atm in permeability problems. - [x] **KTU favourite traps:** forgetting units; mixing up BUR and DDR; ignoring steady-state assumption in Fick's law.

topics.title: Sheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Sheets ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) A **Polymer Sheet** is a flat, continuous, two-dimensional polymeric product whose thickness is greater than **0.25 mm (250 µm)** and whose width is significantly larger than its thickness. By ASTM D883 convention, a flat polymeric product thinner than 0.25 mm is termed a **film**, whereas a product thicker than 0.25 mm is termed a **sheet**. Polymer sheets are produced predominantly by **extrusion through a slot (flat) die**, **calendering**, or **casting**, and may be subsequently converted into finished products by **thermoforming, fabrication (cutting/welding), or lamination**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight — Classification by Thickness (ASTM D883)** > - **Film:** thickness ≤ 0.25 mm (250 µm) > - **Sheet:** thickness > 0.25 mm (250 µm) > - **Plate:** thickness > 6.35 mm (¼ inch) — usually compression molded or cast ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **noodle machine** (pasta maker) in an Italian kitchen. A roller pushes dough (molten polymer) through a flat slit (slot die), and the flat ribbon that emerges is the **sheet**. If you press the dough between two counter-rotating heavy rollers, you are doing **calendering**. If you pour chocolate on a cold marble slab and let it harden into a thin slab, you are doing **casting**. Each method gives a sheet with a different surface finish, thickness uniformity, and mechanical memory. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** Polymer sheets are the starting stock for thermoformed cups, trays, blister packs, refrigerator liners, automotive dashboards, roofing membranes, and decorative laminates. The sheet must be dimensionally stable, free from internal stresses, and possess predictable behavior during downstream forming. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Classification | Parameter | Typical Value | Unit | |-----------|---------------|------| | Sheet thickness range | 0.25 – 25 | mm | | Sheet width | up to 4000 | mm | | Production line speed (extrusion) | 0.5 – 30 | m/min | | Calender line speed | up to 120 | m/min | | Operating temperature (PVC calendering) | 160 – 200 | °C | | Operating temperature (PE/PP extrusion) | 180 – 280 | °C | | Common polymers | PVC, PE, PP, PS, ABS, PMMA, PC, PA | — | | Density (standard metric) | 900 – 1500 | kg/m³ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Primary Manufacturing Routes for Polymer Sheets A polymer sheet can be produced by any of the following industrial routes, each chosen based on polymer rheology, required thickness, optical clarity, and production volume. **Route A — Slot-Die (Flat-Die) Extrusion** - Polymer melt is forced through a wide, thin rectangular die. - The emerging extrudate is calibrated by a **three-roll polishing stack (chill roll unit, CRU)** which also controls thickness and imparts surface finish (matte, gloss, embossed). - Best for: PE, PP, PS, ABS, PMMA, PC, PA. **Route B — Calendering** - Polymer compound (mostly plasticized PVC) is passed through a series of heated counter-rotating rollers (typically 2–4 rolls arranged in **L**, **F**, or **Z** configurations). - Best for: PVC, ABS, CPE, and rubber sheets. - Highest output rate for flexible PVC. **Route C — Solution Casting** - Polymer is dissolved in a solvent, cast onto a moving belt, and dried in an oven. - Best for: cellulosics, biopolymers, very thin optical films. **Route D — Compression Molding of Cast Slabs** - Used for ultra-thick plates (PMMA, UHMWPE) — outside the sheet regime. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Route — Decision Logic 1. **Choose Extrusion** if: polymer is melt-processable at moderate viscosity, throughput > 500 kg/h, sheet thickness 0.5–15 mm. 2. **Choose Calendering** if: polymer is plasticized PVC or rubber, very high throughput, gauge control of 0.05–1 mm with tight thickness tolerance. 3. **Choose Casting** if: polymer degrades at melt temperature, optical clarity is critical, or thickness < 0.1 mm is required. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operation of a Slot-Die Sheet Extrusion Line 1. **Feeding:** Polymer pellets (or powder) enter a single-screw extruder through a gravimetric feeder. 2. **Plasticating:** Screw rotates in a heated barrel; polymer melts and becomes a homogeneous viscoelastic fluid. 3. **Metering & Pressure Build-up:** Compressed melt is pushed toward the die, generating a pressure of **5–35 MPa**. 4. **Die Flow:** Melt passes through a **coathanger (T-die)** or **fishtail die** where the flow is re-distributed to give uniform velocity across the die exit. 5. **Calibration (Chill Roll Unit, CRU):** The web passes over highly polished, water-circulated rolls that: - Solidify the sheet (reduce temperature below Tg for amorphous or below Tm for semi-crystalline polymers). - Set the final thickness. - Impart surface finish. 6. **Haul-off & Edge Trimming:** Rubber pull rolls draw the sheet; edges are trimmed by slitters. 7. **Winding/Sawing:** Sheet is wound (thin gauges) or cut into standard lengths (thick gauges). ### 2.4 Coathanger Die — The Heart of Uniformity A coathanger die uses a **mandrel (restrictor bar)** with a profiled internal geometry that creates a high pressure drop near the die center and a lower pressure drop near the die ends. This compensates for the greater path length and frictional loss at the die ends, producing a uniform flow and hence uniform sheet thickness. > [!NOTE] > The pressure drop across the die lip must be **at least 10× the pressure variation** along the die to guarantee uniform output (this is the **"10× rule"** of die design). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Mass throughput | $Q_m = \rho \cdot v \cdot w \cdot t$ | kg/s | $\rho$ = density, $v$ = line speed, $w$ = width, $t$ = thickness | | 2 | Volumetric output | $Q_v = v \cdot w \cdot t$ | m³/s | = $Q_m / \rho$ | | 3 | Draw-down ratio (DDR) | $DDR = v_{haul} / v_{die}$ | dimensionless | $> 1$ stretches the sheet | | 4 | Die-swell ratio | $B = h_{ext} / h_{die}$ | dimensionless | $B > 1$ for polymer melts | | 5 | Neck-in | $NI = w_{die} - w_{sheet}$ | mm | Width loss due to surface tension & draw | | 6 | Take-up force (orientation) | $F_t = A \cdot \sigma_{draw}$ | N | $\sigma_{draw}$ required for orientation | | 7 | Cooling time (thick sheet) | $t_c = \dfrac{s^2}{\pi^2 \alpha} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_1 -
