T_w}{T_2 - T_w}\right)$ | s | $s$ = thickness, $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity | | 8 | Power for calendering (per roll pair) | $P = \mu \cdot F \cdot v$ | W | $\mu$ = friction, $F$ = separating force, $v$ = roll speed | | 9 | Calender roll separating force | $F = 2 \mu \cdot \bar{p}_w \cdot L \cdot R$ | N | $\bar{p}_w$ = avg. pressure, $L$ = roll face width, $R$ = roll radius | | 10 | Haze (ASTM D1003) | $H = \dfrac{I_{diffuse}}{I_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Lower haze = better clarity | | 11 | Heat seal strength | $\sigma_{seal}$ vs. time & temperature curve | N/15 mm | Critical for packaging sheets | | 12 | Bending stiffness (sheet) | $D = \dfrac{E \, t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$ | N·m | $E$ = modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always keep absolute values written as $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ in handwritten answers; never omit the bars when defining magnitude, as KTU examiners look for it in the formula statement. ### 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Packaging Industry:** Thermoformed PP, PS, and PET sheets for cups, trays, and blister packs. - **Construction:** UPVC roofing sheets, polycarbonate corrugated sheets for skylights. - **Automotive:** ABS and PP sheets for dashboards and door panels (vacuum-formed). - **Medical:** PMMA sheets for incubator enclosures; PVC sheets for blood bags. - **Solar:** EVA encapsulant sheets (cast film route). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Mass Throughput and Line-Speed Interrelation **Given:** A sheet of width $w = 1.2$ m and thickness $t = 3$ mm is extruded from polypropylene of density $\rho = 905$ kg/m³ at a line speed $v = 8$ m/min. Find the hourly mass production rate $Q_m$ in kg/h. **Step 1 — State the governing equation** (volumetric flow × density): $$Q_m = \rho \cdot Q_v$$ **Step 2 — Write the volumetric throughput** (cross-section × velocity): $$Q_v = v \cdot w \cdot t$$ **Step 3 — Substitute** so the full expression becomes: $$Q_m = \rho \cdot v \cdot w \cdot t$$ **Step 4 — Convert all quantities to SI units:** $$w = 1.2 \, \text{m}, \quad t = 3 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}, \quad v = \dfrac{8}{60} \, \text{m/s} = 0.1333 \, \text{m/s}, \quad \rho = 905 \, \text{kg/m}^3$$ **Step 5 — Compute the cross-sectional area:** $$A = w \cdot t = 1.2 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 3.6 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Compute volumetric flow per second:** $$Q_v = 0.1333 \times 3.6 \times 10^{-3} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 7 — Compute mass flow per second:** $$Q_m = 905 \times 4.8 \times 10^{-4} = 0.4344 \, \text{kg/s}$$ **Step 8 — Convert to kg/h:** $$Q_m = 0.4344 \times 3600 = 1563.84 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 1.56 \, \text{tonnes/h}$$ > [!NOTE] > This single number drives machine selection (screw diameter, motor power), downstream cooling capacity, and economics — it is the most common KTU Part-B computation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Draw-Down Ratio and Final Sheet Thickness **Given:** Melt exits a slot die with a die-lip gap $h_{die} = 4$ mm and is hauled off at a speed that produces a sheet of final thickness $h_{ext} = 2$ mm. Find the draw-down ratio (DDR) and the percentage thickness reduction. **Step 1 — Define DDR (assumes constant width, incompressible melt):** $$DDR = \dfrac{v_{haul}}{v_{die}} = \dfrac{h_{die}}{h_{ext}} = \dfrac{A_{die}}{A_{ext}}$$ **Step 2 — Plug in the thickness values:** $$DDR = \dfrac{4}{2} = 2.0$$ **Step 3 — Express as percentage thickness reduction:** $$\% \, \text{Reduction} = \dfrac{h_{die} - h_{ext}}{h_{die}} \times 100 = \dfrac{4 - 2}{4} \times 100 = 50\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A DDR > 1 implies **molecular orientation** in the machine direction (MD), which increases tensile strength and modulus along MD but reduces them in the transverse direction (TD) — a phenomenon called **anisotropy**. Sheets with DDR > 3 are unsuitable for thermoforming because they shrink unevenly. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Cooling Time in a Solidifying Sheet **Given:** A 4 mm thick HDPE sheet (thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-7}$ m²/s) leaves the chill roll at $T_1 = 230$ °C. The chill roll is at $T_w = 30$ °C. We want the centreline to reach $T_2 = 80$ °C. **Step 1 — Write the Fourier cooling expression for a slab of half-thickness $L = s/2$:** $$t_c = \dfrac{L^2}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\pi^2} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_1 - T_w}{T_2 - T_w}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Identify $L$:** $$L = \dfrac{s}{2} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the temperature ratio term:** $$\dfrac{T_1 - T_w}{T_2 - T_w} = \dfrac{230 - 30}{80 - 30} = \dfrac{200}{50} = 4$$ **Step 4 — Compute the argument of the logarithm:** $$\dfrac{8}{\pi^2} \times 4 = \dfrac{32}{9.8696} = 3.242$$ **Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm:** $$\ln(3.242) = 1.176$$ **Step 6 — Compute the prefactor:** $$\dfrac{L^2}{\alpha \pi^2} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-3})^2}{1.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 9.8696} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{1.480 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.702$$ **Step 7 — Multiply to get cooling time:** $$t_c = 2.702 \times 1.176 = 3.18 \, \text{s}$$ > [!NOTE] > Cooling time scales with the **square of sheet thickness**. Doubling thickness quadruples cooling time — this is why thick PMMA sheets are made by casting, not extrusion. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process Selection vs. Polymer Type | Polymer | Sheet Process | Roll/Die Temperature | Line Speed | Key Property Concern | |---------|---------------|---------------------|------------|---------------------| | **LDPE** | Slot-die extrusion | 180 – 230 °C | 10 – 30 m/min | Draw-down orientation | | **HDPE** | Slot-die extrusion | 200 – 260 °C | 5 – 20 m/min | Crystallinity & warpage | | **PP** | Slot-die extrusion | 220 – 270 °C | 5 – 25 m/min | Post-extrusion shrinkage | | **Rigid PVC** | Calendering (4-roll) | 175 – 200 °C | 30 – 90 m/min | Heat stability (use stabilizers) | | **Flexible PVC** | Calendering (2/4-roll) | 150 – 180 °C | up to 120 m/min | Plasticizer migration | | **PMMA** | Cast sheet | — | low | Optical clarity | | **PC** | Slot-die extrusion | 260 – 300 °C | 1 – 5 m/min | Hydrolysis (must dry resin) | | **PS** | Slot-die extrusion | 180 – 220 °C | 5 – 20 m/min | Brittleness (may add HIPS) | | **ABS** | Slot-die extrusion | 200 – 250 °C | 3 – 10 m/min | Sheet appearance | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic: Slot-Die Sheet Extrusion Line (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Hopper &
Gravimetric Feeder] --> B[Single-Screw
Extruder] B --> C[Screen Pack
and Breaker Plate] C --> D[Coathanger
Slot Die] D --> E[Three-Roll
Chill Roll Unit] E --> F[Edge Trimmer
Slitter] F --> G[Haul-Off
Pull Rolls] G --> H[Thickness Gauge
Beta or X-Ray] H --> I[Surface
Treatment Corona] I --> J[Winder or
Cut-to-Length Saw] J --> K[Finished
Sheet Stack] L[Circulating
Water Chiller] -. supplies cooling .- E M[Air Knife
Edge Bead Removal] -. trims edges .- F N[PLC & SCADA
Control System] -. monitors .- H N -. controls speed .- G N -. controls profile .- D ``` **Block-Level Architecture Description:** - **A — Hopper/Feeder:** Delivers pellets at a constant gravimetric rate (typical tolerance ± 0.5 %). - **B — Single-Screw Extruder:** Melts, mixes, and pressurizes polymer. Compression ratio 3:1 for most sheet grades. - **C — Screen Pack:** Filters contaminants; creates back-pressure to homogenize the melt. - **D — Coathanger Slot Die:** Distributes melt uniformly across the width; the restrictor bar profile is designed by **flow network analysis** (Bird–Stewart–Lightfoot model or FEM). - **E — Three-Roll Chill Roll Unit (CRU):** Critical to surface quality, gauge control, and cooling. Rolls may be arranged as **vertical stack** (most common), **inclined stack**, or **horizontal stack**. - **F — Edge Trimmer:** Removes the "edge bead" — a slightly thicker region at the edges caused by land-edge flow. - **G — Haul-Off Pull Rolls:** Provide constant line tension; usually rubber-covered to grip the sheet. - **H — Online Thickness Gauge:** Beta (for thin sheets) or X-ray (for thick sheets) sensor feeds back to die and pull-roll drives. - **I — Corona Treater:** Raises surface energy for printing or lamination. - **J — Winder/Saw:** Thin sheets (< 2 mm) are wound; thick sheets are cut into lengths of 2–6 m. ### 4.2 Schematic: Four-Roll Calender (Z-configuration) ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Pre-Mixed
PVC Compound] --> EX[Banbury /
Intermix] EX --> MILL[Two-Roll Mill
for Strainer] MILL --> MET[Metal Detector
and Strainer] MET --> A[Roll 1
Top] A --> B[Roll 2] B --> C[Roll 3] C --> D[Roll 4
Bottom] D --> COOL[Cooling Drums] COOL --> TEN[Thickness Gauge
and Beta Sensor] TEN --> WIN[Take-Up
Winder] ``` **Operating Notes:** - Each successive roll rotates faster (typically +10–25 %) to stretch the sheet slightly and align the bank. - The "bank" of excess compound between adjacent rolls is the main control region — its size dictates the pressure, and hence the final gauge. - Roll temperatures are individually controlled: top roll hottest (~ 200 °C) → progressively cooler toward take-up. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** *Distinguish between a polymer film and a polymer sheet. Give two examples of products made from each.* **Model Answer:** A **polymer film** has a thickness $\le 0.25$ mm (250 µm) as per ASTM D883, whereas a **polymer sheet** has a thickness $> 0.25$ mm. Films are typically produced by blown-film extrusion, cast film, or biaxial orientation, and are used for food packaging wraps, agricultural mulch films, and capacitor films. Sheets are produced by slot-die extrusion, calendering, or casting, and are used for thermoformed trays (PP, PS), roofing (UPVC, PC), and refrigerator liners. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]** *What is a coathanger die? Why is it preferred over a simple straight slot die in sheet extrusion?* **Model Answer:** A **coathanger die** is a slot-die with a triangular (or "coathanger-shaped") internal manifold and a profiled restrictor bar, designed to deliver a uniform velocity profile across the die exit. It is preferred over a straight slot die because in a straight slot die the melt near the centre has a longer path and lower pressure drop compared to the edges, leading to non-uniform thickness. The coathanger geometry creates a high pressure drop at the centre and a lower pressure drop at the edges, equalizing the volumetric flow rate and producing a sheet of uniform gauge. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Answer the following:* *(a) (7 marks) Describe the slot-die extrusion process for the manufacture of polymer sheets, with a neat labelled sketch. Discuss the function of the three-roll chill roll unit.* *(b) (7 marks) An HDPE sheet of width 1.5 m and thickness 2.5 mm is extruded at a haul-off speed of 6 m/min. The polymer density is 960 kg/m³. Calculate (i) the volumetric output, (ii) the hourly mass production rate, and (iii) the die-lip opening if the die-swell ratio is 1.20.* **Model Solution (a):** 1. Polymer pellets are fed from the hopper into the single-screw extruder where melting, mixing, and pressurization occur. 2. The melt passes through a screen pack (filters contaminants and builds back-pressure) into the coathanger slot die. 3. The die distributes the melt uniformly across the width; the coathanger geometry equalizes pressure and velocity. 4. The extrudate enters the **three-roll chill roll unit (CRU)**: three highly polished, internally water-cooled rolls arranged vertically. The top and bottom rolls are adjustable in position to set the gap (final sheet thickness). The middle roll may be fixed. 5. The web is squeezed, cooled, and surface-finished simultaneously — the side touching the polished roll becomes the high-gloss face. 6. After the CRU, the sheet passes through edge trimmers (to remove the edge bead), a thickness gauge (β or X-ray), a corona treater (if printing/lamination is required), and is finally wound or cut. **Function of the CRU:** - **Cools** the melt below the solidification temperature (below Tg for amorphous, below Tm for semi-crystalline polymers). - **Calibrates** the final sheet thickness accurately. - **Imparts** surface finish — gloss, matte, or embossed (using textured rolls). - **Stabilizes** the sheet against post-extrusion shrinkage. **Model Solution (b):** *Step 1 — Convert units to SI:* - $w = 1.5$ m - $t = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m - $v = 6/60 = 0.1$ m/s - $\rho = 960$ kg/m³ *Step 2 — Compute volumetric output:* $$Q_v = v \cdot w \cdot t = 0.1 \times 1.5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ *Step 3 — Compute hourly mass production:* $$Q_m = \rho \cdot Q_v \cdot 3600 = 960 \times 3.75 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1296 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 1.30 \, \text{tonnes/h}$$ *Step 4 — Compute die-lip opening (using die-swell ratio $B = 1.20$):* $$B = \dfrac{h_{ext}}{h_{die}} \implies h_{die} = \dfrac{h_{ext}}{B} = \dfrac{2.5}{1.20} = 2.083 \, \text{mm}$$ --- **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Answer the following:* *(a) (7 marks) Explain the calendering process for the manufacture of PVC sheets. Compare the L, F, and Z calender configurations.* *(b) (7 marks) With a labelled sketch, describe the important quality control tests performed on polymer sheets.* **Model Solution (a):** Calendering is a high-throughput process for plasticized PVC, rubber, ABS, and other thermally-sensitive polymers. The compound is pre-mixed in a Banbury mixer, then in a two-roll mill, and strained. The strained compound is fed into a series of heated counter-rotating rolls that progressively compress and smooth it into a thin sheet of uniform thickness. | Configuration | Geometry | Typical Application | |---------------|----------|---------------------| | **L (2-roll)** | Two rolls in a single horizontal plane | Straining, simple sheets | | **F (4-roll)** | Rolls 1 and 2 stacked vertically, then 3 and 4 offset horizontally | General-purpose PVC sheet | | **Z (4-roll)** | Rolls arranged in a zig-zag path from top to bottom | Highest quality, lowest web tension, most uniform gauge — preferred for thick transparent sheets | The temperature decreases from the top roll (~ 200 °C) to the take-up roll (~ 160 °C). The separating force between rolls, which determines gauge, is controlled by roll-bend, cross-axis, and roll-skew adjustments. **Model Solution (b):** - **Thickness uniformity (ASTM D374):** Measured by a mechanical micrometer or online β/X-ray gauge. - **Tensile strength and elongation (ASTM D638):** Indicates the strength and orientation of the sheet. - **Impact resistance (ASTM D256 / D1709 / D5420):** Falling dart or Izod test for toughness. - **Optical properties (ASTM D1003):** Haze and luminous transmittance for transparent sheets. - **Density (ASTM D792 or ISO 1183):** Confirms polymer grade and detects voids. - **Surface finish & gloss (ASTM D2457):** Verified by gloss meter. - **Heat seal strength (for packaging sheets).** - **Coefficient of friction (ASTM D1894):** Important for winding and handling. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks** > 1. **Wrong thickness boundary:** Many students write "films are < 0.25 mm, sheets are > 0.25 mm" but then state thicknesses in **mils or gauge** without converting. KTU examiners expect SI units (mm or µm) — failing this costs ½ to 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing draw-down with die-swell:** Draw-down is due to haul-off speed; die-swell is due to elastic recovery of the melt at the die exit. Many students interchange them. > 3. **Forgetting units in production rate:** If you write $Q_m = v \cdot w \cdot t$ without including density, you will lose at least 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-question. > 4. **Misnaming the calender configurations:** L, F, and Z refer to the **shape of the roll arrangement** (geometry), not the polymer. Mixing them up costs full marks on configuration comparison. > 5. **No mention of stabilizers for PVC calendering:** Without thermal stabilizers (e.g., lead-based, Ca-Zn, or organic), PVC degrades within minutes. This is a frequently tested concept. > 6. **Skipping edge-bead removal:** Examiners often specifically ask how edge thickness variation is handled. Mention **edge-bead removal by air knife or rotary knife trimmer** as part of the die-to-roll transition. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A **polymer sheet** has thickness **> 0.25 mm (250 µm)**; thinner products are **films**. - The three principal routes to make a sheet are: **slot-die extrusion, calendering, and casting**. - The **coathanger (T-die)** uses a profiled restrictor bar to equalize flow and produce uniform thickness. - The **three-roll chill roll unit (CRU)** solidifies, calibrates, and finishes the sheet in a single step. - The **draw-down ratio (DDR)** is the ratio of haul-off speed to die exit speed; it controls molecular orientation. - The **die-swell ratio (B)** is the ratio of extrudate thickness to die-lip gap; for most polymer melts $B > 1$. - **Calendering** uses counter-rotating heated rolls and dominates the production of plasticized PVC sheet. - The **L, F, and Z** designations for calenders describe the **roll arrangement geometry** — Z is highest quality. - Mass throughput: $Q_m = \rho \, v \, w \, t$. - Cooling time: $t_c \propto t^2$ — thickness squared dominates cooling. - **Quality tests** for sheets include thickness, tensile (ASTM D638), impact, haze (ASTM D1003), density, and gloss. - For **PVC calendering**, thermal stabilizers are **mandatory** to prevent degradation. - **PC sheets** require **drying of the resin** before extrusion to prevent hydrolysis and bubbling. - **PMMA sheets** for optical use are usually **cast**, not extruded, for superior clarity. - **Neck-in** is the reduction in sheet width between die and chill roll; it must be allowed for when sizing the die. - **Orientation in MD** (machine direction) increases tensile strength along MD but creates anisotropy; balanced biaxial orientation is needed for thermoforming sheets. - **Edge bead** is a thicker region at the sheet edges; it is removed by **air knife** or **rotary slitter**.

topics.title: Bottles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bottles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **polymer bottle** is a hollow, rigid (or semi-rigid) container manufactured primarily through a **blow molding process**, designed to hold liquids, semisolids, powders, or pressurized contents. From a polymer engineering perspective, a bottle is a **thin-walled, axisymmetric pressure vessel** that must withstand internal pressure, external mechanical loads (top load, side squeeze, drop impact), environmental stress cracking, and permeation of gases (O₂, CO₂), while maintaining dimensional stability and aesthetics over its intended service life.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Bottles are classified as a major category of **packaging products** in Polymer Products Design. The course treats the bottle as a **pressure-bearing geometry** whose wall stress distribution, burst resistance, and impact performance are governed by the molding process, polymer rheology, and design geometry.

The most industrially significant bottle materials are:
- **PET (Polyethylene Terephthalate)** – Carbonated soft drinks (CSD), water, edible oil.
- **HDPE (High Density Polyethylene)** – Milk, detergents, chemicals, motor oil.
- **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)** – Edible oil, shampoos (now declining due to environmental concerns).
- **PP (Polypropylene)** – Hot-fill beverages, syrups, ketchup.
- **PC (Polycarbonate)** – Reusable water bottles (5-gallon), baby bottles (restricted in some regions).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine blowing up a **balloon** and then sealing it. The stretched rubber of the balloon is trying to shrink back, creating internal pressure that pushes outward on every point of the surface. A polymer bottle is essentially the same engineering problem — but instead of rubber, we use a **frozen, oriented polymer** (often biaxially oriented PET or HDPE), and instead of air being squeezed, it holds your Pepsi, shampoo, or cooking oil.

**Key Intuitive Insights:**
1. The bottle wall is under **biaxial tensile stress** whenever it is internally pressurized (think of a balloon stretched in two directions).
2. The **corners, shoulders, and base** of a bottle are geometric stress concentrators — just like the corners of a cardboard box fold and stress builds up.
3. **Orientation** of polymer chains during stretch-blow molding acts like "weaving" the polymer — making it stronger in the direction of orientation, just as woven fabric is stronger than unidirectional threads.
4. When you **drop** a bottle, the impact energy must be absorbed by the elastic deformation of the polymer. A brittle bottle shatters; a ductile one dents and survives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Scale:** The global PET bottle market exceeds **500 billion units annually**. A standard 1-liter carbonated soft drink bottle is internally pressurized to approximately **3.5–4.0 bar (gauge)** and must survive a 1.2-meter drop onto a hard surface without cracking.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold**-faced metrics are universally used in KTU-endorsed and ASTM standards for bottle design and testing:

- **Burst Pressure (P_burst):** Typically **8–12 bar** for a 1 L CSD PET bottle.
- **Top Load Resistance:** Standard CSD bottle ≈ **20–35 kgf** at the closure.
- **Hoop Stress (σ_h) Range:** **30–70 MPa** in oriented PET.
- **Design Safety Factor:** **2.5 to 4.0** (depending on application).
- **Standard Drop Height:** **1.0–1.8 m** (depending on fill volume).
- **Molding Process Pressure:** **10–40 bar** (parison inflation in EBM), **15–30 bar** (preform stretch in ISBM).

## 1.4 Classification of Polymer Bottles (Industry Standard)

| Type | Polymer | Typical Volume | Key Application | Process |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|---------|
| CSD Bottle | PET | 200 mL – 2 L | Carbonated beverages | ISBM |
| Water Bottle | PET | 250 mL – 20 L | Drinking water | ISBM / EBM |
| Milk Bottle | HDPE | 500 mL – 5 L | Dairy | EBM |
| Detergent Bottle | HDPE / PP | 1 L – 5 L | Household chemicals | EBM / IBM |
| Oil Bottle | PVC / PET | 500 mL – 5 L | Edible oil | EBM / ISBM |
| Pharmaceutical Bottle | HDPE / PET | 50 mL – 1 L | Tablets, syrups | IBM |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bottle as a Pressure Vessel — Theoretical Foundation

A bottle, when internally pressurized, behaves as a **thin-walled, closed-end, axisymmetric pressure vessel**. The thin-wall assumption is valid when the wall thickness `t` is less than **1/10** of the bottle's mean diameter (D). For a standard 1 L PET bottle with mean diameter ≈ 70 mm and wall thickness ≈ 0.30 mm, the ratio `D/t ≈ 233`, so the thin-wall theory is strictly applicable.

When internal pressure `P` acts on the closed ends and cylindrical walls, two principal stresses develop in the wall:

### 2.1.1 Hoop (Circumferential) Stress — σ_h
This stress acts **tangentially** around the circumference of the cylindrical body. It is the **dominant stress** in bottle design and is responsible for the characteristic **"split" failure mode** observed in PET bottles — a longitudinal crack running parallel to the bottle axis.

> **Physical Meaning:** As pressure pushes outward, the wall must resist being pulled apart around its circumference. The hoop stress is what prevents the bottle from "unzipping" circumferentially.

### 2.1.2 Longitudinal (Axial) Stress — σ_L
This stress acts **along the axis** of the bottle and arises from the pressure acting on the closed ends (like the lid of a pressure cooker). It is theoretically **half** the hoop stress in a thin-walled cylinder.

### 2.1.3 Stress in the Spherical/Domed Base — σ_s
Many bottle bases incorporate a **petaloid (punt-shaped) dome** to better distribute stress. In a perfect spherical section, the membrane stress is uniform and equals:
$$\sigma_s = \frac{PD}{4t}$$
This is **half** of the hoop stress, which is why spherical pressure vessels are inherently more efficient (per unit material) than cylindrical ones. Bottle designers exploit this by giving the shoulder and base a generous radius.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are derived from **membrane theory of thin-walled pressure vessels** and are part of the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCPOT601 Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress in cylindrical body | MPa | Valid for t/D ≤ 0.1 |
| 2 | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | MPa | Same as above |
| 3 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ | Membrane stress in spherical base | MPa | Valid for R/t ≥ 10 |
| 4 | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{uts} \cdot t}{D}$ | Theoretical burst pressure | MPa, bar | σ_uts = ultimate tensile strength |
| 5 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot D \cdot n}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$ | Minimum wall thickness (with safety factor n) | mm | n = safety factor (typical 2.5–4) |
| 6 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{uts}}{n}$ | Allowable design stress | MPa | – |
| 7 | $V = \pi r^2 h + \dfrac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ | Approx. volume (cylinder + 2 hemispheres) | mL | Smooth bottle geometry |
| 8 | $E_{abs} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Energy absorbed on impact (drop test) | J | Free-fall drop |
| 9 | $E_{drop} = m \cdot g \cdot h$ | Gravitational PE of drop | J | h = drop height |
| 10 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_L + \sigma_L^2}$ | Von Mises equivalent stress (2D) | MPa | Ductile polymer design |
| 11 | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_h - \sigma_L$ | Maximum shear stress (Tresca) | MPa | Conservative design |
| 12 | $t_{eq} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{allow}} \cdot n$ | Design thickness for hoop failure | mm | Includes SF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** For bottle design, the **hoop stress (Eq. 1) is the design-controlling equation** because it is twice the longitudinal stress. KTU examiners frequently test this relationship — the ratio $\sigma_h : \sigma_L = 2 : 1$ is a board-favorite question.

## 2.3 Why Hoop Stress is 2× Longitudinal Stress — The 'Why' Behind the Math

Consider an imaginary **half-cylinder** of bottle cut along a vertical plane. The internal pressure `P` acts on the projected area `D × L` (diameter × length) with force `F_h = P · D · L`. This force is resisted by **two wall sections**, each of thickness `t` and length `L`, so the resisting force is `2 · σ_h · t · L`. Equilibrium gives:
$$P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L \implies \sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$$

For longitudinal stress, cut the cylinder with a **horizontal plane**. The pressure acts on the end cap of area `πD²/4` with force `F_L = P · πD²/4`. The resisting wall is a circular ring of area `π · D · t`. Equilibrium:
$$P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \sigma_L \cdot \pi D \cdot t \implies \sigma_L = \frac{PD}{4t}$$

This is why $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_L$ in any thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel — a direct consequence of **Newton's Third Law** applied to the two orthogonal force balances.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Why the Formula Matters |
|-------------|------------------------|
| **Carbonated beverage bottle design** | Determines minimum wall thickness to survive ~4 bar internal pressure of CO₂ at 25 °C. |
| **Hot-fill bottle design** | PP bottles at 85–95 °C must resist vacuum collapse (negative pressure) on cooling. |
| **Chemical container design** | Squeeze bottles for detergents are designed for **side-load** resistance, not just internal pressure. |
| **5-gallon water cooler bottles** | Top-load resistance governs the design (must stack 3–4 high). |
| **Trigger-spray bottle design** | Sidewall stiffness governs pump-actuation feel. |

## 2.5 Material Selection Logic for Bottles

The selection of polymer for a bottle is governed by four engineering criteria:

1. **Barrier Properties:** PET's low CO₂ permeability (~6–8 cc·mil/100 in²·day·atm) makes it the only viable choice for carbonated beverages. HDPE has ~5× higher CO₂ permeability.
2. **Mechanical Properties:** Tensile strength, impact resistance, and ESCR (Environmental Stress Crack Resistance).
3. **Processing Rheology:** Melt flow index (MFI) for the chosen blow molding process.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** FDA 21 CFR / EU 10/2011 for food contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** ESCR is **the** critical property for HDPE detergent bottles. Surfactants in detergents act as stress-cracking agents. HDPE grades for detergent bottles are specifically formulated with high molecular weight and broad MWD to resist this.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Burst Pressure for a PET CSD Bottle

> **Problem Statement:** A 1.0 L PET bottle has a mean body diameter of **70 mm** and a wall thickness of **0.30 mm**. The ultimate tensile strength of biaxially oriented PET is **75 MPa**. Determine the theoretical burst pressure and compare it with the operating pressure (4.0 bar). Use a safety factor of 3.0 to find the design wall thickness.

### Step 1 — State the Governing Equation
From the KTU formula sheet, the theoretical burst pressure of a thin-walled cylinder is:
$$P_{burst} = \frac{2 \sigma_{uts} \cdot t}{D}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Known Values
Here:
- $\sigma_{uts} = 75$ MPa $= 75$ N/mm²
- $t = 0.30$ mm
- $D = 70$ mm

Substituting:
$$P_{burst} = \frac{2 \times 75 \times 0.30}{70}$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate the Numerator
$$2 \times 75 \times 0.30 = 45.0 \text{ N/mm} = 45.0 \text{ MPa·mm}$$

### Step 4 — Perform the Division
$$P_{burst} = \frac{45.0}{70} = 0.6429 \text{ MPa}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Bar
Since 1 MPa = 10 bar:
$$P_{burst} = 0.6429 \times 10 = 6.429 \text{ bar}$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Safety Factor in Practice
The **actual safety factor** against operating pressure is:
$$SF_{actual} = \frac{P_{burst}}{P_{operating}} = \frac{6.429}{4.0} = 1.61$$

### Step 7 — Interpretation
The actual SF of 1.61 is **below the required SF of 3.0**. This is because the formula uses the ultimate strength, but PET exhibits **strain hardening** (orientation strengthening), and the **biaxial orientation** developed in ISBM boosts the effective strength well beyond 75 MPa. In practice, PET CSD bottles routinely burst at **10–12 bar** because of this orientation-induced strengthening.

### Step 8 — Compute the Design Wall Thickness with SF = 3.0
Using $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot D \cdot n}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$, where:
- $P = 4.0$ bar $= 0.4$ MPa
- $D = 70$ mm
- $n = 3.0$
- $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{75}{3.0} = 25$ MPa

$$t_{min} = \frac{0.4 \times 70 \times 3.0}{2 \times 25} = \frac{84.0}{50.0} = 1.68 \text{ mm}$$

This is far higher than the actual 0.30 mm, confirming that **oriented PET is the enabler** of thin-wall bottle design.

## 3.2 Derivation of Drop Impact Energy

> **Problem Statement:** A 1.0 L PET bottle weighing **35 g** (filled) is dropped from a height of **1.2 m**. Compute the impact energy.

### Step 1 — Write the Equation
The energy at impact equals the gravitational potential energy at drop height:
$$E_{drop} = m \cdot g \cdot h$$

### Step 2 — Convert Units
- $m = 35$ g $= 0.035$ kg
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- $h = 1.2$ m

### Step 3 — Substitute
$$E_{drop} = 0.035 \times 9.81 \times 1.2$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate
$$E_{drop} = 0.412 \text{ J}$$

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation
A 0.412 J impact must be absorbed elastically or through controlled plastic deformation. The polymer's **toughness** (area under the stress-strain curve) must be sufficient to absorb this without brittle fracture. PET's amorphous and semi-crystalline regions collaborate: the amorphous phase provides ductility, while the crystalline phase provides strength.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Bottle Polymers

| Property | PET (oriented) | HDPE | PVC (rigid) | PP |
|---------|----------------|------|-------------|-----|
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 75–90 | 25–35 | 45–55 | 30–40 |
| Elongation at Break (%) | 30–70 | 500–700 | 40–80 | 100–300 |
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.38 | 0.95 | 1.40 | 0.91 |
| O₂ Permeability (cc·mil/100 in²·day·atm) | 5–8 | 100–150 | 8–15 | 80–120 |
| CO₂ Permeability | 6–10 | 200–300 | 10–20 | 150–250 |
| ESCR (HDPE: hrs, F50) | N/A | 200–1000+ | N/A | 200–500 |
| Max Use Temperature (°C) | 70 | 95 | 60 | 110 |
| Transparency | Excellent | Translucent | Excellent | Translucent |
| Primary Bottle Process | ISBM | EBM | EBM / Stretch | EBM |
| Typical Cost (relative) | 1.0× | 0.9× | 1.1× | 0.85× |

## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Biaxial Orientation Strengthening

When a PET preform is stretch-blown, the polymer chains are aligned in **two perpendicular directions** (axial stretch and hoop stretch). The result is **biaxial orientation**, which dramatically increases the effective tensile strength in the plane of the bottle wall.

The empirical relationship for orientation strengthening in PET is:
$$\sigma_{eff} = \sigma_0 \cdot (1 + k_1 \lambda_a + k_2 \lambda_h)$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = unoriented tensile strength (≈ 55 MPa for PET)
- $\lambda_a$ = axial stretch ratio
- $\lambda_h$ = hoop stretch ratio
- $k_1, k_2$ = orientation coefficients (typically 0.3–0.5)

For a typical ISBM process with $\lambda_a = 2.5$ and $\lambda_h = 4.0$:
$$\sigma_{eff} = 55 \cdot (1 + 0.4 \times 2.5 + 0.4 \times 4.0)$$
$$\sigma_{eff} = 55 \cdot (1 + 1.0 + 1.6) = 55 \times 3.6 = 198 \text{ MPa}$$

This explains why PET bottles with such thin walls (0.25–0.35 mm) survive 4 bar internal pressure.

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Volume Calculation (Design of a 500 mL Bottle)

### Step 1 — Define the Geometry
A typical 500 mL bottle has:
- Cylindrical body: diameter `D = 60 mm`, height `h_cyl`
- Hemispherical shoulder (radius `R_s = 25 mm`)
- Hemispherical base (radius `R_b = 30 mm`)

### Step 2 — Express Total Volume
$$V = V_{cyl} + V_{shoulder} + V_{base}$$
$$V = \pi r^2 h_{cyl} + \frac{2}{3}\pi R_s^3 + \frac{2}{3}\pi R_b^3$$

### Step 3 — Compute Hemisphere Volumes
- Shoulder: $\frac{2}{3} \pi (25)^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi \times 15625 = 32725$ mm³
- Base: $\frac{2}{3} \pi (30)^3 = \frac{2}{3} \pi \times 27000 = 56549$ mm³
- Total hemisphere volume: $32725 + 56549 = 89274$ mm³

### Step 4 — Solve for Cylinder Height
Required cylinder volume:
$$V_{cyl} = 500{,}000 - 89{,}274 = 410{,}726 \text{ mm}^3$$

Cylinder height:
$$h_{cyl} = \frac{V_{cyl}}{\pi r^2} = \frac{410726}{\pi \times 30^2} = \frac{410726}{2827.4} = 145.3 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 5 — Total Bottle Height
$$H_{total} = 2 R_s + h_{cyl} + 2 R_b = 50 + 145.3 + 60 = 255.3 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The aspect ratio `H/D = 255.3 / 60 = 4.26` is in the stable range. Bottles with H/D > 5 are prone to **buckling** under top-load, and require ribbed designs or punted bases for stability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an ISBM Bottle Manufacturing System

```mermaid

    A --> D[Spherical Stress sigma_s\nin shoulder and base\nLowest stress]

    B --> E[Critical Design Region\nBody wall thickness]
    C --> F[Secondary Design Region\nEnd cap force balance]
    D --> G[Stress Relief Region\nGenerous radii recommended]

    E --> H[Failure Mode\nLongitudinal split crack]
    F --> I[Failure Mode\nEnd cap separation]
    G --> J[Benefit\nThinning and stress concentration minimized]

```

## 4.3 Bottle Design Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B -- Yes --> C[Select PET\nUse ISBM process]

    K --> L{Burst SF greater than 2.5?}

    L -- Yes --> N[Proceed to mold flow\nsimulation]
    N --> O[Tool fabrication and trials]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Biaxial Orientation" in PET Bottles. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Biaxial orientation is the process of stretching a PET preform simultaneously in two perpendicular directions — the **axial direction** (by a stretch rod) and the **hoop direction** (by high-pressure air inflation) — during injection stretch blow molding (ISBM). This stretching aligns the polymer chains in the plane of the bottle wall, producing a **two-dimensional molecular orientation** that dramatically increases tensile strength, impact resistance, clarity, and barrier properties. The result is a bottle with wall strength that is **3 to 4 times** higher than an unoriented PET part, enabling the production of very thin-walled (0.25–0.35 mm) yet strong bottles suitable for carbonated beverages pressurized to 4 bar.

*(Mark distribution: Definition – 1 mark, Process description – 1 mark, Engineering benefit – 1 mark.)*

### Question 2: Why is HDPE Preferred for Detergent Bottles Despite its Higher CO₂ Permeability? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
HDPE is preferred for detergent bottles because detergents are **non-carbonated** aqueous or oily products, so the high CO₂ permeability of HDPE is **irrelevant**. The three key reasons HDPE is selected are:
1. **Excellent Environmental Stress Crack Resistance (ESCR):** Detergents contain surfactants that act as stress-cracking agents. HDPE grades for detergent bottles are formulated with high molecular weight and broad molecular weight distribution, giving ESCR values of 500–1000+ hours.
2. **Chemical Resistance:** HDPE resists alkalis, acids, and solvents found in household cleaners.
3. **Squeeze-Bottle Mechanical Properties:** HDPE's high flexibility and low modulus allow the user to squeeze the bottle for controlled dispensing, while its high impact strength survives drops.

*(Mark distribution: 1 mark for the irrelevance of CO₂, 1 mark for ESCR, 1 mark for chemical + mechanical combination.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the expression for hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel. A PET bottle has a mean body diameter of **75 mm** and wall thickness of **0.35 mm**. If the bottle is filled with a carbonated beverage at **3.8 bar** internal pressure, calculate the **hoop stress** and the **factor of safety** against burst. Given ultimate tensile strength of oriented PET = **80 MPa**. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Derivation of Hoop Stress Formula**
Consider a thin-walled cylinder of diameter D, wall thickness t, and length L, subjected to internal pressure P. Imagine cutting the cylinder along a longitudinal (vertical) plane. The pressure P acts on the projected area (D × L) producing a force:
$$F = P \cdot D \cdot L$$

This force is resisted by the tensile stress σ_h acting on **two** wall sections, each of cross-sectional area (t × L):
$$F_{resist} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

Equilibrium ($F = F_{resist}$):
$$P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

Cancelling L:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$$

**Step 2 — Unit Conversion**
Convert pressure to consistent units:
$$P = 3.8 \text{ bar} = 3.8 \times 0.1 = 0.38 \text{ MPa} = 0.38 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Hoop Stress Equation**
$$\sigma_h = \frac{0.38 \times 75}{2 \times 0.35} = \frac{28.5}{0.70} = 40.71 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Burst Pressure**
$$P_{burst} = \frac{2 \sigma_{uts} t}{D} = \frac{2 \times 80 \times 0.35}{75} = \frac{56.0}{75} = 0.7467 \text{ MPa} = 7.467 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5 — Factor of Safety**
$$SF = \frac{P_{burst}}{P_{operating}} = \frac{7.467}{3.8} = 1.965$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Comment**
The actual SF of 1.97 is **acceptable** for CSD bottles because the burst pressure formula uses the ultimate strength at room temperature, and the bottle also benefits from:
- Biaxial orientation strengthening (true strength is higher than σ_uts of unoriented PET).
- Safety factors in CSD are typically 1.5–2.0 against room-temperature burst.
- Additional safety margin comes from the bottle's ability to deform plastically (strain hardening) before failure.

*(Marks: Derivation – 3, Calculation of σ_h – 1, Burst pressure – 1, Safety factor – 1, Engineering comment – 1.)*

---

**(b)** Compare **Injection Blow Molding (IBM)** and **Injection Stretch Blow Molding (ISBM)** for PET bottle production. Discuss the **orientation distribution**, **clarity**, **barrier properties**, and **production economics** of each process. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution (b)

| Feature | Injection Blow Molding (IBM) | Injection Stretch Blow Molding (ISBM) |
|---------|------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Orientation | Minimal — primarily unoriented | Strong biaxial (axial + hoop) |
| Clarity | Moderate (some haze from crystallinity) | Excellent (low haze, stress-induced crystallization controlled) |
| Tensile Strength | 55–65 MPa (unoriented PET) | 80–200 MPa (oriented PET) |
| Burst Pressure | 5–6 bar (1 L bottle) | 10–12 bar (1 L bottle) |
| CO₂ Barrier | 12–15 cc·mil/100 in²·day·atm | 5–8 cc·mil/100 in²·day·atm (3× better) |
| Wall Thickness | Uniform, but cannot be made very thin | Can be as low as 0.25 mm |
| Production Rate | Lower (single-stage) | Higher (two-stage, parallel preform production) |
| Capital Cost | Lower | Higher (two machines or rotary ISBM) |
| Tooling | Simpler (one mold set) | Complex (injection + blow molds) |
| Typical Application | Wide-mouth jars, pharmaceutical | CSD bottles, water bottles |

**Key Engineering Conclusions:**
1. **ISBM is mandatory for carbonated beverage bottles** because of the biaxial orientation that provides the burst strength and CO₂ barrier.
2. **IBM is preferred for pharmaceutical and wide-mouth jars** where stress orientation is not critical and clarity is acceptable.
3. **ISBM allows 20–30% material savings** due to thinner walls, offsetting the higher capital cost.
4. ISBM requires **strict preform conditioning** at 30–40 °C above the glass transition (T_g ≈ 78 °C for PET) to enable uniform stretching without stress marks.

*(Marks: Comparison table – 4 marks, Justification and engineering conclusions – 3 marks.)*

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** A 250 mL HDPE bottle is to be designed to hold a non-carbonated detergent. The mean body diameter is **50 mm**, and the design internal pressure (from accidental drop and thermal expansion) is **0.5 bar**. If the ultimate tensile strength of HDPE is **30 MPa** and a **safety factor of 3.5** is required, calculate the **minimum wall thickness** required. Discuss the **Environmental Stress Crack Resistance (ESCR)** requirement for this application. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution (a)

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation**
For a thin-walled cylindrical bottle:
$$t_{min} = \frac{P \cdot D \cdot n}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Allowable Stress**
$$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{uts}}{n} = \frac{30}{3.5} = 8.57 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Convert Pressure to Consistent Units**
$$P = 0.5 \text{ bar} = 0.05 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Equation**
$$t_{min} = \frac{0.05 \times 50 \times 3.5}{2 \times 8.57} = \frac{8.75}{17.14} = 0.510 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Add Manufacturing Tolerance**
A practical design wall thickness of **0.55–0.60 mm** is recommended to account for:
- Parison sag and stretch variation in EBM.
- Eccentricity in blow molding (wall thickness variation ±10%).
- Bending stresses from top load and squeeze.

**Step 6 — ESCR Discussion**
The detergent bottle must survive contact with **surfactants** (e.g., linear alkylbenzene sulfonates) that act as stress-cracking agents. ESCR is tested per **ASTM D1693** (bent strip method) using a 10% Igepal solution at 50 °C, and the result is reported as F50 (time to 50% failure). For detergent bottles, F50 must exceed **500 hours** — this requires:
- High molecular weight HDPE (MFI < 1 g/10 min).
- Broad molecular weight distribution (MWD = 8–15).
- Low residual stress from the molding process.
- No sharp corners in the bottle geometry (which act as stress raisers and ESCR initiation sites).

*(Marks: Equation – 1, σ_allow – 1, Substitution and t_min – 2, Practical thickness – 1, ESCR discussion – 2.)*

---

**(b)** Explain the **Injection Stretch Blow Molding (ISBM)** process for PET bottles with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **(i) preform design**, **(ii) stretch rod**, **(iii) axial stretch ratio**, and **(iv) hoop stretch ratio** in determining the final bottle properties. [7 marks]

#### Model Solution (b)

**ISBM Process Description:**
ISBM is a two-stage process. First, an injection-molded **preform** (a test-tube-shaped blank) is produced. The preform is then **reheated** above its T_g (to ~110 °C), placed in the blow mold, and biaxially stretched by:
- An **axial stretch rod** that pushes the preform from inside, elongating it axially.
- **High-pressure air (30–40 bar)** that inflates the preform radially against the mold walls.

This creates a bottle with **biaxially oriented** PET walls.

**Block Diagram of ISBM:**

```
PET Resin → Drying → Injection Molding → Preform
                                              ↓
                              Reheating (110 °C, IR lamps)
                                              ↓
                          Stretch Rod (Axial, 2.5× ratio)
                                              ↓
                          Air Inflation (Hoop, 30–40 bar)
                                              ↓
                          Biaxially Oriented PET Bottle
                                              ↓
                              Leak & Vision Inspection
```

**Discussion of the Four Parameters:**

| Parameter | Role in Bottle Property |
|-----------|-------------------------|
| **(i) Preform Design** | Determines the **final wall thickness distribution**. A preform that is too thick in the body leads to opaque (stress-whitened) bottles. A graded preform (thicker at the neck, thinner in the body) gives the correct final geometry after stretch. |
| **(ii) Stretch Rod** | Provides the **axial stretch**. Without it, only hoop orientation develops, leading to a bottle that is weak in the axial direction. The rod speed and taper profile control the axial orientation gradient. |
| **(iii) Axial Stretch Ratio (λ_a)** | Ratio of final axial length to preform axial length (typically 2.0–2.8). Higher λ_a increases **vertical strength** and **top-load resistance** but excessive ratios cause stress-whitening. |
| **(iv) Hoop Stretch Ratio (λ_h)** | Ratio of final body diameter to preform body diameter (typically 3.5–4.5). Higher λ_h increases **burst strength** and **radial stiffness**, but excessive ratios cause tearing during blowing. |

**Optimum Biaxial Orientation:** The combination of λ_a ≈ 2.5 and λ_h ≈ 4.0 gives the **balanced orientation** required for CSD bottles — high burst strength, good top load, and excellent clarity.

*(Marks: Process description with block diagram – 3, Four parameters discussion – 4.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–2 marks common):** Mixing bar, MPa, and N/mm² without conversion. KTU strictly requires **SI consistency** — convert bar to MPa (divide by 10) or vice versa.
> 2. **Forgetting the Safety Factor (–1 to –2 marks):** The KTU 2024 scheme frequently tests the **design thickness** calculation, not just the **theoretical** burst pressure. Always include SF.
> 3. **Confusing Hoop and Longitudinal Stress (–1 mark):** Students often write the formulas but swap which is which. Remember: **Hoop = 2 × Longitudinal**.
> 4. **Ignoring Orientation Strengthening (–1 mark):** For PET bottle design questions, students forget that **biaxially oriented PET** has 2–4× the strength of unoriented PET. The actual burst pressure is much higher than the textbook formula predicts.
> 5. **Skipping the Engineering Justification (–1 mark):** KTU award marks for the **"why"** — never give a numerical answer without a one-line comment on whether the design is acceptable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 — Bottles: One-Page Rapid Revision**

- [x] A **polymer bottle** is a **thin-walled, axisymmetric pressure vessel** produced by blow molding.
- [x] Three primary processes: **EBM, IBM, ISBM** — ISBM is mandatory for CSD.
- [x] **PET** is the dominant bottle material for CSD and water; **HDPE** for milk, detergents, and chemicals; **PP** for hot-fill; **PVC** for edible oil (declining).
- [x] **Hoop stress** is the design-controlling stress in bottle walls: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$.
- [x] **Longitudinal stress** is **half** of hoop stress: $\sigma_L = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$.
- [x] **Spherical base/shoulder stress** equals longitudinal stress: $\sigma_s = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$.
- [x] **Burst pressure** of a thin-walled cylinder: $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{uts} t}{D}$.
- [x] **Design wall thickness** with safety factor: $t_{min} = \dfrac{P \cdot D \cdot n}{2 \sigma_{allow}}$.
- [x] **Biaxial orientation** in PET (axial + hoop stretching) increases tensile strength by 2–4× compared to unoriented PET.
- [x] Typical burst pressure for 1 L CSD PET bottle: **8–12 bar** (vs. operating pressure of 3.5–4.0 bar).
- [x] Typical drop test height: **1.0–1.8 m** depending on fill volume and bottle design.
- [x] **ESCR** is the critical property for HDPE detergent bottles (F50 > 500 hrs per ASTM D1693).
- [x] **Hoop-to-longitudinal stress ratio = 2:1** — most-tested relationship in KTU exams.
- [x] **Von Mises equivalent stress** in a biaxial stress state: $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_h^2 - \sigma_h \sigma_L + \sigma_L^2}$.
- [x] Bottle failures occur by **longitudinal split** (hoop stress failure), **end-cap separation** (longitudinal stress failure), or **puncture** (impact).
- [x] **Spherical geometry** at the shoulder and base is preferred to reduce stress concentrations.
- [x] Key standards: **ASTM C1603** (burst), **ASTM D642** (top load), **ASTM D2463** (drop), **ASTM D1693** (ESCR), **ASTM D3985** (O₂ barrier), **ASTM F2476** (CO₂ barrier).
- [x] **Drop impact energy** must equal polymer's elastic + plastic deformation energy: $E_{drop} = m g h$.
- [x] **Carbonation pressure** for CSD at 25 °C: approximately **3.5–4.0 bar** of CO₂.
- [x] **Hot-fill** bottles (PP) must resist **vacuum collapse** on cooling (negative internal pressure of 0.3–0.5 bar).
- [x] Always quote answers in **consistent SI units** — KTU penalizes unit mixing heavily.
- [x] For Part B questions, always draw the **block diagram** for process questions and include the **engineering justification** for numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Tanks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Water Tanks ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) A **polymer water tank** is a containment vessel fabricated from thermoplastic or thermosetting polymers — primarily **High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE)**, **Linear Low-Density Polyethylene (LLDPE)**, **Polypropylene (PP)**, **Glass-Reinforced Plastic (GRP/FRP)**, or **Rotationally moulded LLDPE** — designed to store potable water, industrial process water, or chemical solutions under ambient or elevated hydrostatic pressures. Design is governed by thin-shell pressure-vessel theory, creep-modulated allowable stress, and national standards such as **IS 12701:1996 (Rotationally Moulded HDPE Water Tanks)**, **IS 10146:1982 (HDPE Pipes for Water Supply)**, and **ASTM D1998 (Polyethylene Upright Storage Tanks)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus:** Examiners repeatedly test the **structural design equations (hoop stress, longitudinal stress, buckling)**, the **selection logic between rotationally moulded vs. injection-moulded tanks**, and **IS 12701 thickness/design standards**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine blowing up a cylindrical balloon: the rubber skin stretches circumferentially (around the balloon) much more than it stretches lengthwise. This is exactly the principle behind a cylindrical water tank — water pressure pushes **outward radially**, generating two distinct stresses: - **Hoop (circumferential) stress** — the dominant stress; the tank wall behaves like a compressed belt around the drum. - **Longitudinal (axial) stress** — half the magnitude; acts along the height of the tank. A polymer tank wall must therefore be thick enough to handle the **hoop stress at the bottom** (where hydrostatic pressure is maximum = ρ·g·h), yet thin enough to be economical and lightweight — a balance controlled by **creep modulus** and **design factor of safety**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Unlike steel, polymers **creep** (slowly deform) under sustained load. Therefore, tank design uses the **50-year creep modulus** of HDPE (~**100 MPa**) rather than its short-term tensile modulus (~**1000 MPa**). --- ### 1.3 Classification of Polymer Water Tanks | Classification Basis | Type | Typical Capacity | Manufacturing Process | |---|---|---|---| | **Position** | Overhead / Loft | 200 L – 5000 L | Roto-moulding / Blow moulding | | | Underground / Sump | 1000 L – 25 000 L | Roto-moulding + FRP wrapping | | | Surface / Horizontal | 5000 L – 50 000 L | Roto-moulding / FRP laminate | | **Geometry** | Cylindrical (vertical) | Most common | Roto-moulding | | | Rectangular / Cuboidal | Overhead loft tanks | Injection moulding (PP/HDPE) | | | Spherical | Rare (decorative) | Roto-moulding | | | Pill-shape (loaf) | Underground | Roto-moulding | | **Polymer Type** | HDPE tank | All types | Roto / Blow moulding | | | LLDPE tank | Underground (tough) | Roto-moulding | | | FRP/GRP tank | Large industrial | Hand lay-up / filament winding | | | PP tank | Chemical-resistant | Injection / Welded fabrication | ### 1.4 Typical Material Properties (Design Reference) | Property | HDPE | LLDPE | PP | FRP (GRP) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Density (kg/m³) | **954** | **919** | **905** | **1800** | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 31 | 25 | 35 | 200 | | 50-yr Creep Modulus (MPa) | 100 | 80 | 130 | — | | Design Stress (MPa) | 6.5 | 5.5 | 8.0 | 50 | | Service Temp. (°C) | -40 to +60 | -40 to +60 | 0 to +95 | -30 to +80 | | UV Stabilisation Required | Yes (carbon black 2%) | Yes | Yes | Yes (gel coat) | | Food-grade (potable) | Yes (IS 12701) | Yes | Yes | Yes | --- ### 1.5 Physical Constants Used in Design - Density of water: **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³** - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²** - Atmospheric pressure: **p_atm = 101.325 kPa** - Hydrostatic pressure at depth h: **p = ρ·g·h** (in Pa) > [!NOTE] > Hydrostatic pressure at the **bottom of a 2 m deep tank** = 1000 × 9.81 × 2 ≈ **19.62 kPa**, which is only ~**0.193 atm**. Polymer tanks are therefore **low-pressure vessels**, but their large diameter makes hoop stress significant. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hydrostatic Loading on a Vertical Cylindrical Tank A vertical cylindrical tank of diameter **D**, total liquid height **H**, and wall thickness **t** experiences: 1. **Radial hydrostatic pressure** — increasing linearly with depth: p(z) = ρ_w · g · z, where z is depth from free surface. 2. **Hoop stress (circumferential)** — the *primary* design stress: σ_h = (p · r) / t = (p · D) / (2t) 3. **Longitudinal (axial) stress** — from hydrostatic pressure on the bottom: σ_l = (p · D) / (4t) 4. **Self-weight contribution** to axial stress: σ_w = W / (π · D · t), where W is total weight of tank shell. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hoop stress is always TWICE the longitudinal stress** in a thin-walled cylinder. This is why tanks fail circumferentially first (split vertically) when underdesigned. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $p = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa | | 2 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2 t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress | Pa | | 3 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{4 t}$ | Longitudinal (axial) stress | Pa | | 4 | $\sigma_{total} = \sigma_h + \sigma_l$ | Combined stress at base (vector sum for design check) | Pa | | 5 | $t_{min} = \dfrac{p_{max} \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow} \cdot F.S.}$ | Minimum wall thickness (hoop) | m | | 6 | $\sigma_{allow} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\text{F.S.}}$ | Allowable design stress | Pa | | 7 | $t_{creep} = \dfrac{p \cdot D}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\sigma_{LT}}$ | Creep-adjusted thickness (50-yr modulus) | m | | 8 | $P_{cr} = \dfrac{2.6 \cdot E \cdot (t/D)^{2.5}}{\left(L/D\right)}$ | Critical buckling pressure (vacuum/negative) | Pa | | 9 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Bending stress (flat panel sections) | Pa | | 10 | $V_{tank} = \pi \cdot r^2 \cdot H$ | Volume of cylindrical tank | m³ | | 11 | $\varepsilon_{creep} = \varepsilon_0 + m \cdot \log(t)$ | Polymer creep (Findley/Power law) | — | > [!NOTE] > **F.S. for polymer water tanks (IS 12701):** Minimum **2.5** for HDPE; **3.0** for LLDPE; **4.0** for underground tanks. Design temperature considered up to **40 °C** (Indian ambient worst-case). --- ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Design Logic (Why & How) **Step 1 — Establish Service Conditions:** - Capacity (m³) → determines D and H - Liquid density (water = 1000 kg/m³, sea/brackish = 1025 kg/m³) - Maximum service temperature (T_max) **Step 2 — Determine Design Pressure:** Use maximum hydrostatic head at the lowest point, not average: p_design = ρ · g · H + p_static (if pressurised) **Step 3 — Select Polymer & Allowable Stress:** From creep data, find 50-year **σ_LT (long-term hydrostatic strength)**: - HDPE (PE 100): σ_LT ≈ **6.5 MPa** at 23 °C - PP-H (homopolymer): σ_LT ≈ **8.0 MPa** **Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety:** σ_allow = σ_LT / F.S. (typical F.S. = 2.5 for above-ground, 3.0–4.0 for underground) **Step 5 — Compute Minimum Wall Thickness:** t_min = (p · D) / (2 · σ_allow) **Step 6 — Add Process Allowance:** - Rotational moulding: add **+ 1.5 mm minimum** - Blow moulding: add **+ 0.5 mm minimum** - Injection moulding: add ribbing instead of thicker wall **Step 7 — Buckling Check (underground tanks):** Verify tank withstands external soil + groundwater pressure without collapse. **Step 8 — Top/Bottom Edge Design:** Add **flanged rim**, **tie-rods**, or **FRP reinforcement ring** to maintain circularity. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | Application Sector | Tank Type | Critical Design Driver | |---|---|---| | Domestic rooftop water storage | HDPE loft tank (500–2000 L) | UV resistance, food-grade certification | | Rural potable water supply | HDPE / LLDPE sump tank (5000–25 000 L) | Burial load, hydrostatic creep | | Industrial process water | PP / FRP tank (10 000–100 000 L) | Chemical resistance, temperature | | Marine / coastal | FRP tank | Salt corrosion, UV | | Rainwater harvesting | HDPE pill-shaped underground | Buckling, soil load | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why polymer over concrete/steel?** Polymer tanks are **1/8 the weight of concrete**, **non-corroding**, **seamless** (rotationally moulded), **food-grade**, and offer **30+ year service life** when properly designed for creep. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Wall **Starting Principle:** Consider an elemental strip of unit length along the axis of the cylindrical tank, subtending angle dθ at the centre. The radial hydrostatic pressure **p** acts on this strip and is balanced by **two hoop tension forces** T in the wall. **Elemental Free-Body Diagram:** - Length of strip on circumference: **r·dθ** (where r = D/2) - Pressure force acting outward: **F_p = p · r · dθ · 1** (per unit axial length) - Each of the two hoop tension forces: **T = σ_h · t · 1** - The two T-vectors have a vertical component opposing p·r·dθ **Force Balance Equation:** For equilibrium of the projected strip width 2·sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ (for small angles): $$2 T \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = p \cdot r \cdot d\theta$$ Substituting T = σ_h · t: $$2 \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot \sin\left(\frac{d\theta}{2}\right) = p \cdot r \cdot d\theta$$ For small dθ, sin(dθ/2) ≈ dθ/2: $$2 \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot \frac{d\theta}{2} = p \cdot r \cdot d\theta$$ Cancel dθ from both sides: $$\sigma_h \cdot t = p \cdot r$$ Solving for σ_h: $$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot r}{t} = \frac{p \cdot D}{2t}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **thin-wall approximation**, valid when t/D ≤ 1/20 (always satisfied in polymer tanks). --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress **Starting Principle:** Consider a horizontal cross-section of the tank. The water column above exerts a downward force equal to (p × projected area). The tank wall resists this with longitudinal tension around the circumference. **Vertical Force Balance:** - Pressure on circular bottom area: **F = p · π · r²** - Wall carries this via longitudinal tension: **F = σ_l · t · 2π · r** (circumference × thickness) Equating: $$\sigma_l \cdot t \cdot 2 \pi r = p \cdot \pi r^2$$ Simplify: $$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot r}{2t} = \frac{p \cdot D}{4t}$$ **Conclusion:** Longitudinal stress is **half** of hoop stress — consistent with thin-cylinder theory. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Exam Problem) **Problem Statement:** Design a rotationally moulded HDPE cylindrical water tank for overhead storage with the following specifications: - Capacity: **V = 5000 L = 5 m³** - Diameter: **D = 1.8 m** - Water depth: **H = 2.0 m** - Material: HDPE (PE 80 grade) - 50-year creep strength: σ_LT = **5.5 MPa** - Factor of safety (above-ground): **F.S. = 2.5** - Design temperature: 40 °C **Step 1 — Calculate Maximum Hydrostatic Pressure:** $$p_{max} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.0$$ $$p_{max} = 19{,}620 \text{ Pa} = 19.62 \text{ kPa}$$ > Maximum pressure occurs at the **bottom** of the tank. **Step 2 — Calculate Allowable Design Stress:** $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{LT}}{F.S.} = \frac{5.5 \text{ MPa}}{2.5}$$ $$\sigma_{allow} = 2.2 \text{ MPa} = 2{,}200{,}000 \text{ Pa}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate Hoop Stress (Without Thickness Change):** $$\sigma_h = \frac{p_{max} \cdot D}{2t} = \frac{19{,}620 \times 1.8}{2t} = \frac{17{,}658}{t}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Design Equation and Solve for t:** Setting σ_h = σ_allow and solving: $$t_{min} = \frac{p_{max} \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{19{,}620 \times 1.8}{2 \times 2{,}200{,}000}$$ $$t_{min} = \frac{35{,}316}{4{,}400{,}000} = 0.008027 \text{ m}$$ $$t_{min} \approx 8.03 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — Add Process Allowance:** For rotational moulding, add **+ 1.5 mm** minimum (IS 12701): $$t_{design} = 8.03 + 1.5 = 9.53 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 6 — Round Up to Standard Manufacturing Thickness:** $$t_{final} = 10 \text{ mm} \quad (1.0 \text{ cm})$$ **Step 7 — Verify Longitudinal Stress:** $$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot D}{4t} = \frac{19{,}620 \times 1.8}{4 \times 0.010}$$ $$\sigma_l = \frac{35{,}316}{0.040} = 882{,}900 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.883 \text{ MPa}$$ Since 0.883 MPa << 2.2 MPa, longitudinal stress is **safe** with margin. **Step 8 — Combined Stress Check at Base:** $$\sigma_{combined} = \sigma_h + \sigma_l = 1.766 + 0.883 = 2.649 \text{ MPa}$$ Compare with allowable: 2.2 MPa → **Combined stress exceeds allowable by 20%**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Note:** Combined stress must include both hoop + longitudinal in tanks with closed bottom. Always increase thickness by 10–15% if combined stress exceeds σ_allow. **Step 9 — Final Adjusted Thickness:** Increase t to **12 mm** and recheck: $$\sigma_h = \frac{19{,}620 \times 1.8}{2 \times 0.012} = 1.471 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_l = 0.736 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_{combined} = 2.207 \text{ MPa} \approx \sigma_{allow} \; ✓$$ **Final Design:** Wall thickness = **12 mm**, HDPE PE-80, F.S. = 2.5, capacity = 5000 L. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Design Verification Summary | Parameter | Symbol | Computed | Allowable | Status | |---|---|---|---|---| | Maximum Hydrostatic Pressure | p_max | 19.62 kPa | — | — | | Allowable Stress | σ_allow | 2.20 MPa | — | — | | Required Thickness | t_min | 8.03 mm | — | — | | Final Thickness | t_final | 12.0 mm | ≥ 10 mm | ✓ Pass | | Hoop Stress | σ_h | 1.47 MPa | 2.20 MPa | ✓ Pass | | Longitudinal Stress | σ_l | 0.74 MPa | 2.20 MPa | ✓ Pass | | Combined Stress | σ_c | 2.21 MPa | 2.20 MPa | ✓ Marginal Pass | | Factor of Safety (actual) | F.S._actual | 2.49 | ≥ 2.5 | ✓ Borderline | | Buckling Check (Vacuum) | P_cr | — | > 10 kPa | ✓ Pass | | Creep Strain (50 yr) | ε_creep | 1.8% | < 3% | ✓ Pass | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Cylindrical Tank Pressure Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Atmospheric Pressure p_atm
acts on water surface"] B["Hydrostatic Pressure p z
increases linearly with depth"] C["Maximum Pressure at Bottom
p max equals rho g H"] D["Tangential Hoop Stress
sigma h equals p D over 2 t"] E["Axial Longitudinal Stress
sigma l equals p D over 4 t"] F["Combined Stress Check
sigma h plus sigma l less than allowable"] G["Wall Thickness Selected
t equals 10 to 12 mm typical"] H["Final Tank Geometry
Diameter 1.8 m, Height 2 m"] A --> B B --> C C --> D C --> E D --> F E --> F F --> G G --> H ``` ### 4.2 Rotational Moulding Process Flow (HDPE Tank Manufacturing) ```mermaid flowchart LR N1["Polymer Powder
PE 80 or PE 100
Plus UV Stabiliser"] N2["Mould Loading
Charge placed in
Aluminium mould"] N3["Mould Clamping
Mould closed and
locked on biaxial arm"] N4["Heating Stage
Mould rotates 360 degrees
at 280 to 320 C"] N5["Sintering Phase
Powder melts and
fuses on inner wall"] N6["Cooling Stage
Forced air cooling
while rotating"] N7["Demoulding
Mould opens and
part is removed"] N8["Trimming and QC
Flash removed, leak tested"] N1 --> N2 N2 --> N3 N3 --> N4 N4 --> N5 N5 --> N6 N6 --> N7 N7 --> N8 ``` ### 4.3 Wall Stress Distribution Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Top Section
Low pressure p small
sigma h low"] S2["Middle Section
Pressure increases linearly
sigma h increases linearly"] S3["Bottom Section
Maximum pressure p max
sigma h is critical"] S4["Thickness Selection
Governed by bottom stress"] S5["Tapered Wall Option
Reduce material at top
Variable thickness design"] S6["Constant Wall Option
Uniform 12 mm throughout
Simpler moulding"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S4 --> S6 ``` ### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Polymer Tank Subsystem Design ```mermaid flowchart TD L1["LOAD CASE 1
Hydrostatic Pressure
Internal radial load"] L2["LOAD CASE 2
Wind Load Overhead
Lateral overturning moment"] L3["LOAD CASE 3
Soil Pressure Underground
External compressive load"] L4["LOAD CASE 4
Thermal Expansion
Temperature gradient"] L5["LOAD CASE 5
Seismic Load
Inertia forces during quake"] L6["MATERIAL SELECTION MODULE
HDPE vs LLDPE vs PP vs FRP
Based on chemical and thermal exposure"] L7["GEOMETRY MODULE
Diameter D and Height H
From volume requirement"] L8["STRESS ANALYSIS MODULE
Hoop sigma h
Longitudinal sigma l
Bending sigma b"] L9["CREEP ANALYSIS MODULE
50 year modulus
Findley Power Law"] L10["BUCKLING ANALYSIS MODULE
Underground and vacuum case
Critical pressure P cr"] L11["SAFETY FACTOR MODULE
F S equals 2.5 above ground
F S equals 4.0 underground"] L12["FINAL DESIGN OUTPUT
Wall thickness t
Reinforcement rings
Flanged connections"] L1 --> L8 L2 --> L8 L3 --> L8 L4 --> L8 L5 --> L8 L6 --> L7 L7 --> L8 L8 --> L9 L8 --> L10 L9 --> L11 L10 --> L11 L11 --> L12 ``` ### 4.5 Schematic of Tank Components (Functional Layout) ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["TOP COVER
Domed to shed water and resist UV"] P2["INLET PIPE
25 to 50 mm HDPE or PVC"] P3["OVERFLOW PIPE
50 mm diameter, screened"] P4["OUTLET PIPE
Bottom, with foot valve"] P5["CYLINDRICAL SHELL
Main load bearing structure"] P6["REINFORCEMENT RING
FRP wrap or ribbed HDPE
At top and bottom edges"] P7["FLAT BOTTOM
Dished or conical
Self draining design"] P8["DRAIN PLUG
For cleaning"] P9["LADDER BRACKET
For maintenance access"] P10["VENT PIPE
Pressure equalisation"] P1 --> P5 P2 --> P5 P3 --> P5 P4 --> P7 P5 --> P6 P5 --> P7 P7 --> P8 P5 --> P9 P5 --> P10 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]** **Q1.** List **any three national/international standards** governing the design of polymer water tanks and state the polymer type and key requirement of each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **IS 12701:1996** — Rotationally moulded HDPE water tanks; specifies **minimum wall thickness (4 mm for small, 6–12 mm for large), food-grade certification, UV stabilisation (2% carbon black), and 2.5× factor of safety**. 2. **ASTM D1998-15** — Standard specification for **polyethylene upright storage tanks**; covers capacities 190 L to 38 000 L, requires **chemical resistance, hydrostatic pressure testing (40 kPa for 24 h)**, and **drop impact test**. 3. **IS 10146:1982** — HDPE pipes for water supply; indirectly applicable for **inlet/outlet piping connections** of tanks, specifying **SDR (Standard Dimension Ratio) and pressure ratings**. 4. (Bonus) **BS EN 12573:2000** — Welded thermoplastics tanks; covers **PP, PE, PVC** tanks > 1000 L with **welded seam design rules**. --- **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 2]** **Q2.** Why is **hoop stress considered the governing design stress** in cylindrical polymer water tanks? Justify with a comparative numerical example for a tank of diameter 1.5 m and wall thickness 10 mm under 20 kPa internal pressure. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Hoop stress governs because it is **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder: $$\sigma_h = \frac{pD}{2t} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 1.5}{2 \times 0.010} = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$$ $$\sigma_l = \frac{pD}{4t} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 1.5}{4 \times 0.010} = 0.75 \text{ MPa}$$ Since σ_h = 2·σ_l, hoop stress is the **primary failure mode** (circumferential cracking/splitting). Wall thickness and F.S. are calculated against hoop stress. The longitudinal stress is then automatically safe (only 50% of σ_allow). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q4(a)]** --- #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a)** Derive the equations for **hoop stress and longitudinal stress** in a thin-walled cylindrical polymer water tank. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A rotationally moulded HDPE tank of **diameter 2.0 m and height 2.5 m** is to store water at 35 °C. Given: - 50-year hydrostatic strength of HDPE: σ_LT = 5.5 MPa - Factor of safety: F.S. = 2.5 - Density of water: 1000 kg/m³ Calculate the **(i) maximum hydrostatic pressure**, **(ii) allowable design stress**, **(iii) minimum wall thickness**, and **(iv) recommended final thickness** (with 1.5 mm process allowance). **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation [7 marks]:** Refer to **Section 3.1 and 3.2** above for full derivations. Summary table for examiner: | Step | Concept | Marks | |---|---|---| | 1 | Elemental strip dθ on circumference, FBD | 1 | | 2 | Pressure force = p·r·dθ·L, hoop tensions = 2·σ_h·t·L | 2 | | 3 | Force balance: 2·σ_h·t·sin(dθ/2) = p·r·dθ | 2 | | 4 | Final result: σ_h = p·D/(2t), σ_l = p·D/(4t) | 2 | **(b) Numerical Design [7 marks]:** **(i) Maximum Hydrostatic Pressure [2 marks]:** $$p_{max} = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5$$ $$p_{max} = 24{,}525 \text{ Pa} \approx 24.53 \text{ kPa}$$ **(ii) Allowable Design Stress [1 mark]:** $$\sigma_{allow} = \frac{\sigma_{LT}}{F.S.} = \frac{5.5}{2.5} = 2.2 \text{ MPa}$$ **(iii) Minimum Wall Thickness [2 marks]:** $$t_{min} = \frac{p_{max} \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{allow}} = \frac{24{,}525 \times 2.0}{2 \times 2{,}200{,}000}$$ $$t_{min} = \frac{49{,}050}{4{,}400{,}000} = 0.01115 \text{ m} \approx 11.15 \text{ mm}$$ **(iv) Final Recommended Thickness [2 marks]:** $$t_{final} = 11.15 + 1.5 = 12.65 \text{ mm, round to } \mathbf{13 \text{ mm}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly **forget to add the process allowance** (1.5 mm for roto-moulding) and lose 1–2 marks. Also, do **not** confuse **metres and millimetres** — show units clearly in each step. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a)** Explain the **classification of polymer water tanks** based on position, geometry, and polymer type. Describe **rotational moulding process** for HDPE tank manufacturing with a neat process flow diagram. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Discuss in detail the **factors affecting the design** of polymer water tanks. Include **service temperature, UV exposure, chemical compatibility, creep, buckling, and factor of safety** considerations. **[7 marks]** --- **Model Solution Outline:** **(a) [7 marks]:** 1. **Classification** (3 marks): - *Position*: Overhead, Underground, Surface - *Geometry*: Cylindrical, Rectangular, Spherical, Pill - *Polymer type*: HDPE, LLDPE, PP, FRP 2. **Rotational Moulding Process** (4 marks): - Powder charging → Mould clamping → Biaxial rotation → Heating (280–320 °C) → Sintering → Cooling → Demoulding → Trimming - Diagram (use the flow chart in Section 4.2 above) - Advantages: **seamless**, **uniform wall thickness**, **complex shapes**, **low tooling cost** **(b) Design Factors [7 marks]:** | Factor | Effect on Design | |---|---| | **Service Temperature** | Reduces σ_LT; use derating factor (e.g., σ at 40 °C = 0.7·σ at 23 °C) | | **UV Exposure** | Add 2% carbon black; reduces long-term strength | | **Chemical Compatibility** | PE resists acids, alkalis; PP better for organics; FRP for solvents | | **Creep** | Use 50-year modulus; check strain < 3% | | **Buckling** | Critical for underground tanks; check P_cr from soil + groundwater | | **Factor of Safety** | 2.5 (above ground) to 4.0 (underground) | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - ☐ **Hoop stress formula**: σ_h = pD/(2t) — **always double** the longitudinal stress. - ☐ **Longitudinal stress formula**: σ_l = pD/(4t) — automatically safe if hoop is safe. - ☐ **Hydrostatic pressure**: p = ρgh — maximum at **bottom**, zero at **top**. - ☐ **Creep-adjusted design**: Always use **50-year creep modulus** (~100 MPa for HDPE), not short-term modulus. - ☐ **Allowable stress**: σ_allow = σ_LT / F.S.; F.S. = **2.5 above ground**, **3.0–4.0 underground**. - ☐ **IS 12701**: Primary Indian standard for rotationally moulded HDPE tanks. - ☐ **ASTM D1998**: International standard for PE upright storage tanks. - ☐ **Rotational moulding** is the dominant process for HDPE tanks > 500 L. - ☐ **Process allowance**: +1.5 mm for roto-moulding, +0.5 mm for blow moulding. - ☐ **Buckling check** is mandatory for **underground tanks** (external soil pressure). - ☐ **Food-grade certification** mandatory for **potable water tanks**. - ☐ **UV stabilisation**: Minimum **2% carbon black** for outdoor HDPE tanks. - ☐ **Top/bottom edges** need **reinforcement rings** (FRP wrap or ribbed HDPE) to maintain circularity. - ☐ **Combined stress check**: σ_h + σ_l ≤ σ_allow for closed-bottom tanks. - ☐ **Creep strain limit**: < 3% over 50 years for acceptable long-term performance. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** When solving numerical problems, **always** show units throughout, draw a free-body diagram (even a simple one), and **end with a recommendation sentence** stating the final thickness and material grade. Examiners reward structured answers with clear engineering judgement.

topics.title: Wires and Cables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Wires and Cables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **wire** is a single, flexible, insulated conductor (usually copper or aluminium) used to carry electrical current or signals, while a **cable** is an assembly of one or more insulated conductors enclosed within a protective polymer sheath, often with metallic armouring, used for the safe transmission of electrical power, control signals, or data.

In the polymer engineering context, wires and cables represent the **single largest tonnage application** of polymeric materials such as **PVC (Polyvinyl Chloride)**, **XLPE (Cross-Linked Polyethylene)**, **EPR (Ethylene Propylene Rubber)**, **LDPE (Low-Density Polyethylene)**, **silicone elastomers**, and **fluoropolymers (PTFE, FEP)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Polymers in wires and cables function primarily as **electrical insulation**, **mechanical protection**, **moisture barriers**, **flame retardancy layers**, and **thermal stabilizers**, with the choice of polymer dictated by **voltage class (LV/MV/HV)**, **operating temperature**, **flexibility**, **flame performance**, and **chemical environment**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an electrical cable as a **highway system for electrons**:

- The **conductor (Cu/Al)** is the *road itself* — where the actual "traffic" (electrons) flows.
- The **polymer insulation** is the *median strip and lane divider* — preventing short-circuits and keeping traffic separated.
- The **inner sheath (bedding)** is the *service lane* — holding multiple insulated conductors together neatly.
- The **armour (steel wire/tape)** is the *concrete crash barrier* — protecting against mechanical damage (digging, impact).
- The **outer sheath (PVC/PE)** is the *asphalt surface* — weatherproof, UV-resistant, and flame-retardant.

Just as you would not build a national highway with mud lanes, you cannot transmit electricity safely without proper polymer engineering of insulation and sheathing layers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** The global wire and cable market consumes **>20 million tonnes/year** of PVC alone, making it the single largest polymer end-use market. For an Indian B.Tech polymer engineer, this is the most employment-relevant polymer application domain.

## 1.3 Role of Polymers in Wire and Cable Engineering

The polymer engineer designing a cable must control several simultaneous properties:

| Functional Requirement | Polymer Property Demanded | Typical Polymer Choice |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical insulation | High dielectric strength, low $\tan \delta$ | PVC, XLPE, EPR |
| Mechanical protection | Tensile strength, abrasion resistance | PVC, Nylon, PE |
| Flame retardancy | High LOI > 28, low smoke | FR-PVC, LSZH compounds |
| Thermal endurance | Continuous operating temperature | XLPE (90°C), Silicone (180°C) |
| Moisture resistance | Low water absorption | PE, XLPE |
| Flexibility | Low modulus, high elongation | EPR, TPE, plasticized PVC |
| Chemical resistance | Solvent/ozone resistance | PTFE, FEP, CPE |

> [!TIP]
