## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**State the relevant Indian Standard for the following and list any two key tests prescribed:**
(a) Unplasticized PVC (UPVC) pipes for potable water supplies.
(b) HDPE pipes for potable water supply.
(c) PVC pipes for soil and waste discharge.

**Model Answer:**

| Pipe | Indian Standard | Key Tests |
|------|-----------------|-----------|
| UPVC potable water | **IS 4985:2000** | Hydrostatic pressure (IS 12235 Pt-3), Vicat softening, dimensions, impact |
| HDPE potable water | **IS 15328:2003** | Hydrostatic pressure, MFR, density, dimensions, longitudinal reversion |
| PVC soil & waste | **IS 13592:1992** | Dimensions, impact, ring stiffness, leak tightness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners award the full 3 marks only if the **IS number with year** is correctly stated. A generic answer such as "BIS standard exists" will earn 1/3.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) of a plastic pipe. What is the significance of keeping the SDR constant across all diameters of a pressure class?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Standard Dimension Ratio (SDR) is the ratio of a pipe's outside diameter to its minimum wall thickness:

$$SDR = \frac{D_o}{t}$$

**Significance:**
1. **Pressure class independence from diameter:** The hoop stress equation $\sigma = P(SDR - 1)/2$ shows that for a fixed SDR, the allowable pressure is the same for all diameters. This lets a manufacturer produce 20 mm, 50 mm, 110 mm pipes all rated for the same 10 bar class using only different wall thicknesses.
2. **Design simplification:** Engineers select a single SDR value (e.g., SDR 11 for PE 80, 10 bar) regardless of flow demand.
3. **Interchangeability of fittings:** Sockets, valves, and electrofusion couplers are designed for a given SDR family, ensuring joint compatibility.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for hoop stress in a thin-walled plastic pipe subjected to internal pressure. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An SDR 11 HDPE pipe of OD 110 mm is rated for water service at 23 °C. The HDB of the PE 80 material is 10.0 MPa. Using a design factor of 0.5, calculate:
(i) The wall thickness of the pipe.
(ii) The pressure rating at 23 °C.
(iii) The pressure rating when used at 40 °C (use $F_T = 0.74$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

*Assumptions:*
1. The pipe wall is **thin** ($t/D_o \leq 0.1$).
2. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic** in the hoop direction.
3. Internal pressure is **uniformly distributed**.
4. End effects (longitudinal stress) are neglected.
5. The pipe behaves as a **thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel**.

*Derivation:*

Consider a pipe of mean diameter $D_m$ and wall thickness $t$, cut longitudinally. The internal pressure $P$ acting on the projected area pushes the two halves apart.

Pressure force on projected area (length $L$):

$$F_P = P \cdot D_m \cdot L$$

Resisting force from the two cut edges:

$$F_W = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

Equating (static equilibrium):

$$P \cdot D_m \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$

Solving:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D_m}{2 t} = \frac{P (D_o - t)}{2 t} = \frac{P (SDR - 1)}{2}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Wall thickness** from $SDR = D_o / t$:

$$t = \frac{D_o}{SDR} = \frac{110}{11} = 10.0 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Pressure rating at 23 °C:**

Step 1 — Hydrostatic Design Stress:

$$HDS = HDB \times DF = 10.0 \times 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ MPa}$$

Step 2 — Pressure rating:

$$P_{23} = \frac{2 \cdot HDS}{SDR - 1} = \frac{2 \times 5.0}{11 - 1} = \frac{10.0}{10} = 1.0 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar}$$

**(iii) Pressure rating at 40 °C:**

$$P_{40} = P_{23} \times F_T = 10 \times 0.74 = 7.4 \text{ bar}$$

**Conclusion:** The same pipe can carry **10 bar at 23 °C** but only **7.4 bar at 40 °C** — a 26% derating.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **short-term hydrostatic burst pressure test** of a plastic pipe as per ASTM D 1599 / IS 12235 (Part 3). State the formula used to calculate the hoop stress at burst. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A UPVC pipe of OD 200 mm and wall thickness 7.7 mm failed at a burst pressure of 5.2 MPa in the laboratory.
(i) Calculate the hoop stress at failure.
(ii) Comment on whether the failure is in the **brittle** or **ductile** mode, justifying from the test data.
(iii) State the test parameters you would recommend for the **sustained pressure test** on the same pipe. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Test Description (7 marks)**

*Test Rig (ASTM D 1599 / IS 12235 Pt-3):*

1. A pipe specimen of minimum length **5 times OD** (or as specified, typically 250–500 mm) is sealed at both ends with end caps fitted with pressure inlet ports.
2. The assembly is filled completely with water (no air pockets) and connected to a **hydraulic pressure intensifier pump**.
3. Pressure is increased continuously and rapidly at a controlled rate — typically achieving burst within **60–70 seconds** (per ASTM D 1599).
4. A **pressure transducer** and timer record pressure vs. time until the pipe ruptures.
5. The **burst pressure** is recorded; the **mode of failure** (brittle — clean crack with little distortion; ductile — ballooning/whitening) is noted.

*Hoop stress at burst:*

$$\sigma_{burst} = \frac{P_{burst} \cdot (D_o - t)}{2 t}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Hoop stress at failure:**

$$D_m = D_o - t = 200 - 7.7 = 192.3 \text{ mm}$$

$$\sigma_{burst} = \frac{5.2 \times 192.3}{2 \times 7.7} = \frac{1000}{15.4} \approx 64.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Failure mode analysis:**

The burst stress of **~65 MPa** is well within the short-term tensile strength range of UPVC (50–60 MPa for unplasticised PVC; some grades reach 65–70 MPa for very short loading). Because the failure occurred at a relatively high stress with **short load duration (~60 s)**, this is consistent with a **brittle failure** typical of UPVC, which exhibits limited creep at room temperature. UPVC shows no significant yield plateau — once the elastic limit is crossed, fracture propagates rapidly.

> A **ductile** failure would show extensive wall ballooning and a stress below the short-term tensile strength. The data here suggests brittle fracture. ✓

**(iii) Recommended sustained pressure test parameters (ASTM D 1598 / ISO 1167):**

- **Induced hoop stress** = 80% of the short-term burst stress (i.e., ~52 MPa), or per ISO 1167 a defined circumferentially-applied stress.
- **Test temperature** = 23 °C (or 60 °C for accelerated ageing).
- **Test duration** = minimum 1000 hours (or 10,000 hours for HDB regression).
- **Test medium** = water inside, pipe in air outside (or water in/water out).
- **End conditions** = capped ends with free axial movement permitted (only hoop stress generated).
- **Pass criterion** = no failure throughout the test duration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Confusing SDR with DR (Dimension Ratio):** SDR uses *nominal* OD; DR uses *measured* OD. Mark difference in definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting the design factor:** Students often quote HDB directly as the design stress. Always show $HDS = HDB \times 0.5$ for water.
> 3. **Mixing $D_o$ and $D_m$:** Use $D_m = D_o - t$ in the hoop stress equation. Substituting $D_o$ instead of $D_m$ gives a stress that is *too high* and is treated as a calculation error.
> 4. **Skipping unit conversions:** $1 \text{ MPa} = 10 \text{ bar}$. Always show conversion in the final answer.
> 5. **Neglecting temperature derating:** In service-life questions, always check the F_T factor at the actual operating temperature.
> 6. **Wrong standard code:** "IS 4985" for HDPE or "IS 15328" for UPVC is a common slip-up. Memorise pipe-to-standard mapping.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Hoop stress** is the dominant failure stress in pressurised plastic pipes: $\sigma_h = P D_m / (2 t) = P(SDR-1)/2$.
- [x] **SDR = $D_o / t$** is the *manufacturer's design ratio* — a single number defines the pressure class for all diameters in that class.
- [x] **Design factor (DF)** is **0.5 for water**, **0.32 for gas** — never use the long-term strength directly in design.
- [x] **Temperature derating** is mandatory for service above 23 °C — consult the F_T table for the material.
- [x] **IS 4985** = UPVC potable water, **IS 15328** = HDPE potable water, **IS 13592** = PVC soil & waste.
- [x] **ASTM D 1599** = short-term burst (within 60–70 s); **ASTM D 1598** = sustained pressure (≥ 1000 h).
- [x] **ASTM D 2412** = ring stiffness (parallel plate loading) — used for buried drainage classification (SN 2, 4, 8, 16).
- [x] **ASTM D 2444** = falling-weight impact (tup test) — simulates handling/installation shock.
- [x] **ISO 2505** = longitudinal reversion test — detects residual stress from extrusion.
- [x] **ISO 306 / ASTM D 1525** = Vicat softening temperature — sets upper thermal limit.
- [x] **Longitudinal reversion** must be **< 5%** for a good pipe; > 5% indicates poor extrusion cooling.
- [x] **Mean diameter** $D_m = D_o - t$ — always use this in stress calculations.
- [x] **Ring stiffness classification** for buried pipes: SN 2 (PS ≥ 2 kPa) up to SN 16 (PS ≥ 16 kPa) as per EN 13476 / ISO 9969.
- [x] **Potable water approval** additionally requires **BS 6920 / IS 15466** (effect on water quality) and **chemical resistance** per **ISO 4433**.
- [x] For **CPVC hot-water pipes**, **Vicat > 110 °C** is the key acceptance criterion (vs. 79 °C for UPVC).
- [x] Always state the **standard number with year** in the exam — e.g., "IS 4985:2000" not just "IS 4985".
- [x] Final numerical answers: always express in **MPa and bar** for pressure, **mm** for dimensions, **%** for reversion/ovality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Film
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Film

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Polymer Film

A **polymer film** is a thin, continuous, self-supporting polymeric material whose thickness is significantly smaller than its length and width, generally lying in the range of **0.5 µm to 250 µm (micrometers)**. Films represent one of the most commercially important product forms of processed polymers, occupying a critical position in packaging, agriculture, electrical insulation, and advanced engineering applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPOT601, Module 3):**
> Polymer films are flat, flexible webs of polymeric material produced by continuous processing operations such as extrusion, casting, or blowing. They are characterized by a **very high surface-area-to-volume ratio** and exhibit anisotropic mechanical behavior depending on the orientation imparted during processing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: Intuitive Understanding

Think of a polymer film like a **transparent kitchen plastic wrap** that you use to cover food bowls. Although it is made of the same plastic (typically **LDPE - Low Density Polyethylene**) that goes into making thick buckets, it has been stretched and flattened so thin that it becomes a flexible, transparent sheet. The "stretching" during manufacturing aligns the long polymer molecules in specific directions, giving the film surprising strength in certain directions despite being incredibly thin.

Just as a sheet of paper has two major dimensions and one minor (thickness), a polymer film is engineered so that its mechanical, optical, and barrier properties can be precisely tuned by controlling:
- The **resin chemistry** (which polymer is used)
- The **processing method** (how it is made)
- The **orientation** (how the molecules are aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics & Ranges (Bold Highlights):**
> - Film Thickness: **0.5 µm to 250 µm**
> - Sheet Thickness: **> 250 µm to several mm**
> - Width: typically **10 cm to 10 m**
> - Production Speed: up to **500 m/min** (modern blown film lines)
> - Surface Area per kg: can exceed **50 m²/kg** for ultra-thin films

## 1.3 Classification of Polymer Films

Polymer films can be broadly classified based on **three key criteria**:

### A. Based on Manufacturing Process
| Process Type | Film Characteristic | Common Polymers |
|---|---|---|
| **Cast Film** (Extrusion Casting) | Glossy, excellent clarity, low haze | PP, PE, PET, PA |
| **Blown Film** (Tubular) | Balanced properties, high strength | LDPE, LLDPE, HDPE |
| **Calendered Film** | Uniform thickness, good surface | PVC, ABS |
| **Solution Cast** | Ultra-thin, high purity | Cellulose, PVA |
| **Biaxially Oriented Film (BOPET/BOPP)** | High strength, clarity, barrier | PP, PET, PA |

### B. Based on Orientation
- **Uniaxially Oriented**: Stretched in one direction (e.g., shrink film, strapping tape).
- **Biaxially Oriented (BOPP/BOPET)**: Stretched in two perpendicular directions — gives **superior mechanical, optical, and barrier** properties.

### C. Based on Structure
- **Monolayer Film**: Single polymer or blend.
- **Multilayer/Coextruded Film**: 3, 5, 7, or even more layers combining different polymers for combined properties (e.g., EVOH for oxygen barrier + PE for moisture barrier + tie layers for adhesion).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Film Manufacturing Processes

### 2.1.1 Cast Film Extrusion (Chill Roll Process)

**Principle:** Molten polymer is extruded through a flat die (slot die) onto a chilled, highly polished rotating roll. The film is rapidly quenched, yielding a film with **excellent optical clarity** and **minimal orientation**.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Polymer resin is fed into a **single-screw extruder** and melted.
2. Molten polymer passes through a **filter pack** (removing contaminants/gels).
3. Polymer is forced through a **T-die or coat-hanger die** to form a flat web.
4. The web is "pinned" to a **chill roll** using an air knife, vacuum box, or electrostatic pinner.
5. Film is cooled, edge-trimmed, and wound onto a roll.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why chill roll?** Rapid quenching (cooling rates of ~100 °C/s) prevents large crystallite formation, producing an **amorphous, transparent film** with low haze.

### 2.1.2 Blown Film Extrusion (Tubular Process)

**Principle:** Molten polymer is extruded through an **annular die**, and the tube is inflated with air. The bubble is simultaneously stretched **axially** (by take-up rolls) and **transversely** (by internal air pressure), creating a **biaxially oriented** film in one operation.

**Operational Steps:**
1. Polymer is melted in an extruder and pushed through an **annular (circular) die**.
2. Air is introduced through the die center, inflating the polymer tube into a **bubble**.
3. The bubble is cooled by an **air ring** (forced convective cooling).
4. The bubble passes through **nip rolls** at the top, which collapse and seal it into a flat tube ("lay-flat tubing").
5. The flat tube is wound onto rolls or slit and wound as separate sheets.

**Key Engineering Parameters:**
- **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_{\text{bubble}}}{D_{\text{die}}}$
- **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{\text{Die gap}}{\text{Final film thickness}}$
- **Frost Line Height (FLH):** Critical design parameter where the bubble surface becomes opaque due to crystallization.

### 2.1.3 Biaxial Orientation (Tenter Frame Process)

**Principle:** A thick cast sheet is stretched sequentially or simultaneously in **machine direction (MD)** and **transverse direction (TD)** at temperatures above the glass transition ($T_g$) but below the melting point ($T_m$).

**Operational Steps:**
1. **Extrusion:** Polymer sheet is cast on a chilled roll.
2. **Machine Direction Orientation (MDO):** Sheet passes over heated rolls rotating at progressively higher speeds — stretching it 4× to 10× in the MD.
3. **Transverse Direction Orientation (TDO):** Sheet enters a **tenter frame**, where clips grip the edges and diverge in an oven, stretching it 6× to 12× in the TD.
4. **Heat Setting (Annealing):** Oriented film is held at elevated temperature to **relax stresses** and lock in properties.
5. **Cooling and Winding:** Film is cooled and wound.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Biaxial orientation dramatically improves:
> - Tensile strength (up to **5× to 10×** improvement).
> - Stiffness and modulus.
> - Optical clarity (stress-induced crystallization of PP produces BOPP with **haze < 1%**).
> - Barrier properties (gas permeability reduced by **30% to 60%**).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)** | $\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$ | dimensionless | Blown film process design |
| 2 | **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)** | $\text{DDR} = \dfrac{G_d}{t_f}$ | dimensionless | Thickness control in blown film |
| 3 | **Machine Direction Stretch Ratio** | $\lambda_{\text{MD}} = \dfrac{v_2}{v_1}$ | dimensionless | MDO process |
| 4 | **Transverse Direction Stretch Ratio** | $\lambda_{\text{TD}} = \dfrac{W_f}{W_i}$ | dimensionless | TDO process |
| 5 | **Total Areal Stretch** | $\lambda_{\text{area}} = \lambda_{\text{MD}} \times \lambda_{\text{TD}}$ | dimensionless | Overall biaxial orientation |
| 6 | **Tensile Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} = \dfrac{F}{w \cdot t}$ | MPa or N/m² | Mechanical testing |
| 7 | **Elongation at Break** | $\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | % | Ductility assessment |
| 8 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon}$ | MPa | Stiffness measurement |
| 9 | **Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR)** | $\text{OTR} = \dfrac{q}{A \cdot t}$ | $\frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$ | Barrier testing |
| 10 | **Permeability Coefficient** | $P = D \cdot S$ | $\frac{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}$ | Fundamental barrier property |
| 11 | **Water Vapor Transmission Rate (WVTR)** | $\text{WVTR} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot t}$ | $\frac{\text{g}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day}}$ | Moisture barrier |
| 12 | **Haze Value** | $H = \dfrac{I_d}{I_t} \times 100$ | % | Optical property (ASTM D1003) |
| 13 | **Yield (Production)** | $Y = \dfrac{m}{t \cdot v \cdot w}$ | $\frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h} \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{m/min}}$ | Production rate |
| 14 | **Crimp/Unfold Ratio (BOPET)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{L_f - L_i}{L_i} \times 100$ | % | Heat-shrink character |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging Industry:** BOPP and BOPET films account for over **70%** of flexible packaging films globally. Multilayer barrier films extend food shelf life from days to months.
- **Agricultural Films:** LDPE/LLDPE mulch films and greenhouse films improve crop yield by 20–50%.
- **Electrical/Electronics:** BOPET films are used as **dielectric insulators** in capacitors and flexible printed circuits (FPCs).
- **Solar Energy:** EVA (ethylene-vinyl acetate) encapsulant films protect photovoltaic cells for **25+ years** of outdoor service.
- **Medical:** PVDF and PTFE films serve as **breathable membranes** in surgical gowns and protective apparel.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Production Rate of a Cast Film Line

The **production rate** $Q$ of a cast film line (mass flow rate of polymer) is a fundamental design equation that students must master for KTU examinations.

### Given Conditions
A cast film line produces a film of:
- Width $w = 1.2 \, \text{m}$
- Thickness $t_f = 50 \, \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- Line speed $v = 60 \, \text{m/min} = 1.0 \, \text{m/s}$
- Polymer density $\rho = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (typical for LDPE)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the film web:**
The film is treated as a thin rectangular cross-section with width $w$ and thickness $t_f$.

$$A_{\text{cs}} = w \times t_f$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of polymer:**
The volume of polymer extruded per second equals the cross-section swept per unit time by the moving film.

$$Q_v = A_{\text{cs}} \times v = (w \times t_f) \times v$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$Q_v = 1.2 \times (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times 1.0 = 6.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate (production rate):**
Using the density relation $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$, mass per unit time is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q_v$$

Substituting the values:

$$\dot{m} = 920 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-5} = 5.52 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to standard units (kg/hour):**

$$\dot{m} = 5.52 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 198.72 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 199 \, \text{kg/h}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = \rho \cdot w \cdot t_f \cdot v = 199 \, \text{kg/h}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Recap:** This formula is the foundation for any **capacity-planning or cost-analysis** problem on cast film lines. It is *very frequently* asked in KTU Part B (14-mark) problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Blow-Up Ratio and Draw-Down Ratio Relationship

In a **blown film process**, the final film thickness is determined by two independent ratios: BUR and DDR. Let us derive the relationship.

### Given Conditions
- Die diameter $D_d = 200 \, \text{mm}$
- Die gap (die land opening) $G_d = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$
- Bubble diameter $D_b = 600 \, \text{mm}$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute Blow-Up Ratio:**

$$\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} = \dfrac{600}{200} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Relate the bubble cross-section to the die cross-section:**
The polymer cross-section in the die is a thin annulus:

$$A_{\text{die}} = \pi \cdot D_d \cdot G_d$$

The bubble cross-section is (when we consider the flattened tube laid flat, thickness $t_f$):

$$A_{\text{bubble,flat}} = \pi \cdot D_b \cdot t_f$$

(Here, $D_b$ is the bubble diameter, and when flattened, the tube width is $\pi D_b / 2$ on each side, so total perimeter is $\pi D_b$.)

**Step 3 — Mass conservation (incompressible polymer, steady state):**
By conservation of mass, the polymer volumetric flow rate is constant:

$$A_{\text{die}} \times v_{\text{die}} = A_{\text{bubble,flat}} \times v_{\text{film}}$$

Assuming a unit take-up speed $v_{\text{film}}$ for simplicity, the thickness is:

$$t_f = \dfrac{D_d \cdot G_d}{D_b} = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Draw-Down Ratio:**

$$\text{DDR} = \dfrac{G_d}{t_f} = \text{BUR} = 3.0$$

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution:**

$$t_f = \dfrac{1.5 \, \text{mm}}{3.0} = 0.5 \, \text{mm} = 500 \, \mu\text{m}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{t_f = \dfrac{G_d}{\text{BUR}} = 500 \, \mu\text{m}, \quad \text{DDR} = \text{BUR} = 3.0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** When BUR = DDR, the film has **balanced biaxial orientation**, giving nearly equal mechanical properties in MD and TD. Deviation from this equality creates **anisotropic films** (e.g., higher tear strength in one direction). This is a high-yield KTU concept.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem: Oxygen Permeability Calculation

A PET film of **thickness 12 µm** is tested for oxygen permeability. The measured OTR (Oxygen Transmission Rate) is **110 cm³/(m²·day)** at standard conditions (23 °C, 50% RH, 1 atm O₂ partial pressure difference).

**Find the permeability coefficient $P$.**

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the relationship between OTR and $P$:**

$$\text{OTR} = \dfrac{P \cdot \Delta p}{L}$$

where:
- $L$ = film thickness (m)
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference of O₂ across the film (Pa)
- $P$ = permeability coefficient

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $P$:**

$$P = \text{OTR} \times \dfrac{L}{\Delta p}$$

**Step 3 — Convert units to SI:**
- OTR = $110 \, \text{cm}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day})$
- Convert to $\text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$:

$$\text{OTR} = \dfrac{110 \times 10^{-6}}{86400} = 1.273 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^3/(\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s})$$

- $L = 12 \, \mu\text{m} = 12 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $\Delta p = 0.21 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 2.127 \times 10^4 \, \text{Pa}$ (assuming pure O₂ on one side and 0% on the other for test, but for typical OTR test, $\Delta p_{O_2} = 1 \, \text{atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$)

Using $\Delta p = 1.013 \times 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$:

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$P = (1.273 \times 10^{-6}) \times \dfrac{12 \times 10^{-6}}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate:**

$$P = 1.273 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.185 \times 10^{-10} = 1.508 \times 10^{-16} \, \frac{\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}$$

### Final Result

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-16} \, \frac{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{cm}}{\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s} \cdot \text{Pa}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value is **typical for BOPET films**. For comparison, LDPE has $P \approx 30 \times 10^{-16}$ (about 20× more permeable), while EVOH has $P \approx 0.01 \times 10^{-16}$ (about 150× better barrier). This is why EVOH is the **workhorse oxygen barrier** in multilayer food packaging.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cast Film Extrusion Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[RESIN HOPPER] --> B[EXTRUDER BARREL WITH SCREW]
    B --> C[MELT FILTER PACK]
    C --> D[SLOT DIE T DIE]
    D --> E[AIR KNIFE OR VACUUM PINNER]
    E --> F[CHILL ROLL PRIMARY]
    F --> G[CHILL ROLL SECONDARY]
    G --> H[EDGE TRIMMER]
    H --> I[EDGE RECLAIM UNIT]
    H --> J[THICKNESS GAUGE SCANNER]
    J --> K[TREATMENT STATION CORONA]
    K --> L[NIP ROLLS]
    L --> M[WINDER MOTORIZED]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The polymer follows the linear path from raw resin (yellow) → melting (blue) → die shaping (red) → quenching (green) → winding (purple). The branch into the **edge reclaim unit** recovers the trimmed edges for reprocessing, ensuring **near-zero waste** in modern lines.

---

## 4.2 Blown Film Process — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[POLYMER MELT FROM EXTRUDER] --> P2[ANNULAR DIE CENTER FEED]
    P2 --> P3[AIR INJECTION INTO BUBBLE]
    P3 --> P4[INFLATED POLYMER BUBBLE]
    P4 --> P5[AIR RING COOLING]
    P5 --> P6[FROST LINE CRYSTALLIZATION]
    P6 --> P7[COLLAPSING FRAME]
    P7 --> P8[NIP ROLLS]
    P8 --> P9[SLITTER AND WINDER]
    P8 --> P10[LAY FLAT TUBE WINDER]

    subgraph QUALITY_MONITORING
        QM1[BUR SENSOR] -.-> P4
        QM2[FROST LINE HEIGHT CAMERA] -.-> P6
        QM3[THICKNESS GAUGE] -.-> P8
    end

    style P1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P8 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P9 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **QUALITY_MONITORING** subgraph (dotted lines) represents the **non-invasive sensors** that continuously feed data to the line control system. Modern blown film lines use **machine learning algorithms** to predict and correct thickness variations in real time, achieving **±1% thickness uniformity**.

---

## 4.3 Biaxial Orientation (Tenter Frame) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[EXTRUDER AND CAST SHEET] --> R2[MDO PREHEAT ROLLS]
    R2 --> R3[MDO SLOW ROLL]
    R3 --> R4[MDO FAST ROLL STRETCH MD]
    R4 --> R5[TENTER FRAME INLET]
    R5 --> R6[TENTER OVEN STRETCH TD]
    R6 --> R7[HEAT SETTING ZONE]
    R7 --> R8[COOLING ZONE]
    R8 --> R9[EDGE TRIMMER]
    R9 --> R10[CHILL ROLL FINAL]
    R10 --> R11[WINDER BOPET OR BOPP]

    style R1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R4 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R6 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R7 fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R11 fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU:** In the tenter frame, the **clips gripping the edges** are what physically apply the TD stretch. Each clip is connected to a moving chain on a divergent rail, providing uniform transverse stretching across the entire web width.

---

## 4.4 Multilayer Coextrusion Architecture — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EX1[EXTRUDER A OUTER LAYER] --> FB1[FEEDBLOCK]
    EX2[EXTRUDER B CORE LAYER] --> FB1
    EX3[EXTRUDER C INNER LAYER] --> FB1
    EX4[EXTRUDER D TIE LAYER] --> FB1
    FB1 --> CD[COEXTRUSION DIE]
    CD --> CH[CHILL ROLL]
    CH --> WND[WINDER]

    subgraph LAYER_FUNCTIONS
        LF1[OUTER LAYER PRINTABILITY AND SEAL] -.-> EX1
        LF2[BULK LAYER MECHANICAL STRENGTH] -.-> EX2
        LF3[INNER LAYER OXYGEN BARRIER EVOH] -.-> EX3
        LF4[TIE LAYER ADHESION] -.-> EX4
    end

    style EX1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EX2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EX3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EX4 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style FB1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CD fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style WND fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multilayer?** A single polymer **cannot** simultaneously provide printability, strength, gas barrier, and moisture barrier. Coextrusion **engineers** each layer to do one job, and the **tie layers** (typically maleic anhydride-grafted polyolefins) ensure adhesion between chemically dissimilar polymers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
**Define the term "polymer film" and mention any four applications of polymer films in industry.**

**Model Answer:**
A polymer film is a thin, continuous, self-supporting web of polymeric material with a thickness typically ranging from **0.5 µm to 250 µm**, produced by continuous processes such as extrusion, casting, or blowing.

**Four applications:**
1. **Flexible food packaging** (BOPP, BOPET, PE films for snacks, biscuits, ready-to-eat meals).
2. **Agricultural mulch films** (LDPE/LLDPE for soil moisture retention and weed control).
3. **Electrical insulation** (BOPET film used in motor windings, capacitor dielectrics).
4. **Photovoltaic encapsulation** (EVA film for solar panel laminates).
5. (Any other valid application: shrink wrap, cling film, medical barrier films, decorative lamination, etc.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is meant by "Blow-Up Ratio" (BUR) in the blown film process? Write its formula and state its typical range.**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** The Blow-Up Ratio is the ratio of the **diameter of the inflated polymer bubble** at the frost line to the **diameter of the annular die** through which the polymer is extruded.

**Formula:**

$$\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d}$$

where $D_b$ is the bubble diameter and $D_d$ is the die diameter.

**Typical Range:** BUR values generally lie between **1.5 and 4.0** for commercial blown film lines. A BUR of 2.0–3.0 is most common for LDPE/LLDPE film production.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **cast film extrusion process** with a neat block diagram. List its advantages and limitations over the blown film process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast film line is producing a film of width **1.5 m** and thickness **75 µm** at a line speed of **80 m/min**. The polymer is LDPE with density **0.92 g/cm³**. Calculate the **production rate in kg/h** and the **length of film produced per hour**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Cast Film Extrusion Process

**Principle:** Molten polymer is extruded through a flat T-die onto a chilled, highly polished rotating roll where it solidifies into a thin, flat film.

**Process Steps:**
1. **Resin feeding:** LDPE/PP/PET pellets are fed from a hopper into the extruder barrel.
2. **Melting and conveying:** A single-screw extruder melts the polymer and pumps it at a constant rate.
3. **Filtering:** The melt passes through a filter pack (screens) to remove gels, unmelted particles, and contaminants.
4. **Die forming:** A **T-die or coat-hanger die** shapes the melt into a thin, uniform rectangular web.
5. **Quenching:** The web is "pinned" against a chilled roll (chill roll) using an **air knife, vacuum box, or electrostatic pinner** to ensure intimate contact for rapid cooling (~100 °C/s).
6. **Edge trimming:** The edges (beads) are trimmed and sent to a granulator for reprocessing.
7. **Thickness measurement:** A traversing beta or X-ray gauge measures thickness profile across the width.
8. **Surface treatment:** A corona or flame treatment station improves surface energy for printing/metallization.
9. **Winding:** The film is wound onto a motorized winder with tension control.

**Advantages of Cast Film over Blown Film:**
- Higher production speeds (up to **600 m/min** vs. ~200 m/min for blown).
- Better optical clarity and lower haze.
- More uniform thickness profile (gauge variation ±2% vs. ±5%).
- No internal bubble cooling — simpler air system.
- Better flatness (no curling from uneven cooling).

**Limitations of Cast Film:**
- Film is **less balanced** in MD vs. TD properties (less biaxial orientation).
- Capital cost of chill roll and polishing equipment is higher.
- Edge trim waste is typically 5–8% (vs. 1–2% for blown film).
- Limited ability to make tubing (only flat film).

#### Part (b) — Production Rate Calculation

**Given:**
- Width $w = 1.5 \, \text{m}$
- Thickness $t_f = 75 \, \mu\text{m} = 75 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- Line speed $v = 80 \, \text{m/min}$
- Density $\rho = 0.92 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 920 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_{\text{cs}} = w \times t_f = 1.5 \times 75 \times 10^{-6} = 1.125 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times A_{\text{cs}} \times v$$

$$\dot{m} = 920 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-4} \times 80 \, \frac{\text{m}}{\text{min}}$$

$$\dot{m} = 920 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-4} \times \dfrac{80}{60} \, \frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}$$

$$\dot{m} = 920 \times 1.125 \times 10^{-4} \times 1.333 = 0.138 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kg/h:**

$$\dot{m} = 0.138 \times 3600 = 496.8 \, \text{kg/h} \approx 497 \, \text{kg/h}$$

**Step 4 — Length of film produced per hour:**

$$L = v \times 60 = 80 \times 60 = 4800 \, \text{m/h} = 4.8 \, \text{km/h}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 497 \, \text{kg/h}, \quad L = 4.8 \, \text{km/h}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**(a)** Describe the **blown film extrusion process** with a neat diagram. Explain the significance of **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR)**, **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR)**, and **Frost Line Height (FLH)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blown film line uses a die of diameter **150 mm** and die gap **1.2 mm**. The bubble diameter at the frost line is **450 mm**. Calculate the **BUR**, the **final film thickness** (assuming DDR = BUR), and comment on the **orientation balance** of the film. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Blown Film Process and Key Parameters

**Process Description:** Molten polymer from an extruder is forced through an **annular (circular) die** vertically upward. Compressed air introduced through the die center inflates the polymer tube into a **bubble**. The bubble is cooled externally by an **air ring** that directs cooling air along its surface. The solidified bubble is collapsed by a **collapsing frame** into a flat tube ("lay-flat"), pulled by **nip rolls**, and wound.

**Significance of Key Parameters:**

1. **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** $\text{BUR} = D_b / D_d$
   - Controls the **transverse direction (TD) stretch** imparted to the film.
   - Higher BUR → more TD orientation → better TD tensile strength and tear resistance.
   - Typical range: **1.5 to 4.0**.

2. **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** $\text{DDR} = G_d / t_f$
   - Controls the **machine direction (MD) stretch** imparted to the film.
   - Determined by take-up speed relative to extrusion rate.
   - Higher DDR → more MD orientation → better MD tensile strength.

3. **Frost Line Height (FLH):** Distance from the die exit to the point where the bubble becomes **opaque (cloudy)** due to onset of crystallization.
   - Determines the **cooling rate** of the polymer.
   - Low FLH → rapid cooling → less crystallization → more transparent film.
   - High FLH → slow cooling → more crystallization → stiffer, more opaque film.
   - Affects **tensile, optical, and shrinkage** properties.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- Die diameter $D_d = 150 \, \text{mm}$
- Die gap $G_d = 1.2 \, \text{mm}$
- Bubble diameter $D_b = 450 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 1 — Calculate BUR:**

$$\text{BUR} = \dfrac{D_b}{D_d} = \dfrac{450}{150} = 3.0$$

**Step 2 — Calculate final film thickness (assuming DDR = BUR):**
Using the derived relationship $t_f = G_d / \text{BUR}$:

$$t_f = \dfrac{1.2 \, \text{mm}}{3.0} = 0.4 \, \text{mm} = 400 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Comment on orientation balance:**

Since **DDR = BUR = 3.0**, the film has **equal MD and TD stretch ratios**. This results in a **balanced biaxial orientation**, where the polymer chains are aligned equally in both directions.

**Consequences of balanced orientation:**
- Tensile strength is **approximately equal in MD and TD**.
- Tear strength is also balanced.
- The film is **less prone to splitting** along one direction (good for packaging).
- However, **balanced orientation** typically gives lower ultimate strength in *any* single direction compared to a **highly biaxially oriented** film (e.g., BOPP with $\lambda_{MD} \times \lambda_{TD} = 50$).

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{\text{BUR} = 3.0, \quad t_f = 400 \, \mu\text{m}, \quad \text{Balanced biaxial orientation}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Failing to convert µm to m, or m/min to m/s — examiners specifically check unit consistency. *Always write units explicitly*.
> 2. **Confusing BUR with DDR:** These are *different* parameters. BUR controls TD orientation, DDR controls MD orientation.
> 3. **Forgetting to multiply by 60 (or 3600):** When converting production rate from per-minute to per-hour.
> 4. **Not defining terms in part (a):** When asked to "explain", you must define *each* technical term you use (BUR, DDR, FLH, OTR, etc.).
> 5. **Missing the diagram:** A "neat diagram" is worth **2 to 3 marks** by itself. Always draw a labeled block diagram or schematic, even a hand-drawn one.
> 6. **Skipping the "comment" in numerical problems:** When the problem says "comment on..." or "explain the result...", this is typically worth 1–2 marks. Don't leave the answer purely numerical.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Film:**

### Core Concepts
- A **polymer film** is a thin, flexible web with thickness between **0.5 µm and 250 µm**.
- Three primary manufacturing processes: **Cast Film, Blown Film, Biaxially Oriented Film (BOPET/BOPP)**.
- Each process imparts **different molecular orientations**, leading to different properties.

### Key Definitions
- **Blow-Up Ratio (BUR):** $D_b / D_d$ — controls TD orientation.
- **Draw-Down Ratio (DDR):** $G_d / t_f$ — controls MD orientation.
- **Frost Line Height (FLH):** Distance to crystallization onset in blown film.
- **Biaxial Orientation:** Stretching in two perpendicular directions to improve properties.

### Essential Formulas
- Production rate: $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot w \cdot t_f \cdot v$
- Film thickness in blown film: $t_f = G_d / \text{BUR}$ (when DDR = BUR)
- Permeability: $P = D \cdot S$ (Henry's law of barrier transport)

### Critical Comparisons
- **Cast film:** Higher clarity, faster line speed, less balanced orientation.
- **Blown film:** Balanced properties, lower capital cost, more complex air system.
- **BOPP/BOPET:** Highest strength, clarity, and barrier — used in demanding packaging and electronics.

### Must-Remember Facts (High-Yield for KTU)
- BOPP haze: **< 1%** (extremely clear).
- BOPET tensile strength: **~ 200 MPa** (vs. ~ 15 MPa for unoriented PET).
- EVOH is the **best oxygen barrier** polymer — used as core layer in coextrusion.
- Multilayer films can have **3 to 9 layers** to combine different properties.
- Typical **commercial film thickness**: 12–100 µm for packaging, 6–25 µm for capacitor films.

### Quick Mnemonic
**"C-B-B-B"** for film processes:
- **C**ast (T-die + chill roll)
- **B**lown (annular die + bubble)
- **B**iaxial (Tenter frame, BOPP, BOPET)
- **B**arrier (multilayer coextrusion with EVOH)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **polymer sheet** is defined as a flat, continuous, semi-finished or finished polymeric product whose **thickness is significantly smaller than its length and width**, typically having a thickness ranging from **0.25 mm to 12 mm**. Sheets are produced by converting thermoplastic or thermosetting polymers (e.g., PVC, PE, PP, PS, PMMA, ABS, Polycarbonate) through standardized industrial processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Classification Note (Standard Engineering Distinction):**
> - **Film:** Thickness $\le 0.25$ mm
> - **Sheet:** Thickness $> 0.25$ mm (up to 12 mm)
> - **Plate:** Thickness $> 12$ mm

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a baker rolling out a long, flat strip of dough to make a pastry base. The polymer sheet-making process is conceptually identical:
- The "dough" is the **molten polymer melt** exiting the extruder.
- The "rolling pin" can be either a **flat die** (extrusion process) or **counter-rotating heated rolls** (calendering process).
- The "knife" trimming the edges is the **edge trimmer** that gives the final rectangular shape.

In simpler terms, a polymer sheet is a **continuous flat slab** of solidified plastic produced by squeezing or rolling molten plastic into a uniform thin layer and then cooling it down so it keeps that flat shape permanently.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (Bold) for KTU Reference:**
> - Standard sheet width: **1.0 m to 3.0 m**
> - Production speed (extrusion): **0.5 to 30 m/min**
> - Production speed (calendering): **30 to 120 m/min**
> - **Density of PVC sheet:** ~1.4 g/cm³
> - **Density of PP sheet:** ~0.91 g/cm³

### Types of Polymer Sheets

| Category | Examples | Engineering Use |
|----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Rigid Sheets** | PVC rigid, PS, PMMA, PC | Glazing, displays, automotive panels |
| **Flexible Sheets** | Plasticized PVC, LDPE, LLDPE | Packaging, water-proofing membranes |
| **Foamed Sheets** | Foamed PS, foamed PVC, foamed PP | Insulation, signage, packaging |
| **Multilayer/Co-extruded Sheets** | EVOH/PE/PP, ABA structures | Barrier packaging, food containers |
| **Reinforced Sheets** | Glass-mat thermoplastic (GMT) | Automotive structural parts |
| **Laminated Sheets** | PVC/PMMA, ABS/PVC | Decorative surfaces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Under the outcome-based framework, students must be able to **map product requirements to material and process selection** (CO-aligned: CO2 / CO3 depending on syllabus).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sheet Extrusion Process — Step-by-Step

Sheet extrusion is the **primary industrial method** for producing polymer sheets. The process converts polymer pellets into a uniform continuous sheet through a sequence of thermal, mechanical, and cooling operations.

**Process Flow Logic:**

1. **Feeding:** Polymer resin (powder/granules) is fed from a hopper into the extruder barrel via gravity. Additives (stabilizers, plasticizers, colorants) may be pre-blended.
2. **Melting and Conveying (Barrel):** The screw rotates inside a heated barrel. Polymer pellets are **plasticated** (melted and mixed) by a combination of conduction from barrel heaters and viscous shear from the screw.
3. **Metering:** In the metering section, the polymer melt is pressurized to a steady, uniform pressure and temperature suitable for die entry. A **screen pack and breaker plate** filter out contaminants and remove rotational flow (Mandel effect).
4. **Die Feeding (Adapter):** A streamlined adapter pipe transfers melt from the extruder to the die, ensuring laminar flow and equal pressure distribution.
5. **Sheet Die:** The melt is formed into a flat, wide profile. The two main industrial die types are:
   - **Coathanger Die** (most common for sheets): A teardrop-shaped manifold ensures uniform pressure and velocity across the die width.
   - **T-die / Fish-tail Die** (older design): Uses a long tapered manifold.
6. **Calibration / Cooling:** The molten sheet enters a **three-roll cooling stack (polishing stack)** which both cools and gives the surface finish.
7. **Haul-off and Edge Trimming:** Rubber pull rolls pull the sheet at a constant speed. **Edge trimmers** cut off uneven borders.
8. **Cutting / Winding:** The sheet is cut to length by a flying shear or guillotine, or wound on a roll for flexible sheets.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Coathanger die is preferred:** It compensates for the pressure drop along the manifold by progressively increasing the land length, producing **uniform sheet thickness** across the full width.

### 2.2 Calendering Process

Calendering uses a series of **heated counter-rotating rolls** to continuously press molten polymer (pre-compounded and pre-heated) into thin sheets. It is preferred for **PVC sheets** (rigid and flexible) due to the high output and excellent surface finish.

**Three-Roll Calendar Configuration (Inverse L):**
- Feed roll (top) → Polymer enters
- Middle roll → Compressed into thin film
- Bottom roll → Final thickness and surface finish
- Sheet exits downward or sideways depending on layout

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Volumetric Output** | $Q = v \cdot W \cdot h$ | m³/s | $v$ = line speed, $W$ = width, $h$ = thickness |
| 2 | **Mass Throughput** | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot v \cdot W \cdot h$ | kg/s | $\rho$ = melt density |
| 3 | **Calender Roll Velocity (Frictionless)** | $v = \tfrac{1}{2}(R_1 \omega_1 + R_2 \omega_2)$ | m/s | $R$ = roll radius, $\omega$ = angular velocity |
| 4 | **Friction Ratio (Calender)** | $f = \dfrac{v_2}{v_1}$ | dimensionless | $f > 1$: roll 2 faster; $f < 1$: roll 1 faster |
| 5 | **Roll Separating Force (simplified)** | $F_s = p_{\text{avg}} \cdot L \cdot W$ | N | $p_{\text{avg}}$ = avg pressure, $L$ = contact length |
| 6 | **Avg Pressure (Calender — Ardichvili / Gaskell)** | $p_{\text{avg}} \approx \dfrac{3 \mu v W}{4 h^2}$ | Pa | $\mu$ = melt viscosity |
| 7 | **Coathanger Die Pressure Drop** | $\Delta p = \dfrac{12 \mu Q L}{W h^3}$ | Pa | $L$ = die land length |
| 8 | **Die Swell Ratio** | $B = \dfrac{h_{\text{out}}}{h_{\text{die}}}$ | dimensionless | Typically $1.0$ to $1.5$ |
| 9 | **Newtonian Cooling (Roll)** | $q = h_c A (T_s - T_{\text{roll}})$ | W | $h_c$ = heat transfer coefficient |
| 10 | **Stretch Ratio (Thermoforming input)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{L_{\text{part}}}{L_{\text{blank}}}$ | dimensionless | Material thinning factor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions — Board Examiner's Eye:**
> - All lengths in **metres**, time in **seconds**, mass in **kg**.
> - Coathanger die equation assumes **fully developed laminar flow** and **Newtonian approximation** (for design estimates).
> - Calender Ardichvili pressure is valid in the **frictionless Newtonian nip region**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Polymer sheet lines are critical in:
- **Packaging industry:** Thermoformed cups, blister packs, food containers (PP, PS, PET sheets).
- **Construction:** PVC roofing membranes, polycarbonate roofing panels.
- **Automotive:** GMT (glass mat thermoplastic) sheets for bumper beams, dashboards.
- **Signage & Display:** Foamed PVC, acrylic (PMMA) sheets.
- **Agriculture:** LDPE / LLDPE greenhouse films and mulching sheets.
- **Medical:** PVC sheets for blood bags, IV containers.

The **design challenge** is always to obtain **uniform thickness**, **stress-free cooling**, and **desired surface finish** (gloss, matte, embossed) at the highest possible production rate.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Coathanger Die Pressure Drop

**Physical Setup:** A polymer melt flows through a rectangular channel of width $W$, thickness $h$, and length $L$ (the die land), with the melt behaving as a **Newtonian fluid** of viscosity $\mu$ under steady laminar flow.

**Step 1: Governing Equation**
For Newtonian laminar flow in a slit (slot) die, the Hagen–Poiseuille analog gives the **volume flow rate per unit width**:

$$
\frac{Q}{W} = \frac{h^3 \, \Delta p}{12 \, \mu L}
$$

**Step 2: Rearranging for Pressure Drop**
Multiplying both sides by $12 \mu L / (W h^3)$:

$$
\Delta p = \frac{12 \, \mu \, Q \, L}{W \, h^3}
$$

**Step 3: Substituting Typical Industrial Values**
Suppose: $Q = 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s, $W = 1.5$ m, $h = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $L = 0.05$ m, $\mu = 1000$ Pa·s.

$$
\Delta p = \frac{12 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05}{1.5 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^3}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{12 \times 1000 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.05}{1.5 \times 8 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{0.30}{1.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 25 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic:** Numerator $= 0.30$ Pa·m³/s (simplified), Denominator $= 1.2 \times 10^{-8}$ m⁴, giving Pa when divided (consistent).

**Step 4: Engineering Interpretation**
This is a **very high pressure** — therefore dies must be **mechanically robust** (hardened steel), and the die lip must be **finely adjustable** to compensate for any flow imbalance.

### 3.2 Derivation of Ardichvili's Calender Pressure (Newtonian, Frictionless)

**Physical Setup:** Two counter-rotating rolls of radius $R$ with surface speeds $v_1$ and $v_2$ nip a polymer melt of viscosity $\mu$. The half-nip gap is $H_0$ and the actual local gap at angle $\theta$ is $H(\theta)$.

**Step 1: Geometric Relation of Gap**

$$
H(\theta) = H_0 \left(1 + \cos\theta\right) + \sqrt{2 R H_0}\, \sin\theta
$$

For small $\theta$, this simplifies to:

$$
H(\theta) \approx H_0 + \frac{\theta^2}{2}(R + H_0)
$$

**Step 2: Velocity Field (Lubrication Approximation)**
The velocity distribution between rolls satisfies:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = \mu \frac{\partial^2 v}{\partial y^2}
$$

Integrating twice with the boundary conditions $v = v_1$ at $y = -H/2$ and $v = v_2$ at $y = +H/2$:

$$
v(y) = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} + \frac{v_2 - v_1}{H}\, y + \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\left(y^2 - \frac{H^2}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Flow Rate Continuity**

$$
Q = \int_{-H/2}^{H/2} v \, dy = \frac{v_1 + v_2}{2} H - \frac{H^3}{12 \mu}\frac{dp}{dx}
$$

**Step 4: Maximum Pressure Condition**
Setting $dp/d\theta = 0$ (location of peak pressure) and integrating across the nip:

$$
p(\theta) = \frac{3 \mu v}{H_0} \cdot \frac{(2 + f)(1 + \cos\theta) - f}{(1 + \cos\theta)^2} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

where $f = v_1 / v_2$ is the **friction ratio**.

**Step 5: Average Pressure Approximation (Simplified for KTU)**
For a typical calendering scenario ($f \approx 1$), the **average pressure** is given by:

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3 \mu v W}{4 H_0^2}
$$

**Step 6: Substituting Numerical Values**
Assume: $\mu = 500$ Pa·s, $v = 0.1$ m/s, $W = 1.0$ m, $H_0 = 0.001$ m.

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3 \times 500 \times 0.1 \times 1.0}{4 \times (10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{150}{4 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.75 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} = 37.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** The denominator is $(H_0)^2 = (10^{-3})^2 = 10^{-6}$. Dividing 150 by $4 \times 10^{-6}$ yields $3.75 \times 10^{7}$ Pa. This result aligns with industrial calender roll pressures (typically 10–50 MPa).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sheet Extrusion vs. Calendering

| Decision Parameter | Sheet Extrusion | Calendering | Verdict |
|--------------------|-----------------|-------------|---------|
| **Capital Cost** | Lower | Higher (4-roll stack expensive) | Extrusion preferred for low volume |
| **Production Speed** | 0.5 – 30 m/min | 30 – 120 m/min | Calender wins on output |
| **Sheet Thickness Range** | 0.2 – 12 mm | 0.05 – 1.5 mm | Calender: thin flexible films/sheets |
| **Surface Finish** | Good (roll stack dependent) | Excellent (mirror-finish possible) | Calender superior |
| **Material Suitability** | Most thermoplastics | Mainly PVC (also PE, PP, ABS) | Material-bound |
| **Thermoforming Feedstock** | Excellent (controlled gauge) | Excellent (highly uniform) | Both suitable |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Higher melt temp → more energy | Lower melt temp → less energy | Calender more efficient |
| **Width Flexibility** | Easy die change | Roll change expensive | Extrusion more flexible |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A sheet extrusion line produces a PP sheet of width 1.2 m at a line speed of 5 m/min. The sheet thickness is 2.5 mm. Calculate:
(a) Volumetric output (m³/s)
(b) Mass throughput (kg/s), given $\rho_{PP} = 910$ kg/m³
(c) Annual production in tonnes (assuming 7000 working hours/year)

**Solution:**

**(a) Volumetric output:**

$$
Q = v \cdot W \cdot h
$$

Convert units: $v = 5 / 60 = 0.0833$ m/s, $W = 1.2$ m, $h = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m.

$$
Q = 0.0833 \times 1.2 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(b) Mass throughput:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 910 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.2275 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(c) Annual production:**

$$
\text{Mass/year} = 0.2275 \times 7000 \times 3600 = 5.733 \times 10^{6} \text{ kg} = 5733 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Verification Check:** $0.2275 \times 3600 = 819$ kg/h. Then $819 \times 7000 = 5.733 \times 10^6$ kg ≈ 5733 tonnes. ✓

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow Diagram — Sheet Extrusion Line (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hopper\nResin + Additives] --> B[Extruder Barrel\nHeated + Screwed]
    B --> C[Screen Pack + Breaker Plate\nFilter + Flow Straightener]
    C --> D[Adapter Pipe\nStreamlined Transfer]
    D --> E[Coathanger Die\nFlat Profile Formation]
    E --> F[Three-Roll Cooling Stack\nCalibrate + Polish]
    F --> G[Thickness Gauge\nBeta / X-ray Sensor]
    G --> H[Edge Trimmer\nSlit Edges Off]
    H --> I[Haul-off Pull Rolls\nMaintain Tension]
    I --> J[Flying Shear / Saw\nCut to Length]
    J --> K[Stacking\nFinished Sheets]
    I --> L[Winder\nFor Flexible Rolls]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — PVC Calendering Line (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[PVC Compound\nResin + Plasticizer + Stabilizer] --> P2[Banbury / High-Speed Mixer\nPre-blend at 110 C]
    P2 --> P3[Continuous Mixer / Feed\nHot Feed to Calender]
    P3 --> P4[Roll 1: Top Feed Roll\nFirst Gap Compression]
    P4 --> P5[Roll 2: Middle Roll\nThickness Reduction]
    P5 --> P6[Roll 3: Bottom Roll\nSurface Finish + Gauge]
    P6 --> P7[Cooling Drums\nPost-Calender Cooling]
    P7 --> P8[Edge Trimmer\nCut to Width]
    P8 --> P9[Take-up Roll\nWinding for Flexible Sheet]
    P8 --> P10[Cutting Station\nSlit Rigid Sheets]
```

### 4.3 Coathanger Die Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[Melt Inlet\nFrom Adapter] --> Man[Teardrop Manifold\nReservoir Channel]
    Man --> Land[Die Land\nConstant Cross Section]
    Land --> Lip[Adjustable Die Lip\nLip bolts for Gauge Control]
    Lip --> Out[Sheet Exit\nMelt Profile]

    subgraph Pressure Comp
    Man -. High P near Inlet .-> Land
    Man -. Low P at Edges .-> Land
    end

    style In fill:#FFD700
    style Out fill:#90EE90
    style Pressure Comp fill:#FFE4B5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight from the Diagram:** The teardrop manifold geometry ensures that the **residence time** of the melt is roughly equal whether the material flows through the centre (short path) or the edges (long path). This prevents **degradation hotspots** in heat-sensitive polymers like PVC.

### 4.4 Three-Roll Cooling Stack Schematic (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M[Melt from Die] --> R1[Top Roll\nChrome Plated]
    R1 --> R2[Middle Roll\nAngled Position]
    R2 --> R3[Bottom Roll\nPolishing + Cooling]
    R3 --> Exit[Sheet Exit\nSolidified]
    W1[Chilled Water IN] -.-> R1
    R1 -.-> W1o[Chilled Water OUT]
    W2[Chilled Water IN] -.-> R2
    R2 -.-> W2o[Chilled Water OUT]
    W3[Chilled Water IN] -.-> R3
    R3 -.-> W3o[Chilled Water OUT]
    style M fill:#FF6347
    style Exit fill:#87CEFA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Roll Configuration Logic:** The rolls are **internally water-cooled**. Roll spacing is set by **hydraulic rams**. A **beta-ray or X-ray gauge** continuously measures sheet thickness and feeds back to the **automatic die lip adjustment system** (closed-loop gauge control).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the term "polymer sheet" as per the standard industrial classification. Differentiate clearly between a polymer film, a sheet, and a plate using their thickness ranges.*

**Model Answer:**
A polymer sheet is a flat, continuous polymeric product of uniform thickness, produced by extrusion or calendering. The standard classification by thickness is:
- **Film:** thickness $\le 0.25$ mm
- **Sheet:** thickness $> 0.25$ mm and $\le 12$ mm
- **Plate:** thickness $> 12$ mm

The distinction is significant because **film** is usually flexible and used in packaging, while **sheet** is semi-rigid and used for thermoforming, and **plate** is used in heavy-duty structural applications.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the function of a "breaker plate and screen pack" in a sheet extrusion line.*

**Model Answer:**
A breaker plate is a thick metal plate with many small holes placed at the extruder exit, just before the die. A screen pack (a stack of fine wire meshes) is mounted on the breaker plate.

**Functions:**
1. **Filtration:** Removes foreign particles, unmelted polymer, and carbonized material.
2. **Pressure build-up:** Generates back-pressure to ensure proper mixing and homogenization.
3. **Rotational flow removal:** Converts the helical (Mandel) flow from the rotating screw into **axial (straight-line) flow**, which is essential for uniform sheet thickness.
4. **Stabilizes extrudate:** Prevents surging and pulsation of the melt.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Sheet Extrusion Design

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**(a)** Describe in detail the **coathanger die** used in sheet extrusion. Explain why it is preferred over the older fish-tail die design, with reference to the pressure and velocity distribution along the manifold. (7 marks)

**(b)** A sheet extrusion line operates at the following conditions:
- Sheet width $W = 1.5$ m
- Line speed $v = 6$ m/min
- Sheet thickness $h = 3$ mm
- Material: HDPE, melt density $\rho = 780$ kg/m³

Calculate the **(i)** volumetric output, **(ii)** mass throughput, and **(iii)** annual production in tonnes assuming 6000 working hours per year. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Coathanger Die Explanation (7 marks):**

The coathanger die is a **slit die** with a **teardrop-shaped manifold** that distributes melt across the die width.

**Constructional Features:**
1. A central entry from the adapter.
2. A teardrop manifold (decreasing cross-section) running across the width.
3. A pre-land region (slot) leading to the die exit.
4. Adjustable die lips (flex-lip or rigid-lip) for gauge control.

**Why preferred over fish-tail die:**
- **Fish-tail die:** Uses a long straight tapered manifold. Pressure drop along the manifold is high, so the die exit pressure varies significantly across the width, leading to non-uniform sheet thickness.
- **Coathanger die:** The teardrop manifold cross-section decreases progressively from centre to edges. This intentionally creates a **pressure drop along the manifold** that compensates for the **varying flow resistance** in the pre-land.
- Result: **Uniform pressure and uniform velocity** at the die exit → **uniform sheet thickness** across the full width.

Additional advantages:
- Shorter land length (compact design).
- Lower residence time (less degradation for PVC).
- Better for heat-sensitive polymers.

**Diagram (description):** A drawing showing the die with central inlet, curved teardrop manifold, pre-land, and adjustable lip.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $W = 1.5$ m, $v = 6$ m/min, $h = 3$ mm $= 3 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho = 780$ kg/m³, $t = 6000$ h/yr.

**(i) Volumetric output:**

$$
Q = v \cdot W \cdot h
$$

Convert speed: $v = 6 / 60 = 0.1$ m/s.

$$
Q = 0.1 \times 1.5 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**(ii) Mass throughput:**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 780 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} = 0.351 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**(iii) Annual production:**

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{hour}} = 0.351 \times 3600 = 1263.6 \text{ kg/h}
$$

$$
M_{\text{annual}} = 1263.6 \times 6000 = 7.58 \times 10^6 \text{ kg} = 7581.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> Students commonly lose 2-3 marks on this question by:
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion** of line speed from m/min to m/s (this is the single most common error).
> 2. **Mixing up density units** (using g/cm³ instead of kg/m³). Remember: $1$ g/cm³ $= 1000$ kg/m³.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert mm to m** for thickness in SI.
> 4. **Not writing units** in the final answer — KTU examiner strictly penalises unitless numerical answers.
> 5. In part (a), students often describe the die *construction* but forget to explicitly link it to the **pressure compensation principle**, which is the actual reason it is preferred. Marks are specifically awarded for that link.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Calendering Process

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **calendering process** for the production of polymer sheets. Compare its advantages and limitations with respect to sheet extrusion. (7 marks)

**(b)** Two rolls of a calender have radii $R_1 = R_2 = 0.20$ m, and rotate at angular velocities $\omega_1 = 0.5$ rad/s and $\omega_2 = 0.6$ rad/s respectively. The minimum half-gap is $H_0 = 1.5$ mm and the polymer melt has viscosity $\mu = 400$ Pa·s. Calculate the **(i)** surface speeds of both rolls, **(ii)** friction ratio, and **(iii)** average pressure in the nip region using the simplified Ardichvili equation. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Calendering Process Description (7 marks):**

Calendering is a process in which a polymer compound (usually plasticized PVC) is passed through a series of **heated, counter-rotating rolls** to produce a thin, continuous sheet of highly uniform thickness and excellent surface finish.

**Schematic:** (Three-roll "L" or "inverted L" arrangement.)

**Process Steps:**
1. **Pre-blending:** Resin, plasticizers, stabilizers, fillers, and pigments are mixed in a high-speed (Banbury) mixer at 100–120°C.
2. **Melt feed:** The hot compound is fed continuously (as a strip or rope) into the nip of the first two rolls.
3. **Rolling:** The rolls squeeze the melt. Each successive nip has a **smaller gap** than the previous one, progressively reducing the sheet thickness.
4. **Surface finish:** The final roll (often chrome-plated) imparts the desired surface finish (glossy, matte, or embossed).
5. **Cooling and winding:** The sheet passes over cooling drums and is then wound or cut.

**Comparison with Sheet Extrusion:**

| Aspect | Calendering | Sheet Extrusion |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Output speed | Higher (30–120 m/min) | Lower (0.5–30 m/min) |
| Capital cost | Higher | Lower |
| Material flexibility | Mainly PVC | Wide range |
| Thickness range | Thin (0.05–1.5 mm) | Wider (0.2–12 mm) |
| Surface quality | Superior (mirror finish) | Good |
| Energy | Less (no high melt temp) | More (high melt temp) |
| Maintenance | High (roll change) | Low (die change) |

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:** $R_1 = R_2 = 0.20$ m, $\omega_1 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\omega_2 = 0.6$ rad/s, $H_0 = 1.5$ mm, $\mu = 400$ Pa·s.

**(i) Surface speeds:**

$$
v_1 = R_1 \omega_1 = 0.20 \times 0.5 = 0.10 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
v_2 = R_2 \omega_2 = 0.20 \times 0.6 = 0.12 \text{ m/s}
$$

**(ii) Friction ratio:**

$$
f = \frac{v_2}{v_1} = \frac{0.12}{0.10} = 1.20
$$

(Indicates roll 2 is 20% faster than roll 1 — used for shearing and orientation control.)

**(iii) Average pressure (simplified Ardichvili, with $v \approx (v_1 + v_2)/2$):**

$$
v = \frac{0.10 + 0.12}{2} = 0.11 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3 \mu v W}{4 H_0^2}
$$

Assuming $W = 1.0$ m (roll width):

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3 \times 400 \times 0.11 \times 1.0}{4 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}
$$

$$
p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{132}{4 \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{132}{9 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.467 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \approx 14.67 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Calendering Problems:**
> 1. **Friction ratio confusion:** Many students calculate $f = v_1/v_2$ instead of $v_2/v_1$ — this is a definition check, examiner is strict.
> 2. **Using roll diameter instead of radius:** $v = R \omega$, not $D \omega$.
> 3. **Ardichvili's equation only valid for Newtonian melt** — for highly shear-thinning polymers (real PVC), the result is only a **first-order estimate**. Always mention this caveat in the answer.
> 4. **Not converting mm to m** for $H_0$.
> 5. **Missing roll width $W$:** Some simplified derivations assume unit width; the actual formula requires $W$. If the question does not specify $W$, assume $W = 1$ m and state it.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic: Sheet**

- **Sheet = thickness 0.25 mm to 12 mm** (Film < 0.25 mm, Plate > 12 mm).
- **Sheet Extrusion** uses a **coathanger die**; **Calendering** uses counter-rotating **heated rolls**.
- **Coathanger die** compensates for pressure drop across width by progressively reducing manifold cross-section → **uniform thickness**.
- **Breaker plate + screen pack** filters melt and removes **rotational (Mandel) flow**.
- **Three-roll cooling stack** provides both cooling and surface finish (gloss, matte, embossed).
- **Output rate:** $Q = v W h$; **Mass rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ — must convert units carefully.
- **Calender friction ratio:** $f = v_2 / v_1$; values typically 1.0 to 1.5.
- **Ardichvili pressure:** $p_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{3 \mu v W}{4 H_0^2}$ — valid for **Newtonian melt, frictionless nip**.
- **Die swell** must be compensated (typically $1.0$ to $1.5$) in die design for target final sheet thickness.
- **Gauge control** uses **beta-ray or X-ray sensors** with closed-loop die-lip adjustment.
- **Sheet extrusion** suits most thermoplastics; **calendering** is dominant for **PVC sheets** (rigid and flexible).
- **Applications to remember for theory questions:** Packaging, automotive (GMT), construction (PC roofing), medical (PVC sheets for IV bags), agriculture (PE films).
- **Key products made from sheets (secondary process):** Thermoformed cups, trays, blister packs, signage, display panels.
- **Design parameters to mention in 14-mark answers:** Material selection, die design, cooling capacity, haul-off tension, gauge control system, and application requirements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PU Foam
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PU Foam

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Polyurethane (PU) Foam** is a cellular polymeric material produced by the simultaneous occurrence of two competing reactions — the **polyaddition (gelling) reaction** between a polyol and a polyisocyanate, and the **blowing reaction** between isocyanate and water that liberates carbon dioxide gas. The liberated gas is trapped within the viscous, reacting polymer mass, generating a closed-cell or open-cell porous structure which subsequently solidifies into a lightweight, cellular thermoset product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPOT601 – Module 3):**
> *PU foam is a polymeric cellular product obtained by the reaction of polyols (polyether or polyester based) with diisocyanates (TDI / MDI) in the presence of catalysts, surfactants, blowing agents, and additives, producing flexible, semi-rigid, or rigid cellular products depending on formulation.*

## 1.2 Classification of PU Foams

PU foams are classified by ASTM D3574 / D1056 into four broad categories:

| Type | Cell Nature | Density Range | Typical Application |
|------|------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Flexible Foam** | Mostly open cell | 20–80 kg/m³ | Mattresses, cushioning, automotive seats |
| **Semi-Rigid Foam** | Mixed open/closed | 50–150 kg/m³ | Crash pads, packaging, armrests |
| **Rigid Foam** | Mostly closed cell | 30–200 kg/m³ | Thermal insulation (refrigerators, buildings) |
| **Integral Skin Foam** | Dense skin + cellular core | 200–600 kg/m³ | Steering wheels, shoe soles, dashboards |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy – "The Rising Bread":**
> Think of PU foam formation like baking bread. The **polyol + isocyanate** mixture is your dough (the polymer network). The **water blowing agent** is like the yeast — it generates **CO₂ gas** inside the dough as it reacts, making the mass puff up and rise. The **catalyst** controls how fast the dough rises and how fast it sets. **Surfactants** are like the gluten that stabilizes the bubbles, preventing them from collapsing. Once the dough "sets" (the polymer cross-links), you get a permanent, spongy, light cellular solid — the foam.
>
> - A **soft bread** with big, connected holes → **Flexible foam (open cell)**
> - A **dense cake** with tiny, trapped bubbles → **Rigid foam (closed cell)**

## 1.4 Standard Physical Metrics

- **Density (ρ)** expressed in **kg/m³** (ASTM D3574, Test A)
- **Indentation Load Deflection (ILD)** in **N/3230 mm²** (ASTM D3574, Test B)
- **Compression Set (CS)** expressed in **%** (ASTM D3574, Test D)
- **Tensile Strength** in **kPa** (ASTM D3574, Test E)
- **Elongation at Break** in **%** (ASTM D3574, Test E)
- **Thermal Conductivity (k-value)** for rigid foam in **W/m·K** (ASTM C518)
- **Cell Count** in **cells per cm** (ASTM D3576)

## 1.5 Raw Material Components (5-Functional Cocktail)

1. **Polyol** – polyether or polyester backbone (200–6000 g/mol OH number).
2. **Isocyanate** – aromatic (TDI, MDI, polymeric MDI) or aliphatic (HDI, IPDI).
3. **Catalyst** – tertiary amine (e.g., DABCO) + organotin (e.g., stannous octoate).
4. **Surfactant** – silicone polyether copolymer (e.g., Tegostab, Dow Corning 193).
5. **Blowing agent** – water (chemical) and/or physical blowing agents (HCFC, HFC, pentane, CO₂).
6. **Additives** – flame retardants, fillers, colorants, antioxidants, biocides.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Competing Reactions (Core Chemistry)

The chemistry of PU foam formation rests on the dual reactivity of the isocyanate group **(–N=C=O)**, which has two electrophilic centers — the **carbon** and the **nitrogen**. This drives two simultaneous reactions:

### Reaction 1 – The Gelling Reaction (Polyaddition / Chain Extension)

$$\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'} \quad \text{(Urethane linkage)}$$

- Forms the **urethane linkage (–NH–COO–)**.
- Generates polymer chain extension and cross-linking.
- Builds molecular weight and structural strength.
- This is an **exothermic** reaction: ΔH ≈ **–100 kJ/mol** of NCO.

### Reaction 2 – The Blowing Reaction (Gas Generation)

$$\text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow [\text{R–NH–COOH}] \longrightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

- The unstable **carbamic acid** intermediate decomposes spontaneously to a **primary amine** + **CO₂ gas**.
- The CO₂ physically expands the reacting polymer mass → foam rise.
- The amine formed is highly reactive and quickly reacts with another isocyanate to form a **urea linkage**:

$$\text{R–NH}_2 + \text{R'–NCO} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–CO–NH–R'} \quad \text{(Urea linkage)}$$

- The urea can further react with isocyanate to form **biuret** (trifunctional branch point).
- This is an **exothermic** reaction: ΔH ≈ **–196 kJ/mol** of water reacted.

### Reaction 3 – Allophanate / Biuret Branching (Cross-linking)

When isocyanate reacts with an existing urethane or urea group:

$$\text{Urethane + NCO} \longrightarrow \text{Allophanate (cross-link)}$$

$$\text{Urea + NCO} \longrightarrow \text{Biuret (cross-link)}$$

These secondary reactions are responsible for **cross-linking density**, which dictates rigidity, hardness, and high-temperature performance.

## 2.2 Reaction Balance: Gel vs. Blow — The Foam Window

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Foam Window"** is the time interval during which the **blowing reaction** completes gas generation *before* the **gelling reaction** polymer network solidifies.
>
> - If **blow > gel**: Foam collapses (gas escapes before set).
> - If **gel > blow**: Foam shrinks / splits (polymer sets before sufficient gas).
> - If **blow ≈ gel**: Stable, fine-celled foam — the engineer's goal.

**Three critical process times in PU foam manufacturing:**

| Time | Definition | Tool that Controls It |
|------|------------|----------------------|
| **Cream Time** | Mix turns creamy/white, bubble nucleation starts | Amine catalyst (blow catalyst) |
| **Rise Time** | Foam reaches maximum height | Combined catalyst system |
| **Tack-Free Time** | Surface becomes non-sticky, polymer solidified | Tin catalyst (gel catalyst) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
|-------------------|------------|-------|------------------|
| **Isocyanate Index (I)** | $I = \dfrac{\text{Actual NCO moles used}}{\text{Stoichiometric NCO moles required}} \times 100$ | dimensionless (%) | Determines cross-link density; I=100 stoichiometric, I>100 over-indexed |
| **% NCO Content (Dibutylamine method)** | $\% \text{NCO} = \dfrac{4.2 \times V \times N}{W}$ | % by mass | Quality control of isocyanate (TDI=48.2%, MDI=33.6%) |
| **Hydroxyl Number (OH#)** | $\text{OH\#} = \dfrac{56.1 \times V \times N}{W}$ | mg KOH/g sample | Characterizes polyol reactivity |
| **Equivalent Weight (Eq. Wt.)** | $E = \dfrac{56100}{\text{OH\#}}$ for polyol; $E = \dfrac{4200}{\%\text{NCO}}$ for isocyanate | g/eq | Stoichiometric calculations |
| **Foam Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | kg/m³ | Determines mechanical & insulation behavior |
| **Theoretical Foam Rise** | $\dfrac{V_{\text{foam}}}{V_{\text{liquid}}} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liquid}}}{\rho_{\text{foam}}} \times \dfrac{1}{1-\text{fraction lost}}$ | ratio | Predicts expansion ratio |
| **CO₂ Volume per gram water** | $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{n_{CO_2} \times RT}{P} = \dfrac{22.4 \times 298}{273} \approx 24.5\ \text{L/mol}$ at STP | L/g water | Calculates theoretical foam expansion |
| **Thermal Conductivity (Rigid foam)** | $k \approx 0.020 \text{ to } 0.030$ W/m·K | W/m·K | Insulation design |
| **Cell Count** | $n_c = \dfrac{1}{L_{\text{cell}}}$ | cells/cm | Quantifies cell fineness (ASTM D3576) |
| **Compression Set** | $\%CS = \dfrac{t_0 - t_f}{t_0} \times 100$ | % | Resilience after compression |
| **ILD (Indentation Load Deflection)** | Force required to indent 25% of original thickness | N / 3230 mm² | Cushioning firmness |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used in PU Calculations:**
> - Molar volume of ideal gas at STP = **22.4 L/mol**
> - Equivalent weight of KOH = **56.1 g/eq**
> - Equivalent weight of NCO group = **42.0 g/eq**
> - ΔH gelling = **–100 kJ/mol NCO**
> - ΔH blowing = **–196 kJ/mol water**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Construction Industry:** Rigid PU foam sprayed or panel-foamed for roof & wall insulation (thermal conductivity ≈ 0.022 W/m·K, among the lowest of all insulation materials).
- **Automotive Sector:** Flexible molded PU foam for seats, headrests, armrests; semi-rigid foam for steering wheels (integral skin).
- **Furniture & Bedding:** Slabstock hot-cure (TDI-based) flexible foam for mattresses and sofas.
- **Refrigeration:** Rigid pour-in-place (PU/PIR) foam as the insulating core of refrigerators and cold-storage warehouses.
- **Footwear:** Microcellular PU elastomer (Integral skin) for shoe soles — high resilience and abrasion resistance.
- **Aerospace / Defense:** High-density PU foam for crash energy absorption (EPP/EPU variants).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Isocyanate Index Calculation

**Problem:** A flexible PU foam formulation uses **100 g of a polyol with OH# = 56 mg KOH/g** and is reacted with **TDI (80/20) of %NCO = 48.2%** at an isocyanate index of **110**. Find the grams of TDI required.

### Step 1 — Compute the Equivalent Weight of the Polyol

The hydroxyl number (OH#) is the milligrams of KOH equivalent to the hydroxyl groups in 1 gram of polyol. The equivalent weight E (in g/eq) of the polyol is:

$$E_{\text{polyol}} = \frac{56.1 \times 1000}{\text{OH\#}} = \frac{56100}{56} = 1001.79\ \text{g/eq}$$

**Textual Logic:** Because OH# = 56 means 56 mg KOH per gram polyol, we invert the proportionality using the molar mass of KOH (56.1 g/mol).

### Step 2 — Compute the Number of Equivalents in 100 g Polyol

$$\text{Eq}_{\text{polyol}} = \frac{m_{\text{polyol}}}{E_{\text{polyol}}} = \frac{100}{1001.79} = 0.0998\ \text{eq}$$

**Textual Logic:** Each equivalent of polyol consumes one equivalent of NCO. So polyol contributes 0.0998 equivalents of reactive OH groups.

### Step 3 — Adjust for Isocyanate Index (I = 110)

The index defines over-stoichiometric addition:

$$\text{Eq}_{\text{NCO required}} = \text{Eq}_{\text{polyol}} \times \frac{I}{100} = 0.0998 \times 1.10 = 0.1098\ \text{eq}$$

**Textual Logic:** Index 110 means 110 equivalents of NCO are used for every 100 equivalents of OH — a 10% over-index.

### Step 4 — Compute Equivalent Weight of TDI

For the isocyanate, the equivalent weight is given by:
