$$E_{\text{TDI}} = \frac{4200}{\%\text{NCO}} = \frac{4200}{48.2} = 87.14\ \text{g/eq}$$

### Step 5 — Compute Mass of TDI Required

$$m_{\text{TDI}} = \text{Eq}_{\text{NCO required}} \times E_{\text{TDI}} = 0.1098 \times 87.14 = 9.57\ \text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** To produce the formulation, **9.57 g of TDI** is required per 100 g polyol.

### Result Summary Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Value | Units |
|-----------|--------|-------|-------|
| Polyol OH Number | OH# | 56 | mg KOH/g |
| Polyol Equivalent Weight | E_polyol | 1001.79 | g/eq |
| Polyol Equivalents | Eq_polyol | 0.0998 | eq |
| Isocyanate Index | I | 110 | — |
| TDI %NCO | — | 48.2 | % |
| TDI Equivalent Weight | E_TDI | 87.14 | g/eq |
| **TDI Mass Required** | **m_TDI** | **9.57** | **g** |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Foam Density from Blowing Gas Volume

**Problem:** A PU foam formulation has a total liquid mass of **200 g** containing **3 g water**. The reaction goes to completion and all CO₂ is retained. The total polymer volume is **250 cm³**. Find the theoretical foam density.

### Step 1 — Moles of Water and Moles of CO₂ Generated

Water reacts with isocyanate: 1 mol H₂O → 1 mol CO₂ (from stoichiometry).

$$n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = \frac{m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}}{M_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} = \frac{3}{18} = 0.1667\ \text{mol}$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = n_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = 0.1667\ \text{mol}$$

### Step 2 — Volume of CO₂ at Process Temperature (assumed 298 K, 1 atm)

$$V_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{nRT}{P} = \frac{0.1667 \times 0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 4.077\ \text{L} = 4077\ \text{cm}^3$$

### Step 3 — Total Foam Volume

$$V_{\text{foam}} = V_{\text{polymer}} + V_{\text{CO}_2} = 250 + 4077 = 4327\ \text{cm}^3$$

### Step 4 — Foam Density

$$\rho_{\text{foam}} = \frac{m_{\text{total}}}{V_{\text{foam}}} = \frac{200}{4327} = 0.0462\ \text{g/cm}^3 = 46.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Final Answer:** Theoretical foam density ≈ **46.2 kg/m³** (typical of a medium-density flexible foam).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Note:** Real foam density is 5–15% higher than theoretical because:
> - CO₂ diffuses out partially through cell walls before polymer sets.
> - Excess heat causes some gas expansion losses.
> - Cell window drainage in flexible foam reduces open-cell voids.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Water Requirement for Target Density

**Problem:** You want a rigid PU foam of density **40 kg/m³** from a 200 g liquid charge (polymer + isocyanate). Assume all other parameters identical to Derivation 2. Determine the required water content.

### Step 1 — Required Foam Volume

$$V_{\text{foam}} = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{200\ \text{g}}{0.040\ \text{g/cm}^3} = 5000\ \text{cm}^3$$

### Step 2 — Required CO₂ Volume (assuming polymer occupies 250 cm³ as before)

$$V_{\text{CO}_2} = 5000 - 250 = 4750\ \text{cm}^3 = 4.75\ \text{L}$$

### Step 3 — Moles of CO₂ Required

$$n_{\text{CO}_2} = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{1 \times 4.75}{0.0821 \times 298} = 0.1941\ \text{mol}$$

### Step 4 — Mass of Water (1:1 mol ratio)

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}} = n \times M = 0.1941 \times 18 = 3.49\ \text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** Required water = **3.49 g** to achieve 40 kg/m³ density. Higher water → more gas → lower density → softer foam.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Formulation Variables

| Parameter ↑ | Cream Time | Rise Time | Density | Hardness (ILD) | Cell Size | Thermal Conductivity |
|-------------|-----------|-----------|---------|----------------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Water content** | ↑ slower | ↑ slower | ↓ decreases | ↑ increases | larger | — |
| **Amine catalyst** | ↓ faster | ↓ faster | slight ↓ | — | finer | — |
| **Tin catalyst** | — | ↓ faster | slight ↑ | ↑ increases | — | — |
| **Surfactant** | — | stabilizes | stabilizes | — | finer cells | improves (rigid) |
| **Isocyanate Index** | — | ↓ faster | ↑ increases | ↑ increases | finer | improves (rigid) |
| **Polyol OH# (functionality)** | ↓ faster | ↓ faster | ↑ increases | ↑ increases | finer | improves |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PU Foam Formation — Reaction Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Storage] --> B[Polyol Tank]
    A --> C[Isocyanate Tank]
    A --> D[Catalyst Surfactant Blowing Agent Mix Tank]
    B --> E[High Pressure Metering Pump]
    C --> F[High Pressure Metering Pump]
    D --> G[High Pressure Metering Pump]
    E --> H[Mixing Head Impingement]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Reacting Liquid Mix]
    I --> J[Reaction 1 Gelling - Polyol plus NCO - Urethane Linkage]
    I --> K[Reaction 2 Blowing - NCO plus Water - Amine plus CO2 Gas]
    K --> L[CO2 Gas Bubbles Nucleate and Grow]
    J --> M[Polymer Network Crosslinks and Sets]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Stable Cellular Solid PU Foam]
    N --> O[Demold after Tack Free Time]
    O --> P[Curing Oven 70 to 100 degrees Celsius 24 hours]
    P --> Q[Finished PU Foam Product]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PU Foam Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUPPLY[Raw Material Supply Section]
        R1[Polyol Resin Day Tank]
        R2[Isocyanate Day Tank]
        R3[Additive Mix Tank Water Catalyst Surfactant Pigment]
    end

    subgraph METER[Metering and Mixing Section]
        M1[Polyol Metering Pump]
        M2[Isocyanate Metering Pump]
        M3[Additive Metering Pump]
        MIX[High Pressure Mixing Head]
    end

    subgraph REACT[Reaction and Foaming Section]
        RF[Conveyor or Mold Moving System]
        RE[Exothermic Reaction Zone]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post Processing Section]
        C1[Curing Oven or Room]
        C2[Cutter and Trimming Station]
        C3[QC Testing Density ILD CS]
        C4[Packaging and Dispatch]
    end

    R1 --> M1 --> MIX
    R2 --> M2 --> MIX
    R3 --> M3 --> MIX
    MIX --> RF --> RE --> C1 --> C2 --> C3 --> C4
```

## 4.3 Cell Structure Comparison — Open vs Closed Cell

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN[Open Cell Foam - Flexible]
        OC1[Cell 1 - Open Pore]
        OC2[Cell 2 - Open Pore]
        OC3[Cell 3 - Open Pore]
        OC1 -.shared walls.-> OC2
        OC2 -.shared walls.-> OC3
    end

    subgraph CLOSED[Closed Cell Foam - Rigid]
        CC1[Cell A - Sealed]
        CC2[Cell B - Sealed]
        CC3[Cell C - Sealed]
        CC1 === CC2
        CC2 === CC3
    end
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Selecting PU Foam Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start - Choose PU Foam Type] --> Q1{Application}
    Q1 -->|Cushioning Seating Mattress| FLEX[Flexible Open Cell Foam]
    Q1 -->|Thermal Insulation Refrigerator Panel| RIG[Rigid Closed Cell Foam]
    Q1 -->|Crash Pad Steering Wheel Armrest| SEMI[Semi Rigid Integral Skin]
    Q1 -->|Shoe Sole Gasket Roller| MIC[Microcellular Elastomer]
    FLEX --> F1[Use TDI 80 by 20 plus Polyether Polyol OH 28 to 56]
    RIG --> R1[Use Polymeric MDI plus High Functionality Polyester or Polyether OH 350 to 600]
    SEMI --> S1[Use MDI plus Polyether Polyol plus Isocyanate Index 105 to 115]
    MIC --> M1[Use MDI plus High MW Polyester or Polyether plus Chain Extenders]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**Q: Define Polyurethane foam. List the four major categories with one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

PU foam is a cellular thermoset polymer produced by the simultaneous polymerization of polyols with diisocyanates and the in-situ generation of CO₂ blowing gas from the isocyanate–water reaction.

| Category | Typical Application |
|----------|---------------------|
| Flexible Foam | Mattresses / sofa cushions |
| Rigid Foam | Refrigerator insulation |
| Semi-Rigid Foam | Automotive crash pads |
| Microcellular / Integral Skin | Shoe soles / steering wheels |

---

### **Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Q: Differentiate between the gelling reaction and the blowing reaction in PU foam formation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Gelling Reaction | Blowing Reaction |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| Reagents | Polyol + NCO | Water + NCO |
| Product | Urethane linkage (–NHCOO–) | Amine + CO₂ gas |
| Role | Builds polymer chain / cross-links | Generates foaming gas |
| ΔH | –100 kJ/mol NCO | –196 kJ/mol water |
| Catalyst | Tin (Sn) | Tertiary amine |
| Effect on foam | Strength / rigidity | Cell expansion / density |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question B-1(a) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q (7 Marks):** *(a)* Explain with a neat schematic the **chemistry of polyurethane foam formation**, discussing the role of polyols, isocyanates, water, catalyst, surfactant, and the two competing reactions.

**Model Answer:**

The chemistry of PU foam formation is governed by the dual reactivity of the isocyanate group (–N=C=O), which possesses electrophilic centers at both the carbon and the nitrogen atom.

**Step 1 — Raw Materials and Their Roles:**
- **Polyol:** Provides active hydrogen (–OH) sites; determines flexibility, hydrolytic stability, and final foam properties.
- **Isocyanate:** Acts as the co-monomer; supplies –NCO groups. TDI is used for flexible foams, polymeric MDI for rigid foams.
- **Water:** Acts as the chemical blowing agent — generates CO₂ in-situ.
- **Catalyst (Amine + Tin):** Amine accelerates the blow reaction; tin accelerates the gel reaction. Their balance sets the foam window.
- **Surfactant (Silicone):** Lowers surface tension, stabilizes cell walls against coalescence and drainage.

**Step 2 — Reaction 1 — Gelling (Polyaddition):**

$$\text{R–NCO} + \text{HO–R'} \longrightarrow \text{R–NH–COO–R'}$$

This urethane linkage extends the polymer chain, builds molecular weight, and eventually sets the polymer into a solid network.

**Step 3 — Reaction 2 — Blowing (Gas Generation):**

$$\text{R–NCO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow [\text{R–NHCOOH}] \longrightarrow \text{R–NH}_2 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow$$

The CO₂ generated is dispersed as bubbles, expanding the reacting polymer mass. The amine formed immediately reacts with another isocyanate to form a urea linkage.

**Step 4 — Secondary Cross-linking:**
Urea and urethane groups react further with NCO to form biuret and allophanate branches, increasing cross-link density and rigidity.

**Step 5 — Process Sequence:**
1. Mixing of all components in high-pressure mixing head.
2. Cream time — nucleation of CO₂ bubbles.
3. Rise time — bubble growth and polymer thickening.
4. Tack-free time — polymer solidifies.
5. Demold, post-cure at 70–100 °C for 24 h.

> *Schematic is provided in Section 4.1 of these notes.*

---

### **Question B-1(b) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q (7 Marks):** *(b)* A flexible PU foam is to be produced using **polyol with OH# = 200 mg KOH/g (200 g)** and **polymeric MDI with %NCO = 31.5%**. The desired isocyanate index is **105**. Calculate the **mass of MDI required**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent weight of polyol:**

$$E_{\text{polyol}} = \frac{56100}{\text{OH\#}} = \frac{56100}{200} = 280.5\ \text{g/eq}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalents of polyol in 200 g:**

$$\text{Eq}_{\text{polyol}} = \frac{200}{280.5} = 0.7130\ \text{eq}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalents of NCO required at I = 105:**

$$\text{Eq}_{\text{NCO}} = 0.7130 \times 1.05 = 0.7487\ \text{eq}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent weight of MDI:**

$$E_{\text{MDI}} = \frac{4200}{31.5} = 133.33\ \text{g/eq}$$

**Step 5 — Mass of MDI required:**

$$m_{\text{MDI}} = 0.7487 \times 133.33 = 99.82\ \text{g}$$

**Final Answer:** **Mass of MDI = 99.82 g** per 200 g polyol.

| Parameter | Value | Units |
|-----------|-------|-------|
| OH# of polyol | 200 | mg KOH/g |
| Eq. Wt. of polyol | 280.5 | g/eq |
| Equivalents polyol | 0.7130 | eq |
| Index | 105 | — |
| Eq. Wt. of MDI | 133.33 | g/eq |
| **MDI Mass Required** | **99.82** | **g** |

---

### **Question B-2 (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q (14 Marks):** Discuss in detail the **classification, manufacturing methods, properties, and applications of polyurethane foams**.

**Model Answer Outline:**

**(a) Classification (7 marks):** Cover flexible, semi-rigid, rigid, microcellular/integral skin with cell structure, density, polymer matrix type, and representative applications (refer to Section 1.2).

**(b) Manufacturing & Properties (7 marks):** Discuss slabstock (continuous block), molding (hot-cure and cold-cure), spray foam, and pour-in-place processes. Cover key properties — density (ASTM D3574-A), ILD (ASTM D3574-B), compression set (ASTM D3574-D), tensile strength & elongation (D3574-E), tear strength, resilience (ball rebound), thermal conductivity (C518), closed/open cell content, and aging behavior (humid aging, fatigue). End with applications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Deduction Mistakes (Based on KTU Valuation Patterns):**
>
> 1. **Wrong unit for OH#:** OH# is in **mg KOH/g**, NOT g KOH/g. Using it directly without the 56.1 factor loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Isocyanate Index:** Always divide by 100 and multiply by I/100. Many students skip the index and assume stoichiometry.
> 3. **Mixing up gelling & blowing catalysts:** Amine catalyst = blow; Tin catalyst = gel. Reversing them is a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Confusing ΔH values:** Gelling ΔH ≈ –100 kJ/mol; Blowing ΔH ≈ –196 kJ/mol. The blowing reaction is more exothermic.
> 5. **ΔNCO vs. %NCO:** ΔNCO is the **excess** NCO after accounting for water + polyol. %NCO is the **concentration** of NCO in the isocyanate — these are not the same.
> 6. **No diagrams:** A neat reaction schematic (as in Section 4.1) is mandatory for full marks on chemistry questions. Lack of diagram = 1–2 marks deduction.
> 7. **Cell structure confusion:** Flexible = open cell; Rigid = closed cell. Reversing them is an immediate red flag for the examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist for PU Foam:**
>
> - **Definition:** Cellular thermoset polymer from polyol + isocyanate + CO₂ blowing.
> - **Two Reactions:** Gelling (–OH + NCO → urethane) and Blowing (H₂O + NCO → urea + CO₂).
> - **Catalyst Pair:** Amine (blow) + Tin (gel) — their ratio controls foam rise & set.
> - **Surfactant Role:** Stabilizes cell windows; silicone polyether type.
> - **Isocyanate Index (I):** Always state it; I = 100 means stoichiometric; flexible foams typically use I = 105–115.
> - **Key Equations:**
>   - $E_{\text{polyol}} = 56100 / \text{OH\#}$
>   - $E_{\text{iso}} = 4200 / \%\text{NCO}$
>   - $\text{moles of NCO needed} = (\text{Eq polyol}) \times I/100$
>   - $\rho_{\text{foam}} = m_{\text{liquid}} / V_{\text{foam}}$
> - **Cell Structure Rule:** Open-cell → flexible → cushioning; Closed-cell → rigid → insulation.
> - **Water Content Rule:** ↑ water → ↓ density, ↑ hardness (more urea linkages).
> - **Density Range:** Flexible 20–80 kg/m³; Rigid 30–200 kg/m³; Integral skin 200–600 kg/m³.
> - **Standard Tests:** ASTM D3574 (flexible), ASTM D1621 / C518 (rigid).
> - **Applications Snapshot:** Mattresses, car seats, refrigerators, building insulation, shoe soles, steering wheels.
> - **Numerical Problems Always Include:** (i) Equivalent weight, (ii) Equivalents calculation, (iii) Index adjustment, (iv) Mass of isocyanate, (v) Density check.
> - **Foam Window:** Blow ≈ Gel in time — never blow ≫ gel (collapse) or gel ≫ blow (shrink).
> - **Critical ΔH Values to Memorize:** Gelling = –100 kJ/mol NCO; Blowing = –196 kJ/mol H₂O.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plastic Optical Goods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Plastic Optical Goods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Plastic Optical Goods** are precision-engineered polymer components and products designed to transmit, reflect, refract, focus, or guide light for functional or aesthetic applications. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (PCPOT601), they encompass a class of polymer products in which the **optical performance**—defined by transparency, refractive index, dispersion, transmittance, and birefringence—is the primary functional requirement rather than a secondary attribute. This category includes ophthalmic lenses, safety eyewear, instrument covers, Fresnel lenses, light guides, optical fibres (POF), display panels, prisms, mirrors, and decorative transparent components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCPOT601 – Module 3):**
> Plastic optical goods are polymeric products manufactured from transparent/semi-transparent plastics (chiefly **PMMA, Polycarbonate, CR-39 allyl diglycol carbonate, Polystyrene, SAN, and Cyclic Olefin Copolymers (COC)**) where the polymer's interaction with the visible/UV/IR spectrum governs the product's market value and engineering performance.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Polymer Window for Light":**
> Imagine ordinary glass as a "rigid, heavy window" through which light enters. Now replace that heavy glass with a **lightweight, shatter-resistant polymer pane**—this is the conceptual leap behind plastic optical goods. Just as a clear lake allows you to see fish inside because the water transmits light faithfully, optical-grade polymers are chosen because their molecular structure (amorphous, low crystallinity, no internal scattering centres) allows photons to pass through with minimal distortion. The polymer must be:
> - **Optically homogeneous** (no crystalline regions that scatter light)
> - **Dimensionally stable** (no shrinkage that warps the lens curvature)
> - **Aged-resistant** (UV, abrasion, and chemicals must not yellow or craze it)

Think of a polymer chain like a tangled bowl of transparent spaghetti (amorphous regions). If the strands align into ordered bundles (crystalline regions), they form microscopic "frosted patches" that scatter light—reducing clarity. Therefore, **most optical plastics are designed to be highly amorphous** with refractive indices carefully tuned by copolymerization or additive blending.

### 1.3 Why Plastics over Glass for Optics? — Key Justifications

| Property | Plastic Advantage over Glass | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Density** | Plastics ~1.0–1.2 g/cm³ vs glass ~2.5 g/cm³ | Lighter spectacle lenses, aircraft canopies |
| **Fracture toughness** | High impact resistance (especially PC) | Safety eyewear, riot shields |
| **Mouldability** | Complex shapes in single mould cycle | Aspheric & Fresnel lenses at low cost |
| **Tintability** | Dye diffused into bulk polymer | Fashion sunglasses, photochromic lenses |
| **Cost** | Injection moulding vs grinding/polishing | Mass-market ophthalmics |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Reality:** Plastics have lower scratch resistance, lower thermal stability, and slightly lower refractive indices than crown glass. Every design must balance these against the mass-production benefits.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout This Topic (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Refractive Index (n)** — dimensionless ratio of light velocity in vacuum to that in the medium
- **Abbe Number (V)** — dimensionless measure of optical dispersion (chromatic aberration tendency)
- **Luminous Transmittance (τᵥ)** — % of visible light passing through the part
- **Haze (%)** — % of transmitted light scattered > 2.5° from the incident beam
- **Yellowness Index (YI)** — ASTM E313 measure of colour degradation
- **Birefringence (Δn)** — difference in refractive index along orthogonal stress directions

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The Six Pillars of Optical Polymer Performance

A plastic optical product must satisfy **six simultaneous engineering requirements**. Each is governed by a measurable physical principle:

#### Pillar 1: Transparency (Molecular-Level Origin)
- **Why:** Optical clarity requires suppression of all light-scattering centres (crystallites, voids, fillers, phase-separated domains).
- **How achieved:**
  - Use **amorphous polymers** (PMMA, PS, PC, COC) — random coil conformation prevents crystallite formation.
  - Avoid inorganic fillers; use only optical-grade masterbatches.
  - Maintain **mould cleanliness** to prevent contaminant haze.

#### Pillar 2: Refractive Index Control
- **Why:** Determines the bending of light at a polymer–air interface; sets lens focal length.
- **How achieved:**
  - **Chemical structure manipulation:** Aromatic rings ↑ n; aliphatic chains ↓ n.
  - **Halogenation** (Cl, Br, S in backbone) increases n significantly.
  - Typical n values: **PMMA ≈ 1.49, PC ≈ 1.58, PS ≈ 1.59, CR-39 ≈ 1.50, Polyester allyl ≈ 1.55, COC ≈ 1.53**.

#### Pillar 3: Dispersion Control (Abbe Number)
- **Why:** Polychromatic light (white light) splits into colours when n varies with wavelength → chromatic aberration.
- **How achieved:**
  - High Abbe number → low dispersion → less colour fringing.
  - **PMMA: V ≈ 57** (excellent); **PC: V ≈ 30** (worst); **PS: V ≈ 31**; **COC: V ≈ 56**.

#### Pillar 4: Mechanical & Thermal Stability
- **Why:** Lenses must retain curvature under load, thermal cycling, and UV exposure.
- **How achieved:**
  - PC for impact; PMMA for UV stability (better than PC); CR-39 for chemical/scratch resistance.
  - Heat deflection temperature (HDT) and glass transition temperature (T_g) set the upper use limit.

#### Pillar 5: Surface Quality
- **Why:** Surface scratches, sink marks, weld lines, and jetting create light scatter.
- **How achieved:**
  - **Diamond-turning** or **hard-coating** (organosilicate, acrylic, or vacuum-deposited SiO₂).
  - Polish grades: SPI A1/A2/A3 standard.
  - Anti-reflection coatings via sputtering or dip coating.

#### Pillar 6: Weatherability & Aging
- **Why:** UV exposure yellows polymers (especially PC and PS); hydrolysis crazes PMMA.
- **How achieved:**
  - **UV stabilizers** (benzotriazoles, HALS).
  - **Abrasion-resistant coatings** for ophthalmic PC lenses.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Definitions | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$n = \dfrac{c}{v} = \dfrac{\sin\theta_i}{\sin\theta_r}$$ | c = speed of light in vacuum; v = speed in medium; θᵢ = incidence angle; θᵣ = refraction angle | **Snell's Law / Refractive Index** — base equation for lens design |
| 2 | $$P = \dfrac{1}{f} = (n-1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right]$$ | P = lens power (dioptres); f = focal length (m); n = refractive index; R₁, R₂ = radii of curvature (m) | **Lensmaker's Equation** — spectacle & instrument lens design |
| 3 | $$V = \dfrac{n_D - 1}{n_F - n_C}$$ | n_D = index at 589.3 nm (sodium D-line); n_F = index at 486.1 nm; n_C = index at 656.3 nm | **Abbe Number** — chromatic dispersion rating |
| 4 | $$\tau = \dfrac{I_{transmitted}}{I_{incident}} \times 100\%$$ | I = luminous intensity (cd) | **Luminous Transmittance** |
| 5 | $$\text{Haze}(\%) = \dfrac{\text{Diffuse Transmittance}}{\text{Total Transmittance}} \times 100$$ | measured per ASTM D1003 | **Optical clarity / scatter** |
| 6 | $$\Delta n = n_\parallel - n_\perp$$ | Δn = birefringence; n∥, n⊥ = principal refractive indices | **Stress / flow birefringence** |
| 7 | $$YI = \dfrac{100(1.28X - 1.06Z)}{Y}$$ | X, Y, Z = CIE tristimulus values (ASTM E313) | **Yellowness Index** |
| 8 | $$\eta_{rel} = \dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2}$$ | η_rel = relative permittivity contribution from n | **Lorentz-Lorenz relation** (polarizability linkage) |
| 9 | $$\text{OTF}(f) = \dfrac{M(f)}{M(0)}$$ | OTF = optical transfer function; M = modulation | **Image quality assessment** |
| 10 | $$\Delta f_{chrom} = \dfrac{f}{V}$$ | Δf_chrom = longitudinal chromatic aberration | **Lens aberration estimation** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Lensmaker's equation (Eq. 2) and the Abbe number equation (Eq. 3) are the **two most frequently tested formulae** in Polymer Optical Goods problems. Always carry units consistently (f in metres → P in dioptres).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Plastic optical goods serve four major industrial sectors:

1. **Ophthalmic Industry (~70% volume)** — spectacle lenses (single-vision, bifocal, progressive), contact lenses (PMMA historical, now hydrogel/silicone hydrogel), sunglasses.
2. **Automotive & Aerospace** — instrument clusters, headlamp lenses, aircraft cabin windows (stretched PMMA), canopies (PC + abrasion coating).
3. **Photonics & Telecommunications** — Polymer Optical Fibres (POF) for short-haul data (e.g., Mitsubishi's ESKA fibre, PMMA-core POF with n_core ≈ 1.49, n_clad ≈ 1.40).
4. **Solar & Displays** — Fresnel lenses for solar concentrators (PMMA stamped sheets), LCD backlight light guides (PMMA), OLED encapsulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refractive Index from Polarizability (Lorentz-Lorenz Relation)

We start from Maxwell's relation between refractive index and relative permittivity in a non-magnetic medium:

$$
n^2 = \epsilon_r \quad \text{(for } \mu_r \approx 1 \text{)}
$$

Now, $\epsilon_r$ is linked to polarizability $\alpha$ via the Clausius-Mossotti relation:

$$
\dfrac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N\alpha}{3\epsilon_0}
$$

where $N$ is the number density of dipoles (molecules per unit volume) and $\epsilon_0$ is the vacuum permittivity.

Substituting $\epsilon_r = n^2$:

$$
\dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} = \dfrac{N\alpha}{3\epsilon_0}
$$

This is the **Lorentz-Lorenz equation**. For a polymer of molecular weight $M$, density $\rho$, and repeat unit with polarizability $\alpha_u$:

$$
\dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \dfrac{M}{\rho} = \dfrac{N_A \alpha_u}{3\epsilon_0} \equiv R_LL
$$

where $R_LL$ is the **molar refractivity** (Lorentz-Lorenz constant, in units of m³/mol). This relation is fundamental to predicting $n$ from chemistry and density—used in designing high-index ophthalmic polymers.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lensmaker's Equation from Snell's Law

Consider a thin lens with two spherical refracting surfaces, refractive index $n$, in air ($n_{air} \approx 1$). Apply Snell's law at each surface with paraxial (small-angle) approximation: $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, $\cos\theta \approx 1$.

**At the first surface** (radius $R_1$, sign convention positive if centre of curvature is to the right):

$$
\dfrac{n_1}{s_1} + \dfrac{n_2}{s_1'} = \dfrac{n_2 - n_1}{R_1}
$$

With $n_1 = 1$ (air), $n_2 = n$ (lens), and object at infinity ($s_1 = \infty$), we get the image distance $s_1' = f_1$:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\infty} + \dfrac{n}{f_1} = \dfrac{n - 1}{R_1} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{n}{f_1} = \dfrac{n-1}{R_1} \;\Rightarrow\; f_1 = \dfrac{nR_1}{n-1}
$$

**At the second surface** (radius $R_2$), the image from surface 1 acts as object:

$$
\dfrac{n}{s_2} + \dfrac{1}{s_2'} = \dfrac{1 - n}{R_2}
$$

For a thin lens, $s_2 = -s_1' = -f_1$ (signs cancel in the thin-lens limit), and $s_2' = f$:

$$
\dfrac{n}{-f_1} + \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1 - n}{R_2}
$$

Substitute $f_1$ and simplify algebraically:

$$
\dfrac{-(n-1)}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1 - n}{R_2}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{n - 1}{R_1} + \dfrac{n - 1}{R_2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{1}{f} = (n - 1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right]}
$$

This is the **Lensmaker's Equation**. Since lens power $P$ (in dioptres) = $1/f$ (in metres):

$$
P = (n-1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention reminder (KTU standard):** $R$ is positive if the centre of curvature lies on the outgoing-light side of the surface. For a biconvex lens, $R_1 > 0$ and $R_2 < 0$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Abbe Number from Cauchy Dispersion

The refractive index varies with wavelength according to Cauchy's empirical relation:

$$
n(\lambda) = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda^2} + \dfrac{C}{\lambda^4} + \dots
$$

For the three standard spectral lines (Fraunhofer C, D, F):

$$
n_D = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda_D^2}, \quad n_F = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda_F^2}, \quad n_C = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda_C^2}
$$

The Abbe number is defined as:

$$
V = \dfrac{n_D - 1}{n_F - n_C}
$$

Substituting the Cauchy expressions:

$$
n_F - n_C = B\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_F^2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_C^2}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
V = \dfrac{A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda_D^2} - 1}{B\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_F^2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_C^2}\right)}
$$

Numerically with $\lambda_F = 486.1$ nm, $\lambda_D = 589.3$ nm, $\lambda_C = 656.3$ nm:

$$
\dfrac{1}{\lambda_F^2} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_C^2} \approx 2.27 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{nm}^{-2}
$$

This is why Abbe number becomes a material constant summarizing chromatic behaviour.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** A biconvex plastic lens is made of PMMA (n_D = 1.492). The radii of curvature are $R_1 = +50$ mm and $R_2 = -50$ mm. Find:
(a) The focal length in cm.
(b) The lens power in dioptres.
(c) If replaced by PC (n_D = 1.586) with same geometry, find the new focal length.
(d) Comment on which lens produces stronger chromatic aberration (Abbe numbers: PMMA = 57, PC = 30).

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using the Lensmaker's equation:

$$
P = (n-1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right] = (1.492 - 1)\left[\dfrac{1}{0.050} - \dfrac{1}{-0.050}\right]
$$

$$
P = 0.492 \times (20 + 20) = 0.492 \times 40 = 19.68 \; \text{D}
$$

Since $P = 1/f$ with $f$ in metres:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{19.68} = 0.0508 \; \text{m} = 5.08 \; \text{cm}
$$

**(b)** Lens power is **P = +19.68 D** (converging).

**(c)** For PC:

$$
P_{PC} = (1.586 - 1) \times 40 = 0.586 \times 40 = 23.44 \; \text{D}
$$

$$
f_{PC} = \dfrac{1}{23.44} = 0.0427 \; \text{m} = 4.27 \; \text{cm}
$$

**(d)** Chromatic aberration magnitude:

$$
\Delta f_{chrom,PMMA} = \dfrac{f_{PMMA}}{V_{PMMA}} = \dfrac{5.08}{57} = 0.089 \; \text{cm}
$$

$$
\Delta f_{chrom,PC} = \dfrac{f_{PC}}{V_{PC}} = \dfrac{4.27}{30} = 0.142 \; \text{cm}
$$

**PC produces ~60% more chromatic aberration** despite a shorter focal length, confirming the engineering trade-off: PC gives higher refractive index (thinner lenses) but worse colour fringing.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Common Optical Polymers

| Polymer | n_D | Abbe V | Impact Strength | Scratch | UV Resistance | Cost | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PMMA** | 1.49 | 57 | Moderate | Poor | Excellent | Low | Aircraft windows, light guides |
| **Polycarbonate** | 1.58 | 30 | Very High | Poor (needs hard-coat) | Moderate (needs UV stabilizer) | Medium | Safety eyewear, riot shields |
| **CR-39 (ADC)** | 1.50 | 58 | Moderate | Good | Excellent | Medium | Prescription spectacle lenses |
| **Polystyrene** | 1.59 | 31 | Low | Poor | Poor (yellows) | Very Low | Disposable optics, lighters |
| **SAN** | 1.57 | 35 | Moderate | Poor | Moderate | Low | Instrument covers |
| **COC (Topas, Zeonex)** | 1.51–1.54 | 55–60 | Moderate | Good | Excellent | High | CD/DVD, medical optics, lenses |
| **MR-series (high-n)** | 1.60–1.74 | 32–42 | Moderate | Good | Good | High | High-power spectacle lenses |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Plastic Optical Product Development Chain

The following diagram maps the engineering interactions from polymer chemistry through end-use optical performance. It captures the sequential dependencies governing design decisions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Monomer Selection] --> B[Polymerization Chemistry]
    B --> C[Amorphous vs Semi-Crystalline Choice]
    C --> D[Intrinsic Optical Properties]
    D --> D1[Refractive Index n]
    D --> D2[Abbe Number V]
    D --> D3[Yellowness Index YI]
    D --> D4[Stress Birefringence Delta n]
    E[Additives Masterbatch] --> D
    F[Processing Route Selection] --> G[Optical Moulding]
    G --> G1[Injection Moulding]
    G --> G2[Compression Moulding]
    G --> G3[Casting for CR-39]
    G --> G4[Extrusion for POF]
    G1 --> H[Surface Treatment]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> H1[Hard Coat]
    H --> H2[Anti-Reflection Coating]
    H --> H3[UV Absorber Layer]
    H --> H4[Mirror Metallization]
    H --> I[In-Process Quality Tests]
    I --> I1[Transmittance Measurement]
    I --> I2[Haze Test ASTM D1003]
    I --> I3[Refractometry]
    I --> I4[Birefringence Mapping]
    I --> J[Final Product Classification]
    J --> J1[Ophthalmic Lens]
    J --> J2[Safety Eyewear]
    J --> J3[Optical Fibre POF]
    J --> J4[Fresnel Lens]
    J --> J5[Prism and Light Guide]
    J --> K[End-Use Service Validation]
    K --> K1[Impact Test ANSI Z87]
    K --> K2[Accelerated Weathering]
    K --> K3[Humidity Resistance]
    K --> L[Market Release]
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Compression Moulding of Plastic Optical Lens

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Optical Grade Preform Pellets] --> P2[Drying at 80 to 120 C 4 hours]
    P2 --> P3[Loading into Polished Steel Mould]
    P3 --> P4[Heating Above Tg]
    P4 --> P5[Applying Pressure 5 to 20 MPa]
    P5 --> P6[Isothermal Hold for Stress Relief]
    P6 --> P7[Controlled Cooling below Tg]
    P7 --> P8[Demoulding with Vacuum Ejectors]
    P8 --> P9[Edge Trimming and Deflashing]
    P9 --> P10[Hard Coating by Dip Spin]
    P10 --> P11[AR Coating by Vacuum Sputtering]
    P11 --> P12[Final Optical Inspection]
```

### 4.3 Polymer Optical Fibre (POF) — Cross-Sectional Functional Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Light Source LED or Laser] --> CL[Coupling Lens]
    CL --> CORE[PMMA Core n approx 1.49 Diameter 0.5 to 1.0 mm]
    CORE --> CLAD[Fluoropolymer Cladding n approx 1.40 Thickness 10 to 20 micrometer]
    CLAD --> JACK[PE or PVC Protective Jacket]
    JACK --> RX[Photodetector Receiver]
    CORE -.->|Total Internal Reflection| CORE
```

> [!NOTE]
> The core-cladding refractive index difference enables **total internal reflection** at angles exceeding the critical angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_{clad}/n_{core})$. For PMMA-POF: $\theta_c \approx 70°$, yielding a high numerical aperture (~0.5) and easy coupling—why POF dominates short-haul links (≤ 100 m) such as automotive MOST bus, home networks (IEEE 1394), and medical sensor cables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** 
Define the term **Abbe number** of an optical polymer. Why is a high Abbe number preferred for ophthalmic lens applications?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Abbe number (V) is defined as the ratio of the refractive index at the sodium D-line (589.3 nm) minus 1 to the difference in refractive indices at the hydrogen F-line (486.1 nm) and C-line (656.3 nm):

$$
V = \dfrac{n_D - 1}{n_F - n_C}
$$

It quantifies the chromatic dispersion (variation of refractive index with wavelength) of the material. A **high Abbe number** indicates low dispersion, meaning white light is refracted with minimal separation into component colours. For ophthalmic lenses, this minimizes **chromatic aberration**, producing sharper images with no colour fringes around viewed objects. PMMA (V ≈ 57) and CR-39 (V ≈ 58) are preferred for spectacle lenses; polycarbonate (V ≈ 30) shows more colour fringing but is chosen for impact safety.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
List **three reasons** why Polycarbonate (PC) is preferred over PMMA for safety eyewear, and state **one drawback** of PC in this application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three reasons PC is preferred:
1. **Higher impact resistance** — notched Izod ≈ 600–850 J/m vs PMMA ≈ 20 J/m; meets ANSI Z87.1 high-impact standards.
2. **Higher refractive index** (n ≈ 1.58 vs 1.49) → thinner, lighter lenses for the same optical power.
3. **Higher heat deflection temperature** (~135 °C) — survives industrial thermal environments.

**One drawback:** PC has **poor scratch and UV resistance**; it yellows on prolonged sun exposure unless UV-stabilized, and requires an abrasion-resistant hard coating (organosilicate) for daily-use spectacles.

---

### Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
(a) Derive the **Lensmaker's equation** for a thin lens, clearly stating the sign convention used. (7 marks)
(b) A biconvex plastic lens made from **CR-39** (n_D = 1.500) has radii of curvature $R_1 = +40$ cm and $R_2 = -40$ cm. Calculate: (i) the focal length, (ii) the lens power in dioptres, and (iii) the longitudinal chromatic aberration given V = 58. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):** 
Follow the derivation in Section 3.2 step-by-step. Key steps to include:
- Apply Snell's law at the first refracting surface using paraxial approximation. (1 mark)
- Derive $f_1 = nR_1/(n-1)$. (1 mark)
- Apply Snell's law at the second surface using the image of surface 1 as virtual object. (1 mark)
- Use the thin-lens approximation $s_2 = -s_1'$. (1 mark)
- Combine the two surface equations to obtain $1/f = (n-1)(1/R_1 - 1/R_2)$. (2 marks)
- State the sign convention explicitly: $R$ is positive if the centre of curvature is on the side of outgoing light. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Focal length:**

$$
P = (n-1)\left[\dfrac{1}{R_1} - \dfrac{1}{R_2}\right] = (1.500 - 1)\left[\dfrac{1}{0.40} - \dfrac{1}{-0.40}\right]
$$

$$
P = 0.500 \times (2.5 + 2.5) = 0.500 \times 5.0 = 2.5 \; \text{D}
$$

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{2.5} = 0.4 \; \text{m} = 40 \; \text{cm}
$$

**(ii) Lens power = +2.5 D** (converging biconvex lens).

**(iii) Longitudinal chromatic aberration:**

$$
\Delta f_{chrom} = \dfrac{f}{V} = \dfrac{40 \; \text{cm}}{58} \approx 0.69 \; \text{cm}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
(a) Discuss the **material requirements** for plastic optical goods with reference to **transparency, refractive index, Abbe number, birefringence, and weatherability**. (7 marks)
(b) Compare the properties and applications of **PMMA, Polycarbonate, and CR-39** as ophthalmic lens materials. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Material Requirements (7 marks):**

1. **Transparency** (2 marks): The polymer must be **amorphous** (no crystallites) and free from fillers, voids, or contaminating particles. Internal transmittance must exceed 90% in the 400–700 nm range. Achieved by selecting atactic polymers (PMMA, PS) or controlled morphology (COC).

2. **Refractive Index** (1.5 marks): Determines lens thickness for a given power. Modern spectacle design demands n ≈ 1.60–1.74 (high-index resins) to reduce lens thickness for strong prescriptions.

3. **Abbe Number** (1.5 marks): Must be ≥ 40 for ophthalmic use to minimize chromatic aberration. PMMA (57) and CR-39 (58) excel; PC (30) is acceptable only with safety justification.

4. **Birefringence** (1 mark): Stress-induced birefringence must be < 10 nm/cm to avoid double images. Achieved by minimizing residual moulding stress through proper gating, packing pressure, and annealing.

5. **Weatherability** (1 mark): UV stability (no yellowing after 1000 h Xenon arc), hydrolysis resistance, and abrasion resistance (must pass Taber abrasion test after hard-coating).

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Property | PMMA | Polycarbonate | CR-39 |
|---|---|---|---|
| n_D | 1.49 | 1.58 | 1.50 |
| Abbe V | 57 | 30 | 58 |
| Impact (Izod) | Low | Very High | Moderate |
| Scratch | Poor | Very Poor | Good |
| UV Resistance | Excellent | Poor (yellows) | Excellent |
| Weight | Lightest | Light | Light |
| Cost | Moderate | Moderate | Higher |
| Castable? | Yes | No | Yes (thermoset) |
| Main Use | Aircraft windows, light guides | Safety/sport eyewear | Prescription spectacles |
| Special Coatings Needed | Optional | Mandatory hard-coat + UV absorber | Optional AR coat |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign convention error in the Lensmaker's equation:** Students frequently use $R_2 = +40$ cm for a biconvex lens, instead of $R_2 = -40$ cm. This flips the lens from converging to diverging and loses 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing $R$ in mm with $f$ expected in metres causes dioptre errors. Always convert: $R$ in metres → $P$ in m⁻¹ = dioptres.
> 3. **Forgetting to state the paraxial assumption:** The thin-lens equation is valid only under small-angle approximation. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks if this is omitted.
> 4. **Confusing haze with transmittance:** Haze = scattered light fraction; transmittance = total light passed. A polymer can have 92% transmittance but 30% haze (cloudy).
> 5. **Not citing Abbe's full form:** Writing $V = (n_D - 1)/(n_F - n_C)$ without stating the wavelengths loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — Plastic Optical Goods:**

- ⭐ **The four "optical superstar" polymers** are PMMA (best clarity + UV), PC (best impact), CR-39 (best scratch + chemistry), and COC (best balance of properties + low birefringence).
- ⭐ **Lensmaker's Equation** is your master formula: $P = (n-1)(1/R_1 - 1/R_2)$; remember **biconvex ⇒ $R_1 > 0$, $R_2 < 0$**.
- ⭐ **Higher n ⇒ thinner lens**; **higher V ⇒ less colour fringing**; these two requirements are usually in tension.
- ⭐ **Abbe number drops** sharply when aromatic content rises (PS, PC); it stays high with aliphatic chains (PMMA, COC).
- ⭐ **Birefringence** arises from **flow-induced molecular orientation** during moulding; fix with annealing at T_g − 10 °C for 2–4 hours.
- ⭐ **Hard coating is mandatory** for PC ophthalmic lenses; without it, they fail Taber abrasion in < 50 cycles.
- ⭐ **POF numerical aperture** for PMMA-core fibre ≈ 0.5, far higher than silica glass fibre (~0.2) — easier coupling, lower bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Fresnel lenses** in PMMA are made by **compression moulding or hot embossing** of a precision-machined stamper; they reduce weight by > 80% over equivalent glass lenses.
- ⭐ **Refractive index can be raised** by introducing halogens (Cl, Br), sulfur, or aromatic groups into the polymer backbone.
- ⭐ **Test standards to remember:** ASTM D1003 (haze), ASTM D542 (refractive index), ISO 13468 (transmittance), ANSI Z87.1 (safety eyewear), ISO 8980 (ophthalmic optics).
- ⭐ **Casting route** is used exclusively for thermoset optical polymers (CR-39, MR-series allyl resins) because their high crosslink density prevents injection moulding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Food Packing Containers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Food Packing Containers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Food Packaging Containers** are engineered polymeric structures designed to **contain, protect, preserve, and present** food products throughout the supply chain — from processing and storage to transport and final consumer use — while complying with **food-contact safety regulations** (e.g., **FDA 21 CFR, EU Regulation 1935/2004, FSSAI, BIS IS 9845**).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context (PCPOT601), a food packing container is treated as a **multi-functional polymeric product** whose design simultaneously addresses four engineering pillars:

| Pillar | Engineering Focus |
|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Stacking strength, drop impact, rigidity |
| **Barrier** | Resistance to O₂, CO₂, H₂O vapor, aroma, light |
| **Thermal** | Hot-fill, retort, microwave, freezer compatibility |
| **Migration Safety** | Overall Migration Limit (OML ≤ 10 mg/dm²) and Specific Migration Limits (SML) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Bodyguard Suit" Concept

Imagine a food container as a **tailored bodyguard suit** worn by the food:

- The **outer shell** (container wall) blocks **physical punches** — mechanical shocks, stacking loads, and abrasion.
- The **inner lining** blocks **chemical attacks** — oxygen, moisture, microbes, and UV light from spoiling the food.
- The **sizing (fit)** must be exact — too tight and the food gets crushed; too loose and it sloshes around losing freshness.
- The **suit's material** must be **food-grade** — non-toxic, non-leaching, and approved by food-safety regulators, just like a bodyguard's certification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** Food packaging is NOT just "putting food in a box." It is a **design-engineered product** where polymer chemistry directly determines **shelf life, food safety, and consumer health**.

## 1.3 Categories of Food Packaging Containers

Food packing containers are broadly classified by **structural form**, **rigidity**, and **end-use application**:

### A. Rigid Containers
- **Bottles** (water, beverages, oils) — PET, HDPE
- **Jars** (pickles, jams) — PP, SAN
- **Tubs & Cups** (yogurt, ice cream) — PP, PS, PLA
- **Trays** (ready-meals, meat) — PET, PP, EVOH/PE multilayer
- **Clamshells** (bakery, fruits) — PET, rPET
- **Cans/Tins** (metallized polymer-lined steel)

### B. Flexible Containers
- **Pouches** (retort, stand-up) — PET/AL/PE laminates
- **Sachets** (sugar, ketchup) — LDPE, Paper/PE
- **Wraps & Films** (cling film) — PVC, LDPE, PVDC
- **Bag-in-Box** (wine, syrups) — EVOH/PE inner bag + corrugated outer

### C. Semi-Rigid Containers
- **Blister packs** (cheese, pills-adjacent food) — PVC/PVDC
- **Thermoformed cups** (drinking cups, dessert cups) — PS, PP
- **Maple-style trays** — APET/PE

## 1.4 Polymer Materials Commonly Used

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Mapping (Frequently Asked in KTU):** Match the polymer to the food product class.

| Polymer | Abbreviation | Typical Food Application |
|---|---|---|
| Polyethylene Terephthalate | **PET** | Beverage bottles, trays |
| High-Density Polyethylene | **HDPE** | Milk jugs, detergent-style oil cans |
| Polypropylene | **PP** | Microwave containers, yogurt cups |
| Polystyrene | **PS / HIPS** | Cups, cutlery, clamshells |
| Expanded Polystyrene | **EPS** | Fish boxes, hot beverage cups |
| Polyvinyl Chloride | **PVC** | Cling film, blister packs |
| Polyvinylidene Chloride | **PVDC** | High-barrier films, cheese wraps |
| Ethylene Vinyl Alcohol | **EVOH** | Barrier layer in multilayer |
| Ethylene Vinyl Acetate | **EVA** | Lid seals, gaskets |
| Polyamide (Nylon) | **PA** | Vacuum bags, retort pouches |
| Polylactic Acid | **PLA** | Biodegradable cups, cutlery |

## 1.5 Critical Design Functions of Food Packaging

A food container must execute **six core engineering functions** simultaneously:

1. **Containment** — Hold the food without leakage.
2. **Protection** — Shield against mechanical, chemical, biological, and environmental hazards.
3. **Preservation** — Extend shelf life by controlling gas/moisture exchange.
4. **Communication** — Display branding, nutritional info, barcodes (often via in-mold labeling).
5. **Convenience** — Easy opening, resealability, microwaveability, portion control.
6. **Sustainability** — Recyclability, biodegradability, reduced material use (down-gauging).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Selection Criteria — The Engineering Trade-off Matrix

When designing a food packaging container, the engineer selects the polymer based on a **weighted decision matrix** of competing properties. Below is the systematic logic KTU examiners expect you to articulate.

### Step 1 — Identify Food-Specific Requirements

| Food Type | Critical Property Required | Recommended Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| Carbonated beverage | High CO₂ barrier + clarity | **PET** |
| Hot-fill juice (85 °C) | Heat resistance + clarity | **PP** or heat-set **PET** |
| Fresh meat (MAP) | O₂ barrier + sealability | **EVOH/PE multilayer tray** |
| Frozen food | Low-temperature toughness | **HDPE, LDPE** |
| Microwave meal | Microwave-transparent + heat resistance | **PP (CPET for higher temp)** |
| Yogurt | Fat barrier + printability | **PS cup + foil/PE lid** |
| Retort pouch (121 °C) | Sterilization survival | **PET/Al/PA/PP** laminate |

### Step 2 — Match Polymer Properties

The five "engineering levers" the polymer must provide:

1. **Barrier Properties** — Permeability to O₂, CO₂, H₂O, aroma.
2. **Mechanical Properties** — Tensile strength, impact, stiffness (modulus), seal strength.
3. **Thermal Properties** — Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT), Vicat softening, glass transition Tg.
4. **Optical Properties** — Haze, gloss, transparency (for product visibility).
5. **Chemical Resistance** — Resistance to fats, acids, alcohols (food simulants A, B, C, D).

### Step 3 — Process Compatibility

- **Injection Molding** → PP tubs, PET preforms
- **Blow Molding (Extrusion / Injection Stretch)** → PET bottles, HDPE containers
- **Thermoforming** → PS, PET, PP trays and cups
- **Extrusion Coating** → Paper/PE milk cartons
- **Co-extrusion** → Multilayer films (5-, 7-, 9-layer)
- **Lamination** → Pouches with foil or metallized layers

## 2.2 Barrier Properties — The Heart of Food Packaging Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permeability** is the single most-examined property in food packaging design. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes this in Module 3.

### The Permeation Mechanism (Solution-Diffusion Model)

Permeation through a polymer occurs in **three sequential steps**:

1. **Sorption** — Gas/vapor dissolves into the upstream surface of the polymer.
2. **Diffusion** — Penetrant molecules migrate down a concentration gradient (Fickian).
3. **Desorption** — Molecules evaporate from the downstream surface.

This is the **Solution-Diffusion Mechanism** — the universally accepted model for polymer permeation (NOT pore-flow).

### Key Permeability Definitions

- **Permeability Coefficient (P):** Steady-state flux per unit pressure difference, per unit thickness.
- **Permeance (P / l):** Flux per unit pressure (used when thickness varies).
- **Diffusion Coefficient (D):** Measures how fast the penetrant moves through the polymer.
- **Solubility Coefficient (S):** Measures how much penetrant dissolves in the polymer.

$$P = D \times S$$

### Henry's Law of Sorption

$$C = S \times p$$

Where:
- $C$ = concentration of penetrant in polymer (cm³ STP / cm³ polymer)
- $S$ = solubility coefficient (cm³ STP / cm³ polymer · atm)
- $p$ = partial pressure of penetrant (atm)

### Fick's First Law of Diffusion (Steady State)

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

Where:
- $J$ = flux (cm³ STP · cm⁻² · s⁻¹)
- $D$ = diffusion coefficient (cm²/s)
- $\frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ = concentration gradient (cm³ STP / cm⁴)

### Fick's Second Law (Transient State)

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$$

This equation governs **non-steady-state** situations such as **scalping** of flavor compounds or **migration of additives** from container to food.

### Permeability Unit Conversion (Frequently Asked!)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often tests unit conversions. The standard SI unit for $P$ is:
>
> $$[P] = \frac{cm^{3}_{STP} \cdot cm}{cm^{2} \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$$
>
> A common practical unit is: $\frac{cc \cdot mil}{100\,in^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}$

## 2.3 Shelf Life Estimation — The Critical Design Equation

For oxygen-sensitive foods (e.g., beer, wine, cured meats), shelf life is governed by **oxygen ingress** through the container wall.

### Oxygen-Limited Shelf Life Equation

$$t_{sl} = \frac{m_{O_2}^{max}}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot \Delta p_{O_2}} \times l$$

Where:
- $t_{sl}$ = shelf life (seconds)
- $m_{O_2}^{max}$ = maximum tolerable oxygen ingress (cm³ STP)
- $P_{O_2}$ = oxygen permeability (cm³ · cm / cm² · s · cmHg)
- $A$ = container surface area (cm²)
- $\Delta p_{O_2}$ = partial pressure difference (cmHg)
- $l$ = wall thickness (cm)

### Moisture-Limited Shelf Life Equation

For moisture-sensitive foods (crackers, dried snacks), shelf life is governed by **water vapor transmission rate (WVTR)**.

$$WVTR = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O}}{l}$$

Then:

$$t_{sl} = \frac{m_{H_2O}^{max}}{WVTR \cdot A}$$

## 2.4 Migration and Food Safety — The Regulatory Framework

### Overall Migration Limit (OML)

$$OML \leq 10\,mg/dm^{2}$$

This is the **maximum permitted migration** of all non-volatile substances from the packaging into the food simulant, per EU Regulation.

### Specific Migration Limit (SML)

Each substance has a specific SML, e.g.:
- **Antimony (Sb)** from PET: SML = **0.04 mg/kg food**
- **Tin (Sn)** from PVC stabilizer: SML = **100 mg/kg food**
- **Vinyl chloride monomer** from PVC: SML = **0.01 mg/kg**

### Migration Testing — Food Simulants

| Simulant | Represents | Composition |
|---|---|---|
| **Simulant A** | Aqueous foods | Distilled water |
| **Simulant B** | Acidic foods | 3% acetic acid |
| **Simulant C** | Alcoholic foods | 10% ethanol |
| **Simulant D** | Fatty foods | Rectified olive oil or isooctane |
| **Simulant E** | Dry foods | MPPO (Modified Polyphenylene Oxide) |

Test conditions: **10 days at 40 °C** (standard) — simulates long-term contact at room temperature.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table. Every equation here has appeared (or will appear) in KTU ESE Module questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Key Variables | Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solution-Diffusion | $P = D \cdot S$ | P = permeability, D = diffusion coeff., S = solubility coeff. | P: $\frac{cm^{3} \cdot cm}{cm^{2} \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$ |
| 2 | Henry's Sorption | $C = S \cdot p$ | C = concentration, p = partial pressure | C: $\frac{cm^{3}_{STP}}{cm^{3}}$ |
| 3 | Fick's First Law | $J = -D \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | J = flux, D = diffusion coefficient | J: $\frac{cm^{3}}{cm^{2} \cdot s}$ |
| 4 | Fick's Second Law | $\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \cdot \frac{\partial^{2} C}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Transient diffusion | — |
| 5 | O₂ Shelf Life | $t_{sl} = \frac{m_{O_2}^{max} \cdot l}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot \Delta p}$ | Mass of O₂, permeability, area, Δp | $t_{sl}$ in seconds |
| 6 | WVTR Equation | $WVTR = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p_{H_2O}}{l}$ | Water vapor transmission | $\frac{g}{m^{2} \cdot day}$ |
| 7 | Haze Equation | $Haze\,(\%) = \frac{Diffuse\,Transmittance}{Total\,Transmittance} \times 100$ | Optical clarity | % |
| 8 | Heat Deflection Temperature (HDT) | Standard load deflection at 0.45 MPa or 1.82 MPa | ASTM D648 | °C |
| 9 | Oxygen Transmission Rate (OTR) | $OTR = \frac{P_{O_2} \cdot \Delta p}{l}$ | Barrier measurement | $\frac{cm^{3}}{m^{2} \cdot day \cdot atm}$ |
| 10 | Migration Fickian (Crank Eq.) | $\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 2 \left(\frac{Dt}{l^{2}}\right)^{1/2} \left[\frac{1}{\pi^{1/2}} + 2 \sum ...\right]$ | Mass migrated vs. time | Dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **solution-diffusion framework** is the bedrock of:

- **Modified Atmosphere Packaging (MAP)** — selecting barrier layers to maintain a custom O₂/CO₂/N₂ mix.
- **Active Packaging** — oxygen scavengers (e.g., iron-based sachets) layered with EVOH.
- **Intelligent Packaging** — time-temperature indicators (TTI) integrated into PP/EVOH containers.
- **Aseptic Packaging** — Tetra Pak's multilayer paperboard/PE/Al/PE enables 6–12 month ambient shelf life for milk.
- **Retort Pouches** — Military Meals-Ready-to-Eat (MRE) use PET/AL/PA/PP to survive 121 °C sterilization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Takeaway:** Selecting the right polymer is **NOT** about picking the "best" material — it is about **balancing cost, processability, barrier, safety, and sustainability** within the food product's shelf-life window.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Permeation Through a Polymer Film (Solution-Diffusion Model)

### Objective
Derive the **permeability equation** for a gas passing through a flat polymer film of thickness $l$, under a steady-state concentration gradient.

### Given
- Upstream partial pressure: $p_1$ (high)
- Downstream partial pressure: $p_2$ (low, often ≈ 0 in vacuum tests)
- Film thickness: $l$
- Henry's Law holds at both surfaces: $C_1 = S \cdot p_1$ and $C_2 = S \cdot p_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply Fick's First Law at steady state.**

At steady state, the concentration gradient is **linear** across the film thickness (one-dimensional diffusion).

$$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Integrate across the film thickness** (from $x = 0$ to $x = l$).

Since flux is constant at steady state:

$$\int_{0}^{l} J \, dx = -\int_{C_1}^{C_2} D \, dC$$

$$J \cdot l = -D \cdot (C_2 - C_1) = D \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Henry's Law** for surface concentrations.

$$J \cdot l = D \cdot (S \cdot p_1 - S \cdot p_2) = D \cdot S \cdot (p_1 - p_2)$$

**Step 4 — Define the permeability coefficient.**

$$P \equiv D \cdot S$$

Therefore:

$$J \cdot l = P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{J = \frac{P \cdot (p_1 - p_2)}{l}}$$

### Physical Interpretation
- Flux $J$ is **directly proportional** to the permeability coefficient $P$ and the pressure difference $\Delta p = (p_1 - p_2)$.
- Flux is **inversely proportional** to thickness $l$ — doubling wall thickness halves oxygen ingress.
- Higher $D$ (more open polymer) and higher $S$ (more compatible polymer-penetrant pair) both increase $P$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Shelf Life of an Oxygen-Sensitive Beverage in a PET Bottle

### Problem Statement
A **500 mL PET bottle** (wall thickness $l = 0.30\,mm$, inner surface area $A = 400\,cm^{2}$) holds orange juice. The maximum tolerable oxygen ingress before flavor degradation is $m_{O_2}^{max} = 5\,cm^{3}_{STP}$. The bottle is stored at ambient conditions where $\Delta p_{O_2} = 0.21\,atm - 0.05\,atm = 0.16\,atm$. The oxygen permeability of PET at 25 °C is $P_{O_2} = 0.10 \times 10^{-13}\,\frac{cm^{3} \cdot cm}{cm^{2} \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$. Convert to consistent units and calculate the predicted shelf life in **days**.

### Given Data
- $m_{O_2}^{max} = 5\,cm^{3}_{STP}$
- $A = 400\,cm^{2}$
- $l = 0.30\,mm = 0.030\,cm$
- $\Delta p_{O_2} = 0.16\,atm = 0.16 \times 76\,cmHg = 12.16\,cmHg$
- $P_{O_2} = 0.10 \times 10^{-13}\,\frac{cm^{3} \cdot cm}{cm^{2} \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the oxygen-limited shelf life equation.**

$$t_{sl} = \frac{m_{O_2}^{max} \cdot l}{P_{O_2} \cdot A \cdot \Delta p_{O_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values** (all in CGS units).

$$t_{sl} = \frac{5 \times 0.030}{(0.10 \times 10^{-13}) \times 400 \times 12.16}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator.**

$$Denominator = 0.10 \times 10^{-13} \times 400 \times 12.16$$
$$= 0.10 \times 10^{-13} \times 4864$$
$$= 486.4 \times 10^{-13}$$
$$= 4.864 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the numerator.**

$$Numerator = 5 \times 0.030 = 0.15$$

**Step 5 — Divide numerator by denominator.**

$$t_{sl} = \frac{0.15}{4.864 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$t_{sl} = 0.03084 \times 10^{11}\,seconds$$

$$t_{sl} = 3.084 \times 10^{9}\,seconds$$

**Step 6 — Convert seconds to days.**

$$t_{sl}\,(\text{days}) = \frac{3.084 \times 10^{9}}{86400}$$

$$\boxed{t_{sl} \approx 35{,}694\,\text{days} \approx 97.8\,\text{years}}$$

### Engineering Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single-layer PET bottle provides a **theoretical oxygen-limited shelf life of ~98 years** for the stated $m_{O_2}^{max}$. This is why PET works for beverages — the limiting factor in real life is usually **vitamin C degradation** or **microbial spoilage**, NOT oxygen permeation alone. KTU examiners love asking "why is the *real* shelf life much shorter than the *calculated* shelf life?" — the answer lies in **flavor loss, scalping, light degradation, and microbial kinetics**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Non-Steady-State Migration (Fickian Diffusion from Container Wall into Food)

### Problem Statement
A **plasticizer (e.g., DEHP)** at initial concentration $C_0 = 1000\,\text{ppm}$ is uniformly distributed in a PVC container wall of thickness $l = 1.0\,mm$. The container contacts food simulant B (3% acetic acid) on both sides. Calculate the fractional migration $\frac{M_t}{M_\infty}$ after $t = 30\,\text{days}$ at 40 °C. Diffusion coefficient of DEHP in PVC at 40 °C: $D = 1.0 \times 10^{-12}\,cm^{2}/s$.

### Step-by-Step Solution (Crank's Equation for Thin Sheet, Both Faces Exposed)

**Step 1 — Identify the geometry.** This is a **thin sheet** (PVC wall) with migration occurring from **both faces** into food.

For short times ($\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} < 0.5$), Crank's approximation is:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{4}{l} \sqrt{\frac{D \cdot t}{\pi}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dimensionless time term.**

First, convert $t$ to seconds:
$$t = 30 \times 24 \times 3600 = 2{,}592{,}000\,s$$

Compute $D \cdot t$:
$$D \cdot t = (1.0 \times 10^{-12}) \times (2.592 \times 10^{6})$$
$$= 2.592 \times 10^{-6}\,cm^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the square root term.**

$$\sqrt{\frac{D \cdot t}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{2.592 \times 10^{-6}}{3.1416}}$$

$$= \sqrt{8.251 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$= 9.084 \times 10^{-4}\,cm$$

**Step 4 — Compute the migration fraction.**

Wall thickness $l = 1.0\,mm = 0.10\,cm$.

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{4}{0.10} \times 9.084 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 40 \times 9.084 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$= 3.634 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \approx 0.0363 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 3.63\%}$$

### Engineering Interpretation
Only **3.63%** of the available plasticizer migrates in 30 days. The actual mass migrated:

$$M_t = 0.0363 \times C_0 \times V_{wall}$$

For $V_{wall} = 50\,cm^{3}$, density $= 1.4\,g/cm^{3}$, mass $= 70\,g$, $C_0 = 1000\,ppm = 1000\,\mu g/g$:

$$M_t = 0.0363 \times 1000 \times 70 = 2541\,\mu g = 2.541\,mg$$

This must be compared with the **SML for DEHP** (typically $1.5\,mg/kg$ food). For $0.5\,kg$ of food contacting this container, the SML would be $0.75\,mg$ — meaning this PVC formulation **fails** food-contact regulations. This is why DEHP has been phased out of food-contact PVC in many countries.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Selection for Common Food Containers

| Food Product | Container Type | Layer 1 (Contact) | Layer 2 (Barrier) | Layer 3 (Structural) | Process |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Carbonated soft drink | Bottle | PET | — | — | Injection Stretch Blow Molding |
| Milk | Pouch | LDPE | — | — | Extrusion-blown film |
| Yogurt | Cup | PS | — | — | Thermoforming |
| Ketchup | Squeeze bottle | HDPE | — | — | Extrusion Blow Molding |
| Beer | Bottle | PET | — | — | ISBM (with O₂ scavenger) |
| Vacuum-packed coffee | Pouch | PE | EVOH | PET | Co-extrusion Lamination |
| Retort curry | Pouch | PP | AL foil | PET | Lamination + Thermal bonding |
| Microwave pasta | Tray | CPET | — | — | Thermoforming (crystallized PET) |
| Hot dog | Tray | PE | EVOH | PS | Co-extrusion Thermoforming |
| Wine | Bag-in-box | PE | EVOH | PET outer | Co-extrusion |

---

## 3.5 KTU-Style Numerical Practice — Worked Example

### Problem
A PP yogurt cup has wall thickness $l = 0.8\,mm$ and surface area $A = 80\,cm^{2}$. The water vapor permeability of PP at 25 °C is $P_{H_2O} = 6.0 \times 10^{-10}\,\frac{cm^{3} \cdot cm}{cm^{2} \cdot s \cdot cmHg}$. The yogurt is stored at 25 °C with internal RH = 85% and external RH = 50%. Compute the **daily moisture loss** in grams, given that the partial pressure of water at 25 °C is $23.76\,mmHg$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate partial pressure difference.**

$$\Delta p = (0.85 - 0.50) \times 23.76 = 0.35 \times 23.76 = 8.316\,mmHg$$

**Step 2 — Convert mmHg to cmHg.**

$$\Delta p = 8.316\,mmHg = 0.8316\,cmHg$$

**Step 3 — Apply the WVTR equation.**

$$WVTR = \frac{P_{H_2O} \cdot \Delta p}{l}$$

$$= \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.8316}{0.08}$$

$$= \frac{4.99 \times 10^{-10}}{0.08}$$

$$= 6.236 \times 10^{-9}\,\frac{cm^{3}_{STP}}{cm^{2} \cdot s}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by area to get volumetric flux.**

$$Q = WVTR \times A = 6.236 \times 10^{-9} \times 80 = 4.989 \times 10^{-7}\,\frac{cm^{3}_{STP}}{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to daily volume (1 day = 86,400 s).**

$$V_{daily} = 4.989 \times 10^{-7} \times 86{,}400 = 4.311 \times 10^{-2}\,cm^{3}/day$$

**Step 6 — Convert volume of water vapor to mass.** At STP, 1 mol = 22,400 cm³; M of water = 18 g/mol.

$$m_{daily} = 4.311 \times 10^{-2} \times \frac{18}{22{,}400}$$

$$m_{daily} = 4.311 \times 10^{-2} \times 8.036 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\boxed{m_{daily} \approx 3.46 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{g/day} = 0.0346\,\text{mg/day}}$$

### Interpretation
A PP yogurt cup loses only **~0.035 mg of water per day** — extremely low. This is why PP is the **gold standard** for moisture-sensitive dairy products. To increase shelf life further, a thin **EVOH or PVDC barrier layer** could be co-extruded with PP.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Multilayer Food Container

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the typical layer architecture of a 5-layer retort pouch used for ready-to-eat meals, with the functional role of each layer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OuterLayer["OUTER LAYER - Mechanical and Print Substrate"]
        A1["PET - Polyester Film"]
        A2["Role: Print substrate, abrasion resistance, puncture resistance, heat resistance up to 220 deg C"]
    end

    subgraph Adhesive1["ADHESIVE TIE LAYER 1"]
        B1["Adhesive 1 - Polyurethane or Modified PE"]
        B2["Role: Bonds PET to Aluminum foil with peel strength greater than 5 N per 15 mm"]
    end

    subgraph BarrierLayer["CENTRAL BARRIER LAYER"]
        C1["Aluminum Foil - 9 micrometer thickness"]
        C2["Role: Total light barrier, oxygen transmission less than 0.01 cc per square meter per day, moisture barrier, aroma barrier"]
    end

    subgraph Adhesive2["ADHESIVE TIE LAYER 2"]
        D1["Adhesive 2 - Polyurethane or Modified PP"]
        D2["Role: Bonds Aluminum to PA and survives retort at 121 deg C for 30 minutes"]
    end

    subgraph InnerLayer["INNER FOOD CONTACT LAYER"]
        E1["PP - Polypropylene - 70 to 100 micrometer"]
        E2["Role: Heat sealable, food contact safe, moisture barrier, retort stable, SML compliant"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Polymer Selection Decision Logic for Food Containers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Food Product Specification]) --> Q1{Is Food Acidic? pH less than 4.5}
    Q1 -- Yes --> A1[Use Simulant B Testing, Prefer PET, PP, Glass-lined]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is Food Fatty? Oil content greater than 5 percent}
    Q2 -- Yes --> A2[Use Simulant D Testing, Avoid PVC plasticizer migration, Prefer HDPE, PP]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is Food Aqueous?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> Q4{Does it need O2 barrier?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> A3[Specify EVOH or PVDC co-extruded multilayer]
    Q4 -- No --> A4[Use LDPE or HDPE monolayer]
    Q3 -- No --> Q5{Is Food Heat Processed? Retort 121 deg C or Hot-fill 85 deg C}
    Q5 -- Yes --> A5[Use PP or CPET, Confirm HDT greater than process temperature]
    Q5 -- No --> Q6{Is Food Frozen? Storage below minus 18 deg C}
    Q6 -- Yes --> A6[Use HDPE or LDPE, Confirm low temperature impact strength]
    Q6 -- No --> A7{Is Food Microwave Reheated?}
    Q7 -- Yes --> A8[Use CPET or PP with microwave-transparent additives]
    Q7 -- No --> A9[Use general purpose PS, PET, or HIPS]
    A1 --> endNode([Document OML and SML compliance])
    A2 --> endNode
    A3 --> endNode
    A4 --> endNode
    A5 --> endNode
    A6 --> endNode
    A8 --> endNode
    A9 --> endNode
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Manufacturing Process Selection for Food Containers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1([Polymer Resin Selection]) --> P2{Container Geometry?}
    P2 -- Hollow Bottle --> M1[Injection Stretch Blow Molding - ISBM]
    P2 -- Open Cup or Tray --> M2[Thermoforming]
    P2 -- Wide Mouth Jar --> M3[Injection Molding then Blow]
    P2 -- Thin Film or Pouch --> M4[Blown Film Extrusion or Cast Film]
    P2 -- Multilayer Barrier --> M5[Co-extrusion Blow or Co-extrusion Cast]
    P2 -- Laminated Pouch --> M6[Adhesive Lamination plus Heat Sealing]

    M1 --> O1[Preform Injection then Stretch Blow at 100 to 110 deg C]
    M2 --> O2[Sheet Extrusion then Vacuum or Pressure Form]
    M3 --> O3[Parison Injection then Blow Mold with Stretching]
    M4 --> O4[Melt extruded through die, quenched on chill roll or air-cooled bubble]
    M5 --> O5[Two to nine polymer melts combined in feedblock or die, then formed]
    M6 --> O6[Multiple webs bonded with PU or acrylic adhesive, then heat sealed into pouches]

    O1 --> Q[Quality Testing]
    O2 --> Q
    O3 --> Q
    O4 --> Q
    O5 --> Q
    O6 --> Q

    Q --> T1[OTR Measurement per ASTM D3985]
    Q --> T2[WVTR Measurement per ASTM F1249]
    Q --> T3[Migration Test per EU 10/2011 or FDA 21 CFR]
    Q --> T4[Drop Test per ASTM D2463]
    Q --> T5[Seal Strength per ASTM F88]
```

## 4.4 KTU Conceptual Map — Why Polymer Properties Map to Food Packaging Functions

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Food Packaging Container))
    Mechanical
      Tensile Strength
      Impact Resistance
      Stiffness Modulus
      Creep Resistance
    Barrier
      Oxygen OTR
      Moisture WVTR
      CO2 Retention
      Light UV Blocking
    Thermal
      HDT Heat Deflection
      Vicat Softening
      Glass Transition Tg
      Retort Stability
    Safety
      OML Overall Migration less than 10 mg per dm squared
      SML Specific Migration Limits
      Food Simulant Testing
      Non-Toxicity Certification
    Sustainability
      Recyclability
      Biodegradability PLA PHA
      Down-Gauging Lightweighting
      Mono-Material Design
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a functional "engineering decision node." In KTU board examinations, you may be asked to draw a similar flowchart for a given food product (e.g., "design a packaging system for hot-fill mango pulp"). Use these as templates.
