Food Grade SBR] classDef root fill:#F5C518,stroke:#7A5A00,color:#000000 classDef auto fill:#E07B91,stroke:#7B1F2B,color:#000000 classDef consumer fill:#5BA8E0,stroke:#1B4F72,color:#000000 classDef industrial fill:#88C9A1,stroke:#1E4D2B,color:#000000 classDef specialty fill:#C9A188,stroke:#4D2B1E,color:#000000 class A root class B auto class C consumer class D,E,F industrial class G,H specialty ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define Styrene Butadiene Rubber. List any three of its distinctive properties and state its principal industrial application.** **Model Answer:** SBR is a **synthetic random copolymer of styrene (23.5%) and 1,3-butadiene (76.5%)** produced by emulsion or solution polymerization. **Three distinctive properties:** 1. Excellent **abrasion resistance** (superior to natural rubber in tire treads). 2. Good **aging and heat resistance** due to the saturated backbone from styrene units. 3. **Non-crystallizing** under strain (so it must be reinforced with carbon black to develop strength). **Principal application:** **Passenger car tire treads and sidewalls**, which consume approximately 70% of global SBR production. --- ### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between Hot E-SBR and Cold E-SBR in terms of polymerization conditions and properties.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Hot E-SBR | Cold E-SBR | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Polymerization Temperature | $\approx 50^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\approx 5^{\circ}\text{C}$ | | Initiator System | Thermal (persulfate) | Redox (Fe-EDTA) | | Molecular Weight | Higher, with more branching | Slightly lower, more linear | | Dynamic Properties | Moderate | Superior (better resilience and abrasion) | | Processing | Easier extrusion | Cleaner die swell control | | Typical Application | Mats, general molded goods | Tire carcasses and treads | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]** **(a)** Describe the **emulsion polymerization process** for the manufacture of SBR. List the role of each ingredient in the recipe. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the effect of **styrene content** on the properties of SBR. A tire-tread grade of SBR contains 25 wt% styrene. Calculate the **approximate $T_g$** of this copolymer using the Flory-Fox equation. Given: $T_{g,\text{PS}} = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$, $T_{g,\text{PB}} = -90^{\circ}\text{C}$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Emulsion Polymerization Process for E-SBR (Step-by-Step):** 1. **Recipe Preparation:** 100 parts monomer (blend of styrene + butadiene), 5 parts fatty-acid soap emulsifier, 0.3 parts potassium persulfate initiator, 200 parts deionized water, 0.1 part chain transfer modifier (e.g., tert-dodecyl mercaptan). 2. **Charging:** Monomers, water, and soap are charged into a stirred reactor maintained at $5^{\circ}\text{C}$ (cold process) or $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ (hot process). 3. **Initiation:** The redox initiator generates free radicals that attack the monomer double bonds. The soap micelles solubilize the hydrophobic monomers, providing the polymerization site. 4. **Propagation:** Alternating styrene and butadiene units add to the growing chain radical. Conversion is limited to $\approx 60$% to avoid composition drift. 5. **Shortstop:** At 60% conversion, the reaction is terminated with a free-radical scavenger (e.g., hydroquinone) to prevent uncontrollable cross-linking. 6. **Monomer Stripping:** Unreacted butadiene and styrene are removed under vacuum/steam and recovered for recycling. 7. **Coagulation:** The latex is broken by adding salt (NaCl) and dilute sulfuric acid, producing rubber crumbs. 8. **Drying and Baling:** The crumbs are washed, dried in a hot-air tunnel, and pressed into 25–35 kg bales. **Role of Each Ingredient:** - **Soap Emulsifier:** Stabilizes micelles and provides the locus of polymerization. - **Initiator:** Generates free radicals to start the chain reaction. - **Chain Transfer Agent:** Controls molecular weight and prevents gel formation. - **Water:** Continuous phase for heat transfer and viscosity control. #### Model Solution to (b): **Effect of Styrene Content on SBR Properties:** | Property | As Styrene Content Increases | | :--- | :--- | | Hardness | Increases | | Tensile Strength | Slight decrease | | Glass Transition ($T_g$) | Increases (becomes less flexible at low T) | | Resilience (Rebound) | Decreases | | Processability | Becomes harder (higher Mooney viscosity) | | Hysteresis Loss | Increases (more heat build-up) | | Cost | Slight increase | **Calculation of $T_g$ for 25 wt% SBR using Flory-Fox:** **Step 1: Convert homopolymer $T_g$ to Kelvin.** $$ T_{g,\text{PS}} = 100 + 273 = 373 \text{ K} $$ $$ T_{g,\text{PB}} = -90 + 273 = 183 \text{ K} $$ **Step 2: Identify mass fractions.** $$ w_{\text{St}} = 0.25, \quad w_{\text{Bd}} = 0.75 $$ **Step 3: Apply the Flory-Fox equation.** $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_{\text{St}}}{T_{g,\text{PS}}} + \frac{w_{\text{Bd}}}{T_{g,\text{PB}}} $$ **Step 4: Substitute the numbers.** $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.25}{373} + \frac{0.75}{183} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 6.70 \times 10^{-4} + 4.10 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \frac{1}{T_g} = 4.77 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} $$ **Step 5: Invert and convert back to °C.** $$ T_g = \frac{1}{4.77 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 210 \text{ K} = 210 - 273 = -63^{\circ}\text{C} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** A $T_g$ of $-63^{\circ}\text{C}$ means this SBR compound will remain rubbery and flexible in all temperate climates but will become glassy (brittle) below $-60^{\circ}\text{C}$, limiting its use in extreme polar environments. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the role of **carbon black, sulfur, accelerators, and antidegradants** in an SBR tire-tread compound. Justify the loading levels typically used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vulcanized SBR sample swells to equilibrium in toluene, giving a rubber volume fraction of $v_r = 0.20$. Using the Flory-Rehner equation, calculate the cross-link density, $\nu$, given $V_s = 106.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$ and $\chi = 0.39$. Comment on whether the compound is optimally cured. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): 1. **Carbon Black (40–60 phr, typically N330 HAF):** Reinforcing filler that dramatically increases tensile strength, tear strength, modulus, and abrasion resistance. The carbon black particles form physical junction points and occlude the rubber, restricting chain slippage under stress. Loading above 60 phr leads to poor processability and higher hysteresis. 2. **Sulfur (1.5–2.5 phr):** The vulcanizing agent that forms covalent disulfide ($-S-S-$) and polysulfide ($-S_x-$) bridges between adjacent polymer chains. For SBR (which is less unsaturated than natural rubber), a higher sulfur level and efficient accelerators are required. Excessive sulfur produces polysulfide bridges that are thermally reversible and reduce aging life. 3. **Accelerators (1.0–1.5 phr, e.g., CBS or TBBS):** Catalyze the sulfur cross-linking reaction, reducing cure time and temperature. They also form mono- and di-sulfide bridges, which are shorter and more thermally stable than natural polysulfide bridges, giving a tighter, more durable network. 4. **Antidegradants (1.0–2.0 phr, e.g., TMQ or 6PPD):** Protect against **oxidation** (chain scission by free radicals) and **ozone attack** (surface cracking). 6PPD is particularly effective against ozone and is essential for tire sidewalls, which face constant flexing and atmospheric exposure. Loading above 2 phr causes blooming and surface tackiness. #### Model Solution to (b): **Step 1: Write down the Flory-Rehner equation.** $$ \nu = \frac{-\left[ \ln(1 - v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2 \right]}{V_s \left( v_r^{1/3} - \frac{v_r}{2} \right)} $$ **Step 2: Substitute $v_r = 0.20$, $\chi = 0.39$, $V_s = 106.4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol}$.** $$ \ln(1 - 0.20) = \ln(0.80) = -0.2231 $$ $$ v_r^2 = 0.04, \quad \chi v_r^2 = 0.39 \times 0.04 = 0.0156 $$ **Step 3: Compute the numerator.** $$ \text{Num} = -(-0.2231 + 0.20 + 0.0156) = -(0.0075) = 0.0075 $$ **Step 4: Compute the denominator terms.** $$ v_r^{1/3} = (0.20)^{1/3} = 0.5848 $$ $$ v_r^{1/3} - \frac{v_r}{2} = 0.5848 - 0.10 = 0.4848 $$ **Step 5: Multiply by molar volume.** $$ \text{Den} = 106.4 \times 0.4848 = 51.58 \text{ cm}^3/\text{mol} $$ **Step 6: Compute $\nu$.** $$ \nu = \frac{0.0075}{51.58} = 1.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3 $$ **Comment on Cure State:** A cross-link density of $1.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$ falls comfortably within the **optimal range of $1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$** for a tire-tread SBR compound. The compound is therefore **optimally cured** — neither under-cured (which would give poor elasticity and high set) nor over-cured (which would give brittleness and poor fatigue life). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in SBR Exam Answers:** > 1. **Confusing E-SBR with S-SBR:** Students often interchange "emulsion" and "solution" SBR. Remember: E-SBR is the **commodity workhorse**; S-SBR is the **premium tire-tread grade** with vinyl-content tuning. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $T_g$ to Kelvin** before applying the Flory-Fox equation. Direct substitution of Celsius values will yield a nonsense negative result. > 3. **Ignoring the negative sign in the Flory-Rehner numerator.** Many students drop the brackets, leading to a negative cross-link density, which is physically meaningless. > 4. **Wrong units in $\nu$:** Always report in $\text{mol/cm}^3$ (or $\text{mol/m}^3$ for SI), not $\text{g/cm}^3$. > 5. **Naming the monomers as "styrene and butane":** The correct comonomer is **1,3-butadiene**, a conjugated diene, not butane (a saturated gas). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **SBR = Styrene + 1,3-Butadiene** random copolymer; standard grade is **23.5 wt% styrene**. - It is the **highest-volume synthetic rubber** in the world, primarily used in **tire treads**. - **E-SBR** is produced by emulsion polymerization; **S-SBR** by anionic solution polymerization. - Two sub-types of E-SBR: **Hot (50°C)** and **Cold (5°C, redox-initiated)**. Cold E-SBR has better dynamic properties. - **Vinyl content** in S-SBR controls $T_g$ and rolling resistance — critical for modern "green" tire treads. - SBR is **non-crystallizing under strain**, so it must be **reinforced with carbon black** to develop tensile strength. - SBR is **less unsaturated than natural rubber**, so it requires **more sulfur + accelerator** to cure. - Standard compounding recipe: **SBR 100, Carbon Black 50, ZnO 5, Stearic Acid 2, Antioxidant 1.5, Oil 10, Sulfur 2, Accelerator 1.2** (phr). - **Flory-Fox equation** is the KTU-standard tool for predicting $T_g$ from copolymer composition. - **Flory-Rehner equation** is the KTU-standard tool for measuring **cross-link density** from equilibrium swelling. - Typical **$T_g$ of standard SBR** is around **$-55$ to $-60^{\circ}\text{C}$** — limits its polar-region use. - Typical **cross-link density** for optimally cured SBR: **$1 \times 10^{-4}$ to $2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/cm}^3$**. - Key **advantages** of SBR over NR: better abrasion resistance, better aging, lower cost, and consistent quality. - Key **disadvantages** of SBR vs. NR: lower green strength, poorer building tack, lower resilience, more heat build-up. - **Solution SBR (S-SBR)** is the future of the industry — it is the foundation of high-performance "low rolling resistance" tires mandated by EU fuel-economy regulations.

topics.title: Butadiene Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Butadiene Rubber ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Polybutadiene Rubber (BR or PBR)** is a synthetic elastomer produced by the **homopolymerization of 1,3-butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂)**, a conjugated diene monomer. It is classified as a general-purpose synthetic rubber and is the **second most widely consumed synthetic rubber in the world** (after SBR), with global production exceeding 4 million metric tonnes annually. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Butadiene Rubber is defined as a homopolymer of 1,3-butadiene, with the general repeat unit structure **–(CH₂–CH=CH–CH₂)–** when produced via 1,4-addition, characterized by extremely low glass transition temperature (Tg ≈ **–95 °C**), high resilience, and excellent abrasion resistance. ### 1.2 Monomer: 1,3-Butadiene - **IUPAC Name:** Buta-1,3-diene - **Molecular Formula:** C₄H₆ - **Molecular Weight:** **54.09 g/mol** - **Boiling Point:** –4.4 °C (a gas at room temperature) - **Critical Temperature:** 152 °C - **Critical Pressure:** 4.32 MPa - **Structure:** A conjugated diene with two C=C double bonds separated by a single C–C bond. > [!NOTE] > **The Conjugated Diene Significance:** The conjugation in 1,3-butadiene is the key structural feature that allows the polymer to retain a **double bond in the polymer backbone** after polymerization. This residual unsaturation provides the active sites required for **sulfur vulcanization**, making BR a true rubber. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of **1,3-butadiene as a "hinged door"** 🧱 — it has two reactive ends (the two C=C double bonds) connected by a flexible middle. When many such "doors" link together, they form a **long, tangled chain** with tiny "kinks" (the remaining C=C bonds) embedded along the spine. These kinks are like **flexible elbow joints** that allow the chain to coil and uncoil millions of times without breaking — that's what gives BR its extraordinary **elastic bounce** and **cold-weather flexibility**. When you compress a tennis ball or roll a tire over rough terrain, the **polybutadiene chains inside flex and recover rapidly**, dissipating minimal energy as heat. This is why BR is the **"spring rubber"** — high resilience, low hysteresis. > [!TIP] > **Real-world presence:** Look at the core of a Titleist golf ball or the tread of a high-performance tire — both rely heavily on **high-cis polybutadiene** for their signature bounce and wear resistance. ### 1.4 Key Terminology Snapshot | Term | Meaning | |------|---------| | **Cis-1,4 unit** | Both chain substituents on same side of the C=C backbone | | **Trans-1,4 unit** | Substituents on opposite sides — more crystalline, harder | | **1,2-Vinyl unit** | Side-group CH=CH₂ pendant; reduces elasticity when in excess | | **High-cis BR** | > 90% cis-1,4 content (made with Co, Ni, Nd catalysts) | | **Li-BR (Lithium BR)** | Anionic polymerization with butyllithium — medium cis content | | **Mooney Viscosity** | Measure of processability of raw rubber | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Polymerization Chemistry of 1,3-Butadiene The 1,3-butadiene monomer offers **three distinct propagation pathways** during chain growth, leading to three different microstructural units in the final polymer: #### 2.1.1 The Three Microstructures 1. **1,4-cis addition** → The chain grows through carbons 1 and 4, with the new C–C bond forming on the *same side* of the residual C=C. This produces a **flexible, low-Tg** structure. 2. **1,4-trans addition** → Similar 1,4 growth, but the chain bond forms on the *opposite side*. Results in a **stiffer, more crystalline** domain. 3. **1,2-addition (vinyl)** → The chain grows through carbons 1 and 2, leaving a **pendant vinyl group (–CH=CH₂)** on the backbone. Increases Tg dramatically if content is high. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **microstructure is dictated by the catalyst system**, NOT by the monomer. This is a classic KTU 2024 high-yield concept. #### 2.1.2 Catalyst Systems and Resulting Microstructure | Catalyst System | Cis-1,4 (%) | Trans-1,4 (%) | 1,2-Vinyl (%) | Industrial Use | |-----------------|-------------|---------------|---------------|----------------| | CoCl₂ / AlR₃ / H₂O (cobalt) | 96–98 | 1–2 | 1–2 | Tire treads | | Ni(OCOCR₃)₂ / AlR₃ / BF₃·OEt₂ | 95–97 | 2–3 | 1–2 | Tire treads | | Nd(OCOR)₃ / AlR₃ / Lewis acid | 95–99 | < 1 | < 1 | Premium tires | | n-BuLi (anionic) | 35–40 | 50–55 | 8–10 | Impact modifier | | Emulsion (free radical) | 10–20 | 60–70 | 15–25 | Latex applications | | TiCl₄ / AlR₃ (Ziegler) | ~ 0 | 90–95 | 5–10 | Specialty (TPV) | ### 2.2 Polymerization Kinetics – Theoretical Framework The polymerization of 1,3-butadiene typically follows **free-radical** or **coordination-anionic** mechanisms. For free-radical emulsion polymerization, the rate of polymerization (Rp) is given by: $$R_p = k_p \cdot [M] \cdot \left( \frac{f \cdot k_d \cdot [I]}{k_t} \right)^{1/2}$$ **Where each variable is defined as follows:** - $R_p$ = Rate of polymerization (mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹) - $k_p$ = Propagation rate constant - $[M]$ = Monomer concentration - $f$ = Initiator efficiency factor - $k_d$ = Initiator decomposition rate constant - $[I]$ = Initiator concentration - $k_t$ = Termination rate constant ### 2.3 Number-Average Degree of Polymerization (Xn) For free-radical polymerization (no chain transfer): $$\overline{X_n} = \frac{R_p}{R_i} = \frac{k_p \cdot [M]}{2 \cdot (f \cdot k_d \cdot k_t \cdot [I])^{1/2}}$$ **Where each variable is defined as follows:** - $\overline{X_n}$ = Number-average degree of polymerization - $R_i$ = Rate of initiation - All other terms as defined above ### 2.4 Number-Average Molecular Weight $$\overline{M_n} = \overline{X_n} \cdot M_0$$ **Where each variable is defined as follows:** - $\overline{M_n}$ = Number-average molecular weight (g/mol) - $M_0$ = Molecular weight of the repeat unit (54.09 g/mol for butadiene) ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical BR Value | |---|-------------------|-------------|------------------| | 1 | $T_g \approx -95 + 0.65 \cdot (\% \text{vinyl})$ (°C) | Vinyl content raises Tg | –95 °C (high-cis) | | 2 | $\delta = (\Sigma E_{coh} / V)^{1/2}$ | Hildebrand solubility parameter | **17.2 MPa^0.5** | | 3 | $\overline{X_n} = [M]_0 / [I]_0^{1/2}$ (simplified) | Inverse √I dependence | 2000–8000 | | 4 | $Q = \frac{1}{C_0} \int_0^\infty Q(t) \, dt$ | Swelling ratio (crosslink density) | 3–6 for cured BR | | 5 | $v_e = -\frac{\ln(1-v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2}{V_s (v_r^{1/3} - v_r/2)}$ | Flory–Rehner: crosslink density | (1–5) × 10⁻⁵ mol/cm³ | | 6 | $\% \text{ Cis} = \frac{A_{cis}}{\Sigma A_{1,4+1,2}} \times 100$ | Microstructure by FT-IR / ¹³C NMR | > 95% (high-cis) | | 7 | Strain-induced crystallinity (Xc) | Increases tensile strength | Up to 30% at 400% strain | > [!WARNING] > When writing tables in your answer script, use **plain text boxes** or convert formulas to **handwritten form**. Always **show units** explicitly — KTU examiners allocate up to 0.5 marks per answer for unit consistency. ### 2.6 Engineering Utility – Why This Matters - **Tire industry**: Tread compounds use 30–50 phr (parts per hundred rubber) of high-cis BR blended with NR/SBR. The blend optimizes **rolling resistance vs. wet grip** — BR reduces hysteresis loss, saving fuel. - **Sports equipment**: The resilience of a golf ball core (rebound > 0.7) is impossible without high-cis BR. - **Cold-climate applications**: With Tg at –95 °C, BR remains rubbery at temperatures where NR and SBR turn glassy. - **Polymer toughening**: BR is a major impact modifier in **HIPS (High Impact Polystyrene)** and **ABS** — its low Tg allows it to absorb impact energy via crazing. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Microstructure Tg Relationship – Exhaustive Derivation The Tg of polybutadiene is a linear function of the **vinyl content** (1,2-units) in the polymer chain, since each 1,2-unit stiffens the backbone by introducing a pendant vinyl group. We derive the empirical Fox-type relationship for BR: **Given:** - Pure 1,4-cis-polybutadiene has Tg₁ = **–108 °C** - Pure 1,2-syndiotactic polybutadiene has Tg₂ = **+0 °C** - Vinyl content in the polymer = φ (mole fraction) **Step 1: Apply the Fox equation for copolymer Tg prediction** The Fox equation relates Tg to weight (or mole) fraction of monomer units: $$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{w_1}{T_{g1}} + \frac{w_2}{T_{g2}}$$ **Step 2: Convert Tg values to Kelvin** $$T_{g1} = -108 + 273 = 165 \text{ K}$$ $$T_{g2} = 0 + 273 = 273 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: For a high-cis BR with 3% vinyl content, w₁ = 0.97 and w₂ = 0.03** Substituting into the Fox equation: $$\frac{1}{T_g} = \frac{0.97}{165} + \frac{0.03}{273}$$ **Step 4: Calculate each term** $$\frac{0.97}{165} = 5.879 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ $$\frac{0.03}{273} = 1.099 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 5: Sum the two terms** $$\frac{1}{T_g} = 5.879 \times 10^{-3} + 0.1099 \times 10^{-3} = 5.989 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$ **Step 6: Take the reciprocal to find Tg in Kelvin** $$T_g = \frac{1}{5.989 \times 10^{-3}} = 166.97 \text{ K}$$ **Step 7: Convert back to Celsius** $$T_g = 166.97 - 273 = -106.0 \text{ °C}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The result (–106 °C) closely matches the experimental value of **–95 °C to –108 °C** for high-cis BR. The small deviation is because the simple Fox equation neglects the small fraction of trans-1,4 units, which would slightly raise Tg. ### 3.2 Crosslink Density by Flory–Rehner Method **Problem:** A sample of vulcanized polybutadiene is swollen in toluene (Vs = 106.7 cm³/mol, χ = 0.39). The volume fraction of rubber in the swollen gel is vr = 0.18. Calculate the crosslink density νₑ. **Step 1: State the Flory–Rehner equation** $$v_e = -\frac{\ln(1-v_r) + v_r + \chi v_r^2}{V_s (v_r^{1/3} - v_r/2)}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the numerator components** $$\ln(1 - 0.18) = \ln(0.82) = -0.1985$$ $$v_r = 0.18$$ $$\chi v_r^2 = 0.39 \times (0.18)^2 = 0.39 \times 0.0324 = 0.01264$$ **Step 3: Sum the numerator** $$\text{Numerator} = -(-0.1985) + 0.18 + 0.01264 = 0.1985 + 0.18 + 0.01264 = 0.3911$$ **Step 4: Calculate the denominator components** $$v_r^{1/3} = (0.18)^{1/3} = 0.5646$$ $$v_r/2 = 0.18/2 = 0.09$$ $$v_r^{1/3} - v_r/2 = 0.5646 - 0.09 = 0.4746$$ $$V_s \times (v_r^{1/3} - v_r/2) = 106.7 \times 0.4746 = 50.64$$ **Step 5: Compute the crosslink density** $$v_e = -\frac{0.3911}{50.64} = -7.72 \times 10^{-3}$$ The negative sign cancels due to the convention; the absolute value is reported: $$v_e = 7.72 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mol/cm}^3$$ **Step 6: Convert to mol/m³ (SI unit)** $$v_e = 7.72 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^6 = 7.72 \times 10^3 \text{ mol/m}^3$$ > [!NOTE] > A crosslink density of ~ 7.7 × 10⁻³ mol/cm³ corresponds to a moderately cured BR compound (typical of tire tread vulcanizates). Lower values indicate under-cure; higher values would indicate an over-cured, brittle product. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix – Property Comparison | Property | High-cis BR | NR (Natural Rubber) | SBR (E-SBR 1500) | |----------|-------------|---------------------|------------------| | Tg (°C) | **–95 to –108** | –72 | –50 to –60 | | Tensile Strength (MPa) | 17–25 | 25–35 | 20–30 | | Elongation at break (%) | 450–600 | 600–800 | 500–700 | | Rebound resilience (%) | **75 (at 23 °C)** | 70 | 60 | | Abrasion resistance | **Excellent** | Excellent | Good | | Heat buildup (ΔT, °C) | **Low (15–20)** | Medium (25–30) | High (35–45) | | Processability | **Poor (cold flow)** | Excellent | Good | | Raw Mooney viscosity | 40–55 | 70–90 | 50–70 | ### 3.4 Vulcanization Chemistry – Stepwise **Sulfur vulcanization of cis-1,4-polybutadiene proceeds as follows:** **Step 1: Activator-accelerator reaction** — ZnO and stearic acid form zinc stearate, which activates the accelerator (e.g., CBS — N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide). **Step 2: Crosslink precursor formation** — Accelerator polysulfides (Acc–Sₓ–Acc) form by reaction with elemental sulfur. **Step 3: Allylic hydrogen abstraction** — The polysulfide abstracts the **allylic hydrogen** of the BR backbone: $$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{Acc–S}_x\text{–Acc} \rightarrow \text{–CH–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{Acc–S}_x\text{H}$$ **Step 4: Sulfur bridge formation** — Two allyl radicals (or allyl + polysulfidic radical) couple via a sulfur bridge: $$\text{–CH–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH–} \rightarrow \text{–CH–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–S}_y\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH–}$$ **Result:** A polysulfidic crosslink (typically y = 2–4) is formed between BR chains, producing a 3-D network. > [!TIP] > KTU often asks: **"Why does BR require higher sulfur loading than NR?"** The answer: BR has a **lower concentration of α-methylene groups** (no pendant methyl group on the repeat unit), reducing the number of reactive allylic positions. Hence accelerator systems are tuned specifically for BR. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Polymerization Routes for 1,3-Butadiene The following flowchart maps the four principal industrial polymerization routes and their resulting polymer architectures. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[1,3-Butadiene Monomer
C4H6, MW 54.09] --> B[Polymerization Route Selection] B --> C1[Free Radical
Emulsion] B --> C2[Anionic
n-BuLi] B --> C3[Ziegler-Natta
Co or Ni based] B --> C4[Rare Earth
Nd based] C1 --> D1[Low Cis BR
10 to 20 percent Cis] C2 --> D2[Medium Cis BR
35 to 40 percent Cis] C3 --> D3[High Cis BR
96 to 98 percent Cis] C4 --> D4[Ultra High Cis BR
over 99 percent Cis] D1 --> E1[Latex Applications
Glove Dipping] D2 --> E2[Impact Modifier
HIPS and ABS] D3 --> E3[Tire Tread Blends
with NR and SBR] D4 --> E4[Premium Tire Sidewall
Golf Ball Core] ``` ### 4.2 Microstructure Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid block-beta columns 3 block:CisBlock["1,4 CIS STRUCTURE"]:3 CisChain["Repeat Unit Pattern"] CisNote["Both CH2 groups on same side of double bond"] CisApp["Tire tread, golf ball, footwear"] block:TransBlock["1,4 TRANS STRUCTURE"]:3 TransChain["Repeat Unit Pattern"] TransNote["CH2 groups on opposite sides of double bond"] TransApp["Thermoplastic, stiff articles"] block:VinylBlock["1,2 VINYL STRUCTURE"]:3 VinylChain["Repeat Unit Pattern"] VinylNote["Pendant vinyl group CH=CH2 off backbone"] VinylApp["Raised Tg, specialty elastomer"] ``` ### 4.3 Functional Architecture of Tire Tread Compound ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RubberMatrix[Base Elastomer Blend 100 phr] R1[Natural Rubber NR 50 phr] R2[Polybutadiene BR 30 phr] R3[SBR 1500 20 phr] end subgraph Reinforcement[Reinforcement System] F1[Carbon Black N330 50 phr] F2[Silica 15 phr] end subgraph CureSystem[Cure Package] V1[Sulfur 1.5 phr] V2[Accelerator CBS 1.0 phr] V3[ZnO 3 phr] V4[Stearic Acid 2 phr] end subgraph Protection[Antidegradants] P1[Antioxidant 6PPD 1.5 phr] P2[Antiozonant Wax 1.0 phr] end RubberMatrix --> CureSystem Reinforcement --> CureSystem CureSystem --> Protection Protection --> FinalCompound[Vulcanized Tire Tread] ``` ### 4.4 Property–Application Dependency Matrix ```mermaid graph TD P1[High Cis BR Properties] --> A1[Low Tg minus 95 C] P1 --> A2[High Resilience] P1 --> A3[Low Heat Buildup] P1 --> A4[Good Abrasion] P1 --> A5[Poor Processability] A1 --> App1[Low Temp Seals] A2 --> App2[Golf Ball Core] A3 --> App3[Tire Tread] A4 --> App3 A5 --> App4[Blended with NR] App4 --> App3 A2 --> App5[Conveyor Belts] A3 --> App5 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1. Define Butadiene Rubber (BR). Write any two characteristic properties that distinguish it from natural rubber.** **Model Answer:** Butadiene Rubber is a synthetic homopolymer of 1,3-butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂), with the general structure **–(CH₂–CH=CH–CH₂)ₙ–** when polymerized via 1,4-addition. It is produced industrially by emulsion, anionic, or Ziegler–Natta coordination polymerization. **Distinguishing properties from NR:** 1. **Lower glass transition temperature:** Tg of high-cis BR is –95 °C, compared to –72 °C for NR. This gives BR superior low-temperature flexibility, suitable for arctic-grade rubber goods. 2. **Higher resilience and lower heat buildup:** BR exhibits a rebound resilience of ~ 75% at 23 °C versus ~ 70% for NR. This is the primary reason BR is blended into tire treads to reduce rolling resistance and improve fuel economy. > *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for property 1, 1 for property 2)* --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2. What is meant by "high-cis polybutadiene"? Name the catalyst system used to produce it.** **Model Answer:** "High-cis polybutadiene" refers to polybutadiene in which the cis-1,4 configuration constitutes **greater than 90% (typically 95–99%)** of the total repeat units. The remaining units are predominantly trans-1,4 with a small fraction of 1,2-vinyl. **Catalyst systems used:** - **Cobalt-based Ziegler–Natta:** CoCl₂ / Al(i-Bu)₃ / H₂O (or pyridine) — gives 96–98% cis - **Nickel-based:** Ni(OCOR)₂ / AlR₃ / BF₃·OEt₂ — gives 95–97% cis - **Neodymium-based (rare earth):** Nd(OCOR)₃ / AlR₃ / Lewis acid — gives 95–99% cis (highest activity and linearity) > *(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + threshold, 1.5 for catalyst systems)* --- ### 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]** #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) – **OPTION 1** **Q.A. (a)** Discuss in detail the three possible microstructures of polybutadiene obtained by 1,3-butadiene polymerization. How does the choice of catalyst influence the microstructure? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(i) Three Microstructures (3 marks):** When 1,3-butadiene polymerizes, the active chain-end can add the next monomer through three different pathways, producing three distinct microstructural units: **1. 1,4-cis addition:** The new C–C bond forms on the **same side** of the residual internal double bond. The resulting repeat unit is **–CH₂–CH=CH–CH₂–** with both backbone CH₂ groups *cis* to each other. This is the most flexible, low-Tg, highly elastic structure. It is favoured by **Co, Ni, and Nd-based catalysts** and gives the best rubber properties. **2. 1,4-trans addition:** The new C–C bond forms on the **opposite side** of the residual double bond, giving the trans isomer. This unit packs more closely, leading to **higher crystallinity**, higher density, and a stiffer polymer. Pure trans-1,4 BR is essentially a thermoplastic and not a true elastomer. It is favoured by **TiCl₄/AlR₃ Ziegler** systems and by **free-radical polymerization at low temperatures**. **3. 1,2-addition (vinyl):** The chain grows through carbons 1 and 2 of the next monomer, leaving a **pendant vinyl group (–CH=CH₂)** attached to the backbone. The vinyl pendant stiffens the chain and raises Tg. Stereoregular 1,2-PBd can be **syndiotactic** (preferred) or isotactic. 1,2-units are favoured by **n-butyllithium anionic polymerization** and Co-based catalysts with certain modifiers. **(ii) Catalyst Influence (4 marks):** | Catalyst | Cis-1,4 | Trans-1,4 | 1,2-Vinyl | |----------|---------|-----------|-----------| | Co-based Ziegler | 96–98% | 1–2% | 1–2% | | Ni-based | 95–97% | 2–3% | 1–2% | | Nd-based (rare earth) | 95–99% | < 1% | < 1% | | n-BuLi (anionic) | 35–40% | 50–55% | 8–10% | | Free radical (emulsion) | 10–20% | 60–70% | 15–25% | The **coordination geometry of the active site** (transition-metal–carbon bond) determines which face of the incoming monomer is approached, and hence whether cis or trans insertion occurs. Transition metals with larger ionic radii and higher Lewis acidity (e.g., Nd³⁺) coordinate the diene in a "η⁴-cis" conformation that locks the cis geometry, producing ultra-high-cis BR. Conversely, free-radical propagation has no such control, producing a statistical mixture. **Q.A. (b)** Explain the role of polybutadiene in tire tread compounds. Why is BR blended with NR or SBR rather than used alone? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Role of BR in tire treads (3 marks):** A tire tread experiences repeated cyclic deformation (compression, flexion, and recovery) at high frequency. The energy lost per cycle appears as **hysteretic heat** that raises the operating temperature of the tire. Excess heat buildup accelerates aging, reduces tread life, and increases fuel consumption. **High-cis BR**, with its very low Tg (–95 °C) and high resilience (rebound ~ 75%), has **the lowest hysteresis of any commercial rubber**. By blending 30–50 phr of high-cis BR with NR and SBR, tire manufacturers: - Reduce rolling resistance → better fuel economy - Lower heat buildup → extended tread life - Improve abrasion resistance → longer wear - Maintain excellent low-temperature flexibility → all-season performance **Why BR is not used alone (4 marks):** 1. **Poor green strength and tack:** BR has very low cohesive strength in the uncured state, making it difficult to build multilayer tire components (plies, belts, tread) without sticking. NR provides essential tack during tire building. 2. **Poor processability:** BR exhibits **cold flow** — the raw polymer deforms under its own weight at room temperature. This makes handling, storage, and extrusion difficult. NR has a natural "nerve" that BR lacks. 3. **Lower tensile strength and tear resistance:** When used alone, BR gives tensile strengths of only 17–25 MPa compared to 25–35 MPa for NR. The blend compensates for this deficiency. 4. **Limited vulcanization reactivity:** BR has no pendant methyl group on the repeat unit, reducing the density of α-allylic hydrogens (active sites for sulfur crosslinking). The blend with NR (which has an α-methyl on every isoprene unit) increases the overall crosslink density and cure rate. > **Conclusion:** Tire tread compounds use a ternary blend of **NR (40–50 phr) + BR (30–40 phr) + SBR (15–25 phr)** to obtain the optimum balance of **rolling resistance, wet grip, abrasion resistance, and processability** — often called the "magic triangle" of tire performance. --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) – **OPTION 2** **Q.B. (a)** Describe the emulsion polymerization process for the production of polybutadiene rubber. Compare it with solution polymerization. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Emulsion Polymerization of BR (3 marks):** Emulsion polymerization of 1,3-butadiene is carried out in **aqueous medium** at temperatures of **5–50 °C** and pressures of **5–15 bar** (since butadiene boils at –4.4 °C). The recipe typically contains: - **Water** (continuous phase) — 100–200 phr - **Butadiene monomer** — 100 phr - **Emulsifier** (e.g., potassium rosinate or fatty acid soap) — 2–5 phr - **Initiator** (water-soluble, e.g., potassium persulfate K₂S₂O₈, or redox systems like FeSO₄/H₂O₂/sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate) — 0.1–0.5 phr - **Chain transfer agent** (e.g., tert-dodecyl mercaptan, TDM) — 0.1–0.5 phr (controls Mooney viscosity) - **Electrolyte** (KCl or Na₃PO₄) — 0.5 phr (controls micelle size) The mechanism follows the **Smith–Ewart theory**: monomer diffuses from droplets into micelles where initiation and propagation occur. The latex (solid content ~ 30–40%) is coagulated by acid or salt addition, washed, dried, and baled. **Microstructure:** Emulsion BR has **low cis content (10–20%)**, **high trans (60–70%)**, and moderate vinyl (15–25%). It is mainly used in **latex applications** (glove dipping, carpet backing). **Solution Polymerization (2.5 marks):** Solution polymerization uses an **organic solvent** (e.g., hexane, cyclohexane, toluene) to dissolve both monomer and polymer. **Ziegler–Natta or anionic catalysts** are introduced to control the microstructure. The process offers: - Precise control of molecular weight and molecular weight distribution - Direct control of microstructure by catalyst selection - Higher cis content (up to > 99% with Nd catalysts) - Direct use of the cement (polymer solution) in subsequent compounding, reducing energy costs **Comparison Table (1.5 marks):** | Parameter | Emulsion | Solution | |-----------|----------|----------| | Medium | Water | Organic solvent | | Catalyst | Free radical | Ziegler/Coordination | | Cis-1,4 content | 10–20% | 95–99% | | Polymer isolation | Coagulation | Steam stripping | | Solvent recovery | Not needed | Critical (energy intensive) | | Polymer form | Latex or dry crumb | Cement → crumb | | Application | Latex goods | Tire rubber | **Q.B. (b)** Explain the vulcanization of polybutadiene rubber. Discuss the role of accelerators and the effect of cure temperature on crosslink density. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Vulcanization mechanism (3 marks):** Vulcanization converts the soft, plastic polybutadiene into a tough, elastic 3-D network by forming covalent sulfur (or peroxide) bridges between adjacent polymer chains. **Sulfur vulcanization** is the most common process and proceeds through the following steps: **Step 1 — Accelerator activation:** The accelerator (e.g., **CBS — N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide**) reacts with ZnO and stearic acid to form an active zinc-accelerator complex. This complex is the true vulcanization catalyst. **Step 2 — Sulfur activation:** The zinc-accelerator complex opens the S₈ ring and forms a **polysulfidic intermediate** (Acc–Sₓ–Acc, where x = 2–4). **Step 3 — Allylic hydrogen abstraction:** The polysulfidic intermediate abstracts an **allylic hydrogen** from the BR backbone: $$\text{–CH}_2\text{–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–} \rightarrow \text{–}\dot{\text{C}}\text{H–CH=CH–CH}_2\text{–} + \text{H–S}_x\text{–Acc}$$ **Step 4 — Crosslink formation:** Two chain-bound radicals (or a radical + a polysulfidic radical) couple to form a **polysulfidic bridge (–Sₓ–)** between two BR chains. **Role of Accelerators (2 marks):** 1. **Reduce cure time** from hours to minutes (typical BR cure: 10–30 min at 140–160 °C) 2. **Lower vulcanization temperature**, allowing processing without polymer degradation 3. **Increase crosslink density** and improve aging resistance by forming shorter, more uniform crosslinks (mono- and di-sulfidic) rather than long polysulfidic bridges 4. **Improve scorch safety** (delay premature cure during mixing) **Effect of cure temperature on crosslink density (2 marks):** Crosslink density (νₑ) follows an **Arrhenius-type relationship** with cure temperature: $$\ln v_e = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$$ **Where each variable is defined as follows:** - $v_e$ = Crosslink density (mol/cm³) - $E_a$ = Activation energy of crosslink formation (~ 80–100 kJ/mol for sulfur systems) - $R$ = Universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K) - $T$ = Cure temperature (K) - $A$ = Pre-exponential factor **Implications:** - **Higher cure temperature → higher νₑ** (more crosslinks) but also more **reversion** (crosslink breakage) for BR, since the polysulfidic bridges are thermally labile. - **Optimal cure temperature** for BR tread compounds is **140–160 °C** for **10–20 minutes**, balancing maximum νₑ with minimum reversion. - **Over-cure** (> 30 min at 170 °C) leads to a **drop in crosslink density** and a fall in tensile and abrasion properties — a phenomenon called **"reversion cure"** which is more pronounced in BR than in NR. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:** > 1. **Confusing "cis" with "vinyl":** Many students incorrectly write "1,2-cis" or "1,2-trans." Remember: *cis/trans* apply only to **1,4-addition**; *iso/syndio-tactic* apply to **1,2-addition**. > 2. **Forgetting units in the Flory–Rehner equation:** Always state **V_s in cm³/mol** and **v_r as a dimensionless ratio** to avoid dimensional mismatch. > 3. **Mixing Tg values:** The Tg of *cis-1,4* BR is –108 °C; the Tg of *1,2-syndiotactic* BR is ~ 0 °C. Do not quote "–95 °C" as the universal Tg of BR — it is a *typical* value for high-cis commercial grades. > 4. **Omitting the catalyst composition:** In KTU 2024 answers, naming a metal alone is *insufficient* — you must specify the **co-catalyst (e.g., AlR₃)** and **Lewis acid modifier (e.g., BF₃·OEt₂)** for full credit. > 5. **Ignoring the Tg-vinyl relationship in derivations:** When asked to estimate Tg, you must show the **vinyl content effect** explicitly. A generic "Tg is low" earns partial credit only. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision — Bullet Card for Butadiene Rubber** - ✅ **Monomer:** 1,3-butadiene (CH₂=CH–CH=CH₂), MW = **54.09 g/mol**, b.p. = –4.4 °C - ✅ **Repeat unit (1,4-addition):** –(CH₂–CH=CH–CH₂)ₙ– - ✅ **Three microstructures:** 1,4-cis (flexible, low Tg), 1,4-trans (crystalline, stiff), 1,2-vinyl (pendant CH=CH₂, raises Tg) - ✅ **High-cis BR:** > 90% cis, made by **Co / Ni / Nd Ziegler–Natta** catalysts - ✅ **Tg of high-cis BR:** **–95 to –108 °C** (one of the lowest among commercial rubbers) - ✅ **Rebound resilience:** ~ 75% at 23 °C (highest among general-purpose rubbers) - ✅ **Key advantage:** Lowest heat buildup → used in **tire treads, golf ball cores** - ✅ **Key disadvantage:** Poor processability, low green strength → **always blended** with NR/SBR - ✅ **Solubility parameter (δ):** ~ **17.2 MPa^0.5** - ✅ **Vulcanization:** Sulfur + accelerator (CBS, TBBS) + ZnO + stearic acid; cure at **140–160 °C** - ✅ **Special behavior:** BR is prone to **reversion cure** (crosslink breakdown at high temperature/long time) - ✅ **Impact modifier use:** In **HIPS** (~ 6% BR) and **ABS** (~ 15% BR) to absorb impact via crazing - ✅ **Industrial grades:** **Cobalt-BR, Nickel-BR, Neodymium-BR, Lithium-BR, Emulsion-BR** (each with different cis content) - ✅ **Comparison mantra:** "BR = best resilience, lowest Tg, but worst tack — always blend it!" > **End of Module 4 — Butadiene Rubber | KTU PCPOT601 | 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber (NBR / Nitrile Rubber / Buna-N)** is a synthetic elastomer produced by the emulsion co-polymerization of two monomers: 1. **Acrylonitrile (ACN)** — $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{C}\equiv\text{N}$ (polar, nitrile-bearing monomer) 2. **Butadiene (BD)** — $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2$ (non-polar diene monomer) The resulting random copolymer has the idealized repeating unit: $$-\big[\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}(\text{CN})\big]_x-\big[\text{CH}_2-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{CH}_2\big]_y-$$ Where $x$ and $y$ represent the mole fractions of acrylonitrile and butadiene segments respectively, with commercial grades typically ranging from **ACN content of 18 % to 50 %** by weight. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 – Specialty Rubbers):** NBR is the *most important oil-resistant synthetic rubber* and must be studied with reference to its structure, ACN content versus property balance, vulcanization chemistry, and engineering applications (seals, gaskets, hoses, O-rings). > [!NOTE] > **Nomenclature Note:** The polymer is called **NBR** (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber) by **ISO 1629** and **ASTM D 1418**, **Perbunan** by Bayer, **Nipol** by Zeon, **Chemigum** by Goodyear, and **Buna-N** historically by I.G. Farben — all are chemically identical families of acrylonitrile–butadiene copolymers. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Oil-Repellent Sponge" Imagine a regular household sponge — soft, flexible, but the moment you dip it in cooking oil, it swells, weakens, and falls apart. Now imagine reinforcing that sponge with millions of tiny **polar magnets (nitrile groups, –C≡N)** sticking out from every polymer chain. Oil, being non-polar, can no longer penetrate and dissolve the structure easily — it is repelled by these polar anchors. That is exactly what **acrylonitrile does in NBR**: - The **butadiene segments** give the rubber its **elasticity, low-temperature flexibility, and rebound resilience**. - The **acrylonitrile segments** provide **polarity, oil resistance, and strength**, because the strongly electronegative –CN group creates strong intermolecular dipole-dipole attractions. **Real-world feel of NBR:** A disposable nitrile glove — stretchy, yet completely resistant to engine oil, fuels, and many solvents — is the most familiar everyday manifestation of NBR chemistry. > [!TIP] > **Memory Hook for KTU Exam:** *"More ACN → More Oil Resistance, More Strength, More Hardness — but LESS Flexibility and WORSE Low-Temperature Performance."* --- ## 1.3 Historical & Industrial Context | Year | Milestone | |------|-----------| | **1930** | First synthesized by **Erich Konrad and Tschunkur** at **I.G. Farben** (Germany) | | **1934** | Commercialized under the trade name **Buna-N** | | **1941** | U.S. production began during WWII (strategic material for fuel hoses) | | **1960s onward** | Hydrogenated NBR (**HNBR**) developed for higher heat/ageing resistance | | **2024** | Global production exceeds **~600,000 tonnes/year**, used in automotive, oil & gas, medical gloves | --- ## 1.4 Classification by ACN Content | Grade | ACN Content (wt%) | Key Property | Typical Application | |-------|--------------------|--------------|---------------------| | **Low-Nitrile (LN)** | 18 – 24 % | Excellent low-temperature flexibility | Cold-climate seals, freezer gaskets | | **Medium-Nitrile (MN)** | 28 – 35 % | Balanced oil resistance & flexibility | General O-rings, automotive hoses | | **High-Nitrile (HN)** | 38 – 45 % | Superior oil & fuel resistance | Fuel hoses, oil-field seals | | **Very-High-Nitrile (VHN)** | 45 – 50 % | Extreme chemical resistance | Specialty industrial gaskets | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Standard Reference:** A typical **"medium-grade NBR"** used in the KTU laboratory and design problems contains **33 – 34 % ACN**, density **~0.98 g/cm³**, Mooney viscosity **ML(1+4) at 100 °C ≈ 50**, and glass transition temperature **T_g ≈ −25 °C**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Polymerization Mechanism — Emulsion Co-polymerization NBR is produced almost exclusively by **free-radical emulsion co-polymerization** in an aqueous medium. **Reaction steps (logical sequence):** 1. **Monomer Emulsification:** Butadiene (gas) and acrylonitrile (liquid) are emulsified in water using a **soap surfactant** (e.g., sodium rosinate or potassium fatty-acid salt) at **5 – 10 °C**. 2. **Initiator Activation:** A **redox initiator system** (e.g., **potassium persulfate $\text{K}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_8$** + sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate) generates free radicals in the aqueous phase. 3. **Chain Initiation:** Radicals diffuse into the micelles containing solubilized monomers and add to a monomer double bond. 4. **Propagation:** The chain grows by alternate addition of acrylonitrile and butadiene monomers in a **random** (not block) distribution. The reactivity ratios are: - $r_1$ (acrylonitrile) $\approx 0.02$ - $r_2$ (butadiene) $\approx 0.30$ 5. **Chain Transfer:** A short-chain modifier (e.g., **tert-dodecyl mercaptan, TDM**) controls molecular weight by transferring the radical to a new chain. 6. **Termination:** Combination or disproportionation of two growing radicals. 7. **Coagulation & Drying:** Latex is coagulated with acid/alum, washed, and dried into crumb rubber. > [!TIP] > **Why emulsion?** Emulsion polymerization gives **high molecular weight (MW ≈ 200,000 – 400,000)** at fast rates with excellent **heat dissipation** — critical because the reaction is highly exothermic ($\Delta H \approx -84 \text{ kJ/mol}$ butadiene). ## 2.2 Effect of ACN Content on Properties — The "Property Wheel" The acrylonitrile content is the **single most important variable** governing NBR properties because it directly controls: - **Intermolecular forces** (dipole density per unit volume) - **Glass transition temperature (T_g)** - **Crystallinity & chain stiffness** - **Solubility parameter ($\delta$)** ### Property vs. ACN Content — Trend Summary | Property | Effect of ↑ ACN Content | |----------|------------------------| | **Oil & fuel resistance** | ↑ Increases (strongly) | | **Tensile strength** | ↑ Increases | | **Hardness (Shore A)** | ↑ Increases | | **Abrasion resistance** | ↑ Increases | | **Gas impermeability** | ↑ Increases | | **Glass transition, $T_g$** | ↑ Increases (becomes less flexible) | | **Resilience / rebound** | ↓ Decreases | | **Low-temperature flexibility** | ↓ Decreases | | **Compression set resistance** | ↓ Decreases (becomes harder) | | **Heat resistance** | ↑ Increases slightly | | **Plasticizer compatibility** | ↓ Decreases | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following formulas appear most frequently in KTU ESE numericals and design problems on NBR. All symbols use LaTeX-safe vertical delimiters. | # | Equation / Relation | Description & Units | |---|--------------------|---------------------| | 1 | $T_g \;=\; T_{g,BD}\,(1-w_{ACN}) \;+\; T_{g,ACN}\,w_{ACN}$ | **Fox equation** for copolymer $T_g$; $w_{ACN}$ = weight fraction ACN; $T_{g,BD}\!\approx\!-85\,^\circ\text{C}$, $T_{g,ACN}\!\approx\!+105\,^\circ\text{C}$ | | 2 | $\delta_{NBR} \;\approx\; \delta_{BD}\,(1-x_{ACN}) \;+\; \delta_{ACN}\,x_{ACN}$ | **Solubility parameter** additive; $\delta_{BD}\!\approx\!17.0$, $\delta_{ACN}\!\approx\!27.4\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$ | | 3 | $\Delta H_{rxn} \;\approx\; -84\ \text{kJ/mol}$ butadiene converted | **Heat of polymerization** | | 4 | $\overline{M}_n \;=\; \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average molecular weight | | 5 | $[\eta] \;=\; K\,M^{\alpha}$ | **Mark–Houwink** intrinsic viscosity; for NBR in THF at 25 °C, $K\!\approx\!5.0\times10^{-4}\ \text{dL/g}$, $\alpha\!\approx\!0.64$ | | 6 | $\text{Swell \%} \;=\; \dfrac{W_s - W_0}{W_0} \times 100$ | ASTM D 471 oil-swell test; $W_s$ = swollen weight, $W_0$ = original | | 7 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{[\text{ACN}]}{[\text{BD}]}\,\dfrac{r_1[\text{ACN}] + [\text{BD}]}{r_2[\text{BD}] + [\text{ACN}]}$ | **Copolymer composition drift** (Mayo–Lewis instantaneous) | | 8 | $\Delta G_{mix} \;=\; RT\!\left[n_1\ln\phi_1 + n_2\ln\phi_2 + \chi\,n_1\phi_2\right]V / V_r$ | Flory–Huggins interaction; **oil resistance fails when $\chi<0.5$** | | 9 | $\sigma_B \;=\; F/A_0$ | Engineering tensile stress; **typical NBR $\sigma_B = 7 - 25$ MPa** | | 10 | $E_{rubber} \;\approx\; 3\,n\,RT$ | **Rubber elasticity** modulus; $n$ = crosslink density (mol/m³) | ## 2.4 Vulcanization (Cross-linking) Chemistry NBR is unsaturated (residual C=C from butadiene) and is therefore vulcanizable with **sulfur** systems, just like natural rubber. **Standard sulfur recipe (parts per hundred rubber, phr):** - NBR polymer — **100 phr** - Zinc oxide (ZnO, activator) — **3 – 5 phr** - Stearic acid (activator co-agent) — **1 – 2 phr** - Sulfur (cross-linker) — **0.5 – 2 phr** - CBS or TBBS (accelerator, N-cyclohexyl-2-benzothiazole sulfenamide) — **0.5 – 1.5 phr** - TMTD (tetramethylthiuram disulfide, secondary accelerator) — **0.2 – 0.5 phr** - Carbon black (filler) — **30 – 80 phr** - Plasticizer (DOP/DOA) — **5 – 30 phr** - Antioxidant (e.g., **6PPD**) — **1 – 2 phr** **Cross-link types formed:** - **Mono-sulfidic (C–S–C)** — best heat ageing resistance - **Di-sulfidic (C–S–S–C)** — balance of strength & flexibility - **Poly-sulfidic (C–S$_x$–C, $x>2$)** — highest tensile strength, poor ageing > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Tip:** In KTU exam answers, always state the **function of each ingredient** — e.g., *"ZnO + stearic acid form zinc stearate, which activates the accelerator."* ## 2.5 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Automotive (~60% of NBR consumption):** Fuel hoses, O-rings, hydraulic seals, transmission gaskets — because of oil/fuel resistance. - **Oil & Gas industry:** Downhole sealing elements, blow-out preventer seals, where resistance to hydrocarbons and H$_2$S is critical. - **Industrial rollers & printing:** NBR rollers resist printing-ink solvents. - **Medical:** Powder-free nitrile examination gloves (latex-allergy free, puncture resistant). - **Aerospace:** HNBR (hydrogenated grade) for hydraulic seals operating up to **150 °C**. - **Food-contact (special grades):** Conveyor belts, milking inflations. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Glass Transition Temperature by the Fox Equation The **Fox equation** is the workhorse relation KTU examiners use to estimate copolymer $T_g$ from monomer $T_g$ values and composition. **Starting premise:** Free volume contributions from each monomer are additive in the copolymer. $$ \frac{1}{T_g} \;=\; \frac{w_{ACN}}{T_{g,ACN}} \;+\; \frac{w_{BD}}{T_{g,BD}} $$ **Step-by-step transformation to the linear weighted-average form:** Multiplying numerator and denominator by $T_g \cdot T_{g,ACN} \cdot T_{g,BD}$ and rearranging: $$\begin{aligned} T_g \cdot T_{g,BD} &\;=\; w_{ACN}\,T_g + w_{BD}\,T_g\,\dfrac{T_{g,BD}}{T_{g,ACN}}\\[4pt] \text{Since } w_{BD} &\;=\; 1 - w_{ACN}, \text{ we obtain:}\\[4pt] T_g &\;=\; T_{g,ACN}\,w_{ACN} \;+\; T_{g,BD}\,(1 - w_{ACN})\\[4pt] T_g &\;=\; T_{g,BD} \;+\; (T_{g,ACN} - T_{g,BD})\,w_{ACN} \end{aligned}$$ This is a **straight-line equation** with slope $(T_{g,ACN} - T_{g,BD}) = 190\ ^\circ\text{C}$ — useful for plotting $T_g$ versus ACN content. **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style):** A medium-grade NBR contains **34 wt%** acrylonitrile. Estimate its $T_g$. $$ \frac{1}{T_g} \;=\; \frac{0.34}{378\ \text{K}} \;+\; \frac{0.66}{188\ \text{K}} $$ Converting temperatures to Kelvin: $T_{g,ACN} = 105 + 273 = 378\ \text{K}$ and $T_{g,BD} = -85 + 273 = 188\ \text{K}$. $$\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{T_g} &\;=\; 8.994\times10^{-4} \;+\; 3.511\times10^{-3}\\[4pt] \frac{1}{T_g} &\;=\; 4.410\times10^{-3}\ \text{K}^{-1}\\[4pt] T_g &\;=\; \dfrac{1}{4.410\times10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 226.8\ \text{K}\\[4pt] T_g &\;=\; 226.8 - 273 \;=\; \mathbf{-46.2\ ^\circ C} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** The copolymer $T_g$ is intermediate between the two monomers and depends linearly on weight fraction. Higher ACN content pushes $T_g$ toward room temperature and removes flexibility. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mayo–Lewis Copolymer Composition Equation For a free-radical copolymerization with monomers $\text{M}_1$ (ACN) and $\text{M}_2$ (BD), the **instantaneous mole fraction** of $\text{M}_1$ in the polymer ($F_1$) is: $$ F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{r_1 f_1^{\,2} + f_1 f_2}{r_1 f_1^{\,2} + 2\,f_1 f_2 + r_2 f_2^{\,2}} $$ where $f_1$ and $f_2$ are the **feed** mole fractions, and $r_1$, $r_2$ are reactivity ratios. **Step-by-step derivation of the Mayo–Lewis terminal model:** Let the four possible propagation rate constants be: $$\begin{aligned} \text{M}_1^\bullet + \text{M}_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{11}} \text{M}_1^\bullet\\ \text{M}_1^\bullet + \text{M}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{12}} \text{M}_2^\bullet\\ \text{M}_2^\bullet + \text{M}_2 &\xrightarrow{k_{22}} \text{M}_2^\bullet\\ \text{M}_2^\bullet + \text{M}_1 &\xrightarrow{k_{21}} \text{M}_1^\bullet \end{aligned}$$ with reactivity ratios $r_1 = k_{11}/k_{12}$ and $r_2 = k_{22}/k_{21}$. Rates of adding each monomer: $$\begin{aligned} R_{11} &\;=\; k_{11}[\text{M}_1^\bullet][\text{M}_1]\\ R_{12} &\;=\; k_{12}[\text{M}_1^\bullet][\text{M}_2]\\ R_{21} &\;=\; k_{21}[\text{M}_2^\bullet][\text{M}_1]\\ R_{22} &\;=\; k_{22}[\text{M}_2^\bullet][\text{M}_2] \end{aligned}$$ Steady-state for each radical type (cross-propagation equal): $$ k_{12}[\text{M}_1^\bullet][\text{M}_2] \;=\; k_{21}[\text{M}_2^\bullet][\text{M}_1] $$ Therefore the ratio of $\text{M}_1^\bullet$ to $\text{M}_2^\bullet$ radical concentrations: $$ \frac{[\text{M}_1^\bullet]}{[\text{M}_2^\bullet]} \;=\; \frac{k_{21}[\text{M}_1]}{k_{12}[\text{M}_2]} $$ The mole fraction of $\text{M}_1$ entering the polymer is: $$ F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Rate of M}_1\text{ addition}}{\text{Total rate}} \;=\; \dfrac{R_{11}+R_{21}}{R_{11}+R_{12}+R_{21}+R_{22}} $$ Substituting and dividing numerator and denominator by $k_{12}[\text{M}_1^\bullet][\text{M}_2]$: $$ F_1 \;=\; \dfrac{r_1\,[\text{M}_1]/[\text{M}_2] + 1}{r_1\,[\text{M}_1]/[\text{M}_2] + 2 + r_2\,[\text{M}_2]/[\text{M}_1]} $$ Converting to mole fractions $f_i = [\text{M}_i]/([\text{M}_1]+[\text{M}_2])$ yields the standard Mayo–Lewis form stated above. **Numerical Check for NBR at $f_1 = 0.34$ (feed ACN):** $$ r_1 = 0.02,\quad r_2 = 0.30,\quad f_1 = 0.34,\quad f_2 = 0.66 $$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Numerator} &\;=\; (0.02)(0.34)^2 + (0.34)(0.66) \;=\; 0.00231 + 0.22440 \;=\; 0.22671\\[4pt] \text{Denominator} &\;=\; 0.00231 + 2(0.34)(0.66) + (0.30)(0.66)^2\\ &\;=\; 0.00231 + 0.44880 + 0.13068 \;=\; 0.58179\\[4pt] F_1 &\;=\; \dfrac{0.22671}{0.58179} \;\approx\; 0.3897 \;\approx\; \mathbf{39\ mol\%} \end{aligned}$$ The polymer is **richer in ACN than the feed** because $r_1 < 1$ — ACN is more reactive than BD toward both propagating radicals. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rubber Elastic Modulus from Cross-link Density For an ideal rubber, the **small-strain shear modulus** $G$ is related to the cross-link density $n$ (mol of active chains per m³) by: $$ G \;=\; n\,RT $$ For uniaxial extension of an incompressible elastomer, the **Young's modulus** is: $$ E \;=\; 3\,G \;=\; 3\,n\,RT $$ **Numerical worked example (KTU-style design problem):** A vulcanized NBR O-ring has a cross-link density of $n = 1.5 \times 10^2\ \text{mol/m}^3$ at $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C} = 298\ \text{K}$. Estimate its Young's modulus. $$\begin{aligned} E &\;=\; 3\,n\,RT\\[4pt] &\;=\; 3 \times (1.5\times10^{2}) \times 8.314 \times 298\\[4pt] &\;=\; 3 \times 150 \times 2478.97\\[4pt] &\;=\; \mathbf{1.12 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}} \;=\; \mathbf{1.12\ MPa} \end{aligned}$$ **Interpretation:** This modulus is consistent with a soft engineering rubber (typical NBR Shore A 50 ≈ 2 – 5 MPa). Real rubbers deviate because of **non-Gaussian chain finite extensibility**, **filler reinforcement**, and **chain entanglement contributions** that must be added as empirical correction terms. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Oil Swell Volume Fraction and Cross-link Density From **Flory–Rehner equilibrium swelling theory**, the volume fraction of rubber in the swollen gel, $\phi_2$, is related to cross-link density $n$ by: $$ \ln(1-\phi_2) \;+\; \phi_2 \;+\; \chi\,\phi_2^{\,2} \;=\; -n\,V_1\,\phi_2^{\,1/3} $$ where $V_1$ is the molar volume of the swelling solvent and $\chi$ is the **Flory–Huggins interaction parameter** (oil-rubber compatibility). **Step-by-step numerical example for NBR in ASTM Oil No. 3:** Given: - $\phi_2 = 0.40$ (typical for medium-NBR after 70 h at 100 °C) - $\chi = 0.45$ (good compatibility → moderate swell) - $V_1 = 350\ \text{cm}^3/\text{mol}$ (oil average molar volume) $$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &\;=\; \ln(1-0.40) + 0.40 + 0.45(0.40)^2\\ &\;=\; \ln(0.60) + 0.40 + 0.072\\ &\;=\; -0.5108 + 0.40 + 0.072\\ &\;=\; -0.0388\\[4pt] \text{RHS bracket} &\;=\; \phi_2^{\,1/3} = 0.40^{1/3} = 0.7368\\[4pt] -0.0388 &\;=\; -n \times 350 \times 0.7368\\[4pt] n &\;=\; \dfrac{0.0388}{350 \times 0.7368}\\[4pt] &\;=\; \dfrac{0.0388}{257.88}\\[4pt] &\;=\; 1.504 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{mol/cm}^3\\[4pt] &\;=\; \mathbf{1.50 \times 10^{2}\ \text{mol/m}^3} \end{aligned}$$ This matches the independently calculated cross-link density from modulus ($E = 3nRT$) — confirming internal consistency of the characterization data. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Chemical Structure of NBR ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Acrylonitrile Monomer
CH2 CH CN] --> nodeB[Radical
Initiation] nodeC[Butadiene Monomer
CH2 CH CH CH2] --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeD[Growing Chain Radical] nodeD --> nodeE{Next Monomer} nodeE -->|random addition| nodeF[Copolymer Repeat Unit
CH2 CH CN and CH2 CH CH CH2] nodeF --> nodeG[Polymerization Termination] nodeG --> nodeH[Final NBR Macromolecule
random sequence of polar and non-polar units] style nodeA fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style nodeC fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style nodeH fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 ``` ## 4.2 Effect of ACN Content on Property Balance ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeLow[Low ACN 18 to 24 percent] nodeMed[Medium ACN 28 to 35 percent] nodeHigh[High ACN 38 to 45 percent] nodeLow --> nodeA[Best Low Temp Flexibility
Tg near minus 60 C] nodeLow --> nodeB[Poor Oil Resistance] nodeMed --> nodeC[Balanced Performance
Tg near minus 30 C] nodeMed --> nodeD[Good Oil and Fuel Resistance] nodeHigh --> nodeE[Maximum Oil Resistance
Tg near minus 15 C] nodeHigh --> nodeF[Stiff Hard Plastic like] style nodeLow fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style nodeMed fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14 style nodeHigh fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of NBR Vulcanization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Input["Raw Materials on Two Roll Mill or Internal Mixer"] nodeI1[NBR Polymer 100 phr] nodeI2[Carbon Black Filler 30 to 80 phr] nodeI3[Plasticizer 5 to 30 phr] nodeI4[Activator ZnO plus Stearic Acid] nodeI5[Accelerator CBS plus TMTD] nodeI6[Cross linker Sulfur 0.5 to 2 phr] nodeI7[Antioxidant 6PPD] end Input --> nodeM1[Masterbatch Mixing 60 to 80 C] nodeM1 --> nodeM2[Adding Curatives below 110 C] nodeM2 --> nodeM3[Shaping Extrusion or Compression] nodeM3 --> nodeM4[Vulcanization Press 160 C 10 to 30 min] nodeM4 --> nodeOut[Cured NBR Product O ring Hose Seal] style nodeOut fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px style nodeM4 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Property–Structure–Performance Mapping (NEP 2020 Skill Outcome) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeStruc[Molecular Structure
Random Copolymer ACN plus BD] --> nodeProp1[Polar CN Groups] nodeStruc --> nodeProp2[Residual C double bond C from Butadiene] nodeProp1 --> nodePerf1[Oil and Fuel Resistance] nodeProp1 --> nodePerf2[High Tensile Strength] nodeProp2 --> nodePerf3[Sulfur Vulcanizability] nodePerf1 --> nodeApp1[Automotive Seals Hoses] nodePerf2 --> nodeApp2[Industrial Rollers Belts] nodePerf3 --> nodeApp3[O rings Gaskets] style nodeStruc fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd style nodePerf1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style nodePerf2 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 style nodePerf3 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]** **Define Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber (NBR). Explain the role of acrylonitrile content in determining the oil resistance of NBR.** **Model Answer (expected length: ~12 lines, 3 marks):** Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber (NBR) is a synthetic random copolymer of acrylonitrile (CH$_2$=CH–C≡N) and butadiene (CH$_2$=CH–CH=CH$_2$), produced by **emulsion free-radical polymerization** [1 mark]. The acrylonitrile (ACN) content, typically 18 – 50 % by weight, is the **primary structural variable** controlling oil resistance [1 mark]. The strongly polar –C≡N groups in the polymer backbone create strong **dipole–dipole intermolecular attractions**, which raise the **solubility parameter ($\delta$)** of the polymer from ~17 MPa$^{1/2}$ (polybutadiene) to ~20 – 25 MPa$^{1/2}$ in NBR. Since petroleum oils have $\delta \approx 14 - 17\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$, the **thermodynamic affinity decreases** as ACN content rises. Therefore, **higher ACN content → lower oil swell, higher oil resistance** [1 mark]. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]** **List any four typical applications of NBR and state the property responsible for each application.** **Model Answer (expected length: ~10 lines, 3 marks):** | # | Application | Responsible Property | |---|-------------|----------------------| | 1 | Fuel hoses in automobiles | **Oil and hydrocarbon resistance** | | 2 | Disposable examination gloves (medical) | **Tear strength + hypo-allergenicity** | | 3 | O-rings in hydraulic systems | **Oil resistance + elasticity** | | 4 | Printing and coating rollers | **Solvent resistance + abrasion resistance** | *(Any four applications with one matching property each = 3 marks; ½ mark for each correct pair)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Regulation:** Every Part B question carries **14 marks** and **offers internal choice**. Both options A and B are provided below for the topic "Acrylonitrile Butadiene Rubber". --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — NBR Synthesis, Structure & Property Relationships** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]** **(a)** Describe the **emulsion co-polymerization process** for the manufacture of NBR. Highlight the role of each ingredient used. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss in detail how the **acrylonitrile content** influences the following properties of NBR: (i) oil resistance, (ii) glass transition temperature, (iii) tensile strength, and (iv) low-temperature flexibility. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks **Emulsion co-polymerization of NBR — Detailed Steps (each step = 1 mark; logical flow & ingredient roles = balance):** **Step 1 — Monomer Preparation (1 mark):** Butadiene (a gas at room temperature, b.p. = −4.4 °C) and acrylonitrile (liquid, b.p. = 77 °C) are mixed in the desired weight ratio (typically 34 % ACN for medium grade) and emulsified in de-ionized water using **soap surfactants** such as **sodium rosinate** or **potassium oleate** at a concentration of 2 – 5 %. **Step 2 — Reactor Charging (1 mark):** The emulsion is charged into a stirred, jacketed, stainless-steel reactor. Temperature is maintained at **5 – 10 °C** using brine cooling because the reaction is highly exothermic ($\Delta H \approx -84$ kJ/mol butadiene). **Step 3 — Initiator System (1 mark):** A **redox initiator** is used, typically **potassium persulfate (K$_2$S$_2$O$_8$) as oxidant** combined with **sodium formaldehyde sulfoxylate (SFS) as reductant**, often activated by **ferrous sulfate (FeSO$_4$)** as catalyst and **EDTA as chelator**. This generates sulfate radicals •SO$_4^-$ in the aqueous phase at low temperature. **Step 4 — Chain Propagation (1 mark):** Sulfate radicals diffuse into monomer-swollen micelles and add to a monomer double bond. The chain grows by **random sequential addition** of ACN and BD monomers, controlled by reactivity ratios ($r_{ACN} \approx 0.02$, $r_{BD} \approx 0.30$). **Step 5 — Molecular Weight Control (1 mark):** A **chain transfer agent (CTA)** — typically **tert-dodecyl mercaptan (TDM)** at 0.1 – 0.5 phr — is added to regulate molecular weight to the target range (MW ≈ 200,000 – 400,000 g/mol). **Step 6 — Short-stopping & Degassing (1 mark):** At ~60 – 80 % monomer conversion, the reaction is terminated with a **short-stop** (e.g., hydroquinone or sodium nitrite) and unreacted butadiene is vented and recycled. Residual acrylonitrile is stripped under vacuum. **Step 7 — Coagulation, Washing & Drying (1 mark):** The latex is **coagulated** by adding acid (e.g., H$_2$SO$_4$) or alum [Al$_2$(SO$_4$)$_3$]. The crumb is **washed** to remove soap and salts, then **dried** in a fluidized-bed or apron dryer at 80 – 110 °C. The final product is baled NBR rubber with ACN content ±1 % of the target. **Role of each ingredient (summary table for extra clarity):** | Ingredient | Function | |------------|----------| | Acrylonitrile | Provides polarity, oil resistance, strength | | Butadiene | Provides elasticity, flexibility | | Water | Continuous phase, heat transfer medium | | Soap/surfactant | Forms micelles, site of polymerization | | Redox initiator | Generates radicals at low temperature | | Mercaptan (TDM) | Molecular weight regulator | | Short-stop | Terminates polymerization at desired conversion | | Coagulant | Destabilizes latex to recover solid rubber | --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **(i) Oil Resistance (2 marks):** The nitrile group –C≡N is **strongly polar** (dipole moment ≈ 3.6 D). As ACN content rises from 18 % to 50 %, the **inter-chain cohesive energy density (CED)** increases and the **solubility parameter $\delta$** rises from ~18 to ~25 MPa$^{1/2}$. Since petroleum oils have $\delta \approx 14 - 17\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$, the **thermodynamic driving force for swelling ($\Delta G_{mix}$)** becomes less negative (or even positive) — and oil penetration is reduced. **Test data:** A 50 % ACN NBR swells only ~5 – 10 % in ASTM Oil No. 3 at 100 °C for 70 h, whereas a 20 % ACN grade swells ~40 – 60 %. **(ii) Glass Transition Temperature (2 marks):** Per the **Fox equation**, $T_g$ rises linearly with ACN weight fraction. $T_g$ increases from approximately **−60 °C** (18 % ACN) to **−15 °C** (50 % ACN) because the rigid –C≡N groups restrict segmental motion. Above 50 % ACN, $T_g$ approaches ambient temperature and the material loses rubbery character. **(iii) Tensile Strength (1.5 marks):** Strong dipole–dipole interactions between nitrile groups act as **physical cross-links**, raising **cohesive strength**. Tensile strength rises from ~7 MPa (low-ACN) to ~25 MPa (high-ACN) before vulcanization even begins; after sulfur curing, the synergistic effect gives up to **30 MPa** in filled compounds. **(iv) Low-Temperature Flexibility (1.5 marks):** Because $T_g$ rises with ACN content, the **service temperature window** shrinks at the cold end. Low-ACN grades remain flexible down to **−40 °C or below**, while high-ACN grades stiffen at **−10 to −15 °C**. Therefore **ACN content must be optimized** for each application — a 33 % grade is a good compromise for general engineering seals. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — NBR Compounding, Vulcanization & Applications** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]** **(a)** With a suitable formulation, explain the **vulcanization of NBR** using the sulfur system. Discuss the role of each compounding ingredient. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the properties and applications of **NBR, HNBR, and NBR/PVC blend**. Justify why NBR remains the preferred choice for fuel hoses and O-rings despite the availability of newer elastomers. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks **Standard NBR Vulcanization Formulation (1 mark):** | Ingredient | phr | Function | |------------|-----|----------| | NBR (33 % ACN, ML 1+4 = 50) | 100 | Base polymer | | Zinc oxide (ZnO) | 5 | **Activator** (forms ZnS intermediate) | | Stearic acid | 1.5 | **Co-activator** (solubilizes ZnO as zinc stearate) | | Carbon black (N550 / FEF) | 50 | **Reinforcing filler** | | Dioctyl phthalate (DOP) | 10 | **Plasticizer** (improves processability, lowers $T_g$) | | Sulfur | 1.5 | **Cross-linker** | | CBS (accelerator) | 1.0 | **Primary accelerator** (delayed action, scorch safety) | | TMTD (accelerator) | 0.3 | **Secondary accelerator** (boosts cure rate) | | 6PPD antioxidant | 1.5 | **Anti-ageing** (resists ozone & flex cracking) | | **Total** | **~172 phr** | | **Vulcanization Mechanism (3 marks):** 1. At mixing temperature (< 110 °C), CBS dissociates to form **2-mercaptobenzothiazole (MBT)** which complexes with **Zn$^{2+}$** from zinc stearate. 2. Above ~140 °C in the press, the complex reacts with sulfur to form an **active sulfurating agent** (e.g., MBT–S$_x$–Cl). 3. This agent transfers sulfur to the polymer allylic positions (–CH$_2$–CH=CH–CH–), forming **polysulfidic cross-links** (C–S$_x$–C, where $x = 2$ – 6). 4. With efficient vulcanization (EV) systems using TMTD, the cross-links evolve to **mono- and di-sulfidic** structures that give superior heat-ageing resistance. **Cure conditions (1 mark):** Press cure at **160 °C** for **$t_{90}$ + 2 min** (where $t_{90}$ is the time to reach 90 % of maximum torque, typically 8 – 12 min as determined by a Moving Die Rheometer at 160 °C, ASTM D 5289). **Role of Each Ingredient (2 marks — concise table format accepted by KTU valuators):** - **ZnO + stearic acid → zinc stearate:** Activates accelerator; promotes cross-link formation. - **Sulfur:** Primary cross-linker; each sulfur atom bridges two polymer chains. - **CBS:** Delayed-action accelerator; prevents scorch during processing. - **TMTD:** Ultra-accelerator; gives fast cure and high cross-link density. - **Carbon black:** Reinforcement — increases tensile, modulus, abrasion resistance. - **DOP:** Lowers $T_g$ and viscosity; improves low-temperature flexibility. - **6PPD:** Protects against ozone cracking (reacts with O$_3$ before it attacks C=C). --- #### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks **Comparative Property Table (3 marks):** | Property | NBR | HNBR | NBR/PVC Blend | |----------|-----|------|---------------| | ACN content range | 18 – 50 % | 18 – 50 % | 18 – 50 % | | **Tensile strength (MPa)** | 7 – 25 | 15 – 35 | 10 – 25 | | **Elongation at break (%)** | 300 – 600 | 200 – 400 | 200 – 400 | | **Service temperature (°C)** | −30 to +110 | −30 to +150 | −25 to +110 | | **Oil resistance** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | | **Ozone resistance** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent (PVC protects) | | **Hot air ageing** | Moderate | Excellent | Good | | **Cost (relative)** | 1× (baseline) | 2 – 3× | 1.2 – 1.5× | | **Key limitation** | Poor ozone/weathering | High cost | Processability, brittle | **Why NBR Remains Preferred for Fuel Hoses & O-Rings (4 marks):** 1. **Superior cost-performance ratio:** NBR delivers > 90 % of HNBR's oil resistance at ~50 % of the price — critical for high-volume automotive parts. 2. **Proven long-term reliability:** 80+ years of field data; stable supply chain. 3. **Wide ACN grade selection:** Allows precise property tuning per application. 4. **Excellent processability:** Low Mooney viscosity variants enable injection molding of complex O-ring geometries. 5. **Compatibility with broad additive packages:** Easy to compound for flame retardance, conductivity, food contact. 6. **Recyclability:** Devulcanization of NBR is well established; HNBR recycling is more difficult. 7. **HNBR is reserved** for *high-temperature under-bonnet* (e.g., turbocharger hoses, timing-chain seals) where NBR cannot survive > 120 °C service. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NBR Answers (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > > 1. **❌ Writing "NBR is a homopolymer of butadiene."** → 1 full mark lost. NBR is a **copolymer**; always state both monomers. > 2. **❌ Forgetting to mention emulsion polymerization.** → KTU expects the manufacturing route to be specified. > 3. **❌ Stating "ACN content increases flexibility."** → This is the **inverse** of the truth. Always say: *ACN ↑ → oil resistance ↑, low-temperature flexibility ↓.* > 4. **❌ Omitting the function of ZnO + stearic acid** → They form **zinc stearate** — the actual activator. Many students write only "activator" without the chemistry. > 5. **❌ Confusing NBR with HNBR in service-temperature questions.** → NBR max continuous = **110 °C**; HNBR = **150 °C**. Examiners test this distinction. > 6. **❌ Skipping units in numerical problems** (e.g., giving $T_g$ without °C or K). → ½ mark lost per omission. > 7. **❌ Drawing NBR as a regular alternating ABAB structure.** → NBR is a **random** copolymer; the diagram must reflect randomness. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🧠 Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU revision) - **Definition:** NBR = copolymer of **acrylonitrile (ACN)** + **butadiene (BD)**, made by **emulsion polymerization** at **5 – 10 °C**. - **ACN range:** **18 – 50 wt%**; standard medium grade = **34 %**. - **Most important variable:** **ACN content** controls the entire property balance. - **Mnemonic:** **"More ACN → More Oil resistance, More Strength, More Hardness — but Less Flexibility."** - **Glass transition:** Use **Fox equation**: $\dfrac{1}{T_g} = \dfrac{w_{ACN}}{T_{g,ACN}} + \dfrac{w_{BD}}{T_{g,BD}}$ - **Solubility parameter:** NBR $\delta \approx 18 - 25\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$ (rises with ACN); oils $\delta \approx 14 - 17\ \text{MPa}^{1/2}$. - **Vulcanization:** **Sulfur + CBS + TMTD + ZnO + stearic acid**, cured at **~160 °C for $t_{90}$ + 2 min.** - **Standard fillers:** **Carbon black N550** (40 – 80 phr); **DOP** plasticizer (5 – 30 phr). - **Service temperature:** **−30 °C to +110 °C** (NBR); up to **+150 °C** for HNBR. - **Top applications:** **Fuel hoses, O-rings, hydraulic seals, gaskets, nitrile gloves, printing rollers.** - **Reactivity ratios:** $r_{ACN} \approx 0.02$, $r_{BD} \approx 0.30$ → ACN is more reactive → polymer richer in ACN than feed. - **Cross-link density → modulus:** $E = 3 n R T$. - **Oil swell test:** **ASTM D 471** — report as **swell %** after 70 h at 100 °C in ASTM Oil No. 3. - **NOT resistant to:** ozone, strong oxidizing acids, ketones (acetone, MEK), chlorinated hydrocarbons (unless ACN > 40 %). - **Grades to remember:** **Low-Nitrile (18 – 24 %), Medium (28 – 35 %), High (38 – 45 %), Very-High (45 – 50 %).** - **Trade names:** Perbunan, Nipol, Chemigum, Buna-N, Krynac, Europrene N. - **Comparison cousins:** **HNBR** (hydrogenated NBR — better heat/ozone), **NBR/PVC blend** (better flame/ozone), **XNBR** (carboxylated — better abrasion). ### 🎯 KTU 2024 CO Mapping (Outcome-Based Education) | Question Part | KTU Course Outcome (CO) Addressed | Bloom's Level | |---------------|-----------------------------------|---------------| | Define NBR & role of ACN | CO1 – Fundamentals | Remember | | List applications & property match | CO2 – Property mapping | Understand | | Emulsion polymerization steps | CO3 – Processing knowledge | Apply | | ACN vs property relationship | CO4 – Design selection | Analyze | | Vulcanization formulation | CO3 – Processing | Apply | | NBR vs HNBR vs NBR/PVC | CO5 – Material selection | Evaluate | ---

topics.title: Chloroprene Rubber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Chloroprene Rubber

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Chloroprene Rubber (CR)**, also commercially known by its original DuPont trade name **Neoprene**, is a synthetic elastomer produced by the free-radical emulsion polymerization of the monomer **chloroprene (2-chloro-1,3-butadiene)**. It belongs to the family of **specialty synthetic rubbers** and was the first commercially successful synthetic elastomer, introduced in 1931.

The polymerization of chloroprene yields a polymer with a predominantly **trans-1,4-polychloroprene** microstructure, interspersed with cis-1,4, 1,2, and 3,4 isomeric units. The presence of the electronegative chlorine atom on the polymer backbone imparts a unique balance of mechanical, chemical, and flame-retardant properties.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chloroprene Rubber is a key elastomer in the "Specialty Rubbers" segment of Module 4. Examiners frequently test its **monomer chemistry, curing mechanism (metal oxide vulcanization), and applications in adverse service environments**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Flame-Fighting Rubber Jacket"**
>
> Imagine natural rubber (NR) as a plain cotton t-shirt — comfortable, stretchy, but easily torn by sunlight, dissolved by oils, and burns quickly. **Chloroprene Rubber is like a high-tech, flame-retardant, oil-resistant tactical jacket**. The chlorine atoms act like built-in chemical bodyguards: they shield the molecular chain from ozone attack, slow down burning (self-extinguishing), and provide moderate oil/chemical resistance. This is exactly why your **wetsuit, diving booties, and laptop sleeve** (often made of Neoprene) survive saltwater, sun, and rough use for years.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Value (Typical) |
|---|---|
| **Monomer (Chloroprene) Molecular Weight** | **88.54 g/mol** |
| **Polymer Specific Gravity** | **1.23 g/cm³** |
| **Chlorine Content (in polymer)** | **~40% by weight** |
| **Glass Transition Temperature (Tg)** | **−45 °C to −50 °C** |
