## 4.5 Functional Description of Seal Assembly (Text Schematic)

A typical **radial O-ring seal assembly** consists of the following interacting elements, listed in order of fluid contact:

| Layer (from fluid side) | Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mating shaft / piston | Hardened steel | Rotates/translates |
| 2 | O-ring | Elastomer (NBR/FKM) | Sealing element |
| 3 | Groove / Gland | Same metal as housing | Houses O-ring |
| 4 | Back-up ring (optional) | PTFE / Nylon | Anti-extrusion |
| 5 | Housing | Steel / aluminum | Structural support |
| 6 | Retainer / end cap | Metal | Axial positioning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always specify the **back-up ring** in designs where system pressure > 10 MPa OR clearance > 0.15 mm — this is the single most missed item in KTU design answers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🟦 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** 
*Define "compression set" of a rubber seal. Why is it considered the most important indicator of seal life?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Compression set (CS) is the permanent deformation remaining in a rubber specimen after it has been subjected to a specified compressive strain for a fixed time and temperature, then allowed to recover. It is mathematically defined (per **ASTM D395, Method B**) as:

$$CS \;=\; \frac{h_0 - h_2}{h_0 - h_1} \times 100\%$$

where $h_0$ = original thickness, $h_1$ = thickness during compression, and $h_2$ = thickness after recovery.

**Why it governs seal life (2 marks):** 
The sealing force of a rubber O-ring originates from its **stored elastic strain energy** in the compressed state. When CS increases, this stored energy decays because the rubber's cross-linked network **partially rearranges** under prolonged stress and temperature. The contact pressure $P_c$ drops below the fluid pressure $P_{fluid}$, and leakage occurs. Therefore, CS is the **direct measure of residual sealing ability**, and KTU industry standards consider CS > 25% as "end-of-life" for most service O-rings.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** 
*List four elastomers commonly used in rubber seals and state one typical sealing application for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Elastomer | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NBR (Nitrile Rubber)** | Hydraulic cylinder O-rings, automotive fuel system seals (oil & fuel resistance) |
| 2 | **FKM (Viton® / Fluoroelastomer)** | Aircraft hydraulic seals, chemical plant O-rings (high-temp & chemical resistance) |
| 3 | **EPDM (Ethylene-Propylene-Diene)** | Hot water/steam systems, automotive brake systems (steam & weather resistance) |
| 4 | **VMQ (Silicone)** | Food/pharmaceutical tri-clamp gaskets, medical device seals (biocompatibility, wide temp range) |

*Any other valid pairings (HNBR, CR, FFKM) accepted.*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> **Note for KTU 2024:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You must attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Repeated Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Explain the classification of rubber seals with neat sketches. Differentiate between static and dynamic seals with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

**Classification of Rubber Seals (4 marks):**

Rubber seals are classified into three broad categories:

1. **Based on type of motion:**
   - **Static Seals** — No relative motion between mating parts. Examples: O-rings in pipe flanges, gaskets in pump covers, head gaskets in engines.
   - **Dynamic Seals** — Continuous relative motion. Further divided into:
     - **Reciprocating seals** — U-cups, V-packings, rod wipers in hydraulic cylinders.
     - **Rotary seals** — Lip seals (oil seals) on rotating shafts, rotary O-rings.
   - **Hybrid (Limited motion)** — Allow small movements (thermal expansion, vibration). Example: O-rings in hydraulic cylinder glands.

2. **Based on loading direction:**
   - **Radial Seals** — Load applied radially inward; the seal is squeezed between shaft and bore (most common).
   - **Axial / Face Seals** — Load applied along the axis; common in end caps and flanges.

3. **Based on geometry:**
   - **O-rings** (circular cross-section)
   - **D-rings, X-rings (quad rings)** (modified cross-section)
   - **U-cups, V-packings** (lip-type)
   - **Lip seals / oil seals**
   - **Diaphragms, gaskets**

**Static vs Dynamic Seals (3 marks):**

| Feature | Static Seal | Dynamic Seal |
|---|---|---|
| Motion | None | Reciprocating or rotary |
| Squeeze | Higher (18–25%) | Lower (8–18%) to limit heat |
| Friction | Not a concern | Critical (affects wear & power loss) |
| Lubrication | Not required | Essential (grease or fluid film) |
| Failure mode | Compression set, chemical attack | Wear, extrusion, friction heat |
| Example | Flange O-ring | Shaft lip seal |

*(Sketches expected: a simple cross-section showing O-ring in gland for static; U-cup on rod for dynamic.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *An NBR 70 Shore A O-ring with cross-sectional diameter 5.33 mm is to be used as a radial static seal on a 50 mm shaft. The recommended squeeze is 20%. Calculate:*
*(i) The bore diameter* 
*(ii) The gland fill assuming a square groove of 6.0 mm width.*
*(iii) The approximate contact pressure developed.*
*(iv) Comment on the suitability for sealing a fluid at 0.8 MPa pressure.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** d = 5.33 mm, D<sub>s</sub> = 50 mm, S = 20% = 0.20, E (NBR 70 Shore A) = 7.0 MPa, square groove width w = 6.0 mm, P<sub>fluid</sub> = 0.8 MPa.

**(i) Bore diameter (2 marks):**

The uncompressed radial gap with no squeeze is the O-ring diameter:
$$\frac{D_b - D_s}{2} = d$$

With 20% squeeze, the radial gap reduces to:
$$t = d(1 - S) = 5.33 \times (1 - 0.20) = 4.264 \text{ mm}$$

Therefore bore:
$$D_b = D_s + 2t = 50 + 2(4.264) = 50 + 8.528 = 58.53 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Gland fill (2 marks):**

O-ring cross-section area (circle):
$$A_{OR} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (5.33)^2}{4} = 22.31 \text{ mm}^2$$

Square groove cross-section area:
$$A_{groove} = w \times t = 6.0 \times 4.264 = 25.58 \text{ mm}^2$$

Gland fill:
$$GF = \frac{A_{OR}}{A_{groove}} \times 100 = \frac{22.31}{25.58} \times 100 \approx 87.2\%$$

This is within the recommended **85–90%** range for static radial seals. ✅

**(iii) Contact pressure (1.5 marks):**
$$P_c = 0.4 \cdot E \cdot S = 0.4 \times 7.0 \times 0.20 = 0.56 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iv) Suitability (1.5 marks):**

Since $P_c$ = 0.56 MPa < $P_{fluid}$ = 0.8 MPa, **the seal will leak** under the given fluid pressure. The design must be revised by either:
- Increasing squeeze to S = 0.30 (giving P<sub>c</sub> = 0.84 MPa) ✅
- Selecting a harder rubber (90 Shore A, E ≈ 14 MPa, giving P<sub>c</sub> = 1.12 MPa) ✅
- Adding a back-up ring to share the load.

**Verdict:** As designed, the O-ring is **NOT suitable** for 0.8 MPa service.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** *Discuss the design considerations for an O-ring seal with respect to: (i) Material selection, (ii) Squeeze ratio, (iii) Gland fill, and (iv) Stretch.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Material Selection (2 marks):**

Material choice is the single most influential design decision. Key criteria are:
- **Temperature range** — Service T must lie between T<sub>g</sub> (glass transition) and decomposition T. For NBR: −40 to +120 °C; for FKM: −25 to +230 °C.
- **Chemical compatibility** — Use compatibility charts (Parker, Appleby, or Rapra). E.g., NBR fails in ketones and strong acids.
- **Pressure rating** — Higher P requires harder compounds (90 Shore A) or back-up rings.
- **Standards** — FDA, USP Class VI, API, NACE for food/medical/oil-gas service.
- **Cost & availability** — NBR is cheapest; FFKM is 100× costlier.

**(ii) Squeeze Ratio (2 marks):**

Squeeze is the radial compression of the O-ring cross-section as a percentage of the free diameter. Recommended values:
- Static radial: 18–25%
- Static axial: 20–30%
- Dynamic reciprocating: 10–18%
- Rotary: 5–10% (minimize friction heat)

Too little squeeze → leakage; too much → compression set, splitting, and extrusion.

**(iii) Gland Fill (1.5 marks):**

Ratio of O-ring area to groove cross-section area. Optimal:
- Static: 85–90%
- Dynamic: 65–80% (room for thermal expansion)

Over-filled gland (GF > 95%) → O-ring trapped and splits during compression. Under-filled (GF < 70%) → ring rattles and extrudes.

**(iv) Stretch (1.5 marks):**

The O-ring's inner diameter stretches over the shaft. For a 70 Shore A NBR, stretch should not exceed **5%** (3% for stiff FKM). Excessive stretch:
- Reduces cross-section (necking)
- Lowers squeeze (since d decreases)
- Causes premature failure

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** *A static O-ring (NBR 70 Shore A) is compressed by 25% from an original thickness of 4.0 mm. After 2000 hours of service at 90 °C, the recovered thickness is 3.30 mm. Calculate the compression set. If the maximum allowable CS for service is 20%, is the seal still serviceable? If not, estimate the time at which it should have been replaced, assuming linear CS growth.*

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** h<sub>0</sub> = 4.0 mm, S = 0.25, h<sub>1</sub> = h<sub>0</sub>(1−S) = 4.0 × 0.75 = 3.0 mm, h<sub>2</sub> = 3.30 mm, service = 2000 h, T = 90 °C, CS<sub>max</sub> = 20%.

**(i) Compression set (3 marks):**
$$CS = \frac{h_0 - h_2}{h_0 - h_1} \times 100 = \frac{4.0 - 3.30}{4.0 - 3.0} \times 100 = \frac{0.70}{1.0} \times 100 = 70\%$$

**(ii) Serviceability check (2 marks):**
Since CS = 70% ≫ CS<sub>max</sub> = 20%, the O-ring is **NOT serviceable**. The seal has lost 70% of its elastic recovery and is essentially hard rubber with negligible sealing force. **Immediate replacement is required.**

**(iii) Time at which replacement was due (2 marks):**

Assuming linear CS growth:
$$\text{Rate of CS} = \frac{70\%}{2000 \text{ h}} = 0.035\%/\text{h}$$

Time to reach 20% CS:
$$t_{replace} = \frac{20\%}{0.035\%/\text{h}} \approx 571 \text{ h}$$

**The seal should have been replaced after approximately 571 hours (≈ 24 days) of continuous service.** Continuing to 2000 h caused severe over-aging and probable leakage events during the entire post-571 h period.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing compression set with compression strain** — CS is a permanent *residual* deformation, NOT the applied strain. Many students write CS = 25% (the squeeze) and get zero marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in modulus from Shore A** — Gent's equation gives E in MPa; do not leave it dimensionless.
> 3. **Wrong gland fill formula** — The ratio uses *cross-section areas*, not diameters.
> 4. **Omitting back-up rings** in high-pressure designs — KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks if a back-up is not mentioned for P > 10 MPa.
> 5. **Stretch on radial O-rings** — Stretch is on the *inner diameter over the shaft*, not the outer. Mis-statement = 0.
> 6. **Hardness of unused seals** — Always specify Shore A in 5-unit increments (70, 75, 80, 85, 90). Non-standard values like "73" are unrealistic and lose credit.
> 7. **Forgetting to verify P_c > P_fluid** — This is the **fundamental sealing inequality**; never end a design answer without it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD POINTS — must be memorized for KTU ESE:**

✅ A rubber seal seals by **maintained contact pressure** (P<sub>c</sub>) > **fluid pressure** (P<sub>fluid</sub>), NOT by adhesion.

✅ **Squeeze** $S = (d - t)/d \times 100$ — the master design variable. Static radial: **18–25%**, dynamic: **8–18%**, rotary: **5–10%**.

✅ **Gland fill** must be **85–90%** for static, **65–80%** for dynamic seals.

✅ **Stretch** ≤ 5% (NBR) and ≤ 3% (FKM) to prevent necking and reduced squeeze.

✅ **Compression set (CS)** is the single best indicator of remaining seal life. CS ≤ **25%** is acceptable per ASTM D395.

✅ **Hardness:** typical 50–90 Shore A. **Higher hardness = higher modulus = higher P<sub>c</sub> = higher friction & wear.**

✅ **Gent's correlation:** $E \approx 10^{(0.0235\,S_A - 0.6403)}$ MPa. Use this to convert Shore A to modulus.

✅ **Material selection triplet (must memorize):**
- *Oil/Fuel* → **NBR** or **HNBR** or **FKM**
- *Hot water/Steam* → **EPDM**
- *High temp/Chemical* → **FKM (Viton) or FFKM (Kalrez)**
- *Food/Pharma* → **Silicone (VMQ)**
- *Diesel/High-Temp Oil* → **HNBR or FKM**
- *Refrigerant R-134a/410A* → **HNBR or Neoprene**

✅ **O-ring extrusion** occurs at high pressure / large clearance. Fix: use **back-up ring** (PTFE / Nylon) or increase hardness to 90 Shore A.

✅ **Radial vs Axial (face) seal:** radial = perpendicular to shaft (most common in cylinders); axial = parallel to shaft (flanges, end caps).

✅ **Static vs Dynamic:** dynamic seals run at lower squeeze to control friction heat and wear; static seals maximize squeeze for sealing force.

✅ **ASTM standards** to remember: **D1414** (O-ring tension testing), **D395** (compression set), **D471** (fluid aging/swell), **D2240** (Shore A hardness).

✅ **Lubrication is mandatory for dynamic seals** — grease or fluid film — to prevent frictional heat and wear.

✅ **Maximum sealable static pressure** ≈ 4·S·E (empirical for soft NBR). For S = 0.20, E = 7 MPa: P<sub>max</sub> ≈ **5.6 MPa** static.

✅ **Always end an O-ring design answer with: (a) P_c > P_fluid verification, (b) gland fill check, (c) back-up ring mention for P > 10 MPa.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static and Dynamic Seals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Static and Dynamic Seals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Seals** are engineered polymer components designed to prevent the uncontrolled passage of fluids (liquids or gases) and/or solid contaminants between two mating surfaces, while optionally accommodating relative motion, pressure differentials, thermal expansion, and mechanical vibration.

A **Static Seal** is defined as a sealing element that operates between two mating surfaces which have **no relative motion** with respect to each other during service. Examples include flange gaskets, O-rings in stationary housings, and head gaskets in internal combustion engines.

A **Dynamic Seal** is a sealing element that operates between two mating surfaces exhibiting **continuous or intermittent relative motion** (reciprocating, rotating, or oscillating) while still performing the leakage-prevention function. Examples include hydraulic piston seals, rotary shaft seals (oil seals), and pneumatic rod seals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Static and dynamic seals together constitute **~15–20%** of Module 4 marks. Examiner emphasis is placed on (i) the distinction between static and dynamic operating regimes, (ii) polymer material selection rationale, and (iii) fundamental design equations such as compression strain, friction force, and wear rate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are filling a water bottle with a screw-on cap. The rubber washer inside the cap is a **static seal** — once the cap is tightened, the washer sits compressed between two stationary metal/plastic surfaces, and nothing moves. Its job is purely to block the water from leaking out.

Now imagine the same rubber washer but installed around the piston of a syringe. As you push and pull the plunger, the rubber has to slide against the barrel wall constantly. This is a **dynamic seal** — it must block fluid leakage **while** sliding. The challenges multiply: friction, heat generation, wear, and extrusion into gaps all come into play.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** A static seal "only needs to be still and tight." A dynamic seal "must be tight AND slippery AND durable" — three properties that are physically in tension, which is why dynamic seal design is fundamentally harder.

## 1.3 Classification Snapshot (High-Level)

**By Motion Regime:**

| Family | Relative Motion | Typical Polymers |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Static Seals | None | NBR, EPDM, FKM, PTFE, compressed non-asbestos fibre |
| Dynamic Seals | Reciprocating / Rotating / Oscillating | NBR, PU (polyurethane), FKM, PTFE, silicone |

**By Geometry:** O-rings, U-cups, V-rings, lip seals, mechanical seal faces, gaskets (flat, spiral-wound, kammprofile), T-seals, wiper seals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Memorize:** **Hardness (Shore A)** is the single most cited material property in seal design — denoted **H_S** with typical ranges **40–90 Shore A** for elastomeric seals. Lower H_S = softer = better conformability but higher friction; higher H_S = harder = better extrusion resistance but poorer sealing at low pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static Seals — Operating Principle

A static seal works on the principle of **elastic compression and contact stress generation**. When a soft elastomer (modulus **E**) is mechanically squeezed between two rigid flanges, internal contact pressure develops. As long as this contact pressure exceeds the internal fluid pressure of the system, the seal remains leak-free.

### Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Initial Assembly (No Pressure State):** The seal is installed with a deliberate interference fit; cross-section is squeezed by an amount Δh.
2. **Elastic Recovery:** The elastomer wants to spring back to its free height; this generates a baseline contact stress **σ₀ = E · ε**, where ε is the compressive strain.
3. **System Pressurization:** When fluid pressure **P_sys** is applied, it supplements the contact stress on the high-pressure side, effectively "assisting" the seal.
4. **Equilibrium Condition:** Leakage is prevented as long as the **net contact stress at every point along the seal interface ≥ system pressure**.
5. **Long-Term Concern:** Stress relaxation and compression set gradually reduce σ₀ over time → this is the primary failure mode for static seals.

### Design Parameters for Static Seals:

- **Compression ratio (squeeze):** typically **15–30%** for O-rings in static groove.
- **Compression set:** must be **< 25%** (per ASTM D395) for reliable long-term sealing.
- **Hardness:** **70 ± 5 Shore A** is the universal industry default for static O-rings.
- **Thermal expansion allowance:** groove must accommodate ~**2–4%** thermal swell of elastomer.

## 2.2 Dynamic Seals — Operating Principle

Dynamic seals must perform the sealing function under **kinetic friction** and **intermittent or continuous relative motion**. The fundamental engineering challenge is balancing three competing demands:

1. **Sealing efficiency** (high contact stress)
2. **Low friction** (to minimize energy loss and heat)
3. **Long wear life** (to maximize service interval)

### Why and How — Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Hydrodynamic Lubrication Regime:** At high speeds, a thin fluid film forms between seal and shaft — seal "floats" and friction drops, but leakage may increase.
2. **Boundary Lubrication Regime:** At low speeds / high pressures, the seal contacts the shaft directly — friction is high, wear occurs, but sealing is excellent.
3. **Mixed Lubrication:** Most real seals operate here — fluid film partially supports the load; lubricant film thickness **h_min** is comparable to the composite surface roughness **σ_composite**.
4. **Stribeck Curve:** The friction coefficient μ is plotted against the dimensionless Hersey number (η·v/P). Dynamic seal design aims to operate at or near the minimum of this curve.
5. **Frictional Heat:** Heat generation rate **Q = μ · F_n · v** must be dissipated; otherwise, temperature rises, polymer degrades, and seal fails catastrophically.

## 2.3 Failure Modes — High-Yield for KTU

> [!NOTE]
> **Static Seal Failures:**
> - Compression set (loss of elastic recovery)
> - Chemical attack / swell
> - Thermal ageing
> - Extrusion into clearance gaps
> - Improper gland filling (under- or over-fill)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Seal Failures:**
> - Abrasive wear (Archard's law)
> - Frictional overheating / thermal degradation
> - Spalling (cracking under cyclic stress)
> - Extrusion at high pressure
> - Chemical incompatibility with fluid
> - Installation damage (nicks, cuts, spiral failure)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all** key equations you must memorize for the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. Every variable, unit, and boundary condition is included for board-exam style numerical problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical Units | Applicability |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Compressive strain (squeeze) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0}$ | $h_0$ = free height, $h_1$ = installed height | dimensionless | Static & Dynamic |
| 2 | Compression set | $CS = \dfrac{h_0 - h_2}{h_0 - h_1} \times 100\%$ | $h_2$ = recovered height after unloading | % | Static (per ASTM D395) |
| 3 | Contact stress (linear elastic) | $\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | $E$ = Young's modulus | MPa | Static, low strain |
| 4 | Seal-to-pressure balance | $\sigma_c \geq P_{sys}$ | $P_{sys}$ = system pressure | MPa | Static sealing criterion |
| 5 | Friction force (Coulomb) | $F_f = \mu \cdot F_n$ | $\mu$ = friction coeff., $F_n$ = normal load | N | Dynamic |
| 6 | Friction power loss | $P_{loss} = F_f \cdot v = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$ | $v$ = sliding velocity | W | Dynamic |
| 7 | Frictional heat generation | $Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$ | — | W | Dynamic thermal analysis |
| 8 | PV limit (Pressure × Velocity) | $PV = P \cdot v$ | empirical material ceiling | MPa·m/s | Dynamic bearing/seal |
| 9 | Archard wear equation | $V_{wear} = \dfrac{k \cdot W \cdot s}{H}$ | $k$ = wear coeff., $W$ = normal load, $s$ = sliding distance, $H$ = hardness | m³ | All sliding contacts |
| 10 | Wear rate (linear) | $\dot{h} = \dfrac{k \cdot P \cdot v}{H}$ | pressure-velocity form of Archard | m/s | Dynamic seals |
| 11 | Composite surface roughness | $\sigma_c = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2}$ | RMS roughnesses of two surfaces | μm | Lubrication regime |
| 12 | Film parameter (lambda ratio) | $\Lambda = \dfrac{h_{min}}{\sigma_c}$ | $h_{min}$ = minimum film thickness | dimensionless | Stribeck analysis |
| 13 | O-ring groove fill | $V_{fill} = \dfrac{A_{seal}}{A_{groove}} \times 100\%$ | Cross-sectional areas | % | Gland design |
| 14 | Thermal expansion strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | $\alpha$ = linear expansion coefficient | — | Thermal gland design |
| 15 | Stress relaxation modulus | $E(t) = \dfrac{\sigma(t)}{\varepsilon_0}$ | time-dependent modulus | MPa | Long-term static seal |

### Real-World Engineering Utility:

These equations directly govern the design of **hydraulic cylinder piston seals in earth-moving equipment** (Komatsu, Caterpillar), **rotary shaft seals in electric motors**, **O-rings in aerospace fuel systems** (qualified per AS568), and **gaskets in chemical process piping** (ASME B16.21). Mastering them unlocks careers in fluid power, automotive, aerospace, and oil-and-gas sealing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Contact Stress Generation in a Squeezed Elastomer

**Scenario:** A cylindrical elastomeric O-ring of free cross-sectional diameter **d₀ = 5.0 mm** is installed in a rectangular groove such that its installed height is **h₁ = 4.25 mm**. The elastomer has a compressive modulus **E = 7.5 MPa**. Compute the contact stress and determine whether it can seal against a system pressure of **1.0 MPa**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify free and installed heights.**

$$h_0 = d_0 = 5.0 \text{ mm}, \quad h_1 = 4.25 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute compressive strain (squeeze).**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0} = \frac{5.0 - 4.25}{5.0} = \frac{0.75}{5.0} = 0.15$$

This corresponds to a **15% squeeze**, which falls within the standard static O-ring design window of 15–30%.

**Step 3 — Apply the linear elastic contact stress equation.**

$$\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\sigma_c = 7.5 \text{ MPa} \times 0.15 = 1.125 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with system pressure for sealing criterion.**

$$\sigma_c = 1.125 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{sys} = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_c \geq P_{sys} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1.125 \geq 1.0 \quad \checkmark$$

The seal criterion is satisfied with a **safety margin of 12.5%** (i.e., 0.125 MPa).

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

The elastomer generates 1.125 MPa of contact stress merely from being squeezed 15%. This exceeds the 1.0 MPa internal pressure, so the interface remains in compression and no leakage path opens.

## 3.2 Derivation: Friction Power Loss in a Reciprocating Hydraulic Seal

**Scenario:** A polyurethane U-cup seal operates on a hydraulic rod of diameter **D = 50 mm**, system pressure **P = 20 MPa**, sliding velocity **v = 0.5 m/s**, and friction coefficient **μ = 0.12**. Compute (a) the normal force, (b) the friction force, and (c) the heat generation rate.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the projected contact area.**

For a U-cup, the projected contact length on the rod is approximately the seal width **w = 6 mm**:

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot w = \pi \times 50 \text{ mm} \times 6 \text{ mm} = 300\pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 942.48 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute normal force from system pressure.**

$$F_n = P \cdot A$$

$$F_n = 20 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 942.48 \text{ mm}^2 = 18{,}849.6 \text{ N} \approx 18.85 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute friction force (Coulomb's law).**

$$F_f = \mu \cdot F_n = 0.12 \times 18{,}849.6 = 2{,}261.95 \text{ N} \approx 2.26 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Compute heat generation rate.**

$$Q = F_f \cdot v = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$$

$$Q = 2{,}261.95 \text{ N} \times 0.5 \text{ m/s} = 1{,}130.98 \text{ W} \approx 1.13 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation.**

Approximately **1.13 kW** of thermal energy must be continuously dissipated from the seal gland. If this heat is not conducted away through the housing and rod, bulk elastomer temperature can rise by **30–60 °C** within minutes, accelerating ageing and reducing seal life by an order of magnitude.

## 3.3 Derivation: Archard Wear Life Prediction

**Scenario:** An NBR rotary shaft seal runs against a hardened steel shaft. Operating conditions: contact pressure **P_c = 0.8 MPa**, sliding velocity **v = 10 m/s**, seal hardness **H = 70 Shore A ≈ 0.7 GPa (estimated from Shore-to-MPa correlation)**, Archard wear coefficient **k = 1 × 10⁻⁶** (typical for elastomer on steel). The seal lip has projected area **A = 150 mm²**. Predict the linear wear rate and the time to lose **1.0 mm** of sealing lip material.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — State Archard's wear equation in pressure-velocity form.**

$$\dot{h} = \frac{k \cdot P_c \cdot v}{H}$$

This form is preferred when contact pressure and velocity are directly known.

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (with unit alignment).**

Convert all quantities to SI base units:

$$P_c = 0.8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}, \quad v = 10 \text{ m/s}, \quad H = 0.7 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}, \quad k = 1 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator.**

$$\text{Numerator} = k \cdot P_c \cdot v = (1 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.8 \times 10^6) \times 10 = 8 \text{ Pa} \cdot \text{m/s}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = H = 0.7 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 4 — Compute linear wear rate.**

$$\dot{h} = \frac{8}{0.7 \times 10^9} = 1.143 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}$$

Converting to more readable units:

$$\dot{h} \approx 11.43 \text{ nm/s} \approx 41.1 \text{ μm/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Compute time to lose 1.0 mm of material.**

$$t_{life} = \frac{\Delta h}{\dot{h}} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}}{1.143 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m/s}}$$

$$t_{life} \approx 87{,}500 \text{ s} \approx 24.3 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering interpretation.**

Under continuous duty, the seal lip would wear through 1 mm in roughly **24 hours**. In real applications, the seal operates intermittently, and the wear coefficient **k** typically drops by an order of magnitude with proper lubrication — extending life to **hundreds or thousands of hours**. This derivation illustrates why **PV limit** is a critical material specification.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Static vs. Dynamic Seals

| Parameter | Static Seal | Dynamic Seal |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Primary function | Block leakage | Block leakage + accommodate motion |
| Key property | Compression set resistance | Wear resistance + low friction |
| Failure mode | Stress relaxation, set | Wear, overheating, extrusion |
| Design equation | $\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon \geq P_{sys}$ | $F_f = \mu \cdot F_n; \ Q = \mu F_n v$ |
| Typical squeeze | 15–30% | 8–20% (lower to reduce friction) |
| Typical hardness | 70–80 Shore A | 80–95 Shore A (higher wear resistance) |
| PV limit relevance | None | Critical |
| Friction coefficient | Not applicable | 0.05–0.30 (typical for elastomer on steel) |
| Typical material | NBR, EPDM, FKM | PU, PTFE, FKM, silicone |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    RF --> SQC
```

## 4.3 Dynamic Seal — Stribeck / Lubrication Regime Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    VEL --> HERS[Hersey Number eta v over P]:::calc
    PRX --> HERS
    VIS --> HERS

    HERS --> REG{Lubrication Regime}:::dec

    REG -->|Very Low| BD[Boundary Lubrication - High mu, High wear]:::bad
    REG -->|Low| MX[Mixed Lubrication - Moderate mu, Moderate wear]:::mid2
    REG -->|Optimum| HD[Hydrodynamic - Lowest mu but possible leakage]:::ok
    REG -->|Very High| TURB[Turbulent - Unstable, risk of overheating]:::bad

    BD --> WEAR[Wear rate from Archard]:::calc2
    MX --> WEAR
    HD --> WEAR2[Fluid film leakage path]:::leak
```
