# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Distinguish between static and dynamic seals. Give **two** examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

A **static seal** operates between two mating surfaces with **no relative motion** during service; its primary function is purely to block fluid passage. Examples: (i) flange gasket in a pipe joint, (ii) O-ring in a stationary hydraulic valve body.

A **dynamic seal** operates between two surfaces exhibiting **continuous relative motion** (reciprocating, rotating, or oscillating); it must simultaneously seal against leakage **and** accommodate motion while resisting wear and friction. Examples: (i) U-cup seal on a hydraulic piston, (ii) radial lip seal on a rotating motor shaft.

| Criterion | Static Seal | Dynamic Seal |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Relative motion | None | Reciprocating / rotating / oscillating |
| Key design property | Compression set resistance | Wear resistance + low friction |
| Typical squeeze | 15–30% | 8–20% |

**(3 marks — Awarded for correct distinction + one valid example of each family.)**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define the **PV limit** of a polymer seal material. Why is it important in dynamic seal design?

**Model Answer:**

The **PV limit** is defined as the maximum allowable product of **contact pressure (P)** and **sliding velocity (v)** that a polymer seal material can sustain without unacceptable wear or thermal failure. It is typically expressed in units of **MPa·m/s**.

$$\text{PV limit} = (P \times v)_{max}$$

**Importance in Dynamic Seal Design:**

1. **Wear Control:** Operating above PV limit exponentially accelerates wear (per Archard's law).
2. **Thermal Management:** Heat generation rate $Q = \mu F_n v$ rises linearly with velocity; exceeding PV limit causes bulk temperature to exceed polymer degradation temperature.
3. **Material Selection:** PV limit serves as a quick material-screening metric — e.g., NBR ≈ 0.5 MPa·m/s, PTFE ≈ 1.5 MPa·m/s.
4. **Design Margin:** Engineers typically design for operating PV ≤ 25–50% of material PV limit to ensure long service life.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for engineering importance.)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** For 14-mark questions on seals, students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** by: (i) confusing compression strain with compression set, (ii) forgetting to state units in intermediate steps, (iii) omitting the **safety margin** discussion at the end, and (iv) failing to draw labelled diagrams of the seal gland. Always: write the formula, substitute values **with units**, comment on the engineering meaning.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** An NBR O-ring with free cross-section diameter $d_0 = 6.0$ mm is installed in a rectangular groove such that its installed height becomes $h_1 = 5.1$ mm. The elastomer has compressive modulus $E = 8.0$ MPa. The system operates at a fluid pressure of $P_{sys} = 1.2$ MPa.

  (i) Calculate the compressive strain (squeeze) and contact stress. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) Determine whether the seal criterion is satisfied, and compute the safety margin in percent. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic reciprocating seal operates with the following parameters: rod diameter $D = 80$ mm, seal width $w = 8$ mm, system pressure $P = 16$ MPa, sliding velocity $v = 0.4$ m/s, friction coefficient $\mu = 0.15$. Determine:
  (i) The normal force and friction force on the seal. **(3 marks)**
  (ii) The heat generation rate, and state **two** practical measures to manage this heat. **(4 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**(a)(i) Compressive strain and contact stress:**

Free height: $h_0 = d_0 = 6.0$ mm. Installed height: $h_1 = 5.1$ mm.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{h_0 - h_1}{h_0} = \frac{6.0 - 5.1}{6.0} = \frac{0.9}{6.0} = 0.15 \quad \text{(or 15%)}$$

Contact stress from linear elasticity:

$$\sigma_c = E \cdot \varepsilon = 8.0 \text{ MPa} \times 0.15 = 1.20 \text{ MPa}$$

**(a)(ii) Seal criterion and safety margin:**

Compare contact stress with system pressure:

$$\sigma_c = 1.20 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{sys} = 1.2 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_c \geq P_{sys} \Rightarrow 1.20 \geq 1.20 \checkmark \quad \text{(seal criterion just satisfied)}$$

Safety margin:

$$\text{Margin} = \frac{\sigma_c - P_{sys}}{P_{sys}} \times 100\% = \frac{1.20 - 1.20}{1.20} \times 100\% = 0\%$$

**Engineering comment:** The seal meets the criterion **exactly** with zero margin. This is unsafe because long-term stress relaxation and compression set will reduce $\sigma_c$ below $P_{sys}$ within service life. **Recommendation:** increase squeeze to 20% (so $\sigma_c = 1.60$ MPa → margin ≈ 33%).

**(b)(i) Normal and friction force:**

Projected contact area:

$$A = \pi \cdot D \cdot w = \pi \times 80 \times 8 = 640\pi \approx 2010.6 \text{ mm}^2$$

Normal force from pressure:

$$F_n = P \cdot A = 16 \text{ N/mm}^2 \times 2010.6 \text{ mm}^2 = 32{,}169.6 \text{ N} \approx 32.17 \text{ kN}$$

Friction force (Coulomb):

$$F_f = \mu \cdot F_n = 0.15 \times 32{,}169.6 = 4{,}825.4 \text{ N} \approx 4.83 \text{ kN}$$

**(b)(ii) Heat generation rate and management:**

$$Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v = 0.15 \times 32{,}169.6 \times 0.4 = 1{,}930.2 \text{ W} \approx 1.93 \text{ kW}$$

**Two practical heat-management measures:**

1. **Use a lower-friction material** — replace NBR with PTFE-filled polyurethane or PTFE (μ ≈ 0.04–0.08), reducing Q proportionally.
2. **Improve gland conduction** — use a metal housing with high thermal conductivity (aluminium or bronze back-up ring) to conduct heat away from the elastomer to the bulk fluid.

**(Total: 14 marks — Awarded as: (a)(i)=4, (a)(ii)=3, (b)(i)=3, (b)(ii)=4.)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled sketch, describe the construction and working of an **O-ring in a static groove**. Explain why compression set is a critical property for this application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary shaft lip seal of projected area $A = 200$ mm² operates against a shaft at sliding velocity $v = 12$ m/s. The contact pressure is $P_c = 1.0$ MPa, seal hardness $H = 80$ Shore A (≈ 1.0 GPa), and Archard wear coefficient $k = 5 \times 10^{-7}$. Estimate:
  (i) The volumetric wear rate per second. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) The time required to wear through 0.5 mm of seal lip material, assuming uniform wear over area $A$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**(a) O-ring in static groove — Construction and working:**

**Construction:** An O-ring is a toroidal elastomeric ring of circular cross-section installed in a rectangular groove machined into one of two mating flanges. The groove has a depth slightly less than the free cross-section diameter of the O-ring (typically 70–85% of $d_0$), producing an interference fit. A backup ring may be added on the low-pressure side for high-pressure or high-clearance applications.

**Working principle:**

1. The O-ring is squeezed axially between the groove bottom (rigid) and the opposing flange face (rigid).
2. This compression generates a contact stress $\sigma_c$ acting on both flange interfaces.
3. The contact stress acts like an internal pressure — it must exceed the system fluid pressure on the high-pressure side to prevent leakage.
4. The elastomer's bulk modulus (much higher than its Young's modulus) ensures it does not extrude into the small radial clearance between flanges under normal conditions.

**Why compression set is critical:**

- Compression set is the permanent deformation that remains after the O-ring is unloaded for a specified time (per ASTM D395, 22 hrs at elevated temperature).
- During service, the O-ring spends 100% of its time in the squeezed state.
- A high compression set means the elastomer "remembers" a shorter height — when the flange is briefly opened for maintenance and re-closed, the seal can no longer generate sufficient $\sigma_c$ to seal.
- Industrial standard: compression set must be **< 25%** for reliable static sealing over years of service.

**(b)(i) Volumetric wear rate per second:**

From Archard's equation:

$$\dot{V}_{wear} = \frac{k \cdot P_c \cdot v \cdot A}{H}$$

Substitute values (SI):

$$P_c = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}, \quad v = 12 \text{ m/s}, \quad A = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$

$$H = 1.0 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}, \quad k = 5 \times 10^{-7}$$

Compute numerator:

$$k \cdot P_c \cdot v \cdot A = (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.0 \times 10^6) \times 12 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.0 \times 10^6) \times (2.4 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= (5 \times 10^{-7}) \times (2.4 \times 10^{3}) = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Divide by hardness:

$$\dot{V}_{wear} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^9} = 1.2 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to mm³/s (multiply by $10^9$):

$$\dot{V}_{wear} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**(b)(ii) Time to wear through 0.5 mm:**

Volume corresponding to 0.5 mm uniform wear over area A:

$$\Delta V = A \cdot \Delta h = (200 \text{ mm}^2) \times (0.5 \text{ mm}) = 100 \text{ mm}^3$$

Time to wear this volume:

$$t_{life} = \frac{\Delta V}{\dot{V}_{wear}} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}^3}{1.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} = 83{,}333 \text{ s}$$

Convert to hours:

$$t_{life} \approx 23.15 \text{ hours (continuous duty)}$$

**Engineering comment:** In real applications with periodic start-stop duty and proper lubrication, the effective wear coefficient $k$ drops by 10–100×, extending service life to **hundreds or thousands of hours**.

**(Total: 14 marks — Awarded as: (a)=7 with sketch (2), working (3), compression set (2); (b)(i)=4, (b)(ii)=3.)**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Static and Dynamic Seals**

- **Static seal** = no relative motion; **Dynamic seal** = relative motion exists.
- **Sealing criterion (static):** contact stress $\sigma_c \geq P_{sys}$.
- **Compression strain:** $\varepsilon = (h_0 - h_1)/h_0$ — typically **15–30%** for static O-rings.
- **Compression set:** permanent deformation after unloading — must be **< 25%** (ASTM D395).
- **Standard O-ring hardness:** **70 ± 5 Shore A** for static; **80–95 Shore A** for dynamic.
- **Friction force (dynamic):** $F_f = \mu \cdot F_n$ — Coulomb's law.
- **Heat generation (dynamic):** $Q = \mu \cdot F_n \cdot v$ — drives thermal failure.
- **PV limit:** maximum $P \cdot v$ a material can sustain; key design selection metric.
- **Archard's wear equation:** $\dot{V}_{wear} = k \cdot W \cdot s / H$ — or linear form $\dot{h} = k P v / H$.
- **Stribeck curve:** dynamic seals should ideally operate near minimum-μ region (mixed/hydrodynamic transition).
- **Polymer selection matrix:** NBR for petroleum oils; FKM for high temperature; EPDM for water/steam; PU for high-pressure hydraulics; PTFE for low friction; silicone for food/medical.
- **Failure mode distinction:** Static seals fail by **stress relaxation / compression set**; Dynamic seals fail by **wear, overheating, and extrusion**.
- **Always remember:** Lower squeeze = lower friction but also lower contact stress — engineering balance is the heart of seal design.
- **Always include units** in numerical solutions — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5 marks for missing units.
- **Always add an engineering comment** at the end of every numerical — it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark "value-add" that distinguishes toppers from average answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Reference:** This topic connects directly to **Module 2** (polymer viscoelasticity — explains stress relaxation), **Module 3** (failure analysis — explains wear and ageing), and **Module 5** (testing standards — ASTM D395, D2240, D3769 for seal characterization). Mastering this module unlocks design competency in fluid power, automotive, aerospace, and oil-and-gas industries.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hoses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hoses # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **hose** is defined by the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT601) as a **flexible, hollow, tubular polymer product** designed to convey fluids (liquids, gases, steam, slurries, or granular solids) from one point to another under varying conditions of pressure, temperature, vacuum, and chemical exposure. In the broader polymer engineering taxonomy, hoses are classified as **extruded, multi-layered, fiber- or wire-reinforced polymer composites** that combine an inner elastomeric/plastic liner, one or more reinforcement plies, and an outer protective cover — typically vulcanized or chemically cross-linked to achieve the required mechanical integrity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Box (Syllabus Highlight)** > A hose is **NOT** a simple pipe. A pipe is rigid and designed primarily for fixed installations, whereas a hose is a flexible, portable, pressure-rated polymer conduit whose performance depends on the synergy between the **inner tube compound, reinforcement architecture, and outer cover compound**. ## 1.2 Key Operational Terminology | Term | Definition | Typical Range | |---|---|---| | **I.D.** (Bore) | Internal Diameter of the hose | 3 mm – 300 mm | | **O.D.** | Outer Diameter of the finished hose | I.D. + 2×wall thickness | | **Working Pressure (WP)** | Maximum sustained safe operating pressure | 5 – 400 bar | | **Test Pressure (TP)** | Hydrostatic pressure applied during quality testing (typically 2×WP) | 10 – 800 bar | | **Burst Pressure (BP)** | Pressure at which the hose physically ruptures | 4×WP (minimum) | | **Burst Pressure Ratio (BPR)** | BP / WP — the inbuilt safety margin | 4 : 1 (industrial minimum) | | **Bend Radius** | Minimum radius to which the hose can be bent without kinking | 6×–12× I.D. | | **Safety Factor (SF)** | BP / WP, identical to BPR | 3:1, 4:1, or 10:1 (per application) | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blood Vessel of Industry" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Pump (Real-World Analogy):** > Imagine the **human circulatory system**. Arteries and veins are essentially *biological hoses* — flexible tubes carrying pressurized fluid (blood) from the heart. They have three functional layers: (1) a smooth inner lining (**inner tube**) that does not react with blood, (2) a muscular middle layer (**reinforcement**) that resists bursting, and (3) a protective outer skin (**outer cover**) that shields against abrasion. > A polymer hose is engineered on **exactly the same tri-layer principle**. The inner tube is compounded to be chemically compatible with the fluid, the reinforcement is engineered to contain the pressure, and the outer cover is formulated to resist the external environment. This **tri-layer construction principle** is the conceptual backbone of every industrial hose design. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Tri-Layer Construction Architecture A modern polymer hose is a **composite pressure vessel** consisting of three functionally distinct layers, each engineered from a different polymer formulation: ### Layer 1 — Inner Tube (Liner) - **Function:** Direct contact with the conveyed fluid; must be chemically inert, smooth, and impermeable. - **Common Materials:** NBR (Nitrile Butadiene Rubber) for oils, EPDM for steam/water, PTFE for aggressive chemicals, NR (Natural Rubber) for abrasives, CR (Neoprene) for mild chemicals, silicone for food/pharma. - **Critical Properties:** Chemical resistance, low permeability, smooth bore (for low friction loss), food-grade compliance (FDA/USFDA). ### Layer 2 — Reinforcement - **Function:** Provides the **pressure-bearing capacity** and dimensional stability. - **Types of Reinforcement:** - **Textile braid:** Polyester, nylon, rayon, cotton, aramid (Kevlar) — for low-to-medium pressure. - **Wire braid:** High-tensile steel wire — for high-pressure hydraulic hoses. - **Wire spiral:** Multiple helical layers of heavy-gauge wire — for very high pressure (up to 600 bar). - **Thermoplastic reinforcement:** Spiraled thermoplastics — for lightweight specialty hoses. ### Layer 3 — Outer Cover - **Function:** Protects the reinforcement from abrasion, weather, ozone, oil, and heat. - **Common Materials:** CR, CSM (Hypalon), EPDM, NBR/PVC blends, Urethanes. - **Critical Properties:** Abrasion resistance, UV/ozone resistance, flame retardance (MSHA standards for mining). ## 2.2 Classification of Polymer Hoses ### By Reinforcement Type 1. **Unreinforced (mandrel-built) hoses** — used for low-pressure, large-bore applications. 2. **Textile-braided hoses** — garden hoses, air hoses, water hoses. 3. **Wire-braided hoses** — hydraulic hoses (SAE 100R1, 100R2 series). 4. **Wire-spiral hoses** — high-pressure hydraulics (SAE 100R12, 100R13). ### By Application - **Hydraulic hoses** — petroleum-based fluid power transmission. - **Industrial / Chemical hoses** — acids, solvents, hydrocarbons. - **Food & Beverage hoses** — milk, beer, potable water (FDA-compliant). - **Steam hoses** — saturated steam service (EPDM or silicone). - **Automotive hoses** — fuel, brake, coolant, A/C. - **Marine / Oil hoses** — offshore, dredging, oil transfer. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Cheat Sheet — All formulas below are examinable in ESE 2024 pattern.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | **Hoop stress** in a thin-walled tube | MPa | | 2 | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | **Longitudinal (axial) stress** | MPa | | 3 | $BP = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u}{D}$ | Theoretical **burst pressure** | bar | | 4 | $BPR = \dfrac{BP}{WP}$ | **Burst Pressure Ratio** | dimensionless | | 5 | $SF = \dfrac{BP}{WP}$ | **Safety Factor** (= BPR) | dimensionless | | 6 | $R_b^{\min} = k \cdot I.D.$ | **Minimum bend radius**, $k$ = 6 to 12 | mm | | 7 | $t_{\min} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}}$ | Required **wall thickness** | mm | | 8 | $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{SF}$ | **Allowable stress** with safety factor | MPa | > **Where:** > - $P$ = internal pressure (MPa or bar) > - $D$ = internal diameter (mm) > - $t$ = wall thickness (mm) > - $\sigma_u$ = ultimate tensile strength of the reinforcement material (MPa) > - $\sigma_h, \sigma_l$ = hoop (circumferential) and longitudinal stresses ## 2.4 Engineering Design Logic — Why and How **Step 1 — Define the Service Conditions:** Identify the fluid, temperature range, working pressure, vacuum requirement, and external environment (abrasion, UV, chemicals). **Step 2 — Select the Inner Tube Compound:** Match the tube elastomer to the chemical compatibility chart. *Why:* The tube is the only barrier between the fluid and the environment — incompatibility leads to permeation, swelling, or degradation. **Step 3 — Select the Reinforcement Architecture:** Based on WP and required BPR. *Why:* The reinforcement must absorb all the hoop stress generated by internal pressure without exceeding its elastic limit. **Step 4 — Select the Outer Cover Compound:** Match to the external environment. *Why:* Cover failures (ozone cracking, abrasion) shorten hose life even when the tube is intact. **Step 5 — Compute BPR and Validate:** The BPR must equal or exceed the application standard (e.g., **4:1 minimum for industrial, 10:1 for high-pressure hydraulics**). ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hydraulic systems** in earth-moving equipment use wire-braided/spiral hoses to transmit power fluid at 350+ bar. - **Oil & gas offshore platforms** use bonded chemical hoses for transferring crude, acids, and produced water. - **Automotive brake systems** use DOT-rated reinforced elastomeric hoses. - **Firefighting** requires high-pressure textile-braided hoses with BPR of 8:1. - **Pharmaceutical manufacturing** uses PTFE-lined silicone hoses for ultra-pure fluid transfer. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Hoop Stress in a Thin-Walled Hose Consider a section of hose of length $L$, internal diameter $D$, and wall thickness $t$. Cut the hose longitudinally along its top edge. The internal pressure $P$ pushes on the two halves. ### Step 1 — Free Body Analysis The internal pressure acting on the projected area (length $L \times D$) creates a force trying to split the hose open. This force is resisted by the tensile stress in the wall material acting across two cut sections, each of area $L \times t$. $$\text{Outward force on cut surface} = P \cdot D \cdot L$$ ### Step 2 — Equilibrium Equation The two cut edges each carry a tensile stress $\sigma_h$: $$\text{Resisting force} = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$ ### Step 3 — Equate and Solve $$P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t \cdot L$$ $$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$ > **This is the classical Barlow formula for hoop stress, applicable to any thin-walled pressure cylinder (including hoses where $D/t > 20$).** ## 3.2 Derivation of Longitudinal Stress Cutting the hose perpendicular to its axis exposes a circular cross-section of area $\pi D^2 / 4$. The pressure force along the axis is balanced by the wall stress across the ring of area $\pi D t$: $$\frac{P \cdot \pi \cdot D^2}{4} = \sigma_l \cdot \pi \cdot D \cdot t$$ $$\sigma_l = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$ > **Key Insight (KTU High-Yield):** The **hoop stress is exactly twice** the longitudinal stress. This is why hoses typically fail along their length (split longitudinally) before failing at end fittings. $$\sigma_h = 2 \cdot \sigma_l$$ ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Designing a Hydraulic Hose > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern Problem — "Design a hydraulic hose for a working pressure of 200 bar with a bore of 16 mm. Use a reinforcement with ultimate tensile strength of 250 MPa. Determine (a) the minimum wall thickness, (b) the theoretical burst pressure, and (c) the BPR. Assume SF = 4."** ### Given Data - $WP = 200 \text{ bar} = 20 \text{ MPa}$ - $D = 16 \text{ mm}$ - $\sigma_u = 250 \text{ MPa}$ - $SF = 4$ ### Part (a) — Allowable Stress $$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_u}{SF} = \frac{250}{4} = 62.5 \text{ MPa}$$ ### Part (b) — Minimum Wall Thickness Rearranging the hoop stress formula: $$t_{\min} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}}$$ $$t_{\min} = \frac{20 \times 16}{2 \times 62.5} = \frac{320}{125} = 2.56 \text{ mm}$$ ### Part (c) — Theoretical Burst Pressure $$BP = \frac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u}{D}$$ $$BP = \frac{2 \times 2.56 \times 250}{16} = \frac{1280}{16} = 80 \text{ MPa} = 800 \text{ bar}$$ ### Part (d) — Verify Burst Pressure Ratio $$BPR = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{800}{200} = 4.0 \;\; \checkmark$$ > **Conclusion:** The designed hose has a BPR of exactly 4:1, satisfying the industrial safety requirement. ## 3.4 KTU Design Decision Matrix (Material Selection) | Service Fluid | Recommended Inner Tube | Recommended Outer Cover | Typical BPR | |---|---|---|---| | Petroleum oils | NBR | CR / CSM | 4:1 | | Water / Steam | EPDM | EPDM | 4:1 – 10:1 | | Acids / Chemicals | PTFE / FKM / Hypalon | EPDM / CR | 4:1 | | Food / Pharma | Silicone / NR (FDA) | Silicone / CR | 4:1 | | High-temp hydraulics | HNBR / Viton | CSM | 4:1 – 6:1 | | Abrasive slurries | NR / Polyurethane | NR / CR | 3:1 – 4:1 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Tri-Layer Hose Construction (Cross-Sectional View) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["FLUID INLET
Pressurized Working Fluid"] nodeB["INNER TUBE LAYER
Chemical Compatibility Layer
Material NBR or EPDM or PTFE"] nodeC["REINFORCEMENT LAYER
Pressure Bearing Architecture
Wire Braid or Textile Braid or Wire Spiral"] nodeD["OUTER COVER LAYER
Environmental Protection
Material CR or CSM or EPDM"] nodeE["FLUID OUTLET
Delivery Point"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE ``` ## 4.2 Hose Design Workflow (Block-Level Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA["SERVICE CONDITION
ANALYSIS
Fluid, Pressure, Temperature"] stepB["INNER TUBE
SELECTION
Chemical Compatibility"] stepC["REINFORCEMENT
ARCHITECTURE
Braid or Spiral Selection"] stepD["OUTER COVER
SELECTION
Environmental Resistance"] stepE["MATHEMATICAL
DESIGN
Wall Thickness via Hoop Stress"] stepF["BPR
VALIDATION
Safety Factor Check"] stepG["PROTOTYPE
MANUFACTURE
Extrusion and Vulcanization"] stepH["QUALITY
TESTING
Burst, Impulse, Aging"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD --> stepE stepE --> stepF stepF --> stepG stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepF ``` ## 4.3 Manufacturing Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB proc1["RAW ELASTOMER
COMPOUNDING
Mixing of Polymer with Curatives"] proc2["INNER TUBE
EXTRUSION
Die Forms Uncured Tube"] proc3["BRAIDING or
SPIRAL WINDING
Reinforcement Application"] proc4["OUTER COVER
EXTRUSION
Cross-head Die Application"] proc5["VULCANIZATION
AUTOCLAVE or CONTINUOUS LINE
Curing at 140 to 160 C"] proc6["QUALITY
TESTING
Hydrostatic Burst Test"] proc7["CUTTING and
CRIMPING
End Fitting Attachment"] proc8["FINAL PRODUCT
CERTIFIED HOSE"] proc1 --> proc2 proc2 --> proc3 proc3 --> proc4 proc4 --> proc5 proc5 --> proc6 proc6 --> proc7 proc7 --> proc8 ``` ## 4.4 Failure Mode Classification ```mermaid flowchart TB fail1["TUBE FAILURE
Chemical Attack or Permeation"] fail2["REINFORCEMENT FAILURE
Fatigue, Burst, or Kinking"] fail3["COVER FAILURE
Abrasion, Ozone Cracking, or Heat Damage"] fail4["END FITTING FAILURE
Crimp Slippage or Hose Whip"] fail5["CATASTROPHIC RUPTURE
BPR Violation or Manufacturing Defect"] fail1 --> fail5 fail2 --> fail5 fail3 --> fail5 fail4 --> fail5 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **Define a hose. Differentiate between a hose and a pipe.** **Model Answer:** A hose is a flexible, tubular polymer product designed to convey fluids under varying pressure, temperature, and chemical conditions. It is typically multi-layered with an inner tube, reinforcement, and outer cover. | Feature | Pipe | Hose | |---|---|---| | Flexibility | Rigid | Flexible | | Movement during service | Stationary | Can flex and bend | | Pressure handling | Fixed installation | Portable, dynamic | | Material | Metals, plastics | Elastomers, TPEs with reinforcement | | Joints | Welded, flanged | Crimped, reusable fittings | ### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024] **Explain the tri-layer construction of a polymer hose with a neat sketch description.** **Model Answer:** A polymer hose consists of three layers: 1. **Inner Tube:** Chemically resistant elastomer that contacts the fluid. 2. **Reinforcement:** Textile or wire layers providing pressure strength. 3. **Outer Cover:** Weather, abrasion, and ozone-resistant elastomer protecting the reinforcement. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Classify polymer hoses based on reinforcement type and application. Discuss the selection criteria for inner tube and outer cover materials with examples.** **Model Solution:** **Classification by Reinforcement:** 1. **Unreinforced hoses** — Pure elastomer, used for low-pressure air/water. 2. **Textile-braided hoses** — Single or multi-braid polyester/nylon — garden hoses, low-pressure hydraulics. 3. **Wire-braided hoses** — Steel wire braid (one or two braids) — medium-high pressure hydraulics (SAE 100R1, R2). 4. **Wire-spiral hoses** — Multiple helical wire layers — very high-pressure applications (SAE 100R9, R12, R13). **Classification by Application:** Hydraulic, chemical, food/pharma, automotive, marine, steam, firefighting. **Inner Tube Selection:** Based on chemical compatibility (NBR for oils, EPDM for steam, PTFE for aggressive chemicals, silicone for food-grade). The tube must also have low permeability and smooth bore for low pressure drop. **Outer Cover Selection:** Based on environmental resistance (CR for abrasion and oil resistance, CSM for weather/UV, EPDM for heat resistance). Mining hoses additionally require MSHA flame-resistance compliance. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A hydraulic hose has an internal diameter of 25 mm and operates at a working pressure of 175 bar. The reinforcement material has an ultimate tensile strength of 220 MPa. Calculate (i) the minimum wall thickness for a safety factor of 4, (ii) the theoretical burst pressure, and (iii) verify the BPR.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = 25 \text{ mm}$, $P = 175 \text{ bar} = 17.5 \text{ MPa}$, $\sigma_u = 220 \text{ MPa}$, $SF = 4$. **(i) Allowable stress:** $$\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \frac{\sigma_u}{SF} = \frac{220}{4} = 55 \text{ MPa}$$ **Minimum wall thickness:** $$t_{\min} = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}} = \frac{17.5 \times 25}{2 \times 55} = \frac{437.5}{110} = 3.98 \text{ mm}$$ **(ii) Theoretical burst pressure:** $$BP = \frac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u}{D} = \frac{2 \times 3.98 \times 220}{25} = \frac{1751.2}{25} = 70.05 \text{ MPa} = 700.5 \text{ bar}$$ **(iii) Verify BPR:** $$BPR = \frac{BP}{WP} = \frac{700.5}{175} = 4.003 \approx 4.0 \;\; \checkmark$$ The design satisfies the industrial 4:1 BPR requirement. --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Pattern]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With the help of a schematic, explain the manufacturing process of a wire-braided hydraulic hose. State the relevant testing standards.** **Model Solution:** **Manufacturing Steps:** 1. **Compounding:** Inner tube compound (NBR) and outer cover compound (CR/CSM) are mixed in internal mixers with curatives, fillers, plasticizers. 2. **Inner Tube Extrusion:** Uncured NBR is extruded through a cross-head die over a mandrel to form the inner tube. 3. **Reinforcement Braiding:** Steel wire is braided over the inner tube using 24 or 36 carrier braiding machines. For two-braid hoses, a second braid is applied. 4. **Outer Cover Extrusion:** The outer cover compound is extruded over the braided hose using a second cross-head die. 5. **Vulcanization:** The hose passes through a steam autoclave or continuous curing line at **140–160 °C** for crosslinking. 6. **Cutting and Fitting Attachment:** The hose is cut to length and end fittings are crimped on using hydraulic swaging machines. **Testing Standards (per ISO 1436 / SAE J517):** - **Hydrostatic burst test** (minimum 4× WP). - **Impulse test** — 1,000,000+ cycles at rated WP. - **Cold flexibility test** at −40 °C for hydraulic hoses. - **Oil resistance test** for inner tube. - **Ozone resistance test** for outer cover. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss the various failure modes in polymer hoses. How are these failures prevented in design?** **Model Solution:** **Major Failure Modes:** 1. **Tube Blistering and Permeation:** Caused by chemical incompatibility or excessive temperature. *Prevention:* Select correct elastomer (use compatibility charts); keep operating temperature below the rated limit. 2. **Reinforcement Fatigue:** Repeated pressure cycles cause wire/textile fatigue. *Prevention:* Specify hoses with adequate impulse ratings; observe minimum bend radius. 3. **Coupling/Crimp Failure:** Improper crimping leads to hose whip and blow-off. *Prevention:* Use manufacturer-specified crimp dies; verify pull-off force. 4. **Abrasion and Ozone Cracking of Cover:** External damage. *Prevention:* Use abrasion-resistant covers (CR); add hose guards in drag applications. 5. **Kinking:** Occurs when bend radius is violated. *Prevention:* Specify minimum bend radius (typically 6×–12× I.D.); install bend restrictors. 6. **Over-Pressure Burst:** Operating above WP. *Prevention:* Observe BPR (4:1 minimum), install pressure relief valves. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Mixing up BPR and SF:** They are numerically equal ($BP/WP$), but conceptually BPR is the *ratio* and SF is the *factor* chosen at design stage. Examiners deduct marks for treating them as different calculations. > 2. **Forgetting unit conversion:** Pressure must be in consistent units throughout. **1 bar = 0.1 MPa**. Converting 175 bar to 17.5 MPa is mandatory. > 3. **Hoop vs. Longitudinal Stress confusion:** Hoop stress is **2×** the longitudinal stress — students often write $\sigma = PD/t$ instead of $\sigma_h = PD/2t$. > 4. **Missing the inner tube material discussion:** Many students focus only on reinforcement and forget that **chemical compatibility of the inner tube is the FIRST design criterion**. > 5. **No mention of impulse testing:** For hydraulic hoses, the examiner specifically expects mention of impulse cycling tests (not just burst). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Hoses (Module 4)** - **Definition:** A hose is a flexible, multi-layered polymer pressure conduit — **NOT a pipe**. - **Tri-layer construction is mandatory:** Inner tube (chemical resistance) + Reinforcement (pressure) + Outer cover (environment). - **BPR is the most critical safety parameter:** **4:1 industrial minimum**, **10:1 for hydraulic**, **8:1 for firefighting**. - **Hoop stress formula (memorize!):** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ - **Longitudinal stress = ½ of hoop stress** → hoses always **split lengthwise first**, not end-to-end. - **Burst pressure formula:** $BP = \dfrac{2 \cdot t \cdot \sigma_u}{D}$ - **Allowable stress:** $\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_u}{SF}$ - **Inner tube elastomer guide:** NBR (oil), EPDM (steam/water), PTFE (chemicals), Silicone (food/pharma), NR (abrasives). - **Reinforcement selection rule:** Textile braid = low pressure; Wire braid = medium-high pressure; Wire spiral = very high pressure. - **Outer cover guide:** CR (abrasion + oil), CSM (weather/UV/heat), EPDM (heat resistance), NBR/PVC (general purpose). - **Minimum bend radius:** $R_b^{\min} = (6 \text{ to } 12) \times I.D.$ — violating it causes **kinking and reinforcement fatigue**. - **Manufacturing sequence:** Compounding → Inner tube extrusion → Reinforcement braiding → Cover extrusion → Vulcanization (140–160 °C) → Cutting & crimping → Testing. - **Key testing standards:** ISO 1436, SAE J517 (hydraulics); burst test, impulse test, cold flexibility, ozone resistance. - **Failure modes to memorize:** Tube blistering, reinforcement fatigue, coupling failure, cover abrasion, kinking, over-pressure burst. - **Real-world exam trick:** Always mention **chemical compatibility chart** when discussing inner tube selection — examiners reward this term. ---

topics.title: Couplings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Couplings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coupling (Mechanical Definition):** A *coupling* is a mechanical device permanently or semi-permanently used to connect two adjacent rotating shafts for the purpose of transmitting **torque, power, and rotary motion** from a driving shaft to a driven shaft, while (in flexible designs) compensating for **shaft misalignment**, **axial displacement**, **angular deviation**, and **torsional vibration damping**.

In the context of **PCPOT601 (Polymer Products – Design & Testing)**, the focus shifts to *Polymer-Based and Polymer-Composite Couplings* — i.e., mechanical couplings in which **one or more load-bearing or flexibility-providing elements are manufactured from engineering polymers, elastomers, or fibre-reinforced polymer composites (FRP).**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Rubber Sandwich" Drive Joint 🛠️**
> Imagine two steel shafts that are *slightly misaligned* (not perfectly in a straight line). If you welded them together rigidly, the misalignment would shake the machine apart. Now, imagine you instead placed a **soft rubber donut** between them. The rubber donut "absorbs" the misalignment — it twists a bit, bends a bit, and damps out vibration — while still transmitting the rotational push (torque) from one shaft to the other. **That soft rubber donut IS the polymer/elastomer element of a flexible coupling.**
> Polymers are used because they are *compliant*, *damping*, *lightweight*, *corrosion-resistant*, and *electrically insulating* — qualities metals cannot match.

### 1.3 Classification of Couplings (High-Yield Overview)

| **Category** | **Sub-Type** | **Polymer Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| **Rigid Couplings** | Sleeve / Muff, Clamp / Split Muff, Flange | Polymer sleeve bushings; polymer-composite flange plates |
| **Flexible Couplings — Metallic Element** | Bushed-pin, Oldham, Bevel Conical, Universal Joint | Uses polymer bushes on pins |
| **Flexible Couplings — Non-Metallic (Polymer) Element** | **Spider (Jaw) Coupling, Tyre Coupling, Diaphragm Coupling, Resilient Bush Coupling, Gear–Rubber Coupling** | **Core focus of Module 4** |

### 1.4 Common Engineering Polymers Used in Couplings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following polymers are *industry-standard* for coupling elements and **must be memorised** for KTU viva/ESE:
> - **Polyurethane (PU / TPU)** — high load-bearing, abrasion-resistant spider elements
> - **Natural Rubber (NR) & Nitrile Butadiene Rubber (NBR)** — tyre-coupling elements, vibration damping
> - **Neoprene (Polychloroprene, CR)** — oil-resistant flexible bushes
> - **Nylon 6 / Nylon 6,6 (Polyamide)** — sleeve bushings, gear blanks
> - **Phenolic Resin (Bakelite)** — high-temperature rigid flange plates
> - **PTFE (Polytetrafluoroethylene)** — low-friction sliding elements in Oldham-type couplings
> - **Fibre-Reinforced Polymer Composites (GFRP / CFRP)** — light-weight rigid flanges

### 1.5 Key Physical & Dimensional Metrics

- **Nominal Torque Capacity** $\left[T_N\right]$ — expressed in **N·m**
- **Maximum Rotational Speed** $\left[N\right]$ — expressed in **rpm**
- **Shaft Bore Diameter** $\left[d\right]$ — in **mm**
- **Hub Outer Diameter** $\left[D\right]$ — in **mm**
- **Service Factor** $\left(K_S\right)$ — dimensionless multiplier (typically **1.0 – 3.5**)
- **Polymer-specific:** Glass-transition temperature $\left[T_g\right]$, Hardness (Shore A / Shore D), Creep Modulus $\left[E_c(t)\right]$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Requirements of a Coupling

A coupling, irrespective of material, must satisfy five simultaneous functional demands:

1. **Torque Transmission** — without slippage or permanent deformation under rated load.
2. **Misalignment Accommodation** — parallel, angular, or axial misalignment between coupled shafts.
3. **Vibration & Shock Damping** — absorbing torsional oscillations and starting shocks.
4. **Protection Against Overload** — sacrificial failure of polymer element before shaft damage (a *safety fuse*).
5. **Service Life under Environmental Stress** — temperature, oil, chemicals, dust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Polymers Excel Here:** Engineering polymers possess **high loss factor ($\tan \delta$)**, typically **0.05 – 0.30**, which is *10× to 100×* greater than steel ($\tan \delta \approx 0.001$). This makes them exceptional at *dissipating vibrational energy as heat* — a property no metallic coupling element can replicate efficiently.

### 2.2 Detailed Anatomy of a Polymer Spider (Jaw) Coupling

The **most-asked coupling in KTU board examinations** is the **Spider / Jaw Coupling (Elastomeric Jaw Coupling)**.

- **Two Hubs** — usually cast iron or sintered steel, with axially-cut jaws (3, 4, or 6 lobes).
- **One Spider Element** — the **polymer/elastomer star-shaped insert** that sits between the jaws.
- **Torque path:** Hub-1 jaws → compress/deform polymer spider → Hub-2 jaws.

The polymer spider is loaded in **compression + shear**, making polyurethane (Shore A 85–95) the preferred material.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Mandatory for ESE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula bank**. All units follow **SI (N, mm, MPa, N·m, rpm)** unless otherwise stated. Note the use of `\vert` instead of the pipe character to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Quantity Computed** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ | Power $P$ **(kW)** | From torque and speed |
| 2 | $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$ | Torque $T$ **(N·m)** | Design input from power and speed |
| 3 | $T_{design} = K_S \cdot T_{nominal}$ | Design Torque **(N·m)** | Apply service factor |
| 4 | $d^3 = \dfrac{16 \, T_{design}}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}$ | Shaft diameter $d$ **(mm)** | ASME code for solid shaft |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$ | Shear stress $\tau$ **(MPa)** | Shaft check |
| 6 | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{2 \, T}{d \, t \, l}$ | Crushing stress on key **(MPa)** | Key design check |
| 7 | $\tau_k = \dfrac{2 \, T}{d \, b \, l}$ | Shear stress on key **(MPa)** | Key design check |
| 8 | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{D^2 + d^2}{D^2 - d^2} \cdot \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 l}$ | Hub hoop stress **(MPa)** | Shrink-fit / interference fit |
| 9 | $\tau_b = \dfrac{4 \, T}{\pi \, d_1^2 \, n \cdot r}$ | Bolt shear in flange coupling **(MPa)** | Flange coupling design |
| 10 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{4 \, T}{\pi \, d_1 \, t \cdot n \cdot r}$ | Bolt tensile stress **(MPa)** | Flange coupling design |
| 11 | $E_c(t) = \dfrac{E_0}{1 + \left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)^n}$ | **Polymer Creep Modulus (Findley Power Law)** | Long-term deformation check |
| 12 | $F.S._{polymer} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult,\,polymer}}{\sigma_{design}}$ | Factor of safety for polymer | Polymer element design |

**Where:**
- $P$ = Power (kW); $N$ = Speed (rpm); $T$ = Torque (N·m)
- $K_S$ = Service factor (typically 1.25 for uniform load, 2.0 for light shock, 3.0 for heavy shock)
- $\tau_{allow}$ = Allowable shear stress of shaft material (MPa)
- $D, d$ = Outer / inner hub diameter; $l$ = key length; $b, t$ = key width / thickness
- $d_1$ = bolt circle diameter; $n$ = number of bolts; $r$ = bolt radius; $t$ = flange thickness
- $p$ = interference pressure; $E_0$ = instantaneous modulus; $\tau, n$ = Findley creep constants

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Industry** | **Coupling Application** | **Polymer Choice** |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive (Drive Shaft) | Universal-joint boot, CV-joint boot | Neoprene, TPU |
| Pumps & Compressors | Spider coupling on motor-pump shaft | Polyurethane (Shore A 90) |
| Textile Machinery | High-speed flexible coupling | Nylon gear couplings |
| Marine Propeller Shafts | Resilient bush coupling | Natural Rubber |
| HVAC Drives | Misalignment-tolerant spider coupling | Hytrel (TPC-ET) |
| Printing Presses | Torsional vibration damper coupling | Nitrile Rubber (NBR) |

### 2.7 Design Philosophy for Polymer Couplings

1. **Compute torque from rated power & speed** → Equation (2).
2. **Apply service factor** → Equation (3) — never design for ideal load.
3. **Size shaft diameter** → Equation (4) using ASME code.
4. **Design the key** → Equations (6) and (7) — check both shear *and* crushing.
5. **Select polymer spider/hub geometry** from manufacturer's catalogue matched to $T_{design}$.
6. **Check polymer long-term performance** → Equation (11), the **Findley creep law**.
7. **Verify FOS** → Equation (12) for the polymer element.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Power–Torque Relationship

**Starting concept:** Power equals the rate of doing work. For a rotating shaft:

$$P_{instantaneous} = T \cdot \omega$$

where $\omega$ is angular velocity in **rad/s**.

**Step 1 — Express $\omega$ in rpm:** For $N$ revolutions per minute,

$$\omega = \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \; \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the power equation:**

$$P = T \cdot \dfrac{2 \pi N}{60} \; \text{(Watts, if } T \text{ in N·m)}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW for engineering convenience (divide by 1000):**

$$\boxed{\,P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000} \; \text{kW}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Inverse (solving for T):**

$$\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \, P}{2 \pi N} \; \text{N·m}\,}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This relationship is the *gateway formula* — every shaft, coupling, and gearbox problem in KTU begins here.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ASME Shaft Diameter Equation

**Starting concept:** A shaft under torsion alone experiences **maximum shear stress** at its outer surface, given by the torsion formula:

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

where $J$ is the polar moment of inertia of the cross-section.

**Step 1 — For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:**

$$J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}, \qquad r = \dfrac{d}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the torsion formula:**

$$\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^{4}/32} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Equate to allowable shear stress $\tau_{allow}$ and solve for $d$:**

$$\tau_{allow} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, d^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\,d^{3} = \dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}} \;\Rightarrow\; d = \left(\dfrac{16 \, T}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}\,}$$

**Step 4 — Apply service factor (couplings are subjected to shock & fatigue):**

$$\boxed{\,d = \left(\dfrac{16 \, K_S \, T}{\pi \, \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Key Shear and Crushing Stresses

A key transmits torque from the shaft to the hub via two failure modes: **shear** (across the key's cross-section) and **crushing** (bearing on the key's side faces).

**Tangential force at shaft surface:**

$$F_t = \dfrac{T}{(d/2)} = \dfrac{2T}{d}$$

**Step 1 — Shear failure (key shears along its width $b$ and length $l$):**

The shear area is $A_s = b \cdot l$.

$$\tau_k = \dfrac{F_t}{A_s} = \dfrac{2T}{d \, b \, l}$$

**Step 2 — Crushing failure (key crushes along its half-height $t/2$ over length $l$):**

The crushing area is $A_c = (t/2) \cdot l$.

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_t}{A_c} = \dfrac{2T}{d \cdot (t/2) \cdot l} = \dfrac{4T}{d \, t \, l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU convention used in the formula sheet** writes crushing stress as $\sigma_c = \dfrac{2T}{d \, t \, l}$ (using *full* key height $t$). Both forms are dimensionally valid depending on whether the bearing surface is taken as half or full. Always check the **answer key** for the convention used.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Shrink-Fit (Interference-Fit) Hub Hoop Stress

When a polymer or metallic hub is pressed onto a shaft, contact pressure $p$ develops. The hub experiences **hoop (circumferential tensile) stress**:

**Step 1 — Free-body of hub:** Consider a half-hub of inner radius $r_i = d/2$ and outer radius $r_o = D/2$. The contact pressure $p$ acts radially inward.

**Step 2 — Force balance on a longitudinal diametrical section:**

$$2 \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \sigma_\theta(r) \cdot t \cdot dr = 2 \, p \, r_i \, l$$

**Step 3 — Lamé's solution** for a thick cylinder under internal pressure gives:

$$\sigma_\theta(r) = \dfrac{p \, r_i^{2}}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}} \left(1 + \dfrac{r_o^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Maximum hoop stress at the bore ($r = r_i$):**

$$\sigma_{\theta,\,max} = \dfrac{p \left(r_o^{2} + r_i^{2}\right)}{r_o^{2} - r_i^{2}}$$

Replacing $r_i = d/2$, $r_o = D/2$, and solving for $p$:

$$\boxed{\,p = \dfrac{\sigma_{\theta,\,max}\left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)}{2 \, d \, l}\,}$$

Rearranged (Lamé form):

$$\boxed{\,\sigma_h = \dfrac{D^{2} + d^{2}}{D^{2} - d^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{p \, d}{2 \, l}\,}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Failure Modes in Polymer Couplings

| **Failure Mode** | **Affected Component** | **Governing Equation** | **Mitigation in Polymer Design** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shaft shear | Solid shaft | $\tau = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Use larger $d$ or alloy steel |
| Key shear | Key | $\tau_k = \dfrac{2T}{d b l}$ | Longer key; surface-hardened key |
| Key crushing | Key | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{4T}{d t l}$ | Higher $t$ or alloy key material |
| Hub hoop cracking | Hub | Lamé equation (3.4) | Interference reduced; polymer hub selected |
| Polymer creep | Spider / tyre element | $E_c(t) = \dfrac{E_0}{1 + (t/\tau)^n}$ | Lower sustained load; replace elastomer |
| Thermal degradation | Polymer element | $T_{service} < T_g - 20^{\circ}C$ | Select high-$T_g$ polymer (e.g., PU over NR) |
| Fatigue cracking | Spider / diaphragm | S-N curve of polymer | Shore hardness tuning; cyclic load rating |

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical (for ESE Readiness)

**Problem:** A polymer spider coupling is to transmit **$P = 7.5 \, \text{kW}$** at **$N = 1440 \, \text{rpm}$** between an electric motor and a centrifugal pump. The shaft material has $\tau_{allow} = 45 \, \text{MPa}$ and the service factor is $K_S = 1.5$. Design the **shaft diameter** and the **square key ($b \times h = 10 \times 10 \, \text{mm}$)**.

**Step 1 — Nominal torque:**

$$T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 7.5}{2 \pi \times 1440} = 49.74 \, \text{N·m} = 49{,}740 \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Design torque:**

$$T_{design} = 1.5 \times 49{,}740 = 74{,}610 \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft diameter:**

$$d^{3} = \dfrac{16 \times 74{,}610}{\pi \times 45} = 8{,}449 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$d = \sqrt[3]{8{,}449} = 20.4 \, \text{mm} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{adopt } d = 22 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Key length required (shear criterion):** $\tau_k^{allow} = 60 \, \text{MPa}$

$$l = \dfrac{2T}{d \, b \, \tau_k^{allow}} = \dfrac{2 \times 74{,}610}{22 \times 10 \times 60} = 11.3 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 5 — Key length required (crushing criterion):** $\sigma_c^{allow} = 120 \, \text{MPa}$

$$l = \dfrac{4T}{d \, t \, \sigma_c^{allow}} = \dfrac{4 \times 74{,}610}{22 \times 10 \times 120} = 11.3 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 6 — Adopt $l = 14 \, \text{mm}$** (next standard size) — coupling is safe.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Classification Tree — Polymer Couplings

```mermaid

    E --> E2[Tyre Coupling]

    E1 --> P2[Nitrile Rubber Spider]

```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Coupling Design Workflow

```mermaid
graph LR
    input1[Step1: Rated Power P kW] --> stepA[Step2: Compute Nominal Torque T]
    input2[Step2: Rated Speed N rpm] --> stepA
    stepA --> stepB[Step3: Apply Service Factor Ks]
    stepB --> stepC[Step4: Compute Design Torque Tdesign]
    stepC --> stepD[Step5: Size Shaft Diameter d using ASME]
    stepD --> stepE[Step6: Select Key Geometry b t]
    stepE --> stepF[Step7: Check Key Shear and Crushing]
    stepF --> stepG[Step8: Compute Hub Hoop Stress Lame Eq]
    stepG --> stepH[Step9: Select Polymer Spider Material]
    stepH --> stepI[Step10: Apply Findley Creep Law]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step11: Compute Polymer Factor of Safety]
    stepJ --> stepK{Step12: FOS more than 2.0?}
    stepK -- YES --> outputSafe[Design ACCEPTED]

    stepL --> stepH
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Spider Coupling

```mermaid

    shaft1[Driving Shaft] --> hub1[Hub 1 with 3 or 6 Jaws]
    hub1 --> spider1[Polymer Spider Element]
    spider1 --> hub2[Hub 2 with Mating Jaws]
    hub2 --> shaft2[Driven Shaft]
    spider1 --> mechA[Mechanism: Compression and Shear Deformation]
    spider1 --> mechB[Mechanism: Torsional Vibration Damping via tan delta]

    spider1 --> mechD[Mechanism: Overload Protection Sacrificial Failure]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Failure Cascade (Educational)

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Stage1: Initial Operation] --> s2[Stage2: Polymer Element Warms to Operating Temperature]
    s2 --> s3[Stage3: Sustained Load Triggers Creep Deformation]
    s3 --> s4[Stage4: Misalignment Exceeds Polymer Compensation Limit]
    s4 --> s5[Stage5: Hub Jaw Impacts Spider Teeth Region]
    s5 --> s6[Stage6: Localised Stress Concentration and Cracking]
    s6 --> s7[Stage7: Polymer Tooth Shear and Spider Failure]
    s7 --> s8[Stage8: Coupling Acts as Safety Fuse Shaft Remains Intact]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between rigid and flexible couplings. Name two polymer-based flexible couplings.**

**Model Answer:**

A **rigid coupling** is used when the two shafts are *perfectly aligned* (no misalignment possible) and provides a *positive, no-slip* torque transmission. Example: sleeve/muff coupling, flange coupling. They cannot accommodate misalignment and cannot damp vibrations.

A **flexible coupling** can accommodate *parallel, angular, and axial misalignment* between shafts and also provides *vibration damping* and *shock absorption*. They are classified into metallic-element and non-metallic (polymer) element types.

**Two polymer-based flexible couplings:**
1. **Spider (Jaw) Coupling** — uses a polyurethane elastomer spider.
2. **Tyre Coupling** — uses a natural rubber or neoprene tyre.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Why is polyurethane commonly used as the spider element in elastomeric jaw couplings? Mention two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

Polyurethane (PU) is preferred because:

1. **High load-bearing capacity combined with flexibility** — PU has a high tensile strength (30–60 MPa) yet a relatively low modulus (20–80 MPa at Shore A 85–95), enabling it to deform and recover under cyclic torque.
2. **Excellent abrasion and tear resistance** — critical because the spider undergoes repeated compression/shear at the jaw interfaces, where wear would cause backlash.
3. *(Bonus)* Good oil/chemical resistance compared to natural rubber, and a wide operating temperature range (-40°C to +80°C).

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **classification of couplings** with neat sketches. Highlight the place of **polymer-based flexible couplings** in the classification. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **rigid flange coupling** is used to transmit **$P = 30 \, \text{kW}$** at **$N = 250 \, \text{rpm}$**. The shaft and key material has $\tau_{allow} = 40 \, \text{MPa}$ and $\sigma_{c,\,allow} = 80 \, \text{MPa}$. The service factor $K_S = 1.75$. The coupling has **4 bolts** on a bolt circle of diameter $D_b = 180 \, \text{mm}$ and flange thickness $t_f = 25 \, \text{mm}$. Design: **(i) shaft diameter, (ii) square key size ($b \times h$), and (iii) bolt diameter.** **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Classification of Couplings** — *as in Section 2.1 + Section 4.1.* Draw a clean hierarchy: Rigid (Sleeve, Clamp, Flange) and Flexible (Metallic-element: Bushed-pin, Oldham, Universal; Non-metallic: Spider, Tyre, Diaphragm, Resilient bush). Mark polymer couplings inside the non-metallic box.

**(b) Numerical Design:**

**Step 1 — Nominal torque:**

$$T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 30}{2 \pi \times 250} = \dfrac{1{,}800{,}000}{1570.8} = 1146 \, \text{N·m} = 1{,}146{,}000 \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Design torque:**

$$T_{design} = 1.75 \times 1{,}146{,}000 = 2{,}005{,}500 \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Shaft diameter (ASME):**

$$d^{3} = \dfrac{16 \times 2{,}005{,}500}{\pi \times 40} = 255{,}402 \, \text{mm}^{3}$$

$$d = \sqrt[3]{255{,}402} = 63.5 \, \text{mm} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{adopt } d = 65 \, \text{mm}$$

**Step 4 — Key design (assume square key $b = h = 18 \, \text{mm}$).**

Length for shear ($\tau_k = 60 \, \text{MPa}$):

$$l = \dfrac{2 \times 2{,}005{,}500}{65 \times 18 \times 60} = 57.1 \, \text{mm}$$

Length for crushing ($\sigma_c = 80 \, \text{MPa}$):

$$l = \dfrac{4 \times 2{,}005{,}500}{65 \times 18 \times 80} = 85.7 \, \text{mm}$$

Adopt $\mathbf{l = 90 \, \text{mm}}$ (governed by crushing).

**Step 5 — Bolt design.** Each bolt carries shear force $F_b$ at radius $r = D_b/2 = 90 \, \text{mm}$.

Tangential force on bolt circle:

$$F_t = \dfrac{T_{design}}{r} = \dfrac{2{,}005{,}500}{90} = 22{,}283 \, \text{N per bolt}$$

Force per bolt:

$$F_b = \dfrac{F_t}{n} = \dfrac{22{,}283}{4} = 5571 \, \text{N}$$

Assuming $\tau_{bolt} = 30 \, \text{MPa}$:

$$d_b = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 F_b}{\pi \tau_{bolt}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 5571}{\pi \times 30}} = \sqrt{236.5} = 15.4 \, \text{mm}$$

Adopt $\mathbf{d_b = 16 \, \text{mm}}$ M16 bolt.

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **design considerations for polymer spider (jaw) couplings**. Include material selection, geometry, and creep. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer **tyre coupling** transmits **$P = 12 \, \text{kW}$** at **$N = 960 \, \text{rpm}$**. The shaft diameter is $d = 40 \, \text{mm}$, hub outer diameter $D = 80 \, \text{mm}$, and tyre thickness $t = 12 \, \text{mm}$. Compute: **(i) nominal torque, (ii) interference pressure assuming shrink-fit with hub hoop stress limit $\sigma_h = 35 \, \text{MPa}$, (iii) factor of safety if ultimate tensile strength of tyre polymer is $\sigma_{ult} = 45 \, \text{MPa}$.** **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution

**(a) Design Considerations for Polymer Spider Couplings:**

1. **Material Selection** — Hardness (Shore A 80–95 for PU), tensile strength, oil/chemical resistance, $T_g$ well above service temperature, damping factor ($\tan \delta$).
2. **Geometry** — Number of lobes (3 or 6), tooth profile, hub fit (interference or clearance), lubrication.
3. **Creep & Stress Relaxation** — Sustained torque causes time-dependent deformation; Findley equation used.
4. **Temperature Rise** — Hysteresis heating in PU must be limited to ~80°C.
5. **Misalignment Limits** — Typically ±0.2 mm parallel, ±1° angular.

**(b) Numerical Computation:**

**Step 1 — Nominal torque:**

$$T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \times 12}{2 \pi \times 960} = \dfrac{720{,}000}{6031.9} = 119.4 \, \text{N·m} = 119{,}400 \, \text{N·mm}$$

**Step 2 — Interference pressure** (Lamé equation):

$$p = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_h \, l}{d} \cdot \dfrac{D^{2} - d^{2}}{D^{2} + d^{2}}$$

Wait — for an *internal pressure on hub* we use the inverse Lamé form. Given $D = 80$, $d = 40$, and $l = t = 12 \, \text{mm}$:

$$p = \dfrac{\sigma_h \left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)}{2 \, d \, l \cdot \left(\dfrac{D^{2}+d^{2}}{D^{2}-d^{2}}\right)^{-1}}$$

Simplifying using the standard KTU form:

$$p = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_h \, l \left(D^{2} - d^{2}\right)}{d \left(D^{2} + d^{2}\right)}$$

$$p = \dfrac{2 \times 35 \times 12 \times (6400 - 1600)}{40 \times (6400 + 1600)} = \dfrac{840 \times 4800}{40 \times 8000} = \dfrac{4{,}032{,}000}{320{,}000} = 12.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Factor of Safety:**

We compare hoop stress in the tyre (taken as equivalent to interference stress):

$$\sigma_{design} \approx p = 12.6 \, \text{MPa}$$

$$F.S. = \dfrac{\sigma_{ult}}{\sigma_{design}} = \dfrac{45}{12.6} = 3.57$$

The coupling is **safe** (FOS > 2.0 is the KTU-accepted criterion).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the service factor** $K_S$ — most common deduction (–2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up shear and crushing key length formulae** — always compute *both* and adopt the *larger* value.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion** ($P$ in kW demands division by 60,000 — not 60 or 6000).
> 4. **Not adopting standard sizes** — examiners expect $d = 22 \, \text{mm}$ (not $20.4$) and standard key lengths ($14, 18, 20, 25...$).
> 5. **Writing "PU is flexible"** — too vague. Specify *Shore A hardness*, *tensile strength*, and *$\tan \delta$*.
> 6. **Ignoring creep in polymer elements** — KTU 2024 explicitly tests polymer-specific behaviour; mentioning only metallic equations loses 2–3 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Coupling = torque transmitter + misalignment compensator + vibration damper.**
- **Polymer couplings** use elastomers (PU, NR, NBR, CR) and rigid polymers (Nylon, Phenolic, PTFE).
- **Five functional demands:** torque, misalignment, damping, overload protection, environmental resistance.
- **Mandatory starter formula:** $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60{,}000}$ → $T = \dfrac{60{,}000 \cdot P}{2 \pi N}$.
- **Always apply $K_S$** before sizing — service factor is *non-negotiable* in KTU problems.
- **Shaft diameter formula** (ASME): $d = \left(\dfrac{16 K_S T}{\pi \tau_{allow}}\right)^{1/3}$.
- **Key check** — both shear ($\tau_k = 2T/(d b l)$) AND crushing ($\sigma_c = 4T/(d t l)$) — adopt the larger length.
- **Hub hoop stress** — Lamé's equation governs interference fits.
- **Polymer-specific:** Findley creep law $E_c(t) = E_0 / (1 + (t/\tau)^n)$ — unique to polymer design.
- **Spider / jaw coupling** is the *most-asked* coupling in KTU — know its anatomy and material choices.
- **Tyre coupling** is preferred for *high misalignment* and *high damping* applications.
- **Standard FOS for polymer element ≥ 2.0** (some textbooks quote 2.5–3.0 for creep-loaded polymers).
- **Memorise:** PU for spider, NR/CR for tyre, Nylon for bushings, Phenolic for rigid flange plates, PTFE for sliding elements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beltings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT601 - POLYMER PRODUCTS - DESIGN & TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beltings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Polymer Beltings?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Beltings* are **continuous flexible polymeric products** engineered for two principal industrial functions:
> (a) **Conveying** (transportation of bulk solids, unit loads, or packaged goods across distances — *conveyor belts*), and
> (b) **Power Transmission** (transfer of mechanical power between two shafts via friction or positive engagement — *transmission belts* such as flat belts, V-belts, and timing belts).
> They are typically multi-layer composite structures comprising a **polymer matrix (rubber/PVC/PU)**, a **reinforcement layer (fabric/steel cord/aramid)**, and optional **friction/cover surfaces**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Polymer Highway"

Think of a **belt** as a *highway with teeth*:

- A **conveyor belt** is like a **moving pedestrian walkway** in an airport. The polymer cover is the *walking surface* (smooth or rough depending on the material carried), the reinforcement plies are the *steel skeleton under the floor*, and the rubber skim is the *cushion that absorbs shock* when heavy luggage drops on it.

- A **V-belt** is like a *wedge stuck in a groove*. The harder you pull on it, the tighter the groove grips it (this is the **self-energizing friction** principle). The fabric/steel cords inside are like the *steel strands in a tyre* — they carry the load, while the surrounding rubber transfers grip and damps vibration.

- A **timing/synchronous belt** is like a *chain with rubber teeth*. It does not slip because each tooth meshes with a pulley tooth — a *positive drive* with zero slip, like a zipper.

### 1.3 Classification of Polymer Beltings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 4:** Beltings are broadly classified into **Conveyor Belts** and **Transmission Belts**, each with sub-classifications based on construction, drive mechanism, and application.

| Family | Sub-Type | Drive Mechanism | Typical Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conveyor Belts** | Textile (fabric) ply | Friction drive of pulleys | NR, SBR, BR, CR |
| | Steel cord | High-tension conveyance | NR + steel cords |
| | Solid woven / PVC solid woven | Monolithic carcass | PVC, PU |
| **Transmission Belts** | Flat belt | Friction (open/crossed) | Chrome leather (replaced by PU/NR) |
| | V-belt (classical, narrow, cogged) | Wedge friction | NR + polyester cords |
| | Timing/Synchronous belt | Positive (toothed) | CR/PU + glass/aramid cords |
| | Poly-V / Ribbed | Multi-groove friction | CR/EPDM + polyester |

### 1.4 Why Polymers Dominate Beltings

- **Flexibility over millions of cycles** without fatigue failure.
- **High coefficient of friction** (μ ≈ **0.4–0.7** for rubber on steel).
- **Resistance to abrasion, oils, ozone, heat** (selectable via polymer chemistry).
- **Lightweight** (density **0.9–1.4 g/cm³** vs. steel **7.85 g/cm³**).
- **Damping of vibration and shock loads**, protecting both motor and driven machine.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conveyor Belt — Constructional Anatomy

A conveyor belt is a **sandwich composite** of the following layers (from top to bottom):

1. **Top Cover Rubber** — thickness **3–10 mm**; protects the carcass from abrasive/impact loading of conveyed material. Polymer selection depends on material type (heat-resistant SBR/EPDM for clinker, oil-resistant NBR for oily materials, food-grade PU for food contact).
2. **Skim Coat / Tie Gum** — thin layer (**0.5–2 mm**) of high-adhesion rubber cement that bonds the cover to the carcass.
3. **Carcass (Reinforcement Plies)** — the load-bearing backbone. May be:
   - **Textile plies** — cotton, polyester (PES), nylon (PA), or polyester/nylon (EP) woven fabric; rated by tensile strength per ply (e.g., **N/mm** or **kN/m**).
   - **Steel cords** — for long-haul, high-tension belts (mining, port handling).
   - **Solid woven** — single-layer fabric impregnated with PVC.
4. **Bottom Cover Rubber** — thickness **1.5–6 mm**; protects against idler friction, drift, and small particle ingress.

### 2.2 Conveyor Belt — Design Theory

A conveyor belt running over two pulleys experiences three tensions:

- **T₁** — tight-side tension (highest, on the carrying run after the drive pulley).
- **T₂** — slack-side tension (lowest).
- **Tc** — centrifugal tension (proportional to belt mass and square of velocity).

The **effective working tension** for a horizontal conveyor is:

$$T_W = T_1 + T_2 + T_c$$

The **maximum belt tension** at the drive pulley is the sum of all tensions plus the **effective pull** required to move the load (which equals the sum of resistances from material, idlers, elevation, etc.).

### 2.3 Belt Tension Ratio — Eytelwein's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important equation** for friction-driven belt drives (flat belts, V-belts). It is **high-yield for KTU ESE**.

For a belt wrapping a pulley over arc angle **θ** (in radians) with coefficient of friction **μ**, the ratio of tight-side to slack-side tension is:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

For a **V-belt**, the sidewall friction in the trapezoidal groove **amplifies** the effective friction. With groove half-angle **α** and belt-side friction coefficient **μ**, the modified ratio becomes:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta / \sin \alpha}$$

### 2.4 Power Transmission Formula

Power transmitted by a friction belt is the **difference in tensions times belt velocity**:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2) \times v$$

where **v** is the belt velocity given by:

$$v = \frac{\pi D N}{60,000} \text{ (m/s)}$$

with **D** = pulley diameter (mm), **N** = pulley rpm.

The **centrifugal tension** that reduces effective grip:

$$T_c = m v^2$$

where **m** = belt mass per unit length (kg/m). The **effective working tension** on the pulley is therefore:

$$T_{eff} = T_1 - T_2 = (T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 + T_c)$$

### 2.5 Belt Length Equations

> [!NOTE]
> **Open Belt Drive Length:**
> $$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \frac{(D-d)^2}{4C}$$
>
> **Crossed Belt Drive Length:**
> $$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \frac{(D+d)^2}{4C}$$
>
> where **C** = centre distance, **D** = larger pulley diameter, **d** = smaller pulley diameter.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units used in KTU problems:** Forces in **N** (or kN), lengths in **mm** or **m**, velocity in **m/s**, power in **kW**, angles in **radians**.

| # | Formula | Description / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ | Eytelwein's tension ratio (flat/cylindrical) | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta/\sin\alpha}$ | V-belt tension ratio (grooved pulley) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$ | Power transmitted by friction belt | W |
| 4 | $v = \pi D N / 60{,}000$ | Belt linear velocity | m/s |
| 5 | $T_c = m v^{2}$ | Centrifugal tension in belt | N |
| 6 | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D-d)^{2}/(4C)$ | Open belt length | m or mm |
| 7 | $L = 2C + (\pi/2)(D+d) + (D+d)^{2}/(4C)$ | Crossed belt length | m or mm |
| 8 | $T_W = T_1 + T_2$ | Working tension of conveyor (horizontal) | N |
| 9 | $\theta = \pi - 2\alpha$ | Wrap angle on smaller pulley, open belt | rad |
| 10 | $n_p = \sigma_w \, t \, B$ | Ply rating for textile conveyor belt | N/mm |
| 11 | $B = Q / (v \, \rho \, k)$ | Required belt width for conveyor capacity | m |
| 12 | $\sigma = (T_1 - T_c)/A$ | Working stress on belt cross-section | N/mm² |

*(Note: All vertical bar delimiters in equations use $\vert$ to preserve Markdown table integrity.)*

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

- **Mining & Mineral Processing** — Steel-cord belts (up to 30+ km single-flight length).
- **Automotive** — Timing belts for cam-shaft drive (replaced chains for quieter operation).
- **Agriculture** — Harvestor conveyor belts (silicone/specialty rubber covers).
- **Food Industry** — White PU/PVC belts meeting FDA/EC food-contact norms.
- **Textile Industry** — Spindle drive tangential belts (high modulus PU flat belts).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Eytelwein's Equation (Belt Friction)

Consider an elementary belt element of arc length **ds = R dθ** wrapped over a pulley of radius **R**.

**Forces acting on the element:**
- Tension **T** on one side, **T + dT** on the other side.
- Normal reaction **dN** acting radially inward.
- Friction force **μ dN** acting tangentially, opposing impending slip.

**Step 1: Equilibrium of tangential forces**
The difference in tension is balanced by the friction force:

$$dT = \mu \, dN$$

**Step 2: Equilibrium of radial forces**
The two tension components toward the centre must balance the normal reaction:

$$dN = T \, d\theta$$

*Explanation:* For a small arc, the two tensions form a wedge whose radial inward component is **2 T sin(dθ/2) ≈ T dθ** when dθ is small. Summing over both sides gives the radial equilibrium.

**Step 3: Substitute dN into dT**

$$dT = \mu \, T \, d\theta$$

**Step 4: Separate variables**

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\theta$$

**Step 5: Integrate from slack side (T₂) to tight side (T₁) over wrap angle θ**

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \int_{0}^{\theta} \mu \, d\theta$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

**Step 6: Exponentiate**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}\;}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Effective Power Transmitted

**Step 1:** From the tension ratio:

$$T_1 - T_2 = T_2\!\left(e^{\mu\theta} - 1\right)$$

**Step 2:** The belt moves at velocity **v**, so the tension-difference force does work at the rim of the pulley:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$P = T_2\!\left(e^{\mu\theta} - 1\right) v$$

**Step 4:** Belt mass produces centrifugal tension **T_c = m v²**, which loads both sides equally and **does not contribute to torque**. The torque-carrying tensions are **T₁ − T_c** and **T₂ + T_c**.

**Step 5:** Refined power:

$$P = \big[(T_1 - T_c) - (T_2 + T_c)\big] \, v = (T_1 - T_2 - 2T_c)\, v$$

**Step 6:** Substituting T_c = m v²:

$$P = (T_1 - T_2)\,v - 2 m v^{3}$$

**Step 7:** Take **dP/dv = 0** to find the optimum velocity that maximizes transmitted power for a given **T₁**:

$$(T_1 - T_2) - 6 m v^{2} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{T_1 - T_2}{6m}}$$

**Step 8:** Maximum power at this velocity:

$$\boxed{\;P_{max} = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{3}}\,\frac{(T_1 - T_2)^{3/2}}{\sqrt{m}}\;}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Style — 14 Mark)

> **Problem:** A V-belt transmits **15 kW** at a linear speed of **17 m/s**. The diameter of the driving pulley is **300 mm** and that of the driven pulley is **600 mm**. The groove angle is **38°**, coefficient of friction is **0.25**, centre distance is **1500 mm**, and the belt mass per metre is **1.2 kg/m**. Calculate: (a) the tensions in the tight and slack sides, (b) the required belt length.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Tensions**

**Step 1 — Power and velocity relation:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{15{,}000}{17} = 882.35 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Wrap angle on smaller pulley (open belt):**

$$\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{D - d}{2C}\right)$$

$$\theta = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{600 - 300}{2 \times 1500}\right) = \pi + 2 \sin^{-1}(0.1)$$

$$\theta = 3.1416 + 2(0.10017) = 3.342 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — V-belt tension ratio (half-angle α = 19°):**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu \theta}{\sin\alpha}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{0.25 \times 3.342}{\sin 19°}\right)$$

$$\sin 19° = 0.3256 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{0.25 \times 3.342}{0.3256} = 2.566$$

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{2.566} = 13.02$$

**Step 4 — Solve for T₂:**

$$T_1 - T_2 = 882.35 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_2(13.02 - 1) = 882.35 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; T_2 = 73.43 \text{ N}$$

**Step 5 — Compute T₁:**

$$T_1 = 13.02 \times 73.43 = 956.04 \text{ N}$$

**Part (b) — Belt Length (open belt):**

$$L = 2C + \frac{\pi}{2}(D+d) + \frac{(D-d)^{2}}{4C}$$

$$L = 2(1500) + \frac{\pi}{2}(300 + 600) + \frac{(300)^{2}}{4 \times 1500}$$

$$L = 3000 + 1413.72 + 15 = 4428.72 \text{ mm} \approx 4.43 \text{ m}$$

> **Sanity check:** Centrifugal tension $T_c = m v^{2} = 1.2 \times (17)^{2} = 346.8$ N. Since $T_c < T_2$, the belt will not slip due to centrifugal loading alone — design is safe. ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymer Selection for Belting Components

| Belt Type | Cover / Body Polymer | Reinforcement | Hardness (Shore A) | Service Temp |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat-resistant conveyor | EPDM / SBR-Heat grade | EP fabric | 60 ± 5 | -40 to +180 °C |
| Oil-resistant conveyor | Nitrile (NBR) / CR | Nylon/PES | 65 ± 5 | -25 to +120 °C |
| Food-grade conveyor | Polyurethane (PU) | None / PES | 85–95 | -20 to +80 °C |
| V-belt (industrial) | NR / CR | Polyester cord | 70 ± 5 | -30 to +90 °C |
| Timing belt | CR / H-NBR | Glass fibre / Aramid | 75 ± 5 | -25 to +120 °C |
